# Ethical Practice Issues and Concerns

Sebastian Striefel

Personal values and moral beliefs influence the choices people make (Lewis, 2003). Do you understand how your values and personal moral and/or ethical beliefs might impact the decisions you make in dealing with professional dilemmas in daily practice? Do you remain current on relevant practice guidelines and standards, and ethical principles such as those of the Association for Applied Psychophysiology and Biofeedback (AAPB; 2003; Striefel, 2004a)? Do you practice in ways that maximize positive client outcomes, minimize or eliminate potential harm to clients, and manage and minimize risk to yourself (Reeves, 2004)? To gain some current information, read on.

This chapter provides a concise update of ethical and other information that affects professional practice today, references for further reading, and a strong recommendation that all practitioners engage in lifelong learning via continuing education as a means to enhance and maintain professional competence while simultaneously minimizing provider risk. For ethical self-protection, practitioners should be realistic, learn the rules (e.g., laws and ethical principles), develop an ethical sensitivity (e.g., awareness), tighten up procedures used (e.g., informed consent), and seek ongoing consultation and/or supervision (Campbell, Vasquez, Behnke, Kinscherff, 2010; Zuckerman, 2008). So keep these self-protection items in mind as you read this chapter, and think about how you can develop a virtuous character and practice more ethically for the benefit of the clients served (Striefel, 2003a). Remaining current on ethical and legal issues is essential to professional survival.

# Sources  of Guidance

Ethical principles and practice guidelines, and standards alone, are insufficient for resolving the ethical dilemmas encountered in daily practice (Campbell et al., 2010; Jordan & Meara, 2008; Striefel, 2003a). One must also consider the human issues involved (e.g., one’s own values, biases, and emotions, and the client’s pain, affect, and relationship with the practitioner; Striefel, 2003a). Practitioners should make a lifelong commitment to developing a virtuous character, to continuing education, and to maintaining the highest standards of ethical behavior (Striefel, 2003a, 2004a). Developing a virtuous character means striving to develop specific moral or virtuous qualities and to behave morally in all situations (Striefel, 2003a). Some of the virtues a practitioner might strive to acquire and implement include prudence, integrity, respectfulness, compassion and caring, and truthfulness. See Striefel (2003a) and Jordan and Meara (2008) for a more complete discussion of virtues. Those with a well-developed moral character make better moral decisions than those who do not (Kitchener & Anderson, 2010).

Professionals, perhaps out of habit, often try to resolve ethical dilemmas by relying on their own personal values; however, this can be problematic, because not everyone has values that are acceptable to others (Kitchener & Anderson, 2010; Striefel, 2003b; Zuckerman, 2008). “Values” are a provider’s beliefs, attitudes, moral likes, desires, and priorities that provide direction in everyday professional activities (Campbell et al., 2010; Kitchener & Anderson, 2010; Striefel, 2003a, 2003b). “Ethics” focus on engaging in those behaviors that are morally correct and in the interests of those served (Striefel, 1995, 2003a, 2004b); core or foundational “ethical principles” consist of common, moral norms that exist across time and provide the foundation for the ethical codes developed by various health care professions (e.g., the principle of nonmaleficence [do no harm], principle of beneficence [do good], and the principle of autonomy (Kitchener & Anderson, 2010; Striefel, 2003a, 2003b). The “principle of autonomy,” for example, is the basis for several client rights, including informed consent, privacy, and confidentiality (Striefel, 2003a). See Knapp and VandeCreek (2012), Lindsay, Koene, Qvreeide, and Lange (2008), and Striefel (2003a, 2003b) for a more complete discussion of foundational ethical principles. An “ethics code,” or ethical principles, specifies the rules of conduct agreed upon by a professional group such as the AAPB (2003; Campbell et al., 2010; Striefel, 1995, 2003b, 2004b). “Laws,” on the other hand, are the minimal standards of conduct that a society will accept, as specified in legal statutes. Values, laws, foundational ethical principles, a virtuous character, practice guidelines and standards, and ethical codes interact to guide professional behavior, and none of these alone provides specific solutions for how to behave in all specific situations encountered by practitioners (Striefel, 2003b). Each provides general guidance and is subject to interpretation. Moreover, what is expected can change, based on any change in the circumstances involved, current laws, ethical principles, or practice guidelines and standards. Practitioners should adopt a “duty-based approach” and treat clients with respect and as having a right to choose their own destiny (autonomy) (Knapp & VandeCreek, 2012). They should also be proactive rather than reactive (Campbell et al., 2010).

sibilities in which one must choose between two rights (Corey, Corey, & Callanan, 2015; Striefel, 2003b, 2004b). Health care practitioners often do not recognize ethical dilemmas when they encounter them; thus, many ethical violations occur inadvertently, because providers are not aware of how their behavior or lack of behavior may adversely impact clients (Corey et al., 2015; Striefel, 2003b). For example, an ethical dilemma would occur if one is the only biofeedback provider in a geographical area and a client seeks help for a problem one is not competent to treat. Should one try to provide treatment or let the client suffer because one is not competent in that area of practice? The dilemma relates to choosing between doing what is best for that client and operating only in areas in which one is competent. There are many potential solutions to such a dilemma, including obtaining careful informed consent, obtaining appropriate training, seeking consultation or supervision, and even working closely with a practitioner who is competent in the skills needed via a telehealth, two-way audiovisual training arrangement. See also Campbell et al. (2010) for an extensive discussion and examples of not only dealing with situations in which services are not available from competent practitioner but also applying all of American Psychological Association’s ethical principles (2010) in a diverse set of circumstances and situations.

To deal with an ethical dilemma, one must first recognize that a dilemma exists (Striefel, 2003b; Zuckerman, 2008). Some ethical dilemmas are inherent in the process of providing service to clients, in conducting research, and in supervising or training others. It is the practitioner’s responsibility to understand when, where, why, and how ethical dilemmas arise, because it is always the therapist who is the accountable party in a treatment relationship (Lange, 2008; Striefel, 2003b). Most malpractice lawsuits arise from foreseeable problems that might be avoided if practitioners acquire and maintain the needed skills to recognize and anticipate them (Striefel, 2003b; Zuckerman, 2008).

# Ethical F unctioning

# Ethical D il emm as

Professionals encounter ethical dilemmas daily. An “ethical dilemma” is any controversy that involves conflicting moral principles or respon

Practitioners are encouraged to go beyond the minimum required by law, ethical principles, or other rules and regulations when working with clients, and to strive to do what is in the best interests of the client, while simultaneously striving to maximize benefits and minimize harm, even though additional time and effort may be required (Campbell et al., 2010; Striefel 2003a, 2003b). Doing more than the minimum requires thinking about potential situations that are likely to arise and how to deal with them (Striefel, 2003a). For example, do you have a plan for how to deal with suspected child abuse, a suicidal client, or a client who is HIV-positive? By planning ahead, one can be proactive rather than reactive when such situations arise (Campbell et al., 2010). One can develop such plans by reading, attending conferences, or seeking consultation. Planning ahead and being proactive involves functioning at an aspirational level of ethical functioning. When teaching students or supervising others, practitioners would do well to instill in them a desire to function at an aspirational level (Pipes, Holstein, & Aguirre, 2008). When professional groups do not adhere to a reasonable level of ethical functioning, society often restricts professional practice by passing laws to ensure acceptable levels of functioning (Corey et al., 2015; Striefel, 2003b).

# Acquiring App ropriate Ethical  Behavior

Biofeedback practitioners should adhere to the ethical principles, practice guidelines, and standards of professional groups to which they belong (e.g., AAPB, 2003; Striefel, 2004a).1 Ethics committees and courts increasingly hold practitioners accountable for adhering to nationally available ethical principles and practice guidelines and standards, even when practitioners are not members of a specific association (Striefel, 2004a, 2004b; Zuckerman, 2008). Remaining current requires ongoing education and effort. In fact, many states now require practitioners to obtain continuing education hours on ethics to renew their licenses (Striefel, 2003b). Continuing education is needed for several reasons. First, behaving ethically is much more complex today than in the past, and the situations encountered by practitioners are changing rapidly because of new laws, legal precedents, changing standards of care, ethical code changes. An example is the new security regulations concerning confidentiality in the Health Information Technology Act (HIT; Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology [ONCHIT], 2008) and the rapid changes in technology (iPhones, iPads, etc.). Second, managed care continues to raise ethical issues not addressed by existing ethical codes; for example, managed care companies often refuse to pay for biofeedback services or they approve far fewer sessions than are needed to treat a client’s problem. Third, Internet access has raised consumer awareness and access to information about what to expect ethically and legally from practitioners (Nickelson, 2000; Striefel, 2003b).

How does one learn appropriate ethical behavior when each situation requires a different solution, especially since most ethical principles/codes are vague and provide only general guidance? The keys to appropriate ethical practice are self-analysis (Campbell et al., 2010), including awareness of personal moral values and acculturation as a professional; knowledge of foundational ethical principles; ongoing education and a good professional library; written policies and procedures; an ethical decision-making process; good documentation; and the lifelong use of supervision and consultation (Campbell et al., 2010; Striefel, 1995, 2003b). See Striefel (1995, 2003b) for a more complete discussion of each of these items.

The process for learning ethical behavior starts with integrity. Having integrity means that a practitioner takes serious the task of developing a virtuous character and being honest, accurate, and truthful in all of his or her professional activities (Barnett & Johnson, 2008; Striefel, 2003a). The practitioner does not steal, cheat, engage in fraudulent billing, or deceive or mislead clients (Barnett & Johnson, 2008). Rather, he or she realizes that misconduct can have negative consequences for clients, third-party payers, professional colleagues, and his or her reputation and the reputation of the services he or she provides (e.g., biofeedback). The process of learning includes acquiring skills in ethical decision making.

# Ethical Decision Making

Two equally important and interrelated components for ethical decision making are (1) “ethical competence,” which means knowing what to do, and (2) a “commitment” to actually doing what one knows is right. Many practitioners are able to determine what they should do ethically but fail to actually do it (Bersoff, 2008; Bernard & Jara, 2008). For example, would you be willing to report a good friend and colleague if you became aware that he or she had committed a serious ethical violation? If not, what factors would keep you from doing so? What could you do to motivate yourself to actually deal with and/or report the violation? Consultation in such situations is often an important component. Knapp, Gottlieb, Berman, and Handelsman (2008) even provide suggested guidelines to follow if a practitioner believes (even after consultation) that the right thing to do is not obey a specific law (civil disobedience). Not obeying a law can have serious consequences for the practitioner or even the client. Suppose a practitioner decides not to report suspected child abuse in order to keep the perpetrator in treatment, and the perpetrator then injures or kills a child. The practitioner can now become a defendant in a liability lawsuit.

Making decisions with ethical implications is something practitioners do every day. All biofeedback practitioners need to make responsible decisions in a client-centered manner (Zsolnai, 2009) and have available some model for making ethical decisions (Knapp & VandeCreek, 2012; Striefel, 1995, 2003a, 2003b, 2009). It is important to remember that ethics is described more accurately as a process rather than just adherence to a set of rules, and that ethical dilemmas are not necessarily straightforward or easily resolved (Campbell et al., 2010). The decisions one makes should be effective and efficient in producing the desired outcomes (Zsolnai, 2009; Striefel, 2009). It is during the decision-making process that knowledge, competence, and commitment to doing what is right are fine-tuned for guiding appropriate ethical behavior (Striefel, 2003b). One can begin the decision-making process by answering several questions:

See Zsolnai (2009) for a complete discussion of this last issue. The answers to each of these questions can be helpful in ethical decision making, especially if applied systematically within the context of a systematic decision-making model.

Practitioners should use an ethical decisionmaking model repeatedly, so that doing so becomes an automatic habit when an ethical dilemma is encountered. Ethical decision making must be adjusted to the complexity of the issues, the time available for making the decision, and the degree of preplanning in which a practitioner engages to resolve ethical dilemmas (e.g., a plan for dealing with a suicidal client). Decision making starts with the intention of putting the best interests of the client first and includes being nonjudgmental and accepting of the client (Qvreeide, 2008a). Motives, knowledge, and character, as well as principles, should help guide decision making (Knapp & VandeCreek, 2012). There are many ethical decision-making models. See Barnett and Johnson (2008), Knapp and VandeCreek (2012), Striefel (2003b), and Zsolnai (2009) for samples of models and decision making. A good ethical decision-making process is essential for resolving ethical dilemmas, planning for situations that are likely to occur, preventing potential injury to clients, protecting the best interests of clients, and preventing emotional distress and risk for the practitioner.

1. “What should I do or how should I deal with this situation?”   
2. “Would another reasonable practitioner in the same situation make the same decision and behave in the same way?”   
3. “What do my values and character virtues suggest that I should do in resolving the dilemma I have encountered?”   
4. “What do ethical theories, foundational ethical principles, relevant ethical codes, practice guidelines and standards, and laws suggest or mandate as solutions?”   
5. “Whom can I consult with or seek supervision to identify appropriate alternatives for resolving the dilemma encountered?”   
6. “What do those affected by the decision want to have happen, or what is in their best interest?”   
7. “How should the ideal practitioner make this decision?”

# Ethical Appl ications

A number of principles and values are useful in guiding professional practice activities. Many of these principles are part of the AAPB’s ethical principles and practice guidelines and standards (AAPB, 2003; Campbell et al., 2010; Striefel, 2004a). As such, they are discussed only briefly here.

# Respect

Practitioners should protect and promote fundamental client rights (e.g., client autonomy, confidentiality, and self-determination), do no harm, and strive to maximize client benefits by doing good, being honest and trustworthy, and treating everyone fairly by respecting each client. “Respect” means doing what is in the client’s best interests by knowing one’s professional responsibilities, remaining competent via continuing education, restricting professional activities to areas in which one is competent, and knowing and abiding by professional boundaries. See Striefel (1995) and Qvreeide (2008b) for a more detailed list of examples of showing respect for clients.

# Responsibility

All professional health care practitioners, including biofeedback practitioners, are always responsible for what they do and fail to do (Campbell et al., 2010; Lange, 2008; Striefel, 2004a, 2004b, 2009). They also share legal and ethical responsibility for the behavior of those they supervise or employ (Striefel, 2004a, 2004b, 2006). A professional should not make excuses (e.g., blame a client) for his or her own failure to act responsibly (McDowell, 2000; Striefel, 2006). Responsible practice is based on informed, sound, and responsible judgment, and the professional socialization process experienced by a practitioner (Striefel, 2006). The skills needed to maintain a responsible practice are developed by reading; attending classes and workshops; obtaining frequent supervision and/or consultation; conducting self-evaluations, peer reviews and discussions; continuing education; and knowing one’s own areas of competence (Striefel, 1995, 2004b, 2009).

# Competence

Practitioners should “strive to provide services only in areas in which they are competent as based on education, training and/or experience, unless appropriately supervised by a supervisor competent in the relevant skill areas” (Striefel, 2004a, p. 27). A truly competent provider recognizes not only his or her limitations and deficits but also his or her skills and strengths (Campbell et al., 2010; Knapp & VandeCreek, 2012; Striefel, 2003a, 2009).

Ways for determining one’s level of competence include peer review, self-awareness, evaluation of one’s impact on clients, and taking exams, such as those required for state licensing or Biofeedback Certification Institute of America (BCIA) certification (Striefel, 2004a, 2004b). Professionals can assume that they are competent in specific practice areas if competent colleagues evaluate their performance and conclude that they have achieved acceptable standards of practice in those areas (Knapp & VandeCreek, 2012) and/ or if one consistently obtains positive outcomes with specific groups of clients. See Greenberg and Smith (2008) for a more comprehensive discussion of methods for evaluating professional competencies.

Remaining competent requires ongoing training both to maintain and to expand one’s areas of practice (Campbell et al., 2010). It is unethical to exaggerate one’s qualifications or skills, and engaging in such behaviors can lead to problems with clients and ethics committees, as well as possible malpractice litigation (Striefel, 1995, 2003b, 2004a). It is also important to be alert to signs of one’s own fatigue, burnout, and personal/ emotional problems. When such conditions exist, making mistakes and behaving incompetently are more likely. When signs of fatigue occur, corrective action should be taken (e.g., taking a vacation, seeking personal counseling, cutting back on work hours, or engaging in other activities that will alleviate the problem).

# Confidential ity and Privil eged C omm unication

“Confidentiality” refers to the ethical responsibility of a professional to protect a client from the unauthorized disclosure of information obtained in a treatment relationship (Campbell et al., 2010; Corey et al., 2015; Striefel, 1995, 2004b). “Privileged communication” is defined in legal statutes and when it applies, it protects clients from having their confidential information revealed in a legal proceeding without their permission (Striefel, 1995, 2004b). There are practical, but often complex, exceptions to implementation of both confidentiality and privileged communication.

# Confidentiality

At the onset of treatment, clients should be informed of the limits of confidentiality within the informed consent process, preferably with written documentation (Campbell et al., 2010; Striefel, 1995, 2004a, 2009). Since the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) require a written Privacy Notification, the limits of confidentiality may be included therein. Some common limits of confidentiality include laws that require breaching confidentiality, such as reporting suspected and/or actual child abuse or using a third-party payer. See Knapp and VandeCreek (2012) and Striefel (2003b) for a more complete discussion of the limits of confidentiality.

If clients are aware of the limits of confidentiality at the onset of services, they can decide whether they want to enter treatment under these conditions, and if so, how much information to reveal. It is unethical to videotape or audiotape a client without his or her permission. Practitioners should also address the practical components of privacy and confidentiality, such as ensuring that one’s professional space has a sound barrier, so that private information from a treatment session cannot be overheard in the hall or waiting room, or that the receptionist does not use client names on the telephone or give verbal telephone messages that others can overhear (Striefel, 1995, 2003b). A failure to address such practical considerations can be a violation of a client’s right to privacy. Care should also be taken to ensure that one is in compliance with the security of records requirements of HIPAA (American Medical Association, 2001) and the newer HIT (ONCHIT, 2008) concerning computerized and other electronic records (e.g., using passwords, encryption, and backing up the computer system regularly). Special care should be taken to protect confidentiality when electronic methods are used for storing or transmitting confidential information and appropriate informed consent on the limits of confidentiality that arise in such situations (American Psychological Association, 2006,2010; Campbell et al., 2010).

# Privileged Communication

In most jurisdictions, the client owns the “privilege” in privileged communication (American Psychological Association, 2006). Therefore, the provider cannot generally release information without the client’s consent in any legal proceeding (Smith-Bell & Winslade, 2008; Striefel, 1995, 2004b). All states have privileged communication statutes for attorneys, psychologists, and physicians. Some also have laws that cover some other health care professionals. Individual practitioners are responsible for finding out whether their activities are covered by such a law and what the components of the law are (Striefel, 1995, 2003b, 2009). Privileged communication laws have limits and exceptions, and they vary from state to state and by professional discipline (Striefel, 1995, 2004b).

Two good premises for a practitioner to operate under are these: (1) When in doubt, do not give out information—call your attorney instead; and (2) if no compelling reason exists, do not give it out without consulting with your client, even if the client initially requested that you release the information (e.g., the benefits to the client should outweigh the risks, and when that is not so, the client and his or her attorney should be aware of your concerns; Striefel, 1995, 2003b). Whenever a practitioner violates a client’s confidentiality or reveals privileged information, it is critical to document the situation, steps taken, consultation sought, rationale used to justify it, outcome, and anything else that seems relevant (e.g., a court order by a judge). If the client and his or her attorney give permission for information to be released, a Release of Information form should be signed and kept on file.

The testimony and/or records of a practitioner are sometimes subpoenaed in an effort to obtain information for civil or criminal court proceedings (American Psychological Association, 2006). For guidance on how to proceed in such situations, see the American Psychological Association (2006) guidelines, Striefel (2003b), and Zuckerman (2008).

# Practice Areas with E thical Impl ications

A number of practice areas have ethical implications.

# Informed Consent

“Informed consent” is an ongoing ethical and legal obligation to provide information to a client and to obtain his or her permission before engaging in treatment and related activities (Campbell et al., 2010; Knapp & VandeCreek, 2012; Striefel, 2004a, 2004b, 2009). Many federal and state laws require that informed consent be in written form (Campbell et al., 2010). A client is entitled to the information needed, and to share with the practitioner the responsibility for making reasonable treatment and related choices (Striefel, 2003b, 2004b). For example, it is important to discuss various cautions, contraindications, and side effects of proposed treatments (Striefel, 2009). It is also important for practitioners to consider how the information will be shared with the client (Jordan & Meara, 2008). Sensitivity to the client, prudence, and fidelity are all part of an integrated process for deciding what information to share and/or how to do so. Truthful informed consent can help to establish trust and a meaningful relationship with clients by making them a meaningful part of the decision-making process (Campbell et al., 2010). Informed consent is mandated in the AAPB’s practice guidelines and standards (Striefel, 2004a). For a complete discussion of the components of informed consent, see the work of Campbell et al. (2010) and Striefel (2003b, 2004a, 2004b, 2009). Practitioners should ask questions to verify that the client understands the information presented and should carefully document the informed consent process. Clients and research subjects should be informed that they can refuse to participate in any procedure, and that they can withdraw or request changes without any penalty (Striefel, 1995). Elsewhere Striefel (1999, 2002) and Zuckerman (2008) have provided samples of informed consent forms.

# Multiple Relationships and Other Boundary Issues

Multiple relationships (MRs) occur whenever a practitioner interacts with others in more than one role (e.g., as therapist and teacher for a client or as therapist and supervisor for a student; Campbell et al., 2010; Lamb, Catanzaro, & Moorman, 2008; Striefel, 2004b, 2009). The most likely problematic MRs are sexual relationships between practitioners and clients, supervisees, or students; nonsexual social and professional relationships (e.g., dinner with a former client or employing a current supervisee); and financial–business relationships, which are especially common in rural areas (e.g., having car fixed by a client) (Lamb et al., 2008).

Not all MRs can be avoided, nor are all of them problematic if a practitioner is cognizant of the boundary issues involved and knows how to deal with them in an ethically appropriate manner (Campbell et al., 2010; Striefel, 2002, 2004b). Ethically, meeting the client’s needs should guide the practitioner’s behavior when MRs occur. It is best to avoid potentially problematic MRs, because they can impair a practitioner’s objectivity, competence, and/or effectiveness and lead to exploitation (American Psychological Association, 2010; Campbell et al., 2015). For a complete discussion of MRs and boundary issues, see Campbell et al. (2010), Corey et al. (2015), Knapp and VandeCreek (2012), and Striefel (2003b, 2004b).

# Warning Signs

To deal with MRs and other boundary issues, practitioners should learn to recognize when such a relationship or issue has arisen, and they should know how to deal with the dilemma posed (Knapp & VandeCreek, 2012; Striefel, 2003b). When such a dilemma cannot be avoided via referral, a practitioner should be careful to obtain full informed consent from a client. Consent should include discussing the risks and benefits associated with both roles and other boundary issues. The nonpractitioner role may have to be put on hold temporarily or permanently (e.g., social interaction may have to cease); consultation or supervision may be essential to maintaining the best interests of the client; the practitioner’s loyalties and priorities should be clarified for the client; the developed plan for how to proceed needs to include how problems that may arise will be resolved; the practitioner’s family needs to know that there will be times when certain social interactions will not be allowed, and times when there will be no discussion when those situations arise; and the practitioner needs to learn to recognize warning signs that potential problems are arising (Striefel, 2003b). Referral, consultation/supervision, informed consent, continuing education, restricting one’s practice activities to certain kinds of clients, and individual psychotherapy for the practitioner are all potential solutions for dealing with the warning signs. Barnett and Johnson (2008), Knapp and VandeCreek (2012), and Striefel (2003b) discuss various warning signs and how to deal with them.

Circumstances that may justify crossing boundaries include times when (1) the situation is in the best interests of the client and the risk of injury is nonexistent or low, and (2) the situation is totally unavoidable. For example, clients who are bedridden are much more receptive to in-home treatment of mental-health-related problems than to treatment in an office. When proper precautions are taken, successful treatment can be provided to clients who might otherwise not seek or participate in treatment.

# Sexual MRs

Sexual relationships with clients, former clients, students, supervisees, or employees are unethical (Campbell et al., 2010; Striefel, 2009). Practitioners should not engage in such behavior, and should report other professionals who do to the appropriate licensing board and ethics committee (Corey et al., 2015; AAPB, 2003; Striefel, 2004a). Clients are exploited and suffer when practitioners enter into sexual relationships with them, and courts view sexual relationships with patients as grounds for malpractice (Knapp & VandeCreek, 2012). In addition, in some states, it is a felony to have sexual contact with a current or former client (Corey et al., 2015; Striefel, 1995, 2003b). The provider, not the client, is always held accountable for deviations from acceptable standards of care and the consequences that occur (Striefel, 1995, 2003b, 2004a, 2009).

# Treatment

Practitioners generally have the following three functions when accepting clients for treatment: a healing function, an educational function, and a technological function in which various treatment techniques are applied to modify client behavior (Koocher & Keith-Spiegel, 2008). There are numerous ethical guidelines on factors to consider in carrying out each of these three functions. The AAPB (1977) has published a checklist of items to consider in developing ethical treatment plans. The checklist seems relevant to those providing biofeedback and related services, and covers items such as ethical goals, treatment options, client rights, appropriateness or referrals, and practitioner competence. The American Psychological Association Practice Organization (APAPO; 2013) has published a wide array of guidelines that biofeedback practitioners might find useful. They can be downloaded at www.apa.org/practice/prof. html. They include guidelines for working with women; older adults; lesbian, gay, and bisexual clients; and multiculturally diverse populations.

A practitioner has an ethical obligation to accept into treatment only those clients whose problem(s) he or she is competent to treat, to refer and/or consult as needed if a client is not making progress, and not to terminate (abandon) a client in need (Campbell et al., 2010; Knapp & VandeCreek, 2012; Striefel, 2009). Striefel stressed the importance of being knowledgeable about the efficacy of the various treatments available for dealing with a client’s problems and information about the publications attesting to the level of validation for various treatments. Yucha & Montgomery (2008) provide additional information on efficacy of treatments, and Striefel (2009) provides information on cautions and contraindications to consider in providing some forms of biofeedback and related treatments. See the other chapters in this book for specific information on treatment, including Chapter 16 by Schwartz, Schwartz, and Monastra, for more information on cautions and contraindications.

# Supervision

“Supervision” is a process for conveying knowledge and skills from a supervisor to a supervisee (Striefel, 2004a). The supervisor oversees and guides the activities of the supervisee (Striefel, 2004a). AAPB practice guidelines and standards (Striefel, 2004a) state that the level of supervision provided depends on variables such as the knowledge, skills, and experience level of the supervisee, and the complexity or the client’s problem(s). Students and new employees would prudently receive more supervision during assessment of their knowledge, skills, and competence than would clinicians with many years of experience and demonstrated levels of competence (Campbell et al., 2010). A reasonable minimal level of supervision might be that which is often required in state licensing laws (i.e., at least 1 hour of face-to-face or other appropriate supervision for each 40 hours and/or 1 week of services provided by the supervisee).

Supervisors need to be sufficiently available to ensure that each client receives quality service, is unharmed, and that supervisees adhere to all legal and ethical expectations and requirements (Striefel, 2004a). Incompetent, inadequate, exploitive, and abusive supervision are all unethical and can lead to harm to clients and puts supervisees and supervisors at risk (American Psychological Association, 2010; Campbell et al., 2010; Goodyear, Crego, & Johnson, 2008; Striefel, 2004a). Inadequate supervision includes abandoning a supervisee in need of guidance by not being available regularly and when needed. This should dissuade supervisors from employing and trying to supervise large numbers of unlicensed people. See the AAPB practice guidelines and standards for additional supervisory requirements (Striefel, 2004a).

Because of risk of harm to clients, it is unethical to practice outside one’s areas of competence, including competence as a supervisor (American Psychological Association, 2010; Campbell et al., 2010; Goodyear et al., 2008; Knapp & VandeCreek, 2012; Reeves, 2004; Striefel, 2009). Some states now consider experience as a supervisor to be insufficient preparation for being a supervisor and therefore mandate supervisory training for supervisors to ensure at least minimal competency (Granello, Kindsvatter, Granello, Underfer-Babalis, & Hartwig-Moorhead, 2008). Granello et al. outline a peer consultation model that they have found useful for enhancing the skills of supervisors at all levels of skills development.

An ethical and prudent supervisor who is not competent to provide supervision for biofeedback and/or related applied psychophysiological services should engage in one or more of the following activities: (1) hire a practitioner who is competent in the necessary supervisory, biofeedback, and related skills to provide supervision of those activities for the particular organization; (2) obtain additional training and supervision to become competent to supervise biofeedback services; and/ or (3) cease to accept clients for biofeedback services if competent and ethical supervision is not available (Striefel, Whitehouse, & Schwartz, 2003). Supervisors share ethical and legal responsibility with the persons they supervise or ethically should supervise and what those supervisees do or fail to do (Campbell et al., 2010; Striefel, 1995, 2004a, 2004b; Striefel et al., 2003; Zuckerman, 2008). If the supervisee is negligent, the supervisor’s license, reputation, and income are at risk if a lawsuit or ethics complaint is filed; more importantly, a client might suffer harm. As such, it is in everyone’s best interest for the supervisor not to agree to provide supervision in areas in which he or she is not competent. Competent supervision requires training, reading, ongoing monitoring, and feedback. A competent professional knows what to do, how to do it, when to do it, and when to seek consultation first (Striefel, 1995, 2004a).

# Therapist/Practitioner Availability

Accepting a person as a client creates an ethical and legal obligation to be available in a timely manner when needed or to have another competent provider (backup) available (Campbell et al., 2010; Knapp & VandeCreek, 2012; Striefel, 2003b). This availability includes accessibility or backup services during out-of-office hours and in emergency situations. Both the AAPB (Striefel, 2004a) practice guidelines and standards and the American Psychological Association (2010) code of ethics (Campbell et al., 2010) require reasonable efforts to have in place procedures for dealing with interruptions in service (e.g., emergencies, illness of practitioner and/or his or her death or retirement). When clients are in pain (physical or psychological) and cannot reach their service provider, they often become unhappy, and some initiate action against the provider on the basis of abandonment (Striefel, 1995, 2003b). Charges of abandonment can occur when a practitioner is not available and does not have backup coverage available when a client emergency occurs, when the practitioner leaves a client alone in a biofeedback session without informed consent, or when the professional does not have an answering service or pick up messages in a timely manner (Striefel, 2003b, 2004a). A practitioner also has an ethical obligation to prepare clients for his or her absence for vacations, emergencies, or other absences (Striefel, 2003b, 2004a).

A practitioner’s procedure for being contacted during out-of-office hours and for accessing emergency backup should be part of the informed consent process with clients. Clients should be given copies of appropriate telephone numbers and procedures (Striefel, 2004a).

# Referrals and Termination

A practitioner can refuse to accept anyone as a client (Knapp & VandeCreek, 2012; Striefel, 1995, 2003b; Zuckerman, 2008). However, practitioners should not refuse services to a person purely because of his or her age, gender, or race, or in any other way discriminate against any class of people (which is an injustice and illegal) (American Psychological Association, 2010; Campbell et al., 2010). It is also important to accept as clients only those one is reasonably competent to treat within the minimal standards of care (Campbell et al., 2010; Striefel, 1995, 2003b; Zuckerman, 2008). Exceeding one’s areas of competence can readily increase a practitioner’s stress level and may result in problems for clients and the practitioner alike.

In deciding on which referrals to accept as clients, a practitioner needs to know his or her areas of competence and limitations, may need to have available a supervisor/consultant for new areas, and needs to have a systematic process available for collecting the critical information to determine whether the referral is appropriate (Campbell et al., 2010; Knapp & VandeCreek, 2012; Zuckerman, 2008; Striefel, 2003b, 2004b, 2009). A process is also needed for obtaining relevant records from other providers (with a signed release of records from the potential client), for sharing critical information with the potential client (e.g., fees, collection procedures, and parking), and for interacting with the client’s physician about medically related matters.

It is ethically expected that practitioners will refer clients elsewhere and/or terminate services when the client needs a service that the practitioner cannot provide, even if supervision is available (Campbell et al., 2010; Knapp & VandeCreek, 2012; Striefel, 2003b, 2009); the practitioner has become emotionally involved and is unable to be effective (Zuckerman, 2008); the practitioner has personal problems that interfere with his or her being able to meet the client’s needs; the client is not making progress with the services provided, even after several different approaches have been tried, including the use of consultation; the client has achieved the goals of treatment; or the practitioner is moving or will no longer be able to provide services (Striefel, 1995, 2004b, 2009).

Once a referral is made, it is useful to have the client sign a Release of Information form, so that when contacted by the new provider, the practitioner can share information with this provider in a timely manner. It is also useful when terminating or referring a client to review his or her file and write a summary of the services provided (Knapp & VandeCreek, 2012; Striefel, 2003b). The practitioner should record in the file the reason for termination or referral and the details of the process used, information shared, and anything else that is important.

# Ethical Record Keeping

Client files and business records should be thorough and accurate (American Psychological Association, 2007; Campbell et al., 2010; Striefel, 1995, 2004b, 2009; Zuckerman, 2008). It is unethical not to have sufficient documentation for meeting client needs (Campbell et al., 2010; Striefel, 1995, 2003b; Zuckerman, 2008). A client’s records should make clear what was done, when, why, where, how, and by whom. Client records fulfill many different purposes for different stakeholders and include reviewing client progress; motivating the client; communicating with other service providers (with client permission); protecting the practitioner if a client, or someone else on the client’s behalf, initiates litigation or files an ethical complaint against the provider; adhering to state and federal legal requirements; and testifying in court in reference to a client (e.g., workers’ compensation litigation) (Striefel, 1995, 2003b, 2004b).

Failure to maintain adequate records is a basis for a negligence lawsuit, and inadequate records are often grounds for third-party payers (e.g., Medicare) to file fraud charges (American Psychological Association, 2007). Practitioners should know their state laws in reference to records retention, billing and collections, records review, and record ownership (American Psychological Association, 2007; Striefel, 1995, 2004b; Zuckerman, 2008). All computerized and digital records should be backed up regularly, and backups should perhaps be kept in a separate location(s) to ensure that a disaster does not destroy the records (Striefel, 2009). HIPAA (American Medical Association, 2001) and HIT (ONCHIT, 2008) have some specific security regulations that practitioners should be aware of and to which they should adhere.

Zuckerman (2008) and Striefel (2004b) provide listings of the information that should be contained on an ethical and legal release-of-records form. In fact, Zuckerman (2008) provides several samples of forms to use for different release purposes. The American Psychological Association (2007) has published some very comprehensive record-keeping guidelines, including issues related to meeting HIPAA requirements.

# Summ ary

It is important for practitioners to take ethics and their applications seriously. Know the available community emergency resources; engage in lifelong learning; form a network of consultants and supervisors and use them regularly; know and abide by all relevant state and federal laws, policies, and regulations, ethical principles, and practice guidelines and standards; find a competent attorney who specializes in areas related to your practice activities; know your areas of competence and limitations; be sensitive to the professional relationships you develop, especially those with clients and supervisees; document carefully; ensure continuity of care for each client; get full informed consent (preferably in writing); maintain confidentiality; be open in your communication with clients and address their concerns; take seriously all threats of suicide or harm to others; do not accept all referrals, and refer clients to other practitioners when this is appropriate to client needs; think and act preventively and conservatively; be proactive; do not promise cures; make accurate diagnoses; take care of your own needs and health to avoid burnout or exploitation of clients; respect individual differences; terminate services properly; and limit your scope of practice to areas in which you are able to maintain your competence (Campbell et al., 2010; Koocher & Keith-Spiegel, 2008; Kitchener & Anderson, 2010; Striefel, 1995, 1999, 2002, 2003b, 2004a, 2004b, 2009; Zuckerman, 2008). Finally, develop written policies and procedures for guiding your professional behaviors in all of the aforementioned areas as a means of behaving proactively and aspiring to the highest level of ethical functioning (Striefel, 2003b).

1. Both documents can be obtained from the AAPB at 10100 West 44th Avenue, Suite 304, Wheat Ridge, CO 80033-2840.

# Myths, Insurance, and Other Professional Topics and Issues

Sebastian Striefel, Ronald L. R osenthal, Robert Whitehouse, and Mark S. Schwartz

# Myths and Facts

Of increasing importance is the number of myths concerning biofeedback, and the cautions and concerns associated with them. Some of the myths and the facts related to them follow.

•	 Myth 1. Biofeedback devices do not need any oversight before being marketed.

•	 Facts. The U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA), whose rules and regulation are quite complex, provides some level of oversight for biofeedback devices to ensure that they are safe and effective (Striefel, 2002). The level of oversight provided depends on whether the FDA considers the device a Class I (low risk and little oversight), a Class II (most biofeedback devices) or a Class III (high risk and stringent oversight) medical device (Striefel, 2002; www.fda.gov/cdrh/ devadvice). Legitimate biofeedback devices used in clinical work have generally gone through the FDA process and have been approved for specific uses; the manufacturer has filed a 510(k) Premarket Notification form to determine whether the product is safe, or has initiated or completed a registration process for having the device classified as exempt from the premarket notification process (Striefel, 2002; www.fda.gov/cdrh/devadvice). Generally, battery-operated devices used for relaxation training or muscle reeducation are exempted from the premarket notification process because they are deemed safe (Striefel, 2002). The word “safe” generally is used, because it is during/after the registration process that an FDA decision is made on whether a premarket notification is necessary. Exempt devices must be registered and are still subject to other FDA rules and regulations. Interested parties should ask the manufacturer or seller about the FDA status of any instrument being marketed as a biofeedback device. Health care practitioners in particular should address this issue before purchasing any device for clinical use. The FDA generally classifies a biofeedback device as a Class II medical device. For various reasons, it can chose to give a device a different classification (e.g., a classification of Class III is common if a device is intended for a new use). The FDA can choose to restrict or not restrict a prescription device (www.fda.gov/newsevents/testimony/ ucm096272.htm, sections 502f and 520e). The FDA may restrict a device for sale, distribution, or use only upon the authorization of a practitioner licensed by law to administer or use such a device, or upon other conditions that the FDA prescribes (www.fda.gov/newsevents/testimony/ucm096272. htm). If a device is classified as being restricted, then legally it requires the involvement of an appropriately licensed practitioner before it can be sold to or used by the general public. If a device is unrestricted, it can be sold and used by the general public. Most, if not all, battery-operated devices are classified as unrestricted, but there can be exceptions. It can be confusing to ascertain the status of a particular device. The FDA does, however, respond to e-mail requests, and its website provides information on how to contact the FDA to determine the classification and status of particular device. If a device has gone through the FDA process, it means that the use of that device/ equipment has been deemed safe for the approved uses. The practitioner is at risk if he or she uses a nonapproved biofeedback device or uses one for purposes not yet approved, especially if a client has been be injured. Of course, the practitioner is also at risk if he or she does not maintain the purchased equipment and a client is injured because of this lack of maintenance (e.g., the client gets an electrical shock because of a damaged electrical cord; Striefel, 2004). On rare occasions in the past, the FDA seized biofeedback devices that were being used with clients/patients if the devices or their use had not been FDA-approved, usually after a complaint was filed or a client reported injury, real or imagined. It is unclear whether the FDA still takes such action, but it certainly has the authority to do so.

•	 Myth 2. Biofeedback services can be provided anywhere by anyone.

•	 Facts. Clinical biofeedback services should be provided or supervised by appropriately trained and credentialed health care practitioners. Legitimate biofeedback services are generally provided in a professional setting, such as a hospital, clinic, or the office of a health care professional. If a practitioner is licensed in a health care discipline, then it means that he or she has a health care background and has been licensed to provide certain services as defined by law within the scope of practice for that discipline. To provide biofeedback services legally in some states, a practitioner must be licensed in a specific health care discipline, be supervised by representatives of the same, or be exempted from that requirement by some aspect of the law (Striefel, 2004). For example, students who are training to become a healthcare professional are often exempted, provided that they are supervised by a licensed professional. Not all biofeedback practitioners are licensed in a health care discipline. Some unlicensed practitioners work under the supervision of a licensed health care practitioner; others work independently. If one is seeking services from or referring clients to a biofeedback/neurotherapy/neurofeedback practitioner, it might be important to determine whether the practitioner is legally allowed to provide such services within the state of practice (in some states, unlicensed practitioners are not allowed to provide services independently) and to ensure that the practitioner has at least an adequate level of competence (Hammond & Kirk, 2007; Striefel, 2007a, 2007b). A license indicates that the state recognizes that the practitioner has the background, experience, and some level of competence in the services provided by members of that specific discipline. Certification in biofeedback by the Biofeedback Certification International Alliance (BCIA), or some other accredited certification institute, is one indicator of some level of competence in biofeedback. Some areas of application do not require licensure in a health care discipline (e.g., sports psychology).

•	 Myth 3. All the advertisements one sees about biofeedback are truthful and accurate.

•	 Facts. In fact, not all items advertised as biofeedback meet the definition of being biofeedback (see Schwartz, Collura, Kamiya, & Schwartz, Chapter 1, this volume). Nor are all advertisements truthful or even accurate (Striefel, 1995, 1999, 2009). As in all professions, providers of biofeedback services and equipment vary in term of their ethics and morals. While the vast majority of biofeedback professionals strive to serve the public in a professional and compassionate manner, a small minority of professionals seem to be out to make a profit, with little regard for the safety and well-being of their clients or customers. Biofeedback professionals who seek to purchase new systems should be aware that some companies are selling very costly systems that do not meet the basic definition of a biofeedback device. If a manufacturer or sales representative cannot state precisely what is being measured and recorded, treat this as a red flag and take reasonable precautions for determining the legitimacy of the item. Caution should be exercised whenever one sees advertisements that guarantee success, when claims are made that sound too good, and when those providing the biofeedback or other services do not have an appropriate background or refuse to disclose their education and experience. In biofeedback, some sort of sensor is attached to the client’s body (e.g., attaching a thermistor to the fingertip to record skin temperature) and the client receives some sort of feedback (e.g., visual, audio, or both) about what is happening in his or her own body (e.g., the skin temperature at the thermistor attachment site). The operant conditioning literature makes clear that the more instantaneous the feedback, the more rapid the learning. In some phases and some practices, the therapist chooses to receive the feedback during some parts of the session and give immediate or periodic feedback to the client. The client learns to modify his or her own physiology by using the feedback (and/or guidance from the therapist) to develop a strategy that works for him or her. Some devices advertised as biofeedback do not use medically understood sensing technologies or do not involve the client in the “reprogramming” process (Slawecki, 2009). Rather, the process seems to be one in which energy levels, or some other undefined parameter, are manipulated by the practitioner and something is done to the client rather than an action performed by the client. It is unclear whether the client will be able to control the supposed parameter without the presence of the intermediary, or whether the client is even intended to learn some form of control (Slawecki, 2009). Some of the devices do not record feedback or use medically understood physiological information (e.g., feeding back energy imbalances when it is unclear how an energy imbalance is defined; Slawecki, 2009). One might say that devices using unconventional sensing technologies that cannot be readily explained to clients are examples of nonbiofeedback devices (Slawecki, 2009). Perhaps in the future some of these devices will become biofeedback devices as more research becomes available to explain how they work. Licensed health care practitioners and state licensing boards can often tell consumers whether complaints have been filed against a particular practitioner. In addition, one can contact BCIA (bcia@resourcenter.com) for the names of certified biofeedback practitioners and the Association for Applied Psychophysiology and Biofeedback (AAPB; aapb@resourcenter.com) or International Society for Neurofeedback and Research (ISNR; office@isnr.com) for information on the legitimacy of a particular biofeedback device or one being marketed as such.

•	 Myth 4. There is no need to be concerned about electroencephalographic (EEG) biofeedback games that are being marketed directly to the public.

•	 Facts. Caution should also be exercised in relation to the current trend to market game devices that, for example, use electrodes attached to the scalp, so that “one’s brain activity” can be used to fly a helicopter, drive a car, or fly an airplane. In a narrow sense, if the device is in fact using a computer–brain interface, it can be defined as biofeedback. However, the safety of these devices is as yet unknown, since research is scarce and they are not to our knowledge FDA controlled. We do know from legitimate EEG biofeedback use (sometimes called “neurofeedback” or “neurotherapy”) that when a person is trained to modify one aspect of his or her brainwaves, often other aspects also change, and not necessarily in the desired direction (Hammond & Kirk, 2007). Practitioners can provide a service to the public by helping to identify what is safe and what might not be safe in terms of biofeedback services and devices, including those marketed as games.

•	 Myth 5. Biofeedback devices marketed directly to the public to, for example, reduce stress are safe, and such practices are legal.

•	 Facts. In most cases, this is probably true. Published research on many of the devices is scarce or nonexistent, but fortunately the research base is increasing. Still, some caution is warranted before purchasing or using such devices. Many devices that do provide real-time physiological feedback to the user are being marketed directly to the public for purposes such as relieving stress and/or serious medical and psychological problems (Hammond & Kirk, 2007). In some cases, this marketing is in direct violation of FDA requirements, for example, avoiding registration of equipment that should be registered with the FDA or marketing a controlled medical device directly to the public (most often this would be a device that plugs into an electrical outlet, i.e., a non-battery device) that may or may not be optically isolated for safety. The marketed devices cover a wide range of modalities and include medical devices that by law should be registered with the FDA and not marketed directly to the public without the involvement of a licensed health care practitioner, and devices for which oversight requirements of the FDA are less clear (e.g., breathing devices, heart rhythm devices, and galvanic skin response units). It is not clear whether any or all of these devices measure and feed back the physiology as reported in the accompanying literature, whether they are in fact safe for use, whether they can be misused in ways that could compromise the user’s physiology, and whether they are exempt from FDA oversight. Some devices are exempted by the

FDA; usually, if not always, these are battery-operated devices. To date, no published studies indicate that any of these devices have caused harm, and some are quite popular with both the public and practitioners. However, Hammond and Kirk reported that there are numerous reports on public and professional Internet listserve websites about side effects and adverse reactions to EEG biofeedback (i.e., when equipment is marketed directly to the public) that range from mild and transient to very serious (e.g., depression, seizures, and deterioration in cognitive functioning). Whether such risks exist for non-EEG devices is unclear, since research data are scarce. To date, there are no published formulated standards of practice that specify the requirements, if any, before any biofeedback devices outside the scope of FDA jurisdiction are marketed directly to the public. Legally, such standards are not needed if one accepts the premise that the FDA will intervene if it determines that public safety is a concern. The law makes clear the requirements for devices that are within the FDA’s jurisdiction. What kind of backlash would occur for legitimate biofeedback if a member of the general public were injured because he or she purchased and used a non-FDA-controlled or illegally marketed biofeedback device? What action, if any, should be taken by legitimate biofeedback practitioners and associations such as the ISNR and AAPB? More information on such devices is provided by Schwartz and Andrasik (Chapter 9, this volume). Conducting well-controlled research seems to be part of the answer in terms of dealing with home use and direct sales of biofeedback devices to the public.

•	 Myth 6. If a biofeedback device has been approved by the FDA and is sold directly to the public, it is always safe to use it without supervision.

•	 Facts. Just because a device is dispensed within the confines of the law does not mean that all of the general public has the knowledge to use the device safely without some supervision. Nonrestricted FDA-registered devices are sold directly to the public. Some FDA-controlled biofeedback devices are dispensed to the public via a written prescription by a biofeedback practitioner, and a nonrestricted device might be recommended by a practitioner for use by the client. For example, a physician might recommend in a written clinical report that a client purchase or use a particular device, or recommend it verbally, without actually writing a prescription for one. When home practice is a part of the client’s treatment plan, the client should agree to it in the informed consent process. The device might be loaned, sold, or rented to the client, with oversight of the home practice provided by the practitioner via periodic office visits, telephone and/or Internet consultations, or a two-way telehealth audio and video communication arrangement. In addition, practitioner and client have agreed on what the client should do if there are problems, if the client has questions, or if other issues arise (Striefel, 2004). Practitioners are encouraged to take precautions with anyone with whom they have a professional– client relationship. Authorizing home training without careful assessment, review of records, emergency contact arrangements, treatment plan, and confidence that the client can make progress via home training (e.g., because that confidence is based on previous client success in making progress via office based biofeedback sessions or at least going through an informed consent process before recommending the use of a home use device) can place the practitioner at risk if he or she has deviated from the expected standard of care for clients and the client experiences or claim injury (Barnett & Johnson, 2008; Striefel, 2000).

•	 Myth 7. All biofeedback training is clinical treatment or therapy.

•	 Facts. Not all biofeedback fits the requirements of being therapy or clinical treatment. Biofeedback that is used as therapy or treatment is provided to a referred client/patient or to one who seeks help with some diagnosable health problem, physical or psychological. In addition, some or all of the services might be eligible for third-party reimbursement. Some biofeedback applications are provided to individuals who want to improve their physical performance in some sport (e.g., track and field), art form (e.g., playing a musical instrument), recreational activity (e.g., golf), or even some cognitive task (e.g., creative writing). Such individuals do not present or seek help as patients but as well-functioning individuals who wish to improve their peak performance. Such services are not eligible for health care third-party reimbursement. In therapy, the services are provided by a health care practitioner. In peak performance training, the services might be provided by either a health care practitioner or a sports physiologist, a coach, or a recreational specialist. In addition, there is a new trend of individuals buying sport watches, which can provide individuals with real-time feedback on various physiological parameters, such as heart rate, for the purpose of allowing the individual to monitor and improve his or her own functioning.

•	 Myth 8. All side effects of treatment are negative and should be avoided.

•	 Facts. Not all side effects of treatment are negative or to be avoided. Effective biofeedback treatment incorporates changes in physiology, emotions, cognition, and/or behavior. As such, both clients and practitioners should be aware that change will include both expected and unexpected shifts in physiology, behavior, symptoms, emotions, and/or cognition (Striefel, 2007a, 2007b). The presence of side effects means that something is happening, and when this change process is being managed by a competent practitioner, it means that any long-term or serious, negative side effects are unlikely. For example, relaxation-induced anxiety is a common side effect that can easily be managed by a competent practitioner (Schwartz, Schwartz, & Monastra, 2003, Chapter 16, this volume) who does a good job of keeping the client informed, seeks feedback from the client, and is sensitive to the particular client’s need to manage, reduce, and/ or eliminate any negative impacts. Of course, if a serious negative side effect causes harm to a client and can be directly attributed to the treatment, the practitioner may become responsible for costs and treatment, and be at risk for a lawsuit, and so forth. However, to date, any detected side effects that are directly due to biofeedback have readily been amenable to being reversed by biofeedback (Hammond & Kirk, 2007; Striefel, 2007a, 2007b). It does mean that practitioners should take reasonable precautions to become aware of and document carefully any unexpected physiological or other changes, and in some cases may well want to consult or have the client evaluated by an appropriate medical practitioner, if they are uncertain what a particular side effect means. For example, the average practitioner will not know whether a heart arrhythmia is serious. When in doubt about possible medical issues, it is prudent to refer the client to a physician. Given a particular client’s medical history, it might be prudent to have him or her evaluated by a medical practitioner before or while undertaking any sort of biofeedback treatment. Based on careful assessment, treatment needs to be individualized to include the individual client’s physiological data rather than assuming that one treatment protocol fits all clients with a similar presenting problem (Hammond & Kirk, 2007; Striefel, 2000).

•	 Myth 9. All the collected physiological data are either targeted or expected effects of training.

•	 Facts. Not all physiological information that is collected during biofeedback training is expected. One of us (Ronald L. Rosenthal, March 23, 2009) reported that sometimes when doing heart rate variability biofeedback, arrhythmias are detected. In the process of providing biofeedback training, practitioners accumulate extended records of various physiological processes. This information can potentially reveal unusual or abnormal physiological events. For example, a common observation might well be the presence of additional or dropped beats in heart rate records while conducting heart rate variability biofeedback training. These arrhythmias can be observed from at least two sources of heart rate detection, the electrocardiographic (ECG) record and/or blood pulse volume tracings from a photoplethysmograph. In a detailed record of the heart rate, a practitioner may observe a heart beat that is significantly faster or slower than prior beats, and that does not match the typical rhythm that had been occurring. It is important to rule out artifacts as the source of the erratic beats, so one must review the raw signal to determine whether movement or other extraneous factors are responsible for the variability. There are a number of sources of heart rate arrhythmias, and most of them are fairly benign (Fuster, O’Rourke, Walsh, & Poole-Wilson, 2007). Cardiologists speak of “ectopic beats,” which are contractions that arise from sources other than the usual sinoatrial pacemaker (Fuster et al., 2007). These heartbeat arrhythmias may well be previously undetected heart rate abnormalities. Usually the arrhythmias detected during biofeedback data collection are benign and of no concern, and a patient may already be aware, or may become aware, of the change (physician feedback, self-monitoring, or feedback from the biofeedback practitioner). If the client is unaware of the arrhythmia, the practitioner would do well to keep the client informed of what such an arrhythmia might mean and take the precautions of having a cardiologist do an evaluation to determine whether the arrhythmia is of long standing and serious, requiring treatment, and if so, what type of treatment, and so forth. The concern of biofeedback providers relates to what they should tell their patients and what other actions they should take. Once it has been determined that a heart rate arrhythmia is present, the practitioner needs to discuss this finding with the client. It is important to ask the client whether he or she is aware of any issues with his or her heart. In many cases, the client is already aware of the arrhythmia, having been informed of it by his or her primary care physician. If the client is unaware of the arrhythmia, it is important to reassure him or her, then recommend that the client makes an appointment with his or her physician for appropriate assessment and/or treatment. The client should be asked to sign a release of information form so that a copy of a representative tracing of the client’s heartbeat can be sent to his or her doctor, and so the biofeedback practitioner and the physician can interact to ensure that both remain informed and the client receives integrated care. It is interesting to note that some arrhythmias can occur less frequently after biofeedback training. However, there is one notable exception to the previous comments about arrhythmias, the respiratory sinus arrhythmia (RSA) which is the healthy pattern of the heart rate rising with an inhale and slowing with the exhale. In addition to irregularities in the heartbeat, other measures can potentially indicate some serious medical issues. Bilateral differences in temperature or blood pulse volumes may be due to peripheral vascular diseases (noted by Rosenthal). In surface electromyographical (EMG) recordings, involuntary muscle twitches may sometimes be observed. These twitches, or “fasciculations,” are typically benign, but in rare cases they may be indicative of a serious neurological disorder (Drost, Kleine, Stegeman, van Engelen, & Zwarts, 2007). Rosenthal had one case in which he was conducting biofeedback training of the anterior tibialis muscle to improve ankle dorsiflexion. The client was a young man who had sustained an incomplete spinal cord injury in a fall while working on a roof. The practitioner noticed low-amplitude bursts of activity after the completion of training on many trials. Since he did not know what they might be from, Rosenthal referred the patient to a neurologist, and the patient was diagnosed as having amyotrophic lateral sclerosis. To make matters worse, the workers’ compensation carrier tried to terminate benefits after this diagnosis, claiming that the initial fall was due to a preexisting medical condition. Because follow-up was not possible, further data are not available.

•	 Myth 10. Third-party payers such as Medicare will pay for all forms of biofeedback for any and all conditions.

•	 Facts. Third-party payers such as Medicare sometimes pay for some types of biofeedback services, for some types of conditions, and the reimbursement varies from discipline to discipline, state to state, diagnosis, background of the provider and his or her reputation, completeness of records, and the rules governing a particular client’s health insurance. Great care must be exercised by practitioners to ensure that they inform clients as to the limitations and requirements of their insurance providers (e.g., a limit of five sessions or the need for extensive information) and that they obtain the client’s consent to proceed (Acuff et al., 2008; Appelbaum, 2008; Knapp & VandeCreek, 2006). Care must also be taken to ensure that the practitioner complies with all of the third-party payer’s rules and regulations (Striefel, Whitehouse, & Schwartz, 2003). Doing so will maximize the likelihood of being reimbursed for services thus removing much of that burden from the client and it maximizes the likelihood that the practitioner will not violate the requirements in such a way as to face charges of fraud. If a third-party payer’s rules and regulations are not acceptable to a practitioner, he or she should not sign on as a preferred contractor/provider (Haas & Cummings, 2008). The courts have ruled repeatedly that one who signs a contract is expected to adhere to its requirements. Remember that the Current Procedural Terminology (CPT) Coding Committee’s advice (American Medical Association, 2015b) on coding is there to help the therapist choose the most appropriate code for the services provided, yet each insurance company chooses what codes and what services to reimburse for, for what time periods, and this may vary with a given client’s specific policy because policy choices are often made by a person’s employer. They also choose whom they will reimburse. Check with the insurance company for each client. Some insurance companies have posted on the Internet specific policies on whether biofeedback or psychophysiological therapy will be reimbursed at all, or if so, for what conditions.

•	 Myth 11. All practitioners who provide biofeedback services are honest about their credentials; operate according to relevant state and federal laws, rules, and regulations, ethical principles, and practice guidelines and standards; and restrict their practice to those areas allowed by the scope of practice of the licensing laws for their profession and in which they have demonstratable competence or are receiving appropriate supervision and/ or consultation.

•	 Facts. For the majority of biofeedback practitioners the previous statement is probably true.

However, there are exceptions, as documented by the complaints received by state licensing boards and ethics committees such as those of AAPB and BCIA, and by lawsuits filed against health care practitioners. Some of the complaints received by the AAPB and BCIA ethics committees in recent years include misrepresentation of credentials, working outside one’s areas of competence, inappropriate dual relationships, and operating outside of the scope of practice for one’s license (e.g., a dentist using biofeedback to treat incontinence; David Stump & Judy Crawford, personal communication, April 22, 2009). The aforementioned complaints are no different than those received by ethics committees for health care practitioners in various disciplines. For example, the licensing board for psychology in Arizona ruled that a psychologist who was providing nutritional services within his psychology practice was operating outside the scope of practice for his license and required him to stop providing such services from his psychology offices (Striefel, 2001). All biofeedback practitioners must be cautious and accurate in stating their credentials, operate within the scope of practice allowed by their license, avoid problematic dual relationships, and be in compliance with all relevant laws, rules and regulations, ethics codes, and practice guidelines and standards. Even then, problems may arise. In 2007, a social worker in Maryland received a “cease and desist” order from the State Medical Board for practicing medicine without a license, because the Flexyx Neurofeedback System (early version of the Low Energy Neurofeedback System [LENS] developed by Len Ochs) she was using sends a miniscule current to the client’s scalp (Stokes, 2007; Striefel, 2009). The medical board indicated that it was an invasive procedure, and the law restricted the use of such procedures to licensed medical practitioners. Ultimately, she was found innocent of wrongdoing, but the costs in terms of time, energy, dollars, stress, and reputation were great. As a practitioner, if you become aware that another practitioner is making false claims, behaving unethically, operating outside of the scope of practice for his or her license, you have an ethical obligation to take action to resolve the problematic behavior by interacting directly with the practitioner and/ or reporting it to the appropriate licensing board or ethics committee (AAPB, 2003; Striefel, 2004).

•	 Myth 12. Biofeedback is only relaxation training. One insurance company has asserted that biofeedback training has not been shown to be any more effective than “other relaxation therapies”; therefore, the insurance company will not pay for it.

•	 Facts. There are two fallacies in this argument: First, if a review of the research indicates that biofeedback training is no more effective than other therapies that are reimbursed, then why not include biofeedback codes as reimbursable? Second, biofeedback and applied psychophysiology trainers and therapists often utilize relaxation techniques, along with biofeedback instruments, to speed up and refine the learning process, but biofeedback is not necessarily relaxation training. It may be for optimization training, as in sports, music, and other performance applications. It may also have surface electromyographical (SEMG) uses in rehabilitation of injured muscles or paralysis, or down- or up-training of muscle recruitment of perineal muscles for overcoming incontinence, or with EEG retraining of brain function, as in brain injury, attention deficit disorder (ADD), or autism.

# Impl ications of V arious Job T itl es

The implications of various job titles was discussed in great detail by Striefel et al. (2003); therefore, we include only a few summary comments here.
