Care should be taken in developing job titles to ensure (1) that the job title accurately reflect the person’s duties and status; (2) that the person can legally use the title (e.g., the use of the title “psychologist” is restricted by state laws); (3) that if licensed, careful consideration occurs before a person uses a title other than the licensed title, to refrain from misleading the public (after all, health care titles are recognized and respected by clients, other professionals, and by third-party payers); (4) that professionals from traditional health care disciplines in states where licensing for independent practice is not an option (e.g., nursing and physical therapy), and who use biofeedback, consider retaining their respective professional discipline titles, because the titles are accepted and recognized; (5) that those who do not have a degree or license in a recognized health care discipline consider using one of the commonly recognized titles such as “biofeedback therapist,” “biofeedback practitioner,” “biofeedback technician,” “biofeedback assistant,” or “certified biofeedback therapist;” (6) that assignment of appropriate duties be based on a person’s education, training, experience, and competencies, and legal and ethical requirements; and (7) that appropriate levels of supervision be provided to avoid harming clients and to meet legal and ethical mandates (e.g., AAPB, 2003; Striefel, 2004).

A person’s job title should help other professionals understand what to expect, and can help a professional gain respect with that title. It also facilitates the person’s mobility across employment situations and state lines. Standardization of titles and functions is important for those who prefer to use the term “biofeedback” in their titles, or who need to do so because they have no standardized discipline or licensed title.

# Th erapist Presence or Abs ence

Clients generally make better progress when an actively present practitioner/therapist is providing encouragement, support, and coaching (Demos, 2005; Striefel et al., 2003). In fact, children seldom make progress if the biofeedback therapist is absent (Demos, 2005). Interacting with clients during treatment is one part of efficient and successful treatment. It is inappropriate and unethical to provide biofeedback treatment sessions to a patient who is left alone during treatment, unless such sessions are a planned part of the individualized treatment plan for which the patient has given informed consent, after fully understanding the implications of therapist absence (Striefel et al., 2003), and the absence must be in the best interests of the client (Weiner, 1998; Striefel et al., 2003). Cummings (1998) has stated that clients are ethically entitled to the alleviation of pain and suffering, using the shortest and most efficient means possible. One can argue that if the practitioner’s absence is not a part of the treatment plan and/or the practitioner is not available often enough to ensure client progress in achieving treatment goals, treatment “is not occurring” and such behavior on the part of the practitioner falls below the acceptable standard of care and might well constitute fraud. After all, 75% of all patients are insured by some form of managed care (MC; Sanchez & Turner, 2008), and MC has very tight timelines, so treatment must be provided in the shortest time possible without terminating treatment prematurely (Haas & Cummings, 2008), which may happen if the patient is left alone without the sufficient guidance and support offered by the practitioner’s presence and/or if the absence is not a planned part of the client’s treatment program. In a survey of 223 psychologists in Colorado, 64% reported incidents of managed care organizations (MCOs) terminating treatment prematurely (Sanchez & Turner, 2008). In an American Psychological Association survey of 718 respondents, 49% reported that their patients experienced negative consequences when treatment was delayed or denied (Sanchez & Turner, 2008). In addition, when patients are left alone for part or all of a treatment session, such as might happen if a patient is doing home-practice sessions or is engaged in self-practice to determine whether they can maintain progress in the therapist’s absence, or as a part of practice to generalize the skill, a variety of issues must be considered and/or addressed appropriately. These include but are not limited to the following: automating data collection, instructing and motivating the patient, client pretraining (e.g., on equipment use), maximization of learning, reimbursement issues (e.g., Will the insurance company pay for services?), continuity of care, addressing client dissatisfaction, skills generalization, and telehealth as a unique version of therapist absent.

Planned therapist absence also has the following advantages: allowing the patient to practice newly acquired physiological self-regulation skills in a safe and familiar setting, to see whether he or she can produce the desired changes with the therapist absent; helping the patient to produce physiological changes in his or her own daily environment; reducing the cost of treatment if the therapist charges less for times during which he or she is absent; and helping the patient gain confidence in his or her self-regulatory abilities (Striefel et al., 2003).

# Written Profess ional C omm unications

Prudent practitioners are careful in the development, dissemination, and storage of all client information, including written reports, physiological data, session notes, coding and billing, oral communication (e.g., with the referring physician), and letters, so as to maximize the benefit to clients and ensure that they are in compliance with all relevant ethics codes (e.g., AAPB, 2003), practice guidelines and standards (e.g., those of AAPB; Striefel, 2004), state and federal laws, rules, and regulations (e.g., those of the HIPAA; American Psychological Association, 2007), and the newer security requirements of the Health Information Technology (Office of the National Coordinator for Health Information Technology [ONCHIT, 2008) section of the Economic Stimulus Act that is being developed to ensure that, over a period of years, all patient records will be computerized and accessible from any appropriate health care location, with, of course, appropriate safeguards to client privacy and confidentiality). HIPAA has three rules concerning records: (1) the transaction rule, which creates a standard format for electronic transmissions having to do with thirdparty reimbursement; (2) the security rule, which has requirements for the physical infrastructure to protect confidential client information; and (3) the privacy rule, which specifies requirements for policies, procedures, and business agreements concerned with accessing patient records (Barsoff, 2008). Those involved in treating, billing, quality assurance, and training are allowed to share information without patient consent provided that they have received a Notice of Disclosure that informs them about how their information will be protected and/or shared (Barsoff, 2008). Psychotherapy notes, however, require specific authorization before being shared (Barsoff, 2008). Ethically, it is still a good idea to get a signed patient release of records before sharing information. Doing so may be required by state law (which supersedes HIPAA requirements if more stringent; Barsoff, 2008) or by the ethics code for practitioners (see the codes relevant to you and your activities), and more importantly, it continues to keep the patient involved in controlling his or her own destiny by including him or her in all aspects that could have an impact on him or her. The failure of a practitioner to keep proper records and protect patients’ confidentiality is now a basis for a malpractice claim (American Psychological Association, 2007; Zuckerman, 2008). The type and extent of records varies with the discipline, purpose, setting, and context of the services provided (American Psychological Association, 2007).

# Purposes Served by Written Communications

Written communications are developed and stored for many purposes: (1) to help develop, monitor, coordinate, implement, communicate, and improve the services received by clients from different practitioners; (2) to document the services provided to clients to meet legal obligations and third-party payer requirements; (3) to help practitioners defend themselves should legal action or an ethical complaint be filed against them (inadequate documentation is one of the most common grounds for fraud charges being filed against practitioners by Medicare; Foxhall, 2000; Striefel et al., 2003); and (4) to comply with all relevant state and federal laws, rules, regulations, and a profession’s ethical expectations (e.g., to ensure that another provider could take over the client’s treatment if a practitioner were no longer available; American Psychological Association, 2007: Striefel et al., 2003; Zuckerman, 2008). Those who develop records should ensure that they are accurate, complete, clear, and detailed enough that they truly represent and communicate what did and did not occur, and when, where, and by whom. For a more complete discussion on what to include in records see the American Psychological Association (2007), Striefel et al. (2003), and Zuckerman (2008). The American Psychological Association (2007) has published a very comprehensive set of record-keeping practice guidelines that readers my might find quite useful.

The individual practitioner is ultimately responsible for developing, maintaining, and retaining client records, and those records are often used by others as one means to make judgments about the practitioner’s competence. The records need to be complete, accurate, maintained, and stored in ways than ensure client confidentiality in accordance with relevant laws and ethics codes. The degree of detail needed in routine clinical practice has increased dramatically, because inadequate records are now seen as evidence of substandard care, regardless of how good the actual service was (Striefel et al., 2003; Zuckerman, 2008). If something is not written down, the courts and external review bodies assume that it did not occur (Foxhall, 2000; Striefel, 2003; Striefel et al., 2003; Zuckerman, 2008). If a third-party payer is billed for a service that was not carefully documented in accordance with the regulations of that payer (e.g., Medicare requires the record to document “medical necessity”), the provider is subject to charges of fraud, and the penalties for this can be severe, both financially and legally (Foxhall, 2000; Striefel et al., 2003). Do you know what constitutes “reasonable and necessary services” as defined by Medicare in your state? If you serve Medicare patients, it is essential to know how these terms are defined. The rules vary from state to state and across third-party payers. Because Medicare has such low reimbursement rates and the hassles associated with reimbursement take so much time, many physicians and other health care practitioners are no longer accepting Medicare patients (Connelly, 2009). So detailed reports may be better than those with very limited or no detail. The degree of detail depends on the situation, the professional, the client, and sometimes the client’s preferences. Some clients request that certain sensitive information not be included in the record in order to protect privacy, so the practitioner has to balance legal and ethical requirements, risk, and sometimes client preference (American Psychological Association, 2007). A client might even go so far as to require that a minimal record be kept as a condition of receiving treatment (American Psychological Association, 2007). The practitioner must then make a decision about whether to provide services to this client. If the client is not seeking third-party reimbursement for treatment, keeping minimal records is easier to deal with; however, if third-party reimbursement is being sought, the provider has an obligation to make clear to the client the level of detail and extent of record keeping that will be necessary. It is also important to make clear to the client that once information leaves the practitioner’s office (e.g., is sent to a third-party payer), he or she has no control over confidentiality. Thoughtful inclusion of details and interpretations can be educational and enhance credibility.

# Accountabil ity: Eval uating Treatments and O utcomes

Federal agencies, third-party payers, and consumer advocates all ask for evidence of professional service effectiveness in an effort to cut costs and/ or provide quality services (Borrego & Follette, 2003). Accountability is a fact of life in professional practice; as such, assessment and treatment should be individualized (Striefel, 2008a). In fact, many MCOs require individual client effectiveness data in order for a practitioner to be accepted on their panels of preferred providers; utilization reviewers require documentation of outcomes and efficacy (Sanchez & Turner, 2008). Best practice standards are a part of the review process (Sanchez & Turner, 2008). One way to address professional accountability is through systematic evaluation of the services delivered (Borrego & Follette, 2003). Trial-and-error learning is expensive in terms of time and dollars, is not efficient or cost-effective, and is hard to justify in an era stressing evidencebased practice (Striefel, 2008a). On an ongoing basis, practitioners are expected to assess and make decisions about whether a client is making progress, and to change the treatment, reassess the situation, refer the client elsewhere, or take such other action as needed when a client is not making progress (Striefel, 2008b). Client progress is central to quality control and treatment evaluation. Individualized treatment means that the assessment of client progress will vary from client to client, problem to problem, and treatment approach to treatment approach (Striefel, 2008b).

# Research and Clinical Data

Good treatment has a rationale that is based on published research and/or clinical data and experience. Well-designed research studies are used to establish the efficacy and effectiveness of specific clinical interventions. Follow-up data are essential for verifying the durability of such interventions (Striefel et al., 2003). Yucha and Montgomery (2008) published an updated document that summarizes the research and clinical support for treating various conditions with biofeedback and/ or neurofeedback. For each condition listed, they have rated the efficacy of specific biofeedback and neurofeedback interventions based on the existing evidence using the AAPB/ISNR efficacy guidelines (Moss & Gunkelman, 2002). Practitioners may find the Yucha and Montgomery (2008) ratings useful in selecting treatment approaches and in conducting a meaningful informed consent process with clients. A series of white papers summarizing the literature for various treatment conditions is also being published; for example, Monastra et al. (2005) have summarized the published literature that provides support for using EEG biofeedback to treat attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD). Practitioners should remain current on the published clinical issues and data by reading relevant publications (e.g., Applied Psychophysiology and Biofeedback, Biofeedback, Journal of Neurotherapy, and this volume), participating in relevant chat groups, attending relevant conferences (e.g., the annual meeting of AAPB and/or ISNR), and seeking consultation and/or supervision as needed. For information on methods for assessing client progress, see the websites of professional organizations, such as the American Psychological Association, and the many chapters in this volume that deal with specific disorders.

# Heal th Care Insurance Iss ues

Seventy-five percent of people with health care insurance are in some type of MC plan (Sanchez & Turner, 2008). The goal of MC is functional improvement and symptom reduction, not cure, and the context is short-term treatment and cost containment (Sanchez & Turner, 2008). A variety of methods are used to offset costs, including, but not limited to, utilization reviews, practitioner profiling (monitoring a practitioner’s costs and effectiveness and comparing them to those of other practitioners), limiting the number of sessions, reducing impatient stays, requiring patients to share risk by having copayments and deductibles, having primary care physicians serve as gatekeepers on who can access services, having practitioners share risk via capitation plans, and using more practitioners with master’s degrees rather than doctorates (Sanchez & Turner, 2008).

# Rules

Each third-party payer makes its own rules about reimbursement, including who will be reimbursed, for what, and for how many sessions (Rosenthal, 2003; Sanchez & Turner, 2008). Reimbursement rules vary from payer to payer, state to state, discipline to discipline, location to location, policy to policy, and by service. Suffice it to say that it is confusing, and compliance with these rules can be complex; but the rules must be mastered if practitioners are going to protect the interests of the client, assist clients in making informed decisions about what their insurance will and will not pay for, and minimize risk to the practitioner (Striefel & Whitehouse, 2003). The definition of “direct provider” is crucial, because thirdparty payers often reimburse only independently licensed professionals who are directly providing services; although Medicare does reimburse for some services (though not many in biofeedback) that are provided by nonphysicians “incident to” a physician’s care and can be reimbursed at the physician’s rate (if the provider is a W-2 employee of the physician) or at a lower rate if the provider is independently licensed but providing physiciandesignated services (Gosfeld, 2001; Striefel, 2003). Specific rules must be followed to be reimbursed under the incident to care requirements (e.g., the physician must see the client and recommend that specific incident to services be provided) (Gosfeld, 2001).

The biofeedback treatment codes have been defined or interpreted by the American Medical Association’s CPT Coding Committee to be “faceto-face.” Thus, for example, the rules often preclude payment for home-based and/or therapistabsent services. However, the term “direct” does have different connotations for different professionals. It is not always restricted to mean “face-toface” or “without the help of others.” For example, surgeons provide “direct” services and do so with considerable help of supervised operating room personnel; supervised professionals conduct some of these services without a surgeon’s presence. Similarly, physical and occupational therapists in medical settings provide a variety of clinical services without a physician’s presence, yet physicians generally direct and supervise these services. Conscientious supervisors can supervise others providing therapies using clinical biofeedback, while still maintaining a “direct” relationship with the patient and the services. These other professionals should have proper education, training, and credentials as biofeedback therapists. Getting reimbursed, even in medical settings such as a hospital, can be difficult. Issues for being reimbursed for services provided by phone or over the Internet are still being worked out by the various third-party payers. Check with the insurance company to determine its current policy at the time you plan to provide services.

Medicaid and Medicare regulations vary across states in terms of whether licensed providers can be directly reimbursed, whether a physician referral is needed, whether services can be covered if “bundled” into an array of services provided at a clinic or hospital, and whether requirements beyond licensure are required (e.g., some states require listing in the National Register of Health Service Providers; American Psychological Association, 1995; Striefel et al., 2003). Some states allow for reimbursement of services provided by an unlicensed provider (American Psychological Association, 1995). Providers are encouraged to check with their state Medicaid and Medicare office if they have questions about who can be reimbursed and whether biofeedback for a specific client’s diagnosis is covered. It is unclear whether Medicaid will pay for any biofeedback services. The penalties for breaking the rules, although they seem unlikely at this time, can range from having to make paybacks several times larger than the amount received to time in prison for fraud. Providers must also learn what is expected or required by the different third-party payers they bill or that cover the services of their clients. A good way to learn these rules is by establishing a good working relationship with the decision makers working for the different third-party payers.

# Competence and Utilization Reviews in Reimbursement

The first issue is whether a given person’s insurance policy includes reimbursement for any biofeedback services. The second is whether there is reimbursement for the specific client’s diagnosis or diagnoses. The third issue is whether the proposed provider of biofeedback is eligible for reimbursement, and if so, under in-network or out-of-network rates. The fourth issue concerns the rate at which the practitioner will be reimbursed, whether there is a deductible to be met, and what the copay requirement is.

The decision about who can be reimbursed is often made differently by various third-party payers and may even differ from state to state with the same company. Most require licensure in some health care discipline as a first criterion, and competence is often one component in the decision-making process. BCIA certification and state licensing are two criteria for determining competence to provide services. The biofeedback credential continues to increase in credibility and recognition. For example, several states now require BCIA certification or the equivalent to be reimbursed by workers’ compensation (Striefel et al., 2003). Maybe this will become a trend as biofeedback providers educate insurance companies on their standards and the need to reimburse those demonstrating proficiency.

Utilization reviews are a part of daily practice in health care. As such, practitioners should contact their state health care insurance office to determine whether their state has a utilization review law, and if so, what its provisions are. Utilization review laws are common, and some of these laws require third-party payers to disclose their utilization review criteria; others do not (American Psychological Association, 1993).

# Coding, Billing, and Reimbursement

Coding, billing, and reimbursement are complex processes that depend on the following: the provider and the discipline to which the provider belongs, the service, the payer, the client’s specific policy, charges, appropriate codes, who is billing, the governmental and regulatory guidelines governing the provider and service, and the criteria for fraud (Striefel et al., 2003).

# Coding

To be eligible for insurance reimbursement, the client/patient must have a condition and diagnosis that is covered by the insurance policy and an acceptable procedural code. As such, practitioners who provide biofeedback services and wish to be reimbursed by a third party have to bill for their services. This involves the use of a diagnostic code and a procedural code from the CPT manual. Practitioners need to know the commonly used diagnostic codes, including those in the fifth edition of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM-5; American Psychiatric Association, 2013) and the International Classification of Diseases (IDC-10), latest revision (American Medical Association, 2015a), as well as the latest CPT codes published by the American Medical Association (2015b), with updates annually. If the biofeedback provider is not licensed to provide the relevant diagnosis, the diagnosis used must come from a licensed referral source, a licensed supervisor, or a physician who may employ the biofeedback or psychophysiological therapy provider.

Prior to 1997, there were a number of CPT codes specifically for biofeedback. For various reasons (see the article by Whitehouse at www.biofeedbackcalifornia.org/uploads/past_issues/bsc_fall_2000.pdf for details), there are currently only two CPT procedure codes solely for use in biofeedback training (American Medical Association, 2015b). The main code, 90901, is defined as biofeedback training by any modality. It is an untimed code with a low relative value unit (RVU), so reimbursement is often modest. Many companies do not reimburse for this code, and if they do, there are limitations on what and to whom they will reimburse. (Some workers’ compensation programs treat each unit of time as 1 minute rather than, say, 1 hour.) The other biofeedback code, 90911, is used for voiding disorders and includes biofeedback training, perineal muscles, and anorectal or urethral sphincter, and includes EMG and/or manometry. This code has a higher RVU, and reimbursement is usually reasonable when it is covered. This code is recognized by Medicare and covered by most companies, mostly for certain types of incontinence, typically if there is documentation that nothing else has worked, and only for a few sessions, but at a good reimbursement rate. A caveat here is that not all providers will be reimbursed for the use of this code. Check with the insurance company about your credentials and clarify whether or not you must work under a physician or physical therapist.

There are also two psychiatric therapeutic procedure codes that can be used for the combination of psychotherapy and biofeedback: 90875, which is individual psychophysiological therapy incorporating biofeedback training by any modality, faceto-face with the patient, with psychotherapy (e.g., insight-oriented, behavior-modifying, or supportive psychotherapy, approximately 20–30 minutes), and 90876, which is the same as 90875 except the time is approximately 45–50 minutes; American

Medical Association, 2015b). Most insurance companies that reimburse for the psychiatric codes (and currently there are very few) do so only for services provided by practitioners licensed in a mental health discipline, but not necessarily all licensed disciplines, so you must check with the insurance company. Current codes and insurancerelated information can be found at the AAPB website (www.aapb.org).

The dearth of CPT codes specific to biofeedback has led many clinicians to consider using alternative codes from other sections of the CPT code book. There are codes from the rehabilitation and physical medicine section in which the description of the services is similar to those for using biofeedback. One example is code 97532 for cognitive retraining, which is defined as the development of cognitive skills to improve attention, memory, problem solving, and direct patient contact by the provider. If a clinician is providing neurofeedback along with some other training to improve cognitive skills, then it appears that this code may be appropriate to use. A benefit of using the 97532 code is that it is billed in 15-minute units, so you can charge for the specific length of the session. It is also reimbursed more readily than code 90901 by many insurance companies. For quantitative EEG (QEEG), quantitative analysis testing, many practitioners have been able to use the digitized EEG code.

For neuromuscular retraining, the CPT code 97112 has often been recommended as an alternative to coding with 90901. The definition of this code is neuromuscular reeducation of movement, balance, coordination, kinesthetic sense, posture, and proprioception. This description matches very well the goals for biofeedback training with patients who have impaired motor control. However, it turns out that such use is problematic if surface EMG is used. The complete description of code 97112 in the CPT code book lists a specific exclusion for SEMG biofeedback. While many clinicians have been successful in obtaining reimbursement using this code for EMG biofeedback training, an audit by the insurance company would most likely lead to a request to return the payments. There is now a code, 96002 for dynamic SEMG, during walking or other functional activities (1–12 minutes).

In 2002, the American Psychological Association was successful in its efforts to get a new set of codes approved by the American Medical Association’s coding committee. These codes, collectively known as the health and behavior intervention codes (96150 through 96155), were designed for use by health psychologists working in medical settings and deal with the provision of mental health services to patients and/or families in which the primary diagnosis is a physical disorder (supplied by a physician) (American Medical Association, 2015b). The codes cover cognitive, behavioral, social, and psychophysiological procedures designed to ameliorate physical health problems (American Medical Association, 2015b).

The inclusion of the term “psychophysiological indicates that interventions including biofeedback training may be billed with these codes. In order to use 96150 for an evaluation, the assessment should focus on issues relating to a physical disorder that has previously been diagnosed. The code is not appropriate if the primary focus of the evaluation is a psychiatric condition, such as anxiety. The assessment should also deal with other psychosocial aspects that might affect symptoms such as coping skills, attitudes toward illness, compliance with medical treatments, and so on. If you are a mental health practitioner providing comprehensive evaluations of patients with medical conditions (e.g., headache and hypertension) that include some psychophysiological recording, you should consider using the CPT code 96150 for the evaluation and code 96152 for the treatment sessions. Each code is for 15 minutes, so in billing for 1 hour’s time, record the time as 4 × 15 minutes. There is no other evaluation code for biofeedback, though some insurance companies do not reimburse for this code, and if you are providing biofeedback and they approve it, they may tell you to bill it under code 90901 or even under 90801.

On January 1, 2013, some new psychotherapy codes went into effect. New code 90832 is for psychotherapy of 30-minutes duration with patient or family—not 20–30 minutes. Additional codes for longer durations include 90834 (45 minutes), 90837 (60 minutes), and 90840 for each 30 minutes beyond 1 hour (American Psychiatric Association, 2013).

At one time, and this is still true for some insurance companies, to provide biofeedback using the psychotherapy codes was considered fraud and anyone billing this way would be asked to pay back anything reimbursed for these codes, along with fines and perhaps loss of one’s license. However, in recent times, many insurance companies that do not reimburse for the biofeedback codes have told some providers that as long as they are doing psychotherapy and including biofeedback as part of that therapy (and, of course, accurately documenting this within session notes), the psychotherapy codes can be used. When there is any doubt, check with the insurance company. Do not risk being charged with fraud!

To deal with the confusion surrounding the use of biofeedback-related codes, a practitioner might well be advised to attend one of the workshops on coding and billing frequently offered by the American Medical Association or other reputable sources (e.g., for a current list of workshops, go to www.ama.org). Many written materials are also available, as one can see by going to the Internet and doing a search for “CPT codes.” Also see the articles referenced for this chapter for more details. Sometimes AAPB and state societies also offer workshops on coding and billing. Keep in mind that the American Medical Association does not say who can use the codes. Each insurance company decides whom it will reimburse for the codes. However, sometimes, if a billed diagnostic or procedural code is denied, the insurance company will allow you to rebill with a more appropriate code, without considering it to be fraud.

Codes do change, as do their uses. It is important for practitioners to remain current on code availability and how to use the codes correctly (Striefel et al., 2003). The practitioner who bills for service is ultimately responsible for ensuring that the codes are accurate and used appropriately, and that their use conforms to ethical and legal requirements (Striefel et al., 2003). Creative coding (i.e., changing the code used in order to get reimbursed) is unethical and constitutes fraud (Rosenthal, 2003; Sanchez & Turner, 2008; Striefel & Whitehouse, 2003). Be wary of those who tell you how to bill creatively by bundling several codes together to get maximum reimbursement, even if they themselves have done so, because they will likely be caught and charged with fraud. Also remember that if you are legitimately billing biofeedback with the codes 90875, 90876, or the psychotherapy codes, only one code from the psychotherapy category will be reimbursed per day, so if you are billing two codes, or someone else is seeing the same client and billing a psychotherapy code the same day, whichever bill is received last will not be reimbursed, and the date of service cannot be switched to another day.

#

It is the third-party payer who decides whether to reimburse, for how much, for what diagnoses, and for how long. A provider must abide by the relevant scope of practice guidelines and other statutory regulations related to biofeedback. Billing can raise many questions related to issues such as who obtains the preauthorization for services (we recommend that the practitioner or his or her billing office do this); what happens when a recommended service is not covered by a client’s insurance; how copayment requirements are handled; and whether a practitioner should modify his or her fees if the client has no insurance (Striefel et al., 2003).

“Fraud,” as defined in the new Social Security Act (Sections 1909 and 1128A), is a felony and includes “knowingly and willfully” charging in excess of state-established reimbursement rates (e.g., for Medicaid), offering or accepting kickbacks, and practices such as regularly discounting copayments and accepting only insurance-paid fees. Witnessing the peer review processes and some of the fraud investigations across the country should make all practitioners very cautious. A common occurrence seems to be billing for exorbitant amounts using multiple codes, creative coding, or excessive numbers of sessions in cases that routinely appear to be attempts to find ways to get the very most money, much beyond the usual fees for service. Since many companies are adopting the new fraud bill as a standard, more cases are being investigated. Some providers are being asked to reimburse the payer for what was considered double billing, for many years of service. In other words, with billing and coding, it is best for providers not to assume that they know what to bill. Rather, they need to check out what is possible and right, or else be prepared to make things right (even years later).

Sometimes when an insurance company states that it does not reimburse for biofeedback codes, it is telling those licensed in mental health they may provide biofeedback as part of a psychotherapy session as long as that is not the only part (though some others consider this fraud). In many states, biofeedback is listed under the practice guidelines as a modality within psychotherapy, or as being within the scope of practice of psychologists, nurses, and maybe physical therapists and dentists, as well as physicians. In fact, biofeedback is recognized and used within at least 19 different disciplines, regardless of whether it is covered by insurance.

# Reimbursement

One concern related to reimbursement has to do with deciding what to charge. Most practitioners have a usual charge for their services. A practitioner’s charges should be similar to that of other professionals from the same discipline who offer similar services. The charges may be based on minutes, blocks of time (e.g., 15-minute increments), regular services (e.g., 45-to 50-minute sessions, with the remaining time out of an hour reserved for notes, phone calls, scheduling, staffing, or other client-related business), or special services (e.g., conducting or interpreting a specialized evaluation, writing an extensive report, extensive staffing, or testifying). If the time spent with or for a client exceeds the usual time or charge, it may be possible to charge for that service or time. However, this should be done with the client’s informed consent, along with information about whether the third party will pay (and, if not, with a clear understanding of what the bill is for and who pays, and at what rate, full or partial). Bundling and billing using several different codes to cover a 1-hour session may constitute fraud if the total charges significantly exceed the normal hourly rate.

Professionals generally do provide some pro bono or reduced-rate services. It is best to bill for such services, then discount the amount to be written off. However, it is fraud to give everyone the same discounted rate without regard for each person’s circumstances, especially if billing the insurance provider for the full amount. An agreement should be reached with a client at the outset of treatment on whether the client is to pay at the time of service and be reimbursed by the insurance company, or whether the client will pay the copay and have the insurance pay the provider the rest of the fee or what the company considers, “usual and customary” for the type of service. Workers’ compensation and personal injury protection (PIP) plans usually pay the provider. Many require “timely” submission of bills, such as within 30 days of the date of service. Many also now require that treatment plans and notes be submitted along with the bill before they will pay. This may present an ethical dilemma over what is confidential and should not be released without client consent. What does one do about “balances due”? Informed consent must be obtained, particularly if a collections agency is to be used. Sometimes balances due can be adjusted to accommodate a client’s situation or the likelihood of getting reimbursed, particularly if the insurance will not reimburse. If you are part of an insurance network of providers that has contracted to provide services at a particular fee, you may be able to bill the client for the copay but not for your full and customary fee. The latter, known as “balance billing,” is not permitted.

If insurance coverage is denied for a biofeedback service that a provider believes can be justified as a viable and common practice or one that is terminated early, the provider has an ethical and maybe even legal duty to appeal the denial (Acuff et al., 2008; Appelbaum, 2008), provide the needed services, help the client access needed services elsewhere, and/or take other actions to ensure that no client is abandoned while in need or injured. If a practitioner is persistent and makes a good case by furnishing appropriate information supporting the use of biofeedback for that diagnosis (e.g., needed to determine medical necessity), then the appeal may well be honored. In appeals and other actions, such as continuing to provide services, it is essential to not fall below the expected standard of care when a client is in crisis (Acuff et al., 2008; Appelbaum, 2008). The patient can also file an appeal or go to the employer who chooses the insurance plan and options covered by the plan. If a practitioner discovers that another practitioner is billing or treating fraudulently, there are professional guidelines about how to deal with this, such as confronting the individual and/or reporting such conduct (e.g., see AAPB’s code of ethics).

# Is Biofeedback, Neurofeedback, or Appl ied Psychoph ysiol ogy a Separate Profess ion?

The answer to the question “Is biofeedback, neurofeedback, or applied psychophysiology a separate profession?” still seems to be “no.” Whether biofeedback, neurofeedback, and applied psychophysiology can become a licensable or separately licensable health care profession remains to be seen. To establish biofeedback and neurofeedback as recognized and licensable professions will take considerable resources in the form of time and effort. Some positive efforts in this direction have occurred or are occurring. New Jersey now licenses biofeedback practitioners. After an initial grandfathering period to allow licensing of existing practitioners, a specific set of requirements (e.g., training, experience, and degree requirements) for licensure went into effect. The California Biofeedback Association has undertaken a concerted effort to explore the creation of a license for the practice of psychophysiology, biofeedback, and/or neurofeedback (MacDonald & Kerson, 2009). In addition, there are academic programs that train students to meet the requirements for being certified in biofeedback; for example, there is a doctoral program in clinical psychology at Alliant International University in San Diego, California, through which students can meet all of the requirements for BCIA certification, and the new doctoral program in mind– body medicine for nonphysicians at Saybrook at Stanford University also helps students meet requirements for certification. Still, many potential obstacles need to be overcome to create licensable professions in biofeedback, neurofeedback and applied or clinical psychophysiology. The controversy has been discussed in detail by Striefel et al (2003). The issue is one of unlicensed individuals wanting to have a profession that is licensable, so they can better meet their own financial needs and serve more clients.

The AAPB has twice explored the issue of what it might take for biofeedback to become a profession, and both times it concluded that biofeedback did not meet the criteria for being deemed a profession (Glaros, 1998; Walters, 1999). What the training curriculum, academic degrees, and supervision requirements should be have yet to be worked out. Perhaps the California Biofeedback Association will get them worked out.

Still, a license by itself does not create a profession. To be viable, a license would need to be accepted by other health care disciplines, thirdparty payers, and consumers, of course. The licensing would need to have specific requirements to ensure that licensed practitioners are in fact competent to operate as health care professionals; otherwise, referrals from physicians and others are unlikely, as is reimbursement by third-party payers.

# Opp osing E vidence

The evidence against biofeedback as a profession has been discussed in detail by Striefel et al (2003), so it is only summarized here. The arguments would be the same for neurofeedback and applied psychophysiology. At present biofeedback, neurofeedback, and applied psychophysiology do not meet the requirements specified as being characteristics of a profession, as pointed out by Bayles, (1988), Glaros (1998), or DeLeon (1998). DeLeon concluded that biofeedback does not meet the criteria for being deemed a profession, nor is it likely to do so in the future. This is further complicated by the fact that at least 19 different existing professions provide biofeedback services; as such, biofeedback is truly cross-disciplinary and not unique to any one profession.

In addition, there currently does not exist an extensive training program that grants an academic degree in biofeedback, although a doctorate in applied psychophysiology can be obtained via distance learning from the Behavioral Medicine Institute (contact Richard Sherman, PhD at rsherman@nwinet.com). Many academic settings currently offer coursework and experience in biofeedback and neurofeedback. One can obtain a list of such programs from the BCIA (www.bcia.org). Biofeedback, neurofeedback, and psychophysiology do, however, provide a service in society, and that service is likely to become more important over time as more professionals from various health care disciplines become aware of biofeedback, neurofeedback, and other psychophysiological services and their usefulness as “treatments of choice” for various conditions.

# Developing Biofeedback as a Profession

Jack Wiggins stated that being deemed “a profession” is primarily a political issue rather than a clinical one (cited in DeLeon, 1998). If this is the case, how does one identify and deal with the political issues in a way that does not have a negative impact on the acceptability of biofeedback? A strong political barrier to biofeedback’s becoming a profession already exists. This barrier is that, increasingly, licensing laws are changing to include “biofeedback” within the definition of the practice of specific professional disciplines—most notably, psychology. (In fact, the American Psychological Association recognizes biofeedback as one specialty area.) The licensing movement has been fueled in part by the tightening of health care dollars as managed care strives to reduce health care costs. Supposedly, it is easier to get reimbursed for services provided if one can show that the intervention is a legitimate activity for members of one’s discipline. As such, many licensing laws have expanded the definition for what is included in the scope of practice for specific disciplines, to cover all the activities in which their members engage that are ethically acceptable to the members of the discipline and do not violate any existing law. To create a reputable and meaningful profession called biofeedback, neurofeedback, or applied psychophysiology in such states will require mobilizing the support of existing health care professionals, or at least getting enough support that these other professionals do not mobilize in opposition to the proposal of creating such a profession. Perhaps New Jersey advocates achieved this goal in getting a license for biofeedback practitioners.

To create professions called “biofeedback,” “neurofeedback,” or “applied psychophysiology” will require the creation of the needed structure, including the establishment of degree-granting programs in academic settings. At present, no academic setting offers a degree in biofeedback or neurofeedback; but many offer sufficient coursework for meeting the requirements for applying for BCIA certification in biofeedback and/or neurofeedback. Clearly, more such programs are needed for meeting the increasing demand for biofeedback and neurofeedback services, and at least one program is needed that will offer a degree in biofeedback or neurofeedback per se. The more prestigious the academic program and faculty of that program, the more likely the degree will be considered reputable, and the more likely other academic programs will create such degree programs. The AAPB and ISNR might well be encouraged to look at the issues related to what courses, course content, practica, and internship experiences might be needed for a degree-granting program in biofeedback. Perhaps the California Biofeedback Association is already doing this.

If biofeedback is to become a reputable profession, the support of many professionals from other, already reputable professions will be required. Creating the right program and reputation for new professions called biofeedback, neurofeedback, and applied psychophysiology will take time and resources. One cannot help wondering why licensed professionals would want to put resources into creating another profession that would compete with them for shrinking health care reimbursement dollars. It might be easier to ask, “Why do biofeedback providers who are not members of licensed professions not go back to school and get a degree in a licensable health care discipline?” A number of individuals have gone back to school during the last several years to get degrees and later licenses. Doing so is a proactive behavior that is largely under the control of the individuals and is therefore doable. Creating a profession takes the coordinated effort of many individuals, and many factors may not be controllable. In the meantime, biofeedback, neurofeedback and applied psychophysiology might best be described as potentially “emerging professions” and currently an adjunctive methodology.

# Evaluating Research in Clinical Biofeedback

Frank A ndrasik and Mark S. Schwartz

We discuss in this chapter select issues and questions to consider when evaluating biofeedback research. We hope it will serve to increase awareness when researchers conduct research, and report and interpret clinical results.

# Th e Gap between Cl inicians and R esearchers: Some Sugg estions for Cl osing I t

Clinicians and researchers often appear as adversaries engaged in what seems to be the battle of the Hatfields and the McCoys. As Garmezy and Masten (1986) noted some time ago, “the slings and arrows of these opposing camps have as yet to be put away as part of an arms reduction package” (p. 501). Rosenfeld (1987) pointed out that clinicians criticize findings obtained from “strippeddown experimental paradigms” that do not support the specific effects of biofeedback, partially due to the omission of “patient motivational factors.” Practitioners claim that researchers often ask questions that are either incorrect or unimportant. Some clinicians accuse researchers of inadequately conducted research and experimenter bias. “Absence of evidence is not evidence of absence; one cannot in the end prove the null hypothesis. That is, a failed half-hearted attempt to find the needle in the haystack does not prove that it isn’t there. One should really take a good look for it before giving up—if the needle has any value to one, that is” (p. 218; emphasis added).

On the other hand, exaggeration of clinical results is often the claim of researchers. In all fairness, these problems are also present in other clinical, health, and educational settings (Garmezy & Masten, 1986; Hayes, Barlow, & Nelson-Gray, 1999).

Researchers and practitioners sometimes do not appreciate their dependence on one another, nor do they see the benefits from a productive alliance rather than an enduring antagonism between the camps. “To some extent, the disagreement is semantic [and due to] . . . much misrepresentation—probably due to mutual misunderstanding of the respective positions of the opponents” (Rosenfeld, 1987, p. 217). According to Garmezy and Masten (1986, p. 501), “wisdom would dictate a recognition that there were contributions to be made by both talented clinicians and researchers.”

Practitioners are hungry for knowledge about biofeedback, especially when it is relevant to clinical applications. They are hungry for wellinformed research and depend on it for answering myriad questions and increasing the credibility of clinical procedures. There is no substitute for high-quality research. Again, borrowing from Rosenfeld (1987), “If biofeedback is to prosper, there must be acceptance by medical and scientific communities. This is ultimately necessary . . . These . . . communities [ultimately] accept only one kind of evidence, scientific evidence. There [is] some disagreement as to what constitutes scientific evidence” (p. 217).

Researchers need clinicians to help identify the questions that require answers, and clinicians are helpful in providing researchers with viewpoints and ideas on pertinent research questions and procedures. Also, without widespread clinical applications, there would be far less need for research. Thus, researchers and practitioners need to work hand-in-hand to improve the effectiveness of therapeutic interventions (Garmezy & Masten, 1986; Rosenfeld, 1987). Rosenfeld astutely commented that “researcher and clinician ought to begin talking to each other in advance of doing a study, rather than wasting time in a posteriori arguments” (p. 221, emphasis added).

Among our goals in this chapter is to increase the sophistication and clinical usefulness of research and to help consumers of that research. We favor anything that stimulates us all to work closely with one another toward improving the synergistic relationship among research, clinical applications, and reimbursement. Garmezy and Masten’s (1986) directness is also helpful:

The researcher who is uninformed of the observations depicted in clinical case accounts . . . is at risk for generating unsophisticated, inaccurate, and marginal studies . . . The clinician equally uninformed about . . . developments in the areas of basic and applied science relevant to the clinical enterprise is at risk for rigidly adhering to a technique or model that can act as a conceptual straitjacket, containing the therapeutic effort. (p. 501)

Practitioners must be very knowledgeable about research methods and limitations, and researchers need to be well versed in clinical procedures and clinical practice. Neither can afford to have narrow or simplistic conceptualizations of biofeedback or other applied psychophysiological therapies. Editors of journals and organizers of professional meetings need to extend their efforts to help consumers understand and evaluate research, and to place that research in conceptual and practical contexts. Typically, journal articles and books that review the literature appear a year or more after the research has been published. Letters to the editor about research and issues appear infrequently.

Some professionals might applaud seeing journal space devoted to editorial comments and critiques at the time an article is published.

The book by White and Tursky (1982) is a good example to follow, in that it provides a useful “roundtable” discussion of each chapter. This is a valuable and refreshing book, and its format is one that journal editors and book publishers should consider employing more often.

The distrust of research that emerges among practitioners needs to be reduced, and the responsibility for reducing this distrust resides in both camps. We encourage researchers to be mindful of the potential impact on clinical professionals of what they conclude, state, and imply. Everyone should willingly accept data from soundly designed research, whether or not the data conflict with existing beliefs or clinical practices. Practitioners need to remember that it is very difficult to conduct excellent and clinically relevant research, and that even well-conducted research usually represents only a limited set of conditions.

So what are we all to do? Garmezy and Masten (1986) call for reason when they urge professionals to realize that both clinical relevance and mutual suspiciousness “can be overcome when clinician and scientist share a common regard for the other's area of activity, and a recognition that there are contributions to knowledge each can make to the common enterprise, namely to understand human behavior” (p. 502).

We hope that more researchers will become increasingly aware of and responsive to the needs of their clinical peers. More instances of experienced clinicians and researchers working closely together would be very desirable.

# Evidence‑Based Practice

Although the value of evaluating the efficacy of treatments can be traced back to ancient Greece, only fairly recently has this notion become a widespread concern within psychologically based treatments. In 1993, David H. Barlow, then President of the Division of Clinical Psychology (now the Society of Clinical Psychology) (Division 12) of the American Psychological Association, appointed a task force to identify “empirically validated treatments” (more recently the terminology has changed to “empirically supported treatments”; Chambless & Hollon, 1998) within clinical psychology. It was hoped that this effort would help educate therapists, third-party payers, and the public. In June 2001, a similar effort was launched within our field by Donald Moss, then President of the Association for Applied Psychophysiology and Biofeedback (AAPB), and Jay Gunkelman, then President of the Society for Neuronal Regulation (SNR) [subsequently renamed the International Society for Neurofeedback and Research (ISNR)], who appointed a task force to develop standards on research methodology to use in determining the level of empirical support of biofeedback treatments (Moss & Gunkelman, 2002).

The task force (La Vaque, Hammond, Trudeau, Perry, & Lehrer, 2002) extensively reviewed research articles on methodology and efficacy, guidelines developed by other agencies/societies, and documents addressing ethics in outcome research. Its efforts culminated with development of a template designed to provide the field with a rigorous set of methodological standards to use in classifying applications in one of five levels of efficacy, ranging from Level 1–Not Empirically Supported to Level 5–Efficacious and Specific (see La Vaque et al. for specific definitions). The classification includes a level above and beyond those of others, because Level 5 requires that a treatment be not only efficacious but also specifically indicated for a given disorder. This is a tall order given that few treatments of any kind are able to achieve this level. The more common goal is to achieve the status of Level 4–Efficacious. Members of the task force also discussed what might be best considered as aspirational goals when designing and collecting clinical outcome data to increase the confidence one has when judging findings as efficacious.

To date, leading researchers in the field, rigorously employing the evaluation scheme of La Vaque et al. (2002), have prepared seven “white paper” reviews that have appeared in peer-reviewed journals (see Table 19.1).1 An even more rigorous approach has been developed by the Consolidated Standards of Reporting Trials group (CONSORT). It includes 25 areas to address, as well as a detailed chart for tracking participants at all stages of the clinical trial, that increasingly are being utilized by medical journals (see the website www.consortstatement.org) to evaluate randomized controlled trials testing efficacy of interventions.

TA BLE 19.1. Level of Support for White Paper Efficacy Reviews Published to Date
