# Other Considerations in E val uating and Us ing Research R esul ts

Our focus in this chapter now turns to questions and other factors to consider when reading, evaluating, designing, conducting, writing about, and publishing research. Some of our comments elaborate further on points we touched on earlier. These questions are subsumed under the rubrics of therapists, subjects,2 therapy, data management, and individual-subject designs.3 The list is by no means exhaustive. We encourage readers to review the emerging literature on the aptitude × treatment × interaction (A × T × I) model of therapy effectiveness (Dance & Neufeld, 1988; Holloway, Spivey, Zismer, & Withington, 1988) discussed briefly in Chapter 3 and the critique by Smith and Sechrest (1991/1992). Briefly, this model proposes interactions between the person and treatment that may account for outcomes. This notion was perhaps first introduced by Gordon Paul in 1967, when he asked, “What treatment, by whom is most effective for this individual, with that specific problem, and under what specific set of circumstances?” In terms of psychotherapy, Smith and Sechrest (1991/1992) criticized this type of research for yielding dependable interactions. They presented stringent conditions for such research and proposed that research adhering to their criteria “uncover previously ‘hidden' main effects more frequently than interactions” (p. 558). Readers may benefit from guides prepared for assessing research reports in clinical psychology and behavioral medicine (Lovejoy, Revenson, & France, 2011; Maher, 1992).

# Th erapists

We begin by posing questions that merit consideration.

# Competence

1. Who provided the therapy and what were the education, training, credentials, and experience of the therapists? Standardized information about the qualifications of therapists would be of considerable value if available to readers. Some researchers do have good clinical skills, characteristics, and experience. Some have even better skills and training than some practicing clinical therapists. Nevertheless, failure to obtain or replicate therapeutic effects may not reflect a problem with the procedures. Differences may partly be related to different therapists. Thus, do practitioner consumers know enough about the therapists to evaluate the relevance to the practitioner?

# Investigator–Therapist Confidence and Bias

1. What do we know of the investigators' and therapists' beliefs about the therapy? Did they have confidence in the therapy? Were the therapists required to conduct the research as research assistants or as a part of their clinical practice? Answers to these questions are often unclear, and resolving them can be quite complex. Investigators or therapists who are not confident in the therapy they are providing may communicate this indirectly or inadvertently to the subjects or patients. This can influence the style and content of the presentations to subjects and the way the therapist provides the therapy. We are not advocating that only “true believers” conduct therapy research. However, consumer professionals often want information about the beliefs of authors of the research and reviews. Acknowledging one's confidence and support or one's skepticism about a therapeutic strategy is desirable, appropriate, and honorable.

2. Does the investigator, reviewer, or clinician have a reputation for pushing one viewpoint? Wise consumers interpret research and reviews in the context of the author’s other research and the way authors treat others' research and commentaries. Bias is sometimes very subtle. We quickly add that bias is inherently acceptable. An investigator or clinician without bias may be boring and unproductive. The issues are the degree of bias and how the bias interferes with impartiality.

3. Has the investigator, reviewer, or clinician ever reversed his or her position? This can enhance his or her credibility. Did the individual do this based on findings from his or her own or others' research?

# Consistency of Behavior and Adherence to Protocols

1. How do we know that the therapists followed the procedures for all patients in a given condition? A therapist may depart from a standard protocol when a patient/subject varies his or her behavior in certain ways. Are there provisions in the protocol for contingencies that arise during therapy? Did the therapists follow the therapy protocol outline as intended? Did the therapist report the departure to the research investigator(s)? Rather than implying distrust, these questions recognize a normal and understandable human condition. Therapists and research assistants do not always report to their supervisors all that occurs. They may not regard it as important, or they may expect disapproval from the investigator.

Consistency might be a concern when the content of subjects' questions, comments, and informal talk differs during sessions. Therapists might respond differently to different subjects at different times. Certain of the following factors increase the chance of a therapeutically significant discrepancy between an investigator's plans, what occurred, and what the therapist reported: use of multiple therapists; the more the bias of the investigator is known to the therapist providing the therapy, the more the therapist is dependent on the investigator for support and evaluation; the busier the supervisor, the less supervision, the less specific the protocol and the greater the absence of planned contingencies. (For more in-depth discussions of of treatment integrity/fidelity, see Gearing et al., 2011; Hagermoser Sanetti & Kratochwill, 2013; Perepletchikova, Treat, & Kazdin, 2007; and Yeaton & Sechrest, 1981)

# Specific Therapist Characteristics

1. Is there a description of therapist characteristics that could affect the attitudes and behaviors of the patients/subjects? These include age, sex, race, credibility, anxiety, friendliness, and appearance (which can be a delicate issue). However, many professionals believe that therapist characteristics influence patient/subject attitudes, behaviors, and outcome. The point here is that many practitioners and researchers reading others’ research want and need reports of such information.

# Subj ects

# Subject Preparation for and Understanding of Therapy

1. How prepared for the therapy were the subjects? There is widespread agreement among professionals that patient education is important. It affects patients' attitudes about themselves and the therapy, as well as adherence. Some published research either describes for readers the patienteducation content provided to the subjects or informs readers where this content is available. However, most studies report little or no such information. Readers want to know what the researchers communicated to the subjects, who delivered this information, and what educational modalities were employed. We ask that researchers at least summarize this information in the published paper and indicate where the details of the patient education may be found. The absence of it makes desirable comparisons among studies and therapy procedures more difficult.

2. What evidence was presented to support the assumption that the subjects understood, accepted, and learned the patient-education content? Providing patient education does not ensure adequate understanding, acceptance, learning, and mobilization for therapy. For example, did patients understand, accept, and learn the rationale for therapy and the procedures? Research that gives data about knowledge that is understood, accepted, and learned will enjoy a better reception among readers.

# Subject Motivation and Expectations

1. In many clinical and experimental situations, one independent variable may have no main therapeutic effect; however, when combined with another independent variable, the interaction leads to meaningful changes in the dependent variable. Rosenfeld (1987) pointed out that a feedback variable alone may not have an effect on physiological and symptom changes except when combined with another variable, such as motivation.

Thus, practitioners want and need to know about motivational and expectancy variables. Indeed, many of the variables discussed in this chapter may be necessary for the “higher-order interactions” to occur. Consider therapist attitudes and confidence, patient education, the absence of other subject problems interfering with therapy, and specific therapy procedures. Consider also what information is available about the subjects’ motivation for learning psychophysiological selfregulation and reducing their symptoms. Symptoms are often sufficient motivation, but in some instances the severity or frequency is not enough motivation. There are costs associated with receiving treatment, even in research studies that do not charge therapy fees. Such costs include travel, time away from work and other activities, time spent completing a more extensive pretreatment assessment than might otherwise be the case, and the like. One must balance the severity and frequency of the symptoms against all the costs that can affect motivation.

2. There is also the issue of “secondary gain.” Did the investigator(s) assess the possibility of such variables among the subjects? Can we assume that the motivation of all subjects to reduce their symptoms was at a high level? Was symptom reduction more motivating than competing factors? Randomization helps to control for this variable, but with small samples and individual-subject designs, motivations competing with symptom reduction may compromise the results.

3. What were the expectations of subjects? Determining subjects’ expectations about the therapy should be part of most research, especially outcome studies.

# Subjects' Attitudes toward Therapists

1. Did the subjects have confidence and trust in their therapists? What does the research paper tell us about the subjects' attitudes regarding the therapists? Were they equivalent across treatment conditions? Whether or not these attitudes affect outcome, to what degree, and under what conditions are empirical questions.

# Other Medical and Psychological Problems of Subjects

1. What information is available about subjects' other medical problems? Papers often include this information, but when the paper does not, the reader and journal reviewer/editor should request it.

2. What information do readers have about personality and psychopathology variables of potential importance (e.g., depression, absorption, locus of control, self-efficacy, anxiety, relaxationinduced anxiety, anger, and interpersonal comfort)?

3. What information do readers have about subjects' time-use management?

# Discrepancies between Self‑Report Records and Verbal Reports

1. Self-report ratings are a valuable source of data, but they have limitations. Many people have difficulty following even simple instructions. Bias can influence their ratings, and their interpretations of ratings also can change over time. Changes in expectation during therapy stages may influence ratings. What did the investigators do to enhance the subjects' understanding of the instructions for symptom ratings?

2. Were there discrepancies between subjects' self-report symptom records and their verbal reports? Did the investigators assess and report discrepancies, and did they discuss and resolve them with the subjects?

# Control of Relevant Variables

1. Were the experimental and control groups equated or otherwise controlled for potentially relevant variables? Among groups, many variables may differ, including baseline and reactive physiological activity, initial responses to feedback, expectancies, understanding, attitudes toward therapy and the therapist, frequency and severity of symptoms, number and intensity of stressors, use of caffeine and nicotine, and medications. One cannot equate groups for all important variables. However, readers still want the information reported and statistical analyses to assess their relative contribution.

# Possible Prebaseline Improvement

1. Some patients show improvement after they receive reassurance about the nonserious nature of their symptoms. Other patients improve in anticipation of receiving therapy soon. Thus, were any of the subjects improving in their symptoms before baseline recording began or before starting therapy? Was the baseline period representative of subjects' symptom frequency and severity before the baseline?

2. Symptoms fluctuate over time for many conditions, and many patients seek assistance when symptoms are the most intense. Over time, the symptoms may lessen or “regress to the mean” in the absence of therapy (Stigler, 1997). Could improvement be a function of expected fluctuation?

# Th erapy

# Therapy Setting

1. The surroundings can influence the attitudes of subjects toward therapy. Where was the treatment conducted, and how did the subjects perceive it? If one intends to generalize the results to clinical settings, then the surroundings should approximate common clinical environments. Congested or otherwise, unprofessional offices are ill-suited for therapy. If the therapist uses such an environment, then he or she should consider assessing subjects' perceptions about the environment.

# Specific Procedures

1. Subjects need opportunities to test their selfregulation abilities under conditions that at least approximate those of real life. Patients must apply their psychophysiological self-regulatory skills beyond the professional's office. What were the procedures for transfer of training and generalization of physiological self-regulation? How did the researcher assess this?

Specific questions include whether the researcher conducted baseline and feedback phases with (a) the subject's eyes open and closed, (b) in varied body positions, (c) during and immediately after stressful activity without feedback, and (d) during and immediately after physical activity without feedback.

2. Were the subjects alone or with a therapist during the sessions?

3. How do readers know that the procedures described were the procedures performed? This is not easily answered. How involved were the investigator(s) in conducting the research protocol? How closely supervised were the therapists conducting the sessions? This question does not intend to imply intentional distortions of procedures and data. It is a reminder that distortions can and do occur for a variety of reasons, and that readers should at least be provided some information about the supervision of the therapists.

4. A related question involves deviations from the protocol. What were the provisions in the protocol for various contingencies that occur during therapy sessions? In clinical situations, one can never anticipate all events. Were there clear instructions for what the therapist was to do when such events occurred? Were departures from the protocol documented? Did the subjects avoid certain activities before therapy sessions? What do we know about what subjects were doing and thinking about during sessions?

# Subject Application of Physiological Self‑Regulation

1. How often and for how long did the subjects use the self-regulatory procedures in their daily lives? Were they using the procedures at the instructed times during their daily activities? It is not enough for authors to state they instructed subjects to use the procedures in their daily lives. Documenting and reporting this information are desirable.

# Symptom Records and Changes

1. How were the changes in symptoms assessed? Did the investigator(s) or therapists review the self-report symptom forms? What questions did the subjects answer during and after therapy and in later follow-up? Results can differ, depending on the answers to these questions.

2. Research reports should discuss the instructions. How careful were the instructions for completing the self-report records, and were the records complete? Did the report include information about whether there were data-collection problems and, if so, how the investigators managed these problems?

3. There are multiple criteria for significant improvement, not all of which may be present for a specific subject. Were the self-report records analyzed sufficiently to determine whether different subjects improved with different symptom variables? Using one criterion for all subjects does not always reflect significant improvement for some subjects.

4. Were the reported symptomatic changes of clinical significance (Jacobson & Truax, 1991; Jacobson, Roberts, Berns, & McGlinchey, 1999; Ronk, Hooke, & Page, 2012)? What was the operational definition of a change judged to be clinically significant? Was there a combination of criteria used? How did the investigators' criteria match the subjects' perceptions?

# Physiological Data and Changes

1. What were the criteria used to determine whether subjects developed physiological self-regulation? Note that researchers and clinicians sometimes use multiple criteria.

2. Were the physiological data reported in enough detail for the reader to know what occurred in different recording conditions? Summary data often do not provide enough useful information about the psychophysiological activity during different conditions.

# Cognitive Factors in Subject Preparation and Therapy

1. How did the researchers cognitively prepare subjects for biofeedback and other self-regulatory therapies? What were they told? Look for details of the presentations. When presentations are lengthy, authors should indicate whether they are available.

2. Recording and reporting the cognitive activity of subjects are useful endeavors. Did the investigators try to obtain information about what subjects may have been thinking during the therapy sessions while using psychophysiological selfregulatory procedures? Did the study discuss the cognitive activity of the subjects?

# Use of Relaxation Recordings

1. If the researchers used prerecorded relaxation material, what details and content were provided? Were these audio only or did they include visual material as well? Patients, and presumably research subjects, have different perceptions of and reactions to different factors associated with recorded relaxation procedures, such as content, voice, tempo, and type of imagery (see Smith, Chapter 12, this volume). Practitioners should look for information about these aspects.

# Controls and Assessment

1. Patients and subjects often initiate health improvement activities other than those specifically recommended and checked by practitioners. Were the effects of caffeine, nicotine, alcohol, and other dietary factors assessed before, during, and after therapy?

2. Were all pertinent medications checked and controlled for throughout the therapy and followup stages? Look for sufficient details about medications.

3. Were life events and daily stress assessed before, during, and after therapy? Information about their presence, absence, and changes should be documented, because these factors can influence symptoms. Did the analyses control for their possible effects?

4. Aside from biofeedback and other methods of learning physiological self-regulation, what else occurred during the sessions?

5. What information is available about the occurrence and duration of periods of significant symptomatic improvement or remission in the past? For example, patients sometimes have periods of remission or reduction of symptoms during certain times of year (e.g., Raynaud's symptoms may lessen during warmer weather; symptoms may decrease during periods of lessened work responsibilities). Long follow-up periods help to control for such factors, especially when they are of sufficient duration to extend beyond the period when remissions/reductions have been known to occur in the past.

# Other Research Considerations

1. Look for commonly reported information. This includes the number of sessions, age and sex of subjects, duration and severity of symptoms, randomized assignment to groups, sample sizes, proper statistics, and instrumentation and recording details. Also, look for acceptable criteria for single-case designs.

2. Did the investigator(s) analyze differences between the subjects who were successful and those who were unsuccessful? This takes us to individual-subject experimental research.

# Individual ‑Subj ect Experimental R esearch

Individual-subject research should be more commonly conducted and accepted by journals (see Andrasik, 2002). “One of the most difficult challenges for psychotherapy [biofeedback] research has been to demonstrate convincingly the link between what occurs in the treatment hour and patient change” (Jones, Ghannam, Nigg, & Dyer, 1993, p. 381). The relationship between process and outcome is typically unclear with group comparison designs or controlled clinical trials.

Hawkins (1989) describes advantages of individual-subject experimental research. It permits comparison of the relative effects of different treatments for each individual (concerning direction, speed, pattern, and final level of effect). Practitioners receive feedback on their efforts more quickly when individually graphed data are used. This feedback to therapists allows more information for therapists and chances to affect contingent changes in their behavior. For therapists, “the more rapid feedback of data . . . leads to a greater enjoyment of and persistence at the tasks of research, at least for many of us” (Hawkins, 1989, p. 128).

Hawkins (1989) and others (Barlow, Blanchard, Hayes, & Epstein, 1977; Barlow, Nock, & Hersen, 2009; Kratochwill, Mott, & Dodson, 1984; special section of single-case research in psychotherapy in the Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology; Jones, 1993) assert that individual-subject experimental designs potentially allow more generalization of results to natural settings than do most group designs. Practitioners appreciate the researchers using group designs that also report individual data; however, typically, this does not involve enough of the unique advantages of individual-subject ex perimental designs.

# Data Management

Data management and mismanagement are serious topics. “Data mismanagement” refers to faulty quality control, documentation, or retention of data. Deficiencies in data management, even apparently minor ones, increase the risk that errors and omissions will occur and can make them difficult to detect. They also can interfere with data sharing and with secondary analyses of data sets, render archived data sets inaccessible or uninterpretable, make it difficult to confirm that data faithfully correspond to actual results of studies, and prevent replication of statistical results (Freedland & Carney, 1992, p. 643). The problem is serious enough to prompt attention by major scientific organizations, universities, and congressional investigation. Federal regulations now exist, and further ones have been proposed (Office of Scientific Integrity Review, 1990).

Data mismanagement is not uncommon in biomedical research (Redman, 2013). However, there are few systematic studies of it in research fields other than investigational drug trials. The full extent of the problem is not known, but anecdotal data suggest that mismanagement is more likely to occur in less standardized research and in research with less adequate funding (Freedland & Carney, 1992). An analysis of the closed case files from the Office of Research Integrity (ORI; Davis, Riske-Morris, & Diaz, 2007) revealed that the misconduct arose from personal and professional stressors, conducive organizational climates, job insecurities, rationalizations, presence of personal inhibitions, and certain personality factors. Similar findings were reported by Redman, Templin, and Merz (2006) in their analysis of 39 ORI cases of scientific misconduct.

The focus here is on “honest error” up to and including what some consider “negligence.” (This does not include the separate topic of willfully fabricating data and results, which is uncommon in scientific research.) Nevertheless, “negligence is clearly unacceptable in scientific research” (Freedland & Carney, 1992, p. 640). The line is often unclear between unintentional errors, omissions, and inadequate data management on the one hand, and negligence or undue carelessness on the other. Freedland and Carney cite several references supporting the statement that researchers are accountable for careless practices and unintended deficiencies.

Even researchers who are well respected by peers may be unable “to reconstruct previously reported analyses of data from clinical databases . . . [and find it] . . . surprisingly difficult to determine which cases and . . . variables . . . [were] used, despite the fact that the original analyses . . . [were] performed with care” (Freedland & Carney, 1992, p. 641). However, these authors point out that except for U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) auditing of clinical trials since 1977, “audit worthiness” is a higher standard for data management than required of most research until recently.4

For the reasons described earlier, investigators, research assistants, and practitioners need to be conversant with this topic. Reading articles (e.g., Freedland & Carney, 1992) is very enlightening. Planning, designing, and conducting research all call for understanding the problems and pitfalls of data management. This is especially true with the use of computer systems. There are many ways that unintentional negligence can occur with data management. Some may view this area as intimidating; however, an awareness of common deficiencies in data management helps to prevent such negligence from occurring.

We include a brief summary of common mistakes in data management. The interested reader will refer directly to Freedland and Carney (1992) and references cited by them (e.g., Marshall, 1990; Office of Scientific Integrity Review, 1990; Racker, 1989). Interested readers also can consult experts, including those who have proficiency with computers and interactive databases and statistical analysis software.

# Reasons for Common Deficiencies in Data Management

Reasons for data management deficiencies (Freedland & Carney, 1992) include the following:

1. There can be unintended side effects of technological progress in computers (e.g., statistical software that ends data management chores).   
2. Computer users may have problems checking, documenting, and preserving their work carefully. These tasks can be tedious, time

consuming, and complicated. The tasks most likely to be problemmatic are documenting and archiving the data for future access, interpretation, and analyses.

3. Some researchers are unable to diagnose subtle flaws in statistical results or document adequately because of their inadequate understanding of computer hardware and software. Achieving proficiency with computer programming and statistical packages usually requires considerable time and effort. 4. Data management also is often very challenging and can be expensive, and smaller projects often have limited financial resources allocated for this.

# Documentation of Data and Computer Programs

One needs accurate and permanent codebooktype information in the database, such as for variable names and subjects. Some software allows incorporation of this information into the data set. However, other software does not permit permanent documentation, or it stores the information in files separate from the data set. This can result in inadequate documentation of archived data sets. One can lose vital documentation from the original database. This can happen when exporting data to another software package or exporting data to another computer system. Regardless of how careful the original documentation, translated versions may lack this necessary documentation.

# Documentation of Analyses

Programming errors or “bugs” that do not violate software rules or other checks can escape detection by programmers and users of the program. Investigators do not always log essential information and cross-check statistical analyses. Thus, statistical printouts may lack adequate documentation, and checking may involve undetected flaws that are difficult to trace. One needs a hard copy of the statistical analyses; otherwise, there is no hard evidence confirming the performance of the analyses. A hard copy permits others to review it.

A similar problem directly related to biofeedback involves real-time data acquisition and processing of instrumentation-derived data. With computer-based biofeedback there is often no hard copy of the data before the analyses. Often there are no notebook records or documentation of the details of sessions. Filtering, composite data, and analytic algorithms alter the original data and can produce untraceable statistical results.

# Multiple Data Sets

Limitations of some computer resources dictate dividing data among multiple data sets, often linked by advanced database technologies. One problem is updating and editing downstream data sets that do not automatically update upstream data sets.

# Copies of Data Sets

The need for backup for data sets is vital and well known. However, there can be incomplete or unedited copies. Also, copies may not have adequate documentation; thus, one might analyze the wrong copy or erase the corrected one accidentally. During the original active data gathering and analysis phases, such problems are unlikely. However, months or years later, one could forget the intricate details of the backup and data set copies. This would make further use of the data a problem.

# Computed Variables and Subsampling

Research sometimes classifies subjects based on variables not contained within in the original data set. An example is basing classification on a composite of subjects’ scores derived from multiple individual measures. Another example is classifying subjects based on individual items within the data set. One needs to save classified and computed scores permanently to prevent losing them. Also, one should update the master copy of the data set.

When reviewing research, some practitioner consumers may wonder about the data management. The only sources of this information are the investigators and verification from journals, the latter of which does not yet exist. Journals could require verification and publish a statement to that effect as part of journal policy. Such verification would be major step forward.

# Final Comm ents And Further R eading

In this chapter we have attempted to increase understanding of research, reviews, and clinical reports, and to help further sophisticated and clinically useful research. We have tried to remind readers of possible pitfalls, to point out the needs of practitioner consumers, and to stimulate productive discussions. We can well imagine some readers saying, “No research can possibly satisfy all these criteria.” We partially agree. However, we encourage a concerted effort to address the questions and guidelines posed here. Practitioner consumers have a responsibility to ask these questions and the right to expect reasonable responses.

The research “facts” and “truths” of today often become the “myths” of tomorrow. Furthermore, even a well-controlled study probably contains some limitations, and other investigators may not replicate findings with different samples. As Rosenfeld (1987) pointed out, “We can and must do credible scientific research in the clinical setting” (p. 220). Practitioner consumers often want and need information that is missing from research reports. Individual-subject experimental designs are desirable and may be necessary. Quality assurance for data management is a major necessity for researchers and practitioner consumers.

High-quality and meaningful research, especially clinical research, is extremely difficult to design and conduct. The topics of research design, methodological considerations, pitfalls in human research, and related topics are covered in greater detail in other publications (see www. marksschwartzphd.com for additional sources).

Foundation (1988) Office of Inspector General. In addition, there are proposals for routine or random audits by research institutions, funding agencies, and journals. These remain controversial and are not yet widely implemented (Culliton, 1988; Institute of Medicine, 1989; Macfarlane, Zhang, & Pun, 2014; Rennie, 1989; Stewart & Feder, 1987).

# Resources

www.citiprogram.org (Collaborative Institutional Training Initiative)—We recommend this course to all students and professionals considering or planning to do research. It is required at all institutions with an Institutional Review Board (IRB; i.e., all universities and medical institutions).

Data Management in Biomedical Research—For a copy of this conference report, contact the Office of Research Integrity (ORI) at askori@osophs.dhhs.gov.

http://ori.dhhs.gov/html/publications/conference.asp— This website for the ORI contains handbooks and guidelines in MS Word and pdf formats.

www.oig.nsf.gov/pub.htm—This website provides National Science Foundation Office of the Inspector General publications, including misconduct in science; 4201 Wilson Blvd., Suite 1135, Arlington, VA. Tel: 703-292-7100. E-mail: oig@ nsf.gov. Hotline: 800-428-2189.

# Notes

1. Prior to this effort, what was then the Biofeedback Society of America commissioned a set of efficacy reviews for a select number of disorders, with the findings published in a volume edited by Hatch, Fisher, and Rugh (1987). More recently, Yucha and Montgomery (2008) completed an overview of additional disorders, but with less extensive analysis than the “white paper” reviews.

2. The term “subjects” also refers to patients and clients who are part of clinical research.

3. “Single-case” and “single-subject” designs are the common terms (Barlow et al., 2009). We agree with Hawkins's (1989) preference for the term “individual-subject designs” and adopt it for use here. His preference avoids the implications of application only to clinical cases or the use of only one subject in a study. He also steers away from the term “within-subject,” because “it does not discriminate the design from a group design in which subjects in all groups are measured across time” (p. 127).

4. Freedland and Carney (1992) noted that during misconduct investigations, the new regulations by the U.S. Public Health Service (PHS) and National Science Foundation provide for data audits by the PHS Office of Scientific Integrity Review (1990) and the National Science

# Headache

# Frank A ndrasik and Mark S. Schwartz

Applied psychophysiological and related nonpharmacological treatments are now commonly accepted for the most prevalent forms of headache. Since the first publications appeared on the utility of these treatments (late 1960s to early 1970s), we estimate that biofeedback and relaxation therapies have been applied to hundreds of thousands of people (in clinical and research settings) for problematic headaches. Furthermore, these biofeedback1 studies have helped to stimulate research about the causes of headaches, treatment effectiveness, and mechanisms of treatment.

# Headache Cl ass ification and D iagnosis

Prior to 1962, there was no agreed-upon system for classifying primary headache disorders. The 1962 system, which investigators employed intermittently, was significantly lacking in precision. Recognizing this, in 1985, the International Headache Society assembled headache experts from around the globe to enumerate the various types of headaches and develop explicit inclusion and exclusion criteria (along the lines of the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders prepared by the American Psychiatric Association). This effort culminated in publication of an extensive manual in 1988—the Classification and Diagnostic Criteria for Headache Disorders, Cranial Neuralgias and Facial Pain (Classification Committee of the

International Headache Society, 1988), which was updated in 2004 and commonly termed as ICHD-2 (Headache Classification Subcommittee of the International Headache Society, 2004), with a “beta version” often labeled as ICHD-3 (Headache Classification Committee of the International Headache Society [IHS], 2013).

This beta version warrants brief comment. It was occasioned chiefly because of marked advances in headache understanding (prior versions were admittedly based as much on opinion as on evidence; this version is more evidence-based) and efforts to make it compatible or “synchronized” with the World Health Organization’s next revision (11th edition), known as the International Classification of Diseases, or ICD-11. ICD-11 is undergoing field trials and the Headache Classification Committee recommends this be done as well for ICHD-3 (hence, the term “beta” version). It may take 2 to 3 years before each version is finalized. Making ICHD compatible with ICD will greatly facilitate communication among clinicians and researchers and, we hope, reimbursement. This beta version attempts to be much more precise by incorporating a hierarchical classification system (which permits up to five levels of specification for many headache types); requiring a diagnosis for each specific type (prior versions have handled this in various ways), listed in their judged order of importance; incorporating minimal attack frequency as necessary; recommending that detailed headache diaries be utilized when precise classification is unclear; and providing an Appendix to facilitate future research and clarity with respect to certain headache types (new headache types, alternative proposed criteria, and old or former entities that still lack validity).

A list of the current diagnostic criteria for the various forms of headache is beyond the scope of this chapter, but they may be found in Headache Classification Subcommittee of the International Headache Society (2004). The ICHD-2 classification comprises three parts: Part One: Primary headache disorders (or headaches not attributable to another medical condition), with four major categories and 57 subtypes; Part Two: Secondary headache disorders (those attributable to another medical condition), with eight major categories and 152 subtypes and subforms; and Part Three: Cranial neuralgias, facial pain, and “other headaches to be defined.” Migraine and tension-type headaches (TTHs) are the two most common primary headache disorders and are most likely to be seen by nonphysician health care providers. Thus, these and some of their variants, as well as conditions that can exacerbate their presentation, are the main focus on this chapter.

# Migraine

Migraine, a chronic condition with periodic attacks (Haut, Bigal, & Lipton, 2006), is further divided on the basis of attack frequency: episodic migraine, in which headaches occur 14 or fewer days per month versus chronic migraine, in which headaches occur 15 or more days per month and at least eight of these are migraine attacks (see expanded definition in Headache Classification Committee of the International Headache Society, 2006). This distinction is important, because research indicates that individuals with chronic migraine experience greater occupational and academic consequences, headache-related disability, and psychological comorbidities. As a result, they seek out care more often and encounter higher health care costs (Buse, Manack, Serrano, Turkel, & Lipton, 2010). The Global Burden of Disease Survey 2010 ranked migraine as the third most prevalent disorder and the seventh greatest cause of disability worldwide.

Our early pathophysiological models of migraine were overly simplified, with peripheral vascular abnormalities being the main causal factor. We now know that migraine is multidetermined, with biochemical imbalances, neurotransmitter/ receptor dysfunction, and neuronal suppression and sensitization all playing key roles (Gazerani, Wang, Cairns, Svensson, & Arendt-Nielsen, 2006; Burstein & Jakubowski, 2005). Pietrobon and Moskowitz (2013) perhaps say it best: “Once considered exclusively a disorder of blood vessels, compelling evidence has led to the realization that migraine represents a highly choreographed interaction between major inputs from both the peripheral and central nervous systems, with the trigeminovascular system and the cerebral cortex among the main players” (p. 365).

Symptoms of migraine are fairly distinct: pain that is most often unilateral, pulsating, and aggravated by movement/activity. Nausea, vomiting, and/or photo-, phono-, or osmophobia (sensitivity to light, sound, or smells, respectively) frequently accompany migraine. Migraine is three to four times more prevalent in females than in men. Approximately 1 in 5 migraineurs experience a distinct aura that is characterized by focal neurological features/symptoms (mostly visual in nature) that begin up to 1 hour before headache onset. Therefore, migraine is further subdivided into migraine with and without aura. Some believe that patients with aura may be especially responsive to biofeedback treatments, because the aura would signal an impending headache, allowing patients to begin applying self-regulation skills and possibly abort an attack or lessen its severity. However, this notion has not been extensively investigated. Some patients experience a premonitory (hours or days before headache onset) and a resolution phase, characterized by a host of symptoms (e.g., food cravings, repetitive yawning, neck stiffness/pain, fatigue, depression, hypo- or hyperactivity).

# Tension‑Type Headache

TTH has been labeled many different ways over the years—muscle contraction, psychogenic, depression, stress, conversion, and psychomyogenic headache—which highlights the varied views and confusion about its etiology. The IHS-2 system uses the term “tension-type” to reflect the continued uncertainty about its cause and distinguishes three subtypes: (1) “infrequent episodic” (frequency is less than one headache per month); (2) “frequent episodic” (frequency of 1–14 days per month); and (3) “chronic daily headache” (frequency of 15 or more days per month). This breakdown resulted from observations indicating that the more frequent the TTH, the more difficult it is to treat. Some of the pain likely results from myofascial tension (Lenaerts, 2007) and reduced inhibitory activity of brainstem interneurons (Rossi, Vollono, Valeriani, & Sandrini, 2011), which helps to explain the way the pain is typically manifested: dull, nonpulsating quality; bilateral distribution; and mild to moderate intensity. It is not uncommon for TTH to coexist with migraine. In the past, coexisting headaches were termed as “mixed,” “tension–vascular,” or “combined” headache, but current recommendations call for separate diagnoses.

# Cluster Headache

If one sees a large number of headache patients, a third type of primary headache, the cluster headache, may appear from time to time. This type of headache is far more common in males and is characterized by a stabbing, agonizing pain in or around the eye (leading some to refer to it as the “suicide headache”; Markley & Buse, 2006). Cluster headaches typically last about an hour and occur in multiple episodes or “clusters,” some repeating several times per day, with a bout lasting a few weeks to a few months. A small percentage of cluster patients experience nearly continuous or “chronic” cluster headaches. We only mention cluster headache here, because the very limited available research data suggest that biofeedback and relaxation are of limited value with these patients (Blanchard, Andrasik, Jurish, & Teders, 1982a). As one might imagine, this form of headache can lead to considerable distress from the repeated, intense attacks. Training in relaxation and related strategies, however, may be of some adjunctive value in coping with the overwhelming distress this condition creates (Andrasik, 1990; Robbins et al., 2012).

# Medication Overuse Headache

Certain medications commonly used for headache management can actually worsen an existing headache condition, leading to a condition now termed “medication overuse headache” (MOH; formerly termed “rebound,” “drug-induced,” or “medication-misuse” headache). A common clinical presentation is a patient whose headaches initially were intense but infrequent. Over time, medication use increases (taking stronger doses or administering abortive or palliative medications on a more frequent basis, more like a prophylaxis), accompanied by an insidious increase in headache frequency. The three criteria used for diagnosing MOH relate to frequency of use and type of medication. The initial criteria for MOH added a fourth criterion—“headache resolves or reverts to its previous pattern within 2 months after discontinuation of overused medication” (Silberstein et al., 2005). This criterion was subsequently omitted as being clinically impractical (patient would need to be withdrawn from all offending medications and remain untreated for a substantial period to confirm the diagnosis). Fulfilling the three criteria for MOH involves the following:

1. Headache that is present 15 or more days per month.   
2. Regular overuse, for a period exceeding 3 months, wherein one or more of the following acute/symptomatic drugs are consumed: a. Ergotamine, triptans, opioids, or combination analgesics are consumed 10 or more days each month. b. Simple analgesics or any combination of the above are consumed 15 or more days each month, without overuse of any single class alone.

3. Headache developed or became markedly worse during the period of medication overuse.

We have known for some time that overuse of medication can complicate treatment and lessen the effectiveness of otherwise helpful treatments (Kudrow, 1982; Saper, 1987). When MOH is present, the prime objective is to cease use of the offending medication and institute more appropriate treatment, because this condition is a risk factor for progression from episodic to chronic forms of migraine (Bigal et al., 2008). Patients who take excessive amounts of medication are also more likely to experience multiple psychiatric conditions that need attention, most notably anxiety and depression (Radat et al., 2005).

Excess consumption of medication is often motivated by anticipation and fear of future attacks (hence the tendency to self-administer acute medications as prophylaxis; Peres et al., 2007), which makes patients very reluctant to cease their medication intake. Once medication is stopped, relapse rates are high (Grazzi, Andrasik, Usai, & Bussone, 2009). The withdrawal process itself can temporarily intensify headache pain, nausea, vomiting, and other symptoms. Ongoing therapist contact and support, medication to help alleviate withdrawal effects, administration of appropriate medications, and instruction in behavioral coping skills may help patients during this “washout” period. Grazzi et al. (2002) found it necessary to hospitalize a group of patients taking excessive medications for a brief period in order to withdraw from their offending medications and begin more appropriate medication regimens. Some of the patients received biofeedback-assisted relaxation in addition to medication detoxification. At a 1-year follow-up, outcomes were equivalent for both groups. However, at the 3-year followup, patients receiving the combined treatment revealed greater improvement on two of the three outcome measures and significantly lower rates of relapse (see Figure 20.1). More detailed information on MOH and its impact on headache treatment outcome may be found in Da Silva and Lake (2014) and at www.marksschwartzphd.com.

# Medical and N eurol ogical Consul tation and Laboratory T ests

Most headaches are relatively benign, but for 1–3% of patients, the etiology can be life-threatening (Evans, 2001). Nonphysician practitioners need to have medical assurance that the patients they are treating have been properly examined and maintain ongoing contact during treatment, because medical problems can subsequently develop. Headache is a somewhat unusual condition, in that diagnoses are arrived at chiefly by ruling out serious pathology (vs. the more typical ruling in approach). Physicians arrive at a diagnosis based mainly on obtaining an extensive history and performing a detailed in-office examination (focusing on general health, as well as pertinent neurological aspects), ordering tests only if necessary (Olesen & Dodick, 2006). Dodick (2003) has prepared the following list of five “red flags” or “danger signs” that suggest the presence of a serious underlying medical condition and the possible need for an immediate referral to a physician for more in-depth evaluation:

1. Systemic signs of illness (e.g., fever, persistent/ progressive vomiting, weakened immunology).   
2. Neurological signs/symptoms (e.g., seizures, compromised mental status, focal signs).   
3. Onset that is sudden or new (particularly for persons 40 and older).   
4. Other associated symptoms (e.g., headache as a result of trauma, headache that awakens one from sleep).   
5. Prior history that is now markedly different (e.g., pattern, severity, and/or frequency have increased considerably).

![](images/81a13acadac0decc03ba067d29714033e4c21270a5be62dc15e8aba9499503e7.jpg)  
FI GURE 20.1. Percentage of migraine patients who relapsed to analgesic overuse at 3-year follow-up after being assigned to either medication treatment alone or medication treatment combined with biofeedback-assisted relaxation to combat initial analgesic overuse. Data from Grazzi et al. (2002).

Nonmedical providers need to be mindful and observant for such changes in presentation.

It may be tempting to overlook the need for a close working collaboration with a physician headache specialist, especially when treating a patient such as the following. Consider a patient in his or her 30s with a history of many years of clearly unchanging headaches diagnosed as TTH. Consider further that the patient has had multiple medical and/or neurological examinations over the past few years, along with appropriate and thorough laboratory studies. In this scenario, a phone call or other brief contact with the patient’s physician (duly noted in the patient’s file) might be sufficient, without the necessity for insisting of repeating medical or neurological examinations. The risk of a serious underlying medical condition would appear to be quite low here, but any risk greater than zero is unacceptable. In situations like this, it might be helpful to discuss the issue candidly with the patient and suggest and offer to arrange for the medical and/or neurological services on behalf of the patient. Finally, mild abnormalities often show up, but these have not been shown to interfere with or disrupt effectiveness of biofeedback and related treatment (Morrill, Blanchard, Barron, & Dentinger, 1990).

tem that is activated. Psychological and behavioral factors do not play a causal role per se; rather they contribute to headache as factors that trigger, maintain, or exacerbate headache, or as sequelae to continued head pain that subsequently disrupt overall functioning (Andrasik, Lipchik, McCrory, & Wittrock, 2005b).

# Efficacy of Biofeedback and R el ated T reatments

# A Biops ychosocial Model  of Headache

The biopsychosocial model provides a most useful guide for determining the role of biofeedback and related behavioral treatments for individuals experiencing recurrent headache disorders (Andrasik, Flor, & Turk, 2005a) (and many other disorders as well). It stands in marked contrast to the “biomedical model,” which views pain as emanating from the direct transmission of impulses from the periphery to structures with the central nervous system. According to the biopsychosocial model, the likelihood of a person experiencing headache depends on the specific pathophysiological mechanisms that are “triggered” by the interplay of the person’s physiological status (e.g., level of autonomic arousal), environmental factors (e.g., stressors, certain foods, alcohol, toxins, hormonal fluctuations), ability to cope with these factors (both behavioral and cognitive), and consequential factors that may serve to reinforce and increase the person’s chance of reporting head pain (Martin, 1993; Waggoner & Andrasik, 1990). The main determinant for the resulting headache is the pathophysiological biological response sys

Investigations of treatment effectiveness are far too numerous to review study by study, so reviewers have resorted to two approaches: Assemble a panel of experts who comb the literature and evaluate only studies meeting well defined design criteria (more of a qualitative review) or conduct a large-scale statistical analysis (quantitative or meta-analysis) that enters actual outcome data from well-controlled studies to determine the aggregate average magnitude of change or effect size for individual treatments being compared. The most extensive evidence-based review was conducted by a panel of experts assembled under the auspices of the U.S. Headache Consortium, wherein representatives from seven different medical societies participated: American Academy of Family Physicians, American Academy of Neurology, American Headache Society, American College of Emergency Physicians, American College of Physicians–American Society of Internal Medicine, American Osteopathic Association, and National Headache Foundation (Silberstein & the U.S. Headache Consortium, 2000). This group graded the level of evidence supporting various behavioral treatments for migraine management, which is presented in Table 20.1. If this panel were reconvened today, we are certain its members would assign a grade of “A” to the combination of behavioral combined with pharmacological treatments due to the availability of a few additional well-controlled trials on this topic for migraine (in particular, see the large-scale study by Holroyd et al., 2010). This group also pointed out circumstances in which behavioral treatments are likely to be especially beneficial for migraineurs (see Table 20.2).

Of the many meta-analyses conducted to date, all have indicated evidence of the utility of biofeedback, relaxation, stress management training, and combinations of the aforementioned treatments for both migraine and TTH (see Andrasik, 2007; Andrasik & Buse, 2013; Penzien, Rains, &

Grade A: Relaxation training, relaxation combined with thermal biofeedback training, electromyographic biofeedback, and cognitive-behavioral therapy “may be considered as treatment options for prevention of migraine” (p. 9), although there is no evidence to suggest what specific treatment is optimal for which patient.

Grade B: Behavioral therapy “may be combined with preventive drug therapy to achieve additional clinical improvement for migraine relief” (p. 9).

Note. Data from Silberstein and the U.S. Headache Consortium (2000). Grade A evidence is defined as multiple, welldesigned randomized controlled trials (RCTs) that reveal a consistent pattern of positive findings. Grade B evidence is defined as some supportive evidence from RCTs, but it is not optimal support (often because RCTs were few in number or findings were judged to be inconsistent).

For patients who . . .

1. Favor behavioral/nonpharmacological treatments.   
2. Have medical contraindications for specific pharmacological treatments.   
3. Display inadequate or no response to pharmacological treatment.   
4. Exhibit a poor tolerance for particular pharmacological treatments.   
5. Use of analgesic or acute medications over a long duration, often, or excessive amounts. The presence of any of these can aggravate headache conditions.   
6. Either experienced substantial stressful events or have insufficient stress-coping abilities.   
7. Are pregnant, are planning to become pregnant, or are nursing.

Andrasik, 2002). A series of meta-analyses conducted by Holroyd and colleagues have shown that behavioral treatments compare favorably to common prophylactic medications, such as betablockers and calcium channel blockers (Holroyd & Penzien, 1990; Holroyd, Penzien, & Cordingley, 1991; Holroyd, Penzien, Rokicki, & Cordingley, 1992).

The most recent meta-analysis warrants further discussion as it provides a more in-depth analysis of biofeedback treatments for migraine and TTH (Nestoriuc, Martin, Rief, & Andrasik, 2008b). This analysis extracted data from 94 outcome investigations, which included approximately 3500 patients, with follow-up assessments occurring at a mean length of 14 months. This review included “intent-to-treat” analyses when possible, as well as “fail safe analyses” to assess for “file drawer bias” in order to gauge more thoroughly the significance of findings. Over 4000 unpublished studies with zero effects would have to exist (within file drawers) to reduce the mean effect sizes observed from this meta-analysis to a value of zero. Thus, publication bias appears highly unlikely. All prior meta-analyses have focused chiefly on pain outcome measures, but these investigators were also able to include collateral improvements in secondary symptoms related to cognitive and emotional functioning (depression, anxiety, and self-efficacy). As seen in Figures 20.2–20.4, various biofeedback treatments for migraine and electromyography (EMG) for TTH were shown to produce meaningful improvements with respect not only to primary pain outcome measures but also secondary (and untargeted) symptoms. These results both confirm and extend findings from prior metaanalyses focusing chiefly on biofeedback (Nestoriuc & Martin, 2007; Nestoriuc, Rief, & Martin, 2008a).

# Cognitive and Other Psychotherapies

# Rationale

Cognitive and cognitive-behavioral therapies have a long history in the treatment of recurrent headaches, beginning with the work of Holroyd, Andrasik, and Westbrook (1977). One can logically justify the use of cognitive therapies for many patients with headaches, and there is research to indicate their specific value (but whether they increment outcome over and above that obtained with relaxation and biofeedback remains unclear). Below we list some situations wherein these approaches may merit special consideration:

![](images/ab386de6712d6bdf9db5bbc8b2110182c4fab458913cddc7983a4dd4dbb744fc.jpg)  
FI GURE 20.2. Mean weighted effect sizes (and 95% confidence intervals) for migraine pain for various biofeedback methods from a meta-analysis of studies of biofeedback treatment for migraine (Nestoriuc et al., 2008b). k, number of independent effect sizes entered into the calculation; EEG-FB, electroencephalographic feedback; EMG-FB, electromyographic feedback; TEMP-FB, peripheral skin temperature feedback; RT, relaxation training; BVP-FB, blood volume pulse feedback.

![](images/134f464c9b4efb9c646bb0b0cfffcad92ce0cbae11a5f44b53425e71de97f6a1.jpg)  
FI GURE 20.3. Mean weighted effect sizes (with 95% confidence intervals) for various headache outcome measures from a meta-analysis of studies of biofeedback treatment for headache (Nestoriuc et al., 2008b). Results are for all biofeedback procedures combined in the treatment of migraine (Panel A) and for electromyographic biofeedback alone in the treatment of TTH (Panel B). k, number of independent effect sizes entered into the calculation.

![](images/f4159983b03022fb16e12dc2dc82791226e61ca387494a27ec25060644a46e28.jpg)  
FI GURE 20.4. Mean weighted effect sizes (with 95% confidence intervals) for secondary outcome measures related to cognitive and emotional function from a meta-analysis of studies of biofeedback treatment for headache (Nestoriuc et al., 2008b). Results are for electromyographic biofeedback alone in the treatment of TTH (top panel) and for all biofeedback procedures combined in the treatment of migraine (bottom panel). k, number of independent effect sizes entered into the calculation.

1. Many clinical practitioners and researchers believe that cognitive factors and therapies are particularly important.   
2. Practitioners and researchers are always seeking ways to enhance treatment.   
3. Cognitive therapies may be especially well suited for addressing concomitant psychological distress (e.g., anxiety or depression) that often accompanies headache (Holroyd & Andrasik, 1982).   
4. Cognitive therapies typically place a high value on patient education, patient involvement, and patient analysis of factors influencing headache course (whereas other approaches do not always do so).

Appelbaum et al. (1990) summarized other key considerations. They proposed that patients’ perceptions and experiences of stressful situations account for the worsening and/or maintenance of headaches. The automatic thoughts that accompany these perceptions probably mediate stress reactivity and headache onset for many patients.

In turn, one or both of two factors may mediate TTHs. One is the individual’s perception of a lack of control when facing these stressful situations. Another is the chronic muscular tension and sympathetic arousal that occurs in response to these situations.

# Selective Review of Studies

# Tension ‑Type  He ad aches

Pure cognitive therapy was more successful than a self-regulatory approach with relaxation and bifrontal EMG auditory biofeedback for recruited subjects (Holroyd et al., 1977). Results were maintained at a 2-year follow-up (Holroyd & Andrasik, 1980). Adding cognitive therapy to muscle relaxation therapy increased the percentage of patients showing clinically significant improvement within a regular office contact model (Blanchard et al., 1990b); 10 (62.5%) of 16 patients in the combined condition showed at least 50% reduction of headaches, compared to six (31.6%) of 19 patients in the relaxation-only group. However, the overall magnitude of change in a headache index did not reveal a similar advantage for adding cognitive therapy. We should note that this study used progressive muscle relaxation without biofeedback. We also note that the relaxation-only group did not fare as well as expected from other studies, and fared no better than a “pseudomeditation” group. The data support the addition of cognitive therapy. However, it is not clear whether it always yields better results than do relaxation alone or biofeedback.

Combining a tailored cognitive therapy and a relaxation procedure was more effective than relaxation alone in a reduced-contact, self-administered format (Tobin, Holroyd, Baker, Reynolds, & Holm, 1988). Tailoring involved problem solving and cognitive restructuring for each patient. The relaxation therapy was progressive muscle relaxation. One limitation was the lack of a control group.

Another study indicated that cognitive therapy was better than a combination of muscle and autogenic relaxation procedures without biofeedback (Murphy, Lehrer, & Jurish, 1990) for reducing headache frequency and severity of the worst headache for each week. The authors speculated that the reason might be patients’ improved ability to manage stressors. It should be noted, however, that the improvement for both groups was below that typically observed in other studies. For example, the headache index improvement was only about 30% for relaxation and 47% for cognitive therapy. These were both lower than rates found in other studies using similar therapies. The authors admitted doubting whether their subjects in both groups actually used the specific techniques; thus, this may be more a study of exposure to therapy than of the use of therapy. The authors also asked whether the difference from other studies could have resulted from the slightly older ages of their subjects. The mean age was about 40. Other studies (Appelbaum et al., 1990) do not suggest that patients of this age should do worse than younger patients.

A reduced-contact study showed no advantage for combining a fixed set of cognitive procedures and relaxation therapies compared with relaxation alone (Appelbaum et al., 1990). In contrast to the findings of Tobin et al. (1988), the patients were on average 9 years older (37 vs. 28) and had suffered headaches 6 years longer. Furthermore, the cognitive therapy protocol was fixed instead of tailored. These factors could result in reducing the advantage of cognitive therapy. The authors considered whether more time and more cognitive therapy would help. It might take longer than 1 month for the effects of cognitive therapy to show. The authors also considered a possible ceiling effect. In such a situation, adding more strategies to an already valid therapy might prove to be counterproductive.

In conclusion, the potential advantage of adding cognitive therapy to relaxation alone or to a combination of relaxation and biofeedback is not entirely clear. There are conditions in which adding cognitive therapy to relaxation does not increase treatment effectiveness. We also do not know whether brief and tailored cognitive therapy for each patient would be sufficient. Nevertheless, many practitioners continue to believe that cognitive factors and therapies are valuable, at least for selected patients. The logic is too strong to ignore this in clinical practice. The vital questions are these: What types of cognitive therapy are useful, what specific procedures are most useful, for whom are they useful, and when should one introduce cognitive therapy? Prudent clinical judgment, the use of a stepped-care model, and cost containment must remain in the forefront of clinical practice until research answers these questions, and support for them arrives.

# Migr aines and  Combined  He ad aches

The focus here is on selected research with cognitive therapies for migraines and patients with both migraine and tension-type headaches (mixed headaches). One study combined cognitive therapy with temperature biofeedback and compared this combination to temperature biofeedback and relaxation without the cognitive therapy component (Blanchard et al., 1990a). The patients had vascular or mixed headaches. Treatment was administered within a limited-office-contact model. All treatments were better than a symptom-monitoring, waiting-list control group; however, results with cognitive therapy were no better than those without it.

Another study showed that in a regular officecontact model, combining cognitive therapy with temperature biofeedback had about the same results as such biofeedback alone for patients with vascular (migraine and mixed) headaches (Blanchard et al., 1990c). Treatment was better than no treatment. There was no advantage in providing a combination of relaxation and cognitive therapy for patients with migraines treated in a clinic-based model with eight sessions versus a limited-contact model with two sessions (Richardson & McGrath, 1989). Both treatment groups fared better than a waiting-list control group.

# Conclusions and Recommendations for Cognitive Therapies

1. Practitioners can justify combining cognitive therapies with relaxation and biofeedback for selected patients. However, the added value is not consistent for all patients. One should not routinely start with the combination when one is using a stepped-care model and seeking cost containment (see later sections).   
2. A questionnaire such as that employed by Murphy et al. (1990) allows assessments of patients’ ability to prevent headaches and to function with headaches. It also allows assessment of patients’ perceived personal control over their headaches.   
3. Patient education is very important to explain the rationale for cognitive and other therapies and to provide therapy instructions (see Holroyd & Andrasik, 1982, for further discussion).   
4. Practitioners should consider tailoring cognitive therapies.   
5. Results and the ceiling effect from relaxation and biofeedback may depend on several factors.

One should consider asking the following types of questions:

•	 Is biofeedback used to change patients’ cognitions about their sense of control?   
•	 Is the feedback information adequate?   
•	 Is the relaxation with biofeedback tailored to patients? For example, was it from different muscle areas, such as the cervical neck, trapezii, and occipital muscles? Is the feedback with varied postures and activities?   
•	 Are there other therapy changes during and after therapy?   
•	 Is there sufficient patient education to teach the rationale, procedures, and expectations for relaxation and biofeedback?   
•	 What is the frequency and timing of relaxation practice?

One can speculate that these factors may result in approaching or teaching maximum effectiveness. If so, then cognitive therapy might not add much. Each practitioner decides the role of cognitive therapies and tailors treatment for patients.

# Iss ues and Op tions to C onsider during T reatment

Some providers rely primarily on biofeedback and relaxation therapies; others incorporate these approaches with other behavioral and stress management therapies. Research has provided useful information and answers to many questions and issues, some of which are addressed here (see others at www.marksschwartzphd.com).

Clinical biofeedback is justifiable and cost-efficient for treating many patients with headaches. Published research and clinical experience are sufficient to support reimbursement. However, some third-party reimbursement companies are unaware that reimbursing for biofeedback, relaxation, and other applied psychophysiological therapies can reduce costs to them. Practitioners need to remain aware of and be sensitive to the questions and concerns of the very cautious critics, as well as of those providing payments. In particular, practitioners need to be careful about who provides the services, how, for whom, and when. Procedures need to be tailored to individual patients and provided in cost-effective and efficient ways. Before discussing these and other questions and issues, however, we present a very brief, selected review of psychophysiological data bearing on headache diagnosis. (Note that throughout the chapter, terms given in italics are ones defined in the glossary at the chapter’s end.)

# Psychophysiology and Headache

Extensive attention has been given to identifying differences in key psychophysiological variables, most notably EMG and blood flow measures that might reliably distinguish different headache types and headache states. In fact a quick search of PubMed, using the search expression “EMG and primary headache disorders,” revealed nearly 300 citations for this one modality alone. Adequately reviewing this complex and extensive body of research would require all of the space allocated to this chapter.

# Influence of Psychological, Emotional, Stress, and Other Factors

Examining an Age‑Old Assumption

The common and usually accepted hypothesis that psychological, emotional, and stress factors can trigger and/or worsen headaches is an age-old assumption and another complex topic. There is considerable support for the relationship, although it is very difficult methodologically for research to demonstrate a clear, unequivocal, and strong direct link. More complex are the clear demonstration and full elucidation of the mechanisms.

Practitioners, researchers, and patients assume that many factors can affect the likelihood of developing or worsening a headache. Examples include the following:

•	 Major stressors and high-density minor daily stressors.   
•	 Negative cognitive perceptions and appraisal of stress events.   
•	 Excessive or prolonged emotional reactions to stress.   
•	 Inadequate stress management skills and behaviors.   
Personality features (e.g., obsessive–compulsive characteristics, avoidance of expressing anger).   
•	 Lack of stress-moderating factors (e.g., social support).

These examples do not preclude other “risk factors” or “triggers,” nor does the research below suggest or support the existence of migraine or tension headache “personalities,” which once was a prominent theme.

Many practitioners may wonder why this longheld assumption even needs research support. “Everyone already knows this,” some may say. However, consider that, at least for many people with migraines and perhaps for many people with tension-type headaches, many very different types of factors are thought to act as “triggers” and contributing factors to these headaches when present either alone or in combination. Consider, for example, the following list of assumed and patient-reported factors in migraines2 and some TTHs:

•	 Lack of food, as in fasting, delayed meals, or missed meals.   
•	 Specific foods and drinks.   
•	 Sleep abnormalities (both excessive and insufficient sleep).   
•	 Hormones associated with menstruation, menopause, and pregnancy.   
•	 Posture, head and neck positions, other ergonomic factors, sleeping positions, and incorrect pillows.   
• Temporomandibular behaviors and other factors.   
•	 Visual factors (e.g., eye strain, glare, and staring at a video display terminal screen).   
•	 Environmental factors (e.g., barometric pressure, heat, and cold).   
•	 Environmental irritants (e.g., noise, odors, smoke, and allergens).   
•	 Activities such as exercise and automobile travel.   
• “Let-down” phenomena called “weekend headache,” “Sunday headache,” or “relaxation headache.”

Further complicating the understanding and demonstration of a relationship is the following: Many people with these types of headaches and a few or several of the assumed “psychological–affective–behavioral–physiological” risk factors probably also have other major, exclusive, or sufficient causes for their headaches (e.g., postures, muscle tension habits, sleep problems, dietary problems). Even assuming that the relationship in question exists (and we do make that assumption), and assuming the presence of these “risk factors” in a given person, this does not automatically support the conclusion that the risk factors are “causing” the headaches for that person. Furthermore, these assumptions do not necessarily mean that one must treat these cognitive and psychophysiological factors in order to obtain therapeutic effectiveness.

Although some research addresses either migraines or TTHs, this discussion combines both for these reasons:

1. Many studies have addressed both headache types.   
2. Disagreements continue about the distinction between the types.   
3. Some studies do not make a distinction between migraines TTHs.   
4. Frequently, both types exist in the same person.   
5. Even when the two types are distinct, they often interact with each other.   
6. Psychological and emotional factors influence both types.

Evidence from studies on psychological, emotional, and stress factors support their role in contributing directly or indirectly to headaches (see more recent reviews by Ashina et al., 2012; Buse, Silberstein, Manack, Papapetropoulos, & Lipton, 2013; Cathcart, Petkov, Winefield, Lushington, & Rolan, 2010a; Cathcart, Winefield, Lushington, & Rolan, 2010b). Note that stress can serve to contribute to the expression and maintenance of individual headache episodes in at least five ways: (1) as a predisposer due to a preexisting personal vulnerability; (2) as a precipitant; (3) as an exacerbator, one that may also play a role in a headache transforming from an episodic to chronic condition; (4) as a perpetuator, by impacting disability and quality of life; and (5) as a consequence, feeding back as another source of stress (Nash & Thebarge, 2006).

Nevertheless, we offer a brief review of a few of the earlier studies as illustrations. To start, we know that laboratory stress can induce headaches (Gannon, Haynes, Cuevas, & Chavez, 1987; Haynes, Gannon, Bank, Shelton, & Goodwin, 1990). In addition, we know there is support to indicate that ratings of stress are higher during periods before and on the day of migraine headaches (Levor, Cohen, Nalibott, McArthur, & Heuser, 1986). Support for the effect of stress on migraines also comes from a careful, prospective, 6-month study, showing that stress increased the day before the clear-cut migraine occurred for six of the seven subjects, and during the headache day for three more of 13 German postal employees (Kohler & Haimerl, 1990). Of these 13, 11 had “common migraine,” two had “classic migraine” (now termed migraine with aura), and some also had so-called “mixed” headaches. On the day before, 109 of 192 migraine patients scored in the upper third of the distributions on a 10-item, ipsative stress questionnaire. This led the researchers to conclude that stress effects are considerable as regards the occurrence of migraine attacks (Kohler & Haimerl, 1990). There was no significant relationship between low air pressure on the day of the migraines for any of the individuals. However, that factor was significant for the group and occurred on the days of 81 of 192 migraine attacks. Compared with stress the day before, low air pressure had a weaker effect.

An interesting and clinically useful discussion of “weekend headaches” provides insights into potential psychological explanations for this phenomenon (Nattero et al., 1989). People with “weekend headaches” tend to have headaches (usually diagnosed as migraines) during a “let-down” period rather than in the midst of stress. These investigators compared patients with only weekend headaches to those with common migraines without reference to day. The investigators explain their speculations about these patients: “The . . . headache with ‘a loss in his structure of the week’ . . . might be generated by that feeling of emptiness experienced . . . in view of drab weekend days, where the patient has no real interests outside of work. . . . Some upsetting situations in patients’ private lives, such as marital conflicts, can also be considered as precipitants especially during weekends” (Nattero et al., 1989, p. 97).

Based on findings from various psychological inventories, when comparing patients with common migraines to patients with weekend headaches, Nattero et al. (1989) reported the latter as lacking “a real interest in sexual life,” experiencing a poor family and social life, being dissatisfied with what they had accomplished, and possessing low self-esteem. The authors acknowledged that causal statements could not be supported by their study design (whether the patients with weekend headaches experienced a greater stress burden or less capacity to cope with stress could not be determined).

The physiological disregulation model of G. E. Schwartz (1977, 1978, 1979) provides useful concepts to help explain factors affecting the risk of developing any psychophysiological problem, such as a headache. The model, in its simplest form, postulates that four stages are involved in physiological regulation. In Stage 1, an environmental demand is placed on the person, requiring the brain (Stage 2) to engage in some regulation to meet the demand. In Stage 3, certain bodily systems are either activated or inhibited. If the bodily systems encounter excessive strain, a negative feedback loop (Stage 4) comes into play, requiring the brain to engage in another course of action. This feedback is often experienced as pain. Physiological dysregulation results when a person does not or cannot attend to his or her physiological state and, as a result, actions are not taken by the person to restore normal functioning. For more detailed discussions of the role of psychological and cognitive factors in the genesis of and worsening of headaches, and a discussion of the role of cognitive and psychotherapy treatments for headaches, see Martin (1993).
