For some people who are prone to developing headaches and for others who are not so prone, and for those already with headaches, there are psychophysiological (in the broadest sense of that term) “triggers” and “risk factors” that can increase the risk of developing and/or worsening a headache. This presumably occurs through increased muscle activity, biochemical changes associated with autonomic nervous system (ANS) changes, or both (Gannon et al., 1987; Haynes et al., 1990). Therapy often needs to address both these contributions. Marcus (1993) proposes a “combined biochemical–vascular–muscular” or “neurovascular” model. For an excellent review and graphic illustrations of the role of triggers and risk factors resulting in changes in serotonin and other neurotransmitters resulting in headaches, see Marcus (1993) and Olesen and Goadsby (2006).

The present assumed relationship and research support are fundamental and essential for those practitioners engaged in providing cognitivebehavioral and other forms of psychotherapy for patients with these headaches. Despite this longheld belief among practitioners, further research support is needed to bolster these types of intentions and to support improvements in selection of patients for these types of therapies.

This assumption is also useful for practitioners who include stressors in psychophysiological assessments (“stress profiling”; see Arena & Schwartz, Chapter 8, this volume). For example, if one can induce “sufficient” psychophysiological reactivity and assess this and the recovery from the stimulation, and if these psychophysiological measures are useful for patient education, fostering cognitive changes and treatment planning (including biofeedback procedures), then supporting and demonstrating the hypothesized relationships achieve practical importance.

Furthermore, research support in this area helps identify possible precursors of the muscle tension and/or biochemical changes that are the presumed physiological correlates and assumed necessary contributing factors to causing or worsening headaches. (See www.marksschwartzphd.com.)

# Conclusions and Implications for Clinical Practice

It is reasonable to assume that many risk factors and triggers exist for headaches. These include excessive and/or sustained muscle tension, and habits that increase and result in this tension; major daily stress; anger, anxiety, depression, and other personality and mood factors; psychological/ cognitive and psychophysiological effects of exposure to stressors; and inadequate social support. In addition to these, putting psychological and stress factors in proper perspective requires that practitioners be aware of and assess other potential risk factors and triggers—including eating habits, dietary chemicals, drugs, hormonal status, posture and related factors, sleep schedules and abnormalities, visual factors, and other potentially relevant factors (including the days on which and conditions in which the headaches occur). Bigal and Lipton (2006) discuss remediable and nonremediable risk factors in the progression of infrequent migraines to more chronic forms. These potentially addressable factors include attack frequency, obesity, overuse of acute medications and caffeine, stressful live events, depression, and sleep disorders (these do not include gender, age, and race).

Individual differences abound for people who are prone and not so prone to headaches. For a headache-prone person, one or a few risk factors or triggers may be sufficient to elicit a headache. For other people, several more risk factors and triggers within a relatively short time may be needed to result in a headache. Furthermore, headaches are often “time-lagged,” occurring several hours or days after the obvious exposure to the risk factors and triggers. Prudent and knowledgeable practitioners realize that the risk factors are triggers of the moment and often not just those that immediately precede a headache.

Patients with headaches usually require multidimensional assessment and multicomponent treatment options, and planning tailored to the individual. If one assumes the importance of the roles of serotonin and other neurotransmitters in many patients with headaches (e.g., the neurovascular model), the treatment options include avoidance of triggers and/or providing therapies that affect these neurotransmitters in desired directions. The latter are often medications, but the explanation for the mechanism of relaxation therapies, biofeedback, and cognitive therapies probably involves alterations of serotonin and other neurotransmitters. After all, beliefs do have biological correlates and effects. Our treatments are on a rather molar level, but there must be molecular changes that occur.

# Type of Stress Events versus Cognitive Appraisals

Do stressful events themselves often lead to headaches, or is it the perceptions of stressful events that result in headaches? Implicated in the answer to this question is the role of cognitions in the development of headaches. Research suggests that the everyday ups and downs are more pivotal in contributing to headaches and are perceived as more stressful and disturbing than are the events that occasion major life changes (Holm, Holroyd, Hursey, & Penzien, 1986). Upon completing an extensive review of the literature, Wittrock and Myers (1998) concluded that people with TTH may indeed use different coping strategies for dealing with stress and pain (the well-known “cognitive appraisal” hypothesis).

Hovanitz. Chin, and Warm (1989) used ambulatory EMG monitoring of the posterior neck and showed more EMG activity on days with stress than on days without stress. This was true for patients with TTH and for headache-free control subjects. Elevated muscle activity was not associated with pain. However, even with the very small sample, patients reported more subjective negative affect than did controls. This remains one of the more interesting and important studies in the literature, supporting the role of disregulation in the etiology of TTH.

# Comorbid Psychological Disorders

Individuals with recurrent headache disorders often experience comorbid psychiatric as well as medical disorders, and this is especially true for migraineurs (Buse et al., 2010; Scher, Bigal, & Lipton, 2005). Merikangas and colleagues (see Merikangas, Low, & Rasmussen, 2006, for a review) were among the first researchers to examine critically the comorbidity of migraine and other conditions. “Comorbidity” refers to the coexistence of two conditions within the same person. In studies conducted up to the time of their review, odds ratios ranged from 2.1 to 3.6 for the association between migraine and depression, and from 1.9 to 5.3 for that between migraine and anxiety. Merikangas et al. speculated that migraine, anxiety, and depression may be the result of a partially shared diathesis, consequently producing one syndrome, as opposed to three separate entities. Thus, the psychological status of the patient warrants special attention in order to identify conditions that might complicate treatment and need to be managed prior to or concurrent with treatment of headache (Holroyd, Lipchik, & Penzien, 1998; Lake, 2001; Merikangas & Stevens, 1997; Radat et al., 1999). Common comorbid psychiatric conditions include anxiety, depression, obsessive-compulsive disorder, bipolar disorder, panic disorder, and suicide attempts (Buse et al., 2013; Hamelsky & Lipton, 2006; Jette, Patten, Williams, Becker, & Wiebe, 2008). In migraine, anxiety and depression are associated with greater impairment in functional ability and health-related quality of life, and lowered healthrelated quality of life is correlated with increased migraine-related disability (Lantéri-Minet, Radat, Chautard, & Lucas, 2005). Attending to these and other comorbid conditions may well be crucial to the success of both nonpharmacological and pharmacological treatment, at least for a certain number of patients.

Upon examining the available literature, the following conclusions emerge:

•	 The risk for major depression and anxiety disorders is higher for patients with migraines than for no-migraine controls. This influence is bidirectional. Migraine increases the risk of a subsequent episode of major depression (adjusted relative risk = 4.8), and major depression increases the risk of subsequent migraine (adjusted relative risk = 3.3).   
• Comorbid anxiety and depression lead to increases in disability and contribute to headaches’ becoming intractable. Psychological distress is greater in patients with more frequent and chronic headaches. Depression is implicated in transformation of episodic to chronic TTHs.   
•	 Certain personality disorders reveal a higher incidence of headache than otherwise would be expected (Andrasik, 2003).

Further, but still limited, evidence for the importance of considering psychological factors comes from research examining variables associated with outcomes from nonpharmacological treatments. Early investigations consistently indicated that patients displaying only minor elevations on the Beck Depression Inventory (BDI) had a diminished response to self-regulatory treatments (Blanchard, Jaccard, Andrasik, Guarnieri, & Jurish, 1985b; Jacob, Turner, Szekely, & Eidelman, 1983) and even abortive medication (Holroyd et al., 1989). Other variables have been suggested as predictive of treatment response, but none have been cross-validated to date.

# Ass essm ent

Assessment of headaches includes a description and history of the headaches and other potentially related symptoms and conditions. It also includes a medical and neurological physical examination, laboratory tests, and diagnosis (as previously mentioned). Often it includes psychological consultation, self-report measures, and sometimes psychiatric and sleep disorder consultations. Selfreport measures include those of stress, emotions, and personality, as well as a daily self-report log. Assessment requires that practitioners understand the myriad etiological factors that can cause, emit, maintain, and aggravate headaches. It requires knowing the myths and facts about headaches. In this section, we discuss selected assessment topics. There are numerous sources of information and guidelines for assessing patients with headaches, such as Blau (1990), Blanchard and Andrasik (1985), Buse, Andrasik, and Sollars (2015), Olesen and Dodick (2006), and Saper, Silberstein, Gordon, and Hamel (1993). (Also, see Schwartz, Chapter 14, this volume, for more discussion of intake considerations.)

Nonphysician practitioners using biofeedback wisely prefer referrals from and collaboration with physicians, especially those who have much expertise with headaches. Differential diagnosis is basic (e.g., Saper et al., 1993). Nonphysician practitioners need to know the danger signs that suggest the need for immediate referral to a physician, as presented earlier (Table 20.1 and Table 6-1 from Olesen & Dodick, 2006). A practitioner who does not know the diagnostic and danger signs should consult these sources or others.

# Interview and/or Questionnaire

Practitioners should resist the temptation to rely solely on a self-report questionnaire for diagnosing headaches. The careful clinical interview remains the “gold standard.” Some questionnaires may be useful as adjuncts to the interview but not as the primary method of data gathering (see summary provided by Buse et al., 2015).

# Symptom Records: The Headache Diary

There are primarily two types of self-report measures for headaches (Andrasik et al., 2005b). The most common measure is daily rating of headaches, typically hourly or four to six times a day (time sampling at key intervals). These measures typically use a 6-point (0 to 5) or 11-point (0 to 10) rating scale. Other usually recorded information includes frequency of relaxation; the use of medications, caffeine, and alcohol; and comments about the day. The second type of measure is the global rating by the patient. Patients do this periodically or at the end of therapy. Global ratings can be made either orally or in writing (on a printed rating scale). Such ratings are rarely acceptable for evaluating outcome in research, however. Daily ratings are the norm in research and in clinical practice.

Andrasik and Holroyd (1980a) compared the use of a headache questionnaire at the beginning of treatment to the use of continuous daily hourly ratings over the next 2 weeks. The similarity between the two methods with 99 subjects was very poor, with very small and nonsignificant correlations. The questionnaire reports underestimated the frequency of headaches, overestimated intensity, and both overestimated and underestimated headache durations. The questionnaire test–retest reliability was high and significant. However, it did not correspond well to the data obtained with the daily ratings on an 11-point scale. The authors suggested that questionnairetype methods of assessing headache symptoms should be supplemented by daily headache recordings whenever possible.

The relationship between patients’ ratings and those performed by “significant others” was the focus of the study by Blanchard, Andrasik, Neff, Jurish, and O’Keefe (1981). The relationship between patients’ four-times-daily ratings and the ratings obtained from the “significant others” at the end of therapy was significant (r = .44, p < .002). Although the correlation coefficient was modest, it is comparable to correlations between other concurrent measures of change obtained in behavioral research and indicates a significant degree of social validation for improvement detected from the diary. The correlation between patients’ daily ratings and global ratings on a visual analogue scale (VAS) was even more modest (r = .36, p < .002). Analyses between the two global ratings and daily ratings suggested that global ratings may “produce overestimates of patient improvement.”

It is easier to ask a patient for a global estimate of change. For example, professionals frequently ask the following questions: Are patients’ headaches any different than before therapy? Are they any different compared with headaches the prior week or month? These estimates are made verbally or via standard measures such as a VAS. The problem is that patients often overestimate their improvement compared to their hourly and daily records. This is consistent with the results of Blanchard et al. (1981).

The accuracy of the time a patient makes a rating may be a problem as well. Many subjects record their ratings retrospectively (Epstein & Abel, 1977; Hermann & Blanchard, 1993). We do not know whether this is crucial for accuracy, but it is of concern if one assumes that retrospective ratings depart from accurate ratings. This question begs for research.

Consider reasons why patients may overestimate their improvement. Perhaps a patient is responding only to the improvement of the last few days. Perhaps he or she desperately wants to be better and deceives him- or herself into believing the degree of improvement. Another reason for such self-deception may be a desire to end therapy. A patient may be uncomfortable with telling the therapist that he or she wants to end therapy; the patient also may want to please the therapist. Thus, the patient may tell the therapist what he or she thinks the therapist wants or needs to hear.

There are several methods for analyzing patient symptom data. Consider using multiple measures of change. Methods that have commonly been used are (1) headache index/activity (calculated by summing all intensity values during which a headache is present); (2) average headache intensity (dividing the sum derived via method 1 by the number of recorded hours); (3) number of hours of severe- and very-severe-intensity headaches; (4) number of headache-free hours; and (5) number of days that are completely or almost completely headache-free. Scales used for rating intensity have varied.

Committees charged by the IHS (International Headache Society Clinical Trials Subcommittee, 2009, 2012) have developed various guidelines for conducting and evaluating pharmacological agents, and these served as models until the American Headache Society assembled a panel to develop guidelines that were similar but specific for behavioral treatments (Penzien et al., 2005). This committee recommended that attack frequency (number of headaches per month) and headache days per month serve as the primary measures for outcome assessment, with the headache index, headache duration, peak headache severity, and the number of severe headache days per month (for chronic forms of headache) serving as secondary measures of headache outcome. The committee suggested further monitoring for a number of secondary measures, such as quality of life, days of work or school missed, medication consumption, change in psychological symptoms, and patient preference ratings.

In can be difficult to engage patients in collecting key data. Reviewing pain records regularly, socially praising efforts to comply (yet refraining from punishing noncompliance), anticipating problem areas, and having patients mail records to the office when gaps between appointments are large can help to emphasize the importance of and facilitate accurate record keeping (Lake, 2001; see the section on adherence in this chapter, as well as Rains & Schwartz, Chapter 15, this volume).

However, compliance issues demand flexibility and options for some patients. Practitioners should consider having patients make ratings at regular preset times. For example, ratings can be made soon after morning awakening time, at noon, at the end of the workday, and again in the late evening. Another option is every 3–4 hours (e.g., 7:00 A.M., 11:00 A.M., 3:00 P.M., 7:00 P.M., and 11:00 P.M. These two options are useful for patients with pain during nearly all waking hours. However, both options have limitations. For example, when using them, one does not see patterns. One cannot obtain a complete measure of the hours with headache, the exact durations, and the frequency of discrete headaches (as recommended by the behavioral guidelines committee).

It is highly preferable to have patients record their medication usage on a daily basis. This helps document medication changes and assesses whether symptom improvement is the result of medication or of nonpharmacological therapies. In the past, some practitioners chose to rate the potency of each medication and derive a composite medication index, but this is no longer done (due to the increased variety of medications and the difficulty in equating potencies).

A number of alternative and supplementary headache measurement approaches have been used in research and practice (Andrasik, 2001; Andrasik, Buse, & Lettich, 2011; Buse et al., 2015). Examples include separately measuring multiple features of pain (e.g., as with the McGill Pain Questionnaire; Katz & Melzack, 2011) and methods for separating sensory and affective features of pain (Andrasik, Blanchard, Ahles, Pallmeyer, & Barron, 1981; Jensen & Karoly, 2011). Some patients report improvements in their affective reactions to their pain even without pain reduction. Consider a checklist of behaviors such as avoidance, activities, complaints, and help-seeking behaviors (Philips & Jahanshahi, 1986). Finally, increasingly, attention is being focused on the importance of assessing impact on other important aspects of functioning, such as general health or overall quality of life, physical functioning, emotional functioning, cognitive functioning, role functioning, and social well-being (see Andrasik, 2001; Buse et al., 2015).

# Obtaining a Headache History

Obtaining a headache history is well described in many references (Blanchard & Andrasik, 1985; Blau, 1990; Borkum, 2007; Dalessio, 1986; Kunkel, 1987; Olesen & Dodick, 2006; Swanson, 1987). All practitioners with the responsibility for assessment and treatment are wise to avoid relying entirely on history information from other health professionals. This is true even when the prior history has been taken by competent physicians. It is sometimes necessary to forgo this added history taking because of time and cost factors. A practitioner who needs to know specific information should obtain it directly from the patient. The prior written reports can be reviewed aloud with the patient for confirmation. Patients sometimes give different professionals different answers to the same types of questions. Some practitioners misunderstand patients’ statements; other practitioners obtain only the information needed to make a diagnosis, to rule out serious pathology, and to prescribe medication. Patient reports of information such as onset, location, frequency, and duration of headaches are sometimes different when another practitioner asks the questions.

tion. These items include dietary factors, gum chewing, sleep problems, work postures, driving habits, and bed pillows.

# Headache Interview and History Questions

What follows summarizes topics and questions for a headache history. Each item often requires more than one question. Many of the items and questions are mainly for diagnosis; however, most also have implications for assessment and therapy by providers of applied psychophysiological methods. The focus here is less on diagnosis and more on the other uses and implications. Diagnosis is not less important, but other published sources cover the diagnostic implications. We have based the items and questions on multiple sources, including Blau (1990), Swanson (1987), and Dalessio (1986). We recommend to readers Blau’s (1990) erudite, insightful, refined, and skilled commentary on history taking.

# Discrepancies from Prior History

•	 Lower-grade headaches versus bad headaches. One sometimes reads a history of a specified number of headaches per month, such as six headaches each for 1–2 days. Then one finds out that there are nearly daily lower-grade headaches, but the patient told the prior health care provider only about the “bad” headaches. “I have about five to six headaches a month” can mean that the patient does not want to complain about the others of lesser intensity. A practitioner needs to ask specifically whether a patient believes he or she has more than one type of headache—and, if so, to inquire about each separately.

•	 Long history of less frequent and less intense headaches versus recent onset of headaches. Patients sometimes report onset as when their headaches became more intense or more frequent. The recorded history might reveal 1 or 2 years, but the onset could have been many years earlier.

•	 Less frequent headaches but other sites of pain. Some patients report the location of their headaches as the areas in which the pain is the worst or most frequent. Practitioners should ask about other areas that may be important for diagnosis and therapy.

•	 Items overlooked in interviews. Even experienced and competent professionals sometimes miss or overlook potentially important informa

# Number of  He ad ache  Types

Ascertain the number of headaches of different types occurring both recently and in past years.

# Onset

This is Blau’s (1990) first “time” question. How long the patient has been having headaches and age of the patient are important mostly for diagnosis. Here are sample questions: “How long have you had this type of headache? How old were you when this type of headache started? When did the headaches begin? How did they begin? Did you have headaches in grade school, in high school, or in college?”

One is more concerned with a headache beginning in an older adult patient, especially if it starts suddenly and is severe (i.e., is there temporal arteritis or an expanding intracranial lesion, such as from a hemorrhage or brain tumor?). A headache that changes very little over many years is most likely to be benign.

For a practitioner planning biofeedback for headaches, knowledge of onset information is helpful in understanding the patient’s experience with headaches and his or her expectations. A very long history of headaches suggests many treatments, many disappointments, and a lifestyle focused on headaches. These factors must be considered in making a treatment plan as well.

# Frequency , Regul arity , and  Periodicity

If the headaches are episodic, what is their frequency and regularity? This information is necessary for diagnosis and baseline information. It is Blau’s (1990) second “time” question. Questions such as the following should be asked: “When do you get headaches?”; “How often?”; “How many days a week or month do you have no headache at all?”; “Do your headaches increase at certain times of the month or year?”; “Have you ever thought that your headaches increased before or during certain times of the year or events?” Even research subjects in headache-free control groups underestimate or underreport the frequency of their headaches (Wittrock, Ficek, & Cook, 1996).

In searching for potential triggers, one is looking for emotional/stress, dietary, physical, environmental/meteorological, and hormonal factors. These have all been subjectively reported by patients as likely to precipitate headaches, regardless of whether the diagnosis is TTH, migraine, or combination headaches (Borkum, 2007; Scharff, Turk, & Marcus, 1995). Stressful times include holidays, birthdays, or the anniversaries of deaths, divorces, and marriages. Physical factors include sleep changes, exertion, and skipping meals. Environmental factors include glare or flickering light, strong odors, loud noises, and weather changes. Most of the literature is based on subjective self-report. However, as reviewed by (Martin, 2001, 2010a, 2010b), there is support for experimental validation for triggers classified as negative affect (e.g., stress, anxiety, anger, and depression; Martin & Seneviratne, 1997) and visual disturbance (flicker, glare, and eyestrain; Martin & Teoh, 1999). There is much support for the role of daily hassles, negative mood, and/or sleep quality factors preceding headaches, within the prior 2–3 days (Sorbi, Maassen, & Spierings, 1996; Spierings, Sorbi, Haimowitz, & Tellegen, 1996; Reynolds & Hovanitz, 2000; Marlowe, 1998). Readers are referred to Andrasik and Grazzi (2014) for further discussion of factors related to headache and alternative approaches for managing suspected headache triggers (e.g., Martin, 2010a, 2010b; Martin & MacLeod, 2009).

# Timing of  He ad aches

“Timing” refers to whether the headache starts while the patient is awake or during sleep and at the same or different times. The practitioner can ask, “When or at what time of day do they occur? Do they always or usually occur then?” In addition to diagnostic purposes, this information is useful for understanding precipitating events and timing of relaxation. For example, some TTHs typically start or worsen on the way to work, at about the same time at work, or near the end of the work day; this information has implications for when to apply relaxation skills.

# Characteristics of the Pain

•	 Location. It is helpful to ask, “Where does the pain begin? What is the location at onset of pain, and how does it evolve?” For example, it can be useful to know whether the headache starts in the posterior neck, the temples, or the occipital area. “Does the pain move around?” (Blau’s [1990] “site” questions 4 and 5). “Is the pain deep, as in a stomachache? Is it near the surface, like something digging into your skin?” (Blau’s “site” question 6).

•	 Description of pain and intensity (Blau’s question 10 for “quality” and 11 for “quantity”). Ask about “quality.” What is the pain like? Is it aching, burning, throbbing, or stabbing? Practitioners often offer choices from which patients can select; this is usually done orally. Also, questionnaires (e.g., the McGill Pain Questionnaire) may be used.

These questions are more useful for diagnosis than for treatment. For example, a short and stabbing pain occurring up to several times per minute and sometimes occurring in waves suggests trigeminal neuralgia. Band-like sensations or general feelings of tightness, like a tight cap, suggest a TTH. A severity-type question asks about quantity of pain—the intensity. “How bad is the pain?” One can verbally or visually present a rating scale. A related question is “How do the headaches affect your life?” This often unveils signs and discussion of depression and anxiety that may require separate evaluation.

•	 Duration of pain. “Duration” here refers to how long it takes for the headache to reach the maximum intensity (Blau’s “time” question 3). The following are sample questions: “How long do most of your headaches last?”; “Do they last for minutes, an hour or so, a few hours, a half day, a whole day, or more than a day?”; “If the lengths vary, what are the shortest, longest, and usual lengths?”

The value of this information is mostly for diagnosis. However, it is also useful for understanding the impact of the headaches on the person, for treatment, and for assessing progress. For example, are the headaches disabling enough to justify treatment beyond the earliest steps of stepped care? Are the headaches briefer as treatment progresses?

# Psychological Evaluation

Whether to evaluate psychological factors, where to begin, and how soon to introduce questions all depend on the patient, circumstances, and the practitioner’s judgment. In many professional settings, such as medical clinics, even mental health practitioners are wise to begin with a headache history or a review of the available recorded history. However, exceptions abound. For example, there are psychologically minded patients who can describe psychological factors clearly from the beginning. All or most of the headache history information is already available in the recorded history for many patients.

Not all or even most patients need a psychological evaluation. There are often practical constraints that result from a patient’s schedule and distance from home. Many patients also show a limited or total lack of psychological mindedness, or display resistance to such inquiries and evaluation. However, a brief psychological evaluation is often better than none (and later we mention some brief screening measures that increasingly are being used with medical patients, including those with headaches). Asking even a few psychosocial questions can help with rapport and reveal a patient’s receptiveness or resistance to this type of question and treatment. The practitioner can then determine whether a more detailed evaluation is needed, or whether it can be deferred or eliminated. At the very least, the practitioner should infer the patient’s mental status from the interview, unless there is an obvious need for a more direct examination.

At a minimum, the practitioner should ask about the pressures and frustrations in the patient’s life. In some way, he or she should convey to the patient that it is perfectly acceptable to talk about such matters—and, indeed, that this might be important for evaluation and treatment. This patient can be told that even if these matters are not evaluated fully now, they might become more important later. Patient education booklets can help convey this message.

We have based the following list of psychological factors on one by Adler and Adler (1987, pp. 70–83). They suggest evaluation of many factors, including the following:

•	 Patients’ expectations of themselves.   
•	 Perceived expectations by others.   
•	 Existence of past or present family conflicts. Sensitivity to criticism and to emotional expressions.   
•	 Comfort with and skills at assertiveness. Illnesses and hospitalizations.   
Past or present grief or anticipated grief. Medication misuse. Experience, perception, and misperception of health care professionals.   
Perceived emotional triggers or factors that increase risk of a headache.

# Personality and Psychopathology as Cause or Effect of Headaches

We focus in this section on evaluation of personality and psychopathology, including depression, as a cause or an effect of headaches. The implications of this topic for assessment and treatment include the following:

•	 Should the practitioner assess psychopathology?   
•	 Should the practitioner treat the psychopathology?   
• What can one expect during and after nondrug treatment?   
•	 What could account for changes or lack of changes in headaches?

According to clinical lore, psychopathology predisposes people to, contributes to, or causes tension and vascular headaches. A different view is that personality changes, depression, anxiety, and other psychopathology result from living with headaches. Both views have proponents, and both have support. Practitioners and researchers know that the two views represent extremes, and both have practical and heuristic value. Both are probably true for some persons.

A third view is probably also true for many people: Their anxiety, depression, and personality features probably enhance the chance of developing frequent headaches; in turn, the headaches have effects on their moods, lifestyle, personality, behaviors, and reactions to stress (recall our earlier discussion of the partially shared diathesis hypothesis).

Measures of anxiety and depression may show slight positive changes during and after relaxation and biofeedback treatments for headaches, even without changes in headaches. Practitioners and investigators speculate on the explanations. Analyses of the measures need to look closely at the aspects of depression and anxiety that change. For example, measures such as the BDI and State– Trait Anxiety Inventory (STAI) deal with various elements of each emotional construct. Reduction of a total score does not tell us what specific aspects changed. One also must note the changes of specific items and sets of items. For example, the BDI pretreatment scores of 6–11 reported in Blanchard, Steffek, Jaccard, and Nicholson (1991c) are not higher than scores of nondepressed or mildly depressed patients. Among nondepressed medical patients, such scores and even slightly higher scores do not reliably mean that a depression diagnosis is warranted. One can respond to these items and receive slightly to mildly elevated scores for a variety of reasons. Practitioners who use the BDI know this and inspect individual items. However, we agree that some patients who do not show clinically significant improvements in their headaches do report improvements in mood.

A notable example of mood and anxiety improvement without changes in the target physical symptom is found among patients with tinnitus (see Flor & Schwartz, Chapter 25, this volume). These patients often report improved mood, reduced anxiety, and better adjustment despite no objective improvements in the tinnitus. “The ringing in my ears is the same, but I am sleeping much better and feel much better,” some patients say. Such a report could be a response to implied demands of the clinical interview. It could be a way for a patient to reduce cognitive dissonance and justify his or her investment in treatment. It could also reflect nonsynchronous changes in varied dimensions—the affective or reactive dimension versus the intensity or sensory dimension (Andrasik et al., 1981). Patients who report sleeping better and experiencing less anxiety and depression may be referring to the affective/reactive dimension.

One reasonable speculation about implicated reduction of depression and anxiety without changes in physical symptoms comes from Blanchard et al. (1991c), who propose that depression reduction occurs as patients achieve a greater sense of being in control, and that anxiety reduction occurs because of reduced unpredictability (vs. controllability), both of which lead to perceptions of positive change. Both speculations are credible and deserve more research attention.

Practitioners know that depression and other psychological factors can play a role in headache development and maintenance. Antidepressant medications are a major part of treatment programs for headaches. However, the antidepressant effects of the medications are not what account for the improvements. Often the doses used for headaches are much less than those required for effective management of depression. Changes in sleep and in brain biochemistry are two other explanations. Precise mechanisms of change remain uncertain, however. Nonetheless, practitioners should not underestimate the potential value of pharmacotherapy and nonbehavioral psychotherapy in the treatment of many persons with headaches. Assessment of psychosocial behaviors and emotions becomes increasingly important as one accepts the potential role of these factors in causing and maintaining headaches and in interfering with successful treatments.

# Psychological Measures

In early investigations, the Minnesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory (MMPI) served as the main measure of interest (and it was much discussed in prior editions of this text). Over time, briefer and more focused measures have increasingly been utilized, with the purpose of screening for those in need of more extensive and specialized evaluation. Because of its more limited role and the need for special expertise, you will find the discussion of the MMPI at www.marksschwartzphd.com.

# Brief Screening  Instruments

Buse et al. (2015) have reviewed a host of measures that readers might find useful for assessing and monitoring psychological functioning and comorbid conditions. Here we mention three specific validated instruments that have been of value to practitioners during the intake process of screening and monitoring of various psychiatric disorders. The Primary Care Evaluation of Mental Disorders (PRIME-MD) Patient Health Questionnaire (PHQ), a brief, self-administered questionnaire, screened for several Axis I psychological disorders based on DSM-IV criteria (Spitzer, Kroenke, Williams, & the Patient Health Questionnaire Primary Care Study Group, 1999). The PHQ-9, the Depression subscale of the PRIME-MD, can be used independently to screen for depression (Kroenke, Spitzer, & Williams, 2001). Clinically significant anxiety can be evaluated using the Generalized Anxiety Disorder–7 (GAD-7), a seven-item, selfadministered questionnaire (Spitzer, Kroenke, Williams, & Lowe, 2006). Some personality disorders have been linked to poorer outcomes, and these need consideration as well. Whether these measures will map 1:1 onto the various disorders as revised in DSM-5 (American Psychiatric Association, 2013) is unknown at present, but we anticipate no major discrepancies will occur.

# Treatment Of Special  Popul ations

The populations discussed in this section include older adult patients, pregnant women, women with menstrual-related headaches, and children and adolescents. Obviously, each of these categories could involve a separate section or an entire chapter (as was formerly the case for pediatric headaches). The purpose here is to call the reader’s attention to special considerations for each group, summarize selected conclusions, and provide selected references.

# Older Adult Patients with Benign Headaches

The percentage of our population that is older is steadily growing, and this is expected to double in the next 25 years (e.g., see www.marksschwartzphd. com for information on the U.S. Department of Health and Human Services Administration on Aging). Although TTHs and migraine headaches become less frequent with age, they still remain common among older adult individuals (Stovner & Scher, 2006). In fact, some circumstances associated with aging, such as the increased presence of chronic comorbid medical conditions and use of medications (excessive analgesics), can aggravate an existing headache, complicate headache management, or precipitate a new headache (Tonini & Bussone, 2010). For example, Prencipe et al. (2001) performed intensive door-to-door surveys of older adults (age 65 and older) in several small villages in Italy. One-year prevalence rates revealed the presence of headaches in nearly 60% of individuals (with TTH being the most common by far—nearly 45%). Prevalence rates were higher for women (approximately 62%) than for men (approximately 37%).

Data collected in medical settings reveal that headache remains a frequent reason that older adults seek medical care. For example, during a routine office visit in a well-studied older adult population in Dunedin, Florida (Hale, May, Marks, Moore, & Stewart, 1987), 9.1% (117/1284) reported frequent headaches. Female patients reported headaches more than twice as often as male patients (1.0–1.2% of 819 vs. 5.4% of 465 patients). The average age for those reporting headaches was 78, but we do not know the specific diagnoses for each group. In another medical center outpatient sample (Solomon, Kunkel, & Frame, 1990), 4.3% (359/82,893 of those with a diagnosis of headache, excluding temporal arteritis) were age 65 or older. Of these, 31.7% had TTH, 17.8% had migraine, and 15.9% had mixed headaches. Finally, 75 out of 288 older adult Japanese patients with various types and degrees of dementia were reported as having headaches (Takeshima, Taniguchi, Kitagawa, & Takahashi, 1990). Of these, 43 (58.9%) had TTH (12 chronic, 31 episodic), 15 (20.5%) had migraines without aura, and two patients had both types. Fifteen of the 59 patients (25.4%) with dementia of the Alzheimer’s type had headaches, including 11 with TTHs. Among the 160 with cerebrovascular disease (CVD), 135 had vascular dementia, and 34 (21.3%) had TTH without a direct relationship to their old CVD episodes. Twenty of these had TTH, and seven had migraines without aura. Others had combinations or uncertain diagnoses. We mention these few studies to give the reader a sense of the prevalence of headache among older adults, both those within the community at large and those seeking medical care, and to point out the increasing need for further developing nonpharmacological treatments for older adult patients with benign forms of headache.

Age and neurocognitive impairments compromise the quality of life among many of these people. They are unlikely to complain about their headaches for many reasons; these include impaired memory, impaired verbal expressive abilities, depression, and lack of opportunity. We should note that Takeshima et al. (1990) specifically excluded patients who lacked the ability to report complaints of headache. Practitioners can therefore expect more coexisting symptoms, diseases, and use of various medications among their older adult patients, all of which can affect and complicate diagnosis and treatment.

For many of these people, medications are insufficient or contraindicated because of medical conditions. This further supports the potential value of nonpharmacological treatments.

# Biofeedback and Self‑Regulatory Treatment for Headaches in Older Adult Patients

Early studies, primarily retrospective single or anecdotal cases, researching the value of relaxation and biofeedback for treating headaches in older adult patients, were unremarkable. The led to the general belief that these treatments were not successful or appropriate for older patients, especially those with very chronic symptoms. Fortunately, some determined investigators initiated prospective studies of older patients and found that when certain adjustments were made, these treatments were of similar value for older patients (Arena, Hightower, & Chang, 1988; Kabela, Blanchard, Appelbaum, & Nicholson, 1989; Arena, Hannah, Bruno, & Meador, 1991). In these investigations, patients ranged from 60 to 77 years of age.

One can only commend Arena, his colleagues, and others for their sensitivity and efforts to tailor the instructions and treatment for older subjects. The therapists simplified instructions, spoke more slowly, and summarized information as often as needed. They made extra efforts to be patient and to spend more time listening. The outcome of these series, totaling 34 patients, showed 21 or 62% (21/34) with improvement of 50% or more. Others showed improvements but to lesser degrees. The number of treatment sessions varied from three to 19 sessions; most patients received between 8 and 12 sessions. This included patients treated only with limited portions of treatment, such as only frontal EMG, only relaxation instructions, or only three office visits. The authors encouraged daily practice. These series are encouraging, and the results have opened the door to improvements. Tailoring treatment to the individual and using good professional judgment are still advisable practices for treating older adult patients. The authors correctly pointed out the limits of their studies. For example, we do not yet know the efficacy of these treatments for older adult patients of nonEuropean descent, or how these treatments may be impacted by the presence of major psychological problems and concomitant medical and neurological problems. Nevertheless, the results are encouraging and help to justify these treatments for this population, bearing in mind the following:

1. Practitioners should consider treating older adult patients, including some patients with dementia, with relaxation, biofeedback, and related procedures.   
2. Patients should be interviewed carefully about their headaches, and caretakers should be encouraged to do the same.   
3. Medical, neurological, and/or psychiatric evaluations should be performed. Symptoms that could affect accurate diagnosis and/or affect relaxation and biofeedback treatments should be monitored.   
4. All medications that might affect treatment should be noted and monitored.   
5. Practitioners should develop special patient education material and devote special care to the cognitive preparation of older adult patients, to reassure them and to increase their understanding and adherence.   
6. Depression and sleep disorders should be assessed and treated as needed, at least before and/or during applied psychophysiological treatments. Treating depression and/ or sleep problems may help decrease headaches in some patients.   
7. Older adult patients’ spouses, other family members, or caretakers should be included in evaluation and treatment as necessary.   
8. Treatment may require a greater number of office sessions (10–19) with some older adult patients. However, fewer office sessions may be successful with some patients.

# Pregnant Women

Medications are often ill-advised or contraindicated for treating headaches during pregnancy (Airola et al., 2010). Especially for migraines, at least 50% of pregnant women report relief from headaches (particularly after the first trimester), but some report increased headache activity. Some women without a history of headaches report onset of migraines during pregnancy. Scharff, Marcus, and Turk (1997) followed the headache activity of 30 women through their pregnancies and up to 12 weeks postpartum. There was a tendency toward an increase in headaches in the third trimester among multiparous women. There were fewer headaches throughout pregnancy and the postpartum period for primiparous women.

An early, uncontrolled report (Hickling, Silverman, & Loos, 1990) used a combination of applied psychophysiological treatments for vascular headaches during pregnancy. The authors reported elimination of headaches or significant improvement for all five women who were treated with a combination of muscle and autogenic-type relaxation, EMG and thermal biofeedback, and cognitive psychotherapy. Treatment ranged from four to 12 sessions. Improvement usually started in the second trimester and was maintained at follow-up of 4 to 17 months.

The most extensive investigation of nonpharmacological treatments for headaches to date was conducted by Marcus, Scharff, and Turk (1995). They delivered an eight-session treatment: four sessions devoted to progressive relaxation combined with skin-warming biofeedback, with the remaining sessions focused on physical therapies involving instructions for neck stretching and strengthening, and use of heat and ice. In two separate studies, the second being a controlled study that included an attention control group, the combined treatment was found to be very effective, with significant improvement maintained over 6 months. The immediate postpartum period is often associated with increased headaches, but not in these treated subjects. A 1-year follow-up (Scharff, Marcus, & Turk, 1996) showed good maintenance of improvement, with two-thirds maintaining significant improvement; results were also independent of breastfeeding (i.e., independent of changing hormones). Thus, the approaches seem both feasible and useful.

# Menstrual Migraine

Migraine is linked to the menstrual cycle for many women (MacGregor, 2012; Vetvik, MacGregor, Lundqvist, & Russell, 2014), with 35–50% of female migraineurs experiencing menstruallyrelated migraine. The first classification by the IHS did not include menstrual migraine as a diagnostic entity, leaving researchers to develop their own criteria (which made across-study outcome comparisons difficult, if not impossible). The second classification system now distinguishes two types: pure menstrual migraine (PMM) without aura and menstrually related migraine (MRM) without aura. The distinguishing feature is the time window of headache occurrence. If headaches occur at times other than 2 days before and within 3 days of cessation of menstruation, the latter term applies.

Findings from early biofeedback investigations suggested that menstrual migraines do not respond well to treatment. Subsequent studies led researchers to question this conclusion (Holroyd, 2002), but little work has been done to date, leaving this an open question in need of more careful research. There is some support, although weak, for the use of thermal biofeedback and relaxation procedures for MRM. It is reasonable to introduce this type of treatment if medications are not enough and if the headaches are interfering with a woman’s life. Practitioners should ask patients to keep clear symptom records and clearly define the criteria for MRM versus other migraine headaches. Patients should try to note midcycle ovulation headaches and consider them to be another type of MRM.

Kim and Blanchard (1992) attempted to control for various factors possibly affecting outcome (time and mode of onset, headache presence during nonmenstrual times, etc.), while they applied various combinations of relaxation, temperature biofeedback, and cognitive therapy with extended or limited office-based programs. As a group, these treatments were similarly effective for both menstrual and nonmenstrual migraines; they were significantly more effective than nontreatment. Among the group of 38 women reporting what we would now term PMM, 16 (42%) reported at least 50% improvement, compared with 33/60 (55%) of those with nonmenstrual migraines. Other percentages of improvement were also similar. Those with menstrual migraines showed slightly less improvement in headaches and medication reduction; however, they did improve compared to pretreatment.

Because of possible limitations of the first study, the researchers studied another 15 subjects reporting both menstrual and nonmenstrual migraines. They reported using temperature feedback, but presumably augmented with relaxation home practice. This group did not do as well as the previous cohort. However, four patients reduced their nonmenstrual migraines by at least 50% (average = 74%). Three of these also reduced their menstrual migraines by at least 50% (average = 88%). Another subject improved her menstrual migraines by nearly 81%. Note that four subjects worsened when the 4 weeks after treatment were compared to the pretreatment baseline. In conclusion, these treatments can result in significant improvements for many women with MRM. One can certainly justify this treatment approach. Practitioners should adopt the newly developed definitions of MRM and should verify these with headache logs.

# Headaches in Children and Adolescents

# Prevalence and Prognosis

Headaches are common at all ages and, in fact, are one of the leading causes of pain in children and adolescents (Ozge et al., 2011; Sillanpää & Anttila, 1996). Even at the age of 3, headaches are present in 3–8% of children, and this increases to about 20% at age 5, 37–52% at age 7, and 57–82% from ages 7 to 15 (Lipton, Maytal, & Winner, 2001). A U.S. study included 3158 children, ages

12–17, whose families were contacted directly by telephone (Linet, Stewart, Celentano, Ziegler, & Sprecher, 1989). Among the many findings were that 56% of the males and 74% of the females reported a headache in the past 4 weeks; 27% of the males and 41.4% of the females reported two or more headaches; and 4.5% of the males and 9.4% of the females reported four or more headaches in the past month. The average intensity was moderate on a 1- to 10-point scale (4.5 for males, 4.7 for females), and the mean duration was 5–6 hours. The pain and suffering children experience can impact every aspect of their daily lives, impairing their psychosocial adaption both in and out of school, and leading to increased absences, inefficiencies in completing homework, afterschool exhaustion, reductions in leisure-time activities, peer and family relationship difficulties, and so forth (Bandell-Hoekstra, Abu-Saadm, Passchier, & Knipschild, 2000; Battistutta, Aliverti, Montico, Zin, & Carrozzi, 2009; Bellini et al., 2013; Fuh et al., 2009; Grazzi, D’Amico, Usai, Solari, & Bussone, 2004; Guidetti & Galli, 1998; Hershey et al., 2004; Kröner-Herwig, Morris, & Heinrich, 2007; Karwautz et al., 2008; Laurell, Larsson, & Eeg-Olofsson, 2005; Smith, Martin-Herz, & Womack, 1999).

Unfortunately, epidemiological data suggest that the incidence of pediatric headache has actually increased significantly over the past three decades. This precipitous rise has been attributed to untoward lifestyle changes and increased levels of overall stress (see Ozge et al., 2011, for a summary). Data collected via electronic diaries showed that changes in the intensity level of daily stressors, as well as decreased sleep quantity, reliably predicted headache occurrence (Connelly & Bickel, 2011).

Many continue to believe that pediatric headache does not need to be taken seriously, because it will be outgrown with time; indeed some children do outgrow it. Regrettably, this does not hold true for many children, as illustrated long ago by the longitudinal work of the Swedish pediatrician Bo Bille and others. Nearly six decades ago (the mid 1950s), Bille began a landmark study of about 9000 Swedish schoolchildren ranging in age from 7 to 15. His first publication (Bille, 1962) told us much about headache occurrence by gender and age. For 40 years Bille was able to follow a subset of these children, all of whom were diagnosed as having migraine at a very young age. The majority continued to be troubled by headaches at this final follow-up assessment (Bille, 1997), and this was more so for females.

TA BLE 20.3. Eight-Year Follow-Up of Late-Adolescent Headache Sufferers   
![](images/8149cc17a8535988a863bcd282624b1480d53679abd36e55328da241659dbdfa.jpg)
Note. Data from Guidetti et al. (1998).

Type of headache does not reliably predict headache continuation, but increased headache frequency and severity and the presence of gastrointestinal (GI) distress (vomiting in particular) appear to do so (Bille, 1997; Brna, Dooley, Gordon, & Dewan, 2005; Laurell, Larsson, Mattsson, & Eeg-Olofsson, 2006). Another predictor appears to the presence of comorbid conditions. Guidetti et al. (1998) evaluated a large number of adolescents diagnosed at time 1 with migraine or TTH, examined them for the presence of various coexisting conditions, and assessed for the continued presence of headache 8 years later. Patients were classified as “same or worse,” “improved,” or “headache free.” As shown in Table 20.3, adolescent headache sufferers with multiple comorbid conditions fared most poorly over the intervening period. These findings conform with those emerging from recent studies of adult headache sufferers in which an association has been reported between headache frequency and experiencing a number of adverse events during one’s childhood years (e.g., emotional, physical, or sexual abuse; witnessing domestic violence; substance abuse; Anda, Tietjen, Schulman, Felitti, & Croft, 2010; Tietjen & Peterlin, 2011).

The enduring nature of at least a portion of childhood headaches reinforces the importance of early intervention for ameliorating current symptoms and preventing adult symptoms.

# Diagnosis and Assessment

Diagnosis and medical evaluation for pediatric patients with headache proceeds much as it does for adult patients (Lay & Broner, 2008; Winner & Guidetti, 2006), with few differences. One difference is how and when to involve parents in the process. We concur with Lay and Broner (2008) about the importance of interviewing adolescents alone; furthermore, we believe that it is important to interview the adolescent prior to interviewing the parents to demonstrate the importance of his or her input and to help establish a good working therapeutic relationship (Rach & Andrasik, 2013). We next interview the parents, then bring everyone back together to share feedback and discuss discrepant reports. During all contacts, it is important to remember that children and adolescents with headache may have qualitatively different medical, psychological, family, and educational presentations and needs (Andrasik & Schwartz, 2006; Hershey & Powers, 2013; Powers & Andrasik, 2005; Rach & Andrasik, 2013), and that their level of cognitive development greatly influences their ability to understand their illness (Marcon & Labbé, 1990; Seshia, 2013). Consequently, children and adolescents with headache should be treated by health care providers with training and experience with this specific population. The basic tenets of the biopsychosocial model of assessment should be applied, including a focus on educational, social, and developmental factors. McGrath and Koster (2001) provide a number of helpful suggestions for conducting the initial interview, assessing and quantifying headaches, and tracking change over time.

Measurement of key headache parameters and headache impact are important as well, including daily pain diaries and measures prepared and normed for children and adolescents. Pain rating scales found to be useful with adults are appropriate for pediatric patients as well and, if possible, therapists are advised to obtain ratings from both patients and parents when possible. It is important to bear in mind that discrepancies have been found between child and parent interview reports and pain ratings collected on a daily, prospective basis (Andrasik, Burke, Attanasio, & Rosenblum, 1985).

Buse et al. (2015) discuss several measures suitable for assessing impact with child and adolescent patients. The six-item pediatric Migraine Disability Assessment Questionnaire (MIDAS), the PedMIDAS, modeled after its adult counterpart, was developed to assess functional impairment and burden caused by migraine (Hershey et al., 2001, 2004). This modified version focuses on disruptions in education, at home, and during sport and social activities rather than in the work setting. Grazzi et al. (2004) described another variation of the adult MIDAS—the MIDAS-Junior. The self-administered Quality of Life Headache in Youth (QLH-Y) questionnaire, developed by Langeveld, Koot, Loonen, Hazebroek-Kampschreur, and Passchier (1996), is designed to assess psychological, physical, and social functioning, and functional status in patients ages 12–18. A final brief, migraine-specific outcome measure, is designed to help determine quality of life and functioning in adolescent patients (Hartmaier, DeMuro-Mercon, Linder, Winner, & Santanello, 2001). Researchers and practitioners may also find the Pediatric Quality of Life 4.0 (PedsQL 4.0) measure of value, as it is prepared in a disease-independent manner (Connelly & Rapoff, 2006; Varni, Burwinkle, Seid, & Skarr, 2003).

# Treatment: A Stepped‑Care Approach

As for adult headaches, we advocate a stepped or stratified approach to the treatment of pediatric headaches, possibly beginning with identification and modification of obvious triggers and contributing factors (Andrasik, Blake, & McCarran, 1986; Andrasik & Schwartz, 2006; McGrath & Hillier, 2001). Dietary factors are commonly addressed at an early stage (Rossi, Bardare, & Brunelli, 2002; see also Block, Gyllenhaal, & Schwartz, Chapter 10, this volume). Silberstein (1990) suggests the following:

•	 Reassure the family that the condition is benign.   
•	 Recommend adjustments in the child’s lifestyle, including going to bed at a regular time, keeping to a reasonable meal schedule, and avoiding activity overload.   
•	 Help the family identify and eliminate typical headache triggers (exertion, noise, hunger, traveling, glare, suspect foods, etc.).   
• Consider psychotherapy when depression or abuse is suspected, along with any other significant disturbances at home.

Prudent practitioners also consider medications, depending in part on the frequency of the headaches, their severity and duration, and the effectiveness of simple analgesics. Prophylactic medications are useful for some children with chronic headaches, especially when the headaches are frequent, severe, and reveal complicated neurological symptoms (Silberstein, 1990). Note that the use of medications does not preclude the use of biofeedback and relaxation.

As with adults, various nonpharmacological therapies have been investigated for treating recurrent headaches in children; chief among these are relaxation, biofeedback, and cognitivebehavioral therapies. Larsson and Andrasik (2002) found over 10 investigations of varied forms of relaxation, applied in varied settings (clinics and schools) and by various personnel (therapists, teachers, nurses, etc.). Generally positive effects have been obtained for all procedures, pointing to the robustness of this approach. These treatments have typically involved the following components:

•	 Discrimination training that focuses on identification of tense and relaxed larger muscle groups.   
•	 Differential relaxation.   
•	 Cued relaxation.   
•	 Mini-relaxation, focusing on a limited number of muscles in the head, neck, or shoulder.   
•	 Application of techniques in everyday life.

Among biofeedback modalities, thermal (autogenic) and surface electromyographic (SEMG) biofeedback have been studied the most extensively for pediatric headache. Efficacy reviews (Duckro & Cantwell-Simmons, 1989; Holden, Deichmann, & Levy, 1999; Kröner-Herwig, 2011; McGrath, Stewart, & Koster, 2001) and meta-analyses (Cvengros, Harper, & Shevell, 2007; Eccleston, Yorke, Morley, Williams, & Mastroyannopoulou, 2003; Hermann & Blanchard, 2002; Hermann, Kim, & Blanchard, 1995; Nestoriuc et al., 2008a; Palmero, Eccleston, Lewandowski, Williams, & Morley, 2010) confirm their clinical utility and comparative efficacy with regard to certain medications, as well as provide evidence of improvements in overall well-being (increased ability to cope and reduced feelings of helplessness). A recently completed large-scale, randomized controlled trial found that the addition of a comprehensive course of cognitive-behavioral therapy (eight weekly sessions that included a biofeedback component, plus subsequent booster sessions) increased the effectiveness of a standard course of amitriptyline more than did the addition of headache education. This study is of particular note because it included migraineurs whose headaches were chronic (occurring 15 or more days per month) and widely regarded as quite difficult to treat (Powers et al., 2013).

Promising findings have been reported for a novel electroencephalographic biofeedback approach, one that targets the contingent negative variation response (Siniatchkin et al., 2000), and a family-based approach (Gerber et al., 2010), but these await replication. Several investigations have revealed reasonable maintenance effects over time (Grazzi et al., 2001; see studies reviewed in Andrasik, Larsson, & Grazzi, 2002; Larsson & Andrasik, 2002), although at least one has been less positive (Kuhn & Allen, 1993). Unfortunately, minimal attention has been directed at identifying predictors of initial and enduring responses to treatment. A recent retrospective analysis of 132 children who attended a minimum of two biofeedback sessions at an outpatient clinic found that ability to raise hand temperature by more than 3°F at the last visit (as well as use of selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors [SSRIs]) was the only reliable predictor of outcome, both for episodic and chronic forms of pediatric headache (Blume, Brockman, & Breuner, 2012). Anxiety, depression, and somatization were not related to outcome.

A final meta-analysis has shown that these biofeedback treatments lead to more positive clinical outcomes (but not greater levels of physiological control) when used with children than when applied with adults (Sarafino & Goehring, 2000; see Figure 20.5). These findings support the notion that children may be especially good candidates for biofeedback (Attanasio et al., 1985).

Cognitive therapy or cognitive stress coping training has been much less investigated, but it too has promise. With one exception (Richter et al., 1986), cognitive approaches have been combined with other major treatment modalities, and the experimental designs employed have not made it possible to partial out the source of effects.

The PLOT cost containment approach mentioned earlier has been extended to treating pediatric headache patients as well. Preliminary investigations of children with migraines suggest that autogenic feedback may work equally well when delivered in the PLOT format, with either a child or a parent serving as the main treatment agent for the home instruction (Allen & McKeen, 1991; Burke & Andrasik, 1989; Guarnieri & Blanchard, 1990; Hermann, Blanchard, & Flor, 1997; see Haddock et al., 1997, for a quantitative review). This approach increases the need for family involvement and support, leading Guarnieri and Blanchard (1990) to speculate that environmental factors may be particularly important when such limited-contact treatments are employed (e.g., effects may be lessened when the home is somewhat chaotic or nonsupportive). This makes sense for a pediatric population, but it requires further study. Cautious practitioners will check the home environment before deciding upon this approach in clinical practice. Data bearing on this point come from the investigation of Allen and McKeen (1991). Several of the treated children complied with relaxation, but their parents did not follow the guidelines for behavior management that were a part of the treatment package. These children then gradually worsened over the course of treatment and subsequently did not do as well as the others. At a later follow-up, though, the children who initially responded had regressed, and differences were no longer apparent (Kuhn & Allen, 1993). The effectiveness of single-session behavioral treatment has also been examined (Powers et al., 2001). Although statistical significance was obtained, symptom reductions were only 10, 25, and 25% for headache severity, frequency, and duration, respectively, at a modest follow-up (average of 21 weeks). One is left to conclude that more intensive therapeutic effort is needed.

![](images/685d4984d2d09b3bcf270a1071c7982944a9ab56305912b33c30e93e99478652.jpg)  
FI GURE 20.5. Mean percentage improvement in headache activity for children and adults by thermal and EMG biofeedback (BF). Values are subject-weighted means. The number of subjects and the number of studies upon which the values are based are as follows: children’s thermal BF (65/6), children’s EMG BF (19/4), adults’ thermal BF (243/15), and adults’ EMG BF (238/25). Data from Sarafino and Goehring (2000).

Another approach to cost containment concerns group administration. This approach has been used regularly by Larsson and colleagues with relaxation approaches administered in school settings (see Larsson & Andrasik, 2002). Preliminary evidence supports the utility of a brief group behavioral treatment designed to be easily administered by a neurologist and to place minimal demands on both provider and patient; this treatment could therefore actually be applied in various day-to-day medical practice settings. In this investigation (Andrasik et al., 2003), 34 children (ages 9–16 years) with episodic TTH were seen in small groups (three to five individuals of similar ages) once per week for 8 weeks, with sessions limited to a maximum of 30 minutes. Each session followed the same format: practice of progressive muscle relaxation training with eight muscle groups (lower arms, upper arms, legs, abdomen, chest, shoulders, eyes, and forehead), and discussion of ways to apply relaxation to cope with headache and headache-related distress. A tape recording of the first session was provided to guide home practice. Patients were instructed to practice with the tape once per day during treatment and twice per week thereafter. A neurologist, who conferred with a behavioral psychologist, provided treatment. Statistically significant effects were found for several variables (except analgesic tablets), and most of these changes were clinically meaningful as well (≥ 50%). The improvements noted at the end of treatment held throughout the 1 year of follow-up. Although the reduction in analgesic tablet consumption was not statistically significant, it was sizable from a clinical perspective (i.e., it exceeded 50%). Even though this investigation was uncontrolled, the magnitude of effects rivaled those of typical, more effort-intensive behavioral treatments and surpassed those that are typical for placebo effects (Hermann et al., 1995). Further research on this type of approach, with larger samples, appears warranted.

We must also point out that, as for adults, factors mediating treatment effects for children are not clear. Few straightforward relationships have been found between symptom changes and physiological changes during and across treatment sessions. Various psychological processes, such as self-efficacy and perceived control, have been proposed as important mediators (Allen & Shriver, 1997; Hermann & Blanchard, 2002; Kröner-Herwig, 2011; Nestoriuc et al., 2008a).

# Clinical Considerations and “Tips” for Applying Biofeedback and Related Treatments to Pediatric Headache

From clinical experience in working with children and adolescents ranging in age from 6 to 17, Attanasio et al. (1985) identified a number of advantages in working with younger individuals that may account for their enhanced treatment response (see Table 20.4). Certain difficulties are encountered as well, but these potential problems are easily addressed by tailoring language to children’s comprehension levels, taking the time to ensure optimal understanding, decreasing the length of biofeedback trials, adding rest periods, and employing contingency management strategies to sustain performance when motivation lags.

Green (1983) provides a number of very helpful suggestions and verbatim scripts to use in teaching

TA BLE 20.4. A dvantages and Disadvantages of T reating Children with Biofeedback   
![](images/9bb8a618899a92b55df8aa0d215bf3d3489339be70dbf24d54b070e97dcacff4.jpg)

# Disadvantages

• Briefer attention span   
• Off-task behaviors during session   
• Fear and apprehension about equipment   
• Intolerance of minor discomfort in removing sensors   
• Possible complications created by emotional and psychological problems   
• Reduced ability to comprehend treatment rationale and procedures   
• Possible complications of scheduling   
• Lack of standardized electrode placements

self-regulatory skills to very young children. Her recommendations for the practitioner include the following:

•	 Invite the family unit to the initial session, to prevent the child from being singled out as the “problem” or the “sick one.”   
•	 Introduce yourself as a “biofeedback teacher”— someone who teaches ideas and skills, who likes to be asked questions and in turn likes to ask questions.   
• Demonstrate biofeedback with a response that is easily controlled or that produces a quick, discernible response (EMG from the forearm, electrodermal response while playing a guessing game). Incorporate adjunctive techniques, such as belly or diaphragmatic breathing (see Gevirtz, Lehrer, & Schwartz, Chapter 13, this volume), body scanning, the “limp rag doll” technique, and imagery.

Although fairly straightforward translations of biofeedback and related treatments developed for adult patients have met with much success in pediatric populations, it is likely that these effects could be enhanced by adding a developmental perspective to evaluation and treatment. Marcon and Labbé (1990) discussed cognition, self-regulation, psychosocial factors, and other issues that arise at various stages of development. Some of the examples reviewed concern conceptualizations of pain; differences in language, time perception, and approaches to tasks; and varied abilities to comprehend the notion of severity. Marcon and Labbé also stressed the importance of considering environmental influences on headache, specifically, attention from family members and teachers.

Allen and Shriver (1998) provided a concrete illustration of this last point. They randomly assigned children and adolescents (ages 7–18) with migraines to either standard thermal biofeedback or biofeedback combined with “pain behavior management” training for parents (modeled on the principles of operant conditioning developed long ago for pain patients by Fordyce [1976], in which parents were taught how to attend differentially to child behavior). Parents assigned to the latter condition were instructed to minimize reactions to pain behavior displays, discontinue pain status checks, and administer medications only as prescribed; to insist on participation in normal, planned activities to the extent possible even when ill; to praise and support biofeedback practice and efforts to self-regulate; to treat pain episodes as an illness by sending children to their bedrooms and not permitting them to watch television, play, and so forth; and to encourage others to respond in a like manner. Thermal biofeedback led to significant improvement (as expected), but the addition of parent training provided a further significant increment to treatment. The combined treatment group obtained greater overall reductions in headache frequency, had a larger percentage of patients displaying clinically significant improvements (reductions greater than 50%), and revealed better adaptive functioning (i.e., pain led to less interference in daily activities). Benefits from the addition of parent behavior management training have not been uniformly demonstrated, however (KrönerHerwig, Mohn, & Pothmann, 1998).

As noted earlier, a PLOT approach can work for pediatric patients, but this approach increases the need for family involvement and support. The practitioner should check the home environment before deciding on this approach.

# Summary and Conclusions

Relaxation and varied biofeedback treatments for pediatric patients with headache have been repeatedly investigated and shown to be of clinical value. In fact, the magnitude of improvement is greater than that typically seen when these same procedures are applied with adult patients. The limited work with cognitive-based approaches is similarly supportive. A stepped-care approach is appropriate for many of these patients, although the treatment must be tailored to the individual patient. In working with pediatric patients, the pediatric and adolescent migraine Headache Toolbox, developed by the American Headache Society, may be of some educational value for parents, children, and adolescents (Lopez & Kabbouche, 2012).

# Cost Containment C onsiderations

This section focuses on the rationale for and factors involved in the stepped-care model for providing health care. This model starts with effective therapies that are less expensive and typically less complicated.

The major treatments for TTH and migraine headaches often proceed with medication management, dietary changes, relaxation and biofeedback therapies, physical therapies and ergonomics, stress management, and psychotherapy. This order does not imply a preference or standard of practice; it is just one logical order. The order also logically starts with relaxation with limited biofeedback and limited office visits, then proceeds to extensive biofeedback (due to the need for more specialized treatment and equipment). Different medications and different dietary changes may appear at both ends.

The potential cost-effectiveness of biofeedback for treating people with headaches was first described in O’Grady’s (1987) impressive and noteworthy study (yet unpublished), which illustrated how biofeedback can reduce medical utilization and medication usage among patients with chronic headaches. In a large health management organization setting, 63 patients with chronic headaches completed six to 20 sessions of biofeedback treatment. O’Grady compared these patients with 17 others completing five or fewer sessions. At 1-year follow-up, those with more sessions had 75% fewer physician office visits, used 56% less medication, had 19% fewer emergency room visits, and also made 16% fewer phone calls to their physicians. Office visits for headache remained consistently low over 5 years after treatment. In the year before treatment, patients had made on average more than six office visits for headaches. Over the next 5 years, the average was under two visits per year. A retrospective investigation of medical costs alone showed a considerable reduction when the 2 years prior to relaxation and biofeedback treatment were compared with the 2 years after treatment (Blanchard et al., 1985b), thus complementing the findings of O’Grady (1987).

# Prudent Limited Office Treatment

Practical factors, such as limited patient and/or therapist time, cost prohibitions, lessened thirdparty payment, and limited geographical access may preclude intensive individual therapies (Rowan & Andrasik, 1996). This, considered with the finding that biofeedback and related procedures can be cost-effective, led researchers to evaluate whether these treatment could be delivered in more cost-economical formats, yet retain their same level of effectiveness. The first alternative approach operated within the traditional 1:1 focus but markedly decreased the number of office visits. This was made possible by supplementing treatment with instructional manuals and audio/ DVD recordings that patients utilized on their own at home or at work. Thus, the cost savings accrued from a lessened need to travel to the office and miss work and other activities. The amount of time and effort required on the part of the patients was similar, but it could be on their own time according to their preferred schedule.

The “prototypical” reduced therapist contact intervention included an initial office visit (for diagnosing, medically clearing patients, and beginning treatment), a midtreatment office session, and a final session or two near the end of 8 weeks.
