# Temporomandibular Muscle and Joint Disorders

Alan G. Glaros and Leonard L. Lausten

Temporomandibular muscle and joint disorders (TMJDs) are a heterogeneous collection of disorders involving the muscles of mastication and the hard and soft tissues of the temporomandibular joint (TMJ). They are sometimes called TMJ disorders or temporomandibular disorders (TMD). Psychological and emotional factors play key roles in the etiology, maintenance, and treatment of TMJDs. This biopsychosocial approach creates opportunities for clinicians using biofeedback in the treatment team for TMJDs.

Many fine, comprehensive reviews of TMJDs are available (e.g., Suvinen, Reade, Kemppainen, Kononen, & Dworkin, 2005), and excellent patient education is available from the National Institute of Dental and Craniofacial Research (NIDCR; www.nidcr.nih.gov/oralhealth/topics/tmj/ tmjdisorders.htm). This chapter presents data on TMJDs that can help practitioners using biofeedback understand the relationship between psychological, behavioral, or emotional factors and TMJD symptoms. This understanding should then serve as a basis for treatment appropriate to the underlying pathogenesis. (Throughout the chapter, italicizing a term on first use indicates that the term is included in the glossary at the chapter’s end.)

# Anatomy and Physiol ogy of the T MJ and Ass ociated Muscul ature

The TMJ is a dynamic complex that includes the mandibular condyle, the articular disc, the articular fossa of the temporal bone, and the associated membranes, fluids, and ligaments (Figure 21.1). The condyles arise as vertical projections of the U-shaped mandible, and they articulate with the cranium in the concave mandibular fossae of the temporal bones. The central portion of the disc consists of dense fibrous connective tissue; it lacks nerves and blood supply.

Normal function of the TMJ is complex, with movement that involves two separate components. Upon initial opening, the joint works on a balland-socket or hinge principle in which the condyle rotates within the fossa. The disc remains in the fossa between the condyle and the temporal bone. As the jaw continues to open, the condyle translates (“dislocates”) over a bony articular eminence in the upper jaw. The articular disc slides between the eminence and the condyle throughout the opening movement, and may act as a biological “Teflon” that facilitates smooth, pain-free translation of the condyles past the eminences.

![](images/90f00e3128f2263f4cce8ca9f2cd4c4186797969278babe1510213c7d41b2a39.jpg)  
FI GURE 21.1. Anatomy of the temporomandibular joint (TMJ).

Many conditions can adversely affect the joint. Degenerative disorders can lead to erosion and flattening of the condyle or can form undesirable growths called “bone spurs.” Both conditions may result in decreased function, pain, or both. The articular disc itself can function abnormally. Temporary displacement of the disc from its normal position during movement can cause clicking or popping noises during jaw movement. Permanent displacement can produce difficulty in opening or closing. A large portion of the population experiences TMJ noises. Although annoying to the affected individuals, joint noises in the absence of pain, limitation of movement, or changes in opening pattern do not warrant treatment (Okeson, 2008).

The jaw functions are controlled by the muscles of mastication. The most important muscles for jaw function are the masseter, temporalis, and medial and lateral pterygoid muscles. The masseter and temporalis muscles elevate the mandible during mastication, and the temporalis muscles also retract the mandible. The lateral (external) pterygoid muscles protrude and depress the mandible. They also aid lateral jaw movement. The medial (internal) pterygoids close the jaw, produce lateral movements to the opposite side, and aid in protrusion. Both these muscles can be palpated intraorally, although distinguishing them is difficult (Conti et al., 2008).

Both peripheral and central neurological factors may contribute to the pain of TMJD. TMJD pain may result from muscle “overwork,” inflammation in the TMJ, and trigger points in the masticatory muscles. Continued overwork or inflammation can lead to central sensitization of the nervous system, possibly resulting in chronic pain.

# Symp toms and E tiol ogy of T MJDs

The primary symptoms of TMJDs include (1) pain in the muscles of mastication, in the preauricular area (i.e., immediately in front of the ear), or in the TMJ; (2) clicking, popping, or grating sounds in the joint; (3) difficulty opening the mouth wide; (4) the patient’s perception that his or her occlusion (“bite”) is “off”; and (5) jaw locking in the open or closed position. In addition to masticatory muscle pain and other TMJ-related symptoms, a patient with a TMJD may report a wide variety of other conditions, including headache; other facial pains; earache; dizziness; tinnitus; neck, shoulder, and upper and lower back pain; and tooth pain, accelerated wear on the dentition, fractured or mobile teeth, or defective and broken restorations (“fillings”) without other known causes (Okeson, 2008).

The complicated nature of TMJDs requires that practitioners maintain a good working relationship with a dentist competent in the treatment of TMJDs and with other properly trained health professionals. The symptoms of TMJDs can mimic a variety of physical conditions (e.g., headache, dizziness, tinnitus). Thus, proper assessment and effective early intervention for TMJDs may reduce individual and societal costs associated with the care of these patients (Stowell, Gatchel, & Wildenstein, 2007).

Our coverage of the etiologies of TMJDs addresses the roles of parafunctional activity, occlusion, psychological variables, psychophysiological variables, trauma, and abuse.

# Parafunctional Activity

Clenching and grinding are parafunctional behaviors, and both are important causes and aggravating factors for TMJD symptoms. Parafunctional oral activities, such as the chewing of pencils, erasers, gum, ice, cheeks, and lips, may also be related to TMJDs. To examine the role of parafunctional tooth contact, Glaros, Williams, and Lausten (2005) examined three groups of patients with TMJDs (two with pain, one without pain) and a control group without pain or TMJDs, using experience-sampling methodology. As illustrated in Figure 21.2, the two TMJD groups with pain reported more frequent and more intense parafunctional activity than the non-TMJD controls.

Glaros and colleagues have also conducted a series of studies examining the effect of low-level parafunctional clenching on pain (e.g., Glaros & Burton, 2004; Glaros, Tabacchi, & Glass, 1998; see Figure 21.3). In these studies, healthy, normal individuals with no evidence or history of TMJDs participated in biofeedback training to increase and/or decrease the activity of the temporalis and masseter muscles. Subjects reported significantly greater pain when increasing masticatory muscle activity than when decreasing this activity. Slightly more than one-fourth of the participants developed symptoms sufficiently intense to warrant the diagnosis of myofascial pain and/or arthralgia by trained, blinded examiners (Glaros, 2008). In all the studies, the correlation between mean electromyographical (EMG) activity and pain was significant and accounted for a significant proportion of the variance in self-reported pain (r = .60 to .80). These findings provide clear, replicated evidence that parafunctional clenching increases pain and can cause symptoms of TMJDs.

![](images/f8b04c2199eb834f05fd8756fd45962b853a6f7b0d7e250f25e78f67c92994e4.jpg)  
FI GURE 21.2. “Effort” (intensity and proportion of time in tooth contact) in four groups. Data from Glaros, Williams, and Lausten (2005). Effort measured in arbitrary units. NC, non-TMJD control; DD, disc displacement; M, myofascial pain; MA, myofascial pain and arthralgia.

![](images/98f376167584adebdf16049da7bed14fff12cb0bed10fc643861cc20ff193419.jpg)  
FI GURE 21.3. Effect of parafunctional clenching on least and worst self-reported pain. Data from Glaros, Tabacchi, and Glass (1998).

The role of nocturnal parafunctional activity (“bruxism”), which can include both clenching and grinding, is less clear (Manfredini & Lobbezoo, 2010). Within an individual, the level of parafunctional activity from night to night varies considerably. Evidence of nocturnal grinding obtained through sleep polysomnography, not self-report, shows that the rate of nocturnal tooth grinding in those diagnosed with the myofascial pain of TMJD is very similar to the rate observed in those without TMJD (Raphael et al., 2012). Neither self-report nor accelerated wear on the teeth, a possible artifact of previous tooth grinding, should be viewed as reliable evidence of current nocturnal bruxism.

# Occlusion

Malocclusion is the relative failure of the maxillary and mandibular teeth to fit together properly. Occlusal theories of the cause of TMJDs remain popular among many dentists, although reviews strongly suggest that occlusion plays a minor role at best (Gesch, Bernhardt, & Kirbschus, 2004). Deviations from ideal occlusion are statistically normal in both child and adult populations. Furthermore, there is no compelling experimental evidence to support the hypothesis that occlusal disharmony causes teeth clenching and grinding behaviors (Gesch et al., 2004; Koh & Robinson, 2009).

# Psychological Variables

The psychological correlates of TMJD as a chronic, painful disorder are similar to those reported for other chronic pain conditions. Patients with chronic TMJDs have significantly higher levels of somatoform disorders (particularly pain disorder) and mood disorders (typically involving depression) (Giannakopoulos, Keller, Rammelsberg, Kronmuller, & Schmitter, 2010; Manfredini, Borella, Favero, Ferronato, & Guarda-Nardini, 2010). In contrast, newly diagnosed patients with TMJDs are significantly more likely to report symptoms consistent with one or more anxiety disorders (Gatchel, Garofalo, Ellis, & Holt, 1996). The presence of psychological distress appears to be more predictive of future TMJD than hypothesized genetic markers (Slade et al., 2007).

However, the distribution of emotional distress is not equal across all types of TMJDs. Dahlström, Widmark, and Carlsson (1997), for example, classified patients with TMJDs as “dysfunctional,” “interpersonally distressed,” and “adaptive copers,” based on their responses to the Multidimensional Pain Inventory. They further categorized the TMJDs of these patients as either myofascial pain or disc displacement. Patients diagnosed with myofascial pain were much more likely to fall within the interpersonally distressed coping category than patients diagnosed with disc displacement.

These findings show that psychological problems are more likely to be present in patients diagnosed with myofascial pain than in those with disc displacement. Since epidemiological studies have suggested that patients with muscle disorders outnumber those with joint-only problems (Drangsholt & LeResche, 1999), practitioners should be prepared to consider the possibility that a patient with a TMJD, particularly one with myofascial pain, may have a concomitant psychological disorder.

# Psychophysiological Variables

Laskin’s (1969) psychophysiological model of TMJDs has been the focus of considerable research. According to this model, people react to stress with different bodily systems. Of those who react via the head and neck muscles, some develop TMJDs. A number of studies exploring the psychophysiological model proposed by Laskin have found that patients with TMJDs (typically those with myalgia or myofascial pain dysfunction) show facial muscle activity responses to experimental stressors. However, not all studies have reported such psychophysiological responsiveness to experimental stressors, and the general concept underlying Laskin’s model has come under sharp criticism (Murray & Peck, 2007).

Patients with TMJD may suffer from dysregulation of autonomic and hypothalamic–pituitary– adrenal axis functions, similar to those reported for patients diagnosed with fibromyalgia, headache, and failed back syndrome (Balasubramaniam et al., 2007; Glaros, Urban, & Locke, 2000c). These studies suggest greater similarities among these painful disorders than their separate diagnostic labels suggest.

# Trauma and Abuse

Trauma to the masticatory system can range from mild to severe and can be delivered directly and indirectly. For example, repeatedly raising the shoulder to hold a telephone receiver to an ear puts pressure on the TMJ and increases tension in the neck and shoulder. This repeated behavior can create abnormal muscle tension in the neck and shoulder, and produce pain or exacerbate a preexisting problem. Poor positioning of a computer keyboard or monitor can increase or maintain pain in patients with TMJDs. Whiplash injury, often following a motor vehicle accident, can also lead to TMJDs (Sale & Isberg, 2007).

More direct physical trauma to the TMJ, whether by accident or deliberate abuse, is an important etiological factor in TMJDs. Patients with TMJD who have a history of physical abuse report more pain, anxiety, and depression than those with no physical abuse (Campbell, Riley, Kashikar-Zuck, Gremillion, & Robinson, 2000). The prevalence of sexual abuse in patients with TMJD appears to be no different than its prevalence in patients with other chronic pains (Fillingim, Maixner, Sigurdsson, & Kincaid, 1997). Those who suffer from posttraumatic stress disorder are more likely to report TMJD pain (i.e., myofascial pain) (Afari, Wen, Buchwald, Goldberg, & Plesh, 2008). Studies suggest that patients with histories of abuse are likely to present with a variety of physical and psychological problems (Green, Flowe-Valencia, Rosenblum, & Tait, 2001).

The important point for our discussion is that practitioners must be alert to the possibility that a patient is not going to respond to a dental or behavioral treatment in the ways outlined below. Many patients view biofeedback as a medical treatment and not strictly a psychological treatment. Therefore, it may attract those patients who would otherwise reject psychological interpretations for their symptoms. However, once such a patient is in the office, the rapport established between provider and patient may allow the patient an opportunity to discuss his or her traumatic past. Prudent clinicians need training to evaluate and treat such problems, or need to refer such patients to someone who can.

# Ass essm ent

The comprehensive assessment of a patient with a suspected TMJD contains multiple elements, including (1) a history of current complaints and symptoms, including medical and dental history (the impact of the symptoms on activities of daily living and emotional functioning should also be assessed); (2) physical examination consisting of measures of range of motion of the mandible, palpation of the TMJ and masticatory muscles, and observation of opening and closing patterns (the examination should also rule out ongoing dental disease, periodontal disease, or other diseases and disorders of the oral cavity); (3) imaging of the TMJ, as appropriate, to rule out gross pathology in the joint; and (4) identification of psychosocial stressors and other psychological factors that influence the current problem and can affect a patient’s response to treatment.

Self-report measures for pain (i.e., visual analogue scales) are both useful and inexpensive. Self-report measures of symptoms specific to TMJDs (e.g., the TMJ Scale; www.tmjscale.com/ clinician-resources/the-tmj-scale) may also be useful. TMJD-related behaviors (e.g., clenching and grinding) should be assessed cautiously, because patients are often initially unaware of their parafunctional behaviors. As they become more aware of these behaviors, they may report more of them. This increase may reflect greater awareness of the behaviors, but not unsuccessful treatment. In addition, the definitions of some behaviors may differ from patient to patient. For example, the level of activity that qualifies as “clenching” for one individual may be different in another individual (Glaros & Waghela, 2006). Definitions of parafunctional activities that are less open to interpretation should be used whenever possible (e.g., “tooth contact” rather than “clenching”).

Advances in cell phone technology and the availability of free applications (e.g., PIEL Survey; https://pielsurvey.org) make it possible for practitioners to use sampling methods easily and at potentially very low cost to assess pain, behavior, and mood states.

Psychophysiological devices that detect EMG activity relevant to parafunctional clenching and grinding are increasingly available to biofeedback practitioners. Unfortunately, most devices for monitoring EMG activity are visually obvious and may be difficult to use. The social consequences of wearing such devices may limit their acceptability for use during the day, although patients may be more willing to use them during sleep.

# Treatment

Treatments for TMJDs typically involve one or more elements, including home care, dental techniques, medications, and behavioral interventions. The selection from among and within these elements depends on the information obtained during the comprehensive assessment and should be individualized for each patient. Patients can benefit from authoritative information on the different types of TMJDs (Table 21.1) and the relationship of proposed treatments to their conditions. This information can also increase the willingness of patients to participate actively in effective behaviorally based treatments.

TA BLE 21.1. Main Diagnostic Categories for T MJDs   
![](images/44e7ef54ea6c76450ad4f0c888359acf3b54f6ad107d580b4176f2f2d7337027.jpg)
Note. Data from Dworkin and LeResche (1992).

# Home Care

Patients with TMJDs benefit from instruction in the biomechanics of the TMJ and from encouragement to avoid parafunctional behaviors, such as clenching, grinding, gum chewing, fingernail biting, or chewing ice. Patients, especially those with myofascial pain or arthralgia, may be encouraged to use hot packs, cold packs, over-the-counter analgesics, massage, and stretching exercises to relieve their pain (Riley et al., 2007).

# Dental Techniques

The most common dental treatment for TMJDs is an interocclusal appliance or mouth guard called an “intraoral splint” that covers the maxillary or mandibular teeth. The device prevents grinding behavior from causing additional damage to the teeth, but it does not necessarily reduce the amount of grinding. Theoretically, splints reduce masticatory muscle activity and spread the forces associated with the remaining activity over a larger area of the teeth. However, EMG studies suggest that deliberate contact with a splint can increase masticatory muscle activity (Roark, Glaros, & O’Mahony, 2003). Perhaps the purported efficacy of interocclusal appliances is based on their “cueing” function (Kreiner, Betancor, & Clark, 2001). That is, these splints may remind patients with TMJDs to relax the masticatory muscles and avoid parafunctional activity.

Occlusal adjustment treatment involves equilibration, or adjustment of the fit between the upper and lower teeth. Dentists accomplish this by selectively grinding the teeth to fit better. As noted earlier, occlusal factors do not typically contribute to the etiology of TMJDs and occlusal adjustments are seldom needed. Since the effects of equilibration are not reversible, current recommendations strongly discourage its use (see the National Institute of Dental and Craniofacial Research [NIDCR] at www.nidcr.nih.gov/oralhealth/Topics/ TMJ/TMJDisorders.htm).

# Medications

Both nonsteroidal antiinflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) and tricyclic antidepressants (in low doses) can be used to treat TMJD-related pain. However, the use of NSAIDs increases the risk of gastrointestinal disorders. Benzodiazepines and muscle relaxants are commonly used by practitioners, but research provides uncertain evidence that they are effective in reducing TMJD-related pain (Mujakperuo, Watson, Morrison, & Macfarlane, 2010).

Botulinum toxin (“Botox”) can be injected as a treatment for both TMJD-related pain and nocturnal bruxism. Although the number of controlled, randomized trials using botulinum toxin is small, the results show less pain in the botulinum-treated group (e.g., Lee, McCall, Kim, Chung, & Chung,, 2010). Research has yet to demonstrate whether these effects are long-lasting, whether side-effects are tolerable, or whether complications are minimal.

# Behavioral Interventions

Behavioral treatments include biofeedback-based strategies for relaxation, alarm systems, cognitivebehavioral/stress management programs, self-regulation, and habit reversal. Sample sizes reported in trials of behavioral treatments are generally small, although the results are often encouraging. According to the standards published by the Association for Applied Psychophysiology and Biofeedback, many behavioral treatments for TMJDs are efficacious or probably efficacious.

# Biofeedback‑Based Strategies for Relaxation

A meta-analysis of biofeedback-based treatments for TMJDs indicated that the treatments are effective and that the effects are long-lasting (Crider, Glaros, & Gevirtz, 2005). Follow-up data showed maintenance of gains or continued improvement for up to 2 years. The masseter or temporalis are the most common sites involved in biofeedback, combined with other relaxation procedures or stress management. Treatment typically ranges from six to 12 sessions. Many patients need to learn to position the jaw and teeth properly. They also need to learn how mild, sustained tooth contact increases EMG activity in the temporalis and masseter muscles. Learning these skills helps patients progress quickly.

The mechanism by which biofeedback works is not clearly understood. Patients with TMJDs receiving biofeedback typically show decreases in facial muscle activity and self-reported pain. One hypothesis suggests that biofeedback works directly by promoting decreased EMG activity, which in turn is responsible for the reduction in self-reported pain. The assumption of high baseline levels of EMG activity has mixed experimental support. Although the evidence strongly indicates that biofeedback reduces the EMG activity in targeted muscles, pre- to posttreatment changes in EMG activity are not correlated with the degree of clinical improvement (Burdette & Gale, 1988).

A second hypothesis is that EMG biofeedback is effective because it enhances awareness of facial and masticatory muscle activity, and thereby improves patients’ ability to detect, label, and voluntarily reduce muscle tension before it reaches uncomfortably high levels. Reduction of masseter and temporalis activity is typically accompanied by separation of the posterior teeth. If patients acquire better awareness and control of these muscles and keep their teeth separated, they may avoid low-level parafunctional activity that, by itself, can produce myofascial pain and arthralgia.

A third hypothesis is that EMG biofeedback alters patients’ perceptions of control over their symptoms (Hijzen, Slangen, & Van Houweligen, 1986). Success in learning to regulate EMG activity helps patients adopt a more generalized belief in their ability to manage their psychophysiological states, which in turn leads them to initiate and persist in efforts to cope with stressors, and with subsequent psychophysiological reactions that may be associated with TMJDs. Unfortunately, direct tests of this hypothesis have not been reported, although studies of patients with headache (Rokicki et al., 1997) provide indirect support for this approach.

# Alarm Systems

A nocturnal alarm system may help patients who clench and/or grind during sleep. A nocturnal alarm monitors EMG activity from a masseter or temporalis muscle as the patient sleeps. An alarm sounds when EMG activity exceeds a threshold (frequently set at 20 µV) for a specified time, or when a certain number of suprathreshold EMG events occur within a brief period. During treatment with nocturnal alarms, patients typically show reductions in nocturnal EMG events, particularly when sounding of the alarm is associated with a task that requires wakefulness (Cassisi, McGlynn, & Belles, 1987).

The use of alarm systems may result in sleep disturbance, at least until the procedure significantly suppresses nocturnal bruxing activity. The efficacy of nocturnal alarms may be limited to the active treatment period (Hudzinski & Lawrence,

1992). Some patients may experience rebound effects (i.e., after stopping the use of the alarm system, patients may experience higher levels of clenching or grinding; Cassisi et al., 1987). Practitioners need to be aware that many commercially available devices for nocturnal monitoring of TMJD-relevant EMG activity cannot discriminate parafunctional clenching and grinding from normal swallowing or gross motor activities that may occur during sleep (e.g., turning over in bed). One can usually monitor from only one EMG site with a single device, and it is difficult to assess the meaningfulness of the data obtained by such devices.

Alternatives to nocturnal alarms include taste aversion and contingent electrical stimulation devices. The taste aversion device (Nissani, 2000) embeds a small amount of aversive-tasting fluid within an intraoral device placed on the teeth. When a patient grinds, the capsule holding the fluid bursts, waking up the patient. In a case study, Nissani (2000) reported treatment success with an otherwise treatment-refractory patient. A recently marketed device provides contingent electrical stimulation when grinding behaviors occur. In an open trial with 14 subjects, the number of EMG episodes characteristic of grinding diminished significantly (Jadidi, Castrillon, & Svensson, 2008). The device is notable for using patient-specific signal algorithms to detect nocturnal grinding. In addition, the use of low-intensity stimulation does not appear to disrupt sleep.

# Cognitive‑Behavioral/Stress Management Programs

Cognitive-behavioral and stress management programs utilize a variety of techniques to help patients identify cognitive, behavioral, and environmental triggers for pain; to develop strategies for coping more effectively with pain and its consequences; and to reduce the number and impact of the triggers for pain. Several studies have examined the utility of cognitive-behavioral and stress management programs for TMJDs. Crider and colleagues (2005) found little evidence that combining stress management techniques with EMG biofeedback training is superior to biofeedback alone, although the number and quality of studies using cognitive-behavioral techniques was generally better than studies that assessed biofeedback alone. Cognitive-behavioral techniques alone can reduce TMJD pain, but they do not appear to be superior to biofeedback alone (Mishra, Gatchel, & Gardea, 2000). Since TMJDs may be chronic disorders, some patients may have high levels of psychological disturbance. The addition of cognitive components to their treatment regimen may be especially helpful to these distressed individuals (Turp et al., 2007).

# Self‑Regulation

Individuals experiencing chronic TMJD pain frequently differ from matched controls on multiple psychophysiological measures (e.g., heart rate variability; Schmidt & Carlson, 2009). The experience of chronic pain can also reduce an individual’s ability to self-regulate (Solberg Nes, Carlson, Crofford, de Leeuw, & Segerstrom, 2010) and better manage their pain. To reverse these effects, clinicians can employ self-regulation therapy (Sauer, Burris, & Carlson, 2010), which includes techniques for managing fatigue, pain, and anxiety, along with physical regulation techniques involving breathing, postural relaxation, and proprioceptive reeducation.

# Habit Reversal

Habit reversal may be a useful technique for managing daytime clenching. Habit reversal training consists of three main steps:

1. Making patients more aware of the unwanted behavior. In the case of TMJDs, frequent contact via cell phones, possibly supplemented by diary keeping, may be used to increase the patients’ awareness of parafunctions.   
2. Developing an alternative to the unwanted behavior. For patients with TMJDs, the alternative to parafunctional tooth contact would be relaxation of the masticatory muscles and subsequent elimination of tooth contact. A variety of techniques could be used to teach such skills, including relaxation training and biofeedback.   
3. Substituting the alternative behavior for the unwanted behavior. Each time patients are contacted or become aware that their muscles are becoming tight or their teeth are touching unnecessarily, they can substitute masticatory muscle relaxation as needed. As patients improve in their ability to detect and avoid episodes of parafunctional tooth contact, their pain and discomfort should diminish. Preliminary work suggests that habit reversal techniques can effectively reduce facial pain at potentially low cost (Glaros, Kim-Weroha, Lausten & Franklin, 2007a).

# A Strategy for T reating the Patient w ith  a T MJD

Our approach to treating the patient with a TMJD involves stepped care, beginning with assessment and moving to various treatment modalities. Available data show that biofeedback-based treatments are effective in the management of TMJDrelated pain (Crider et al., 2005) that is not related to another medical or dental condition. There is no evidence that biofeedback-based treatments are effective with patients who complain only of disc displacement or degenerative joint disease. Accordingly, our discussion focuses on the myofascial pain and arthralgia of TMJD. The following outline serves as a guide for each segment of assessment and treatment. We emphasize biofeedback-based treatments below, but there is evidence that combined behavioral and dental treatments may be more effective than either type individually (Aggarwal, Tickle, Javidi, & Peters, 2010). We again urge biofeedback practitioners to develop effective professional collaborations with dentists, so that patients can benefit from their combined efforts.

I. General assessment.

A. Rule out other medical factors. B. Obtain a dental consultation to assess the condition of the TMJ and masticatory musculature. If information from the dentist does not include information about the patient’s response to muscle and TMJ palpation, ask the dentist to perform this evaluation, or consider performing the palpation yourself if you have proper credentials and training. See Dworkin and LeResche (1992) for specific directions for performing such palpations.1 C. Obtain a detailed history of the condition, including prior treatments. D. Assess psychological, psychophysiological, and behavioral patterns that might be salient. E. Obtain relevant self-monitoring data, including daytime logs of parafunctional tooth contact or presleep cognitive logs that target worrying and dysfunctional cognitions. II. Psychosocial and behavioral assessment. Assess and determine the amount of: A. Depression, sleep disturbance (including sleep-onset and sleep-maintenance insomnia), obsessive worrying, or anxiety.

B. Daily/weekly life stressors.   
C. Parafunctional tooth contact and grinding.   
D. Other parafunctional oral habits, such as chewing ice, biting fingernails, chewing gum, biting on the inside of the cheek, and chewing pencil tip erasers.   
E. Adequacy of skills to cope with situational stressors.   
F. Reinforcers for pain.   
G. The patient’s attributions about symptoms and motivation for treatment. Patients may hold the belief that their symptoms are best treated by a dentist or physician. Some such patients may view a referral to a biofeedback provider as evidence that their dentist or physician does not take their complaints seriously, and they may be poorly motivated to participate in a biofeedback treatment program. The assessment techniques (see below) may help shape their beliefs about TMJ dysfunction versus occlusion versus psychophysiological muscle habits and reactivity to stress.   
H. Other relevant factors.

III. Psychophysiological assessment. The goal of psychophysiological assessment is to detect salient modalities for treatment and to demonstrate to patients their physiological reactions to individual stressors. Psychophysiological reactivity may be greater to situations and cognitions identified by the patient as stressful (Ohrbach et al., 1998). Thus, we recommend practitioners incorporate appropriate individual stressors and avoid sole reliance on a standardized protocol.

A. Recording from at least two muscle placement sites, preferably the masticatory muscles (masseter, temporalis) and other psychophysiological modalities, as appropriate.   
B. Assessing changes in psychophysiological measures resulting from participation in various activities (e.g., reclining, sitting up, during manual tasks).   
C. Assessment of reactivity and recovery of psychophysiological variables during stress simulation.   
D. If appropriate, sleep-time assessment, including nocturnal activity frequency, amplitudes, durations, and time of night.

IV. Patient education: setting the stage for treatment. Confirm that the patient understands the basics of the explanation for the pain syndrome. Ensure that the patient has an accurate understanding, based on the role of muscle hyperactivity. Patients often benefit from a demonstration of the impact of relaxation, tooth contact, and moderate clenching on the activity of the masticatory muscles. For patients who cannot “think muscle,” practitioners can use tooth contact as a proxy for masticatory muscle activity. Assess understanding by asking the patient to repeat the rationale for treatment. There will be limited or no treatment success if the patient continues to believe inaccurately that the pain is caused by poor occlusion rather than muscle activity.

A. Describe the normal anatomy and physiology of the TMJ and associated muscles.   
B. Describe the patient’s data about the physical state of the TMJ and muscles.   
C. Demonstrate the effects of stress and parafunctional activities on the TMJ and masticatory muscles.   
D. Describe the relationship between the assessment data and the proposed treatment.   
E. Describe proposed treatment, and provide take-home pamphlets and other informational sources (e.g., www.nidcr.nih.gov/ oralhealth /topics/tmj/tmjdisorders.htm). Patient education brochures are available in both print and electronic versions from the NIDCR.   
F. Assess the patient’s understanding of the disorder at the start of treatment and periodically during treatment. The patient should have a working model in mind for each treatment phase.

V. Initial treatment.

A. Have dentist fabricate intraoral splint, if appropriate. Emphasize the utility of the splint as a device to remind the patient not to touch his or her teeth and to relax the masticatory musculature (Glaros, Owais, & Lausten, 2007b).   
B. Use analgesics and/or tricyclic antidepressants, if appropriate.   
C. Demonstrate effects of parafunctional activities on masticatory muscles. Demonstrate that tooth contact, even at very

low levels, increases the activity of the masticatory muscles.

Begin office-based EMG feedback from masseter and/or temporalis placements. To locate the belly of the temporalis or masseter muscles for electrode placement, place your fingers directly above the angle of the mandible and on the anterior portion of the temples and ask the patient to clench. Include resting, stressor, and activity reactivity and recovery. Include relaxation procedures for daily use, including the use of relaxation tapes. Teach the patient to find jaw positions that maximize relaxation of the masticatory muscles. Instruct the patient to slowly open his or her mouth, as if to eat a small piece of food. This usually produces the lowest EMG readings. Then use other maneuvers until the patient can easily relax the jaw. Use feedback thresholds to help the patient achieve very low levels of muscle activity in this region. For patients who have considerable difficulty identifying muscle tension, instruct them to use tooth contact as a surrogate for increased activity in the masticatory muscles (i.e., inform patients that tooth contact or “setting” of the jaw is the same as increased muscle activity). As patients learn to identify tooth contact episodes, ask patients to increase their focus directly on muscle sensations.

VI. Second-stage treatment if the first stage is not adequate.

A. Cognitive therapies focusing on coping strategies for pain.   
B. More feedback sessions, incorporating additional muscles and other psychophysiological variables.   
C. Alarm system, if appropriate.

VII. Third-stage treatment, if the second stage is not adequate.

A. More extensive cognitive therapy for pain-related issues (e.g., depression). B. Reevaluation of presenting complaints.

When the preceding treatments are insufficient, seriously reconsider the initial diagnosis of TMJD or reconsider the treatments. For example, patients with TMJDs may also have concurrent medical conditions such as fibromyalgia. Referral to a physician for further evaluation may be warranted. Alternatively, examine more closely the possible roles of depression and other psychological or behavioral problems.

# Concl usion

Biofeedback and other applied psychophysiological therapies have a place in the treatment of TMJDs. Working closely with dentists and other health care professionals is critical to increase treatment effectiveness. This chapter emphasizes the importance of identifying proposed mechanisms that play an important role in the etiology of TMJD symptoms and of matching treatments to these mechanisms. Research suggests that the behavioral and psychological interventions presented in this chapter can be effective in treating these patients. Most patients will experience considerable relief from their symptoms if they conscientiously use these techniques.

# Gl oss ary

Articular disc. A dense fibrous connective tissue located between the articular fossa and the condyle of the mandible. Intermittent displacement of the disc can produce clicking or popping sounds when the jaw opens or closes, and a permanent displacement can make it impossible for the jaw to open fully or to close fully.   
Articular fossa. The TMJ “socket” part of the temporal bone.   
Central sensitization. Increased response of the central nervous system to a constant stimulus, often resulting in perception of pain.   
Condyles. Bony structures arising from the U-shaped mandible. The “ball” part of the TMJ. Located directly in front of the ears.   
Equilibration. Adjustment of the occlusion (“bite”) between the upper and lower teeth by selective grinding of the teeth.   
Habit reversal. A behavioral therapy in which the person detects the preclench state, substitutes an incompatible behavior in place of clenching, and overlearns this response in every situation where clenching might occur. It is a useful technique for managing daytime clenching.   
Interocclusal appliance. An orthotic device or mouth

guard called an “intraoral splint” covering the maxillary or mandibular teeth. Intended to protect the teeth from wear from grinding (“bruxism”), and/or to reduce TMJ and masticatory muscle pain.

Mandibular teeth. Teeth of the lower jaw (mandible).

Masseter muscles. Muscles that originate on the zygomatic arches (i.e., cheekbones) and extend downward and slightly backward to insert on the inferior border of the mandible, just ahead of the mandibular angles. These muscles lift the mandible.

Maxillary teeth. Teeth of the upper jaw (maxilla).

Nocturnal alarm systems. A treatment system for nighttime grinding or clenching that interrupts sleep, typically with a loud auditory stimulus, when EMG activity exceeds a preset threshold of intensity, duration, and/or frequency. Potentially useful, but with limitations.

Occlusion. Dental bite or alignment of maxillary and mandibular teeth when one closes the jaw or makes functional contact. “Occlusal disharmony” or “malocclusion” means improper or bad bite.

Parafunctional activity. Clenching and grinding of teeth, or chewing of materials other than food. Nonfunctional activities involving the masticatory muscles.

Pterygoid muscles. Two pairs of muscles (medial or internal pterygoid and lateral or external pterygoid) controlling jaw movements. Accessible intraorally to palpation. The lateral pterygoid muscles assist in opening the mandible (jaw) by pulling the head of the mandible forward. They also protrude the mandible and move it to either side. The medial pterygoid muscles close the jaw and assist in lateral movements.

Temporalis muscles. Muscles that originate on the lateral surfaces of the skull at the temporal bones. These muscles extend downward under the zygomatic arches (i.e., cheekbones) and taper to narrow bands where they insert into the coronoid processes of the mandible. These muscles lift the mandible upward.

Trigger points. Taut bands of muscle tissue that are tender to palpation.

# Note

1. The diagnostic criteria for TMJD are under revision [e.g., Anderson et al., 2010; see www.rdc-tmdinternational. org; Journal of Oral Rehabilitation, 2010, Vol. 37(10)]. Readers are encouraged to use the most recent, approved versions for performing examinations.

Clinical Oral Implants Research, 18(Suppl. 3), 138–150.

# Raynaud’s Disease and Raynaud’s Phenomenon

Maria K atsamanis, Mark S. Schwartz, and K eith Sedlacek

# Definitions, Symp toms , Diagnosis, and C auses

Raynaud’s symptoms involve spasms of arterioles and small arteries in the digits of the hands and feet (Block & Sequeira, 2001; Wigley, 2002). Triphasic skin color changes are classic: They involve whiteness (blanching or pallor), blueness (cyanosis), and redness (rubor or reactive hyperemia) (Wigley, 2002). (As in other chapters, italicizing a term on first use indicates that the term is included in the chapter’s glossary). Some people show biphasic skin color changes involving cyanosis, then reactive hyperemia. Occasionally, symptoms include the nose and tongue. They rarely involve the thumb. The duration of spasms ranges from minutes to hours. Cold exposure is the usual stimulus for the spasms; however, emotional and other psychological events can also provoke an attack in many patients. Estimates vary widely for the incidence of this type of stimulus.

An important distinction is between “primary Raynaud’s” or “idiopathic Raynaud’s” (Raynaud’s disease), which has no known cause, and Raynaud’s symptoms secondary to another condition, sometimes called “secondary Raynaud’s” or “Raynaud’s phenomenon” (RP). Examples of such conditions are connective tissue disorders, such as rheumatoid arthritis (RA), systemic lupus erythematosus (SLE), and, most commonly, scleroderma (progressive systemic sclerosis, or PSS). Nearly all patients with PSS experience these vasospastic episodes. Others include mixed connective tissue disease (MCTD), Sjögren’s syndrome, polymyositis, and dermatomyositis (Pope, 2007).

Other conditions that cause Raynaud’s symptoms include obstructive arterial diseases, such as thromboangitis obliterans and arteriosclerosis obliterans (Coffman, 1991; Coffman, 2000; Pope, 2007). Trauma, as from traumatic vasospastic disease (vibration-induced), is another secondary cause. Carpal tunnel syndrome and thoracic outlet obstruction syndromes are other common causes (Coffman, 1991, 2000; Pope, 2007). Reflex sympathetic dystrophy (RSD), dysproteinemias, polycythemia vera, myxedema or adult hypothyroidism, primary pulmonary hypertension (PPH), and renal diseases add to the list (Coffman, 1991; Pope, 2007).

Drugs can also result in the spasms and are secondary causes. Notable examples are ergot preparations, methysergide, beta-adrenergic blocking agents (beta-blockers), and imipramine hydrochloride (Coffman, 1991; Pope, 2007). Prudent practitioners understand the necessity of proper medical examination and testing, so that any underlying disorder may be found.

Prevalence rates of Raynaud’s phenomenon differ by gender (Gardner-Medwin, Macdonald, Taylor, Riley & Powell, 2001). Raynaud’s disease usually affects females from preadolescence to early middle age. The estimated ratio of females to males is about 4:1 (Coffman, 2000; Weinrich, Maricq, Keil, McGregor, & Diat, 1990). It is characterized by (1) bilateral involvement, and (2) the presence of symptoms for at least 2 years without progression, and without evidence of another cause. Occasionally, it takes longer for the underlying disease to become manifest. Some patients initially diagnosed as having idiopathic Raynaud’s disease later learn that their symptoms are secondary to another disorder that went unrecognized earlier in the history of the symptoms. Trophic changes in the skin and gangrene, if present at all, are minimal in persons with the primary type.

In 1862, Maurice Raynaud (Raynaud, 1862) first defined this clinical syndrome as episodic digital ischemia provoked by cold, cyanosis, and emotion. Raynaud (1888) suggested that hyperactivity of the sympathetic nervous system (SNS) caused the increased vasoconstrictive response to cold. Current theories still include increased activity of the SNS and local faults in the digital arteries, and there is supportive evidence for each position or the combination (Coffman, 1991). Other theories of pathophysiology include serotonin, platelets, and blood viscosity. The theories of a general vascular abnormality cannot account for the differences in drug responses and neurogenic controls among the different circulatory beds. In conclusion, many factors may contribute to the vasospasms in digits.

# Treatments

There are no universally accepted medical treatments for Raynaud’s disease. Stepped-care treatment and sensible first treatments involve protecting the body and extremities from cold, avoiding tobacco use because nicotine causes blood vessel constriction, and limiting or ceasing caffeine consumption.

Advances in pharmacotherapy show promise and are part of current medical practice (Berkow & Fletcher, 1992; Malenfant, Catton, & Pope, 2009). Calcium entry blockers are the most effective drugs for the treatment of Raynaud’s phenomenon (Rodeheffer, Rommer, & Wigley, 1983; Schmidt, Valentin, & Nielsen, 1989). However, the drugs are nonspecific, and negative side effects are problematic; thus, medication treatments have their limitations (Belch & Ho, 1996). A discussion of pharmacotherapy is beyond the scope of this chapter. However, we briefly mention several medications for interested readers. One notable example is nifedipine, a calcium entry blocker (Thompson & Pope, 2005). Others include other calcium entry blockers, sympatholetic agents (e.g., reserpine and guanethidine), and prazosin. Again, these drugs often have bothersome side effects that cause poor compliance or discontinuation. Others in this class have mixed or poor results, and/or intolerable side effects. See Coffman (1991, 2000) for a discussion of all these drugs and others.

We note certain types of medications because their use is contraindicated in Raynaud’s disease. They induce digital vasoconstriction and may actually worsen the condition. Notable examples are beta-blockers, clonidine, and ergot preparations (Berkow & Fletcher, 1992; Coffman, 2000).

Regional sympathectomy often has major drawbacks and complications. There is limited longterm success, especially for the hands. When considered at all, regional sympathectomy is usually a last resort for patients with Raynaud’s disease who are suffering from progressive disability. It is contraindicated for secondary RP (Coffman, 1991, 2000), and is of “doubtful value in the primary disease” (Coffman, 1991, p. 600). However, relief may last from 1 to 2 years for the upper extremities, and there are reports of considerable and lasting benefit for lumbar sympathectomy to relieve the spasms in the toes (Coffman, 1991).

Well-controlled research supports the use of thermal-biofeedback-assisted treatment for idiopathic Raynaud’s disease (Coffman, 2000). See the review by Karavidas, Tsai, Yucha, McGrady, and Lehrer (2006). Combining thermal biofeedback with cold stress challenges can improve both initial and long-term results. However, including cold stress challenges is not yet a standard or common clinical practice because of very limited access to equipment that could elicit the cold challenges.

# Th e Us eful ness of Th ermal Biofeedback in T reating Raynaud’s Disease and Secondary R P

The reduction of peripheral vasoconstriction and vasospastic attacks often lessens significantly with various forms of physiological self-regulation. Some practitioners and researchers still believe that autogenic therapy and similar relaxation techniques can be effective without biofeedback. In our view, however, biofeedback appears to result in improved greater acquisition of hand-warming skills (Freedman, Ianni, & Wenig, 1983, 1985; Freedman et al., 1983; Karavidas et al., 2006; Sedlacek & Taub, 1996).

There are several publications describing the use of thermal biofeedback and other biobehavioral therapies for Raynaud’s disease (Grove & Belanger, 1983; Sedlacek, 1984, 1989; Rose & Carlson, 1987). Ten studies evaluating thermal biofeedback for Raynaud’s phenomenon, have been identified (see Table 22.1). The most convincing and best-controlled between-groups experimental study remains the one conducted by Freedman et al. (1983). This study and the follow-up reports (Freedman, 1987) over 3 years strongly support the advantage of thermal biofeedback over autogenic relaxation for treating Raynaud’s disease. The ambitious treatment study employed a focal cold stimulus as part of the therapy with biofeedback. The authors reported a 32.6% reduction of vasospastic episodes in the group receiving autogenic therapy. However, there was a 66.8% reduction in the group receiving thermal biofeedback, and a 92.5% reduction in the group receiving thermal biofeedback plus the cold stress challenge. Major methodological advantages of this study included a well-controlled design, lengthy daily diary entries measuring attack rates, careful attention to seasonal temperatures, ambulatory monitoring, and a 1-year initial follow-up during the same cold months in which therapy began. The results lasted at least 3 years following treatment (Freedman et al., 1983; Freedman, 1987).

By contrast, in the Raynaud’s Treatment Study (RTS)—a comparison of sustained-release nifedipine and temperature biofeedback—the investigators (Thompson & the RTS Investigators, 2000) concluded that the biofeedback was inferior to the sustained-release medication in treating RP. Previously, with regular nifedipine for RP, from 30 to 100% of patients had had adverse side effects (e.g., tachycardia, headache, dizziness, flushing, and edema). Because of these side effects, many of the patients had stopped using nifedipine. However, with the new sustained-release formulation of nifedipine in this double-blind study of RP, 24% reported edema, 17% reported headache, and a small percentage reported flushing (8%) and tachycardia. Only 57% of participants continued to take the full 60-mg dosage. Thus, using the new sustained-release nifedipine, 24, 8, and 3%, respectively, reported edema, flushing, and tachycardia, compared with 56, 30, and 23%, respectively, did so when regular nifedipine was used in previous studies.

The RTS showed that the “new” nifedipine resulted in a 60% reduction in RP symptoms, while the biofeedback temperature feedback did not. This study showed that 67% of normal participants learned to increase finger temperature, while only 35% of participants with RP were able to satisfy successful learning criteria. (The criterion for improvement of RP was greater than a 60% reduction in RP attacks at the end of the first and second winters.) The RTS results therefore differ from the successful learning seen in studies of primary and secondary Raynaud’s disease in the laboratory (Freedman et al., 1983; Freedman, 1987) and in clinical practice (Freedman, 1987, 1991; Sedlacek & Taub, 1996).

Some qualifications of the RTS results should be noted, however. Among the causes for the disparate learning rates in the healthy normal controls versus the participants with RP were inexperienced therapists for the latter; a primitive training protocol, in which evidently only eight sessions using temperature feedback were provided; and instructions to participants with RP basically to try their own ideas on how to increase their finger temperature. There were no home charts or home temperature feedback devices, and only with the second group (cohort) was “coaching” allowed, because successful learning in the first cohort was less than expected. (Home temperature devices are available at a cost of from \$0.10 to \$10–20). In addition to these problems, it appears that the biofeedback “providers” evidently had little or no clinical experience.

In a second report, Middaugh et al. (2001) discussed some of the learning that did and did not take place in the RTS. They pointed out that research and clinical studies (Freedman, 1987; Sedlacek & Taub, 1996) indicate that about twothirds of patients with Raynaud’s disease or RP learn finger temperature biofeedback skills. This is similar to the learning rate for the healthy subjects in the RTS. Furthermore, Middaugh’s group noted that RTS participants with RP were instructed to use the feedback display to “increase hand temperature using mental strategies of their own choosing. No adjunctive relaxing techniques were taught” (pp. 255–256).

Middaugh et al. (2001) also looked at the five different RTS training sites, in which learning rates varied from 12.5 to 75%, despite use of the same research protocol. They concluded that successful learning of hand-warming skills was associated with clinic sites and coping strategies, as well as anxiety and gender, but not RP severity. Since the two clinics with lowest learning rates for hand warming also had the lowest success rate for electromyographic (EMG) frontalis relaxation, this suggests that the laboratory environment is a major factor in the lack of learning for the participants with RP. Middaugh et al. cited Taub and School (1978), who found that a “friendly” versus “impersonal” approach to teaching finger warming may be a large factor, along with the use of adjunctive techniques. In their article, Taub and School discuss many of the relevant clinical training issues for biofeedback treatment of Raynaud’s disease or RP.
