# Essential Hypertension

Wolfgang Linden and A ngele V . McGrady

This chapter begins with a brief description of the regulation of blood pressure (BP). It proceeds to the classification of BP, the definition of hypertension, and describes the risks conferred by hypertension for development of cardiovascular disease and stroke. The chapter delineates current recommendations for pharmacotherapy in hypertension and describes nonpharmacological therapies other than biofeedback and relaxation. For the most part, the chapter comprises a description of a composite psychophysiological treatment plan for essential hypertension and describes the current state of evidence on effectiveness. This plan utilizes and references key research from several biofeedback treatment centers and psychophysiological laboratories. We develop characteristics of baseline, treatment and follow-up, discuss patient education, and list outcome evaluation criteria. The last section of the chapter considers special populations and suggests that biofeedback and relaxation may be more effective for hypertensive patients with specific psychophysiological characteristics. The chapter ends with suggestions for further research in applied psychophysiology and hypertension.

Also germane to the behavioral treatment of patients with essential hypertension are (1) the general principles of biofeedback measurement, (2) considerations in developing low physiological arousal, (3) the intake process, (4) cognitive preparation of patients, (5) adherence, and (6)

generalization. In this chapter, however, we discuss only those topics that are specific to treatment of essential hypertension.

# Normal Regul ation of Bl ood Press ure

A basic understanding of the physiology of blood pressure (Guyton & Hall, 2006; Widmaier, Raff, & Strang, 2006) is necessary before the biofeedback practitioner can implement a treatment plan for BP reduction. A full appreciation of the complexities of neural and endocrine influences on BP or the mechanisms of action of all the antihypertensive drugs is not essential. However, the practitioner must (1) understand the variables that determine BP and the elements of BP regulation, (2) have a working knowledge of common antihypertensive medications, and (3) be able to explain to patients in lay terms the rationale for treatment of elevated BP with biofeedback and relaxation.

Blood pressure values are expressed as systolic/ diastolic in millimeters of mercury (mm Hg). Systolic blood pressure (SBP) is the maximum pressure during ejection of blood from the heart, and diastolic blood pressure (DBP) is the minimum pressure that occurs during cardiac relaxation. Mean arterial pressure (MAP) is the average pressure driving blood through all organs except the lungs to ensure adequate perfusion of tissues. MAP is the product of cardiac output and the total peripheral resistance. One calculates cardiac output by multiplying heart rate (beats per minute) by stroke volume output. The latter is the amount of blood ejected during each beat of the heart.

Variations in heart rate are linked to breathing inhalation and exhalation in a complex pattern (heart rate variability [HRV]), which we describe later (Lehrer, 2007). Factors controlling heart rate and stroke volume include sympathetic and parasympathetic nerve activity. Total peripheral resistance (TPR) is the impediment or resistance to the flow of blood in the arteries, arterioles, and to a minor extent, the veins. Sympathetic activity, circulating substances in the blood, and local conditions in the tissues all influence TPR. For example, if sympathetic activity increases, arterioles constrict and present a larger resistance to the flow of blood, raising BP. It is important to remember that MAP can remain constant despite changes in either cardiac output or TPR as long as the product of cardiac output and TPR is the same (Widmaier et al., 2006).

Neural, renal, and hormonal factors all exert an influence on BP. The nervous system is very important in the moment-to-moment control of arterial pressure. During exercise and in stressful situations, the nervous system organizes a rapid increase in BP by constricting the arterioles and veins, and increasing heart rate. Special stretch receptors (baroreceptors) located in the walls of the heart and blood vessels constantly monitor BP. When BP is too high, the baroreceptors respond to distention caused by increased pressure by sending an electrical signal through nerves to the vasomotor center in the medulla of the brain. Nerve responses begin within a few seconds and can decrease pressure significantly by dilating the blood vessels and slowing heart rate within 5–10 seconds. So, the baroreceptor system is most important for short-term regulation of arterial pressure, whereas long-term regulation requires mechanisms based in the kidney (renal) and the endocrine systems (Guyton & Hall, 2006).

A diurnal pattern of BP exists in both normal individuals and in those with essential hypertension. During the daytime, awake hours, BP varies with physical activity and is influenced by emotional states, such as anxiety, anger or sadness. During sleep, decreases of up to 20% occur in normotensive individuals; this is called nighttime dipping. Dipping is considered a healthy homeostatic pattern and its absence is considered maladaptive. Note that the nondipping pattern occurs in some individuals with essential hypertension, in particular those with renal disease (Pickering, Shimbo, & Haas, 2006).

Blood volume is the most important determinant of long-term BP level, because it affects venous return, stroke volume output, and cardiac output. For example, when the circulatory system contains too much fluid, arterial pressure rises. Increased BP in turn signals the kidneys to excrete the excess fluid (pressure diuresis). Loss of fluid through the urine subsequently returns fluid balance to normal and decreases BP. The hormone aldosterone, released from the adrenal cortex by the renin–angiotensin system, is also important in BP regulation, because it facilitates reabsorption of sodium by the kidney. In turn, the renin–angiotensin hormonal complex releases aldosterone. For example, decreased plasma volume, as in dehydration, instigates a neural and endocrine reflex response that consists of increased renin–angiotensin and aldosterone, resulting in increased sodium reabsorption. Since sodium draws water with it, fluid loss through the urine decreases, and balance is restored (Ganong, 2005). The individual also experiences thirst, so drinking fluids can help to rebalance the system.

Maintenance of blood flow to the vascular beds, such as those in skin and muscle, requires a certain level of BP. If BP is too low, the tissues will not receive enough blood. The variation in the proportion of blood flow to different tissues depends on the metabolic needs of those tissues. The end products of metabolism build up in active tissues; then dilation of blood vessels occurs to ensure delivery of more oxygenated blood. This is local regulation. On the other hand, some substances circulate within the blood and affect tissues distant from where the substance was originally produced and secreted. For example, the adrenal medulla releases epinephrine, which dilates the vessels in skeletal muscles. The atrial natriuretic peptide, secreted by the heart, antagonizes the actions of various vasoconstrictor chemicals. Other vasoactive compounds are released by the walls of blood vessels (e.g., endothelium-derived relaxing factor) and affect tone and vessel diameter, thereby affecting TPR and BP.

In summary, homeostatic processes work in concert to maintain MAP sufficient in times of rest or activity. Receptors (monitoring structures in this case) keep track of BP. Change in BP alerts the nerve cells in the vasomotor center. Response to the change in BP involves modulation of the two basic determinants of BP: cardiac output and total peripheral resistance by neural, renal, or hormonal action. Rapid responses are organized by the nervous system, and long-term changes require renal and endocrine involvement (Ganong, 2005).

# Measurement of Blood Pressure

Although one can quickly learn how to measure blood pressure, practitioners must recognize the many possible errors in BP assessment. The patient him- or herself, the professionals, the devices, and the entire measurement situation merit consideration. Properly calibrated aneroid sphygmomanometers are standard choices, but many providers depend on wrist band monitors or finger devices. Practitioners should recalibrate the aneroid sphygmomanometer every few months depending on the frequency of its use. For office professional use, relatively inexpensive automatic BP monitors attached to the upper arm or wrist can provide reliable measures of SBP, DBP, and heart rate. Before purchase, however, the monitor should be carefully evaluated, because even devices with similar measurement methodologies can differ substantially in their accuracy. An incorrect cuff size may significantly alter BP readings. A cuff that is too small produces a falsely high BP reading. As a result, one could incorrectly classify obese patients as having hypertension. Practitioners often purchase cuffs of different sizes for the clinical setting so that a properly sized cuff is available when needed.

Although the cuff size is designated in centimeters, an easy conversion can be made by multiplying centimeters times 0.39 to determine inches. For arm circumferences of less than 33 centimeters, we recommend a regular size adult cuff (12 × 23 centimeters). The recommendation for arm circumferences between 33 and 41 centimeters is a large size (15 × 33 centimeters). A thigh size cuff of 18 × 36 centimeters is indicated for those arms larger than 41 centimeters. Ambulatory monitors are programmed for BP measurements at specific intervals during a 24-hour period. The information gained from ambulatory monitoring reflects the mean BP, the variations in BP during the day and night, and can capture the duration of drug effects (Pickering et al., 2006).

It is very important to remember that office readings may falsely lead to a positive diagnosis because of measurement apprehension (so-called “white-coat hypertension”; Selenta, Hogan, & Linden, 2000). Furthermore, the patients who have high BP all day but present with normal readings in the clinic (white-coat normotension) may be missed with only clinic readings (Liu et al., 1999; Selenta et al., 2000). These unfortunately frequent diagnostic errors can be minimized when a 24-hour ambulatory monitors is used.

No matter the type of instruments used in the clinic, it is recommended that patients regularly monitor their own BP. Practitioners should help their patients to select good quality, easy-to-use instruments and train them in their use. Given recent progress in device development and validation, practitioners should recommend electronic BP instruments that provide a digital readout that avoids measurement errors due to inadequate training. These may also be helpful for home use by individuals with poor hearing. Medical supply stores stock several types of monitors and staff are able to help patients with assembly and calibration of their instruments.

Standardized and repeated BP measurements in the office and at home are critical to establish the patient’s actual pretreatment BP and to evaluate therapy effects. Many factors, including those listed below, either artificially increase or decrease the values obtained. The four categories that subsume these factors are patient, practitioner, setting, and equipment (adapted from Joint National Committee [JNC] on Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Treatment of High Blood Pressure, 2003; Padwal et al., 2009).

# The Patient

1. The position and level of the patient's arm. The arm and back should be supported; position the arm at heart level. One should initially measure BP in both arms.   
2. The position of the patient: sitting, standing, supine. Measurements should begin after the patient has been sitting quietly for 5 minutes.   
3. The emotional state of the patient shortly before or at the time one obtains the BP. Slow breathing should be encouraged. Consider the patient’s attitudes toward both BP measurement and the professional doing the measuring.   
4. The length of time since the patient has eaten, exercised, or used nicotine, caffeine, or other chemicals. Patients should not have had caffeine or smoked within 30 minutes of BP measurement.   
5. Type and dosage of antihypertensive medications and when the medicine was last taken; other prescription and over-the-counter medicines, as appropriate.   
6. Usual activity/inactivity, sleep–wake cycle. Note whether the patient works a day or a night shift.

# The Practitioner

7. The initials of the person (health care provider) measuring the BP should be recorded and remain consistent for repeated measures. 8. The interval between BP measurements in the same session. Use at least two readings separated by 5 minutes. 9. It is best that the practitioner not talk to the patient during measurement because conversation is known to increase blood pressure.

#

10. The site and setting of BP measurement (e.g., work; home, professional's office).   
11. The patient's familiarity with the environment in which one measures the BP.   
12. The date and time of day.

# Equipment

13. The instrument used, whether it be manual or electronic. The same instrument should be used each time.   
14. The placement of the cuff on the arm, appropriate cuff size, and the rate of deflation of the cuff.   
15. Coherence between ambulatory BP monitoring, home and office measurements.

# Classification of BP

The JNC, a group of experts in the diagnosis and treatment of hypertension, meets every few years to study and discuss results of epidemiological investigations and therapeutic trials. Its published reports guide practitioners who care for patients with hypertension. The seventh and latest report presents the classification of BP shown in Table 23.1 (JNC, 2003). The category “prehypertension,” formerly “high normal,” denotes SBP between 120 and 139 or DBP between 80 and 89 mm Hg. There are two stages of hypertension (Stage 1 and Stage 2) in contrast to the four stages of severity in the sixth report (JNC, 1997). Whitworth and the World Health Organization (WHO) International Society of Hypertension (ISH) Writing Group (2003) prefer the terms “grades” 1, 2, and 3, because the term “stages” assumes a progression over time that does not always occur. BP ranges associated with low, medium and high risk are higher than those stated in the seventh JNC report (2003). There is general agreement, however, that target BP is influenced by the number of risk factors (presence of diabetes or hyperlipidemia), and that the goal BP in patients who are at high risk is 130/80 mm Hg or less.

TA BLE 23.1. Classification of Blood Pressure   
![](images/9338fa704e8a19ce01980d2336fe5d564c21307d2c302cbb0044bf318dd59387.jpg)
Note. SBP, systolic blood pressure; DBP, diastolic blood pressure. From the seventh report of the Joint National Committee on Prevention, Detection, Evaluation, and Treatment of High Blood Pressure (2003).

# Hypertension: Definition and Ch aracteristics

“Hypertension” is a complex disease characterized by sustained elevations of SBP, DBP, or both. “Essential” or “primary” hypertension, elevated BP of unknown etiology, is the diagnosis for more than 85% of people with chronically elevated BP (Ganong, 2005). Central to the development of essential hypertension is autonomic imbalance (i.e., sympathetic overactivity and underactivity of the parasympathetic system; Brook & Julius, 2000).

The etiology of hypertension can sometimes be identified; a few of these causes are kidney disease, sleep apnea, steroid therapy, and aldosteronism. Primary aldosteronism is a type of volume loading hypertension; excess aldosterone in the body decreases the amount of salt and water that is excreted through the kidneys (JNC, 2003). There is empirical support that effective treatment of sleep apnea is also associated with prompt and significant drops in BP, measured with ambulatory devices (Otsuka, Ribeiro de Almeida, Lowe, Linden, & Ryan, 2006). Medical and surgical management of these identifiable hypertensive disorders must supersede psychophysiological therapy. Thus, it is unlikely that physicians would refer these individuals for biofeedback specifically for their hypertension, although patients with chronic serious illness are good candidates for stress management and supportive psychotherapy.

The detrimental effects of hypertension are well documented. Sustained elevations of BP can damage the brain, kidneys, heart, and blood vessels. In fact, greater understanding of the effects of “high-normal” BP on risk led to the definition of “prehypertension.” Untreated hypertension contributes to premature death through stroke, heart attack, or kidney failure (Chalmers et al., 2003). The presence of certain laboratory values outside the normal range, reduced HRV, and elevated BP increases the incidence of complications of hypertensive disease (Ganong, 2005).

Epidemiological studies such as the NHANES III (Third National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey) illustrate that in adults ages 18–34 years, 4% have BP greater than 140/90 mm Hg, whereas about 25% of those between 35 and 64 years, and 59% of those 65–74 years of age meet these criteria (Joffres, Hamet, MacLean, L’italien, & Fodor, 2001). The risk factors associated with the development of essential hypertension include increasing age, obesity, presence of medical comorbidities, particularly Type 2 diabetes and hyperlipidemia, and lifestyle factors such as alcohol, high-sodium diet, and inactivity. In general, BP increases with age, and men have higher BP than women until their mid-60s (Reckelhoff, 2001). African Americans are much more likely to have elevated BP; their BP is higher and they have more severe metabolic syndrome than European Americans (Wright et al., 2008; Winkleby, Kraemer, Ahn, & Warady, 1998). About 25% of children with one hypertensive parent will have sustained elevated BP. If both parents have hypertension, then about 50% of their children will develop the disease. Hypertension develops about 10 times more often in persons who are overweight by 20% over normal body weight than in those who maintain normal body weight (JNC, 1997).

Socioeconomic status is correlated inversely with incidence and severity of primary hypertension. Exposure to harsh conditions during childhood and lack of parental education were predictive of the rate of change in BP over 10 years (Lehman, Kiefe, Taylor & Seeman, 2009). With regard to psychological factors, much of the recent research has focused on depression, anxiety, and anger (Kubzansky, Davidson, & Rozanski, 2005). In the Coronary Artery Risk Development in Young Adults study (CARDIA; Yan et al., 2003), time urgency and hostility were predictive of hypertension 15 years after baseline in both genders and across racial groups. Although the mechanisms by which distress impacts BP and long term cardiovascular health are unclear, autonomic function, indexed by HRV has drawn interest (Hughes & Stoney, 2000). Psychosocial factors may accentuate hereditary risk for hypertension or worsen existing high BP (Kaplan, 2003). The practitioner is well advised to remember that negative emotional states influence behaviors such as exercise, diet, and alcohol consumption, which are particularly relevant to maintenance of normal BP.

In summary, scientists cannot reliably identify a single characteristic or risk factor as the causal factor in the development or maintenance of elevated BP. Attempts have been made to describe the pathophysiology of hypertension in terms of disordered cardiac output or total peripheral resistance, or both. The hypersympathetic state of initial BP elevation depends on high cardiac output. As the degree of hypertension progresses, high resistance predominates (Ganne, Arora, Karam, & McFarlane, 2007; Julius, 1991). Some 30% of hypertensive individuals exhibit variations in BP that are dependent on dietary sodium. BP decreases mean arterial pressure (MAP) by greater than 10 mm Hg, with sodium and fluid depletion, and risk for cardiovascular events decreases approximately 25% (Chobanian & Hill, 2000). Dietary Approaches to Stop Hypertension (DASH diet; high in fruits, vegetables, low in fat) also significantly reduced BP and associated risk (Conlin et al., 2000; Levitan, Wolk, & Mittleman, 2009). It is clear that the diagnosis of essential hypertension alone tells us very little about causes and psychophysiological characteristics of this heterogenous condition. Rather, a mosaic of behavioral, psychological, and physiological factors contributes to the development of chronically elevated BP, and the presence of multiple risk factors is correspondingly associated with a higher likelihood of developing hypertension over time (Goldbacher & Matthews, 2007).

# Lifestyle Modifications

Both the seventh JNC (2003) and WHO/ISH (2003) encourage lifestyle modifications for all individuals and recommend specific behavioral changes for patients whose BP approaches or resides in the hypertensive range. Ways to improve patient adherence to both behavioral and pharmacotherapeutic recommendations are discussed by Rains and Schwartz (Chapter 15, this volume), but are also relevant here. The principles of motivational interviewing (i.e., building motivation from within the person instead of imposing motivation) should be considered (Miller & Rolinick, 2002). Patients usually experience increased feelings of self-efficacy when lifestyle modifications are implemented, none of which are associated with side effects common to medication (WHO/ISH, 2003). However, a complex treatment strategy that involves multiple behavioral changes in addition to medication may overwhelm the patient, and the presence of negative psychological states further complicates the design of an effective treatment.

The JNC recommends the following lifestyle modifications in order of expected benefit in lowering BP:

•	 Lose weight if overweight.   
•	 Adopt the DASH eating plan.   
•	 Reduce sodium intake to less than 100 millimoles per day (2.4 grams of sodium or 6 grams of sodium chloride).   
•	 Exercise aerobically on a regular basis.   
•	 Limit alcohol intake to less than 1 ounce per day of ethanol for men and ½ ounce per day for women (24 ounces of beer, 10 ounces of wine, or 1.5 ounces of 80 proof whiskey).

Health care professionals who offer psychophysiological therapies, including biofeedback, should question patients about their lifestyle during the initial interview, then consider recommending changes in behavior. Providers of biofeedback and associated therapies, however, would do well to enlist the services of dieticians, smoking cessation experts, and individuals with established exercise programs to complement the biofeedback and relaxation therapies. From a research perspective, combining other therapies with relaxation and biofeedback makes it more difficult to determine the relative contributions of each to lowered BP. In the clinical setting, the analysis of separate efficacy of each treatment is often less important, because the overall objective is to lower BP to normal levels and to decrease medication requirements if possible. However, knowing the relative contribution of each intervention can sometimes help to foster adherence, and make it easier to justify the cost and time commitment necessary for a multimodal treatment program.

The introduction of lifestyle changes should include an explanation to those patients who are being asked to change their behaviors and habits. Explain to patients that because sodium causes retention of fluid, the increased blood volume increases BP. Also explain that caffeine and nicotine are nervous system stimulants that acutely increase heart rate and, therefore, cardiac output and BP. Remind patients that risk factors are additive and that excess caffeine consumption during high-stress conditions can exacerbate BP increases (Shepard, al’Absi, Whitsett, Passey, & Lovallo, 2000). Encourage an exercise program, especially aerobic exercise. Discuss the conditioning effect of exercise on the muscles. For example, explain that because active muscles need more blood, dilation of blood vessels decreases the resistance to blood flow and thereby lowers BP. The sedentary patient should get proper medical screening and approval before beginning a program of moderate or strenuous aerobic exercise. Set a contract with the patient: “Let's try life-style changes first. We'll recheck monthly for 4–6 months, then discuss alternatives.” The intervals between checkups are based on the patient’s BP and his or her adherence history with the practitioner (Padwal et al., 2009).

# Pharmacological Therapies

Pharmacotherapy is the core of treatment for persons with Stage 1 and Stage 2 hypertension, in particular those persons with other risk factors for cardiovascular complications. With regard to the prehypertension group with no risk factors, one may delay medication with careful monitoring, while the person attempts to implement lifestyle changes. Compliance with taking medication may be poor in individuals who do not have noticeable symptoms, as is the case with most patients with hypertension. The practitioner is challenged to design a multicomponent treatment plan, educate the patient, build motivation, and monitor adherence. Many patients do not understand the importance of therapy and the necessity of implementing lifestyle changes, as well as taking medication. Education that is based on empowerment of the patient seems to be more successful than compliance-based intervention (Figar et al., 2006). However, the rewards of effective management are great, because it is clear that aggressive treatment of high BP decreases the incidence of morbidity and mortality from cardiovascular events (Cushman, 2001; Israiti, Hernandez-Hernandez, & Valasco, 2007). The design of a multicomponent treatment plan should consider the following factors:

1. The age, gender and ethnicity of the patient.   
2. The patients' present lifestyle and motivation for change.   
3. The desire of many patients to be involved in their own self-care.   
4. Any significant comorbidity, such as psychiatric illness, diabetes, hyperlipidemia.

The major types of antihypertensive drugs include diuretics, beta blockers, angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors, angiotensin II receptor antagonists, and calcium channel blockers. Diuretics increase sodium and water excretion by the kidney and thereby decrease total peripheral resistance (TPR). Beta-blockers reduce cardiac output and indirectly decrease blood volume. The ACE inhibitors lower vascular resistance and decrease sodium retention via aldosterone reduction. Similarly, the angiotensin II receptor blockers decrease TPR by lowering the concentration of angiotensin in plasma. The calcium channel blockers inhibit blood vessel wall contraction, thus decreasing TPR (Luke, 2005; Widmaier et al., 2006). The JNC (2003) recommends beginning pharmacological therapy for Stage 1 hypertension, in most cases, with diuretics or beta-blockers because of demonstrated reduction in morbidity or mortality. Interestingly, a recent review concluded that thiazide diuretics are superior to beta-blockers as drug of first choice (Wright et al., 2008). For patients with Stage 2 hypertension, the treatment algorithm recommends a two-drug combination (a diuretic and a choice of beta-blocker, ACE inhibitor, or calcium channel blocker). If BP response is inadequate, further therapy may include an increase in drug dose and substitution or addition of a drug from another class. Physicians who treat patients with “compelling indications” such as diabetes or past myocardial infarction are recommended to follow the guidelines listed in the seventh JNC (2003). The reader may also consult Gaffney et al. (2008) for synopses of clinical practice guidelines and clinical trials. In summary, based on the ramifications of untreated hypertensive disease, according to Chobanian (2008), how BP is controlled is less important than reaching the goal of normal BP.

# Rational es for Psychoph ysiol ogical T reatment

Developing effective treatments is easiest when the etiology of a disorder is simple and well understood. However, as we discussed earlier, there is no simple explanation of how hypertension develops or uniquely psychological factors that can “cause” high blood pressure. Psychological stress is widely considered to contribute to the development of essential hypertension, and the epidemiological evidence for the link between stress and high blood pressure is very convincing. But, the biopsychological pathway that would explain how stress can lead to disease is less clear, in good part because even the operational definition of “stress” is vague and somewhat contentious (Linden, 2005). Consistent with research that links the presence of quantifiable stressors to hypertension, psychological treatments are designed to reduce stress through one of two possible avenues. One approach is to emphasize arousal reduction and autonomic self-regulation training by means of relaxation training, meditation, autogenic training, and/or biofeedback—all of which are designed to improve autonomic functioning. A second approach is to conceive of stress as a multistep process involving triggers, emotional responses, coping behaviors, cognitions, and physiological stress responses (Linden, 2005). Treatment approaches using this second model of links between stress and BP target deficient cognitive and behavioral stress coping skills.

# Sel f‑Regul ation/Rel axation Treatments: Rational e and Methods

Practitioners use self-regulation training as unimodal treatment or in combination with biofeedback modalities in stepped psychophysiological approaches for treatment of essential hypertension. Although the relaxation therapies differ in instructions and processes, each shares a common goal of enhanced calmness and reduced sympathetic arousal. Frequently practiced types of relaxation/self-regulation methods include (Lehrer, Woolfolk, & Sime, 2007; Lehrer, 1996):

•	 Progressive relaxation   
•	 Autogenic therapy; modified autogenic therapy Hypnotic relaxation Transcendental meditation Benson's “relaxation response” Yogic meditation including deep breathing   
•	 Mindfulness meditation   
•	 Biofeedback

Researchers and practitioners use several different types of biofeedback in treating essential hypertension. These include thermal, direct BP, electromyographic, and/or electrodermal feedback. More recently, guided respiration and HRV biofeedback have been added to the arsenal.

Elaborate descriptions of each of these approaches would be redundant, because they have been described in detail by many previous writers (Lehrer et al., 2007) and elsewhere in this text, and because evidence for differential longterm outcomes in lowering BP with different types of biofeedback is sparse (Lehrer, 1996; Linden & Chambers, 1994). Similarly, the effects of selfregulation methods do not seem to vary in their effectiveness in a variety of anxiety-related problems (Grossman, Niemann, Schmidt, & Walach, 2004). However, as we will show below, the claim of equal effectiveness is not universally true for different modalities of biofeedback. It is likely that biofeedback and relaxation operate through somewhat different physiological mechanisms. On the one hand, relaxation may have a generalized effect in reducing sympathetic nerve activity that affects heart rate and thereby decreases stroke volume. On the other hand, biofeedback may work primarily through vasomotor control given that BP can increase and decrease (with biofeedback) without a concomitant change in heart rate. Thus, biofeedback and relaxation may actually be complementary rather than alternative methods in BP control.

# A Stress Reduction/Skills Buil ding App roach

The second approach, to conceive of stress as a multistep process involving triggers, coping behaviors, cognitions, and physiological stress responses (Linden, 2005), often requires the teaching of a broad array of problem-solving skills. This more complex approach precludes strict adherence to a manual. Research using this second strategic approach targets deficient cognitive and behavioral stress coping skills and requires more individually tailored multicomponent interventions, because the presumed critical skills deficits are not likely the same across all hypertensive patients, nor are patients’ stimulus environments presumed to be the same (Linden, 2006). The second approach also requires the therapist to have a higher level of skill and more training in psychological therapies.

# General Treatment T ips

Irrespective of one’s ultimate choice of technique (or combination of techniques), there are many commonalities in a patient-tailored, comprehensive approach to treatment. These features not only focus largely on not only patient education but they also pay attention to a sound sequencing approach to treatment (Linden, 2006).

# Patient Education

Health care professionals provide various types of patient education throughout all stages of treatment, and we have already described these general recommendations. Furthermore, during treatment, the therapist can provide the rationale for psychophysiological therapies as each procedure is introduced. One should also provide detailed information about each intervention, in lay terms, matched to the patient’s level of education and previous knowledge. Most patients comprehend the basic biology of BP and how specific biological, chemical, and psychological factors affect their BP. Practitioners can develop written materials or recommend relatively simple textbooks to enhance oral explanations, which are particularly useful in teaching groups of patients. Accessible and easyto-understand websites such as www.heart.org, sponsored by the American Heart Association, contain information for “healthy living,” as well as explanations of “conditions.”

# Clinically Meaningful Assessment of BP

We urge all clinicians working with BP problems to make it a habit to carefully monitor change in BP. Doing this well may involve some time and effort, but it provides important information for patients, enhances their motivation, and, of course, guides the actions of nonmedical therapists and patients’ physicians. As discussed earlier, exclusive reliance on BP measures taken in the physician’s office can lead to incorrect diagnoses in up to half of all cases (Selenta et al., 2000). The most promising avenue to help researchers and clinicians avoid the reliability and validity problems of office measures is via the use of ambulatory BP monitoring (ABPM) devices to obtain 24-hour BP averages in the natural environment. The advantages of ABPM include (1) much improved test–retest stability given the increased number of measures and wider sampling, and (2) a greater potential for differentiating true hypertensives from measurement-reactive patients, also known as “white coat” responders. Furthermore, no other current method identifies non-BP dippers. The use of ABPM for psychological interventions is still rare and this puts the evaluation of nondrug treatments at a significant disadvantage, as described below.

Linden has described the typical approach in his own practice under the title “hypertensionindividualized treatment” (HIT; Linden, 2006). This approach has been used in a randomized controlled trial and was shown to produce a large effect size relative to a no-treatment control (Linden, Lenz, & Con, 2001). Furthermore, a 6-month follow-up revealed that patients continued to improve even after the end of active treatment. The first treatment stage in HIT is a baseline period during which the therapist repeatedly monitors and records BPs. Decreases in office BP to normal levels do not always coincide with equal declines in home BP, so both office and home BPs should be available to the practitioner (Selenta et al., 2000). Although the mechanism underlying the reduction in BP with repeated monitoring is unknown, decreased anxiety or desensitization to BP measurements may be relevant factors (McGrady & Higgins, 1990).

During the intake session, the client and therapist discuss various life spheres (work, health, relationships, psychosocial variables) while the client is hooked up to a BP monitor. Only when therapist and patient have identified relevant stressors using a review of ABPM records and acute responses to therapy themes do they jointly devise a treatment strategy. For example, one patient may report a conflictual relationship and an inability to relax; this individual may then receive one form of relaxation training and possibly couple therapy. Furthermore, given the many effective choices for relaxation therapies, the therapist is likely to describe them to the client, both in terms of rationale and technique, to learn which method has the most credibility in the client’s eyes.

# Outcome

In this section, we show how (1) choice of treatment and (2) conceptual, (3) measurement, and (4) clinical trial design features affect observed outcomes for psychological interventions.

In our previous review of this literature (McGrady & Linden, 2003), we went into a considerable detail on individual studies. At the current time, there have been additional studies and meta-analytic reviews, including a white paper on outcomes endorsed by the Association for Applied Psychophysiology and Biofeedback (Devine & Reifschneider, 1995; Linden & Chambers; 1994; Linden & Moseley, 2006; Nakao, Yano, Nomura, & Kuboki, 2003; Rainforth et al., 2007; Yucha et al., 2001) revealing information about what works best. At this stage, lengthy descriptions of individual studies would likely confuse more than help. Where needed, researchers are invited to seek detailed clinical biofeedback protocols elsewhere (Lehrer et al., 2007; other chapters in this volume).

We are reporting here a description of available reviews with outcomes expressed as raw mean changes and as effect sizes (d) for (1) pre–post behavioral treatment, then (2) pre–post versus notreatment or waiting-list controls (see Table 23.2) and expanded by including pertinent data from the more recently published review by Rainforth et al. (2007). Positive signs associated with either means or effect sizes reflect increases in BP, and negative signs reflect improvements. Not all cells in Table 23.2 are filled, because the reported outcome parameters vary across different reviews, and there also is much variety in how authors clustered intervention types into supposedly similar groups.

Although Table 23.2 presents ample information and all other reviews were evaluated using the same reporting method, the data remain difficult to interpret. Nevertheless, imperfect standardization remains a problem, because each underlying review categorized treatments differently, used varying inclusion criteria, and reported outcomes differently (some in effect sizes, others in raw means; some reported pre–post changes, whereas others reported differences in outcome between experimental and control groups). The numbers in Table 23.2 clearly indicate that psychological interventions are quite variable in their potential to reduce BP and that some interventions are ineffective. The single most potent predictor of BP change after treatment is BP level at entry (Linden & Moseley, 2006; Nakao et al., 2003). This one study feature alone accounts for 40% of the variance in BP change. Many researchers do not pay attention to this or are handicapped by the fact that patients referred to the practice are those who are on medication and whose BP is at least somewhat under control, so there is not much room for improvement. The new category of prehypertension defined in the seventh JNC (2003) presents a new opportunity for intervention. Wang et al. (2010) reported on the effects of surface electromyographical (SEMG) biofeedback, abdominal breathing, and home practice in a group of 22 patients. Results showed clinically significant decreases in both SBP and DBP.

When observed treatment effects are aggregated into active psychophysiological treatments versus attention control, then we see fairly consistent small-to-moderate effect sizes. On the other hand, there is no consistent evidence that one active treatment is superior to another, although the data look promising for meditation (Rainforth et al., 2007) and multicomponent stress management approaches (Linden & Chambers, 1994; Linden et al., 2001).

TA BLE 23.2. Summaries of Results from Quantitative Reviews   
![](images/96574b8b621c4c1d6196943a0a71b01d7d8ca6e84605750bd4e988124f4f2700.jpg)
Note. Recreated with permission from Linden and Moseley (2006). For computation of effect sizes, we imputed standard deviations of 8 for SBP and DBP for those studies not reporting standard deviations. This number was derived from reviews of other reports for hypertension treatments that had reported standard deviations. \*p = statistically significant at less than .05.

The greatest variation in outcomes is found among “the family” of biofeedback interventions. All biofeedback shares a similar rationale, namely, that skill at autonomic self-regulation can be enhanced with the simultaneous feedback of a biological signal that reflects breathing, vascular flow, muscular relaxation, and/or signs of cardiac electrophysiology. Interestingly, the type of biofeedback that appears to target the intended endpoint most directly, namely BP biofeedback, is the least effective. Similarly, feedback of muscle tension, a likely indicator of stress responding, produces minimal benefit (Yucha et al., 2001). Thermal and electrodermal activity biofeedback appear to be the most effective types of biofeedback, which is intriguing, because neither approach directly teaches patients to change the ultimate endpoint, namely, BP. Relaxation and meditation effects also vary greatly across different reviews. At times meditation is reported to be more effective than relaxation (Eisenberg et al.,

1993; Rainforth et al., 2007), but the reverse is found elsewhere (Jacob, Chesney, Williams, Ding, & Shapiro, 1991). Hence, to date, there is no clear evidence that relaxation differs from meditation in its effect on BP, nor is there a consistent picture of the effects of the same method. This finding is congruent with the observed similarity in relaxation versus meditation outcomes for other endpoints such as stress or anxiety (Linden, 2005, p. 124). The fact that even the same technique does not consistently reveal the same outcomes is likely accounted for by variations in BP level at entry or other protocol differences.

Ultimately, in order to make clinical recommendations based on reviews, it will be important to separate conclusions about effectiveness of different methods relative to each other from absolute changes. We posit that the effect sizes and raw means for absolute change in BP are particularly important for clinical practice recommendations, and the observed mean changes are heavily affected by the nature of the clinical protocol used in these clinical trials.

On the other hand, critical protocol variations in measurement and levels of BP at treatment entry (further discussion below) have no known effect on the comparison of relative differences between various techniques, because almost all reported studies share the same weaknesses, namely, low pretreatment BP levels and a predominant choice of BP office measures. The ultimate impact of these protocol weaknesses is therefore a constant in these reviews.

# Innovative App roaches  to Biofeedback

Over the last decade, biofeedback clinicians and researchers have directed much of their attention to breathing training and the effect of breathing on overall heart function. Breathing training has long been a major ingredient to the practice of meditation and yoga. The importance of breathing training has been solidly backed by empirical research on breathing and cardiac interactions, revealing a high degree of interdependency (Maurya, Mahajan, Singh, & Jain, 2009). Based on this reasoning, a commercial company has developed and tested a paced breathing training device called RESPeRATE. To date there have been a number of case studies and uncontrolled evaluations, as well as three randomized controlled trials evaluating the effectiveness of the RESPeRATE device for lowering BP in hypertensive patients (Logtenberg, Kleefstra, Houweling, Groenier, & Bilo, 2007; Elliot et al., 2004; Grossman, Grossman, Schein, Zimlichman, & Gavish, 2001; Schein et al., 2001). Interpreted together, these studies reveal that device guided breathing reduced BP to a significant degree (i.e., change scores typically in the range of 3 to 8 mm Hg). These positive effects were very much tied to the dosage effects, such that more training and successful training to criterion also enhanced treatment benefits. On the other hand, this approach has not yet been evaluated using the “gold standard” of ABPM, nor is there evidence to date that the observed benefits are superior to other treatments that can be considered self-regulatory and that have been discussed in this chapter.

Other developments in the biofeedback literature have been to locate pathophysiological processes more directly and target these for change. Given the current strong belief that hypertension can be conceptualized as imbalance of sympathetic and parasympathetic activity (Sakakibara & Hayano, 1996; Sakakibara, Takeuchi, & Hayano; 1994; Brook & Julius, 2000), researchers have identified HRV as an important marker of such imbalance and have developed software and treatment protocols to provide patients with HRV biofeedback (Lehrer, 2007). As we show below, this approach is promising but insufficiently tested to date. Its rationale and methods are explained below.

SNS and PNS activation should be studied simultaneously; however, parasympathetic activity (which is best indexed via measurement of vagal tone) cannot be directly measured itself and has received limited attention because of this difficulty with quantification. This situation has changed with the arrival of sophisticated software for spectral frequency analyses of heart rate patterns that allow continuous displays of various spectral functions. This ease of in-depth measurement in turn permits trustworthy inferences about underlying vagal and sympathetic influences on the heart and BP control.

Online calculation (using spectral analysis) of HRV is now possible and offers displays of the relative dominance of different frequency bands within HRV. The first frequency band of interest is the “high-frequency band,” which is considered to reflect respiratory sinus arrhythmia (i.e., the influence of each breath on the heart rate). It falls between .15 to .40 Hz and is controlled by parasympathetic pathways to the sinoatrial node. The second band, labeled the “low-frequency band,”

covers a relatively narrow range of .08 to .11 Hz. The low-frequency (LF) band represents the influence of endogenous BP rhythms on the heart. A healthy cardiovascular system should show the compliance of heart rate with blood pressure changes. The third band/rhythm, the “very lowfrequency (VLF) band,” is considered to reflect the sympathetic pathways possibly involved with visceral and thermal regulation (covering frequencies below 0.8 Hz). This frequency appears to be quite sensitive to ruminative or worrisome thoughts. A healthy, relaxed HRV pattern therefore shows minimal activity in the VLF band with very high levels of activation in the LF band. We have observed in individual case studies that as clients follow the instruction to breathe more slowly and diaphragmatically, they show a systematic alteration in the spectral pattern of HRV, such that a high percentage of LF frequencies appear. This is an essential first step in HRV biofeedback.

The usual respiratory rhythm shifts start to line up with the baroreceptor rhythm at about .10 Hz (i.e., six breaths/minute). This pattern has been called a “resonant frequency,” which appears when the summation of these two phenomena occur. We have treated eight patients with HRV biofeedback, and all eight patients in our pilot study have learned to reach this pattern as early as Session 3 but not later than Session 6. When such a resonant frequency is reached, homeostatic reflexes, muscle recovery, cardiopulmonary function, and baroreceptor sensitivity are at their relatively highest functional levels. Clients report a subjective sensation of profound calmness and clarity (Linden, 2006).

This perhaps dry-sounding description can be best fleshed out by using single-case data from my own (W. Linden) laboratory, where I have worked with HRV biofeedback for a few years. For this case study, I documented BP changes occurring from (1) the beginning of the feedback session to (2) the actual attainment of the resonant frequency patterns to,(3) discussion of a stressful topic, then (4) reestablishment of resonant frequency (using an A-B-A-B design; see Table 23.3).

As the reader can see, the BP values of this patient revealed great variability. The rises and declines can be meaningfully interpreted as a function of the conversation topic, as well as whether she was in a resonant frequency phase. The corresponding BP values were 128/74 mm Hg, then 112/73 mm Hg; BP increased to 160/91 mm Hg during the stressful discussion and returned to 130/80 mm Hg during relaxation.

TA BLE 23.3. Case Study of Blood Pressure Changes in an HR V Biofeedback Session   
![](images/0f383d2449ada5f12d75f8c9d9f00231120c33684e558412ff6262b66537a91b.jpg)

Consistent with our own experience, Del Pozo, Gevirtz, Scher, and Guameri (2004) also reported that six to eight sessions are typically sufficient for clients systematically to change various dominant frequencies in the right direction and quickly reach a state in which they spend the majority of their time in the resonant frequency state. Interestingly, these clients also report that the HRV feedback allows them to become sensitive to increased levels of arousal outside of the research office, and that the self-regulation strategies acquired during biofeedback are helpful in their usual environment as well.

During the last 5 years, pilot treatment of eight patients with hypertension was completed using a six- to eight-session HRV biofeedback treatment protocol. The average 24-hour ambulatory BP reductions, pretest to posttest, were –8.7 mm Hg SBP and –6.0 mm Hg DBP, with effect sizes of d = –1.38 and –1.09. As the reader saw in descriptions of other biofeedback and self-regulation treatments, the HRV biofeedback outcomes compare very favorably with the observed effects of other treatments. Furthermore, it is noteworthy that these effects were seen on 24-hour ambulatory measures that are now considered the “gold standard” for trustworthy measurement.

# Special  Popul ations

A distinct population of individuals with BP problems is pregnant women. BP elevations may occur very quickly, and when they do, action is needed without delay. Blood pressure level in the second trimester is a significant predictor of birth complications, and about 8% of maternal mortality is associated with hypertension (preeclampsia; Allen, Joseph, Murphy, Magee, & Ohlsson, 2004).

Although antihypertensive medication is effective in lowering BP in the mothers, pharmacological therapy must be considered carefully, because many drugs pass directly through to the unborn and may be toxic (James & Nelson-Piercy, 2004). Traditional treatment for hypertension in pregnancy is bed rest and obstetrical monitoring. Frequently, women find compliance particularly difficult because they have other family obligations. Hence, bed rest is effective but very expensive for patients, and costly for the health care system.

In a clinical trial, women with pregnancyinduced hypertension (PIH) were divided into three groups (Somers, Gevirtz, Jasin, & Chin, 1989). The first group received standard medical care, whereas the second group was provided an additional 4 hours of education to increase compliance to bed rest. The active intervention group received treatment that comprised self-monitoring of blood pressure, thermal biofeedback-assisted relaxation, hand and foot warming, and diaphragmatic breathing. Total therapy time was also 4 hours. Instructions to participants were to practice the techniques twice daily at home in bed. Subjects in the biobehavioral group maintained their BP at a significantly lower level compared to the group on bed rest alone or the group with enhanced compliance to bed rest. Thus, biobehavioral intervention may be used as a low-risk adjunct to standard treatment in mild PIH (Somers et al., 1989).

Some population groups, namely, older adults and African Americans, have particularly high prevalence and incidence of hypertension but are often underrepresented in clinical trials. The good news is that different psychophysiological interventions have been tested and appear to be as effective for these populations as they are for younger patients and European Americans (Alexander et al., 1996; Barnes, Treiber, Turner, Davis, & Strong, 1999; Linden et al., 2001; Schneider et al., 2005).

2003), there also is ample opportunity for patient education and primary prevention. Suggested lifestyle changes benefit individuals at all stages of the disease. Aside from directly treating patients with the psychophysiological interventions described earlier, psychologists and other clinicians with behavioral science training can participate in implementing changes in eating, smoking, and exercise habits. There is extensive evidence from over 100 randomized controlled trials that behavioral treatments reduce BP to a modest degree and this change is greater than what is seen in waitinglist or other inactive controls. Mean changes and related effect sizes are quite variable, and therapists need to be aware of this variability to develop realistic goals for patients. The field of biofeedback for hypertension is continuously adding new, promising treatments to its repertoires. However, there continues to be a scarcity of protocols that properly control for floor effects, and potential measurement confounds (weak methods) jeopardize progress of the field and weaken its public image. The new classification of prehypertension may present a unique opportunity for patients in this category to utilize psychophysiological therapies as part of lifestyle modifications.

# Gl oss ary

Adrenal cortex. Outer region of each adrenal gland, which secretes aldosterone, cortisol, and androgens.

# Concl usion

Adrenal medulla. The medulla or inner core of the adrenal glands. Epinephrine (adrenaline) and a small amount of norepinephrine (noradrenaline) are secreted by the medulla.   
Aldosterone. Hormone secreted by the adrenal cortex that regulates balance of electrolytes.   
Aldosteronism. An abnormality of electrolyte metabolism caused by excessive secretion of aldosterone.   
Angiotensin II . Hormone that stimulates secretion of aldosterone from adrenal cortex. Promotes smooth muscle contraction.   
Angiotensin-converting enzyme inhibitors (ACE inhibitors). Drugs used to lower BP by decreasing vascular resistance. Example: enalapril (Vasotec).   
Autonomic nervous system (AN S). Part of the peripheral nervous system that consists of sympathetic and parasympathetic subdivisions. Innervates cardiac muscle, smooth muscle, and glands. (See Schwartz, Collura, Kamiya, & Schwartz, Chapter 1, for more on the ANS.)   
Beta-blockers. Drugs used to lower BP by decreasing cardiac output by adrenergic blockade at either beta

There is a very rich body of literature on the etiology and treatment of high BP (McGrady, 2003). The research spans traditional medicine and multidisciplinary, psycho-physiological studies. Treating patients with hypertension allows use of many treatment modalities that can be applied in isolation or in combination. Given the complex etiology of hypertension with synergistic impact of multiple risk factors (McGrady, Bourey, & Bailey, or beta2-adrenergic receptors, or at both. Example: propranolol (Inderal).

Calcium channel blockers. Drugs used to lower BP by decreasing vascular resistance. Example: verapamil (Calan).

Cardiac cycle. One contraction–relaxation cycle of the heart.

Cardiac output. Blood volume pumped by each ventricle per minute. The major natural glucocorticoid hormone released from the adrenal cortex. Regulates aspects of metabolism (see Thatcher, Chapter 7, this volume.)

Desensitization (systematic desensitization). A common type of behavior therapy to help patients decrease fear, phobias, and arousal responses associated with certain stimuli. There is gradual exposure for very limited periods from the least anxiety-producing to increasingly anxiety-producing stimuli. Exposure is imagined, with artificial stimuli (e.g., pictures, video), known as in vitro stimuli, and/or with real-life stimuli, known as in vivo stimuli. Example: gradually exposing a person to blood pressure instruments and procedures so the person can become accustomed to them.

Diastolic blood pressure (DBP). Minimum blood pressure during relaxation of the heart that occurs during the cardiac cycle.

Diuretics. Drugs that increase the excretion of urine thus decreasing plasma volume. Example: hydrochlorothiazide (Diazide).

Epinephrine. Hormone secreted by the adrenal medulla; involved in regulation of metabolism. Also a neurotransmitter.

Hemodynamic. Pertaining to the movements involved in the circulation of the blood.

Hg. Mercury; millimeters Hg (mm Hg) are units of pressure.

Mean arterial pressure (MAP). The average pressure during the cardiac cycle. It is calculated by adding diastolic pressure to one-third pulse pressure.

Metabolism. Total chemical reactions that occur in a living organism.

Neural. Having to do with nerve cells or nervous system. Noot related to nerve cells or the nervous system.

Norepinephrine (also noradrenaline). A natural neurohormone and neurotransmitter released by postganglionic adrenergic nerves and the adrenal medulla (see Thatcher, Chapter 7, this volume). Also found in the brain. One type of catecholamine, a body compound having a sympathomimetic action. Others include epinephrine and dopamine. Has mostly alpha-adrenergic activity and some beta-adrenergic

activity. Pharmaceutical name is norepinephrine bitartrate (also called levarterenol bitartrate).   
Parasympathetic. A subdivision of the ANS whose fibers originate from brainstem and sacral region of spinal cord.   
Plasma. Liquid portion of the blood.   
Renin. Enzyme secreted by the kidney that catalyzes formation of angiotensin; important in BP regulation.   
Sphygmomanometer. Device that consists of a cuff, which can be inflated, and a pressure gauge used for measuring blood pressure. In the aneroid type, the pressure gauge does not contain liquid. In the mercury type, the pressure gauge contains mercury.   
Stroke volume. Amount of blood ejected by a ventricle during one heartbeat.   
Sympathetic. Subdivision of the ANS whose fibers originate in the thoracic and lumbar regions of the spinal cord.   
Systolic blood pressure (SBP). Maximum arterial blood pressure during cardiac cycle.   
Total peripheral resistance (TPR). The sum of all resistances to blood flow in all the systemic blood vessels.   
Vascular. Pertaining to blood vessels or indicative of a copious blood supply.

# Diabetes Mellitus

Angele V . McGrady and D eloris M. Lakia

Diabetes mellitus (DM) comprises a group of metabolic disorders in which the foremost characteristic is elevated blood glucose (sugar). Deficiencies in insulin secretion and/or insulin action result in hyperglycemia (high blood glucose). Poorly controlled diabetes leads to serious acute and/or chronic complications (American Diabetes Association [ADA], 2015a).

Diabetes is reaching epidemic proportions in the United States, as well as worldwide. The global estimate for diabetes is an alarming 347 million people. In fact, the World Health Organization (2013) projects that diabetes will become the seventh leading cause of death worldwide by 2030. In the United States alone, diabetes affects an estimated 25.8 million people, or 8.3% of the U.S. population. Unfortunately, 7 million Americans are not yet aware of the diagnosis. By 2050, up to one in three U.S. adults may have diabetes provided that current trends continue (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention [CDC], 2011). Factors contributing to the increased incidence and prevalence of diabetes in the United States include the aging population (McBean, Shuling, Gilbertson, & Collins, 2004), the rising obesity rate (CDC, 2012), growth in high-risk minority populations (CDC, 2011) and the increase of Type 2 DM in youth (Mayer-Davis, 2008).

Regional variations are observed in the United States, with the highest incidence in the South, followed by the Northwest, West, and Midwest. Nine of the 10 states in the highest quartile of age-adjusted diabetes incidence are in the South and include Alabama, Florida, Georgia, Kentucky, Louisiana, South Carolina, Tennessee, Texas, and West Virginia. The other state in the highest quartile is Arizona (CDC, 2008).

# Pathoph ysiol ogy

Glucose is one of the products that result from the breakdown (metabolism) of ingested food, principally carbohydrates. Glucose is the body's main fuel source and typically the brain's only energy source. Blood glucose numbers are recorded as milligrams per decliter (mg/dl) or in millimoles per liter (mmol/l) (McGrady & Bailey, 2003).

The glucose level in the blood normally rises after a meal. This increase prompts the pancreas, an organ behind the stomach, to secrete the regulatory hormone called insulin. By combining with cell surface receptors, insulin increases cell entry and storage of fatty acids, amino acids, and glucose. The counterregulatory hormone, glucagon, is also released by the pancreas. Glucagon mobilizes glucose, fatty acids, and amino acids from storage sites into the blood when needed. Other counterregulatory hormones, namely epinephrine, growth hormone, and cortisol, are also important to maintain normal blood glucose between meals (McGrady & Bailey, 2003).

Amylin is a hormone that is co-secreted, along with insulin, by the beta cells of the pancreas in response to food intake. Amylin slows gastric emptying, suppresses the release of postprandial (after meal) glucagon secretion and promotes satiety. Amylin is considered to be a partner hormone with insulin. Patients with diabetes may have an absolute or relative deficiency of both insulin and amylin (Ryan, Briscoe, & Jobe, 2008).

The incretin hormones, glucagon-like peptide 1 (GLP-1) and glucose-dependent insulinotropic peptide (GIP), are gastrointestinal hormones that are also important in glucose homeostasis. GIP is secreted from K cells in the distal small intestine. Secretion of GIP is stimulated by food intake, primarily carbohydrates and fats. GLP-1 is secreted from L cells in the intestine. GLP-1 promotes glucose-dependent insulin secretion by direct activation of beta cell receptors. GLP-1 slows gastric emptying, suppresses inappropriate postmeal glucagon secretion and promotes satiety. The incretins are important in postprandial glucose regulation. The discovery of the neuroendocrine hormones, amylin and the incretins, have led to the development of novel therapeutic treatment modalities and away from an earlier insulinocentric view of glucose regulation (refer to Tables 24.1 and 24.2; see Crean & Maggs, 2006).

The capillary blood glucose level in people without diabetes is less than 100 mg/dl (5.6 mmol/l) preprandial (fasting) and less than 140 mg/dl (7.8 mmol/l) 2 hours postprandial (ADA, 2015a). In people with diabetes, either the pancreas is not able to secrete enough insulin or the cells are unable to use insulin efficiently, or both. In either case, hyperglycemia ensues because the cells are not able to use glucose for energy, and the liver continues to release glucose from glycogen stores or make more glucose from precursors (gluconeogenesis) (McGrady & Bailey, 2003).

# Diagnosis

Health care providers make the diagnosis of DM based on clinical presentation and laboratory results. A fasting (no caloric intake for at least 8 hours) plasma glucose concentration ≥ 126 mg/ dl (7.0 mmol/l) and/or a 2-hour plasma glucose ≥ 200mg/dl (11.1 mmol/l) during an oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT), or a random (any time of day, without regard to time from last meal) plasma glucose level ≤ 200 mg/dl (11.1 mmol/l), accompanied by signs and symptoms of diabetes, meet the diagnostic criteria for DM. If signs and symptoms are absent, two abnormal laboratory values need to be present on separate days to confirm the diagnosis (ADA, 2015a).

TA BLE 24.1. T ypes and Actions of Subcutaneous U-100 Insulin
