Note. Data from Lexicomp in Up-to-Date (2014); National Library of Medicine (2013); White and Campbell (2008).

TA BLE 24.2. N on-Insulin Injectables   
![](images/b67eca3e7dccef81a2d055e3100063858b13e1add21f8b638ee5b60297526e7d.jpg)
Note. Data from Lexicomp in Up-to-Date (2014); White and Campbell (2008).

Classic signs and symptoms of DM include polydipsia (excessive thirst), polyuria (excessive urination), and unexplained weight loss. Fasting plasma glucose concentrations of 100–125 mg/dl (5.6–6.9 mmol/l) constitute impaired fasting glucose (IFG). A 2-hour plasma glucose of 140–199 mg/dl (7.8– 11.0 mmol/l) represents impaired glucose tolerance (IGT). Patients with IFG or IGT are referred to as having prediabetes, which places them at high risk of developing diabetes in the future, unless lifestyle modifications are made (ADA, 2015a).

Hemoglobin A1c reports an estimated average blood glucose level during the previous 2–3 months. Historically considered the “gold standard” in the long-term monitoring of diabetes, hemoglobin A1c has also been endorsed as a suitable diagnostic test for the diagnosis of diabetes in most individuals. Hemoglobin A1c is more convenient for patients, because no fasting is required. A hemoglobin A1c result ≥ 6.5% represents diabetes. Individuals with hemoglobin A1c assays of 5.7–6.4% are considered to be at high risk for diabetes and should receive interventions with known efficacy, including weight reduction and exercise (ADA, 2015a).

pancreas makes minimal or no insulin; therefore patients with Type 1 DM are dependent on exogenous insulin to sustain life. Children and adolescents are most commonly affected, although it is important to remember that Type 1 DM can occur at any age. Patients most often present before the age of 30 with abrupt symptoms of hyperglycemia, including polydipsia, polyuria, polyphagia, weight loss, and, in severe cases, ketoacidosis and coma. About 5–10% of the population with diabetes has Type 1 DM (ADA, 2014a). The incidence of Type 1 DM is highest in non-Hispanic white youth (CDC, 2011).

Type 2 DM accounts for approximately 90–95% of the cases of diabetes (ADA, 2014a). Insulin secretion is variable and may either be reduced, normal, or even elevated, depending on disease progression. The onset of Type 2 DM is most commonly after age 30, with symptoms of hyperglycemia appearing gradually or even absent. Patients are typically obese and have a family history of the disorder. African Americans, Hispanics/ Latino Americans, Native Americans and some Asian Americans and Native Hawaiians or other Pacific Islanders are at higher risk for Type 2 DM than people of European descent (CDC, 2011). Although Type 2 DM has historically been thought of as a disease of adults, an increasing number of U.S. children and adolescents are being diagnosed with Type 2 DM. The increased incidence of Type 2 DM is highly correlated with the rise of obesity in this age group. Unhealthy lifestyles that include inactivity and overeating set the stage for Type 2 DM in youth (Mayer-Davis, 2008).

# Cl ass ification

Most cases of diabetes fall into two main categories referred to as Type 1 DM and Type 2 DM. Other common forms of diabetes include gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) and secondary DM. GDM is the onset of hyperglycemia during pregnancy. Secondary DM is diabetes that results from another condition, such as cystic fibrosis or consequent to the use of certain medications, including steroids, antipsychotics, and diuretics, among others (ADA, 2015b). The focus of this discussion is on Type 1 DM and Type 2 DM.

Type 1 DM is a result of an autoimmune process that destroys pancreatic islet cells. As a result, the

Insulin resistance is present in approximately 90% of patients with Type 2 DM (Haffner et al., 1999) and is linked with a collection of metabolic abnormalities known as the metabolic syndrome. In addition to hyperglycemia, characteristics of the metabolic syndrome include hypertension, dyslipidemia, central abdominal obesity, vascular inflammation, and hypercoagulation, among others. Treatment of Type 2 DM requires a multifactorial approach that treats the entire scope of metabolic abnormalities (ADA, 2014a, 2014b). Hyperglycemia may be controlled by oral medications or may require the administration of insulin in Type 2 DM.

# Traditional  Management

As yet there is no cure for either Type 1 or Type 2 DM. Diabetes however can be managed with a combination of pharmacological therapies and lifestyle modifications that include diet, physical activity, and stress management. Management of diabetes requires a lifelong commitment by patients, with daily attention to this chronic condition. Standards of Medical Care for Patients with Diabetes (ADA, 2015b) indicate that education for self-management is a necessary component of the overall management plan, and the individual’s role in caring for him- or herself needs to be addressed at the initial and ongoing care visits. Empowering patients with diabetes by providing information and teaching the necessary skills to promote effective self-care is best accomplished through problem-focused collaborative practice (Funnell & Anderson, 2003; Haas et al., 2014). Because of the complex nature of diabetes and the increasing specialization of health care workers, an interprofessional diabetes care team can best provide patient-centered comprehensive health care and education.

# Education

Patients have much to learn in order to effectively manage their own diabetes self-care. Patients and significant others need a basic understanding of the pathophysiology of diabetes and information about the various diabetes treatment options that are currently available. Educated consumers work best with health care providers to arrive at a mutually agreeable plan of care that yields optimum glucose control. Primarily, patients need to comprehend the specific instructions regarding their oral diabetes medication and/or be able to administer insulin to lower their blood glucose level. Selfmonitoring of blood glucose (SMBG) and learning to interpret results are essential.

Specific dietary principles must be understood and patterns of unhealthy eating identified. Practitioners and diabetes educators assist patients in this process and encourage adaptation of a healthier lifestyle that includes healthy meals and snacks, physical activity, and stress management strategies. The diabetes educator acts as a coach, assisting patients to develop lifestyle changes that promote improved glycemic control and achievement of an ideal body weight. Diabetes educators support the efforts of patients in establishing personal goals to achieve the desired lifestyle modifications. Setting both short-term and long-term goals helps patients to meet challenges successfully in reaching their desired self-management targets. At any given time, patients fall along a continuum of readiness to change lifestyle behaviors. Change is typically made slowly and in stages. Assessing the patient’s readiness to change and motivation is important in collaboratively establishing shortand long-term goals (Prochaska & Velicer, 1997).

Practitioners and diabetes educators also direct education toward the prevention, early recognition, and management of the acute and chronic complications of the disease. Patients are encouraged to utilize resources provided by the ADA and the American Association of Diabetes Educators (AADE) to support their efforts in the initiation and maintenance of healthy lifestyles. Consult the resources section at the end of the chapter for additional information.

# Adherence/Compliance

Health care professionals have begun to appreciate the failure of traditional adherence and compliance models of care in chronic disease states such as diabetes. Patients with diabetes fluctuate in their adherence to various self-management behaviors, so labeling a patient as compliant or noncompliant lacks accuracy. Additionally, both nonadherence and noncompliance carry a negative connotation, because both terms imply disobedience by the patient to orders given by the health care provider. A more beneficial approach to diabetes care places the patient in charge of his or her own diabetes, since the majority of day-today care decisions are made by the patient. Studies support the view that empowering patients to realize and draw on their own inherent capacity to master their disease improves overall glycemic control, as well as other patient outcomes (Anderson & Funnell, 2005; Funnel & Anderson, 2003; Funnell, Nwankwo, Gillard, Anderson & Tang, 2005).

# Pharmacotherapy

Medications are indicated along with prescribed lifestyle changes that promote optimal nutrition, activity, and stress reduction in the management of Type 1 and Type 2 DM. While research studies support pharmacological therapy in lowering hemoglobin Alc and in the prevention of microvascular (Diabetes Control and Complications Trial Research Group [DCCT], 1993) and macrovascular complications (U.K. Prospective Diabetes Study Group [UKPDS], 1998); taking medications remains a challenge for many patients with diabetes (Odegard & Capoccia, 2007). Furthermore, though many new pharmacological options are available to treat diabetes, over 43% of U.S. adults fall short of meeting the ADA glycemic goal of a hemoglobin Alc result under 7% (Hoerger, Segal, Gregg, & Saaddine, 2008).

# Insulin

Preparations vary in onset of action, peak, and duration. Insulin must be injected, because gastric secretions destroy insulin if taken orally. The subcutaneous tissue of the abdomen is the preferred location because absorption is most consistent. Other potential subcutaneous injection sites include the upper arms, anterior thighs, and buttocks. Rotation of injections within a location using a standardized rotation schedule is recommended. Random rotation between locations (for example: belly and thigh) is thought to lead to fluctuations in blood sugar and is discouraged because of different rates of absorbtion.

Insulin therapy is tailored to accommodate patients’ metabolic needs and lifestyle. Providers prescribe specific insulin(s), dose(s) and administration schedule(s) based on a patient’s weight, desired glycemic control, meal schedule, physical activity, general health state, as well as patient preference. A variety of insulin delivery systems are now available for patients to choose from, including the traditional vial and syringe, insulin pens, jet injectors, and continuous infusion pumps. The type of insulin and delivery system selected determines how soon glucose lowering begins, the peak time as well as the duration of the glucose lowering effect (refer to Table 24.1). Insulin dosing that most closely mimics normal physiological insulin release is the most likely to obtain the best glycemic control.

# Non‑Insulin Injectables

Pramlintide (Symlin) is a synthetic form of amylin (refer to earlier discussion in the section on pathophysiology). Pramlintide is administered by subcutaneous injection with mealtime insulin in the treatment of Type 1 DM. Pramlintide may also be administered in conjunction with oral agents and mealtime insulin in the treatment of Type 2 DM (see Table 24.2). When combined with insulin therapy, pramlintide lowers postprandial glucose levels in Type 1 and Type 2 DM (Ryan et al., 2008).

Exenatide (Byetta) is a synthetic version of a naturally occurring incretin mimetic, derived from the venom of a lizard found in the southwestern United States (refer to the previous discussion in the pathophysiology section). Exenatide is injected subcutaneously twice daily at mealtimes to treat Type 2 DM. It can be administered as a monotherapy or in conjunction with oral agents. Exenatide lowers postprandial and fasting glucose concentrations in Type 2 DM (see Table 24.2).

# Oral Glucose‑Lowering Agents

Diabetes pills or tablets are prescribed for patients having Type 2 DM who still make some insulin. The agents vary in action according to drug class (refer to Table 24.3) and consist of the following six classifications: (1) sulfonylureas, (2) biguanides, (3) thiazolidinediones, (4) alpha-glucosidase inhibitors, (5) meglitinides, and (6) dipeptidyl peptidase IV (DPP-IV) inhibitors.

Sulfonylureas lower blood glucose by stimulating the pancreas to secrete more insulin. The first drugs of this type (first generation) are rarely used today because of the frequency of side effects; therefore, they are not discussed in this update. The newer (second-generation) sulfonylureas are prescribed almost exclusively because of their higher safety profile. Like sulfonylureas, meglitinides also stimulate the pancreas to secrete more insulin; however, they do so only in the presence of high glucose levels. Biguanides improve glucose uptake in tissues by improving insulin resistance and reducing the amount of glucose released by the liver, thereby, lowering blood sugar.

Thiazolidinediones make tissue cells more sensitive to the effects of insulin, improving the effectiveness of a patient’s own insulin or insulin that is injected. Alpha-glucosidase inhibitors slow digestion of starches by blocking the enzyme needed to digest starch. DPP-IV inhibitors block the major enzyme responsible for binding and degrading the incretins, thereby increasing the physiological action of GIP and GLP-1.

It is important to understand that oral diabetes medications are not insulin; therefore, they are not indicated in the treatment of Type 1 DM.

TA BLE 24.3. Commonly Prescribed Oral Diabetes Medications   
![](images/3234c7ef08a25ff9746bee4a0beda95f1f7b7998d900b8fb712b474d7015df7d.jpg)
Note. Data from White and Campbell (2008).

Additionally, oral diabetes medications are generally contraindicated in acute illness and during hospitalization. Oral agents may be administered alone, in combination therapy (two medications in one tablet), and/or with other pharmaceutical treatment modalities.

# Glucose Monitoring

Although hemoglobin A1c is the preferred method of monitoring the effect of treatment changes on glycemic control over time, the results provide little information regarding daily glucose fluctuations. Because diabetes is mainly a self-managed disease, patient monitoring is a critical factor in promoting adequate glucose control.

# Self‑Monitoring of Blood Glucose

Home glucose monitors measure the concentration of glucose in plasma or whole blood and correlate within 20% of laboratory values. Advances in technology have led to new monitoring devices that significantly improve the ability of patients to manage their diabetes. Current blood glucose monitors are more accurate, faster, and require a smaller sample size than earlier monitors, enabling patients to make changes in medication titration, diet, and exercise in a more efficient manner. New models also offer greater flexibility in testing sites. Patients may test using palms, forearms, or thighs rather than only fingertips. The tailoring of frequency and timing of SMBG is based on individual glycemic goals and patient needs. Patients controlled with diet and exercise alone generally monitor less frequently than do patients on oral diabetes medications or injections of insulin, pramlintide, or exenatide. Multiple daily insulin injections (MDI) and insulin pump therapy may necessitate SMBG eight to 10 times per day.

Research findings are inconclusive regarding the value of SMBG in improving glycemic control. Studies support SMBG in Type 1 DM; however, results are ambiguous in Type 2 DM (McAndrew, Schneider, Burns, & Leventhal, 2007). Learning to perform correct glucose monitoring technique, and ways to problem-solve based on the results, improves the likelihood of patients making appropriate changes in self-care that will lead to improved blood glucose control.

# Continuous Glucose Monitoring

Continuous glucose monitoring (CGM) measures the glucose concentration of interstitial fluid, which highly correlates with plasma glucose concentrations. Through the use of a subcutaneous sensor that is attached to a monitor, continuous readings of a patient’s glucose concentration are available throughout the day and night. CGM is particularly helpful in patients having extreme variability in blood sugars and/or hypoglycemia unawareness. Patients, however, are advised to continue SMBG with a handheld glucose meter, because the continuous glucose monitor requires calibration, and SMBG results are needed to make immediate treatment changes (ADA, 2014b).

# Compl ications

Both acute and chronic complications can occur in persons with Type 1 and 2 DM. The acute complications discussed below are hypoglycemia, diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA), and hyperglycemic hyperosmolar nonketotic syndrome (HHNS). The chronic complications we discuss later are usually observed in patients who have had diabetes for many years, particularly if glycemic control has been compromised for extended periods. Years of poorly controlled diabetes can seriously affect any organ system of the body.

# Acute Complications

# Hypoglycemia

Hypoglycemia refers to a low blood glucose level below 70 mg/dl (or 3.85 mmol/l). Common causes of hypoglycemia can usually be discovered by looking backward through a patient’s SMBG log and commonly include too high a dose of insulin or oral glucose lowering agent, a delayed or skipped snack/meal, insufficient carbohydrate intake, more physical activity than usual, ethanol or drug ingestion, or a combination of these. Hypoglycemia can occur in either Type 1 or Type 2 DM, although it is more common in Type 1 DM. Older adults are especially at risk for hypoglycemia and incur higher morbidity and mortality as a result.

The initial warning signs and symptoms (autonomic nervous system symptoms) are those associated with increased sympathetic nervous system activity, including sweating, hunger, palpitations, anxiety, tremulousness, and paresthesias. However, symptoms of hypoglycemia are frequently distinctive and unique to an individual. The cerebral stage of hypoglycemia occurs as untreated hypoglycemia progresses and there is a severe lack of glucose supply to the brain, termed “neuroglycopenia.” Neuroglycopenic symptoms include difficulty thinking and concentrating, dizziness, warmth, weakness, fatigue, sleepiness, emotional lability, and slurred speech that is sometimes confused with inebriation. Inadequately treated hypoglycemia can progress to the point of unresponsiveness, seizures, and coma (Cryer, Davis, & Shamoon, 2003). About 40% of severe hypoglycemia occurs during the night (Diabetes Control and Complications Trial [DCCT] Research Group, 1993). Patients experiencing nocturnal hypoglycemia may report vivid dreams and/or nightmares. Advising patients to check blood glucose at 2:00 or 3:00 A.M. is likely to uncover nighttime episodes of hypoglycemia.

Persons with diabetes need to check their blood glucose level when they experience mild signs and symptoms of hypoglycemia. If blood glucose monitoring equipment is unavailable or it is impractical to test, patients must assume that the symptoms are the result of hypoglycemia and provide immediate self-treatment.

Treatment of hypoglycemia involves eating or drinking something containing carbohydrate to quickly raise the blood glucose level. The person should ingest 15–20 grams of a quick-acting carbohydrate. Examples of foods that can provide this amount of carbohydrate are 4 ounces of fruit juice, 4 ounces of a regular (not sugar-free) soft drink, 8 ounces of low-fat milk, a tablespoon of honey, six to eight Lifesavers, or three to four glucose tablets. High-fat foods, such as chocolate, are not recommended in the treatment of hypoglycemia. The fat blocks the absorption of available carbohydrate, leading to a slower response to treatment, and adds additional calories. After self-treatment, it may take 10–15 minutes for the person to feel better. Some people are frightened by hypoglycemia and overtreat themselves, causing rebound hyperglycemia. Blood glucose monitoring is recommended 15–20 minutes after treatment to verify that the blood glucose level has increased and returned to normal. If the next scheduled snack or meal is more than 30 minutes away, the person should eat a starch and protein (e.g., cheese and crackers) to prevent the blood glucose from going down again.

The threshold of plasma glucose at which patients experience signs and symptoms of hypoglycemia depends upon the glycemic control of the patient. Patients with higher hemoglobin A1c may perceive symptoms at blood glucose readings in the normal range (relative hypoglycemia), whereas patients with lower hemoglobin A1c due to intensive control may not recognize symptoms until much lower glucose readings are reached. Patients who report symptoms at blood glucose readings of greater than 70 mg/dl should be cautioned against overtreatment of symptoms. Treatment of relative hypoglycemia consists of ingesting 5 grams of rapid-acting carbohydrate (Guy, Sandoval, & Davis, 2006). For episodes of severe hypoglycemia (SH) in which the person is unresponsive and unable to swallow, one should give the person an injection of glucagon (see Table 24.4) to raise the blood glucose level. If glucagon is not available, enlist emergency medical assistance immediately.

Some individuals with diabetes may develop hypoglycemia unawareness (HU), losing the capacity to recognize early warning signs and symptoms of low blood sugar. This happens because of an absent or diminished counterregulatory hormone (epinephrine) response. An increase in the frequency of hypoglycemic episodes also contributes to decreased awareness. Frequently, the first symptoms experienced are the neuroglycopenic symptoms. Impaired thinking often occurs, making it difficult for the person to self-treat. As a result, patients may experience episodes of SH, requiring administration of a glucagon injection by someone other than the patient. Patients with Type 1 DM may experience HU as early as 1–5 years after diagnosis (Guy et al., 2006).

Patients who experience HU are encouraged to monitor their blood glucose levels regularly. They need to be instructed to treat themselves with glucose promptly if their blood glucose level is below 70 mg/dl, or if they recognize signs and symptoms of hypoglycemia. Family members need education about hypoglycemia and training to observe signs and symptoms of low glucose. A glucagon emergency kit is available by prescription and should be kept for home use. Patients experiencing SH requiring glucagon injection or emergency intervention should report the occurrence to their health care provider, because a change in the treatment plan is likely indicated.

# Diabetic Ketoacidosis

DKA is a serious, life-threatening complication resulting from profound insulin deficiency. DKA is most often a complication of Type 1 DM, but it can occur in Type 2 DM, although infrequently. Key features of DKA usually include a blood glucose level greater than 250 mg/dl (13.9 mmol/l) a positive test for ketones, dehydration, and electrolyte imbalance. The most common precipitating factor leading to DKA is infection. Other precipitating factors include acute illness, stroke, heart attack, alcohol abuse, pancreatitis, trauma, drugs, eating disorders, or inadequate insulin dose (Kitabchi & al., 2001).

# Hyperglycemic Hyperosmolar Nonketotic Syndrome

HHNS is the result of a relative lack of insulin and is more common in persons with Type 2 DM, especially older adults. HHNS is characterized by high (>600 mg/dl) blood glucose levels, severe dehydration, and neurological deficits. There is usually an absence of significant ketoacidosis (Umpierrez & Smiley, 2006). Both DKA and HHNS are medical emergencies that require emergency treatment in a hospital setting.

TA BLE 24.4. A dministration of Glucagon   
![](images/d473affd06ab121e248d64972a5d3d5c069e8d30f977b59ac514c2c20afd4f85.jpg)

# Chronic Complications

Persons with either Type 1 or Type 2 DM are at risk for developing chronic microvascular and neuropathic complications (see the following section). These serious health problems can develop in individuals who have had diabetes for years. However, it remains unclear why some people with diabetes develop them, whereas others do not. Nonetheless, recent research emphasizes the importance of good glycemic control and use of pharmacological therapy to prevent or delay the occurrence of complications.

# Landmark Studies

The Diabetes Control and Complications Trial (DCCT) and the U.K. Prospective Diabetes Study (UKPDS) were large-scale, multicenter trials that examined the effect of glycemic control on the long-term microvascular and neuropathic complications of diabetes. The DCCT (1993) enrolled more than 1400 patients with Type 1 DM, while the UKPDS (1998) studied the effect of glycemic control in over 5000 patients with newly diagnosed Type 2 DM. The striking findings in these two research studies conclusively determined that intensive treatment of hyperglycemia in Type 1 and Type 2 DM resulted in improvements in glycemic control, with significant reductions in retinopathy, nephropathy (DCCT, 1993) and neuropathy (DCCT, 1993; UKPDS, 1998). These studies led to a paradigm shift in the treatment of DM, moving away from acceptance of marginal glycemic control toward more aggressive treatment of hyperglycemia.

# Micro vascul ar  Complic ations

Complications involving the small blood vessels in the eye (retinopathy) and kidney (nephropathy) can occur as a result of poorly controlled DM. Damage occurs to the small blood vessels in the retina, causing leakage or bleeding in the eye. Damage to and hemorrhage of the fragile blood vessels of the eye, if not treated, may result in loss of vision. Patients should schedule at least an annual comprehensive eye examination with an ophthalmologist.

About 44% of patients starting dialysis have diabetes (CDC, 2011). Damaged filtering units of the kidneys called “glomeruli” can no longer adequately filter waste products, resulting in increased accumulation of these waste products in the blood. Modifiable risk factors of diabetic nephropathy include cigarette smoking, chronic high blood sugar, uncontrolled hypertension, and untreated hyperlipidemia. How long the patient has had diabetes and family history are also factors. Fortunately, the age-adjusted incidence rate of diabetic nephropathy in the United States continues to decline, probably due to increased and early screening, trends in more aggressive control of hyperglycemia, hypertension and hyperlipidemia, as well as the use of renoprotective medications such as angiotensin-converting enzyme (ACE) inhibitors/angiotensin receptor blockers (Gross et al., 2005; Kaufman, 2008).

# Macro vascul ar  Complic ations

Macrovascular complications of poorly controlled DM result in damage to the large blood vessels of the body. There are three major types of macrovascular disease: coronary artery disease (CAD), cerebrovascular disease (CVD), and peripheral vascular disease (PVD). Macrovascular disease occurs at an earlier age and with greater severity in people with diabetes than in those without diabetes (Kaufman, 2008). Stroke and death from heart disease occur two to four times more frequently in adults with diabetes than those without it (CDC, 2011).

# Diabetic Neurop athy (Problems with  Ner ve Function Due to  Diabetes )

About 60–70% of people with diabetes have mild to moderate nervous system damage (CDC, 2011). Prevention by optimization of blood glucose and lipids and smoking cessation is the most effective treatment of neuropathy, as there is no known cure. Improved glycemic control may help to slow the progression of neuropathy. Other interventions focus on relief of symptoms and improvement in functioning and quality of life. Major classifications of diabetic neuropathy include distal symmetrical polyneuropathy, or diabetic peripheral neuropathy (DPN), autonomic neuropathy and focal neuropathy (Kaufman, 2008).

DPN is the most common form of diabetic neuropathy. Sensory symptoms are most common and include numbness, burning, tingling, or crawling sensations of the hands and lower extremities. Patients may experience diminished pain and temperature sensation, placing them at risk for unrecognized trauma and accidental burns. Symptoms commonly occur in a “stocking–glove” pattern. Sometimes symptoms may be more understated, including feelings of numbness or coldness in feet. Occasionally patients are asymptomatic (Kaufman, 2008).

Autonomic neuropathy is damage to the autonomic nervous system as a result of uncontrolled DM. The autonomic nervous system is important in the regulation of the involuntary control of bodily functions. Examples include blood pressure, heart rate, and bowel and bladder function. Autonomic symptoms are typically restricted to one or two body systems. Resultant symptoms are reflective of the part of the autonomic nervous system that is affected. Examples include neurogenic bladder, sexual dysfunction, gastroparesis (delayed emptying of stomach contents), fecal incontinence and nocturnal diarrhea, orthostatic hypotension, painless heart attack, hypoglycemia unawareness, anhydrosis (lack of sweating), and abnormal pupillary dysfunction (Kaufman, 2008).

Focal neuropathy involves individual or multiple peripheral nerves of the body and occurs less frequently than DPN. Symptoms are acute and in general self-limiting. Episodes are unpredictable, not specific to diabetes, and not associated with duration of the disease. Focal neuropathies include mononeuropathy (single peripheral nerve), multiplex neuropathy (multiple peripheral nerves), cranial nerve neuropathy, plexopathy (e.g., brachial, lumbosacral plexuses and femoral nerve), or the nerve root neuropathy (radiculopathy). Diagnosis is made by the exclusion of other causes of neuropathy, such as nerve entrapment or compression. Treatment is usually palliative (Kaufman, 2008).

# Th e Rol e of Psychol ogical Stress  in D iabetes The Effects of Stress on Metabolism

Several models describe the impact of stress on metabolism as it relates to the etiology and chronicity of diabetes (Peyrot, McMurry, & Kruger, 1999; Rosmond, 2005; Surwit, Schneider, & Feinglos, 1992). Emotional distress or events perceived as challenging by the individual activate several physiological pathways that may result in excessive release of glucose into the blood or increased tissue resistance to insulin. Stimulation of the sympathetic nervous system releases glucagon from the pancreas and decreases secretion of insulin. Decreased parasympathetic (vagal) nerve activity also results in less available insulin. These autonomic imbalances result in lower blood levels of insulin, decreased entry of glucose to cells, and hyperglycemia. In addition, sympathetic stimulation causes the release of catecholamines (epinephrine and norepinephrine) from the adrenal medulla. A relationship exists between insulin resistance and sympathetic nerve activity in Type 2 DM. In an important and rare long-term study, 80 subjects were tested for sympathetic activity at baseline and 18 years later. Norepinephrine response to cold pressor test was found to be a positive predictor of future insulin resistance (Flaa, Aksnes, Kjeldsen, Eide, & Rostrup, 2008).

The other pathway activated during stress is via the anterior pituitary gland. It causes the adrenal cortex to release cortisol, and the pituitary to release growth hormone. It also causes the thyroid gland to release thyroxine, but this hormone is more relevant to long-term regulation than to acute variations in blood glucose. These hormones stimulate biochemical processes (glycogenolysis and gluconeogenesis) in the liver, resulting in increased blood glucose. Furthermore, the inability of tissues to use glucose increases hepatic and adipose tissue lipolysis (metabolism of fat for energy). Increased blood levels of fatty acids and ketones in the urine may result in hyperlipidemia and DKA (Ganong, 2005).

These models propose that psychological stress increases blood glucose through sympathetic and adrenal medullary pathways. Imbalance in the function of the hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal axis heightens the probability of accumulation of fat in the viscera, which increases insulin resistance (Björntorp & Rosmond, 1999). Riazi, Pickup, and Bradley (2004) showed that in a sample of 54 people with Type 1 diabetes studied over 21 days, 20% showed a positive relationship between blood glucose levels and stress on the same day that they identified hassles. Stress-reactive individuals demonstrated significant correlations between emotion-focused coping and blood glucose levels. The rest of the sample did not react with increased blood glucose levels on that same day or the next day.

Chronic severe stress is more likely to have a continuing disruptive effect on blood glucose, elevating average blood glucose levels and increasing their variability. Repeated stress over 5 months was associated with an increase in body fat around the abdominal region, elevated levels of insulin, hemoglobin Alc and triglycerides in 16 previously healthy young men (Branth et al., 2007). Whether stress is directly associated with the onset of Type 1 and Type 2 DM remains unclear. However, many individuals attempt to use food to reduce feelings of anxiety and distress. Since stress-induced overeating results in obesity, which is a risk factor for Type 2 DM, we can say that poor dietary habits are indirectly correlated with the onset of diabetes.

# The Effects of Stress on the Person with Diabetes

Psychological factors can impact the individual's ability to adjust to diabetes, whether the disease starts in adolescence or adulthood (Cox & GonderFrederick, 1989). Initially, there may be a sense of relief at the diagnosis, because the person was feeling very sick, weak, and tired without knowing why. However, many individuals go through a grief process after being told the diagnosis, because of the perceived loss of independence and loss of a sense of oneself as a healthy person. The diagnosis of diabetes in young adults is particularly traumatic because of the peer influences in that age group. In 92 participants with Type 2 DM, 35% reported depressive symptoms; mean hemoglobin A1c levels were higher in these participants compared to those without depression (Hislop, Fegan Schlaepai, Duck, & Yeap, 2008). Immediately after diagnosis, many patients feel overwhelmed by the amount of information they must absorb and what they need to do to implement and maintain glycemic control. Sometimes, many educational sessions are necessary before the person feels competent to manage his or her disease. Nonetheless, knowledge gained does not mean that the person has accepted the disease, will adhere to the treatment regimen, or achieve control. Diabetes is a chronic, lifelong illness, so adjustment to it is a continuing process. Worsening of the disease or development of complications presents further challenges to control and acceptance.

The diagnosis of diabetes and the resultant burden of the required self-management behaviors impact the quality of life in patients with Type 2 diabetes. For example, they may miss an insulin dose, overeat, skip a meal, and decrease the frequency of blood glucose monitoring. During times of frequent daily stress, some people with diabetes sacrifice their usual self-care activities and instead concentrate on managing the stress. This can lead to poor glycemic control. Forgetting or deliberately omitting insulin is a common precipitating cause of ketoacidosis requiring hospitalization (Umpierrez & Kitabchi, 2003). A person's perception of stress and its effects can strongly influence the neural and endocrine impact on blood glucose. In contrast, individuals who manage stress competently may show only minor, short-lasting blood glucose instability under stressful conditions. Patients treated with insulin are usually more distressed about their diabetes than those treated with oral medications or diet and exercise alone. Patients requiring insulin expressed a great deal of worry and anxiety about their disorder and what the future would be relative to complications of the illness (Delahanty et al., 2007). Psychological distress was also correlated to the necessity for multiple insulin shots per day (Hislop et al. 2008).

The number of stressful life events experienced by an individual has been correlated with Type 2 diabetes. In a study of 308 men and women employed in London civil service department, biological indicators of the metabolic syndrome and measures of daily stress were assessed. Risk for the metabolic syndrome was related to the degree of work stress. Employees who were chronically stressed at work were more than twice as likely to exhibit the syndrome as employees without frequent stress at work. The socioeconomic gradient and self-care behaviors also factored into the relationship (Chandola, Brunner, & Marmot, 2006).

Persons of low socioeconomic status are at higher risk for diabetes, partially due to the increased stress of a noisy environment, financial challenges, and crowded living conditions (Wamala, Wolk, & Orth-Gomer, 1997). Persons exposed to frequent or long-lasting stress may develop an attitude of hopelessness if they are unable to cope with the demands made upon them (Mooy, de Vries, Grootenhuis, Bouter, & Heine, 2000). In fact, depressive symptoms have a major impact on the daily lives of persons with diabetes, whether or not they meet full criteria for a diagnosis of a mood disorder (L. Fisher et al., 2007). The probability of clinical depression in patients with diabetes is twice that in patients without an active mood disorder (Anderson, Freedland, Clouse, & Lustman, 2001). Glycohemoglobin values were higher in those individuals (either Type 1 or Type 2 diabetes) with current mood or anxiety disorders (Talbot & Nouwen, 2000). In summary, psychophysiological and behavioral factors constantly interact in the person with diabetes. Stress can trigger unhealthy changes in behavior, mood disruption, and physiological responses that subsequently increase blood glucose, as well as impede self-management (Peyrot et al., 1999; E. B. Fisher, Thorpe, DeVellis, & DeVellis, 2007).

# Th e Team App roach In Us ing Biofeedback‑Ass isted Rel axation in Patients with D iabetes

The team approach is optimal for work with patients who have diabetes. Assessing the psychological and physiological effects of stress, stress management, and biofeedback on blood glucose control is best performed by providers with specific types of expertise. In addition to a medical endocrinologist, a nurse practitioner with special expertise in diabetes, the team comprises at the least an appropriately trained biofeedback practitioner, a certified diabetes educator, and the patient. The team members works together in evaluating the effects of treatment on the physiological and psychological aspects of glycemic control. The quality of the relationship between the team and the patient is critical for successful management. Good communication and trust between the patient and the provider are associated with better adherence and lower glycosylated hemoglobin levels (Ciechanowski, Katon, Russo, & Walker, 2001).

# The Biofeedback Practitioner

“Biofeedback practitioner” is a general term that includes professionals with varied qualifications and competencies in addition to biofeedback. For our discussion, we assume this is a properly qualified and credentialed professional who can conduct the psychological components of the evaluation and treatment. Most biofeedback practitioners do not have expertise in diabetes education and management. Thus, when these practitioners treat patients with diabetes, they need to know the basic pathophysiology of diabetes and the fundamentals of medical diabetes management. Many practices include a registered dietician as part of the team; this person reviews patients’ food diaries and makes recommendations about food choices and weight reduction as appropriate. In summary, the ideal team should involve a physician, nurse, a credentialed biofeedback provider, diabetes educator, dietician, psychologist or other mental health provider, and the patient.

# The Role of the Mental Health Practitioner Who Uses Biofeedback

The practitioner carries out an initial interview with the patient with diabetes to determine stressrelated physical and emotional symptoms. One assesses the patient's perception of the effects of stress on blood glucose, perceived capabilities, and management strategies (see Schwartz, Chapter 14, this volume). Psychological testing also may be used to assess the person's levels of depression, anxiety, and anger, the so-called “negative affective triad.” The diagnostic evaluation also establishes the presence of emotional symptoms specifically related to diabetes, as well as to comorbid illnesses. This practitioner also conducts a psychophysiological assessment to monitor several physiological parameters. These often include muscle tension, skin conductance, blood flow in the hands (via skin temperature), heart rate variability, and breathing. We recommend measurements of surface forehead muscle tension by surface electromyography (SEMG), heart rate variability, blood pressure, and finger temperature while patients sit quietly with their eyes closed, then under mental stress conditions, and finally while patients attempt to relax without instructions (see Arena & Schwartz, Chapter 8, this volume). The practitioner provides biofeedback, relaxation therapies, and stress management. Relaxation and biofeedback can help patients feel more in control of their physiological and psychological responses to stress and, by association, their diabetes. Furthermore, decreased plasma levels of stress hormones and sympathetic activity mediate lower arousal (McGrady, 2003) and result in less severe hyperglycemia.

The licensed mental health provider can also treat the co-morbid emotional illnesses with psychotherapy. In particular, cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) has been effective in improving mood and in facilitating self-management of blood glucose in persons with Type 2 diabetes (Lustman, Freedland, Griffith, & Clouse 1998) and Type 1 diabetes (Snoek et al., 2008). Based on recent treatment guidelines for depression, some patients may already be treated with antidepressants. Thus, the provider needs to be aware of the specific effects of psychotropic medications on blood glucose. A substantial literature exists on the hyperglycemic effects of classes of drugs in psychiatry. The provider is referred to several articles for this information, as the details are beyond the scope of this chapter (Andersohn, Schade, Suissa, & Garbe, 2009; Rubin et al., 2008; Zimmerman, Kraus, Himmerich, Schuld, & Polimacher, 2003).

# The Role of the Diabetes Educator

The diabetes educator, nurse, and physician can interpret blood glucose values because he or she understands the effects of hypoglycemic medications, diet, and exercise on blood glucose. In addition, the educator should consider collaborative goal setting with the patient to emphasize the importance of patient self-care (Anderson & Funnell, 2005). In Type 1 diabetes, structured diabetes education is associated with feelings of greater independence, better self-care, and improved blood glucose over the short and the long term (Howorka et al., 2000). The diabetes educator conducts a comprehensive patient assessment before beginning diabetes education. The following outline is useful in eliciting information that is important in establishing an education plan.

# History

1. Year of diagnosis and symptoms that led to the diagnosis.   
2. Family history of diabetes.   
3. Other medical problems.   
4. Use of prescription medication, nonprescription medication, and nutritional and herbal supplements.   
5. Self-concept/self-image relative to diabetes.   
6. Previous diabetes education.   
7. Problem solving.   
8. Risk reduction.   
9. Health literacy.   
10. Support systems.

# Medical Treatment Regimen for Diabetes, Knowledge, and Management

1. Medications

a. Injections: insulin, pramlintide, exenatide; type(s), source, frequency of injections, prescribed doses, injection sites, problems with preparing or giving injections, knowledge of onset, peak, and duration of action. Number of times per week patient does not administer injections.   
b. Oral glucose-lowering agents types, dosage, and knowledge of effects. Number of times per week patient does not administer oral medications for diabetes.   
2. Diet, usual caloric intake, restrictions, mealtimes, types and amounts of food, meal-planning skills, adherence problems, and eating disorders.   
3. Activity/exercise types, frequency, duration, impediments to activity.   
4. SMBG: type of blood glucose meter, frequency and time(s) of testing, memory capability, and any monitoring problems.   
5. Weight, height. Calculate the body mass index.

Acute/Chronic Complications of Diabetes, Knowledge, and Management

1. Hypoglycemia: frequency of episodes, signs and symptoms, and usual causes. a. Treatment(s): use of a glucagon emergency kit and availability of quick-acting carbohydrate source. b. Precautions: wearing a Medic-Alert bracelet or something similar and carrying a wallet card.   
2. Ketoacidosis and HHNS: number of episodes since diagnosis, cause(s) for episode(s), knowledge of testing of urine for ketones when ill.

# Education and Instruction

With the preceding information, one identifies the patient's knowledge, current self-management, self-care deficits and problems, and capabilities to make appropriate decisions and manage his or her disease. This information provides the basis for instructing the patient about diabetes care and addressing problems with daily management during later sessions.

# Us ing Biofeedback and R el axation w ith  Patients w ith D M

The reader is referred to McGrady and Bailey (2003) for a research summary, beginning with early research in the mid-70s related to relaxation and biofeedback in patients with diabetes. Herein, we reference selected researchers who have tested the effects of relaxation, biofeedback, and other mind–body therapies in Type 1 and Type 2 DM.

# Choice of Patients and Timing of Intervention

Psychosocial adjustment to both Type 1 and Type 2 DM often is problematic, as we discussed earlier. Therefore, counseling and supportive psychotherapy can be useful during the early weeks and months after diagnosis. A group-based program (Karlsen, Idsoe, Dirdal, Hanestead, & Bru, 2004) was shown to improve coping with diabetesrelated issues and to improve psychological wellbeing. Although there were no significant differences in hemoglobin Alc in the group provided with counseling compared to the control group, starting levels were not significantly elevated. Problem-solving approaches that were taught to patients included emotion-focused and problemfocused coping as applicable to specific situations relative to managing diabetes and living with this illness (Karlsen et al., 2004).

In contrast, beginning a rigorous biofeedbackassisted relaxation program may not be appropriate soon after diagnosis. The addition of clinic appointments for biofeedback, and the home practice requirements that are necessary to learn relaxation techniques, might overload the resources of the patient. Furthermore, it might be difficult to attribute improvement in glycemic control to biofeedback, because the patient is implementing multiple new behaviors concurrently. Another reason for deferring biofeedback during the first year after diagnosis of Type 1 DM is the so-called diabetic honeymoon period. This phenomenon is the partial or complete remission of the signs and symptoms of diabetes soon after the onset of Type 1 DM, when the pancreas temporarily produces insulin. The blood glucose level may stabilize at close to normal, and the need for exogenous insulin may decrease significantly or completely. This period may last one, several, or, rarely, 12 months (Abdul-Rasoul, Habib, & Al-Khouly, 2006). One might mistakenly attribute a decreased need for exogenous insulin to the biofeedback and stress management treatment instead of temporary pancreatic insulin production. When the honeymoon period ends and the patient's beta cells are no longer capable of producing insulin, the patient could misattribute the renewed need for exogenous insulin as a total failure of the self-regulation process.

Within a stepped-care model, the therapist would consider starting more conservative relaxation therapy within 12 months after diagnosis for selected patients. For example, one might start with relaxation instructions and printed patient educational materials about relaxation. The material should include information to help the patient avoid misattributions about blood glucose changes during the honeymoon period. Office-based biofeedback-assisted relaxation might begin after 1 year.

# Patients’ Acknowledged Impact of Stress

Patients must at least partially accept the idea that stress can have a negative impact on glycemic control. Increased average blood glucose, a wider range of values, an increase in fasting blood glucose, a higher insulin requirement, and sometimes more frequent hypoglycemia are common stress effects reported by patients. Patients may initially reject information about the correlation between stress and blood glucose because they feel guilty or feel they are being blamed for uncontrolled blood glucose. It is true that for some patients stress has less of an impact than diet or inactivity. In either case, practitioners are advised to assist patients in understanding the effects of stress on physiological function in general and on blood glucose in particular. The patient needs to be educated about stress and its relationship to blood glucose in order to improve awareness of the effects of daily hassles. The likelihood of success of psychophysiological therapy increases when the patient understands the negative effects of stress and the positive effects of stress management as adjuncts to standard care in normalizing blood glucose levels.

# Cooperation with the Patient's Physician or Nurse Practitioner

The primary medical provider must approve the referral for biofeedback and should agree to be available for consultation as needed. The practitioner should document the patient's glucose data before, during, and after a biofeedback-assisted relaxation treatment program. He or she should share this with the patient's medical provider during and after treatment. This person should also disclose pertinent data about the patient’s other medical problems, particularly those related with diabetes, such as hypertension and hyperlipidemia, and the current therapy for those disorders.

The primary medical provider gives the patient guidelines to adjust his or her daily insulin dosage depending on his or her blood glucose values. The patient can adjust the dosage when the blood glucose level exceeds an acceptable level using a predefined algorithm. Dosage reduction of insulin or oral hypoglycemic agents may become necessary as a patient makes progress in psychophysiological therapy. Equally important, patients with severe and unresolved glycemic control problems should be instructed to contact their physician or nurse practitioner for guidance and possible changes in their medical treatment regimen.

# Adherence to the Prescribed Medical Regimen

Patients who consistently do not comply with recommendations for diet, prescribed glucose-lowering medication, and/or blood glucose monitoring are not likely to be good candidates for biofeedback-assisted relaxation. They probably will not comply with relaxation practice assignments. However, when adherence problems are identified, motivational interviewing may be used to promote patient understanding of the benefits of controlled blood glucose and foster ownership of treatment goals (Miller & Rollnick, 2002). In the research setting, improved adherence to the medical regimen during psychophysiological therapy undoubtedly results in better glucose control, but it also confounds interpretation of the specific effects of biofeedback treatment on blood glucose.

# Physical Setting

An office where practitioners treat patients with diabetes should contain a meter to test for blood glucose, and the necessary strips and lancets. The office should have sources of quick-acting carbohydrates. Examples are fruit juice, glucose tablets, and regular (not sugar-free) soft drinks. The equipment to download and calculate average values is helpful but not necessary. A diabetes manual such as Therapy for Diabetes Mellitus and Related Disorders (Lebovitz, 2009) is useful. Many offices now have a monitor for glycohemoglobin, which derives the average blood glucose for the past few months using blood obtained from a fingerstick.

# Evaluation of Outcome

The primary treatment outcome measure is hemoglobin A1c. We use the following indicators of progress:

1. Hemoglobin A1c (the average blood glucose for the past 2–3 months)   
2. Average blood glucose (calculated from about 50 measurements)   
3. Percentage of the values above 200 mg/dl   
4. Percentage of morning (fasting) values between 80 and 120 mg/dl (more important in Type 1 DM than in Type 2 DM)   
5. Number of hypoglycemic episodes (or values ≤ 50 mg/dl)

A secondary outcome variable is dosage of blood glucose-lowering medication. Compare the number of tablets of oral glucose-lowering medication or number of units of rapid, intermediate and long-acting insulin used before and after therapy.

# General Cl inical  Procedures

The goals of biofeedback-assisted relaxation are as follows:

1. Increase the person's ability to relax and effectively manage stress.   
2. Demonstrate control of the physiological variable being monitored (SEMG, temperature, heart rate variability) and the effects of increasing control on blood glucose.   
3. Decrease the stress-mediated neural and endocrine systems' effects on blood glucose and insulin utilization.   
4. Reduce glycosylated hemoglobin, average blood glucose; increase the percentage of fasting blood glucose values at target range.   
5. Reduce dosage of hypoglycemic medication when blood glucose levels are well controlled.

# Baseline: Blood Glucose Monitoring

Practitioners must have sufficient data to establish the baseline before starting biofeedback for patients with Type 1 or Type 2 DM. We suggest at least 2 weeks of blood glucose data and one recent hemoglobin A1c value. Morning and evening values are minimum requirements. For patients with Type 1 or Type 2 DM who are on insulin therapy, the recommendation is four times per day. The ideal is fasting, before each meal, 2 hours after meals, bedtime and a 2:00 or 3:00 A.M. check once or twice a week. Most meters contain a memory feature and provide 14- and 30-day averages. Gross errors in self-reported blood glucose occur less frequently now than in the past (Mazze et al., 1984). Because some patients wish to provide “good data” for the provider, one must review their blood glucose data carefully, and with sensitivity and consideration of their efforts in maintaining good glycemic control. Encourage a positive attitude toward good self-management. It also may be helpful for patients to record daily stressful events to identify any correlations between stress and variations in blood glucose. This exercise also facilitates later stress management or counseling interventions.

One must check body weight, particularly in Type 2 DM persons who are overweight and on psychotropic medication, at the beginning of treatment and at regular intervals. Weight loss and accompanying decreases in blood glucose level may require adjustment of the medication dose. Furthermore, when significant weight loss occurs during treatment, one cannot attribute decreases in blood glucose solely to relaxation or biofeedback therapies. We recommend weekly weights for patients with Type 2 DM, and pretreatment and end-of-treatment weights for patients with Type 1 DM.

# Relaxation Therapy

Relaxation therapies involve slow, diaphragmatic breathing (see Smith, Chapter 12, this volume), meditation, autogenic phrases, and/or progressive muscle relaxation (Davis, Eshelman, & McKay, 2008), all of which have been implemented in patients with diabetes. We recommend that the person's blood glucose be measured before and after at least the first relaxation session. In our program, most sessions include instruction and practice of progressive and/or passive relaxation. Providers emphasize the importance of daily practice of relaxation and the eventual application of relaxation to managing stress. It is best to avoid office sessions at times of low blood glucose or within an hour after strenuous exercise. Do not proceed with a relaxation or biofeedback session if the patient is experiencing hypoglycemic symptoms, because relaxation may lower glucose levels even more. It is prudent to wait until symptoms have resolved, and it has been confirmed (by measurement) that the blood glucose levels have risen above the hypoglycemic level before continuing with the session. Several examples of the use of different types of relaxation therapy and stress management incorporating relaxation follow.

Progressive relaxation was compared to a standardized physical exercise program in black females with Type 2 DM. Both groups received education. After 12 weeks, subjects in the relaxation group sustained a very similar decrease in hemoglobin A1c compared to the exercise group (Van Rooijen, Rheeder, Eales, & Becker, 2004). The effects of 16 weeks of Transcendental Meditation (TM) were compared to a health education active control in patients with coronary heart disease and the metabolic syndrome. The TM group had significantly lower systolic blood pressure and insulin resistance, and increased heart rate variability (particularly high-frequency power) in contrast to the control patients. Repeated practice of TM was associated with reduction in multiple physiological responses to stress (Paul-Labrador et al., 2006).

The effects of stress management on blood glucose control (indicated by hemoglobin A1c) in patients with Type 1 DM were investigated. After 3 months, there was a significant improvement in coping scores and in hemoglobin A1c in the intervention group in comparison to the control group (Attari, Sartippour, Amini, & Haghighi, 2006). Methods of coping in specific populations may be more important than first realized. In a study of 185 African Americans with Type 2 DM, active problem-solving coping styles were associated with fewer negative appraisals and better self-efficacy (Samuel-Hodge, Watkins, Rowell, & Hooten, 2008). A 31-week intervention comprising three home visits was provided to subjects with Type 2 DM. The intervention comprised stress management, psychological counseling, and relaxation. Marked improvement in blood glucose control, blood pressure, and sense of well-being were reported by subjects receiving the intervention. Subjects with higher levels of hemoglobin A1c at the onset responded more dramatically, but all groups showed some improvements (Krook, Holm, Pettersson, & Wallberg-Henriksson, 2003). Surwit et al. (2002) were the first to show that group-based stress management improves glycohemoglobin values over a 1-year period. The reductions were statistically significant but of a small magnitude.

# Biofeedback

The type of biofeedback that is used depends on the psychophysiological profile conducted during the first session and patient preference. Literature to date supports the use of SEMG and thermal biofeedback in patients with diabetes, but the effects of heart rate variability feedback or neurofeedback are unexplored at this time.

In our clinic, we offer therapy encompassing ten 1-hour sessions. These consist of counseling for 15 minutes, a short baseline, 15 minutes of biofeedback with relaxation, 5 minutes of debriefing, and 15–20 minutes of reviewing blood glucose logs. A description of key studies of different types of biofeedback follows.

McGinnis, McGrady, Cox, and Grower-Dowling (2005) studied the effects of 10 sessions of biofeedback and relaxation therapy in patients with Type 2 DM and concurrent depression. Progressive and passive relaxation were used, in addition to EMG and thermal biofeedback. Patients with depression tended to discontinue participation due to the demands of the research study, which included frequent blood samples and daily practice of relaxation. The intervention was associated with significant decreases in hemoglobin A1c in the nondepressed group; the changes were stable at 3 months. These findings indicate that depressive symptoms should be addressed before beginning biofeedback-assisted relaxation, because low mood affects ability to adhere to provider recommendations. In earlier studies, focused on SEMG and thermal biofeedback in patients with Type 1 DM, reductions in average blood glucose were encouraging (McGrady & Horner, 1999).

Thermal biofeedback can be used with most patients with diabetes, despite concerns about diabetic neuropathy, because loss of thermal sensation in the hands is uncommon and occurs late in the progression of neuropathy. Patients with diabetes can utilize biofeedback to improve circulation to the hands and the feet (Rice, 2007; Galper, Taylor, & Cox, 2003). In patients with this disorder, the temperature of the feet is particularly important, because this area tends to be most compromised by diabetic neuropathy. Rice (2007) recommends a single session of teaching and continued home practice with a small, inexpensive handheld thermometer (e.g., alcohol in glass). Decreases in neuropathic pain are a result of the improved peripheral blood flow. More severe lower extremity sympathetic autonomic and sensory neuropathy does interfere with the ability to warm the feet (Fiero, Galper, Cox, Phillips, & Fryburg, 2003).

# Home Practice of Relaxation

We recommend that patients begin with practice of relaxation for 15–20 minutes once daily, then, gradually increase to practice sessions twice daily (McGrady & Bailey, 2003). The importance of practice is explained to patients by drawing parallels to regular physical exercise to maintain conditioning. In order to be able to use mental skills, one must practice them to build up tolerance to stress and to feel proficient. The biofeedback practitioner, the diabetes educator, and the patient determine the optimal time(s) for practice. This is important, so that relaxation practice does not coincide with times of peak insulin action and increase the risk for hypoglycemia. Avoid relaxation practice after 9:00 P.M. in patients with Type 1 DM to prevent potential exacerbation of nighttime hypoglycemia (Bendtson, Gade, Thomsen,

Rosenfalck, & Wildschiodtz, 1992). Patients with Type 2 DM are at significantly lower risk for nighttime hypoglycemia and can safely use relaxation techniques at bedtime.

At about the midtreatment point, we recommend starting 30- to 60-second mini-relaxation periods during the day. The person with diabetes may use the mini-relaxation periods, such as deep breathing when monitoring his or her blood glucose. Between the application of blood on the test strip in the glucometer and the display of the result, there is usually a brief waiting period. The suggestion to use this time for a mini-relaxation is usually very well accepted by patients. Patients with Type 1 DM can also use mini-relaxation after 9:00 P.M.

# Adjunctive Therapies to Relaxation and Biofeedback

Adjunctive interventions to relaxation and biofeedback in patients with diabetes include CBT, acceptance and commitment therapy (ACT), and yoga. Several examples follow. Training in yoga postures and deep breathing was provided over a 40-day period for 24 subjects with Type 2 diabetes. Significant decreases in blood pressure and hemoglobin A1c were observed. Postprandial (after meals) blood glucose levels also decreased significantly after completion of the yoga regimen (Singh, Malhotra, Singh, Madhu, & Tandon, 2004).

CBT had a positive impact on hemoglobin Alc in patients with high baseline depression scores. The benefits lasted up to 1 year after the intervention. Neither blood glucose awareness training nor CBT had significant effects on the hemoglobin Alc in the entire population, only in the group with elevated baseline depression (Snoek et al., 2008). Thus one can recommend the addition of CBT to a biofeedback/relaxation regimen for patients with diabetes to facilitate positive attitudes to treatment. Eighty-one subjects attended a 1-day educational workshop and following this were randomly assigned to continue education alone or to combine education with ACT, which is a type of psychotherapy that emphasizes mindfulness, actions based on personal values, and acceptance of the moment (Hayes, Strosahl, & Wilson, 2003). Those in the ACT group were able to utilize the coping strategies to manage their diabetes better and were more likely to have hemoglobin A1c values in the target range (Gregg et al., 2007).

Practitioners of biofeedback and stress management may be contacted by individuals who are at risk for development of diabetes and cardiovascular disease, because of the rising prevalence of obesity in the population. In an interesting article, Bijlani et al. (2005) tested the effects of an intervention based on yoga that included yoga postures, breathing exercises, and relaxation in the individuals on Days 1 and 10. None of the subjects had been diagnosed with diabetes, but results showed improvement in all of the physiological factors associated with risk for diabetes (Bijlani et al., 2005).

# Long‑Term Follow‑Up

There are no follow-up studies lasting longer than 1 year of biofeedback or stress management with diabetic populations. However, we suggest periodic follow-up sessions, as is common practice when treating other chronic disorders. The practitioner and the patient determine the timing of the followup office sessions. At follow-up, glycemic control, self-care, and use of relaxation techniques are evaluated. Reinforcement for continued regular relaxation and positive coping is given, and patients are encouraged to remain in contact with the provider.

# Facil itating Referrals for Biofeedback in Patients w ith D M

Many practitioners and researchers work in large hospitals or medical clinics that treat many patients with DM. Professionals in other settings can locate patients who may benefit from biofeedback-assisted relaxation treatments through various sources. Local hospitals may have diabetes care centers. Patient support groups are common. The local office of the ADA may print a monthly newsletter and hold regular meetings for people with Type 1 or Type 2 DM. For information about basic and clinical research in diabetes, several professional journals are good sources of information. Examples are Diabetes Care, The Diabetes Educator, and Practical Diabetology. Diabetes Self-Management and The Diabetes Forecast are useful lay publications for patients.

# Poss ibl e Contraindications  to T reatment

The following concerns were brought to our attention and require comment.

1. Patients can misinterpret hypoglycemia as anxiety. This can happen because many of the symptoms associated with hypoglycemia, such as sweating, cold hands, hyperalertness, and tachycardia, are also characteristic of an anxiety reaction. Indeed, hypoglycemic and stress symptoms are both caused by excessive sympathetic, adrenal medullary, and adrenal cortical activity. But in the latter case, release of epinephrine and glucagon are critical to compensate for hypoglycemia (Ganong, 2005). If the person is experiencing hypoglycemia but interprets it as anxiety, his or her blood glucose could continue to decrease to dangerous levels while he or she attempts to “relax away” the symptoms. Therefore, patients need to be instructed in the appropriate response to the symptoms of hypoglycemia. First, test blood glucose to check for low blood sugar. Then treat appropriately with a quick-acting source of carbohydrate, followed by retesting. Relaxation will not stop the symptoms of hypoglycemia.

2. People with diabetes use hyperventilation to counteract acidosis; therefore, do not teach them deep breathing. This is not true. Acidosis stimulates hyperventilation. In persons with and without diabetes, the respiratory system attempts to compensate for shifts in blood acidity (see Gevirtz, Schwartz, & Lehrer, Chapter 5, this volume; Fried, 1993; Ganong, 2005; Timmons & Ley, 1994). People with diabetes may automatically hyperventilate in an attempt to reregulate blood acidity through respiratory compensation. However, instructions or training to breathe slowly and deeply with the diaphragm cannot override the strong signals from the brain's respiratory center. If the patient is in acidosis, attempts at voluntary control of breathing are overridden quickly. Patients should also test their urine for ketones to distinguish between acidosis and hyperventilation secondary to anxiety. Question patients about history of ketoacidosis and assess hyperventilation (see Gevirtz, Schwartz, & Lehrer, Chapter 5, this volume). Observe and measure breathing during initial baseline assessment and when patients practice abdominal breathing.

3. People with diabetes and depression may experience increased blood glucose and weight gain; therefore, biofeedback-assisted relaxation programs will not be effective. Whether this is true is unknown. Diabetic patients with clinical depression can benefit from biofeedback-assisted relaxation programs (McGinnis et al., 2005), but their depressive symptoms cannot be overlooked. The serotonin reuptake inhibitors seem to have an independent blood glucose lowering effect (Goodnick, Henry, & Buki, 1995). But as depression treatment regimens have become more complicated, assessment of all the medicines by a physician may be necessary.

4. Deep relaxation has a tendency to lower blood glucose to hypoglycemic levels. This is as unlikely as the chance of syncope after relaxation in a person with normal blood pressure. The risks of using biofeedback-assisted relaxation in Type 1 and Type 2 DM are low if SMBG is in place, and if a trained professional is available to interpret those glucose records. The practitioner should be aware of the possibility of hypoglycemia if patients are not maintaining proper nutrition, or are exercising excessively and not eating additional food to compensate for the synergistic effect of exercise and insulin. Patients with either type of diabetes who are not monitoring blood glucose and medication data are not good candidates for biofeedback or relaxation therapy.

# Concl usion

Research on biofeedback, relaxation, and stress management as adjunctive treatments for persons with DM is encouraging. The theoretical framework underlying stress-induced increases in blood glucose and stress-reduction-mediated decreases supports continued trials of biofeedback. However, treatment of these patients using behavioral therapies is often complicated by comorbid psychiatric disorders. Further controlled trials of biofeedback in diabetic patients in which mood and anxiety disorders are identified and concurrently treated should help to clarify the efficacy controversy. Recent advances in knowledge of the immune aspects of diabetes may further the understanding of psychoneuroimmunological factors in the regulation of blood glucose. Rapid improvements in technology allow patients with diabetes and their health care providers to anticipate fewer invasive procedures, better control, and a more normal lives.

Learning relaxation techniques with biofeedback may be a valuable adjunct to usual medical care of this chronic illness. Certainly, the adjunctive techniques cannot substitute for diet, activity, hypoglycemic medication, or blood glucose monitoring. However, patients with diabetes can achieve better self-care by learning psychophysiological self-regulation, including various types of stress management. Patients frequently report a greater sense of control as a result of biofeedback, which in turn facilitates a sense of empowerment. Because self-care is indeed the cornerstone of glycemic control, the self-regulatory therapies, such as relaxation with biofeedback, are logical components of overall management of diabetes. More study is necessary to determine ideal selection of patients, and the most efficacious relaxation and biofeedback procedures.

Gl oss ary   
Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA). An acute complication of diabetes caused by a deficiency of insulin, resulting in hyperglycemia, ketosis, acidosis, dehydration, and electrolyte imbalance.   
Hemoglobin A1c. A laboratory blood test measuring the percentage or fraction of hemoglobin that has glucose attached. The test is used to evaluate glycemic control for the preceding 2–3 months.   
Honeymoon period. A phenomenon that may occur shortly after the onset of Type 1 DM, in which there is partial or complete remission of signs and symptoms of the disease. It may last up to 12 months.   
Hypoglycemia. An acute complication of diabetes in which the blood glucose level is low (<70 mg/dl). This can result from an imbalance of activity, food intake, and/or hypoglycemic medication.   
Hypoglycemia unawareness. Lack of recognition of the early warning signs and symptoms of low blood sugar. This may result from an absent and/or diminished counterregulatory hormone response. The person may experience episodes of severe hypoglycemia requiring the use of glucagon and/or emergency medical treatment.   
Insulin resistance. A condition in which glucose is unable to enter cells because there are fewer insulin receptors on the cell surface. Combination between insulin and these receptors is necessary for glucose entry.   
Ketones. Chemicals formed during the metabolism of fat. Normal blood and urine concentration is low. Excessive ketones may accompany insulin deficiency in Type 1 DM.   
Macrovascular complications. Damage to larger blood vessels of the body involving the coronary arteries, cerebral arteries, and peripheral blood vessels.   
Neuropathy. Damage to nerve cells of the body that results from diabetes.   
Polyphagia. Excessive hunger.   
Polyuria. Excessive urination.

# Resources

American Diabetes Association (ADA) www.diabetes.org

National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases (NIDDK)   
www.niddk.nih.gov

Joslin Diabetes Center www.joslin.harvard.edu

The American Association of Diabetes Educators (AADE) can be contacted at 1-800-TEAM-UP-4 to obtain a list of educators in each state. In addition, the ADA can provide a list of education programs (e.g., inpatient, outpatient, physician offices, clinics) that have met the National Standards for Diabetes Self-Management Education Programs. This information can be obtained from the ADA website or through one of its publications, The Diabetes Forecast.
