# Fibromyalgia Syndrome

Peter T . D orsher and Mark S. Schwartz

In 1852, Virchow first described in Western medicine a clinical syndrome of widespread muscular pain that he termed “muscular rheumatism,” and approximately five decades later, Gowers (1904) termed this condition “fibrositis.” Though a variety of other terms for the clinical phenomenon of widespread muscular pain and tenderness have been offered since that time, the American College of Rheumatology (Wolfe et al., 1990) recommended unifying these diagnoses into a single diagnosis: fibromyalgia (fibro for fibrous tissue such as tendon and ligament, myo for muscle, and algia for pain). Fibromyalgia is not a disease but a “syndrome” (a constellation of clinical signs and symptoms with no identifiable cause). Symptoms associated with fibromyalgia include pain in 100% of patients, fatigue in 96%, insomnia in 86%, joint pain in 72%, headaches in 60%, restless legs in 56%, paresthesias in 52%, impaired memory in 46%, leg cramps in 42%, impaired concentration in 41%, nervousness in 32%, and depression in 20% (Wolfe et al., 1990). Inherent in the term “fibromyalgia” (FM) is the absence of inflammation (“itis”) in pathological studies of the tender muscle sites (Gronemann, Ribel-Madsen, Bartels, Danneskiold-Samsee, & Bliddal, 2004).

Myofascial pain syndrome (Travell & Simons, 1983) likewise is a pain condition characterized by tender soft tissue (especially muscle) sites, but there are significant clinical differences between FM and myofascial pain syndrome. FM afflicts predominantly females (a 7:1 female-to-male ratio), while myofascial pain syndrome afflicts genders equally (Wolfe, Ross, Anderson, Russell, & Hebert, 1995). Myofascial pain syndrome is generally a regional pain condition, though widespread myofascial pain syndrome has been described (Bergman, Herrstrom, Jacobsson, & Petersson, 2002).

In 1990, FM was defined, in part, by the clinical presence of widespread soft tissue tenderness at ≥ 11/18 characteristic tender sites (see Soft Tissue Tenderness Figure at www.marksschwartzphd. com) for at least 3 months in at least three body regions in the absence of other medical conditions that may produce a similar clinical picture, such as hypothyroidism, polymyalgia rheumatica, or adrenal insufficiency (Wolfe et al., 1990). That definition is still in use. However, there is now a proposed update by Wolfe et al. (2010). As they discussed, although the original criteria were useful (e.g., providing homogeneity) in clinical research and well used in specialty clinics (e.g., rheumatology), they were not used much by primary care physicians. The criticisms of the original criteria by Wolfe et al. (2010) included the following:

•	 Although most patients with FM are in primary care, these physicians rarely performed trigger point (TP)1 counts, in part because they do not know how to do so, they avoid it, or they do so incorrectly, if at all. Thus, the FM diagnosis is typically based on symptoms.

•	 The importance of other symptoms (e.g., fatigue, cognitive symptoms) that had not been part of the original criteria became apparent.

•	 FM became more of a spectrum disorder (i.e., fibromyalgia syndrome [FMS]) rather than being defined by either present or absent criteria. •	 When TPs decreased and/or other symptoms improved, the original classification diagnosis might no longer be useful for classifying patients. •	 The strict criteria for diagnosis led to excessive homogeneity among patients.

Thus, Wolfe et al. (2010) relied on these points as part of the rationale for developing “simple, practical criteria for clinical diagnosis of fibromyalgia that are suitable for use in primary and specialty care and that do not require a tender point examination, and to provide a severity scale for characteristic fibromyalgia symptoms” (p. 600).

From the upgraded criteria a revised and simple clinical case definition and revised diagnostic criteria were developed:

•	 Widespread Pain Index (WPI) of ≥ 7 of 19 areas of pain over the past week (see Wolfe et al., 2010, for the list of 19 areas) and Symptom Severity (SS) scale of ≥ 5 (with fatigue, waking unrefreshed, cognitive symptoms, and severity of somatic symptoms each scored on a 0-3 scale; 12 max) (see www.marksschwartzphd.com) or WPI score of 3–6 and SS scale score of ≥ 9. Symptoms present for 3+ months. •	 Absence of another disorder to account for the pain.

A major advantage of these revised proposed criteria is that they classified more cases (e.g., 88%) in the Wolfe et al. (2010) multisite sample of 829 patients compared to about 75% classified by the original criteria, and no physical or TP examination is needed with the revised criteria.

However, other discussions of the revised criteria noted that although deleting the TP examination, the required symptom evaluation made diagnosis more difficult (www.arthritis-research.org/ research/fibromyalgia-criteria).

Further extension of the criteria was provided by Wolfe et al. (2011) to allow research (e.g., survey and epidemiological research).

# Ep idemiol ogy and Imp act

Widespread body pain is believed to affect approximately 3.6% of the adult population in the United States (Hardt, Jacobsen, Goldberg, Nickel, & Buchwald, 2008), while the prevalence of FM is estimated to be at least 2% of the population, or over 5 million individuals (Wolfe et al., 1995; Lawrence et al., 1998). FM is more prevalent in women (3.4%) than in men (0.5%) in the U.S. adult population (Rudin, 2003; Goldberg, 1995). It has a more deleterious effect on functioning than other rheumatological disorders (Hoffman & Dukes, 1997). Even though FM causes no apparent structural damage to the musculoskeletal system, it nonetheless causes some degree of disability in up to 26.5% of afflicted persons (Wolfe et al., 1997) and may completely disable nearly 10% (Wallace & Hallegua, 2001). Researchers have observed that patients with FMS experienced and reported far more disability than assessors could measure when observing them performing tasks, which was thought to be an example of the disturbed perception that patients with FM may have regarding their health status (van Santen et al., 2002; Goldberg, 1995). It has been estimated (Wolfe et al., 1997) that \$16 billion per year is spent in the United States in direct costs treating FM, and that the nation loses 1–2% of its work productivity due to the disorder. Patients with FM seen at rheumatology clinics are seven times more likely than the overall U.S. adult population (16.2 vs. 2.5%) to receive Social Security disability payments (Wolfe et al., 1997).

Based on the literature, overview conclusions are as follows:

•	 The causes of FMS are insufficiently understood.   
•	 Treatments are typically unsuccessful or insufficient   
•	 Several contributing factors are known.   
•	 People with FMS can learn to manage the symptoms with tailored: | Physical exercises (e.g., aerobic) | Stress management methods | Psychological interventions (e.g., cognitivebehavioral therapies, hypnotherapy) | Applied psychophysiological interventions (e.g., relaxation therapies, various modalities of biofeedback) | Medications

We discuss in this chapter the issues and selected information that are sought or needed when evaluating and treating persons with FMS, the rationale for including relaxation, biofeedback, other applied psychophysiological interventions, and other nonmedical therapies, as parts of multicomponent treatment for FMS.2

# Why is Th ere Still a Ch apter on F MS?

In most review articles in recent years that we reviewed, one might assume that biofeedback is no longer considered a viable treatment. A further rationale for continuing to include biofeedback includes the following:

•	 Relaxation interventions are typically part of cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT) and biofeedback-assisted relaxation methods are ways to assist and enhance noninstrumentation methods of relaxation.   
•	 Hypnosis continues to be included as a treatment, and there is a limited but supportive literature on assisting and enhancing hypnosis with biofeedback (Somer, 1995).   
• Stress management (e.g., for anxiety, psychophysiological arousal) often includes and may focus on relaxation methods; thus, biofeedbackassisted relaxation has a role in stress management.   
•	 Specialized types of biofeedback (e.g., surface electromyography [sEMG], electroencephalography [EEG], and heart rate variability [HRV]) have recently received support for FMS. Patients with FMS are still referred to practitioners who use biofeedback and other applied psychophysiology methods.

However, in spite of the rationale for including biofeedback and FMS in this edition, there is limited new research with regard to biofeedback beyond the chapter in the third edition of this volume.

In addition, and relatedly, is this summary of the rationale for using psychological interventions for FMS:

•	 For many patients, stress exacerbates FMS symptoms.   
•	 Among clinical samples of patients with FMS, there are reports of comorbid psychiatric diagnoses (Loevinger, Shirtcliff, Muller, Alonso, & Coe, 2012; Malt, Berle, Olafsson, Lund, & Ursin, 2000).   
•	 Among patients with FMS, visceral pain thresholds become lower as a result of stress, and stress, via the brain, provokes problems in mood, cognitive functioning, sleep dysregulation, and increased pain.   
•	 There is good research, with many randomized controlled trials (RCTs), supporting a variety of psychological interventions (e.g., CBT, hypnosis, ACT), resulting in substantial improve

ments of FMS symptoms, emotional functioning, and quality of life [QOL]). There is research supporting multicomponent therapy resulting in substantial improvement of FMS symptoms (Arnold et al., 2012).

# Fibromyalg ia Syndrome Measures

Measures used in research and in evaluating FMS include the Fibromyalgia Impact Questionnaire (FIQ), the Brief Pain Inventory (BPI), the Multidimensional Fatigue Inventory (MFI-20), the Medical Outcomes Study (MOS) Sleep scale, and the Multiple Ability Self-Report Questionnaire (MASQ) (Williams & Arnold, 2011). It is beyond the scope of this chapter to discuss further measures. (See www.marksschwartzphd.com for copies of those measures that are part of public domain or for which we have permission to reproduce.)

# Nonph armacol ogical I nterventions

# Exercise

The importance of exercise in mitigating the severity of FM symptoms has been well documented since 1976, when Moldofsky and Scarisbrick first demonstrated that fit people were less likely to develop FM symptoms when their Stage 3 and Stage 4 sleep was intentionally disrupted. A meta-analysis of 49 FM treatment outcome studies indicated that exercise is associated with significant improvements in several types of outcome measures: physical status, self-reported FM symptoms, psychological status, and daily functioning (Rossy et al., 1999). A systematic review has concluded that supervised aerobic exercise training has beneficial effects on physical capacity and FM symptoms (Busch, Schachter, Peloso, & Bombardier, 2002). A program of progressive strength training and cardiovascular exercise has been shown to be safe, well tolerated, and effective at improving muscle strength, cardiovascular endurance, and functional status in women with FM, without exacerbating symptoms, and may actually contribute to a reduction in the severity of several symptoms (Rooks, Silverman, & Kantrowitz, 2002).

An extensive and scholarly review of the rationale and strong support for including physical exercise as an early treatment intervention for persons with FMS is provided by Vierck (2012). For this chapter, however, it is more important to underscore major parts of his rationale, which includes the following:

•	 Autonomic dysregulation as part of chronic psychological stress as “the root cause of FMS” (p. 7).   
•	 Reduction of peripheral blood flow from chronic stress, which, in turn, results in widespread muscular ischemia and muscular pain.   
• Widespread deep tissue pain (e.g., muscle pain) is the primary FMS symptom. Stress, via the central nervous system (CNS), affects mood, cognitive clarity (e.g., fibro fog), sleep dysregulation, and hence more pain.   
Therapeutic interventions need to focus on stress and/or pain.   
•	 Research with typically fixed, standardized physical exercise programs have generally reflected moderate effects on symptoms of FMS, with much variability among subjects.   
•	 In practice, it is preferable and there is a need to tailor exercise programs to individuals.   
•	 Peripheral blood flow (e.g., to muscles) measures are affected by exercise programs and affect changes in pain.   
•	 To relieve the deep tissue pain in persons with FMS, one must significantly reduce peripheral vasoconstriction and psychological stress.   
•	 Programs to relieve psychological stress needs to occur concomitantly with exercise

Among the important implications from the Vierck (2012) review and conclusions is that psychological and applied psychophysiological interventions are logically part of treatment planning for persons with FMS (i.e., multicomponent treatment). It is interesting that nowhere in Vierck’s (2012) extensive review is there mention of relaxation therapies or biofeedback, or any other applied psychophysiological or behavioral therapies. The focus was on exercise.

# Behavior Therapies

As we have stated in other parts of this chapter, the complex research on interventions for FMS is further complicated by the heterogeneity of not only FMS but also the many “treatment” rubrics (e.g., exercises, CBT, and applied psychophysiological interventions, including biofeedback).

In this section we focus broadly on behavior therapies, and more specifically on the so-called second and third generations of behavior therapies (Hayes, Luoma, Bond, Masuda, & Lillis, 2006): CBT and contextualistic approaches, for example, ACT. In this section, one conclusion is that “CBT is not CBT is not CBT.”

This heterogeneity, as it applies to FMS, is well discussed by Hassett and Williams (2011), who concluded that CBT, among other nonpharmacological interventions (e.g., exercise) has shown good efficacy at least as part of multicomponent treatments and as stand-alone interventions for a variety of chronic pain conditions, including FMS and chronic widespread pain (CWP).

As Hassett and Williams (2011) point out, CBT is a “class of interventions” that includes many different modules and skills designed for different outcomes depending on the therapy target (e.g., mood, pain management, functional improvement). Furthermore, the heterogeneity, hence the flexibility, of CBT is not only a strength but also a source of misinterpretation as a single intervention. A description of CBT is beyond the scope of this chapter and is discussed in many references (e.g., Hassett & Williams, 2011). However, we include a brief outline gleaned from Hassett and Williams (2011) as a reminder to readers.

# CBT Skills Sets

•	 Education. Education is a crucial part of CBT •	 Relaxation. The “most commonly used behavioural skill in CBT for pain management is a form of the relaxation response used to diminish autonomic arousal (e.g., through reduced muscle tension, heart rate and breathing).” (p. 303) The authors note that there is no agreement regarding the best ways to teach relaxation techniques. The implication for including various modalities of biofeedback is clear. Methods include progressive muscle relaxation, visual imagery, hypnosis, and biofeedback.

•	 Graded activation. “Time-contingent pacing” is a method of pacing that can improve physical functioning while minimising the likelihood of pain flare-ups.

•	 Pleasant activity scheduling. This encourages persons with chronic pain to include pleasant activities into their days to compensate for the commonly decreased pleasant activities found among persons with chronic pain. The purposes are to improve mood and confidence that they can improve their functioning.

•	 Behavioural methods for improving sleep. •	 Cognitive difficulties (e.g., problems with memory, cognitive clarity, attention/concentration probably associated with the impaired restorative sleep, as well as the negative impact on information processing by the distracting effects of persistent pain). Within CBT, interventions include “structured rehearsal methods” (e.g., repetition), behavioral stress management (e.g., relaxation).

•	 Problem-solving strategies. These help to break down large problems into solvable parts. •	 Cognitive restructuring and reframing. These include cognitive skills that change people’s learned and automatic beliefs about their own problems and their abilities to effectively manage solutions (e.g., changing their strong sense of helplessness and pessimism). •	 Coping skills training. These include reinterpreting pain sensations and distraction. •	 Interpersonal skills (e.g., assertiveness training).

A major part of the rationale for CBT and ACT in the treatment of FMS is to manage better and modify individuals’ avoidance (e.g., pain and distress), because avoidance contributes to increasing disability but does not reduce symptoms. The rationale and procedures involved in ACT, including in the treatment of chronic pain in general and FMS in particular, are beyond the scope of this chapter. See Hayes et al. (2006) for a detailed description of the ACT model, processes, and research. Also see Wicksell et al. (2013) for the first research on applying ACT to FMS.

The beneficial yet only moderate “relatively modest” (p. 2) effects of CBT for FMS were pointed out by Wicksell et al., (2013), thus justifying their rationale for studying, for the first time and with an RCT, ACT as part of the continued evolution of CBT. Using 12 weekly group sessions with 40 women, they concluded that ACT resulted in significantly improved pain-related functioning and self-efficacy, reduced anxiety and depression, improved QOL, and reduced psychological inflexibility. They concluded that reduced psychological inflexibility mediated improvements at 3-month follow-up.

The mechanism(s) of various forms of CBT are not well understood, but there is supportive evidence for the role of the orbitofrontal cortex (OFC) and/or the ventrolateral prefrontal cortex (vlPFC) involvement with CBT, resulting in changes of cortical activation as part of the individual response to pain (i.e., processing of pain). This is the first neuroimaging (i.e., functional magnetic resonance imaging [fMRI]) study of the effects of CBT on pain, although there have been several other neuroimaging studies of the effects of CBT on various psychiatric disorders and irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) (Jensen et al., 2012). According to Jensen et al., most of these studies supported the impact of CBT on increased PFC or lessened activity in the limbic system, thus improving executive control. In the Jensen et al. neuroimaging study, the data were consistent with reduced anxiety, increased vlPFC activity during pain, and subjective impressions of CBT-induced clinical effects on FMS. Those investigators speculate that

It is possible that the reduction in anxiety triggers a change in the cerebral pain loop, consisting of afferent input, emotional, and cognitive processing; leading to higher access to prefrontal regions such as the vlPFC and thereby a higher ability to reappraise pain and report positive treatment effects. Another possibility is that the cognitive interventions of CBT lead to a subjective perception of decreased clinical symptoms, resulting in a secondary reduction in negative affect and lowered STAI-S [State–Trait Anxiety Inventory–State] scores, reflected in greater vlPFC activity. (p. 1502)

For the benefit of readers interested in the research methodology, we include limitations of the Jensen et al. (2012) study, which they include in their article.

1. Instead of a mock intervention that would have provided better control for the social features of CBT group, they used a waiting-list control.   
2. There was no pharmacological (e.g., selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor [SSRI]) treatment that also has been shown to lead to increases in PFC activity in general anxiety (Maslowsky et al., 2010); thus, the vlPFC activity might not be CBT-specific.

Cautions and caveats regarding CBT for FMS is offered in a systematic review and meta-analysis of RCTs regarding the efficacy of CBTs for FMS by Bernardy, Füber, Köllner, and Häuser (2010). This report cautiously supports both the efficacy of CBT for improving depression and self-efficacy relative to pain after treatment and later. However, reductions of pain, fatigue, sleep disturbances, and health-related quality of life (HRQOL) were not seen as being due to CBT. They point out that their meta-analysis did “not confirm the conclusion of a qualitative systematic review of the German FMS guideline group that CBT was superior to controls in most key domains of FMS at the end of therapy and at follow-up” (Bernardy et al., 2010, p. 2002). The authors note the varied quality of methodologies and also the “considerable heterogeneity existed for the outcomes of pain, sleep, fatigue, HRQOL, and self-efficacy pain posttreatment and at follow-up, and this could mainly be explained by clinical and methodological differences between the studies” (p. 2002).

Even the studies excluded from the meta-analysis showed that for most key FMS symptoms, there was a lack of CBT efficacy. Nevertheless, the effect size is large for CBT effect on self-efficacy relative to pain. The bottom line for Barnardy et al. (2010) favors reconsidering the “high grade of recommendation given to CBT” in American guidelines (Burckhardt et al., 2005) and the German guidelines (Häuser et al., 2009).

Tailoring various types of CBT, including ACT, to FMS clusters and the varied key symptoms of FMS will ideally be the focus of future research.

# Biofeedback

Multiple modalities of biofeedback have been investigated in the treatment of FMS. The modalities differ enough to be considered different treatments; the only similarity is that they are subsumed under the rubric of biofeedback. Thus, biofeedback is not biofeedback is not biofeedback, in the same way that exercises vary considerably and are quite different, and that medications vary and are not equivalent. This is similar to the discussion of FMS patients being categorized into four distinct clusters or groups and being sufficiently different from each other to indicate the need to consider them separately.

For our discussion, we categorize biofeedback into three modalities, each with different goals and implications. SEMG biofeedback, EEG biofeedback/neurofeedback, and HRV (heart rate variability).

# SEMG Biofeedback

The original studies with biofeedback for FMS involved SEMG focused on reducing general muscle activity. The SEMG was from the frontal muscles area, although this does not directly generalize to the rest of the body. The results were reviewed in the corresponding chapter in the third edition of this volume (for those interested in details about the study, see www.marksschwartzphd.com). No further studies with SEMG were found for this chapter update.

Of the SEMG studies, only two were RCTs. The first studied 12 FM patients, with half receiving “true” and half receiving sham biofeedback (Ferraccioli et al., 1987). The “true” biofeedback group showed statistically significant improvements with respect to tender point index, visual analogue pain score, morning stiffness, and self-reported symptoms of FM. An RCT of SEMG versus sham biofeedback in patients with FM demonstrated that true biofeedback improved pain scores and significantly reduced the number of tender points to 10 in sham biofeedback versus 6 in true biofeedback, and the reduction from pretreatment was larger for the true feedback group. SEMG biofeedback also improved functional measures including the FIQ scores and a 6-minute walk test (Babu, Mathew, Danda, & Prakash, 2007).

Two trials comparing exercise and biofeedback have used usual treatment and attention controls. Although baseline and postintervention scores did not differ between groups in one study (van Santen et al., 2002), in the other study, tender point indices were significantly lower among subjects receiving exercise and biofeedback compared to controls (Buckelew et al., 1998). A group in the latter study that received exercise and biofeedback maintained their clinical improvements the best at 2-year follow-up, though these subjects also were instructed in daily relaxation techniques.

# HRV Biofeedback

The focus of this modality is reducing autonomic dysfunction. Many features of FMS are associated with autonomic dysfunction, as summarized by Hassett et al. (2007), and many studies have focused on autonomic dysfunction (e.g., sympathetic arousal and diminished parasympathetic activity). Diminished HRV is among other autonomic functions involved (Cohen et al., 2000; Martinez-Lavin, Hermosillo, Rosas, & Soto, 1998) with FMS. HRV biofeedback specifically focuses on autonomic reactivity.

In an open-label study, 12 women, ages 18–60, with FM completed 10 weekly sessions of HRV biofeedback that involved learning to breathe at their resonant frequency. They were followed-up 3 months after the last session. Improvements in HRV were immediate, while blood pressure, baroreflex, and therapeutic effects were delayed. This is consistent with data on the relationship between stress and hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal (HPA) axis activity. The authors concluded that this type of biofeedback may be a useful treatment for FM, perhaps mediated by autonomic changes (Hassett et al., 2007). We have not seen more studies with HRV.

# EEG Biofeedback/Neurofeedback

An early rationale for EEG biofeedback and EEGdriven stimulation3 (EDS) was discussed by Donaldson and his colleagues (Donaldson, Nelson, & Schulz, 1998b; Mueller, Donaldson, Nelson, & Layman, 2001). The latter study of 30 patients focused on EDS treatment sessions, an average of 37 sessions (range 16–80). The treatment package included two EMG, five to six physical therapy, and seven massage therapy sessions. There were patient reports of “increased cognitive clarity, improved sleep, increased mental and physical energy, reduced affective disturbances, and increased awareness of localized as opposed to generalized pain” (Mueller et al., 2001, p. 939). There was a decrease in use of prescription and nonprescription drugs.

Further elaboration of this approach was described by Esty and Perman (2003). Then, a randomized controlled clinical trial of the Flexyx system was published (Kravitz, Esty, Katz, & Fawcett, 2006). The latter stated that “only the clinician-rated global impression scores detected a treatment-related response . . . [that] persisted through one-week post-treatment only” but “tempered by the finding that . . . clinicians’ ratings [were] more optimistic than those of participants” (p. 56). Although the latter concluded that “the most important finding may be that for fibromyalgia patients EEG treatment alone is not sufficient for recovery” (p. 56), the article contained an excellent discussion of potential issues and explanations that can affect this type of research and that needs attending in future research. The next published study found in this series (Nelson et al., 2010), a randomized, double-blind, placebocontrolled clinical trial, shows no difference between active LENS intervention versus sham intervention on a variety of outcome measures after treatment or follow-up (up to 6 months) thus concluding that “LENS cannot be recommended as a single modality treatment” for FM.

A more recent, and more encouraging, published study used sensorimotor rhythm (SMR) EEG biofeedback4 in a randomized, controlled, and rater-blind study of 18 patients provided 20 SMR biofeedback session compared to a medication (escitalopram; trade name: Lexapro) group (Kayiran, Dursun, Ermutlu, & Karamürsel, 2010).

Both groups experienced significant improvement after treatment, but the EEG feedback group showed “greater benefits than conrols” for multiple measures including the FIQ and the Short Form 36. The authors acknowledge the limitation of not knowing “of any process which can lead to explain the mechanism of [neurofeedback] intervention for FMS” (p. 300). This preliminary study offers new hope that EEG biofeedback will have a legitimate place in the treatment of FMS.

Prudent practitioners who still incorporate EEG-based interventions for FMS probably incorporate this within a multidisciplinary approach tailored to the individual. Among the challenges for researchers and clinical practitioners are identifying the factors guiding selection of patients, and tailoring the selection, combination, and sequencing of interventions.

A systematic review of the literature (Holdcraft, Assefi, & Buchwald, 2003) concluded that the evidence to support the use of biofeedback for FM is limited, though the available evidence generally shows improved outcomes for patients with FM.

# Acupuncture

One review (Holdcraft et al., 2003) concluded that two RCT studies of acupuncture for FM, among the eight studies reviewed, provided strong evidence (Level A) supporting the use of acupuncture as a treatment for FM, although it recognized methodological limitations even for those two studies. Other reviews (Langhorst, Klose, Musial, Irnich, & Hauser, 2010, Rohini, Rachel, & Edzard, 2012; Deare et al., 2013) provided less supportive conclusions regarding acupuncture for FMS. The type of acupuncture to use, duration and frequency of treatments, the degree of acupoint stimulation, and effects on subgroups with specified criteria are in need of clarification in future studies. Methodological improvements, including the use of objective measures, sham acupuncture, adequate sample sizes, and long-term follow-up, are needed in future studies.

# Massage

There is moderate evidence to support the use of massage in the treatment of FM, according to a recent systematic review of the literature (SarziPuttini, Buskila, Carrabba, Doria, & Atzeni, 2008).

Two RCTs indicated improvement when massage therapy was used to treat FM. The first study administered 15 connective tissue massages to 48 FM patients to compare to a control group that received either no intervention or a discussion group (Brattberg, 1999). Of those receiving massage, 85% reported a reduction in pain and 30% reduced their analgesic use as well. Depression and self-rated quality of life improved, though no improvements were observed in sleep, activity levels, or anxiety. A recent RCT of massage for treating FM demonstrated that Swedish massage improved mobility, as well as a trend toward decreased perceived helplessness when compared to usual medical care (Alnigenis, Bradfley, Wallick, & Emsley, 2001). These subjects had no improvement in their reported pain, depression, well-being, or other functioning, and massage benefits became nonsignificant after 28 weeks.

Limitations in both studies include high dropout rates; the choice of control groups, which precludes blinding to the intervention; and a single practitioner offering massage in the second study.

# Other Complementary and Alternative Interventions

A recent systematic review of the literature (Holdcraft et al., 2003) concluded that most of the studies of alternative medical systems (homeopathy), energy therapies (magnetic therapies), and mind– body interventions (including relaxation and hypnotherapy) are methodologically flawed, and that no definite conclusions can be drawn as to their usefulness. Similarly, chiropractic care for FM is not recommended, as there are no well-designed studies or studies with favorable results.

# A Neuroph ysiol ogical Model  of F M

Many of the common clinical conditions seen in FM can be viewed as imbalance or instability of the normal autonomic nervous system balance. Abnormalities of the SANS in a number of the conditions associated with FM have been described in the specialty literature, including migraine (Peroutka, 2004), irritable bowel syndrome (Mazur et al., 2007), interstitial cystitis (Pacak, 2001), endometriosis (Possover, Rheim, & Chiantera, 2005), idiopathic urethritis (Hussman, 2006), chronic prostatitis (Yilmaz, Liu, Berger, & Yang, 2003), and temporomandibular joint pain (Appelgren, 1999). SANS activation increases resting muscle tone, while PNS activation reduces resting muscle tone (Roatta, Windhorst, Ljubisavljevic, Johansson, & Passatore, 2002). Thus, it would appear that an abnormality in the efferent hypothalamus outflow of the autonomic system is critical in the development of FM symptoms. The circadian rhythms of sleep (Saper, Scammell, & Lu, 2005), as well as appetite regulation (Neary, Goldstone, & Bloom, 2004), and mood (Müller, Uhr, Holsboer, & Keck, 2004), are also regulated at the hypothalamic level, and are often clinically impacted in patients with FM.

The hypothalamus also controls release of cortisol and norepinephrine through the HPA axis, which provides a systemic mechanism of SANS activation with slower onset and longer duration (Molavi, van Essen, Price, & Burton, 1997). Hypothalamic output also controls descending inhibition of pain responses in the dorsal horn of the spinal cord through descending pathways (Benarroch, 2008).

The systemic norepinephrine release via the HPA axis and accentuated SANS tone through the hypothalamic output to the interomediolateral cells of the spinal cord in conjunction with reduced descending pain inhibition at the spinal cord level is then postulated in this model to cause sensitization of primary nociceptors in FM.

This is clinically supported by research findings that plasma catecholamine concentrations are increased in patients with FM (Torpy et al., 2000), and elevated catecholamine levels are associated with hyperalgesia (Khasar, McCarter, & Levine, 1999). Approximately 8% of spinal nerve fibers are postganglionic sympathetic fibers (McCorry, 2007), and those fibers also invest the arteries that accompany spinal nerves and their branches to the extremities.

Neurogenic inflammation is produced when efferent (antidromic) outflow from the spinal cord (dorsal root reflexes) results in nociceptive C-fibers release of substance P, calcitonin generelated peptide, and somatostatin from their terminal axons (Lin, Wu, & Willis, 1999). Release of these substances in turn produces release of bradykinin, histamine, and serotonin from the local vasculature (plasma), platelets, and macrophages that serves to activate those nociceptive neurons (Fields, 1987). Thus, a local positive feedback loop is produced as neurogenic inflammation ultimately produces release of substances from the terminal axons that activate the primary nociceptors. These nociceptive neurons can also be further sensitized by efferent SANS activity. The abnormally high metabolic activity seen in the thalamus, amygdala, hippocampus, cingulate gyrus, and other limbic system structures in FM patients (Williams & Gracely, 2006) then could be consistent with abnormal CNS autonomic efferent activity contributing to nociceptor sensitization and therefore neurogenic inflammation peripherally. Psychological stress alone has been shown to cause degranulation of mast cells (many of which are estrogen receptor positive) that can initiate neurogenic inflammation (Alexacos et al., 1999; Eutamene, Theodorou, Fioramonti, & Bueno, 2003). This may in part explain the predominance of FM in females. Furthermore, the neurogenic inflammation leads to local edema (FM nodules) and widespread myofascial tenderness (FM tender points) without overt presence of inflammatory cells (Huguenin, 2004).

Neurogenic inflammation, through dorsal root reflexes and the propriospinal pathways, causes ascending and descending sensitization of nociceptors in adjacent spinal levels, providing a mechanism for the spread of tender regions from a small area to much wider areas of the body (Fields, 1987). As primary nociceptors become sensitized, their efferent output causes sensitization of wide dynamic range neurons in the deeper lamina of the spinal cord, which have much wider cutaneous receptive fields, as well as afferent sensory input from viscera. This model is consistent with the findings of Shah et al. (2008) that trigger points not only have markedly increased concentrations of inflammatory mediators, but also that muscle sites distant from the trigger points in those subjects have lesser elevations of these inflammatory mediators (higher than normal), which suggests systemic sensitization of the nervous system as predicted by this model.

Primary nociceptors relay through the lateral spinothalamic tract to the lateral thalamus to the somatosensory cortex to localize painful stimuli, while wide dynamic range neurons send afferent signals through the paleospinothalamic tract to the anterior and medial thalamus relay to limbic system structures that subserve the emotional and behavioral reactions to the painful stimuli (Fields, 1987). Though these ascending pathways are largely independent of each other, there are some interconnections. Abnormal activation of the neospinothalamic and paleospinothalamic pathways then forms the final link in a positive feedback loop (Figure 27.1) as this accentuated afferent input to the thalamus, neocortex, and limbic system further increases abnormalities in the regulation of autonomic balance centrally. Abnormal activation of limbic system structures and the hypothalamus would provide an anatomic substrate to explain the clinically excessive behavioral reaction of chronic pain patients to noxious stimuli (Gracely, Petzke, Wolf, & Clauw, 2002; Bradley et al., 1999). This may represent the central mechanism of the lowering of pain perception threshold (“thermostat”) in chronic pain patients, which in its extreme progresses from hyperpathia to allodynia.

There is also pharmacological evidence that supports this neurogenic model of FM. As shown in Table 27.1, the drugs demonstrating the most efficacy for treating FM are in the anticonvulsant and antidepressant classes, which act on the CNS and PNS. FM symptoms are relatively resistant to opioids and anti-inflammatory drugs, which do, however, demonstrate good efficacy in treating musculoskeletal pain conditions. (See www. marksschwartzphd.com for more discussion.)

![](images/6583acab33186085be1763c2abfdbf50a54c3ed964bccad67f4b856cfb8cffb3.jpg)  
FI GURE 27.1. Positive feedback loop in fibromyalgia.

TA BLE 27.1. Medications Demonstrating Efficacy for Fibromyalgia   
![](images/c06466d0a9421579d6eb0164af7151dac617a2f647689d35dea15b4918f6ea75.jpg)
Note. NSAID = non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs.

Fatigue, weight gain, and generalized discomfort can also be associated with hypothyroidism; so appropriate bloodwork to screen for that condition should be a part of the initial workup of patients with suspected FM. Thyroid abnormalities are not caused by FM but can produce a similar clinical picture or exacerbate FM symptoms.

# Discuss ion

fMRI and neurophysiological studies have demonstrated objective evidence over the past two decades that while FM remains a syndrome that does not have a demonstrable cause, there is objective evidence of abnormal CNS pain sensitization, as well as evidence that neurogenic inflammation occurs in FM, both in vivo and in vitro. A neuroendocrinologic model of FM presented here offers an anatomically and physiologically integrated conceptualization of how the myriad clinical findings in FM that represent central pain sensitization and visceral hyperalgesia and dysfunction might occur, which accommodates the experimental findings of abnormal HPA axis activation in patients with FM. Abnormal and/or unstable ANS balance can serve to unify (explain) the pain and organ dysfunction seen in patients with FM.

FMS can then be initiated by excessive noxious input to any point along this loop. Excessive psychological trauma or stress is postulated to initiate this positive feedback loop at the level of the paleocortex (limbic system), visceral injury, or recurrent insult (myocardial infarct, “leaky gut syndrome” after antibiotic administration, recurrent prostatitis) through abnormal visceral afferent activation and severe and/or ongoing musculoskeletal or peripheral nerve injuries activating the positive feedback loop through a-delta and c-fiber activation with neurogenic inflammation. Thus, a wide variety of insults can ultimately lead to the same clinical presentation.

Medications that demonstrate the most efficacy for treating FM are in the anticonvulsant and antidepressant classes, consistent with a neurological basis for the signs and symptoms of FM. Supplements such as magnesium and S-adenosyl methionine (sAMe) have also demonstrated some benefit in reducing FM pain. Other supplements and specialized diets have not been demonstrated to improve the symptoms of patients with FM.

Acupuncture has good evidence of efficacy for treating FM, and there is moderate evidence that massage may also benefit persons with FM. There is no evidence that chiropractic care improves the signs and symptoms of FM.

There is excellent evidence that exercise benefits both pain reduction and function in patients with FM, particularly supervised progressive endurance exercise. It is likely more effective if combined with CBT, biofeedback, and other elements of a more comprehensive rehabilitation program.

Though the experimental evidence to support the use of biofeedback for FM is limited in quantity, it generally supports the rationale for considering SEMG, EEG, or HRV biofeedback to help persons with FMS. Furthermore, the rationale for including various exercise intervention programs is consistent with the inclusion of other applied psychophysiological interventions, including biofeedback. There is support for including various types of CBT in the multicomponent interventions for FMS. Within CBT there are commonly various types of relaxation procedures for which multiple modalities of biofeedback can be used to enhance the cultivation of relaxation skills for selected persons with FMS.

Selected research recommendations include the following:

•	 Match treatments with FMS clusters (e.g., trials of various types of biofeedback, types and components of CBT and ACT for different clusters). Expectations: There is a need to preassess expectations regarding CBT, hypnosis, and so forth.   
•	 For hypnosis: There is a need to preassess hypnotic suggestibility.   
•	 Control for contact time for all therapies versus controls.

# Notes

1. Trigger points are focal, hyperirritable sites in skeletal muscle associated with palpable nodules in taut bands of skeletal muscle that, when stimulated, may produce pain distant to the site (“referred pain”).

2. Some professionals advocate CBT and other stress management methods for FMs, and exclude relaxation therapies and biofeedback from treatment. However, we assume in this chapter that many health care professionals include relaxation therapies as part of multicomponent treatment for FMS in selected patients for whom such therapies are indicated. Another assumption here is that biofeedback is justified as part of helping some persons learn relaxation skills and/or develop self-efficacy about their relaxation skills. An additional assumption is that some professionals use relaxation procedures and biofeedback to modify patients’ cognitions about their ability to regulate aspects of their physiology that they believed to be beyond their control. Many patients with FMS also have psychological and psychophysiological conditions (e.g., anxiety, headaches) for which relaxation therapies and biofeedback are part of the treatment.

3. Although EEG-driven stimulation/Flexyx/low energy neurofeedback system (LENS) is sometimes referred to as a form of biofeedback, it is not consistent with the official definition of biofeedback adopted in 2008 by three major organizations (Schwartz, 2010).

4. This is truly EEG biofeedback.
