# Irritable Bowel Syndrome

Mark S. Schwartz

Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) is the most common functional gastrointestinal (GI) disorder (Naliboff, Fresé, & Rapgay, 2007) and the second most prevalent chronic GI disorder after GERD (Saito, Talley, & Locke, 2007). In U.S. community-based studies, prevalence rates have varied from 3 to 20%—with the variation likely reflecting methodological differences rather than true regional differences, which also might be possible (Saito Talley, & Locke, 2007; Naliboff et al., 2007). The same authors report that international estimates are 6 to 22% in Western countries and 2 to 17% in Asian countries. Direct and indirect yearly costs exceed \$20 billion for physician visits, more than 500 million hospital inpatient stays, nearly 100,000 emergency room (ER) visits, work lost, and related costs (Saito et al., 2007).

Based on the literature, the following overall conclusions emerge:

•	 The causes of irritable bowel syndrome (IBS) are insufficiently understood, and treatments focused on GI factors are often unsuccessful.   
•	 Several contributing factors, however, are known.   
•	 People with IBS can learn to manage the symptoms by incorporating: | Changes in diet and lifestyle | Various stress management methods

| Psychological interventions (e.g., cognitivebehavioral therapies, hypnotherapy, brief psychodynamic psychotherapy) | Applied psychophysiological interventions (e.g., relaxation therapies, various modalities of biofeedback) | Certain medications at times (e.g., specifically for IBS, antidepressants).

This chapter includes discussion of issues and selected information sought or needed when evaluating and treating persons with IBS, the rationale for including relaxation, biofeedback, other applied psychophysiological interventions, and other nonmedical therapies, as parts of multicomponent treatment for IBS.

# Why Is Th ere Still a Ch apter on I BS? 1

In most articles I have reviewed, including some by very notable gastroenterologists who are well known for their research and publications about IBS, biofeedback is either not mentioned or it is included only within a list of complementary, alternative, or psychological interventions, such as stress management and cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT). Based on these articles alone, one might assume that biofeedback is no longer considered a viable treatment. However, Internet searches for IBS reveal that many major medical websites (e.g., webmd.com, medicinenet.com, and mayoclinic.com) include mention of biofeedback as a treatment per se.

Further rationales for continuing to include biofeedback, not mentioned in any of these sites or in any published articles that I found, are the following:

•	 Relaxation interventions are typically part of CBT, and biofeedback-assisted relaxation methods are ways to assist and enhance noninstrumentation methods of relaxation.   
•	 Hypnosis continues to be included as a treatment, and there is a limited but supportive literature on assisting and enhancing hypnosis with biofeedback (Somer, 1995; Palsson & Whitehead, 2012).   
• Stress management (e.g., for anxiety, psychophysiological arousal) often includes and may focus on relaxation methods; thus, biofeedbackassisted relaxation has a role in stress management.   
• Specialized types of biofeedback (e.g., anorectal for pelvic floor dyssynergia of pelvic floor muscles that contribute to constipation-like symptoms) have received recent support for IBS.   
•	 Patients with IBS are still referred to practitioners who use biofeedback and other applied psychophysiological methods.

However, in spite of the rationale for including biofeedback and IBS in this edition, there is not much that is new with regard to biofeedback beyond what was covered in the third edition of this volume. Furthermore, the literature and status of medications have not only changed but they continue to evolve and are expected to change more rapidly than editions of this book.

In addition, and relatedly, is this summary of the rationale for using psychological interventions for IBS and other FGIDs as gleaned from Palsson and Whitehead’s (2012) recent review:

•	 For many patients, stress exacerbates IBS symptoms.   
•	 Increased prevalence of IBS is associated with a history of traumatic life events (e.g., sexual and/ or physical abuse).   
•	 Among clinical samples of patients with IBS, there are reports of 50 to 94% of these patients with comorbid psychiatric diagnoses (e.g., general anxiety disorder or major depression; Whitehead, Palsson, & Jones, 2002), and anxiety and depression are correlated with worse results for persons with a functional GI disorder (FGID), including IBS (e.g., Drossman, 1994; Levy et al., 2006).   
•	 Among patients with IBS, visceral pain thresholds decrease in response to stress, and stress, via the brain, provokes motility in the colon and ileum.   
• There is good research, much of which includes randomized controlled trials (RCTs), supporting some psychological interventions resulting in substantial improvements of IBS symptoms, emotional functioning, and quality of life (QOL).

# Diagnostic Criteria, Clinical Evaluation, Alarming Symptoms, Tests, and Measures

# Diagnostic Criteria

The diagnosis of IBS involves the presence of certain symptoms, and ruling out other and more serious conditions. There is no biological test or marker that is indicative of this disorder. The most widely employed and recognized set of criteria is the Rome III criteria (Longstreth et al., 2006) summarized as follows:

Repeated episodes of abdominal discomfort2 or pain with frequency of 3 or more days per month, in the prior 3 months, and associated with at least two out of three features:

1. Better with defecation and/or   
2. Change in frequency of bowel movements associated with onset and/or   
3. Onset associated with a change in form (appearance) of stool.

In addition, these criteria must be met at least 6 months before the diagnosis. They occur in the absence of structural and metabolic abnormalities to explain the symptoms.

# Clinical Evaluation

Diagnosing IBS involves recognizing symptoms consistent with this disorder and excluding medical conditions that resemble IBS.

Lower abdominal pain aggravated by meals and relieved by defecation, small stools, persistent or recurrent symptoms, and worsening of symptoms during periods of stress all tend to add to an IBS diagnosis. The role of diet (i.e., lactose, alcohol, caffeine, fat or gas-producing products) as the cause of IBS is varied and confusing; Saito et al. (2007) note that dietary factors do not solely cause IBS.

The diagnostic approach includes consideration of several disorders, based on the dominant clinical symptom. (See www.marksschwartzphd.com for a partial list of disorders that may be considered in the evaluation of IBS and suggested medical examination components.) When the initial evaluation is completed, symptomatic treatment can be started and the condition reevaluated in 4–6 weeks. This approach allows a conservative assessment over two or three points in time before additional studies are considered.

# Alarming Symptoms

Patients with IBS do not have a structural or mucosal inflammatory disease. Therefore, the presence of any of the symptoms listed below should raise the possibility of an alternative diagnosis and prompt further diagnostic medical investigations:

1. Fever.   
2. Rectal bleeding or Hemoccult-positive stools.   
3. Symptoms that awaken the patient from sleep.   
4. Unexplained weight loss and or decreased appetite.   
5. Anemia.   
6. Abnormal findings on physical examination (patients with IBS have a normal examination, with the exception of a palpable and tender sigmoid).

# Tests

Although no laboratory study can help in the diagnosis of IBS, laboratory, radiographic, and endoscopic tests are frequently conducted in patients with suspected IBS, especially and primarily severe IBS, to exclude other disorders resembling this condition. (See www.marksschwartzphd.com for more information about medical tests and rationale.) In persons with IBS, all of these studies are typically unrevealing.

# Measures

A symptom diary or log consists of rating the severity of the common and major IBS symptoms. Points to consider in designing a log of IBS symptoms are compliance, practicality, symptom selection, rating system, frequency of ratings, other data (e.g., medications, dietary, stress, smoking, activity), consistency with published symptoms, coexisting symptoms and disorders, and avoidance of relying on global ratings.

In a standardized program treating many patients and when collecting clinical research data in a standardized program, practitioners probably include several or all symptoms. However, in clinical practice, it is wise not only to tailor the selection of symptoms to individual patients but also to include some that are not specific to IBS. Consider the following:

•	 Abdominal pain or discomfort.   
•	 Abdominal pain or discomfort relieved with bowel movement.   
•	 Diarrhea (loose or watery stool).   
•	 Loose stool or more frequent stools at onset of pain.   
• Urgency.   
Soiling. Constipation or hard stool. Straining to have a bowel movement. Feeling of incomplete emptying. Bloating or feeling of abdominal distension. Passage of mucus.   
•	 Bowel gas/flatulence.   
•	 Nausea or vomiting.

A rating scale in which 0 = not present, 1 = mild, 2 = moderate, 3 = severe, and 4 = disabling is one option. It is helpful to define each term for patients. For example, some researchers have used 0 to indicate that a symptom was not a problem and 4 to reflect a debilitating problem. However, this does not appear to allow for the clear nonexistence of a symptom. Furthermore, the term “debilitating” is a complex one. Certainly IBS can be debilitating (i.e., weakening, devitalizing, or enfeebling). However, a person may feel that way with severe symptoms, yet not report disability in functioning and working. I assume that a practitioner also wants to know whether the symptoms disabled or actually incapacitated a person. Thus, I prefer the terms “disabling” or “incapacitating.”

Ratings for IBS symptoms could follow a 6-point rating scale: 0 = no symptoms, 1 = slight or not a problem, 2 = mild intensity, 3 = moderate intensity, 4 = severe, and 5 = disabling or incapacitating. Bennett, Tennant, Piesse, Badcock, and Kellow (1998b) used these frequency-based ratings: 0 = not a problem, 1 = once only in the 2-week period,

2 = occurred more than once each week, and 3 = occurred almost every day, or daily. Bennett et al. (1998a) also used the following severity ratings: 0 = symptom did not occur, 1 = mild severity (no remedy sought, no remedy tried), 2 = moderate severity (remedy sought, but daily activities were not affected), and 3 = severe (interfering with daily activities).

It is important to include medications in the diary, especially those that can cause IBS symptoms and those prescribed to treat symptoms. Also consider including dietary information (e.g., ingestion of caffeine, milk products, or nondigestible carbohydrates such as cabbage and beans) and information on other factors (e.g., stress that could affect IBS symptoms).

Another decision is whether to obtain these ratings once (e.g., in the evening) or multiple times per day. Increased compliance and fewer data to review and analyze are advantages of once-a-day ratings. However, recalling in the evening the symptoms that occurred 12 or more hours earlier may be less accurate than obtaining ratings periodically throughout the day. Therefore, a practitioner should consider obtaining multiple ratings each day (e.g., early morning, late morning or midday, late afternoon or early evening, and late evening), and tailor the number and specific times to each person.

Yet another possibility is the Composite Primary Symptom Reduction (CPSR) score, developed by the State University of New York (SUNY)– Albany group. One calculates separately a Symptom Reduction Score (SRS) for two or three of the primary presenting symptoms. The Albany group used abdominal pain, diarrhea, and constipation. To determine each SRS, one subtracts the average symptom rating for a follow-up period from the average rating during a baseline period. The baseline period is 2–4 weeks, and the Albany group typically used 2 weeks for the follow-up period. One then divides this by the baseline average and multiplies this by 100 to obtain each SRS (e.g., a Diarrhea Reduction Score, a Constipation Reduction Score, and a Pain and Tenderness Reduction Score). Then one can assess the percentage of improvement for the composite of symptoms (e.g., much improved = 75–100%, improved = 50–74%, slightly improved = 20–49%, unimproved = 0–20%, and worse = less than 0%) (Blanchard, Schwarz, Neff, & Gerardi,1988; Blanchard et al., 1992). The CPSR Score is the total of the SRSs divided by the number of scores (e.g., two or three). That is, one adds the SRSs and divides this sum by the number of symptoms.

One can argue whether this is a better way to assess improvement in clinical practice. One must have baseline and follow-up periods of equal duration, as well as the time to perform the calculations (or a computer program to ease the calculations). One could analyze the differences for each symptom. Ideally, practitioners collect pretreatment baseline data and also follow-up data for at least 1 year. However, collecting pretreatment baseline data is not always feasible or prudent in clinical practice. Practitioners often need to adapt their procedures from the ideal to the pragmatic.

Do not rely entirely on global ratings, because they sometimes markedly overestimate benefit compared to daily symptom data. For example, in one study, 71% of the patients whose symptoms did not improve significantly rated themselves as quite competent in learning to overcome IBS symptoms (Blanchard et al., 1992). Global self-improvement ratings at 1-year follow-up showed about 70% improvement among 17 patients, compared to about 35% improvement as indicated by the CPSR score (Schwarz, Blanchard, & Neff, 1986). This difference is substantial. However, inspection of the table (Schwarz et al., 1986) of the CPSR score and global ratings showed adequate consistency between these two measures for eight of the 14 subjects.

The three common independent outcome measures—the CPSR score, patient global ratings, and physician global ratings—are significantly related (Meissner, Blanchard, & Malamood, 1997). Some consider the CPSR score to be close to a “gold standard” as an IBS outcome measure. However, as these authors note, the CPSR score (as used by the Albany group) assesses only three of the many IBS symptoms, and one might ask patients to select their own three most troublesome symptoms, creating a composite score tailored for each person. The problem with the latter approach is that it would create problems with comparing across both subjects and studies. Other limitations of the CPSR score are that it does not assess QOL, life interference, or medical care utilization.

The IBS-QOL Questionnaire was developed by Patrick, Drossman, Frederick, DiCesare, and Puder (1998) and further validated by Drossman et al., 2000), who showed that it changes significantly with treatment, at least in referral-based patients with moderate and severe IBS. This questionnaire contains 34 items with 1–5 responses each. It is listed in the Appendix in Patrick et al. (1998), but to reproduce or use it one needs permission from one of the senior authors3 and from Novartis

Pharmaceuticals Corporation. The IBS-QOL is an accurate assessment of treatment response (Drossman et al., 2000).

Practitioners are advised to incorporate daily ratings whenever possible. Remember that IBS is at least sometimes coexistent with other disorders (e.g., fibromyalgia syndrome, migraines, chronic fatigue, mood disorders, and anxiety disorders). These can affect the specificity of the symptoms, compliance with maintaining the log, and the accuracy of the log. These potential complications suggest the need for using less ambiguous symptoms and ratings.

# A Biopsychosocial Model of IBS: Effects on the Development, Persistence, and Illness Behavior

A biopsychosocial model of IBS is viable and reviewed in detail by Surdea-Blaga, Babn, and Dumitrascu, (2012). The model (shown in Figure 28.1) includes the following:

•	 Early life experiences affect the enteric nervous system (ENS) and intestinal physiology, and the central nervous system (CNS). •	 Interactions between the CNS and the ENS (the brain–gut axis).

![](images/9d7254046bb79a671df2c8d4fd0b972d98d349f07fca35ea3e6d39c1c96eeaeb.jpg)  
FI GURE 28.1. The biopsychosocial model of irritable bowel syndrome: Pathogenesis and clinical expression in relation to psychosocial factors. CNS, central nervous system; ENS, enteric nervous system; IBS, irritable bowel syndrome. From Surdea-Blaga, Babn, and Dumitrascu, (2012). Copyright 2012 by Baishideng Publishing Group Co., Limited. Reprinted by permission.

•	 Interaction between the CNS and psychosocial stressors, and between the CNS and IBS symptoms and illness behavior.   
•	 Interaction between the ENS/intestinal physiology and IBS symptoms and illness behavior.   
•	 Interaction between IBS symptoms and illness behaviors, and outcome.

These authors review psychological and social factors in IBS in these categories: environmental influences including abuse history, psychosocial stressors, personality traits, health beliefs, coping strategies, and negative emotions and psychiatric diagnoses.

They conclude that

environmental and psychosocial factors are an important part of the biopsychosocial model of IBS, being involved in dysregulation of the brain–gut axis, leading to the onset of IBS, persistence of symptoms, or abnormal illness behavior. There is a wide range of environmental and psychosocial stressors, acting at different moments in one’s life; . . . but only susceptible individuals will develop IBS. . . . Such personality traits and psychiatric disorders are probably the elements which make one susceptible to the development of IBS. (p. 623)

This review provides support for including psychological and psychophysiological evaluations and interventions.

It remains to be seen which comes first (the psychological problem or the IBS). Naliboff et al. (2007) provide an excellent review and discussion of the rationale for mind–body psychological factors and treatments for IBS.

# Additional Selected Factor: Health Care Seeking

In the general population, as many as 85% of persons with IBS symptoms do not seek medical help (Wald, 2009; see www.uptodate.com/contents/ irritable-bowel-syndrome-beyond-the-basics). Many authorities agree that people with IBS who seek medical care are more likely to have significant anxiety and stress than those who do not seek help. Moreover, in this population, the presence and role of psychological factors in IBS symptoms do not receive similar support in all studies (Whitehead, 1992a, 1992b). Most of these individuals are psychologically healthy. Some reports show that most community people with IBS believe that psychological stress stimulated their IBS symptoms. The reactions to stress and to experiencing emotional distress can exacerbate IBS, and the relationship between psychological factors and IBS has much research support. However, Saito et al. (2007) noted data consistent with the position that there are no differences between consulters with IBS and persons with IBS who do not consult with physicians.

Persons with IBS symptoms who seek medical care may be more fearful of having a serious disease; may be more depressed and/or more emotional; may view their overall health as less satisfactory; and/or may be more somatically focused than individuals with IBS symptoms who do not seek medical treatment (Drossman et al., 1988; Drossman, 1994; Van der Horst et al., 1997; Whitehead, Bosmajian, Zonderman, Costa, & Schuster, 1988). For many patients with IBS, psychological issues may not be major aggravating factors. Nevertheless, health care professionals often encounter many other people who seek help and show psychological distress, indicating a relationship between this distress and their symptoms.

# Treatments, Sel ected

The rationale for focusing on RCTs when evaluating treatment outcome was discussed in depth by Palsson and Whitehead (2012), such as the importance of incorporating random assignment to minimize selection bias, placebo controls especially because of a risk of heightened placebo effects for psychological interventions, and additional groups to help control for therapeutic responses separate from continued medical care.

# Cognitive‑Behavioral Therapies4

CBTs are appropriate for many patients with IBS (e.g., Boyce, Talley, Balaam, Koloski, & Truman, 2003; Craske et al., 2011; Drossman et al., 2003; Moss-Morris, McAlpine, Didsbury, & Spence, 2010; Naliboff et al., 2007; Palsson & Whitehead, 2012; Payne & Blanchard, 1995). Drossman et al. (2003) compared CBT to an educational control and desipramine for a large group of women with IBS who were randomly assigned to 12 weeks of CBT or other treatments. Using a composite outcome measure, CBT resulted in a 70% positive response rate versus 37% for the education control condition. Satisfaction with treatment, symptoms and QOL were included in the measure. The CBT group also fared significantly better when compared to the group receiving the tricyclic antidepressant. Interested readers are encouraged to examine the original article.

Practitioners who provide biopsychosocial/psychophysiological interventions for IBS need to be able to include CBT. The rubric CBT encompasses varied components and emphases including exclusively or primarily cognitive or behavioral therapy. Practitioners vary in terms of incorporating various types of relaxation. For example, I tailor the intervention to the patient, often utilizing CBT, which widely varies in the incorporation of relaxation procedures without and sometimes with peripheral biofeedback-assisted reduction of autonomic arousal. Although a major part of the rationale for employing CBT is that “maladaptive thoughts are the causes of psychological symptoms such as anxiety and depression, which in turn cause or exacerbate physical symptoms” (Palsson & Whitehead, 2012, p. 3), it is also feasible to consider that the thoughts are “one” of multiple contributing factors (psychophysiological autonomic nervous system [ANS] activity/reactivity that warrants interventions; e.g., relaxation, peripheral biofeedback to assist reduction of sympathetic nervous system [SNS] arousal, and/or increased peripheral nervous system [PNS] activity).

The therapist aims to help the patient recognize maladaptive thoughts and self-defeating behavior patterns that are adversely affecting life functioning, symptom experience, and mental well-being. Therapy tasks commonly include increasing awareness of the association between stressors, thoughts, and symptoms; examining and correcting irrational beliefs; countering automatic negative thoughts; observing and problem-solving factors that exacerbate symptoms; and identifying and adopting alternative, more effective coping strategies to handle challenging life situations and deal with GI symptoms. In between therapy visits, patients are typically asked to complete homework assignments related to the treatment tasks.

Of interest and import, too, is that of 18 published RCTs that compared CBT with other varied interventions for IBS in adults, CBT was superior in 15 studies regarding reduced IBS symptoms, and improved QOL and emotional status, for typically up to 3 months but also for as long as 8 months to 2 years (Craske et al., 2011; Moss-Morris et al., 2010; Van Dulmen, Fennis, & Bleijenberg, 1996).

# Hypnosis

field, 2006; Webb, Kukuruzovic,Catto-Smith, & Sawyer, 2007; Whitehead, 2006; Whorwell, Prior, & Faragher, 1984; see also www.ibshypnosis.com for annotated references and other information about hypnosis for IBS). Many readers conceptualize hypnosis within applied psychophysiology and are aware of the synergistic role of biofeedback for enhancing hypnosis for some recipients.

Hypnosis can have a considerable positive effect on IBS (e.g., Gholamrezaei, Ardestani, & Emami, 2006; Gonsalkorale, Miller, Afzal, & Whorwell, 2003; Naliboff et al., 2007; Palsson & Whitehead, 2012; Roberts, Wilson, Singh, Roalfe, & Green

As Palsson and Whitehead (2012) noted, “Clinical hypnosis is a verbal intervention that uses a special mental state of enhanced receptivity to suggestion to facilitate therapeutic psychological and physiological changes” (p. 210). These authors note seven RCT trials focused on IBS, wherein six revealed “significantly greater improvement in gastrointestinal symptoms” (p. 210) compared to comparison groups. One of these studies (Gonsalkorale et al., 2003) reported on 204 patients with IBS who were reevaluated yearly for several years after their hypnosis therapy. They noted that 81% of these patients maintained their improvement for 5 or more years. Thus, overall, the data strongly indicate that hypnosis treatment is an effective intervention for IBS. Note that these studies do not involve biofeedback. Nevertheless, as noted earlier in this chapter, there is a legitimate literature on assisting and enhancing hypnosis with biofeedback.

# Relaxation Procedures and Therapies

Of the seven RCTs noted by Palsson and Whitehead (2012) that used various relaxation procedures alone as an intervention for IBS, six reported significant benefits of relaxation compared to various comparison groups. The one exception (Boyce et al., 2003) showed no significant difference between multiple types of relaxation and CBT that included relaxation versus standard medical care. In the latter study, all groups significantly improved. The authors discuss the strengths and multiple limitations (e.g., high attrition in relaxation and CBT groups). In the first and only study of autogenic therapy (AT) effects on IBS, Shinozaki et. al. (2010) provided a small pilot study supporting the effectiveness of AT in significantly reducing IBS symptoms. Thus, there is support for procedures for IBS that reduce autonomic arousal and reactivity.

# Biofeedback‑Assisted Reduction of ANS Arousal and Dysregulation

This literature was reviewed in a prior edition of this chapter (Schwartz, 1995), but I have not located any additional studies that focus on this type of biofeedback used alone for IBS. The rationale for considering inclusion of this type of biofeedback in the treatment of any specific patient with IBS is discussed elsewhere in this chapter (i.e., to assist in relaxation used alone or in the context of CBT).

# Pelvic Floor Biofeedback for Dyssynergia Defecation and Functional Constipation Sometimes Associated with IBS

This involves functionally impaired relaxation (i.e., paradoxical contractions) of the pelvic floor muscles when a person attempts a bowel movement, thus resulting in functional constipation. Surface electromyography (SEMG) of the pelvic floor muscles provides biofeedback during simulated defecation that facilitates learning how to relax (i.e., stop contracting) the pelvic floor muscles. A therapist provides verbal instructions and encourages the patient during this process (Palsson & Whitehead, 2012). This form of biofeedback is indicated for patients with IBS when there is functional constipation due to dyssynergic defecation (anismus), but not when the IBS is associated with slow transit constipation without the dyssynergia. There were nine RCTs involving constipated adults reported by Palsson and Whitehead (2012). In six of these, this type of biofeedback was significantly better compared to that of various control groups. One of the remaining three studies (Koutsomanis, Lennard-Jones, Roy, & Kamm, 1995) had a serious methodological flaw, because it did not limit patients to those with pelvic floor dyssynergia (thus, effects for biofeedback could not be isolated for this condition alone). In the other two studies (Farid et al., 2009; Faried, Nakeeb, Youssef, Omar, & Monem, 2010), the comparison was with Botox injections and/or puborectalis surgery.

The best methodological and largest studies indicated clinically significant and substantial improvement of patients receiving biofeedback compared to those in control conditions (Chiarioni, Salandini, & Whitehead, 2005; Chiarioni, Whitehead, Pezza, Morelli, & Bassoti, 2006; Heymen et al., 2007; Rao et al., 2007). The outcomes of 70–86% of patients were substantially better than the 22–48% in other groups, and these gains persisted for one or more years. Thus, on balance, empirical support of biofeedback for this condition was uniformly strong for the trials rigorously addressing this approach.

A useful, yet uncontrolled, study in Thailand by Patcharatrakul and Gonlachanvit (2011) laid important groundwork in the field of pelvic floor biofeedback for dyssynergic-based constipation, including benefits for patients with and without IBS. This study concluded that 55% of the patients with IBS (16/29) and 67% of those without IBS (14/21) improved their dyssynnergia and their bowel symptoms. Furthermore, and reportedly a new and important finding, according to Rao (2011), of the 29 patients with IBS, 12 improved their IBS symptoms. Speculations are offered by Rao about the slightly lower improvement rate of 60 versus about 75% reported in other studies. He raises the possibility of differences in the “study population, learning curve with biofeedback, and lack of sensory training” (p. 573).

Part of the reason that this study is important is that patients with IBS have been excluded from a diagnosis of dyssynergic defecation by the Rome III criteria. Thus, until this study, these authors noted that it was unclear whether practitioners should consider biofeedback therapy for such patients with both symptoms. Based on this study, this type of biofeedback-assisted therapy is indicated whether IBS symptoms are or are not also present. Furthermore, among the important implications from Patcharatrakul and Gonlachanvit (2011), as also stated by Rao (2011), is the recommendation to evaluate possible dyssynergic defecation among patients with symptoms involving defecation difficulties. Rao concludes that practitioners should evaluate for possible dyssynergic defecation in patients with persistent IBS symptoms and, if dyssynergia is present, then this treatment is indicated and might improve IBS symptoms.

# Biofeedback for the Colon or Anorectal Biofeedback

# Colonic Motility  Sounds

Attempting to modify bowel sounds or colonic motility sounds using an electronic stethoscope is a very direct biofeedback method, and it was the first type of biofeedback used for IBS with five patients (Furman, 1973). The only published attempt to replicate the original results produced equivocal results with the few patients studied (Radnitz & Blanchard, 1988, 1989). Whitehead (1992b) concluded that this method is not very successful; he stated that it produces “relatively weak treatment effects and the technique is rarely used” (p. 69).

# Rect al  Feedb ac k

Another direct biofeedback technique involves inserting a special balloon transducer capable of detecting rectal contractions. Visual feedback helped 14 of 21 patients with IBS to reduce contractile activity (Bueno-Miranda, Cerulli, & Schuster, 1976). However, a replication from the same laboratory found that muscle relaxation and systematic desensitization-type treatments were better than the rectal balloon method and more practical (Whitehead, 1985). Based on that study and those by Radnitz and Blanchard (1988, 1989), and personal communications with Whitehead (1992a) about unpublished studies, Whitehead (1992b) relegated rectal pressure feedback to the archives of biofeedback techniques for IBS. Thus, direct attempts to change colonic activity are not part of current practice.

# Other Therapies to Consider

Some patients need couple therapy, child management and parenting instructions, help with time management, interventions for improving quantity and quality of sleep, and/or therapy to work through prior traumas in their lives (including abuse). Time use management covers topics such as time wasting, procrastination, perfectionism, being and feeling overburdened, and prioritizing for self-care.

Research using time series analysis of sleep disturbance and next-day IBS symptoms indicates that disturbed sleep is linked to the occurrence or worsening of IBS symptoms the following day (Goldsmith & Levin, 1993). Whether this is a direct or indirect relationship is not known, nor are the mechanisms involved known; however, the implication is that it is reasonable to provide treatments to improve sleep (Goldsmith & Levin, 1993).

In today’s health care climate, prudent practitioners reserve the previously described approaches for those patients who clearly need them. These interventions are for patients with moderateintensity symptoms and a few with severe symptoms that are unresponsive to other approaches.

Naliboff et al. (2007) presented a detailed rationale for considering mindfulness-based stress reduction (MBSR) and acceptance and commitment therapy (ACT) in future research with psychological treatments for IBS.

Yogic intervention for 2 months, specifically surya nadi pranayama (right-nostril breathing) and 12 asanas (poses), resulted in significantly less state anxiety and IBS symptoms (Taneja et al., 2004) in a group of nine males with diarrheapredominant IBS, compared with a conventional treatment with loperamide.

# Steps to Help Increase Successful Referrals for Psychological Interventions

Here, too, Palsson and Whitehead (2012) have provided sensible, useful advice, and guidelines for health care professionals involved in referring patients with IBS for psychological interventions.

•	 Introduce patients early on to the brain–gut relationship and psychological interventions, so that if and when a referral for psychological intervention is made, the patient interprets it properly as being part of the management plan from the beginning.   
•	 Reassure patients that their functional/psychophysiological diagnosis is correct.   
•	 From the beginning of the relationship, clearly establish a partnership with the patient and a commitment to working toward ideal reduction of symptoms, enhancement of QOL, and discussion of options.   
•	 It is vital to explain carefully the rationale for a referral for psychological interventions. This includes (1) how the brain affects bowel activity (e.g., lowering or intensifying perceptions of pain); (2) the effects of stress and intense emotions, (3) the fact that psychological interventions can counteract emotional impact; and,(4) the specific psychological intervention planned can improve outcomes better than only using medical treatment. This was also the focus of Drossman, Camilleri, Mayer, and Whitehead (2002).   
•	 Before discussing referral with patients, determine suitable providers with experience using psychological/psychophysiological interventions with IBS.   
•	 Patients with IBS and other FGIDs often cannot communicate the referral reason and goals to the provider of psychological interventions; thus, the referring physician should consider providing a referral letter explaining the request for a course of adjunctive interventions and whether the purpose is to treat affective and/ or gastrointestinal symptoms, and a request for feedback and coordination of care.

# Selecting Psychological Interventions

Palsson and Whitehead (2012) recommend CBT, hypnosis, and/or relaxation interventions for IBS symptoms, based on multiple RCT studies, and they recommend pelvic floor biofeedback for pelvic floor dyssynergia that causes or aggravates the constipation with IBS.

Based on research evidence, when the focus is on reducing secondary or comorbid emotional symptoms (e.g., anxiety and/or depression) associated with or caused by maladaptive thinking (e.g., catastrophizing, preoccupation with bowel symptoms), and resulting impaired QOL, CBT is recommended. Assuming the patient recognizes that life stress and/or stress-related symptoms are major factors, then relaxation therapies are recommended by Palsson and Whitehead (2012).

# Multicomponent Approaches

Multicomponent approaches are appropriate and probably needed for many persons with IBS. The active treatment components, the necessary components, and the sufficient components in a multicomponent treatment of IBS remain unknown. Practitioners may rightfully embrace a recommendation for a multicomponent treatment package that includes education, dietary changes, CBTs, relaxation procedures, and other stress coping strategies (e.g.,biofeedback-assisted relaxation and/or hypnosis, biofeedback-assisted pelvic floor therapy, and perhaps one or more medications).

Within a stepped-care model, prudent practitioners can consider starting with selected components and assessing the effects on the IBS symptoms. Assertiveness and anger management therapy may be considered for selected patients (Lynch & Zamble, 1989). Consider this for people with moderate or severe symptoms, especially when this is clinically indicated by history of patients’ report and perception of relevance for them, and/or when one expects or observes that other therapies are not sufficient.

# Patient Education and Reassurance

Treatment prudently starts with patient education to explain clearly the roles of diet, stress, aerobic exercises, and psychological and psychophysiological factors. One should reassure patients that IBS is by no means life-threatening, but acknowledge the frustrating and distressing nature of the symptoms for them. Prudently and realistically discuss positive expectations.

# Dietary Considerations

Practitioners often encourage people with IBS to focus on dietary intake, especially when they suspect that specific foods contribute to individuals’ symptoms. Some studies support a role for dietary factors in terms of sensitivities to foods (e.g., Dainese, Galliani, De Lazzari, Di Leo, & Naccarato, 1999; Georges & Heitkemper, 1994; Jarrett, Heitkemper, Bond, & Georges, 1994; Locke, Zinsmeister, Talley, Fett, & Melton, 2000). However, persons with IBS often cannot correctly identify offending foods (Dainese et al., 1999, p. 1896). Some authorities express caution regarding dietary factors and IBS because of the complexity and considerable variability of diet and IBS (Saito et al., 2007). These authors therefore propose that dietary factors are not, in themselves, the cause of IBS. Authoritative websites (e.g., www.mayoclinic. com) typically suggest the following:

•	 Avoid problem foods. Common offending foods include alcohol, chocolate, caffeine, dairy products, sugar-free sweeteners such as sorbitrol or mannitol. Problems with gas are worsened by beans and foods from the cabbage family. Consuming fatty foods, chewing gum, and using a straw for drinking can all increase the amount of gas.   
•	 Avoid skipping meals and eat on a regular schedule.

Other dietary recommendations for some people to consider include taking care with dairy products if one is lactose intolerant, drinking plenty of water, avoiding carbonated drinks, and exercising regularly.

# Pharmacotherapy for the Colon and/or Psychotropic Treatments of IBS

The literature base on the use of psychotropic (e.g., Jackson, O’Malley, & Tomkins, 2000) medications and/or the use of pharmacotherapy aimed at the gut is extensive. Summarizing these research and clinical areas is beyond the scope of this chapter.

# Symptom Monitoring and Modification

Include a symptom log especially for patients with moderate or severe symptoms. Include at least the specific symptoms, time, and rated degrees of symptom severity. Include information about eating, drinking, medications, and smoking.

# One Other Factor: “Changes without Awareness”

Another factor to consider is that some patients make changes in their lifestyles without informing their doctor, therapist, or experimenter. For example, when people come for treatment, they often read more about their symptoms and discuss their symptoms and treatments with others. They often pay more attention to factors they already knew or suspected would affect their symptoms but had ignored before treatment. They may recall information from articles. Some such changes are too subtle even for some patients to realize themselves. For example, their expectations may change. They may notice subtle signs of anxiety, walk more slowly, breathe differently, and relax slightly more often. They may change their eating and drinking habits. The point here is that an attention placebo is often not an ineffective experience. It can set the occasion for many patients to make therapeutic changes. Coming for therapy may become a discriminative stimulus that leads to healthier behaviors.

# Concl usions: Impl ications and Guidel ines for T reatment

The guidelines and implications for treatments of IBS depend partly on the nature and severity of the symptoms, practitioner preferences and training, available treatments, patient preferences, and practical factors. Prudent practitioners tailor graduated treatments to patients. About 70% of people with IBS have mild or infrequent symptoms with no disruptions of activities (Drossman & Thompson, 1992; Drossman et al., 1994). Primary care physicians typically see these people who have no major functional impairment or psychological disturbance. The 25% of patients with moderate symptoms experience periodic disruptions in their activities, often show more psychological distress, and use health care services more often. Gastroenterologists often see these people. The estimated 5% of patients with severe or intractable IBS symptoms fear a serious underlying disease and gravitate to tertiary medical centers. They are typically not aware of the relationship between IBS and psychological symptoms that is usually present. Their IBS symptoms do not correlate with specific stimuli, such as meals, activities, or hormonal changes.

The graduated approach described by Drossman and Thompson (1992) is still an excellent model, with many useful recommendations. It is similar to, but not exactly the same as, a stepped-care model. In a pure stepped-care model, practitioners move through a graduated list or hierarchy of evaluation and treatment procedures. Each step depends on the results of the prior step. It is assumed that one can start patients with the first steps.

However, in clinical practice, people present with different degrees of severity, variations of symptoms, and comorbidity with other conditions, including psychological conditions. Patients also hold various beliefs about their primary diagnosis. One can start with different combinations of treatment rather than waiting to observe the outcome of each. Prudent practice usually involves stepped care, a multicomponent approach, and long-term follow-up.

A medical examination is recommended for all patients with symptoms that suggest possible IBS. This is to rule out organic colonic disease in patients. After the diagnosis of IBS is made and if dyssynergic defecation is indicated, then the medical practitioner (e.g., gastroenterologist) can evaluate and/or refer for treatment as indicated by qualified practitioners for pelvic floor biofeedback. The medical practitioner also has the option to treat with pharmacotherapy if indicated and preferred, and/or to refer the patient to another physician (e.g., psychiatrist) for psychotropic mediation if it is indicated and preferred. The medical practitioner (e.g., gastroenterologist) also considers options that include referral to a suitable psychologist or other practitioner who can provide various psychologically based interventions within a biopsychosocial/psychophysiological model.

A multicomponent treatment can include various combinations of the following:

•	 Patient education including early and periodic discussion about the biopsychosocial/ psychophysiological model, the rationale, and potential inclusion of psychologically based interventions as a normal part of the intervention plan.   
•	 Periodic and clear reassurance about the benign nature of IBS.   
•	 A symptom log when practical.   
•	 Dietary considerations.   
•	 Pharmacotherapy directed at specific symptoms (predominant pain, diarrhea, or constipation) as indicated.   
•	 Psychotropic/psychopharmacological medications as indicated.   
•	 Psychological evaluation and applied psychophysiological/behavioral treatments, CBTs, relaxation therapies, biofeedback-assisted relaxation, hypnosis, and other stress management and/or psychological therapies (e.g., couple therapy, child management and parenting instruction, and/or work on trauma/ abuse). Intensive behavioral/specific pain management treatment for severe or intractable symptoms.5

# Gl oss ary

Colonic motility sounds. Colon sounds that can be heard with an electronic stethoscope.

Discriminative stimulus. A specific condition in which reinforcement of a behavior occurs, in comparison to other conditions (stimuli). “Discriminative” refers to properties of the stimulus, not the person discriminating.

Flexible fiberoptic proctosigmoidoscopy (FlexSIG). Examination of the lower part of the colon (sigmoid) and rectum using a flexible lighted tube. One can see nearly 50% of colorectal cancers or polyps with flexible fiberoptic proctosigmoidoscopy (FlexSIG). Also used in diagnosing Crohn’s disease and ulcerative colitis. One can get samples of tissue through this instrument.

Global ratings. Patients’ estimates of their symptoms without using a specific rating scale or procedure. Often refers to retrospective estimates (e.g., “I feel 50% better,” “The diarrhea is 50% better”). Often this is not an accurate measure of symptom change.

Inflammatory bowel disease (IBD). Usually refers to Crohn’s disease and ulcerative colitis. The term “colitis” applies to inflammatory disease of the colon. The term “spastic colitis” or “spastic colon” is a misnomer for IBD.

Ulcerative colitis. A chronic inflammatory disease in the colon anywhere in the GI tract. Signs and symptoms include bloody diarrhea, abdominal pain, urgent bowel movements with pain, fever, weight loss, joint pain, and skin lesions.

vices, 4333 Brooklyn Ave, NE, Rm 14-101, University of Washington, Seattle 98195. Email: donald@uw.edu.

4. Although many studies and reports focus exclusively on CBTs, some others include therapies (including relaxation therapies), although they call the treatment “cognitive-behavioral.”

5. Tailor the order to the individual patient.

# References

# Notes

1. Some professionals advocate cognitive-behavioral therapies and other stress management for IBS, and exclude biofeedback and sometimes relaxation therapies from treatment. This chapter assumes that many health care professionals include relaxation therapies as part of multicomponent IBS treatment for selected patients for whom such therapies are indicated. Another assumption here is that biofeedback is justified as part of helping some persons learn relaxation skills and/or develop self-efficacy about their relaxation skills. An additional assumption is that some professionals use relaxation procedures and biofeedback to modify patients’ cognitions about their ability to regulate aspects of their physiology that they thought were outside their control. Many patients with IBS also have psychological and psychophysiological conditions (e.g., anxiety, headaches, fibromyalgia) for which relaxation therapies and biofeedback are part of the treatment.

2. “Discomfort” is defined as an uncomfortable sensation that the person does not call pain. 3. Donald L Patrick, PhD Department of Health Ser

# Asthma

# Paul M. Lehrer and F rederick Wamboldt

# Th e Seriousness of As thm a as a Publ ic Heal th Probl em, and the Potential Util ity of E ffective Nonmedication Th erapy

In the United States, asthma affects 14 to 15 million people and accounts annually for 1.2 million emergency department visits, 445,000 hospital days, and an economic burden of \$5.1 billion (Smith et al., 1997). Worldwide, the prevalence, morbidity, and mortality of asthma in industrialized countries has increased over the past two decades (Kussin & Fulkerson, 1995), again with huge associated costs (Corso & Fertig, 2004; National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute, 2002). Accordingly, both in the United States and worldwide, improving asthma treatment has become a top public health priority, and various high-quality, evidence-based guidelines have been developed (Bateman et al., 2008; Kroegel, 2007).

Current guidelines define “asthma” as a chronic inflammatory disorder of the airways associated with reversible airways obstruction and increased airways responsiveness to a variety of stimuli (National Asthma Education Prevention Program, 2007). Accordingly, early and aggressive treatment with anti-inflammatory agents, particularly inhaled corticosteroid medication (ICS), is recommended by all existing guidelines for all forms of persistent asthma. Indeed, an extensive literature supports both the efficacy and effectiveness of ICS to improve clinical asthma outcomes and reduce the morbidity and mortality of asthma (Barnes, 1998; Kelly, 2009).

Unfortunately, adherence to ICS medications in real-world settings is poor in all patient groups, with average ICS adherence rate for both children and adults with asthma estimated at about 50% (Strunk, Ford, & Taggart, 2002; World Health Organization, 2003). Furthermore, there is solid evidence linking poor ICS adherence to adverse asthma outcomes (Milgrom et al., 1996). Finally, interventions to improve ICS adherence have shown, at best, extremely limited ability to improve patient behavior in clinically relevant and/or sustainable fashions (Bender, Milgrom, & Apter, 2003). Although, given the importance of such work, ongoing efforts are justifiably encouraged and necessary. The reasons so many patients exhibit poor adherence to ICS medications, despite educational efforts, are certainly complex and largely unknown. A recent review of the existing literature found that the most common reasons reported by patients include concerns about medication safety and cost; the belief that one’s asthma is not severe enough to require daily treatment; worry about dependence or diminished effectiveness from long-term use of medication; and a general distrust of the medical establishment (Bender & Bender, 2005).

Data concerning real-world use of ICS medications have prompted considerable effort within the pharmaceutical industry to develop alternative, non-ICS asthma medications. On the consumer front, there is solid evidence that large numbers of patients actively seek nonmedication treatments for asthma (Langmack, 2001; Ziment, 1997). A survey of 4741 U. K. patients with asthma indicated that 59% of these patients tried nonpharmaceutical treatments (Ernst, 1998). The most common techniques used were breathing-based therapies (30%), homeopathy (12%), herbalism (11%), yoga (9%), and acupuncture (7%). Smaller percentages of other methods were reported. When asked about the extent to which these therapies provided improvement in their asthma, 10% stated that symptoms improved “to a great extent,” and 23% “somewhat.” Similarly, in an interview study of 601 adults with asthma, 8% reported reliance on herbal remedies, 6% on use of tea and coffee, and 6% on over-the-counter (OTC) epinephrine or ephedrine at some point in the prior year to control their asthma (Blanc, Kuschner, Katz, Smith, & Yelin, 1997; Blanc, Kuschner, Katz, & Yelin, 2000). It is important to underscore that this level of reliance on these alternative treatments occurs despite little to no evidence concerning the safety and efficacy of such treatments. Indeed, reliance on herbal remedies, tea, and coffee was associated with a more than twofold increased risk of hospitalization for asthma.

# Ass essm ent of As thm a and T reatment O utcome

Asthma is an episodic disease characterized by reversible decreases in lung function that can be measured directly by assessing respiratory resistance or passage of air in a forced expiratory maneuver. The most accepted and standardized measures of airway function in asthma involve spirometry (American Thoracic Society, 1995). In this method the individual takes a maximal capacity breath, blows into a tube as hard and as long as possible, then inhales again to maximal capacity as quickly as possible. The “gold standard” spirometer uses displacement of water by exhaled air in a “water seal” spirometer. A canister floating in the water rises in proportion to exhaled air, yielding a curve proportional to volume of air exhaled over time. In recent years, water seal spirometers have mostly been replaced by “pneumotach spirometers,” which measure pressure and flow of air from the mouth. Pneumotach measures correlate almost perfectly with those taken from water seal spirometers, and are much easier and more inexpensive to obtain. The most common spirometric measure is the forced expiratory volume in the first second of a maximal effort forced exhalation from full vital capacity (FEV1). Another measure often used in home assessments is peak expiratory flow. This measure is more effort-dependent than FEV1, and is therefore less reliable, although it is very widely used because it is easy and inexpensive to record. Another approach to asthma assessment in the behavioral literature is direct measurement of respiratory resistance. Although this can be done several ways, the most common method in behavioral studies is use of the forced oscillation technique (Dubois, Brody, Lewis, & Burgess, 1956). In this method, a sound is introduced to the airways through a loudspeaker attached to a resonating chamber, which, in turn, introduces calibrated sounds into the airways through a tube to the mouth. The pressure and flow of air bouncing off the airways is then used to assess airway resistance and several other mechanical parameters, such as reactance, which may reflect stiffness of the airways. Earlier models of forced oscillation devices assessed resistance using a sound of only a single frequency, usually 10 Hz. More recent instruments use multifrequency sounds between 6 and 32 Hz. Resistance is particularly elevated at the lower frequencies in this range during an asthma exacerbation or other source of airway obstruction. Although forced oscillation has been found to be more sensitive than spirometry (Goldman et al., 2002; Zerah, Lorino, Lorino, Harf, & Macquin-Mavier, 1995), and is used routinely for assessing infants and animals, and some norms have been established for this method (Delacourt et al., 2000), it is not generally accepted in clinical practice in the United States. With spirometry, decreases of 15–20% from “personal best” (usually defined as the best measures taken within the past 2 weeks) is considered to be a clinically significant exacerbation; conversely, an increase of 15–20% is considered to be a clinically significant improvement in asthma. For forced oscillation, changes of 40% are required for clinical significance.

Because of the episodic nature of asthma, however, pulmonary function testing is not always the most important criterion for determining asthma severity or improvement. Indeed, at times it may be completely normal. The National Heart, Lung, and Blood Institute has defined four levels of asthma severity, which include frequency and intensity of asthma symptoms, measures such as peak flow variability at home, as well as frequency of criterion-level deviations from “personal best”

during symptom periods and actual spirometry scores, expressed as percent expected based on norms for sex, height, weight, age, and ethnicity (National Asthma Education Prevention Program, 2007). Change in level of asthma severity would define a clinically significant change, although this criterion is quite conservative.

Other criteria also have been used as measures of asthma severity. Although asthma is generally considered to be an inflammatory disease, actual measures of inflammation are rarely used, primarily because of expense and difficulty in measurement. The currently best accepted measure of airway inflammation is percentage of nitric oxide in exhaled air (Ashutosh, 2000). No behavioral studies as yet have used this criterion, although it is commonly used in drug studies. Another hallmark characteristic of asthma is “twitchiness” of the airways (i.e., a tendency for the smooth muscles of the airways to constrict). In standardized challenge tests using methacholine or histamine (Cain, 2001; Cockcroft, 2003), the criterion is the dose of either substance needed to produce a 20% decrease in FEV1. It is notable that the airway smooth muscles are under both parasympathetic and alpha sympathetic control, such that increases in either autonomic function produce bronchoconstriction. (Indeed, the methacholine challenge test involves direct parasympathetic stimulation.) Airway twitchiness is thought to be produced primarily by inflammation in asthma.

# Biofeedback as  an As thm a T reatment

# Direct Regulation of Airway Parameters

Some of the early biofeedback applications involved direct voluntary control of pulmonary function (Kahn, Staerk, & Bonk, 1974; Steptoe, Phillips, & Harling, 1981). Although these applications looked quite promising, this research has not been followed by further confirmatory research. For example, Mass, Dahme, and Richter (1993) replicated modest decreases in respiratory resistance with this technique, but with little consistency or generalization of effects. They concluded that the technique may not be useful for treating asthma. It cannot be determined whether this is due to subsequent unpublished nonconfirmatory results, or whether unavailability or expense of commercial measurement equipment inhibited further research. One successful study gave direct biofeedback to reduce trachea noise (Mussell, 1986; Mussell & Hartley, 1988).

Some successful early case studies also involved neurofeedback (Tansey, 1982) and thermal biofeedback (Lerro, Hurnyak, & Patterson, 1980). There is reason to think that finger warming, insofar as it decreases alpha sympathetic activity (Freedman, 1991; Freedman et al., 1988), may cause bronchodilation by this mechanism (Kaliner, Orange, & Austen, 1972; Lehrer, Isenberg, & Hochron, 1993), just as phenylephrine, an alpha sympathetic stimulant, causes bronchoconstriction in asthma (Grandordy et al., 1995). Neither of these methods, however, has been subjected to controlled evaluation.

# Electromyographic Biofeedback

Additional biofeedback research on asthma focused on relaxation training, sometimes accompanied by surface electromyographic (EMG) biofeedback. Some statistically significant findings have been obtained for EMG biofeedback training (Davis, Saunders, Creer, & Chai, 1973; Scherr & Crawford, 1978), particularly for relaxing the facial muscles (Kotses & Glaus, 1981; Kotses, Glaus, Crawford, Edwards, & Scherr, 1976; Kotses et al., 1991) and for muscle relaxation methods generally.(Vazquez & Buceta, 1993a, 1993b, 1993c) The theory underlying this application was the known interaction between the trigeminal and vagus nerves (Kotses & Glaus, 1981). The trigeminal nerve is activated during tension of facial muscles, while increased activity in the vagus nerve, the principal pathway of parasympathetic influence in the upper body, particularly the cardiovascular and respiratory systems, causes an increased tonus in the smooth muscles of the lung, which in asthma is seen as bronchoconstriction. However, several critical reviews of this literature concluded that results are neither consistent nor of clinically significant magnitude (Erskine-Milliss & Schonell, 1981; Richter & Dahme, 1982), Our own research has similarly found significant but small effects from muscle relaxation that are of doubtful clinical magnitude (Lehrer et al., 1997; Lehrer et al., 1994; Lehrer, Hochron, McCann, Swartzman, & Reba, 1986). The poor results may be due to insufficient training or control of the muscles through the training provided; none of the studies required very low EMG levels as a criterion for skills acquisition. More likely, however, the results may reflect the relative indirect relationship between muscle tension and smooth muscle tone in the airways. Indeed, parasympathetic rebound effects produced by withdrawal of sympathetic arousal in muscle relaxation may, at least in the short run, exacerbate asthma (Lehrer et al., 1997),

# Biofeedback and Respiratory Retraining Methods Aimed at Decreasing Partial Pressure of CO

It has long been known that hyperventilation symptoms are prevalent among patients with asthma (Belloch, Perpina, Pascual, Martinez, & De Diego, 1997; Carr, 1998; Demeter & Cordasco, 1986; Deshmukh, Toelle, Usherwood, O'Grady, & Jenkins, 2007; Gardner, Bass, & Moxham, 1992; Goodwin et al., 2003; Kinsman, Luparello, O'Banion, & Spector, 1973; Ritz, Bobb, Edwards, & Steptoe, 2001). They may be causally related, bidirectionally. Increased airway resistance causes an increase in respiratory drive (Abbrecht, Rajagopal, & Bryant, 1983; Hussain, Pardy, & Dempsey, 1985; Ostlund & Linnarsson, 1998) that, in turn, can provoke hyperventilation (Sargunaraj et al., 1996). Additionally, anxiety associated with asthma symptoms (Deshmukh, Toelle, Usherwood, O'Grady, & Jenkins, 2008), or independently, can provoke hyperventilation. Indeed, hyperventilation symptoms associated with asthma can be unrelated to asthma severity (ten Brinke, Ouwerkerk, Bel, & Spinhoven, 2001) or to measures of lung function (Stromberg & Gustafsson, 1996). However, hyperventilation can provoke bronchoconstriction and worsen asthma symptoms (Freed, McCulloch, & Wang, 2000; Nielsen & Bisgaard, 2005), perhaps because of exposure to greater volumes of outside air, which is almost always cooler and drier than air in the airways, and these characteristics are known to induce bronchoconstriction (Nielsen & Bisgaard, 2005). Also, anxiety and emotion, whether induced by asthma symptomatology or independently, can produce physiological changes in the airways that worsen asthma symptoms.

The co-occurrence of asthma and anxiety or panic, in which hyperventilation symptoms are also prominent, is higher than expected for either disorder (Deshmukh et al., 2007; Martinez-Moragon, Perpina, Belloch, & de Diego, 2005; Weiser, 2007), with particularly high levels of morbidity for both disorders (Deshmukh et al., 2007; Feldman, Lehrer, Borson, Hallstrand, & Siddique, 2005; Hasler et al., 2005). The high rate of this comorbidity is troublesome because of frequent symptom confusion between the two disorders, often caused by overlap in symptoms (most predominantly dyspnea, which can be produced by both hyperventilation and airway obstruction). This often causes patients to self-treat the wrong disorder, sometimes with disastrous (occasionally fatal) consequences (Dirks & Kinsman, 1982; Friedman, 1984; JansonBjerklie, Ferketich, Benner, & Becker, 1992; Tietz, Kahlstrom, & Cardiff, 1975). A need for treatment packages for the comorbidity has been prominently expressed (Nardi, 2005), with preliminary results in which a cognitive-behavioral package indicated success in decreasing panic while stabilizing asthma symptoms and decreasing overuse of albuterol (Lehrer et al., 2008).

Various methods used specifically to treat hyperventilation as a characteristic of asthma have revealed clinically significant effects. These methods have included teaching slow, relaxed, abdominal breathing, and biofeedback to increase end-tidal CO (ETCO ), which is highly related to partial pressure of CO (pCO ) in the blood (Ritz, Meuret, Wilhelm, & Roth, 2009; Thomas et al., 2009). These trials have suggested, however, that changes in ETCO do not correlate highly with changes in airway function or other clinical indicators of asthma, so the effects may be attributable more to the interrelationship between anxiety and asthma symptoms than to direct effects on airway function. A slow breathing method advocated by Buteyko has been widely applied to asthma, particularly in Russia, with good effects on asthma symptoms and quality of life (Bruton & Lewith, 2005; Cowie, Conley, Underwood, & Reader, 2008; McHugh, Aitcheson, Duncan, & Houghton, 2004; McHugh, Duncan, & Houghton, 2006; Shaw, Thompson, & Sharp, 2006), although effects on pulmonary function are mixed, and asthma improvement is often unrelated to changes in ETCO2.

# Heart Rate Variabil ity Biofeedback for Al tering  Breathing

An early approach to autonomic nervous system biofeedback for asthma treatment was to increase sympathetic arousal by teaching people to increase their heart rate (Harding & Maher, 1982). However, the magnitude of the effects was of doubtful clinical importance (with peak flow differences of \~9%, while criteria for clinical significance are usually in the range of 15–20%. Usage of asthma medication and frequency of self-reported “asthma attacks” decreased in the experimental group but not the control group, but the significance of between-group differences was not reported. The rationale for the method is that sympathetic activity dilates smooth muscles in the airways, while vagus nerve (parasympathetic) activity produces bronchoconstriction. The investigators found significant increases in heart rate, suggesting either an increase in beta-sympathetic activity or a decrease in vagus nerve activity. A significant decrease in heart rate variability (standard deviation) suggests that the latter may have been the case among subjects in this study.

Much more research has been done on biofeedback methods that target increasing heart rate variability (HRV). These methods teach people to breathe slowly, at a particular rate at which resonance effects in the cardiovascular system produce an interplay between respiratory sinus arrhythmia (RSA) and baroreflex activity and a maximal increase in HRV at the resonance frequency, which approximates 0.1 Hz or six breaths/minute in most people (Vaschillo, Lehrer, Rishe, & Konstantinov, 2002; Vaschillo, Vaschillo, & Lehrer, 2006). RSA is the fluctuation in heart rate that occurs at respiratory frequencies. When people breathe at their resonance frequency (and only at this frequency), RSA occurs exactly in phase with heart rate rising during inhalation and falling during exhalation (Vaschillo, Vaschillo, & Lehrer, 2004). It is generally accepted that the phenomenon is mediated by the vagus nerve (Berntson et al., 1997). The baroreflex is one of the modulating mechanisms for control of blood pressure (Eckberg & Sleight, 1992). One component of the baroreflex, which can be modeled as a “heart rate closed loop” in a systems analysis (Vaschillo et al., 2002), is the interaction between blood pressure and heart rate. Decreases in blood pressure trigger increases in heart rate, and vice versa, for increases in blood pressure. The mechanical effects of changes in blood flow, produced by changes in heart rate, then produce modulating effects on blood pressure, with a delay caused by inertia in the blood supply, which in turn determines the resonance frequency of the baroreflex system (Vaschillo et al., 2006). The heart rate closed loop in the baroreflex system is also controlled by the vagal system (Parati, Di Rienzo, & Mancia, 2000), just as in RSA.

At first glance, it is remarkable that a procedure that stimulates both RSA and the vagal baroreflex should help asthma. It is well known that vagus nerve stimulation causes bronchoconstriction; indeed, anticholinergic drugs such as atropine and ipratropium bromide that specifically block vagal activity produce bronchodilation (Maesen, Smeets, Smeets, Wald, & Cornelissen, 1995; Yanai et al., 1991), and are used regularly in asthma therapy.

Nevertheless, HRV biofeedback (HRV BF) has shown reliable, clinically significant positive effects on asthma (Lehrer et al., 2004). It has been applied clinically in Russia for many years for asthma, as well as other disorders influenced by psychosomatic factors (Chernigovskaya, Vaschillo, Petrash, & Rusanovsky, 1990). The method involves teaching the individual to increase the amplitude of heart rate accelerations that occur during inhalation and decelerations during exhalation, thus increasing the amplitude of RSA, which is the variation in heart rate that accompanies breathing: Increases in heart rate accompany inhalation and decreases accompany exhalation. For each individual, there is a specific respiratory frequency between \~4.5 and 7.0 breaths/minute at which amplitude of RSA is maximized (Vaschillo et al., 2002). This is a resonance frequency for the heart rate component in the baroreflex system (Vaschillo et al., 2006). When people breathe at this frequency, the baroreflex is systematically stimulated and baroreflex gain increased (Lehrer et al., 2003). At the same time, RSA and respiration move into perfect phase with each other (zero degrees phase relationship) only at resonance frequency (Vaschillo et al., 2004), thus improving gas exchange efficiency (Hayano & Yasuma, 2003) . A recent controlled study of HRV BF in 94 patients with asthma (Lehrer et al., 2004) found an average improvement from moderate to mild persistent asthma, with improved pulmonary function, fewer asthma exacerbations, and fewer asthma symptoms.

However, the mechanism by which HRV BF works is not known. In particular, prior research has not supported the hypothesis that changes in autonomic nervous system functioning account for the observed clinical improvements. Although research has shown consistent increases in baroreflex gain during HRV BF in healthy people, these findings were not replicated with asthma patients, nor were the clinical improvements in asthma found to be correlated with any autonomic changes. Another possible mechanism for the effects of HRV BF on asthma symptoms is an effect on airway inflammation, but this mechanism also has not yet been investigated. Because inflammation has been associated with depressed vagus nerve function, it is possible that HRV BF may affect immune function by increasing vagus nerve activity. Another possibility is that the positive results of HRV BF are due to changing mechanical properties of the airways or retraining smooth muscle activity through deep breathing and stretching of the airways, which may show systematic dysfunction in asthma (Frieri, 2005). Similarly, airway narrowing in asthma may be related to mechanical processes that are directly targeted by deep breathing (Brusasco, Crimi, & Pellegrino, 1998). This possibility also has not been investigated. It also is possible that improvement in gas exchange efficiency has indirect effects on airways reactivity in ways that are yet to be discovered.

There are some caveats in interpreting clinical efficacy of HRV BF for asthma. Most importantly, controlled clinical findings on asthma have emanated mainly from our laboratory. Cross-validation of our results in more disinterested sites is necessary. Also, although prescribing physicians were blinded to experimental conditions in our study, the study was not fully double blind (i.e., therapists were aware which condition was the active treatment), so the possibility of an experimenter bias effect remains. However, in a smaller study reported earlier from our laboratory, HRV BF was compared with an active relaxation treatment, and found to have superior effects (Lehrer et al., 1997).

the anti-inflammatory drug montelukast sodium (Kanniess, Richter, Bohme, Jorres, & Magnussen, 2002), a drug of known clinical effectiveness and in wide use for treating asthma. Our research found significant improvement in lung function measured by forced oscillation pneumography, although not reaching the criterion of 40% improvement, probably for the same reasons that precluded significant spirometry findings.

Using accepted criteria for treatment effectiveness, HRV BF should be considered “probably efficacious” for treating asthma. Efficacy has been demonstrated in a randomized placebo-controlled trial with rigorous diagnostic and outcome criteria. Although additional studies with similar results have been reported in this laboratory, independent replication has not yet been reported in other laboratories. HRV BF appears to reduce requirements for ICS medications, while improving symptoms and pulmonary function, and reducing frequency of asthma exacerbations. Further study of this promising therapy for asthma is recommended.

Additionally, although existing research using HRV BF for asthma has shown that study participants can achieve and maintain asthma improvement with less ICS medication during a biofeedback treatment program lasting about 4 months, it is not known whether long-term adherence to a biofeedback method is either obtainable or efficacious, or whether the method can be used for acute management of asthma flares. The comparative dose-dependent effects vis-à-vis various kinds of asthma controller medications (e.g., ICSs) are also not known. Accordingly, it is currently impossible to state definitively that HRV BF should be used as an ”alternative” treatment (instead of ICS medication) or a “complementary” treatment (along with ICS medication).

Finally, although one multiple case study has found significant asthma improvement using the standard measure of pulmonary function, spirometry (Lehrer, Smetankin, & Potapova, 2000), design of our controlled trial precluded this as an outcome measure. Whenever spirometry improved, ICS medications were reduced, thus holding spirometry approximately constant. Similar nonsignificant spirometry results were found using the same methodology to evaluate effects of

# Other Breathing  Methods

There is growing evidence, although with some remaining controversy, that various breathing exercises are helpful in treating asthma (Fluge et al., 1994; Holloway & West, 2007; Pearson, 2007; Thomas, 2009; Thomas et al., 2009), including yoga (Manocha, Marks, Kenchington, Peters, & Salome, 2002), although some studies have found no significant treatment effects (Fluge et al., 1994; Khanam, Sachdeva, Guleria, & Deepak, 1996; Sabina et al., 2005). Effects on asthma symptoms and quality of life tend to be stronger than effects on pulmonary function (Vedanthan et al., 1998). However, yoga methods differ from one study to the next, so it is difficult to make generalized conclusions about the effects of yoga on asthma. Even playing a musical wind instrument or singing has been reported to improve asthma (Kreuter, Kreuter, & Herth, 2008; Lucia, 1994).

# Concl usions

Various biofeedback approaches have been used to treat asthma, targeting various physiological systems, including the muscles, the autonomic nervous system, and respiration. At this time, methods that involve slow breathing show the greatest promise as asthma treatments. HRV BF appears to show the greatest and most consistent effects on measures of pulmonary function, although many breathing methods show consistent effects on asthma symptoms and quality of life.

However, the exact mediating mechanisms for these methods are unknown. In addition to autonomic modulation (through increased baroreflex sensitivity) and improved respiratory gas exchange efficiency, it is possible that slow deep breathing produces mechanical changes in the airways (e.g., stretching of some of the smooth muscles of the airways) that may relax them. It also is possible that the increased vagal activity produced by the method has direct anti-inflammatory effects, as suggested in data showing a close relationship between inflammatory activity and vagus nerve activation (Pavlov et al., 2009; Sloan et al., 2007). It also is known that breathing at a rate of approximately 0.1 Hz creates resistance to hyperventilation (Joseph et al., 2005), and that improvement in asthma is related to increases in pCO in the blood (Ritz et al., 2009). It is up to future research to determine the mechanisms by which they work, whether the various treatments differ in effectiveness, and whether they may possibly serve as alternatives to various asthma control medications, rather than as complements to these medications.

# References

Ashutosh, K. (2000). Nitric oxide and asthma: A review. Current Opinion in Pulmonary Medicine, 6(1), 21–25.

# Attention‑Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder

Vincent J. Monastra and Joel F . Lubar

Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) is a psychiatric condition characterized by symptoms of inattention, impulsivity, and hyperactivity that can occur alone or in combination. As currently defined in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual of Mental Disorders (DSM-5; American Psychiatric Association, 2013), there are three primary subtypes of this disorder: ADHD, predominately inattentive presentation; ADHD, predominately hyperactive presentation; and ADHD, combined presentation. The prevalence of ADHD has been estimated to be approximately 5% of school-age children in the United States, with international rates estimated to range from 2 to 29% (see reviews by Barkley, 2006; Bird, 2002; Monastra, 2008). Prevalence rates similar to those noted in the United States have been found Australia, Brazil, Canada, China, Germany, Italy, Japan, New Zealand, and The Netherlands, with substantially higher rate of occurrence reported in countries where poverty and malnutrition are more common (e.g., India). ADHD occurs in both males and females, with an estimated ratio of 3.4 males to 1.0 female (Barkley, 2006).

Longitudinal studies of ADHD have clearly supported the perspective that children do not typically “grow out” of ADHD. Examination of the symptom retention rates in children diagnosed with ADHD reveals that 30–80% continue to display a significant number of core ADHD symptoms into adolescence and adulthood (August,

Stewart, & Holmes, 1983; Biederman et al., 1996; Cantwell & Baker, 1989; Claude & Firestone, 1995; Mannuzza, Klein, & Addalli, 1991). In the U.S. samples studied, approximately 4% of adults met DSM-IV criteria for this diagnosis (Barkley, 2006), which is consistent with prevalence rates in children and adolescence.

Examination of the causes of this disorder supports the perspective that ADHD is a condition with a high degree of heritability (.75; Swanson & Castellanos, 2002). Genetic abnormalities have been found on chromosomes responsible for the development of dopamine receptors and reuptake transporters, as well as serotonin transporters, and norepinephrine transporters and receptors (Bobb, Castellanos, Addington, & Rapoport, 2005). Neuroimaging and quantitative electroencephalographic (QEEG) studies have confirmed that brain regions with a high density of dopamine and norepinephrine receptors and transporters appear essential for the performance of tasks requiring attention, sustained concentration, behavioral inhibition, and working memory (e.g., frontal lobes, Rolandic cortex, and cerebellum) (see reviews by Barkley, 2006; Monastra, 2008).

Without effective treatment, individuals with ADHD exhibit evidence of significant functional impairment at home, school, work settings, and in their social relationships.
