As thoroughly reviewed by Barkley (2006, 2008) and Hinshaw (2002), the vast majority of patients with ADHD experience a high degree of peer and parental rejection, underachieve in educational settings, are more likely to “drop out” of school, be unemployed or underemployed, to have a higher rate of arrests and convictions, to develop alcoholism and drug addictions, and to be at higher risk of accidental injury.

Because of the significant health and social problems associated with ADHD, there has been a substantial effort to develop effective pharmacological and behavioral treatments for this condition (see review by Monastra, 2008). At present, several types of medications for ADHD have been determined to be effective by the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA). These include methylphenidate-based medications (e.g., Ritalin, Concerta, Focalin, and Metadate-CD, Quillivant-XR), mixed amphetamine salts (e.g., Adderall, Vyvanse), atypical antidepressants (e.g., Strattera), and antihypertensives (e.g., Intuniv, Catapres, and Tenex). Other types of medications, although not specifically approved by the FDA, are also commonly used in treating comorbid symptoms of anxiety, depression, and aggression (e.g., selective serotonin reuptake inhibitors (SSRIs)-type antidepressants, mood stabilizers, anticonvulsants, and antipsychotic medications). Although pharmacological treatments for ADHD have been demonstrated to be effective in randomized, double-blind studies, in clinical practice, treatment initiation and retention rates are quite low for this type of treatment.

As reviewed by Monastra (2005), and Barkley (2008), the majority of parents of children with ADHD either refuse to initiate pharmacological treatment or discontinue use of medication in their children within 2–6 months. The range of nonadherence in published studies ranges from approximately 40% of patients (MTA [Multimodal Treatment Study of ADHD] Cooperative Group, 1999) to as many as 84% of children and 86% of adults (Perwien, Hall, Swensen, & Swindle, 2004). The primary reasons for low treatment initiation and retention rates are fear of the use of medication or the development of adverse medication side effects (Angold, Erkanli, Egger, & Costello, 2000; Costello et al., 1996; Monastra, 2005a). Such concerns prompt parents to explore a variety of behavioral and counseling procedures developed for the treatment of ADHD (see review by Pelham, 2002). However, only one of these treatments (electroencephalographic [EEG] biofeedback, more recently referred to as “neurofeedback”) has been shown to exert a significant clinical effect on the core symptoms of ADHD (see reviews by Monastra, 2008;

Arns, deRidder, Strehl, Breteler, & Coenen, 2009; Arns, Heinrich, & Strehl, 2014). Well-controlled studies of the effects of “social skills” and other types of behavioral therapy have indicated that not even the provision of intensive, social skills training exerts a significant effect on the core symptoms of ADHD (Molina et al., 2009; Riddle et al., 2013). Our purpose in this chapter is to provide an overview of the neurological and neurophysiological foundations of EEG biofeedback, and the application of this treatment in clinical practice.

In clinical research and practice, “EEG biofeedback” or “neurofeedback” is defined as a type of biofeedback in which reinforcement principles are used to increase a specified type of neuronal activity (as measured by an EEG) and/or inhibit another specified type of neuronal activity) for the purpose of reducing symptoms of a specific psychiatric or medical condition. Historically, the frequency, amplitude, or duration of activity such as theta (4–8 Hz), alpha (8–13 Hz), or beta (13 Hz or greater from certain scalp or brain locations), as well as measures of coherence and phase, has been trained in clinical research studies. Neurofeedback can also be linked to components of auditory, visual, or somatosensory event-related potentials (ERPs) or slow direct-current shifts in cortical excitability, as shown by Schneider et al. (1992) and others in the research group in Tubingen, Germany, in their work with schizophrenia and other conditions. (See Neumann, Strehl, Birbaumer, & Kotchoubey, Chapter 6, this volume, for an introduction to EEG instrumentation written by some of the Tubingen group.)

In this chapter we first provide a description of ADHD and its characteristics, a brief historical review, and a description of some of the existing therapies for this disorder. Next we present a detailed description of the EEG characteristics of ADHD, along with the rationale for new databases derived from EEG measures. We then discuss the rationale for neurofeedback, including the criteria for an effective intervention with specific individuals. We also describe the details of instrumentation and treatment protocols that are recommended for this type of training, and how they vary as a function of age, intelligence, and severity of the disorder. Pre- and posttreatment measurements used to determine the effectiveness of neurofeedback constitute the next topic. We then describe the results from our clinics, our university-based research, and those of others. Subsequently, we consider the integration of neurofeedback with other therapies, including the use of medications, family therapy, individual therapy, and parent support groups. Finally, we outline directions for future research, with an emphasis on studies examining the longterm benefits of this treatment.

As we begin to consider the use of neurotherapy in clinical practice, we want to emphasize that the application of neurotherapy in the treatment of ADHD is complex process that is guided by a number of considerations. From our perspective, it is essential to determine the type(s) of EEG abnormalities that are present in a patient prior to beginning neurofeedback. Such a consideration is critical in determining the scalp locations for training; the amplitude, frequency, and connectivity measures of the EEG that need to be enhanced or decreased; and what electrical parameters need to be incorporated into the instrumentation. Careful management of these specific variables will greatly determine the effectiveness of the treatment.

It is also critical that practitioners recognize that none of the existing pharmacological and behavioral treatments for ADHD (including neurofeedback) have been determined to effectively treat the wide range of functional impairments associated with ADHD. From our perspective, certain types of treatment are more likely to treat core symptoms of ADHD effectively (e.g., neurofeedback; medication), whereas other types of treatment (e.g., parent counseling, social skills training, academic support and accommodations at school, adult coaching) are likely to be needed to help patients learn the types of social skills they need to function effectively at home, school, work, and other social settings.

In addition, it is imperative that clinicians who plan to use neurofeedback receive sufficient training. Like any type of effective treatment, inappropriate application of the neurofeedback can lead to adverse effects. Consequently, new practitioners should receive intensive training that includes direct observation and supervision when treating patients with ADHD. It is our experience that regardless of a therapist’s prior educational background, the equivalent of a 1- 2-year internship is needed in order to become a competent neurofeedback practitioner. One needs to understand the complexity of ADHD, its many components, and how they interact and affect the lifestyle and family systems of individuals with this disorder. The Biofeedback Certification Institute of America (BCIA) now has a separate certification in neurofeedback for health care and educational professionals.

# Description of AD HD

The current scientific consensus is that ADHD is an inherited, neurologically based psychiatric disorder that is characterized by symptoms of inattention or hyperactivity/impulsivity that can occur alone or in combination (see Barkley, 2006; Monastra, 2008). The earliest published reference to children with this disorder was reported by G. F. Still, a British physician, in The Lancet (1902). He referred to the complex of behaviors we now call ADHD as “abnormal deficits in moral control . . . and wanton mischievousness and destructiveness” (p. 1011). Although Still’s perspective was that “organic” factors were causing these symptoms, considerable debate ensued about whether these symptoms were “volitional” or “organic” in nature. A shift from this “volitional” perspective on the causes of inattention, impulsivity, and hyperactivity to a neurological model emerged following an outbreak of encephalitis in the United States between 1917 and 1918. As the cognitive and behavioral consequences of this disease became evident, there was an increased awareness that such symptoms could be caused by brain disease, and the term “post-encephalitic behavior disorder” was used to classify this condition (Ebaugh, 1923; Strecker & Ebaugh, 1924; Stryker, 1925).

During the period from 1925 to 1950, it became apparent that other types of medical conditions could cause symptoms of “ADHD” (e.g., birth trauma, measles and other infections, lead poisoning, epilepsy, and head injury). Although specific medical factors could be identified in certain instances, other children displayed these symptoms without an identifiable medical cause. The term “minimal brain dysfunction” began to be used in the 1960s as a way to conceptualize the causative factors in such patients (Anderson, 1963). At that time, it was hypothesized that there was something structurally abnormal within the central nervous system (CNS).

Without clear delineation of the underlying neurological foundations of chronic symptoms of inattention, impulsivity, and hyperactivity, diagnostic classification of these symptoms continued to be based on behavioral features. In the late 1960s, DSM-II defined the condition only in terms of hyperactivity (Hyperkinetic Reaction of Childhood). However, by the early 1970s, a more comprehensive perspective on the multiple clusters of symptoms evident in these children was proposed (Wender, 1971; Douglas, 1972). As has been borne out by the thousands of studies on the core and associated symptoms of ADHD, patients with this disorder have varying degrees of impairment across a number of functional capabilities, including fine motor coordination, attention, working memory, behavioral inhibition, learning, interpersonal relationships, and emotional regulation. Douglas’s perspective on the presence of multiple symptom clusters serves as the foundation of the diagnostic criteria determined in each of the subsequent DSM revisions.

# Ass essm ent of AD HD

As specified in DSM-5 (American Psychiatric Association, 2013), the diagnosis of ADHD is determined on the basis of five criteria. Criterion A: A child needs to demonstrate (1) six or more symptoms of “inattention” from a listing of nine separate categories (however, for patients age 17 or older, the minimum number of symptoms for diagnosis is reduced to five and/or (2) six or more symptoms of “hyperactivity and impulsivity” from a listing of nine distinct categories (with the required number of symptoms being reduced to five for adolescents age 17 and older). These symptoms must have lasted for at least 6 months and occur to an extent that is maladaptive and inconsistent with the child or adolescent’s developmental level. Onset of symptoms must have occurred before the age of 12 (Criterion B), and some impairment caused by these symptoms needs to be present in two or more settings (Criterion C). In addition, there must be clear evidence of clinically significant impairment in social, academic, or occupational functioning (Criterion D). Criterion E: Finally, similar to the diagnostic criteria for other psychiatric disorders, the practitioner also needs to differentiate ADHD from other medical and psychiatric conditions that can cause chronic symptoms of inattention, impulsivity, and hyperactivity.

As reviewed by Monastra (2008), medical conditions that can “mimic” ADHD include sleep disorders, seizure disorders, traumatic brain injury, visual disorders (acuity, tracking, and convergence), hearing disorders, poor dietary habits, anemia, hypoglycemia, and thyroid disorders; deficiencies of vitamins D and B, zinc, calcium, and magnesium; and certain food allergies (e.g., corn, wheat, gluten, eggs, nuts, cocoa, and dyes). Psychiatric disorders that can impair attention, concentration, and behavioral control include pervasive developmental disorders, mental retardation, schizophrenia, mood disorder, anxiety disorder, dissociative disorder, personality disorder, and conduct disorders. As emphasized by Monastra (2008), such conditions need to be systematically and directly evaluated in order to meet diagnostic criteria.

One of the primary problems in differential diagnosis results from the high degree of comorbidity between ADHD and other psychiatric disorders. Reviews of the scientific literature reveal that the vast majority of patients diagnosed with ADHD also meet diagnostic criteria for another psychiatric disorder (e.g., Barkley, 2006; Klein & Mannuzza, 1991; Monastra, 2008; Pliszka, 2000; Biederman, Wilens, Mick, Spencer, & Faraone, 1999). Table 30.1 summarizes the comorbidity rates across studies.

Given this high rate of comorbidity, it is relatively rare in clinical practice to treat an individual who meets diagnostic criteria for ADHD alone. However, due to the profound impact that untreated ADHD exerts on mood, anxiety, anger control, substance abuse patterns, and behavioral disorders, practitioners are advised to screen/treat other relevant medical conditions, and subsequently treat ADHD before proceeding with pharmacological and psychological interventions for these comorbid conditions (Barkley, 2006; Monastra, 2008). Unfortunately, medical screening is rarely conducted in clinical practice, with fewer than 26% of primary care physicians and pediatricians reporting use of even minimal hematological studies prior to initiating pharmacological treatment for ADHD (Chan, Hopkins, Perrin, Herrerias, & Homer, 2005). This lack of comprehensive medical evaluation prior to initiating medication for ADHD is one of the primary reasons cited by parents who decline to begin treatment for their children (Costello et al., 1996; Angold et al., 2000; Monastra, 2005a) and seems likely to contribute to low rates of medication response reported in community studies.

TA BLE 30.1. Comorbidity Rates of ADHD and O ther Psychiatric Disorders   
![](images/f9f9bdc51ac0a91040cdfc0d4532c4532b8c10899c576d4492bd9a51040deeda.jpg)

Detailed descriptions of the process for determining a diagnosis of ADHD and associated comorbidities have been published (Barkley, 2006; Monastra, 2008). As emphasized by both authors, interviews, review of educational and medical records, targeted hematological and other laboratory procedures, and administration of normreferenced assessment tools are needed. Initially, an interview with the patient and parent(s), guardian(s), spouse is helpful in identify the primary causes of concern. Semistructured interview formats such as the Clinical History Questionnaire (Monastra, 2008) can be subsequently administered in order to identify concerns overlooked or “forgotten” by informants during clinical interviews.

In addition to interview and review of educational, developmental, and medical histories, norm-referenced behavioral rating scales are commonly used in order to determine whether the frequency of symptoms is atypical given the patient’s age. Measures such as the Conners-3 Parent and Teacher Rating Scales (Conners, 2014), the Child Behavior Checklist (Achenbach, 2001), the Behavioral Assessment System for Children (BASC-2; Reynolds & Kamphaus, 2004), and the Attention Deficit Disorders Evaluation Scales (McCarney, 2004) provide such a comparison, which is essential in the determination of a diagnosis of ADHD. Such behavioral rating scales also assist in the determination of the severity of common comorbid symptoms (e.g., oppositionalism, anxiety, mood disorder). In adults, the use of instruments such as the Minnesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory (Hathaway & McKinley, 2001) and the Conners Adult ADHD Rating Scales (Conners, Erhardt, & Sparrow, 1999) are helpful for this purpose.

Because observations of the frequency of core ADHD symptoms can vary based on rater style, bias, and the context in which the child is observed, continuous performance tests (CPTs) are often helpful in obtaining a more “objective assessment “of an individual’s capacity for sustained attention and behavioral control. CPTs such as the Test of Variables of Attention (TOVA; Greenberg, 2004) and the Integrated Visual and Auditory Test of Attention (Sanford, 2004) provide an evaluation of such abilities in a standardized format. Both rating scales and CPTs are useful tools not only in the process of diagnostic formulation but also as indicators of response to treatment. Although other types of neuropsychological tests may be of interest from a clinical research perspective, and may provide an indication of the range and severity of functional impairment associated with ADHD, they are not essential for the diagnostic process. Similarly, tests of intelligence and academic achievement can be helpful in determining comorbid learning disorders; however, they are not highly sensitive or specific for ADHD.

# Treatment of AD HD

The earliest reported treatment of ADHD involved the use of psychostimulants in the late 1930s. Bradley (1937) observed that amphetamine sulfate significantly decreased the hyperkinetic behavior of children and allowed them to function better in social and academic settings. At that time, the reason for the positive results from using stimulants with hyperactivity was not understood. During the latter part of the 20th century, two hypotheses were proposed to explain the neurological foundations of ADHD and how stimulants might be useful for patients with ADHD: the low-arousal hypothesis developed by Satterfield and Dawson in 1971 and further elaborated by Satterfield, Lesser, Saul, and Cantwell (1973) and the noradrenergic hypothesis, developed over years and elaborated by Zametkin et al (1990) and Castellanos et al. (1996). More recently, researchers have postulated that impairments of alerting, behavioral inhibition, working memory, and affective control were due to a dysfunction of cortical arousal and modulation (DCAM; Nigg, 2000; Pliszka, 2005; Sergeant, 2005; Hermens, 2006). This model reflects more recent neuroimaging and QEEG results indicating that patients with ADHD can demonstrate either low or excessive cortical arousal.

Essentially, the first two hypotheses propose that children with ADHD/attention deficit disorder (ADD) experience low arousal. This low arousal comes about as the result of decreased impact of sensory stimuli, in all modalities, acting on the CNS mechanisms for sensory integration and resulting in these children’s engaging in stimulus-seeking behavior. Self-stimulation and atypical object play are primary features of the hyperactive (hyperkinetic) syndrome in particular (Lubar & Shouse, 1976, 1977; Shouse & Lubar, 1978). Children with hyperactivity often exhibit intense but brief interest in new stimuli. For example, they rub objects against their bodies, smell them, taste them if they can, and often engage in other stimulus-seeking behaviors (e.g., excessive movement, spinning around, running from place to place within a room, or picking up one object after another and examining it). If they are in a room where there is very little stimulation, they very often fall asleep after a brief flurry of activity.

The basis for this stimulus-seeking behavior may lie in decreased noradrenergic activity, particularly in the brainstem reticular formation, and perhaps in the basal ganglia as well. Heilman, Voeller, and Nadeau (1991) and Riccio, Hynd, Cohen, and Gonzalez (1993) proposed that in many of these children, as well as adults with this disorder, there is a defect in response inhibition—particularly in the nigrostriatal system, more on the right side of the brain than the left side. Motor restlessness may reflect not only frontal lobe dysfunction but also possible impairment of the dopaminergic system. Basically, we think of arousal as being mediated by (1) connections from the brainstem reticular formation, which receives inputs from all of the sensory modalities except olfaction; (2) the transmission of this information to the diffuse thalamic (reticular) projection system; and (3) the basal forebrain, which does receive olfactory projections. These input systems, in conjunction with the basal ganglia and the cerebellum (which is involved in programming the output of the motor cortex), are all affected by decreased noradrenergic activity. Norepinephrine, a key transmitter in this system, is produced by a very important brainstem nucleus located in the midbrain, the locus coeruleus. This nucleus has extensive projections with these mesolimbic, striatal, and forebrain systems. Not only is the neuroanatomy of these systems complex, but the resulting neurochemical dysfunctions and/or neuropathologies associated with them are also complex. The latter include movement disorders and mood disorders. As we discuss below, these abnormalities in neurochemistry and neurophysiology are reflected in EEG measures, in ERP measures, and in regional brain metabolism.

In addition to norepinephrine, the neurotransmitter dopamine serves an essential role in attentional mechanisms. There are two dopaminergic systems in the human brain. One originates in the substantia nigra, and one of the extrapyramidal motor structures is involved through the basal ganglia in the modulation of motor activity via the motor cortex. The second originates in a region called the ventral tegmental area in the midbrain and is often referred to as the ventral dopaminergic system; it streams anteriorly through the subcallosal cortex under the anterior portion of the corpus callosum, then is widely distributed to the prefrontal lobes and the anterior cingulate gyrus. This system has been identified as the attentionmodulating system (Posner & Raichle, 1994).

Such “low arousal” has been proposed as the foundation of a wide range of psychiatric disorders, in addition to ADHD (Blum, Cull, Braverman, & Comings, 1996). These authors refer to this condition as the “reward deficiency syndrome” (RDS) and propose that this condition occurs when there are abnormalities in dopamine production, transmission, and reuptake. These authors also postulated that RDS can be associated with similar abnormalities involving the neurotransmitters norepinephrine and serotonin, a finding initially noted by Zametkin and Rapoport (1987). Sometimes it is additionally associated with abnormalities in norepinephrine and serotonin metabolism. Serotonin is important in the modulation of mood states. Individuals experiencing RDS often have ADHD combined with substance use disorders. There are other symptoms of RDS: obsessive behavior, compulsive gambling, carbohydrate bingeing, excessive cigarette smoking, and other reward-seeking and thrill-seeking behaviors, all of which are linked to abnormal alleles associated with either dopamine production, dopamine transport across the synapse, or the breakdown of dopamine into metabolites that are eventually excreted. Evidence of dopamine dysfunction can even be seen in neuroimaging studies involving the single-photon emission computed tomography (SPECT) scan, as described by Amen and Carmichael (1997); these studies again confirm how pervasive attentional disorders are from a structural and neurochemical point of view from childhood to adulthood.

More recently, the DCAM model has been proposed (Hermens, 2006; Monastra, 2008). In contrast to prior models that emphasized the role of dopaminergic pathways, the DCAM model considers the interaction among multiple cortical regions and the role of multiple neurotransmitters in conceptualizing the neurological foundations of ADHD. As noted by Monastra (2008), the DCAM appears to be more consistent with emerging neuroscience findings that implicate multiple neurotransmitters (dopamine, norepinephrine, serotonin) and cortical regions (cerebellum, basal ganglia, corpus callosum, frontal lobes) in symptom onset and maintenance.

The DCAM perspective is that arousal, cortical activation, and modulation involves an interaction involving multiple regions of the brain, beginning with the ascending reticular activating system (ARAS), which includes noradrenergic, serotonergic, and dopaminergic pathways. External stimuli trigger the activation of noradrenergic pathways originating in the brainstem, priming the posterior attention system of the brain, and activating the release of adrenaline. The locus coeruleus appears particularly integral in this process, stimulating multiple brain regions that are involved in arousal, attention, and orienting functions. The release of adrenaline further helps to prepare the body to react and simultaneously activates frontal regions (which control executive functions and are primarily mediated by dopaminergic neurons). On QEEG examination, this process is reflected by a shift from slow brain waves (delta, theta) to faster frequencies (alpha, beta) appearing over various frontal locations.

As frontal regions of the brain are activated, a secondary process of arousal control can be initiated. As the degree of frontal cortical excitation increases, the frontal lobes provide feedback to the ARAS, triggering the slowing or “braking” of the system via serotonergic pathways. From the DCAM perspective, both ascending and descending pathways are essential for the regulation of arousal and attention. The DCAM proposes that the core symptoms of ADHD can occur from excessive or deficient activation of neuronal systems involved in cortical arousal and regulation. Patterns of both deficient (i.e., hypoarousal) and excessive activation (hyperarousal) have been consistently reported in QEEG studies of patients with ADHD (see review by Snyder & Hall, 2006; Monastra, 2008).

As would be expected, based on such a perspective, medications for ADHD exert their effects by enhancing the activity of divergent neurotransmitter pathways. For instance, certain FDA-approved psychostimulants inhibit reuptake of the neurotransmitter dopamine (e.g., Ritalin, Focalin, Concerta, Metadate-CR). Another class of psychostimulants increase release of dopamine and norepinephrine and block reuptake of these neurotransmitters (e.g., Adderall-XR; Vyvanse). Still other FDA-approved medications inhibit the reuptake of norepinephrine (atomoxetine) or suppress cortical arousal via adrenalin pathways (alpha-agonists such as guanfacine, clonidine, and Intuniv). Although QEEG assessment can facilitate identification of the type of dysregulation of cortical arousal in a specific patient and aid in medication selection (see review in Monastra, 2008) such an assessment is not commonly used in clinical practice. From our perspective, this lack of matching between type of neurological dysfunction and type of medication contributes to insufficient medication response and the emergence of adverse side effects. It can also lead to an underestimation of the effect of neurotherapy in clinical research studies and to insufficient response to this type of treatment in clinical practice.

In clinical practice, the primary challenge centers on identifying treatments that effectively treat the underlying brain dysfunction in a specific child or adult. In particular, treatment of abnormalities of the prefrontal lobes of the brain appear essential, because they are the “executive” portions of the brain that control planning and judgment (Fuster, 2015). While inappropriate behaviors occurring today can lead to very significant future consequences, children with ADHD ignore such considerations and often live for the present. It has very little meaning for them when they are told that they may get a bad grade at the end of a grading period if their assignments are not completed. This is especially true if the end of the grading period is several weeks or months away. One of the goals of behavior therapy is to make consequences more salient and immediate (e.g., rewarding the child for completing particular assignments, perhaps punishing the child by withdrawing rewards or by applying “time outs” when assignments are not completed). These techniques may work well for a particular circumstance, but the effects may not generalize well, and they may need to be repeated on a day-to-day basis. Individuals without ADHD do not have the types of dysregulation of cortical arousal and modulation noted in patients with ADHD. As a result, their ability to understand why not getting the assignment done will be important for them is not impaired. They more readily stop engaging in nonproductive behaviors that lead to punishment or withdrawal of rewards, because they can plan for the future and understand the consequences of behaviors. By modulating activation of pathways controlling such behaviors, medications can exert a positive effect on the core symptoms of ADHD.

Among the psychological treatments for ADHD (e.g., cognitive-behavioral therapy; reinforcement and punishment of inattentive, impulsive and hyperactive behaviors; social skills training; parent counseling; family therapy; neurofeedback), only neurofeedback has been demonstrated to be an effective treatment for ADHD (see reviews by Monastra, 2008; Arns et al., 2009, 2014). This would be expected; of the available psychological treatments, only neurofeedback (like medication) specifically targets the treatment of the abnormal neurological foundations of ADHD.

The main hypothesis underlying the use of neurofeedback is simply stated as follows: If ADHD is associated with neurological/biological dysfunction, particularly at the cortical level and primarily involving prefrontal lobe function, and if the underlying neurological deficit can be corrected, a child with ADHD may be able to develop strategies and insights (paradigms) that children without ADHD already have. With the use of these paradigms, the abilities to organize, plan, and understand the consequences of inappropriate behavior are facilitated. As a result, we would anticipate a much stronger carryover of the effectiveness of behavioral approaches, once treatment of the neurological causes of ADHD is established. Consequently, the primary task of the practitioner using neurotherapy is to determine the type(s) of abnormal activation patterns in an individual, and develop and implement a feedback strategy to modify such abnormal EEG “behaviors.”

# EE G Ch aracteristics  of AD HD/ADD

EEG abnormalities were reported in children who would now be classified as having ADD or ADHD as early as the 1930s (Jasper, Solomon, & Bradley, 1938). Much of the early literature was reviewed in the supplement to the Journal of Child Neurology, published in 1991. Basically, the early EEG studies indicated excessively slow activity in central and frontal regions of the brain. These studies are supported by recent positron emission tomography (PET) and SPECT studies, which also indicate abnormalities in cerebral metabolism in these particular brain areas. More recent EEG research by Chabot and Serfontein (1996), Clarke and Barry (2004), and our research team (Monastra et al., 1999; Monastra, Lubar, & Linden, 2001) has revealed several neurophysiological “subtypes” of patients with ADHD. These findings are consistent with SPECT evaluations conducted by Amen and Paldi (1993) and Amen and Carmichael (1997), who reported three neurological “subtypes” of patients with ADHD. One subtype involves frontal lobe deactivation. These children manifest decreased metabolism in prefrontal regions of the brain and, according to these investigators, are good candidates for the use of stimulant medications, as well as neurofeedback. Other children show deficits in limbic system activity and are characterized as having oppositional behavior, emotional outbursts, and impulsiveness. These children also show significant decreased metabolism in their prefrontal lobes and the anterior cingulate gyrus. They are good candidates for the use of tricyclic antidepressants, such as imipramine or desipramine. The third subgroup comprises individuals who have increased activity in the medial superior frontal gyrus. Although these individuals experience ADD, they are often characterized as having an attention deficit with obsessive– compulsive disorder. They have a very short attention span and are often impulsive and oppositional. They take an excessive time to complete required work, because they become obsessed with sometimes irrelevant material and cannot seem to discriminate between what is essential and what is nonessential. These patients seem more likely to respond to clomipramine or antihypertensives. By far the most common group of children with ADHD is those with excessively slow activity in frontal and central brain regions (Chabot & Serfontein, 1996; Monastra et al., 1999, 2001; Clarke & Barry, 2004; Snyder & Hall, 2006).

Our examination of QEEG characteristics began with a group of 25 children with ADD without hyperactivity or learning disability and 27 controls, carefully matched for IQ and socioeconomic status (Mann, Lubar, Zimmerman, Miller, & Muenchen, 1992). They were all righthanded males. Differences between the groups during baseline studies and reading and drawing challenges were examined and displayed in topographic maps. During the academic challenges, there were significant increases in slow (\~8 Hz) theta activity along the midline and in the frontal regions, and decreased beta activity, especially along the midline and posteriorly. This was the first quantitative, multichannel neurological study to show that children with pure ADD versus ADHD are statistically different in terms of their EEG characteristics.

Subsequently, we collected data on the Lexicor NRS24 system (Boulder, CO) to develop a database examining one particular measure of EEG activity. This measure is the power ratio of theta activity (4–8 Hz) divided by beta activity (13–21 Hz). This baseline study has involved the following protocol: A cap (Electro Cap, Inc.) containing all 19 of the electrodes according to the International 10-20 System of EEG electrode placement is fitted to the participant’s head. There are different caps, depending on head size (see Figure 30.1). The cap is connected both to a harness that holds it tightly in place and to a band that is placed around the chest. Electrode gel is placed into each of the electrode cups embedded in the cap, and impedances of less than 5 kohms are obtained before recording can be started. Linked ears are used as references for the 19 cortical channels.

The subject is then placed in front of an easel, which is used to present materials at different times during the test protocol. The test consists of six parts, each with an approximate duration of 3 minutes. It begins with an eyes-open baseline, which is followed by an eyes-closed baseline. The subject is then requested to read material silently that is grade- or age-level-appropriate. (Participants are first asked to read out loud, so that the experimenter can assess fluency before the material is presented silently.) The participant next reproduces figures from the Beery–Bender Visual– Motor Gestalt Test, then completes Raven’s Progressive Color Matrices Test. The next task involves the experimenter’s reading to the subject content that continues the material that the child has read silently before. Additional tasks sometimes follow the formal database testing. These may involve the Coding task from the Wechsler Intelligence Scale for Children—Revised (WISCR) or WISC-III, or measures from the Halstead– Reitan Battery, to see whether there are unusual patterns of cognitive activity associated with these neuropsychological tasks.

The data from the 19 recording channels are analyzed by compressed power spectral analysis and displayed as a multicolored topographical brain map showing the absolute activity in microvolts for each of the different bandpasses of interest. A second set of maps compares all of the bandpasses on the same numerical scale, so that it is possible to evaluate where the primary abnormality is occurring—that is, theta, beta, or some other bandpass.

![](images/7daedb144e0c3a88cd26df302d9503f523eb271f4cf08d34c83171cdaeff49ff.jpg)  
FI GURE 30.1. Locations of the standard 19 electrode placements, according to the International 10-20 System.

The database originally developed by Mann et al. (1992) was based on 16 channels of information recorded with electrodes placed on the International 10-20 System sites and attached with Nihon Kohden Elefix paste. The data from the Nihon Kohden EEG were analyzed by an offline analysis system called Stellate, developed by 1990. Some of these data were presented in a review paper (Lubar, 1991). Significant differences (p < .05 and p < .001) were obtained for many central and frontal locations. The current single-channel database replicates these findings and extends them to older groups (Monastra et al., 1999).

Several large-scale studies have been published since this original work in 1992 (see review by Snyder & Hall, 2006). Chabot and Serfontein (1996), for example, studied 407 children with ADD and 310 controls. Using a discriminant function analysis, they were able to distinguish the children with ADD from the control group, with a specificity of 88% and a sensitivity of 94%. They then showed that two major subtypes of ADD could be accounted for by 96% of the EEG profiles. These subtypes were distinguished by either slowing in frontal regions or increased activity in the frontal areas. Hypoarousal, as evidenced by increased slow activity in frontal and central areas, is a primary marker for children with the inattentive type of ADHD and for many of those who also have hyperactivity. Hyperarousal, as evidenced by increased beta activity in the frontal region, is often seen in individuals with ADD in combination with comorbid symptoms of anxiety or impaired anger control.

Lazarro et al. (1998) replicated our original 1992 work by showing that theta activity is increased in adolescents with ADHD in the anterior regions, and that beta activity is decreased in posterior areas. Subsequent large-scale studies reported by Clarke and his associates (e.g., see Clarke & Barry, 2004) were supportive of the perspective that ADHD is primarily associated with cortical “hypoarousal” over frontal and midline central locations and that a secondary pattern characterized by “hyperarousal” in these regions is evident in a minority of patients with ADHD.

Our research has also shown that it is not always necessary to evaluate activity in all 19 channels of the traditional International 10-20 System of EEG electrode localization. For example, in our multicenter study (Monastra et al., 1999), we evaluated 482 individuals between ages 6 and 30 during four 90-second conditions: an eyes-open baseline, and reading, listening, and drawing tasks. Because our prior research had indicated that the greatest differentiation between individuals with ADHD and nonclinical controls was noted at central midline locations (Cz, Fz), as well as across the frontal lobes (F3, F4), we were interested in evaluating whether a simplified “QEEG Scanning Process” that analyzed EEG recordings at the vertex could serve as a basis for assessing cortical activation patterns in individuals being screened for ADHD. We measured the ratio of theta activity (4–8 Hz) to beta activity (13– 21 Hz) in terms of electrophysiological power at the vertex (Cz) on each of these tasks. The average of these power ratios across the four conditions was then calculated (termed the “Attention Index”). We found for all age groups (and now have data on persons up to age 64) that there were significant differences in the Attention Index between controls and individuals diagnosed with ADHD, predominately inattentive, hyperactive/impulsive, or combined types. The importance of this approach is that it provides a simple screening method for making an initial assessment of presence/type of dysregulation of cortical arousal and modulation. The norms for individuals ages 6 through 30 have been published (Monastra et al., 1999) to provide practitioners with a simple guide for assessing cortical activation at the vertex. Table 30.2 summarizes our initial findings in patients diagnosed with ADHD and healthy age peers.

Using a cutoff ratio of +1.5 standard deviation (SD), we found that cortical hypoarousal was found in 86% of patients with ADHD. Only 2% of individuals without ADHD demonstrated a significantly elevated Attention Index. Cortical hyperarousal (i.e., atypically low theta/beta ratios) has been found in approximately 5–10% of patients screened in our validation and cross-validation studies (Monastra et al., 1999, 2001). Subsequently reported meta-analyses have revealed variability in the frequency of this secondary, neurophysiological “subtype” among individuals examined in over a dozen articles that subsequently have been published (see meta-analyses by Snyder & Hall, 2006; Arns, Conners, & Kraemer, 2013).

TA BLE 30.2. Critical Values for ADHD   
![](images/92a83d4fc4286c20ff6e5588f1e74ffe4853516c8719e76286157b9b28bf73cc.jpg)
Note. Data from Monastra et al. (1999).

The results of our initial study were cross-validated in a subsequent study that we conducted (Monastra et al., 2001). In this study, 129 individuals ages 6– 20 (98 males and 31 females) were evaluated using rating scales, CPTs, structured clinical interviews, and computerized power spectral analysis of the EEG. The EEG measures employed were the same as those in our previous study: power ratios of theta to beta at location C , the vertex. Participants were accurately classified as experiencing DSM-IV-defined ADHD of the inattentive type or of the hyperactive–impulsive or combined type. Of the participants classified as having ADHD, 90% scored positively for ADHD on the quantitative EEG, and 94% of the participants classified as nonclinical controls scored negatively for ADHD on the quantitative EEG evaluation. A second study reported in that same publication involved 285 additional individuals ages 6–20, with a male-to-female ratio of 3:1. A test–retest correlation from the quantitative EEG analysis performed on two occasions indicated considerable stability in the measures. The correlation was .96, indicating that the findings derived from a simplified QEEG-scanning process are very consistent over time.

Although the theta/beta ratio and other neurophysiological indices can be reliably measured at any time, analysis of QEEG patterns across the lifespan reveals a significant maturational effect. Such maturational effects on the theta/beta ratio are evident in our database, as well as commercially available ones (e.g., Neuroguide; Applied Neuroscience, Inc.). For example, many published studies (see review by Barry, Clarke, & Johnstone, 2003) illustrate age-related differences in the distribution of EEG activity in different bands from low delta to high beta. In general, maturation effects are characterized by a reduction in the theta/beta ratio over frontal and central midline regions, reflecting a combination of decreased delta and theta power, with corresponding increases in alpha and beta. In our studies, we have consistently noted a correlation of approximately –.40 for age and the theta/ beta ratio (p < .01).

# Rational e for N eurofeedback

Patients with ADHD display impairment of abilities mediated by the frontal lobe. They have difficulty orienting to significant aspects of their educational, home, and social environments, and they tend to shift more rapidly the focus of their attention. They display impairment in their ability to inhibit extraneous movement as they attend to aspects of their environment and can be restless, impulsive, and hyperactive. Their informationprocessing speed is often delayed, and they make a far greater number of errors on tasks that require them to observe stimuli, and differentially respond to some stimuli and inhibit response to others (“go/no-go” tasks). Other executive functions, including working memory and creative problem solving, are also impaired.

Parents of children with ADHD and adults with this disorder present for treatment because of such functional impairments. Children with this condition are typically referred because of difficulty attending in class, comprehending while reading, organizing and completing writing tasks, and difficulty recalling information during tests and examinations. Those with symptoms of hyperactivity/impulsivity present with additional problems resulting from their tendency to “act first and think later,” which contributes to a high rate of disciplinary referrals at school and criminal offenses in the community. Adults presenting for treatment typically have a combination of core ADHD symptoms and mood and anxiety disorders that are the result of decades of “failure” at home, school, and work settings.

Neurofeedback seeks to ameliorate such symptoms by changing the abnormal patterns of activation noted over frontal and central midline brain regions. The underlying hypothesis of neurofeedback is that changing these abnormal activations patterns will result in a significant improvement in attention, behavioral inhibition, working memory, and problem solving, and lead to more rapid acquisition of the kinds of skills needed to function more successfully at home, school, work, and other social settings. Like medications approved for the treatment of ADHD, neurofeedback has been associated with changes in abnormal patterns of cortical activation in patients with ADHD. However, similar to medication, it does not “teach” essential life skills. Rather, the primary purpose is to improve attention, capacity for sustained concentration, behavioral inhibition, working memory, and problem solving. Like medication, various types of academic tutoring, social skills training, parent counseling, family therapy, and life skills coaching are typically necessary in order to achieve optimal treatment response.

As will be subsequently reviewed in detail, neurofeedback has been demonstrated to be an effective treatment for ADHD in individuals age 6 through adulthood, who are of at least average intelligence and have a primary diagnosis of ADHD. However, it is important to emphasize that because a number of medical conditions can “mimic” such symptoms, individuals seeking neurofeedback treatment for ADHD need to be thoroughly screened for such conditions. Similarly, it is important that therapists closely monitor the dietary and sleep patterns of their neurofeedback patients, since significant variations in attention and capacity for learning occur in response to sleep deficits and inadequate protein intake at breakfast and lunch.

In addition, practitioners are advised that neurofeedback has not been demonstrated to be an effective treatment for individuals who have been diagnosed with mental retardation, psychosis, or for individuals with ADHD who are also being treated for a seizure disorder with anticonvulsant medications. Similarly, individuals who have been diagnosed with alcoholism or other psychoactive substance abuse disorders have not shown significant improvement in the treatment of ADHD until they have achieved sustained, full remission of their abuse/dependence (6–12 months sustained abstinence).

Finally, practitioners treating children in families in which there is significant parental disagreement regarding the use of neurofeedback would be well advised to postpone offering this type of treatment until such a disagreement can be resolved. In our clinical work, we typically encourage parents who are divorced, separated, or experiencing significant marital discord to proceed from the standpoint of mutual agreement. For instance, if there is disagreement over the benefits of neurofeedback, we encourage the parents to proceed with whatever other types of intervention in which there is agreement (e.g., requesting a meeting to develop a 504 Accommodation Plan or Individual Education Plan; obtaining comprehensive medical screening/treatment for other relevant medical problems; improving the child’s dietary and sleep habits; proceeding with a brief trial of behavioral therapy or parent counseling; introducing stimulant medication). We have found that by stressing the value of mutually agreeable treatments, we can establish a working therapeutic relationship with both parents that can lead to a more successful outcome for the child.

Often practitioners express concern that concurrent treatment with stimulant therapy can interfere with the effectiveness of neurotherapy. We have not found this perspective to be supported by the literature or our published clinical studies. For example, as reported by Monastra, Monastra, and George (2002) in their study of 100 individuals who had been thoroughly evaluated and diagnosed with ADHD, each of the 51 participants treated with neurofeedback and stimulant therapy demonstrated significant improvement on behavioral, neuropsychological, and QEEG measures. These changes were noted at 12-, 18-, and 24-month follow-up. Such improvements were also sustained during follow-up assessments conducted with and without administration of stimulant medication. In addition, it was noteworthy that all of the participants who received neurotherapy (and stimulant therapy) in that study were able to reduce their dose of medication. Approximately 40% were able sustain clinical gains without use of any stimulant medication.

Because children with significant hyperactivity and impulsivity can engage in behaviors that result in significant educational and social consequences, practitioners providing neurofeedback for patients with ADHD, predominately hyperactive/ impulsive presentation and ADHD, combined presentation may find it beneficial to use a combination of medication and neurofeedback. However, in our clinical research and practice, we have found that children treated with both interventions can begin to show adverse side effects (and require reduction in dose or change of potency of medication) approximately midway through their neurofeedback treatment (Sessions 15–20). Symptoms including increased irritability, moodiness, aggression, and restlessness in a child who was previously responding well to a regimen of stimulant therapy can occur as neurofeedback begins to alter previously abnormal patterns of cortical activation over frontal and central, midline regions of the brain.

# Specification Criteria for N eurofeedback I nstrumentation

At the present time, there are nearly a dozen instruments designed and developed for the use of neurofeedback for ADD/ADHD. The main requirements for appropriate instrumentation include very accurate signal processing, with the ability to discriminate changes as small as 0.1 mV for purposes of setting thresholds. The ideal instrumentation must allow the researcher, clinician, and patient to observe the raw signal and to see how it is processed, in order to produce reward or inhibition of reward and to observe the relationship between changes in events in the raw EEG and the feedback. Current systems employ both analogue and digital processing. Such systems have been described in detail previously (Lubar & Culver, 1978; Lubar, 1991; Lubar & Lubar, 1999).

Individuals engaged in neurofeedback treatment have to fulfill several well-defined physiological criteria simultaneously in order to receive “reinforcement.” They have to increase either sensorimotor rhythm (SMR; between 12 and 14 Hz), especially if they are hyperactive, or beta activity (often defined as 16–20 Hz)—and they must do so without concurrently producing excessive theta (4–8 Hz, theta–alpha; 8.5–11.0 Hz: “thalpha”), movement or surface electromyographic (EMG) activity (52–58 Hz). The task is to isolate the SMR or beta activity, and to increase its duration, its prevalence, and (if possible) its amplitude, while simultaneously decreasing the amplitude and percentage of theta, thalpha, EMG activity, or gross movement. To do this, the participant has to be not only very alert but also relaxed.

Children with ADD/ADHD have great difficulty staying on tasks that are prolonged, of low interest, and repetitive (e.g., school-related tasks). Although they can sustain attention for prolonged periods while engaged in tasks that are exciting and of high interest (e.g., computer and video games), there has been no report of significant transfer of training in those types of games to school settings. The essence of neurofeedback treatment involves engagement in a continuous performance-type task under an “altered” and more attentive EEG state for prolonged time periods.

There are different explanations regarding the mechanism of how neurofeedback works. For example, one might propose that individuals can learn to become aware of the different EEG states and discriminate when they are producing alpha, SMR, theta, beta, or other frequencies. From such a perspective, individuals simply learn to produce the desired EEG pattern in the appropriate setting. They learn to produce beta when they need to concentrate, theta and/or low-frequency alpha when they want to relax and experience a considerable amount of visual imagery, and higher alpha when they want to relax in a more blank mind, “open-focus” state. However, other individuals undergoing neurofeedback have shared with us the perspective that they do not know exactly how they produce the different EEG patterns. However, they, too, are able to do so on request.

At one level, this is evidence that there may be unconscious processes operating in this type of learning phenomenon, and that an individual can learn without direct awareness. Evidence to support learning without awareness has been discussed and debated (e.g., Kamiya, 1979) for more than a quarter of a century. Individuals without ADHD are very aware of changes in their levels of alertness. If a person is reading a very exciting passage or engaged in listening to a particularly powerful audio presentation or speech, he or she usually becomes very alert, fixates on the speaker or on the material being presented, and actively processes the content. If, after a while, the individual experiences fatigue or wandering attention, or forgets the content, the EEG shows a shift toward lower frequencies. Sometimes individuals experiencing such an attentional drift can force themselves into an attentive state, and this is associated with higher-frequency EEG activity. However, individuals with ADHD are likely to have greater difficulty making these discriminations and making such “shifts.” Therefore, they may require additional information (in the form of feedback) to be repeatedly presented and reinforced before they begin to develop (in demand settings) an almost “reflexive” normal response to shifts in attention. This allows them to increase their level of concentration and focusing “on demand.”

Instrumentation appropriately designed for this type of treatment must provide easily recognizable and understandable feedback stimuli that indicate when training criteria (parameters) have been met. Some of the instruments use game displays, such as color wheels that light up sequentially with different colors every time a burst of beta activity occurs with a specific duration and specific amplitude. Other training games display an airplane flying above a specified threshold level line and a tone that changes in frequency or intensity, depending on the EEG signal generated by the patient. Another training game displays a river with fish jumping when all reward and inhibit criteria are simultaneously met for a brief period (typically, .5 seconds). Both signals correspond to the amplitude and duration of the rewarded activity. These instruments also provide warning lights that are activated when the person produces theta (or, in some cases, alpha) above a set threshold or EMG activity above threshold. Gross body movement will usually activate the theta filter. In some cases, filters with lower bandpasses in the region of 0–2 or 0–4 Hz are also used to detect gross body movements.

More recent systems employ complex animation or animated sequences and music from compact discs or synthesized wave tables that are regulated by the EEG activity patterns produced by the patient. Some of these symptoms can present DVDs of movies that can change in size, brightness, clarity and other features depending on the patient’s production of targeted reward and inhibit frequencies. The very latest technology rewards the patient when he or she is producing EEG patterns that are within 1.0 SD of the Z-scores derived from a normative database. This type of feedback can be based on measures of amplitude and power within specific frequency bands, as well as connectivity measures such as coherence, phase, and symmetry. We hope that this type of feedback will lead to more rapid learning.

High-quality instrumentation should also record and score data for each session. The most useful EEG parameters to record are (1) average microvolt levels of rewarded or inhibited activities; (2) percentage of rewarded or inhibited activities above or below their appropriate thresholds; (3) threshold settings; and perhaps (4) ratios of fastto-slow activity, measured in either power units, percentage units, or amplitude units. The following session details need to be recorded as well: (5) session duration; (6) number of points achieved, if a point system is used; and (7) the activities taking place during the session. These data should be maintained on a session-to-session basis. They should also either be transportable into a database or graphing programs (e.g., Excel, Statistical Package for the Social Sciences [SPSS], or Statistical Analysis Software [SAS] programs), or have the capability to display the data graphically either within and/or over sessions. Such data can then be compiled in order to examine group difference in controlled studies. Ideally, designed instruments allow the therapist or researcher to access this graphical material and print it out in either black and white or multicolor, for purposes of reports to patients, schools, insurance companies, or referral sources, or for publication.

Instruments that have all of these capabilities— including computer systems capable of running at 2-4 gigahertz or greater, with printers—range in price from less than \$1,000 to over \$10,000. The more expensive instruments often have multichannel capacity; the additional channels are primarily used for assessment purposes such as quantitative QEEG or other feedback modalities (e.g., muscle tension; skin conductance; heart rate variability). In clinical practice, feedback is typically based on either a bipolar (sequential) electrode montage with ear reference or a monopolar referential montage with a forehead ground and ear references. At present, data are insufficient for determining which is the better montage. Each has its advantages and disadvantages.

# Bipolar–Sequential and Referential Montages

The bipolar or sequential montage has the advantage that there is common-mode rejection of signals that occur simultaneously in phase at both of the electrode inputs. This includes 60-Hz activity, perhaps certain types of movement activities, and cardiac and other physiological artifacts. However, the signal that is processed represents the algebraic subtraction of the EEG activity at two different points. It does not tell us about the absolute activity at each individual electrode site. Referential recording uses a single electrode and covers a smaller area than bipolar sequential recording. However, the EEG that is being recorded represents the actual electrical activity at that point as compared with a reference (either a ground reference and/or ear references, which are supposedly electrically more neutral). Consequently, larger signals are obtained from referential recordings. However, these are much more prone to artifact because of the lack of common-mode rejection. Hence, there may be more inhibit circuit activity, and therapy may be somewhat more difficult. Although, it would be instructive to conduct a controlled study in which one group receives neurofeedback based on monopolar recording and the other receives neurofeedback based on bipolar sequential recording, such a study has not been conducted to date.

Another type of montage involves recording from multiple channels simultaneously and processing the summated activity from all of these channels together in order to provide feedback. This type of recording (involving a linear combination of channels) provides information from a much larger area of the scalp. However, at the present time, little information is available using this multichannel montage. Thus, it is not known whether this approach is more or less beneficial than the referential or bipolar sequential recordings that are currently in use. When a bipolar (sequential) electrode montage is used, it is essential that the distance between the electrodes be carefully measured every session. For example, if the distance between the electrodes for a person is 4.5 centimeters, it should remain at that distance, plus or minus 1 millimeter, for all sessions. Otherwise, data relating to magnitude (amplitude in microvolt levels) will be inaccurate, and any conclusion based on these data about learning will be erroneous. Before using any sequential montage, it is essential to test for phase cancellation of the bandpasses to be rewarded or inhibited. If this is done and there is phase cancellation of in-phase signals, then one can only train each location referentially.

# Electrode Materials

Electrodes usually are made of silver or tin or are plated with gold, and contain a hole in the middle for the extrusion of excess electrode paste. Impedances between the electrode and skin should be 5 kohms or less, and for some instruments, it is extremely important that there be no significant offset voltage between the electrodes and the skin. Offset voltages occur when electrodes act as batteries and impose a voltage in addition to that already imposed by the EEG. This can cause a baseline shift of the entire signal that is sent to the amplifier, and sometimes actually distorts the waveforms. In some cases it is impossible for the signal to be processed, and only noise appears on the screen. Many manufacturers offer an impedance meter that measures both impedance between the electrodes and voltages offset. We have found that with offsets of less than plus or minus 10 microvolts and an impedance of less than 5 kohms, high quality, reliable recordings are obtained.

# Electrode Locations

Electrode preparation is very simple. Measurements are taken as follows: For training the SMR, we place electrodes at International 10-20 locations C3 or C4. In order to locate these two points, it is necessary to locate C , the vertex. To do this, a tape measure is applied from the nasion (at the top of the bridge of the nose, where the forehead is indented) to the inion (just underneath the occipital condyle, the bump at the back of the head). Half this total distance is marked with a dry marker, the same type of marker that is often used on whiteboards. The tape is then placed from the preauricular notch of the left ear through this marked spot to the preauricular notch on the right ear. The midpoint is again located. The intersection of the two measurements locates Cz. When Cz is obtained, then 20% of that total distance from ear to ear is recalculated. That distance is then marked from the vertex toward the left ear and the right ear along the line between the two ears. These are the locations of C and C . When working with beta training, we have found that the best location is a point 10% of the total distance from nasion to inion, in front of C and 10% behind C . These two points are halfway between Cz and Fz and halfway between C and P , and are called F and Cpz. For referential training, Cz is commonly used for training purposes.

Once the appropriate locations are marked, the marking dot is removed with Omniprep or Nuprep (D. O. Weaver Company, Aurora, CO 80033), a gel that contains a small amount of pumice. This gel is then removed by cleaning the area with an alcohol wipe. Next, a small mound of electrode paste is placed over the cleansed spot. Nihon Kohden Elefix paste, 10-20, or Grass Instrument Co. conductive paste can be used. The electrode is then pushed down on the mound until the paste extrudes through the small hole in the middle of the electrode. Our preference has been to use Grass E5SH electrodes; in some cases, the smaller E6SH electrode works equally well. A small cotton ball or piece of cheesecloth is finally pushed down on top of the electrode in the mound of paste, and this completes the electrode application for the head.

For the ear, a Grass Instrument Co. ear clip electrode is used. This involves two cup electrodes placed in a small plastic holder. The ear is simply cleaned by rubbing it with Omniprep or Nuprep, removing the preparation, then placing some electrode paste in the cups of the electrode and placing the clip on the ear. Bipolar (sequential) placements for SMR training usually involve electrode positions C1–C5 in the left hemisphere, or sometimes corresponding locations in the right hemisphere. C1 lies halfway between Cz and C3, and C5 is halfway between C3 and T3.

Some neurofeedback systems require only three electrodes, two active electrodes for the scalp and one ear clip. Others require the active electrodes on the head, an ear clip, and a forehead ground. Three-electrode referential montages involve placing one active sensor on the scalp with two ear clips. These requirements are outlined by the specific manufacturers. Once the electrodes are placed, the subject experiences no significant discomfort. There may be some awareness of “rubbing” on the scalp during the cleaning with Omniprep, Nuprep, or alcohol, but this rubbing does not routinely cause pain. Because children as young as age 5 tolerate this procedure well, it is not a major problem. In adolescents and adults, there is hardly any response at all to this preparation procedure. Typically, a well-trained therapist can perform the entire electrode connection in 2 minutes or less. At the end of the session, one simply removes the electrodes by lifting them from the head. The scalp location is cleansed with either mild soap and warm water or isopropyl alcohol. Patients tolerate the removal of electrodes without difficulty. The term “sensor,” is better to use than “electrodes,” especially with children.

Currently, we prefer to obtain a high-quality QEEG for all 19 channels in the 10-20 system during eyes-closed, eyes-open, and academic tasks. We strive for 10-minute recordings for each condition. Comparisons can then be made with normative databases (e.g., Applied Neuroscience Lifespan Normative Database in the Neuroguide program; www.appliedneuroscience.com). In evaluating patients with ADHD who are thoroughly screened for other medical problems and present with no significant comorbid psychiatric disorders, a simplified QEEG scanning process that screens for evidence of cortical hypo- and hyperarousal at the vertex may provide initial neurophysiological data that can be used for assessment and treatment purposes.

# Treatment Protocols

Because our goal is to provide comprehensive treatment for persons with ADHD, it is important to conduct a pretreatment evaluation that assesses the primary areas of functional impairment. For example, many children with ADHD have difficulty with reading, writing, spelling, listening, and mathematical tasks. In order to monitor the effects of neurofeedback on such critical areas of functional impairment, it is important to conduct a pretreatment evaluation that assesses such abilities before beginning neurofeedback. Other areas of concern include comorbid psychiatric symptoms and social skills deficits. Once a therapist knows in which areas a child is experiencing the most difficulty, neurofeedback can be designed to promote both task-specific and more generalized attention. Consequently, pretesting often involves psychoeducational measures, IQ determination, a CPT, ADHD-specific and global psychiatric rating scales, and a thorough evaluation of family problems and social adjustment.

The selection of the cortical sites and frequency bands to be reinforced and inhibited is determined by a review of the results of the QEEG examination conducted prior to training. Because our neurofeedback approach is founded on studies indicating that patients with ADHD display significant abnormalities within theta (4–8 Hz) and beta (13– 21 Hz) frequency bands over central midline (Cz; Fz) and across frontal regions (F3; F4), our training typically focuses on changes patterns of cortical arousal and modulation at these locations.

The typical neurofeedback session, for example, consists of a 2-minute baseline in which there is neither auditory nor visual feedback. The computer screen can be turned off, and the data are gathered in order to determine various baseline measures. These measures include (1) microvolts of theta, (2) microvolts of beta, (3) percentage of theta below the theta threshold, (4) percentage of beta above the beta threshold, (5) ratios of theta-to-beta activity, and (6) threshold settings. If training involves SMR enhancement or suppression of thalpha or alpha, then these need to be recorded and graphed over sessions.

After a complete baseline, the child or adult engages in an initial feedback task, typically lasting 5 minutes. During this portion of the session, the participant sits with eyes open in front of the screen in an upright chair and receives an orientation to the feedback features of the various training screens. Although different training screens provide a variety of visual and auditory stimuli, a consistent source of feedback is the number of “reinforcements” or “points” earned by the patient. Typically, the patient is reinforced by a counter, in addition to other visual and auditory stimuli, that records each time period (typically .5 second) in which the patient is producing EEG activity that is below the “inhibit threshold” (defined as a specific amplitude of an EEG frequency band that is to be reduced) and above the “reward threshold” (defined as a specific amplitude of a predetermined EEG frequency band that is to be increased). The patient is encouraged to obtain as high a score as possible using the various feedback displays.

During the next 5-minute training period of the first session, the patient is directed to engage in a reading task, during which he or she receives auditory feedback and a count of the number of “reinforcements” earned. The goal of this portion of the session is to read and, at the same time, produce the desired EEG activity. If the person starts producing too much theta, the therapist can cue him or her to stop reading, try to restart the feedback, then continue reading. The goal is for the individual to be able to perform the academic tasks while producing the desired EEG response. For a reading task, auditory feedback is the appropriate modality, as visual feedback would interfere with the task.

Following completion of the 5-minute reading task, the patient engages in a training task using both auditory and visual feedback alone, without additional academic tasks. Again, the therapist plays an active role in the training process, helping the patient to “make a connection” between displayed EEG activity and his or her “level of attention.” During training segments, it is helpful for the therapist to observe periods of excessive theta activity, pause the feedback, and ask the patient to reflect on the level of attention that was occurring at that time. Similarly, when periods of sustained, desirable beta or SMR activity occur, the therapist can point out such periods and encourage the patient to reflect on his or her state of attention at that time.

During the final training period of a session, the therapist can then present another academic component, such as a task in which the patient listens to presentation of academic material (e.g., science, history, novel, short story). During this time, the patient receives visual feedback while listening to a story.

Other conditions can involve an initial baseline, several feedback components, and several academic components. These academic components might involve math problems, spelling, and handwriting, each combined with feedback. The type of feedback should be appropriate for each task—so as not to interfere with the task, but to give the person cues in the background linked to changing the EEG while he or she performs the task. It is very important for the therapist to be involved in the feedback process. With all children and adolescents, the therapist should always be in the room; adults sometimes prefer to work alone.

The process that we have used for establishing initial training thresholds was presented in detail in Monastra et al., 2002). The inhibit frequency (typically theta) is monitored, and the inhibit training “threshold” is set so that the child’s amplitude of theta is consistently observed to be below this set point approximately 60% of the time. The reward frequency (beta or SMR) is also monitored, and the reward training threshold is set so that the amplitude of the reward frequency is consistently above the set point “threshold” 40% of the time. Both inhibit and reward criteria must be met in order for the child to earn reinforcement. EMG activity is also monitored, and the training parameter is set so that the amplitude is observed to be consistently below the set point “threshold” at least 50% of the time. Using such a process for establishing initial training parameters and thresholds, the patient typically earns 15–20 rewards per minute during the early stages of training.

In our neurofeedback approach, these initial training parameters are maintained during the first five training sessions. After the fifth session, we average the patient’s scores across tasks and sessions (e.g., mean amplitude for theta, beta, SMR, EMG) and use these as the basis for adjusting training thresholds. The inhibit threshold is reset at 1 mV below the mean amplitude for theta. The reward threshold is set at the mean amplitude for beta or SMR. The EMG threshold is reset at the mean, as well. Time per task is maintained at 5 minutes for the first five sessions. During our published study, we increased time per task to 6 minutes for the sixth to 10th sessions. Thereafter, time was increased by 1 minute per task, every 10 sessions (up to 9 minutes per task) for a total of 40 training sessions.

In clinical practice, we often increase time depending on patient performance, in order to encourage a student to “do their best” each time. After the first five sessions, we increase time per task to 6 minutes. Thereafter, we increase time per task by 1 minute if the patient is able to sustain theta/beta power ratios within the mean for age peers (using a published normative database) on each of the five training conditions for three consecutive neurofeedback sessions. We typically conclude training when the patient is able to sustain theta/beta ratios consistent with age expectations for four 9-minute tasks over three consecutive sessions and can maintain such ratios during a session in which they perform four 9-minute academic tasks without feedback. Periodic “booster” sessions are provided as needed.

Because children with ADHD display variation in interest level and motivation during training, therapists have learned to use a variety of strategies to sustain motivation. Such strategies include providing tangible reinforcers (stickers, small prizes, movie passes, food coupons, money) based on scores or perceived “effort.” Other therapists provide the child with the opportunity to engage in play activities (games) if certain performance levels are met on a particular task.

However, if the child does not meet feedback goals during a specific training session, the therapist should be understanding and encouraging. Just as with any type of physical training, a person may not see improved scores every practice session. However, this does not mean that the session was a “failure.” Instead, just as the runner may not “beat his or her time” every practice session, persistence of practice is likely to lead to improved results. The child can also be reminded that treatment is taking place after school, at a time of day when children are most fatigued (and medication effects are beginning to fade). If children are able to perform well under these conditions, the transfer into school settings is even better than if they complete all of their treatment in the early morning—the time of day when they are most alert.

We do not use reclining positions for this type of treatment, because in classrooms, children sit at desks and maintain upright postures. In this way, EEG neurofeedback for ADHD is very different from relaxation therapy for anxiety disorders or stress reduction. From time to time, a therapist should question a child or adult to determine whether the patient is aware of EEG changes, what is making the displays work, and what makes the displays stop—in other words, what is producing beta and SMR, and what is producing theta, thalpha, and EMG. This awareness is important, because the information gathered can be used by teachers in the classroom setting to cue the child who is not attending.

# Pre‑ and Posttreatment Measures

The assessment of treatment effects is essential in both clinical applications and research settings. Consequently, practitioners are strongly advised to obtain pre- and posttreatment measures in areas of primary clinical concern, as well as related areas of functional impairment. These measures can include measurements from intelligence tests, psychoeducational assessments, CPTs, and singlechannel or multichannel QEEG assessments. Behavior rating scales are also excellent but less reliable than other behavioral measures because of their subjectivity, especially when completed by each parent or by teachers. It is particularly important to obtain follow-up data from patients for as long as possible and to provide periodic “booster” sessions, if indicated.

# Efficacy of EEG Biofeedback/Neurofeedback

During the past three decades, numerous controlled case and group studies examining the clinical effectiveness of EEG neurofeedback have been conducted and published (see reviews by Arns et al., 2009, 2014).
