These studies proceeded from extensive scientific evidence supporting the perspective that atypical patterns of cortical arousal over frontal and central midline regions represent physical “symptoms” of ADHD and other medically based disorders of attention and behavioral control (see reviews by Snyder & Hall, 2006; Monastra, 2008; Arns et al., 2013). In each of the published studies, neurofeedback techniques were examined in order to determine whether the frequency of core ADHD symptoms would be reduced by reinforcing the production of EEG activity (or “brain waves”) associated with attention and behavioral control, while inhibiting the production of brain waves associated with inattention, impulsivity, and hyperactivity. As reported by Arns et al. (2013) the effect size (ES) of neurofeedback on symptoms of inattention is comparable to that of methylphenidate (neurofeedback = 0.81; methlyphenidate = 0.85). The effect of neurofeedback on symptoms of hyperactivity/impulsivity is less robust, but still significant (ES = 0.4 to 0.69; methylphenidate: ES = 1.01).

Despite a consistent finding of improvement in core ADHD symptoms in every published study of the effects of neurofeedback with patients diagnosed with ADHD, this type of treatment has not been widely accepted as an efficacious treatment. The primary limitations of prior published research cited in earlier reviews of the efficacy of neurotherapy (Loo & Barkley, 2005) included small samples size in published studies, concerns about the process by which participants were diagnosed with ADHD, the absence of randomization of participants to the neurofeedback or alternative conditions, and the absence of placebo or bona fide treatment comparisons. In addition, these reviewers expressed concerns about the absence of information regarding side effects and long-term benefits, as well as the costs of neurofeedback. Finally, Loo and Barkely repeatedly alluded to the need for “blindness” of evaluators in order to reduce the likelihood of biased reporting of results. Our purpose in this section is to present an updated review of more recently published neurofeedback studies in which randomization of participants was incorporated in experimental designs, using participants diagnosed with ADHD by licensed health care providers who were qualified to make such a diagnosis based on DSM-IV criteria.

# Guidelines for Determining Efficacy

In order to be considered “efficacious” a psychological treatment must meet the following standards established by the American Psychological Association (Chambless & Hollon, 1998), the Association for Applied Psychophysiology and Biofeedback (AAPB) and the International Society for Neurofeedback and Research (ISNR) (LaVaque et al., 2002), and the American Academy of Neurology and the American Clinical Neurophysiology Society (Nuwer, 1997):

1. In a comparison with a no-treatment control group, alternative treatment group, or sham (placebo) control, using randomized assignment, the investigational treatment is shown to be statistically superior to the control condition, or the investigational treatment is equivalent to a treatment of established efficacy in a study with sufficient power to detect moderate differences.   
2. The studies have been conducted with a population treated for a specific problem, for which inclusion criteria are delineated in a reliable, operationally defined manner.   
3. The study used valid and clearly specified outcome measures related to the problem being treated.   
4. The data are subjected to appropriate data analysis.   
5. The diagnostic and treatment variables and procedures are clearly defined in a manner that permits replication of the study by independent researchers.   
6. The superiority or equivalence of the investigational treatment has been shown in at least two independent studies.

In order to be considered “efficacious and specific,” the investigational treatment must also be shown to be statistically superior to credible sham therapy, pill, or alternative bona fide treatment in at least two independent research studies using random assignment to group.

In reviewing these criteria, it should be noted that there is no requirement that the efficacy of a psychological treatment needs to be determined by studies in which those providing treatment are “blind” regarding the treatment status of an individual. Although the potential for biased assessment of treatment outcomes supports the use of experimental designs in which those individuals evaluating treatment response are “blinded” regarding group membership, the “blinding” of therapists alters the application of neurofeedback as provided in clinical practice.

Behavioral treatments, such as neurofeedback, are based on the principle that learning occurs when the desired behavior is “reinforced,” and undesired behaviors are either ignored or “punished.” Accurate, informed instruction, guidance, verbal reinforcement, and encouragement of patients by their therapists are typically provided to patients as part of the “reinforcement” provided in the clinical application of neurofeedback. Although “blinded protocols” have been employed in studies of neurofeedback, the use of experimental designs in which a credible sham, “placebo,” or bona fide treatment serves as the comparison are considered sufficient for the determination of efficacy.

# Randomized Clinical Studies

The studies described below have been published in peer-reviewed scientific articles and include participants diagnosed with ADHD using DSMIV criteria by licensed psychologists and/or physicians and randomly assigned to the investigational treatment or a waiting list, placebo, or bona fide treatment group for comparison purposes.

# Controlled Studies of Neurofeedback Using Random Assignment

1. Linden, Habib, and Radojevic (1996). Eighteen children diagnosed with ADHD were randomly assigned to EEG biofeedback (theta suppression; beta enhancement) or a no-treatment waiting-list control. Each group included six individuals diagnosed with ADHD and three individuals diagnosed with ADHD plus a learning disorder. Forty biofeedback training sessions were provided to the treatment group. Pre- and posttreatment analysis indicated that participants treated with neurofeedback demonstrated a significant increase on measures of intelligence and a reduction in symptoms of inattention on a standardized behavioral rating scale (p < .05).

2. Carmody, Radvanski, Wadhwani, Sabo, and Vergata (2001). Eight children diagnosed with ADHD were randomly assigned to EEG biofeedback (theta suppression; beta or SMR enhancement) or a waiting-list control. An additional eight “age-matched” peers without ADHD or learning disorders also received neurofeedback (to promote increased acceptance of the program within the school context). The number of sessions ranged from 36 to 48. Results indicated a significant reduction in the frequency of inattentive behaviors on standardized rating scales between treated and untreated participants with ADHD. CPT results revealed a significant reduction in the number of errors of commission in patients diagnosed with ADHD who had been treated for ADHD (p < .05).

3. Levesque, Beauregard, and Mensour (2006). Twenty children diagnosed with ADHD were randomly assigned to a waiting-list control group (n = 5) or neurofeedback (theta suppression; beta and SMR enhancement; n = 15). The number of sessions was 40. Pre- and posttreatment measures included functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) during the completion of the counting Stroop task and a go/no-go task (as well as behavioral rating scales). On the Conners Parent Rating Scale—Revised, significant reduction was noted in symptoms of inattention (p < .001) and hyperactivity (p < .05) in the children treated with neurofeedback. Significant improvement was noted on the Integrated Visual and Auditory CPT in treated children. In addition, only those children who had received EEG biofeedback demonstrated significant activation of the right anterior cingulate cortex, the left caudate nucleus, and in the lateral regions of the prefrontal cortex (bilaterally) compared to pretreatment.

4. Zhang, Zhang, and Shen (2006). Children diagnosed with ADHD were randomly assigned to either medication (methylphenidate) or neurofeedback (theta suppression/beta enhancement). Group composition was as follows: 20 children were assigned to neurofeedback; 20 were assigned to neurofeedback after they failed to respond to medication after 3 months (stimulant nonresponders); 16 were randomly assigned to medication and were “stimulant responders.” Essentially, this study compared patients who were assigned to EEG biofeedback with stimulant responders and those who received EEG biofeedback after failure to respond to methylphenidate. The results of the study revealed significant decreases in scores on all clinical factors of the Conners Parent Rating Scales following neurofeedback (minimum p < .05). The medication group demonstrated a similar degree of improvement on all scales except anxiety and psychosomatic symptoms (minimum p < .05). No significant difference was noted between the EEG biofeedback and medication groups. The persistence of treatment gains was noted at 1-, 3-, and 6-month follow-up evaluations. In addition, Zhang, Zhang, and Shen assessed performance on a number cancellation test and on the trailmaking test. During the neurofeedback treatment phase, speed of performance on the cancellation test was slower in the biofeedback group than in the group being treated with medication. However, at 6-month and 1-year follow-up assessments, the cancellation speed of the neurofeedback group was significantly faster than that in the medication group. On the trail-making test, no significant difference was noted between the EEG biofeedback group and the medication group during treatment. Six months to 1 year posttreatment, the trail-making times in the EEG biofeedback group were significantly less (i.e., better) than in the medication group.

5. Drechsler et al. (2007). Randomly assigned 30 children diagnosed with ADHD were assigned to a neurofeedback training program (n = 17) or an alternative treatment (structured group therapy program in which social skills and metacognitive strategies for improving attention and working memory were taught (n = 13). Clinical diagnosis was confirmed using a computerized operational criteria checklist and diagnostic algorithm for DSM-IV and ICD-10 (HYPESCHEME). When a discrepancy occurred between previous diagnostic classification (made prior to entry in the study) and HYPESCHEME, the diagnosis was based on judgments by two independent clinicians.

Pre- and postassessment included a German standardized DSM-IV questionnaire for ADHD (FBB-HKS), the Conners Parent Rating Scale (CPRS), the Conners Teacher Rating Scale (CTRS), and the parent and teacher versions of the Behavior Rating Inventory for Executive Functions (BRIEF). To screen for comorbid clinical conditions, the Child Behavior Checklist was completed by parents. In addition, the evaluation included an abbreviated version of the WISC-III (in German), neuropsychological testing of attention (Test for Attentional Performance), and the trail-making test.

The neurofeedback training consisted of 30 sessions of slow cortical potential (SCP) training over a 10-week period. This training encourages participants to develop the ability to control amplitude of SCPs, which are defined as changes of cortical activity lasting from several hundred milleseconds to several seconds. Participants learn either to increase or decrease positivity or negativity over their sensorimotor cortex as measured at Cz.

The comparison condition, a group therapy program for children with ADHD, was a structured group that incorporated social skills training, self-management, metacognitive training, and enhancement of self-awareness “modules” drawn from published programs (e.g., the therapy program for children with hyperactive and oppositional problem behavior [THOP]; Doepfner, Schuermann, & Froelich, 2002). Groups of five to six children participated in 14–15 “double” sessions (90 minutes) conducted once or twice per week over a 12–15 week period. Experienced child psychologists conducted these group therapy sessions.

The results of this study indicated significant improvement (p < .01) in attention and cognitive abilities following neurofeedback compared to group therapy, as reported by parents. Significant difference in the degree of reduction in symptoms of hyperactivity/impulsivity was not reported at the group level. No significant improvement in symptoms of inattention, impulsivity, or hyperactivity was reported by the parents of children who participated in a “social skills” type of group therapy. No significant group differences were noted on neuropsychological measures, because both groups tended to demonstrate improved performance over time on these tasks.

Subsequent examination revealed that approximately half of the neurofeedback group demonstrated the ability to control SCPs. Comparisons between those who had learned control of SCPs (n = 8) and “poor performers” (n = 9) revealed several significant differences. First, a significant improvement in symptoms of hyperactivity and impulsivity was noted in those children who were “good performers” (p < .05). Second, the degree of parental support and the implementation of behaviorally based parenting strategies were associated with significant reduction in symptoms of inattention in both groups.

The finding of improved attention and behavioral control in individuals who had demonstrated neurophysiological “learning” supported the perspective that this variable was a necessary component for treatment (rather than the amount of contact with a health care provider or verbal instruction in behavioral techniques). In addition, the finding that parenting style was a moderating variable is consistent with prior published neurofeedback findings in which systematic parental use of reinforcement techniques was associated with significant gains on behavioral measures of ADHD at home (Monastra et al., 2002). Finally, as noted in Heywood and Beale (2002) and Monastra et al. (2002), children who demonstrated neurophysiological “learning” during neurofeedback exhibited significant reductions in core ADHD symptoms.

6. Gevensleben et al. (2009). One hundred two children diagnosed with ADHD using DSM-IV criteria were randomly assigned to either a combined neurofeedback protocol (NF: theta/beta training and SCP training) or an award-winning German Learning Software program (attention skills training [AST]: Skillies) that exercises visual and auditory perception, vigilance, sustained attention, and reactivity. The number of children was determined by statistical analysis prior to conducting the study. The researchers selected a moderate effect size (0.5), anticipated a dropout rate of 5%, and set a power of .8 (one-sided, p < .05; ratio of NF to AST = 3:2) and calculated that approximately 100 students were needed.

Gevensleben et al. (2009) screened 102 students for their study. Of these 102 children, 59 students received neurofeedback; 35 received AST. Eight children were excluded from the study due to the need for medical treatment, disorganization of the parents, loss of motivation to continue in the study, or protocol violation. The severity of ADHD symptoms was rated as “moderate” in both groups (i.e., frequency of symptoms was greater than 1.5 SD on The German ADHD Rating Scale (FBB-HKS/ADHD; Dopfner et al., 2006).

The two treatment conditions were as follows. Children in the NF group received 16 minutes of training in the production of “positive” and “negative” SCPs and 5–10 minutes of training in which they were reinforced for reducing theta frequencies and increasing beta frequencies. They were also encouraged to “practice their focused state” at home.

Children in the AST group worked on three computer-administered tasks of attention and working memory from the Skillies program. The duration of their training was 25–30 minutes. Like the children in the NF group, they were encouraged to practice their focused state at home.

None of the children in either group was being treated pharmacologically for ADHD. Each child received 36 sessions, conducted in two blocks of treatment over a 4-week period (8 weeks of total training). Pre- and postassessment included a combination of behavioral rating scales by the parents of each child, including the FBB-HKS, and the German Rating Scale for Oppositional Defiant/Conduct Disorders (FBBSSV; Gortz-Dorten, Hautmann, Walter, & Dopfner, 2014), and the Home Situations Questionnaire, German version (HSQ; Dopfner, Schurmann, & Frolich, 2002).

The results of the study indicated positive response to both treatments on the FBB-HKS (NF: p < .001; AST: p < .03). The benefit included significant improvement on both the Inattention and Hyperactive/Impulsive subscales of the FBBHKS. Comparison between groups revealed that the degree of improvement following NF was significantly greater than that following AST (Inattention: p < .005; Hyperactivity/Impulsivity: p < .05). On measures of oppositionalism and conduct disorder, the NF condition yielded significantly better responses than AST (p < .05). Both groups significantly improved on measures of family problems and homework problems (p < .01). In addition to clinical measures, Gevensleben et al. (2009) examined parental attitudes toward each treatment and each child’s level of motivation. No significant differences in parental attitude or child motivation were noted between the treatments.

Since 2009, Gevensleben et al. (2010) and Wangler et al. (2011) published follow-up studies of their initial sample. Gevensleben et al. (2010) reported that the duration of clinical benefits persisted at a 6-month follow-up. Wangler et al. (2011) noted that reduction in behavioral symptoms was associated with improvement on EEG and ERP measures.

# Open Clinical Studies

Three other studies are commonly referenced in reviews of the efficacy of neurofeedback (Rossiter & LaVaque, 1995; Monastra et al., 2002; Fuchs, Birbaumer, Lutzenberger, Gruzelier, & Kaiser, 2003). In each of these studies, patients diagnosed with ADHD were permitted to select EEG biofeedback or medication. The biofeedback protocol consisted of theta suppression/beta or SMR enhancement. Rossiter and LaVaque (1995) and Fuchs et al. (2003) compared participants receiving EEG biofeedback with individuals receiving ongoing stimulant medication therapy. Monastra et al. (2002) treated all of the patients with stimulant medication and 51 (of 100) received EEG biofeedback. The Monastra et al. study also included follow-up neuropsychological and neurophysiological assessments, with and without use of medication (1-week medication washout). In each of these studies, EEG biofeedback yielded improvements that were comparable to stimulant medication on rating scales and other measures of attention. In addition, Monastra et al. demonstrated that the beneficial effects of neurofeedback persisted even when medication was withdrawn from those treated with neurofeedback in combination with medication. This improvement persisted in evaluations conducted 6, 12, and 24 months posttreatment (Monastra, 2005b).

# Contradictory Findings

A study conducted by Heywood and Beale (2003) has been cited as an indicator that the efficacy estimates for neurofeedback may be inflated. In this study, seven children diagnosed with ADHD participated in a program in which each student received both bona fide biofeedback training (decrease theta and high beta/increase SMR) and noncontingent placebo “training.” In the placebo training, a series of randomly determined bandwidths was reinforced or inhibited. It is important to note that this study did not employ random assignment to group, as all participants received both conditions.

The results of the study were analyzed in two ways. Because two of the participants “dropped out” of the study, statistical analysis was conducted with and without their data. When data from those who completed the study were analyzed, the results indicated significant improvement on behavioral measures and neurophysiological indicators of attention following completion of EEG biofeedback training. When data from the “noncompleters” were included, these effects were “washed out.” Although this study highlights the importance of reporting the number of patients who discontinue experimental treatments in any study, the inclusion of data from “noncompleters” is inappropriate in a study of treatment efficacy in which duration of treatment is a critical factor.

An analogy would be a study that examined the use of antibiotics to treat pneumonia. Such treatments typically require approximately 10 days to be effective. It would hardly be considered scientifically valid to conclude that the investigational antibiotic was ineffective if approximately 25% of the participants dropped out before the course of treatment was completed. The appropriate statistical analysis would compare patients who had completed treatment with those who had not received the investigational treatment. Post hoc analysis of the factors leading individuals to discontinue treatment would also be useful from a scientific perspective.

An unpublished paper by Fine, Goldman, and Sanford (presented at the annual convention of The American Psychological Association, 1994) has also been cited as an indicator that neurofeedback is no more effective than a “placebo” treatment. As noted by Loo and Barkley (2005), the 71 children in this unpublished study were randomly assigned to neurofeedback, a no-treatment waiting-list, or a computerized training program developed to improve attention, working memory, and other cognitive skills (Captain’s Log, BrainTrain, Richmond, VA). Although described by Loo and Barkley as a “placebo” control group, Captain’s Log is marketed as an intervention to improve attention and working memory, similar to the German program “Skillies” used in the Gevensleben et al. (2009) study. Consequently, the Fine et al. (1994) study represents a randomized controlled group study involving two investigational treatments and a waiting-list control.

The results of this study indicated that both of the investigational treatments yielded significant improvement on a number of behavioral, laboratory, and performance measures when compared to a “waiting-list” control group. Comparisons between the two investigational treatments, however, did not support the position that neurofeedback yielded a level of improvement that was statistically superior to the Captain’s Log program on the majority of behavioral and performance measures. Although neurofeedback yielded superior results on a limited number of dependent measures, overall, these two behaviorally based treatments yielded comparable results.

The results of the Fine et al. (1994) study supports the perspective that in addition to neurofeedback, there are several other types of computer-based attention training protocols that may eventually prove effective in the treatment of ADHD. Among these computer-based training programs, Captain’s Log (www.braintrain.com) and CogMed (www.cogmed.com) have been examined in a limited number of published scientific studies. Most recently, McNab et al. (2009) demonstrated increased density of dopamine D receptor binding in prefrontal and parietal lobes in 13 healthy 20- to 28-year-old males, following a 5-week, 35 minute per day training program using tasks from the CogMed software. Although this study did not examine such changes in patients diagnosed with

ADHD, the development of computer software programs that are intended to improve attention and working memory via daily exercise of these abilities represents an alternative approach to treating core ADHD symptoms that merits additional investigation.

# Conclusions

Based on published guidelines for determining the efficacy of an investigational treatment, neurofeedback meets American Psychiatric Association, AAPB and ISNR criteria to be considered an efficacious treatment for ADHD. To date, six controlled group studies, using random group assignments, have examined the effects of neurofeedback. Three of these studies used a “waiting-list” control group, two used a control group that was treated with an alternative computer-based attention training program, and one used a comparison with a bona-fide treatment (methylphenidate). Each of the studies evaluated the treatment of participants who had been diagnosed with ADHD by qualified health care providers (e.g., licensed or board certified psychologists, psychiatrists) using DSM-IV criteria. Dependent measures included norm-referenced behavioral rating scales, as well as performance tests of attention and working memory. Consequently, the four controlled group studies conducted since 2005 have controlled for the types of confounding variables cited by Loo and Barkley (2005) and demonstrated a significant effect of neurofeedback on core ADHD symptoms. An additional study incorporating random assignment to group and comparison between neurofeedback and either a bona fide treatment or credible sham treatment is needed in order to meet criteria for neurofeedback to be considered an “efficacious and specific” treatment for ADHD.

# Cost and Long‑Term Effectiveness

Although neither the cost nor the analysis of long-term benefits is required for a treatment to be determined to be efficacious, neurofeedback has been described as too costly (e.g., Loo & Barkley, 2005). In addition, the duration of clinical benefits has been questioned. In this section, we consider both of these issues.

# Cost

In clinical practice, cost of a neurofeedback session can range from \$60–150 per session, depending on the geographic region, qualifications of provider (i.e., physician, licensed psychologist, licensed social worker, other certified counselor), and experience level of a practitioner. This is consistent with the cost for other outpatient psychological services, as established by third-party insurers (e.g., Blue Cross/Blue Shield; Aetna; MVP). Hence, the criticism of neurofeedback as being atypically high in cost per session is not substantiated. In addition, based on the determination that neurofeedback is considered to have the “best support” among evidenced-based child and adolescent psychosocial treatments for ADHD by the American Academy of Pediatrics (2013), insurance reimbursement for this type of treatment should be comparable to that provided for psychological interventions for ADHD.

The total cost for completion of a course of neurofeedback therapy has also been cited as excessive. In published studies, using random group assignment, 30–40 neurofeedback sessions have been associated with significant reduction of core ADHD symptoms. Using an average cost of \$100 per session, the total cost per child for neurofeedback would be estimated in the range of \$3000– \$4000. However, this cost is no greater than the cost of a child or adolescent being treated with individual cognitive-behavioral therapy, family therapy, social skills training, or any other psychological treatmentn and none of these treatments has been determined to be efficacious in the treatment of the core symptoms of ADHD.

Intensive summer treatment programs for treating children and adolescents with ADHD have also been developed (Pelham, 2002) and evaluated in well-controlled randomized clinical studies (MTA Cooperative Group, 1998; Molina et al., 2009). The cost of these summer camp programs varies from \$2500 to nearly \$9000 for 6–8 weeks. Although described by organizations such as Children and Adults with Attention-Deficit/Hyperactivity Disorder (CHADD) as “a critical part of treatment for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder” (National Resource Center on ADHD, 2015), the results of well-controlled follow-up studies indicate that inclusion of such a program in the treatment of children and adolescents with ADHD does not significantly improve outcome (Molina et al., 2009). Conversely, as reported by Molina et al., students whose treatment included a carefully titrated stimulant therapy in combination with participation in an intensive social skills treatment program developed by Pelham “fared worse than the local normative comparison group on 91% of the variables tested” (p. 484). From a cost perspective, neurofeedback is comparable to a child participating in weekly to biweekly psychotherapy sessions, is substantially less than intensive summer camp programs, and is the only type of psychological intervention that has been demonstrated to be an effective treatment for core ADHD symptoms.

Pharmacological treatments for ADHD vary greatly in price, depending on the type of stimulant prescribed. The range of costs is approximately \$60/month (for certain types of methylphenidate) to nearly \$200/month (for certain preparations of mixed amphetamines). That translates to a yearly cost ranging from \$700 to \$2400 per year. Since ADHD is an enduring condition and patients can be maintained on medications for decades, the use of neurofeedback may result in a substantial reduction in treatment cost, provided that neurofeedback can be demonstrated to exert an enduring effect on core ADHD symptoms, and reduce the need for ongoing medication and other types of clinical care.

# Long ‑Term  Outcome

To date, evidence of the long-term benefits of neurofeedback includes individual and group case studies (Tansey, 1990; Lubar, 1989, 1991, 1992; Lubar, Swartwood, Swartwood, & Timmermann, 1995); 6-, 12-, and 24-month follow-up of the Monastra, Monastra, and George’s open clinical study (2002) reported by Monastra (2005b); and two follow-up studies of Gevensleben et al.’s (2009) randomized controlled trial, with an active control condition (Gevensleben et al., 2010; Wangler et al., 2011). Although the results of these studies have been encouraging and indicate that beneficial effects were sustained up to 10 years (Lubar et al., 1995), only Gevensleben et al. (2009) incorporated random assignment to group. Consequently, carefully designed studies incorporating random group assignments and blinded assessment procedures are clearly needed in order to assess the nature and duration of any long-term benefits of neurofeedback, and to clarify the specific processes underlying any sustained change.

# Integration of N eurofeedback with O ther Th erapeutic App roaches

From our perspective, the successful treatment of ADHD requires interventions targeting the underlying dyregulation of cortical arousal and modulation noted in these patients, as well as, “instruction” in essential life skills, and accommodation/intervention at schools to address the adverse effects of ADHD on learning and educational performance. As we have summarized in this chapter, both pharmacological treatments and neurofeedback have been associated with significant changes in the core behavioral characteristics of ADHD and the underlying neurological foundations of this disorder. However, when these treatments are combined, it is important to monitor neurophysiological changes closely in order to reduce the risk of adverse side effects associated with excessive stimulation of the frontal cortex that can occur with the combination of two therapies intended to increase cortical arousal. Paradoxically, children receiving a combination of these therapies can benefit from a reduction of dose or potency of stimulant (rather than an increase in dose or addition of other medications) when adverse effects (irritability, moodiness, restlessness) occur.

There is little evidence that the use of stimulant medications interferes with neurofeedback. However, many parents and children prefer to avoid them, fearing medication side effects and potential physical consequences associated with long-term stimulant use. Our personal view is not to promise medication reduction or elimination as part of a neurofeedback-based treatment program. However, if a person responds very well to neurofeedback, and the individual (and parents, if the subject is a minor) expresses a desire reduce medication dosage, such a process can be successfully initiated with the collaboration of the patient’s therapist and physician.

We have found that the best regimen for elimination of methylphenidate-based medication is to reduce it gradually, even though stimulants clear the system very quickly. For example, if a child is on 20 mg of Ritalin in the morning, 10 mg at noon, and 5 mg in the late afternoon, the medication reduction might be as follows: 20, 10, 0; then 20, 5, 0; then 10, 5, 0; then 5, 5, 0; then 5, 0, 0; and finally 0. One should carry out this stepwise reduction at the rate of about one step every 7 days. If, in the process of medication reduction, the child or adolescent appears to be losing control over hyperkinesis or impulsiveness, one should stop the medication reduction at that point (or perhaps even increase the medication).

One can reduce mixed amphetamines very much in the same manner as Ritalin. Sometimes medication reduction appears to work very well for a period of 1–2 months, then the child begins to show signs of needing medication again. If this is the case, one can reintroduce the medication if requested by a physician, the parent, or the school. In our published study (Monastra et al., 2002), approximately 40% of patients treated with neurofeedback were able to completely eliminate medications, with follow-up periods of up to 2 years or longer without reintroducing medication at any time. These are the most positive outcomes and probably represent one end of a continuum.

Because other psychiatric problems are comorbid with ADHD (e.g., aggression, anxiety, depression, and tics), physicians may consider medications other than stimulants. Tricyclic antidepressants such as desipramine (Norpramin) and imipramine (Tofranil) are prescribed by some physicians not only for depression but also occasionally for impulsiveness and treatment of nocturnal enuresis (which occurs in approximately 33% of patients with ADHD). Another antidepressant (bupropion) has been associated with improvement in symptoms of inattention, in addition to depression. SSRIs such as fluoxetine (Prozac) and paroxetine (Paxil), the antipsychotic risperidone (Risperdal), and mood stabilizers (e.g., Abilify) are sometimes used to treat aggression, mood, and anxiety problems. Because of the pharmacokinetics of these medications, medication reduction must be carried out much more slowly and under very careful medical supervision. Only alpha-agonist, antihypertensives such as clonidine (Catapres), guanfacine (Tenex), and Intuniv (a sustained release version of guanfacine) have been approved by the FDA for treatment of ADHD and associated aggression, mood, and anxiety problems. They are also helpful in treating tics and insomnia. Because these antihypertensive medications have been approved by the FDA for treatment of ADHD in children and adolescents, their use would seem preferable to off-label administration of other types of medication.

Many patients being treated with neurofeedback treatment and/or medications are likely to continue to display a wide range of educational and social problems, despite improvement in their capacity for attention and behavioral control. Consequently, a comprehensive functional assessment (see Monastra, 2008) is often helpful in evaluating the types of skills that need to be developed through behavioral instruction and social skill training. Monastra (2004) has developed a simple, effective program to instruct parents in strategies to assess and treat such skills deficits. Social skills training, based on Pelham’s (2002) model, may also be helpful for such purposes.

In addition to needing to provide such care (or referral to other practitioners for parenting or social skills programs), practitioners may also find that untreated ADHD in a parent or issues of abuse within the family are negatively impacting response to neurofeedback. Such issues need to be addressed in order to maximize treatment response. Other parents, who have adopted children with a variety of emotional problems secondary to a reactive attachment disorder, will require additional therapeutic assistance in order to address the combination of ADHD and these conditions.

Because of the high rate of comorbid psychiatric disorders evident in children and teens with ADHD, it is very important that practitioners and parents not view neurofeedback as a cure-all or as a stand-alone approach for the treatment of children and adolescents. The best neurofeedback instrument cannot undo the harm done by a poor therapist who is not empathetic to the needs of the person and does not understand the complexity of the disorder. Similarly, an outstanding health care professional can only go so far in helping a person manage this disorder, unless that therapist can intervene pharmacologically and/or neurologically to change the underlying neurophysiology of the system.

# Concl usion and F uture D irections

The development of neurofeedback in the treatment of ADHD has progressed slowly and steadily since the late 1970s. During the past decade, multiple studies examining the effects of neurofeedback in carefully diagnosed patients randomly assigned to groups and treated with well-defined, neurofeedback protocols, have indicated that neurofeedback meets scientific guidelines to be considered an “efficacious” treatment for ADHD. As a result, many more health care professionals are now being trained to provide this type of treatment throughout the world. However, despite increased acceptance and availability of this type of treatment, significant clinical and scientific questions require further investigation.

First, the long-term effects of neurofeedback are largely undetermined. Although proponents of neurofeedback can cite evidence that this treatment has resulted in significant short-term reduction in core ADHD symptoms, there is limited scientific evidence that this type of treatment has enduring effects. Given the potential individual and societal benefits of identifying treatments for ADHD that have enduring effects, well-controlled studies examining the long-term effects of neurofeedback are clearly needed.

Second, although published research findings support the efficacy of neurofeedback, they have not demonstrated that the reinforcement and inhibition of specific EEG frequencies, recorded at specific cortical locations, are specifically responsible for the clinical improvements noted following neurofeedback. It might be argued that neurofeedback is merely a computerized, reinforcement generator that provides individuals with frequent “high-tech” encouragement as they perform academic tasks. As such, this type of treatment would appear similar to dispensing electronic “tokens” to children when seated quietly performing school work. To evaluate this hypothesis, “sham” feedback studies have been proposed (see review by Arns et al., 2009, and the article by Kerson & Collaborative Neurofeedback Group, 2013).

In “sham” feedback studies, reinforcement in the form of tones, changes on display “counters,” and movement of characters on video screens occurs but is not contingent on the neurophysiological “behavior” of the patient (sham group). In contrast, the neurofeedback group receives contingent reinforcement (e.g., deDeus & Kaiser, 2011). Therapists are blinded to reduce confounding due to bias. Although such studies have the potential to clarify the degree to which contingent feedback of specific neurophysiological behaviors results in significant clinical improvement, examination of the designs of such studies reveals a significant limitation. Although considerable effort has been made to ensure that patients and therapists are “blind” regarding the nature of the feedback (i.e., real or sham), systematic examination of the degree to which “sham” neurofeedback inadvertently reinforces the desired neurophysiological parameters (e.g., reduction of theta/increase of beta or smr) has not occurred.

As a result, when “sham” neurofeedback has been used as a comparison group, it is difficult to interpret the meaning of the outcome. If, in fact, sham neurofeedback is providing a “variable schedule of reinforcement” (i.e., providing reinforcement of the neurophysiological behaviors of interest at varying intervals or ratios) rather than “continous” reinforcement of such behaviors, then improvement of the core symptoms of

ADHD would still be anticipated. Such variable reinforcement of the neurophysiological behavior of interest confounds efforts to assess the specificity of neurofeedback, since both groups would be receiving reinforcement of the same behavior (just at different frequencies). Consequently, in sham feedback studies, elimination or monitoring of the frequency of reinforcement for the “targeted” neurophysiological behaviors in the sham group is needed in order to assess the specificity of the neurofeedback protocol being examined.

Third, because research seeking to evaluate the clinical effects and underlying neurological changes associated with neurofeedback requires parents and children to participate in a course of treatment that requires months rather than days to complete (e.g., medication), participant “drop out” rates can reduce the power of the study and confound accomplishment of research goals. Consequently, studies that systematically define and evaluate neurofeedback protocols that are based on a more precise understanding of the specific neurological abnormalities in a specific patient with ADHD symptoms (e.g., those derived from low-resolution electromagnetic tomography [LORETA] imaging) may lead to a more rapid clinical response and reduce drop out rates.

Fourth, during the past two decades there has been increased interest in the examination of computerized “home” attention training programs using repeated practice of “games” intended to promote vigilance, capacity for sustained attention, working memory, and behavioral inhibition (e.g., Captain’s Log, CogMed, and the online site Lumosity). Although encouraging short-term results have been reported using such techniques (e.g., McNab et al., 2009), the long-term effects of this type of treatment are undetermined and merit further examination. In addition, studies examining the combined effects of neurofeedback and computerized training may lead to a reduction of the number of sessions required to achieve clinical benefits and may constitute a low-cost, highly accessible strategy to boost attention on an ongoing basis.

Finally, systematic examination of the effects of neurofeedback on the aging process merits consideration. The loss of attention, memory, planning, judgment, and reasoning that occurs with aging is cause for considerable personal concern and significant societal cost. If researchers examining neurofeedback can identify ways to sustain attention and higher cognitive functions, perhaps we can enhance neurocognitive functioning and promote more prolonged, independent lifestyles. From such a perspective, neurofeedback holds promise for everyone if it can be shown to enhance human potential, creativity, and promote a better quality of life.

# Gl oss ary

A-B-A design. An experimental design in which a baseline period (A) precedes an intervention or treatment (B), followed by another baseline (A). Often used in single-case research.   
Alleles. One of two or more different genes that occupy corresponding positions (loci) on paired chromosomes. They are usually indicated by a capital letter for the dominant gene and a lowercase letter for the recessive gene.   
Basal forebrain (forebrain, prosencephalon). One of three major subdivisions of the brain (the others are the midbrain and the hindbrain)—the most forward part. It includes the telencephalon (front or end brain) containing the basal ganglia (see below), limbic system, olfactory bulb and tract, and lateral ventricles. It also includes the thalamus of the diencephalon (posterior portion of the forebrain).

Basal ganglia. Collection of four masses of gray matter located deep in the cerebral hemisphere and mainly under the anterior region of the neocortex and to the side of the thalamus. Involves extrapyramidal regulation of motor activity and probably other functions, and includes sequencing complex movements into a smoothly executed response. Often referred to as the “extrapyramidal system.”

Bipolar (sequential) electrode montage. An electrode configuration in which activity between two electrodes is subtracted and compared to a reference or neutral ground electrode.

Brainstem reticular formation. The brainstem connects the cerebral hemispheres with the spinal cord. Reticular means “in the form of a network.” The reticular formation is a diffuse network of groups of cells and fibers throughout the brainstem. They connect the ascending and descending tracts. They are important (essential) in their influence on and control of alertness, waking, sleeping, and several reflexes. The reticular formation includes the “reticular activating system,” which initiates and maintains wakefulness and directs attention, and extends to the entire cerebral cortex.

Cerebellum. A large structure behind and above the pons and medulla oblongata of the brainstem, which plays an important role in coordination and regulation of voluntary movements of skeletal muscles. It does not initiate movements but interrelates with brainstem structures in execution of many movements, including posture, balance, walking, running, writing, dressing, eating, playing musical instruments, and eye movement tracking. It controls speed, acceleration, and trajectory of movements.

Common mode rejection. The ability of a bipolar sequential recording to reject signals that are identical in both electrode leads simultaneously and are also in phase—for example, in-phase eye blinks, gross body movements, electrocardiographic activity, and other artifacts that might contaminate the EEG signal. (See also Neumann, Strehl, Birbaumer, & Kotchoubey, Chapter 5, this volume.)

Comorbidity. The occurrence of other disorders at the same time as the target disorder. An example would be the co-occurrence of depression with the target disorder ADHD.

CZ, the vertex. EEG electrode placement on the top of the head.

C and C . Specific sites for two of the EEG surface electrodes near and lateral to Cz; C stands for “central.”

Dopaminergic system. The system of tissues influenced by dopamine, a catecholamine neurotransmitter that is the immediate precursor in norepinephrine synthesis.

Ear reference. Reference electrode placed on an ear. The point of reference against which the voltage from other active electrodes is gauged.

Event-related potential (ERP). A scalp surface measure of evoked cortical potential. ERPs are changes in electrical activity in response to physical stimuli associated with psychophysiological processes and/ or in preparation for motor responses. Special computer analysis of the EEG allows measurement of the latency, duration, and amplitude of selected cortical waveforms. (Again, see Neumann et al., Chapter 5, this volume.)

Inion. The most prominent point or center of the external protuberance of the occipital bone.

International 10-20 System. Standard system of sites for surface EEG electrodes. (See Neumann et al., Chapter 5.)

Locus coeruleus. Structure in the midbrain that produces most of the norepinephrine in the central nervous system.

Nasion. Skull location or landmark immediately above the nose, roughly at the depression at the nose root, and just lower than the position of the eyebrows.

Neurofeedback. The term that typically refers to EEG feedback designed to alter a condition for which there is evidence that it has a neural basis. A form of EEG biofeedback training.

Nigrostriatal system. Projection from the substantia nigra to the corpus striatum (one of the parts of the basal ganglia). Substantia nigra (“black” or deep gray substance) is involved with extrapyramidal motor control. It is located from the upper edge of the pons into the subthalamic region. The neurotransmitter is dopamine. (See basal ganglia.)

Nihon Kohden Elefix paste. EEG electrode paste made by Nihon Kohden.

Noradrenergic activity. Activated by or secreting norepinephrine. (See norepinephrine as defined in M. S. Schwartz & Sedlacek, Chapter 16, this volume.) The source in the CNS is the locus coeruleus.

Occipital condyle. The bump at the back of the head.

Offset voltage. Voltage produced when electrodes act as batteries and impose a voltage.

Positron emission tomography (PET). A technique based upon images created by the interaction of positrons and electrons. These subatomic particles annihilate each other, releasing photons that are collected and through appropriate analysis lead to a formation of brain images. PET scanning is particularly useful for visualizing glucose metabolism and oxygen utilization in different brain tissues.

Preauricular notch. A notch in front of the auricle of the ear. The “auricle” is the part of the external ear not within the head; also called the “flap” or “pinna.” The notch (incisura anterior auris) is the depression between the crus of the helix (upper end of outer, hook-like structure) and the tragus (small knob-like protuberance lower and to the side of the opening to the ear canal (external acoustic meatus). The preauricular notch is just anterior to this ear location and posterior to the hair as at the top of a sideburn.

Referential montage. A combination of EEG electrodes that obtain information about the electrical activity of specific areas of the brain, compared with a relatively neutral ground or reference.

Response inhibition. The ability to inhibit responding to inappropriate stimuli. Measured by computerbased continuous performance tests. Used in the assessment of ADHD.

Single-photon emission computerized tomography (SPECT). Similar to a PET scan (see above). A technique that allows for the evaluation of blood flow and metabolism in the living brain.

Test of Variables of Attention (TO VA). A computerbased continuous performance test for assessing very detailed information about sustained attention. Used in the assessment of ADHD.

Wender, P. H. (1971). Minimal brain dysfunction. New York: Wiley.   
Zametkin, A. J., Nordahl, T. E., Gross, M., King, A. C., Semple, W. E., Rumsey, J., et al. (1990). Cerebral glucose metabolism in adults with hyperactivity of childhood onset. New England Journal of Medicine, 323, 1361–1366.   
Zametkin, A. J., & Rapoport, J. L. (1987). Noradrenergic hypothesis of attention deficit disorder with hyperactivity: A critical review. In H. V. Metsler (Ed.), Psychopharmacology: The third generation of progress (pp. 837–842). New York: Raven Press.
