This activity provides resting tone to the pelvic floor, which supports the visceral structures and maintains closure pressure at the urethra and anal canal. As the volume in the rectum or bladder increases, the PFMs gradually become more active to maintain positive closure pressure at the urethra or anal canal. Increased PFM efferent activity is also associated with greater afferent activity, which inhibits smooth colorectal or bladder muscle through sacral and surprasacral reflex loops. The inhibition of the smooth bladder and colorectal muscle allows for the accommodation of urine or stool during the storage phase.

Conversely, when evacuation or urination is desired, there is a precipitous drop in PFM activity at the respective areas. This relaxation allows the angle at either the vesicle neck or the anorectum to straighten, resulting in dilation of the urethra or anal canal. Urethra or anal canal pressure drops, reducing resistance to passage of urine or stool. PFM relaxation is associated with reduction in somatic pudendal nerve afferent activity, which removes some of the spinal inhibition to the smooth muscle of the bladder or colorectum. With the disinhibition, an efficient contraction of the bladder or colorectum can occur in order to propel stool or urine from the body (de Groat, 2006). If the sequence of PFM relaxation followed by smooth muscle contraction is disturbed during voiding or evacuation, various bowel and bladder symptoms may develop. Symptoms associated with PFM failure to relax may include voiding hesitancy and interrupted stream, straining with evacuation or voiding, constipation, incomplete evacuation, elevated postvoid residual volumes, bladder infection, or bowel and bladder irritability. These symptoms are seen in conditions such as overactive bladder syndrome (OAB), postevacuation incontinence, encopresis, and enuresis. These conditions potentially represent abnormalities in bowel and bladder control that in many cases are thought to have as an underlying mechanism, PFM failure to relax. With the disruption in the normal interaction between the smooth muscle of the colon and bladder and the PFM, other structural deficits such as bladder trabeculation, megarectum, rectocele, and partial PFM denervation might develop.

The PFMs also participate in sexual arousal and orgasm, and may aid in the transport of sperm to the ovum (Ganong, 2005). For example, the ischiocavernosus muscles compress the crura of the clitoris to impede venous return and therefore maintain erection of the clitoris. At the end of the plateau stage of the sexual response and during orgasm, impulses from the pudendal nerve produce rhythmic contractions of the bulbocavernosus, ischiocavernosus, and EAS muscles (Masters, Johnson, & Kolodny, 1994). Many women referred to our clinic for problems such as dyspareunia, vulvodynia, and vaginismus respond well to PFM neuromuscular reeducation and biofeedback.

In summary, the PFMs mediate the following functions:

1. Support the pelvic visceral structures.   
2. Provide passive occlusive force at the urethra and anal canal at rest to maintain storage.   
3. Provide dynamic occlusive force at the urethra and anal canal to maintain storage with increases in intra-abdominal pressure (e.g., a cough or sneeze).   
4. Inhibit activity in the smooth muscle of the bowel and bladder to control urgency and maintain storage.   
5. Allow for the passage of stool and urine during evacuation.   
6. Disinihibit colorectal and bladder activity to facilitate smooth muscle propulsive activity, which assists the evacuation of urine and stool.

# Bladder Function: Micturition and Urine Storage

The lower urinary tract includes the distal sections of the ureters and their ureterovesical junctions at the base of the bladder. The body of the bladder is composed of smooth muscle referred to as the detrusor muscle, which forms the bladder wall and is highly compliant. The detrusor provides the propulsive force that expels urine through the urethra. The prostate in the male, and the smooth and striated muscle of the urethra, are also part of the lower urinary system. Finally, the PFMs connect to and support the bladder neck, the vagina (in women), and the anal canal, and have a significant influence on bladder control.

A complex set of learned and reflexive control mechanisms mediates the functions of urine storage and micturition. Children develop voluntary control of bladder function from ages 2 to 4. More precise regulation continues for several more years. Before that time, bladder storage and micturition are reflexively organized within the brainstem and peripheral levels. With maturation and social conditioning, the voiding reflexes become inhibited by cortical control that is coordinated at several levels of the nervous system, extending from the frontal lobe and sensorimotor cortex to the peripheral nervous system (Hald & Bradley, 1982; de Groat, 2006). With maturation and learning, bladder compliance to larger volumes occurs, with bladder capacity in children doubling between the ages 2 and 4 years.

Thus, normal bladder function depends on the integrative functions of the frontal lobes, the sensorimotor cortex, the thalamus, the hypothalamus, the basal ganglia, the cerebellum, specific centers in the brainstem, the spinal cord, nerve roots, and the peripheral nerves. Damage at any of these levels may lead to bladder dysfunction that is characteristic of the level of the lesion (Torrens & Morrison, 1987).

The processes of storage and micturition involve intact efferent and afferent activity from the sympathetic and parasympathetic branches of the autonomic nervous system. Parasympathetic nerves coming from sacral roots S2–S4 innervate and are primarily excitatory to the detrusor muscle. The smooth muscle of the detrusor and the proximal portion of the urethra also receive innervation from T10–L1 segments of the sympathetic nervous system. The sympathetic nerves to the bladder and urethra primarily inhibit detrusor activity. Somatic nerves from sacral levels S2, S3, and S4 innervate the striated periurethral and levator ani muscles, primarily through the pudendal nerve. Below, we briefly discuss some of the functional components that interact with the central nervous system to maintain normal bladder function (Hald & Bradley, 1982).

The bladder must have normal compliance to maintain continence while filling. “Compliance” refers to the bladder’s ability to adapt to continuing diuresis. If the bladder is compliant, intravesical pressure (bladder pressure) remains relatively low compared to intraurethral pressure during the storage phase. Normal bladder compliance ensures a positive pressure gradient at the urethra.

Low intravesical pressure during the storage phase is disrupted

•	 When the bladder becomes stiff and noncompliant due to fibrosis.   
•	 When the bladder wall becomes edematous. With cancerous infiltration of bladder tissue. With overactivity of the detrusor muscle. When detrusor contractility is chronically diminished, allowing the bladder to become overdistended.   
•	 When there is a sensory deficit and the bladder

becomes chronically overdistended because it is allowed to fill to its physiological limit. Continued diuresis or increased intra-abdominal pressure then increases bladder pressure to a level that exceeds urethral pressure. The result is overflow leakage.

# Bladder Neck Position and Urethral Sealant Quality

During storage, the bladder neck and trigone are maintained in position just posterior to the lower third of the pubic bone (Tanagho, 1991). If there is adequate sympathetic input to the smooth muscle of the proximal urethra, the bladder neck remains closed. The urethra must be sufficiently pliant to produce a sealant-like closure. The levator ani and pelvic ligament structures must provide sufficient support to the pelvic structures, bladder, and bladder neck. Together, these factors prevent urine loss when pressure is transmitted to the bladder and the bladder neck that might occur with activities that increase intra-abdominal pressure, such as coughing, sneezing, or lifting.

# Bladder Neck Suspension and Levator Support

The levator ani muscles lateral to the bladder neck are an essential component that supports the bladder neck (DeLancy, 1988, 1989, 1990). The levator muscles are primarily slow-twitch muscle fibers—striated muscle fibers that maintain static muscle tone and give support to the pelvic structures (Gosling, Dixon, Critchley, & Thompson, 1981; Critchley, Dixon, & Gosling, 1980; Koelbl, Strassegger, Riss, & Gruber, 1989). In contrast, fast-twitch muscle fibers comprise about 20–30% of the PFMs. Weakness in the levator muscle and ligamentous support can allow for displacement of the bladder neck. The displacement allows the base plate to funnel during the storage phase. When funneling occurs, a rise in intra-abdominal pressure causes a decrease rather than an increase in urethral resistance. This increases the likelihood of UI.

# Urethral Smooth Muscle

Smooth muscle, innervated by adrenergic sympathetic neurons, comprises the innermost layer of the proximal urethra. Estrogen receptors are also located in the muscle and the epithelium of the urethra. Normal urethral function depends upon the integrity of the smooth muscle in the urethra.

A clinical example indicating that this function can be compromised is found in women who note an onset of stress UI when they start taking certain hpertensive medications such as adrenergic antagonists (Wall & Addison, 1990; Dwyer & Teele, 1992).

# Striated Muscles of the Urethra and Pelvic Floor

The distal one-third of the urethra and periurethral area are composed of voluntary muscles that interconnect to the levator ani complex (DeLancy, 1989). The fibers of the urethral sphincter are predominantly slow-twitch muscle fibers that maintain constant tone but still have the ability to contract with greater force when needed (DeLancy, 1991). It is known that muscle activity, as measured by electromyography (EMG), normally increases in the distal urethra in proportion to increased bladder volumes.

# Connective, Vascular, and Epithelial Tissue

Since the urethra is a contractile tube, it must be soft and compliant to close off the lumen. The health of collagen and elastin within urethral tissue is needed for appropriate urethral “sealantlike” closure. In postmenopausal women, with the loss of estrogen, there often develops atrophy of the epithelium, which is associated with vaginitis and irritability of urethral tissue. This irritability might contribute to bladder symptoms such as urgency and incontinence.

# Types of UI

The names of different types of UI refer to the presenting symptoms associated with each type. However, symptoms and etiologies often overlap within the same individual.

“Stress UI” occurs when intra-abdominal pressure exceeds urethral pressure, as with coughing or sneezing, in the absence of a detrusor contraction. The striated PFMs normally support the bladder neck and exert a closing force on the urethra during conditions of heightened intra-abdominal pressure. PFM weakness or laxity can contribute to stress incontinence. Stress incontinence is more prevalent in women as a result of pelvic floor partial denervation injury that occurs during childbirth (Allen, Hosker, Smith, & Warrell, 1990). Stress incontinence is also seen after prostatectomy, due to the disruption of the urethral sphincter or its nerve supply.

Overactive bladder syndrome (OAB) refers to symptoms of urgency, with and without incontinence. OAB symptoms are suggestive but not diagnostic of urodynamically demonstrable involuntary bladder contraction (Stewart, 2003).

“Urge incontinence” is loss of urine associated with OAB, often characterized as a precipitous, intense, and urgent need to urinate that cannot be inhibited. Associated symptoms include urinary frequency and low-volume urination. Urge incontinence can result from neurogenic detrusor overactivity or non-neurogenic overactivity, conditions marked by uninhibited bladder contractions (Koelbl et al., 2009). In the past, urge incontinence without demonstrable uninhibited bladder contraction was called sensory urge incontinence.

Another form of incontinence that is often seen in older adults is detrusor hyperactivity with impaired bladder contractility. Patients with this condition show symptoms of stress incontinence and experience urgency and frequency of urination. However, they may strain to empty their bladders and may have elevated postvoid residual volumes characteristic of overflow incontinence (Resnick & Yalla, 1985; DuBeau, Kucher, Johnson, Palmer, & Wagg, 2009).

Some patients have symptoms of both stress and urge incontinence, a condition called mixed incontinence. Normally, PFM afferent activity inhibits the bladder. When the PFMs are weak, there is a decrease in PFM inhibition of the bladder; as a result, urge symptoms may develop. In addition to the effect of diminished PFM inhibition of the detrusor, stress incontinence possibly may contribute to sensory urgency when people develop a frequent voiding habit to avoid incontinence. Over time and the possible effect of classical conditioning, bladder volumes remain at low levels. This strategy may have the effect of lowering sensory thresholds for urge to void.

Overflow incontinence occurs when the bladder does not empty efficiently. The bladder becomes overdistended and incontinence occurs when, as a result of ongoing diuresis, bladder pressure exceeds urethral pressure. Overflow incontinence can develop in any condition that limits bladder emptying. This includes urethral obstruction caused by prostatic hyperplasia, when bladder sensation is impaired, or when bladder contractility is decreased (e.g., bladder denervation resulting from diabetic neuropathy). When there is a sensory deficit, the bladder may become chronically overdistended because it is allowed to fill to its physiological limit. Continued diuresis or increased intra-abdominal pressure then increases bladder pressure to a level that exceeds urethral pressure. The result is “overflow” leakage.

“Reflex incontinence” occurs when normal neurological control of the bladder is disrupted as a result of trauma or disease (e.g., stroke, spinal cord injury, multiple sclerosis, Parkinson’s disease, or dementia). Due to disruption in neural controls, which are primarily inhibitory, incontinence occurs when the bladder fills to its physiological limit.

# Bowel Function: Evacuation and Storage

The rectum is the most distal portion of the colon. It extends from the sigmoid segment to the anal canal. It is 12–15 centimeters long in adults and, like the rest of the colon, consists of circular and longitudinal smooth muscle layers. When gas or feces move into the rectum, the rectum expands to accommodate the larger mass. Sensory nerves within the rectal walls and nearby striated muscle provide a subjective sensation of fullness and cause the urge to defecate. Contraction of the large colon and rectum provides the propulsive force to move stool toward and through the anal canal.

The anal canal pierces the PFMs posteriorly. The levator ani gives off muscle fibers that funnel down to, and interdigitate with, the fibers of the EAS that surround the anal canal. The puborectalis muscle portion of the levator ani forms a sling that extends from the pubis back and around the junction between the rectum and the anal canal. During bowel storage, it is thought that the puborectalis, in a state of resting contraction, maintains an acute angle between the rectum and the anal canal. According to some authorities, this anorectal angle is a critical factor in the maintenance of continence (Henry & Swash, 1985). For example, if trauma or birth defect compromises the puborectalis, incontinence is almost inevitable. On the other hand, a strong puborectalis can partially compensate for a disordered internal anal sphincter (IAS) or EAS. For a more complete discussion of the PFMs, see any comprehensive anatomy text (e.g., Netter, 1989; Moore, Dalley, & Agur, 2013).

As shown in Figure 32.2, the terminal portion of the rectum narrows into the anal canal. The anal canal length is 2.5–3.0 centimeters. It has a thickening of the circular smooth muscle layer that extends down from the rectum. This thickened portion becomes the IAS and normally is in a state of near-maximal contraction (Henry &

![](images/4228796d57f5fa0cfed8ed0c1540558de30fd627635022645152f929c208f422.jpg)  
FI GURE 32.2. Anatomy of the anal canal, rectum, and distal colon, showing some of the physiological mechanisms for preserving continence. From Madoff, Williams, and Caushaj (1992, p. 1003). Copyright 1992 by the Massachusetts Medical Society. All rights reserved. Reprinted by permission.

Swash, 1985) during the storage phase. According to the classic view, the EAS is composed of three separate bundles of striated muscle fibers. Two EAS bundles (the deep and superficial) surround the IAS at the proximal portion of the anal canal. The third, the subcutaneous, is caudal to the IAS and encircles the terminal portion of the anal canal. The EAS and PFMs always maintain slight muscle tension during the storage phase.

The IAS may relax several times per hour, and this allows small amounts of rectal material to enter the anal canal. This material reaches the receptor-rich anoderm, which allows for the discrimination of air, liquid, or solid. When the IAS is tonically contracted, its resting pressure provides a passive barrier to the small amounts of gas or feces that might otherwise seep out. However, when the IAS relaxes to sample the rectal contents, the EAS must contract momentarily to prevent fecal seepage. When defecation is desired, the puborectalis, the EAS, and the IAS relax simultaneously and completely. This allows for the unresisted passage of stool through the anal canal (Ganong, 2005).

Because rectal distension is the stimulus for IAS relaxation, the EAS must contract immediately when the rectum distends to prevent accidental loss of stool. Contraction of the EAS is brief. However, it is of sufficient duration to allow the rectum to accommodate to the new volume of rectal contents and for the IAS to regain its contracted state. The urge to defecate ends with rectal accommodation until more material moves into the rectum, again producing distension.

The EAS contraction to rectal distension is so well learned that it occurs without conscious attention and is frequently labeled an unconditioned reflex. However, research indicates that the response does not occur if a person fails to sense rectal distension, even when the distension is of sufficient magnitude to produce IAS relaxation. In addition, patients can inhibit the EAS response to distension when instructed to do so, indicating that the response is in fact under voluntary control (Whitehead, Orr, Engel, & Schuster, 1981).

# Manometry

Much of our understanding of anorectal function comes from anorectal manometry. Manometry is also used for biofeedback in many gastroenterology and colorectal surgery departments. The different types of manometry instruments include a water perfused probe, a solid-state probe with strain gauge transducers, and a pneumatic balloon pressure probe. The specifics of these instruments are not covered in this chapter. Most of biofeedback principles used to alter anal sphincter/PFM function using EMG can be applied to manometric techniques as well.

Essentially, manometry measures characteristics of anorectal function to include EAS baseline pressure, EAS pressure during maximum and sustained contraction, the IAS inhibitory reflex, and the rectosphincteric response to rectal distension. During a manometry study, a balloon may be positioned in the rectum, through which air is infused to test rectal sensation and provide the stimulus that evokes the anal responses to rectal stimulation. The reader is referred to Corman (2005) and Ganong (2005).

During manometry, a normal EAS baseline measure is fairly stable. The IAS and rectal tracings may show a slow waveform associated with respiration. When a small bolus of air is infused into the rectal balloon, there is a sharp pressure rise in the rectal tracing as air enters the balloon. Withdrawal of the air results in a quick return to baseline in the rectum. When the volume of rectal distension is above the patient’s sensory threshold (15 cubic centimeters is normal), the IAS tracing will show an initial pressure increase,then drop to below baseline levels. The initial increase represents the puborectalis stretch response, and the pressure drop indicates the IAS inhibitory reflex. The IAS inhibitory response may occur even if the patient does not experience the subjective sensation of distension. The magnitude and duration of the IAS relaxation have a positive correlation with the magnitude of rectal distension. Relaxation of the IAS occurs even when one does not immediately withdraw the distending volume. If the distension is maintained, IAS relaxation may be prolonged, but after a while, the IAS will return to baseline as the rectum accommodates to the distending volume.

The EAS tracing will show a sharp increase of pressure with an acute distension of the rectal balloon. The pressure will gradually return to baseline over the next 5–10 seconds (see Figure 32.3A). Prolonged rectal distension results in an EAS response of longer duration. As is true for the IAS, the greater the rectal distension, the larger the EAS response (see Figure 32.3B).

When a voluntary EAS contraction (voluntary squeeze, VS) is produced without rectal distension, a pressure increase is seen in both the IAS and EAS tracings (see Figure 32.3C). The IAS balloon records the increase in pressure because the puborectalis and EAS surround the IAS. Without rectal distension and the associated IAS relaxation reflex, the IAS recording displays contraction pressure in the proximal anal canal.

Some individuals show a rise in pressure in the rectal balloon tracing during an EAS voluntary contraction (see Figure 32.3D). This response reflects an increase in intra-abdominal pressure from abdominal muscle contraction and breath holding. Although a slight increase in rectal pressure is normal with EAS contraction, a large increase is maladaptive, because pressure in the rectum pushes stool out and is the pattern of evacuation.

# Pathophysiology of Bowel Incontinence and Therapy Implications

Various medical and postsurgical conditions can contribute disruptions in anorectal function, which may lead to FI. General categories of anorectal dysfunction are listed below. An individual patient may certainly exhibit more than one of these deficits (Madoff, Williams, & Caushaj, 1992; Madoff, Parker, Varma, & Lowry, 2004).

![](images/8688247fcffc4c265294f5d7fa51a3cc9f0d5fb1076a45e645fb44f432ca8052.jpg)  
FI GURE 32.3. Sample polygraph records showing normal sphincter function. (A) Normal EAS response (≥ 25 mm Hg) and normal IAS response (initial pressure rise followed by relaxation) to rectal distension by 50 cc of air. (B) Normal EAS and IAS responses to prolonged rectal distension. (C) Normal EAS contraction in response to verbal instructions to “squeeze” without rectal distension. IAS pressure increases, since there is no rectal distention to stimulate the relaxation reflex, and the EAS muscle may indirectly produce pressure on the IAS balloon. (D) Same responses as in C, but holding of the breath results in a weaker EAS contraction.

3. Poor resting tone of the IAS.   
4. Diminished rectal capacity   
5. Incomplete EAS/puborectal relaxation or paradoxical contraction during evacuation.   
6. Disruption of other factors in the anus that would limit complete closure (scarring, diminished vascular cushion).

Damage to afferent nerve fibers or to sensory areas in the brain can impair rectal sensation. Chronic dilation of the rectum that occurs with severe constipation can also reduce rectal sensation. Regardless of the etiology, failure to perceive rectal distension has deleterious effects on continence. Normally, IAS relaxation occurs frequently through the day. This intermittent IAS relaxation allows some rectal contents to contact the anoderm, which is rich in sensory receptors. This “sampling” enables the individual to determine whether the contents of the rectum are gas, liquid, or stool. The person can then plan a course of action. That is, he or she can pass gas, find a toilet, or contract the EAS to inhibit the bowel movement. If rectal sensation is impaired, however, an EAS contraction does not occur concurrently with the IAS relaxation, and there is no closure of the distal anal canal as the proximal canal expands. Fecal matter sampled in the anal canal seeps outward without increased pressure at the outer anal canal.

Manometry tests rectal sensation and the EAS response to rectal distention. Normally, the EAS automatically contracts at all levels of rectal distention that are perceptible. This contraction is called the “rectosphincteric response.” The amplitude of the rectosphincteric response varies as a function of both the volume of the rectal distension and the patient’s perception of the stimulus. It is therefore an index of the integrity of the sensorimotor system.

An attenuated rectosphincteric (stretch) response to distension can contribute to bowel urgency or incontinence, even with normal sensation and squeeze pressures. Diminished stretch responses are seen in a muscle with low resting tone and suggest that the sensorimotor response loops of that muscle are impaired. Sensations for rectal distension occur not only in the walls of the rectum but also within the PFMs (Henry & Swash, 1985). Thus, hypotonic PFMs reduce the rectosphincteric response to distension by limiting the proprioceptive signals typically produced by that distension. Without an adequate proprioceptive signal, the patient has limited warning for the movement of stool into the rectum. As a result, he or she cannot respond with a timely EAS contraction to close the anal canal and push the fecal material back into the rectum. Patients with this problem often perceive and report the need to have a bowel movement as being “too late.” Improving sphincter tone with exercise may enhance the efficiency of the stretch receptor response, thus providing such a patient with a more timely warning for loss of stool.

Impaired EAS strength or muscle tone may result from partial denervation injury to the PFMs and anal sphincter. A frequent cause for partial denervation is stretch injury during childbirth, especially where there is a prolonged second-stage labor (Allen et al., 1990). Reduced EAS strength may also result from damage to the sphincter muscle itself, such as from anal surgery, radiation, or a vaginal–rectal tear during childbirth. In these cases, incontinence occurs because the pressure from descending stool and contracting colon overcomes a low EAS squeeze pressure.

Poor IAS resting tone is often caused by damage to the IAS during fourth-degree tear during vaginal delivery, and anal or rectal surgery. Examples are imperforate anus repair, ileal–anal anastomosis, or hemorrhoidectomy. Poor IAS tone also may result from chronic dilation of the sphincter with constipation. In this case, normal resting tone may be restored after the patient has been disimpacted and the rectum remains empty with regular toileting. One can palpate diminished IAS resting tone during digital examination.

If rectal sensation and EAS contraction are adequate, individuals with poor IAS resting tone will not usually experience gross incontinence. However, they may experience seepage of liquid stool and gas in amounts too small to produce a perceived rectal distension stimulus. The EAS will then fail to contract. Alternatively, they may complain of an urgent call to produce stool where there is a limited ability to inhibit a bowel movement after perceiving the sensation. This urgency may occur because the IAS inhibition and surrounding puborectalis tone contribute significantly to the sensation for impending fecal loss. These muscles provide the first place of resistance to loss of stool or gas. When the inner anal canal has low tone, the functional length of the canal shortens. This means that the compromised anal canal has a limited area in which to elevate pressure effectively to provide resistance to fecal loss.

Rectal compliance, which is the ability of the rectum to adapt to a fecal mass without a sustained elevation in rectal pressure, is determined by the elasticity of the rectal tissue and the degree to which the activity of the smooth muscle of the colon and rectum can be inhibited. Rectal capacity decreases when the rectal walls become noncompliant as a result of inflammation or from a reduction of the size of the rectum or colon. Noncompliance may accompany active ulcerative proctitis or colitis, scarring from rectal surgery, or other chronic inflammatory disease. If the rectum does not stretch and remains noncompliant to accommodate increasing amounts of stool, there is a resultant increase in rectal pressure, which may overwhelm even the strongest EAS contraction. Also, when the colon is irritable and thus hypersensitive to even small stool volumes in the rectum, strong colonic contractions may inhibit a weak sphincter through neural feedback loops. The spontaneous EAS contraction that normally occurs with rectal distension not only increases pressure in the anal canal but also inhibits colonic contraction. This reduces rectal pressure until a desired evacuation time. Thus, this inhibitory effect usually reduces the need for a sustained EAS contraction to maintain continence. However, when sensory or motor deficits diminish the intensity of the EAS response, there is disinhibition of the colon from this source of control. With the loss of inhibition, incontinence is more likely to occur, because rectal pressure overwhelms the anal canal squeeze pressure. Signs of limited rectal capacity include frequent and unformed bowel movements, urgent calls to produce stool, and explosive bowel movements.

“Paradoxical PFM contraction” refers to an abnormal pattern of sphincter contraction, rather than full relaxation below its resting state, during defecation attempts. The evacuation pattern is considered abnormal if the sphincter fails to relax below its resting pressure or if there is an actual paradoxical contraction that increases pressure above the resting state. This coordination deficit causes an outlet obstruction that results in constipation and straining with stool. Failure to relax the sphincter during defecation should always be assessed when one is treating incontinence, because many patients become incontinent as a result of procedures done to correct the very complications that result from long-term constipation and inappropriate defecation patterns—for example, repair of rectocele or prolapse, as well as hemorrhoidectomy. Furthermore, chronic straining with stool can cause or extend PFM denervation that contributes to PFM weakness and incontinence. Therefore, patients presenting with incontinence can have both EAS weakness and a disordered defecation pattern, which prevent complete evacuation of stool with toileting. One symptom of incomplete evacuation is postdefecation seepage. See further discussion below.

The degree to which biofeedback will be successful often depends on the degree to which the underlying anatomy and physiology have been disrupted. When EAS strength is impaired but rectal sensation is adequate, biofeedback therapy is usually very effective if treatment is directed toward increasing EAS strength and endurance, and reducing maladaptive responses that would increase rectal pressure. If the sensory deficit is slight to moderate, rectal sensation often improves with therapy. Less notable outcomes are obtained in those patients who, after a bowel training program to keep the rectum empty, do not come to sense large volumes of rectal distension (e.g., 50 cubic centimeters). Individuals with inadequate IAS resting tone and an absent IAS inhibitory reflex may benefit from biofeedback therapy, but the outcomes are inconsistent. These patients may develop greater control over gross incontinence, but they may continue to have some seepage. Because biofeedback procedures do not directly influence the IAS, which is an involuntary muscle, improvement in patients with poor IAS resting tone is a function of increasing the tone and strength of the striated puborectalis muscle, which surrounds the IAS and contributes to the functional length of the anal canal. Improving puborectalis muscle strength may also improve sensitivity for rectal distension by improving the efficiency of the stretch response.

After a surgical reduction of the size of the rectum or when there is scar tissue, rectal compliance may not be significantly alterable. In this case, biofeedback may still improve continence by improving EAS strength to offset elevations in rectal pressure. If the noncompliance is physiological (e.g., rectal spasm), biofeedback may improve compliance as a result of greater rectal inhibition provided by a more effective EAS contraction.

In addition to the physiological deficits discussed above, incontinence may result from the following:

•	 Decreased motivation to emit the EAS response (e.g., among senile, psychotic, or mentally retarded patients).   
•	 Chronic functional constipation that leads to overflow.   
•	 Functional diarrhea, fast colonic transit with large stool volume.

In these cases, bowel management is the primary intervention (Norton & Chelvanayagam, 2001).

# Pelvic Floor Disorders Associated with Failure to Relax

Open, relaxed sphincters and a propulsive force from the bladder or colorectal smooth muscle are required for evacuation or voiding. When the PFMs fail to relax below their resting baseline during evacuation or voiding, the interaction between the striated PFMs and the colorectal or bladder muscle is disrupted. For example, if the EAS and the puborectalis muscles fail to relax during defecation, the anal canal does not dilate, and there is resistance to the passage of stool that may lead to abdominal straining. Straining with stool can contribute to partial denervation injury of the PFMs, which may lead to incontinence (Swash, 1990). Straining is also associated with the development of fissures in the anal canal, rectal prolapse, rectocele, and hemorrhoids.

Physiologically, the failure of the PFMs to relax during defecation can also decrease the propulsive action of the smooth colorectal muscle, because if the PFMs remain contracted, the afferent activity from their contraction may inhibit activity in the colorectal muscle. A cycle develops, which leads to constipation or incomplete evacuation, because the contractility of the colorectal muscle is diminished, and straining alone is ineffective in emptying the rectum completely. As straining with stool continues, the EAS becomes weakened, and its function is disrupted. Fecal seepage may then occur when stool remains in the proximal portion of the anus or distal rectum.

Children who withhold stool serves as a clinical example of how the failure to relax the PFMs disrupts smooth muscle function. A common precipitating factor for the withholding of stool in young children is an aversion to toileting that is acquired during an earlier, painful passage of a large, hard stool. Withholding stool is associated with increased voluntary PFM activity that inhibits peristalsis and leads to the accommodation of larger amounts of stool in the colon. Chronic accommodation of stool distends the fibers of the colorectal muscle and ultimately diminishes its propulsive ability and reduces rectal sensitivity. As a large volume of stool accumulates, the colon and rectum become distended. The EAS is reflexively inhibited by this chronic distention, and this allows dilation of the anal canal, which allows fecal matter to seep around the bolus of stool that remains in the rectum. This disorder is called “encopresis.” Reports indicate that biofeedback treatment, combined with bowel management techniques, is effective in decreasing abnormal activity of both the PFMs and the EAS, and can improve encopresis and constipation in children (Brown, Donati, Seow-Choen, & Ho, 2001; Choi et al., 2001; Dailianas et al., 2000; Emmanuel & Kamm, 2001; Mimura, Roy, Storrie, & Kamm, 2000). Moreover, Emmanuel and Kamm (2001) have found that constipated patients who learn to relax the PFMs and alter the outlet obstruction also show improved colonic transit times after therapy, thereby reversing the abnormal inhibitory effect of excessive PFM activity on the smooth colorectal muscle.

The failure to relax muscles that surround the urethra and bladder neck prior to and during urination can have equally dire effects on bladder function. If the PFMs fail to relax with voiding, pressure at the urethra remains high at the onset and during voiding. In this case, bladder pressure must increase in order to propel urine through the urethra. The heightened bladder pressure results from a complex set of sacral and brainstem reflexes that increase the intensity of the detrusor contraction to overcome urethral resistance. If the reflexive increase in bladder pressure is not sufficient to overcome urethral resistance, the individual will augment bladder pressure by increasing intra-abdominal pressure or strain to empty the bladder, or urine will remain in the bladder. Chronic straining to void is nearly always an indication of the presence of a voiding dysfunction, either because the urethral is physiologically or mechanically impaired, the bladder contraction is inefficient, or both.

Failure to relax the pelvic floor and periurethral muscles during voiding is termed detrusor–sphincter dyssynergia. This condition occurs with certain forms of neural impairment, but it may also be functional in the sense that it is idiopathic and/ or develops from maladaptive learning. It may be associated with bladder irritability that develops as a result of the abnormally elevated pressure in the bladder during voiding. As the voiding dysfunction continues, the bladder wall may become thickened or trabeculated. With these changes, the threshold for the sensation of fullness and contraction is lowered, leaving the bladder irritable and unstable. Ultimately, functional bladder capacity decreases, and the patient may experience bladder urgency and frequency, with and without incontinence. Although not a standardized term, functional bladder capacity refers to the degree of diuresis that can occur before bladder symptoms appear, as opposed to “absolute bladder capacity,” which is an objective assessment of bladder’s capacity to hold a certain volume. Other symptoms associated with dyssynergic voiding include elevated postvoid residual volumes and painful bladder spasm. In severe cases, detrusor–sphincter dyssynergia can lead to chronic bladder infection, ureter reflux, and kidney disease.

In our clinics, we see children referred for enuresis, with and without daytime wetting. We observe that in addition to having a small bladder capacity, these children often display a voiding dysfunction characterized by a failure to relax the PFMs just before and during voiding, when measured on our biofeedback instruments. We feel that in many of these cases, the voiding dysfunction, with its associated changes in bladder physiology, is an underlying contributor to the enuresis. With all referrals for enuresis, therefore, we assess for the presence of a voiding disorder and treat it specifically. Our observations are supported by several reports that demonstrate the effectiveness of behavioral treatment and biofeedback in reducing voiding dysfunction and associated incontinence in children who have failed to improve with more traditional medical treatments or with behavioral treatments such as the pad and bell (Combs, Glassberg, Gerdes, & Herowitz, 1998; De Paepe et al., 1998, 2000; Pfister et al., 1999; McKenna, Herndon, Connery, & Ferrer, 1999; Yamanishi et al., 2000).

When constipation is not associated with organic disease or drug-related, it is regarded as a functional disorder. When patients do not respond to fiber and fluid supplementation or other forms of bowel management, it is often thought that an outlet dysfunction may be the cause for the constipation complaints (Chiarioni & Whitehead, 2008).

# Biofeedback as  a T reatment for Bowel and Bl adder D isorders

In the 1960s, a major question emerging from the fields of psychophysiology and learning theory was whether operant procedures could directly change autonomically mediated responses. Although this question is of theoretical importance, it seems less so in clinical applications, because skeletal and visceral responses are inextricably linked as part of the centrally integrated response patterns (Miller, 1978). The colorectal and urinary systems provide examples in which the autonomic nervous system and the somatic branch interact to maintain bowel and bladder function. This integration provides the opportunity for the use of operant procedures, such as biofeedback, to alter disordered bowel and bladder function.

Although Kegel (1948, 1951) did not introduce biofeedback theoretically as an operant procedure, in practice, his papers did report the first use of instrumented feedback to treat UI. He observed that if the PFMs were hypotonic, bladder suspension surgery for stress incontinence was less effective. To improve contraction of the pubococcygeus muscle portion of the levator ani muscle, Kegel (1948) invented the pressure perineometer. From within the vagina, his perimeometer measured the contractile force of surrounding muscle and displayed the associated pressure changes on a pressure gauge. In uncontrolled clinical observations of many women, Kegel (1951) reported significant improvements in continence. Over the years, unfortunately, clinicians have taught Kegel, or PFM, exercises without the use of his biofeedback device.

# Biofeedback for Fecal Incontinence

The use of operant procedures to improve anorectal physiology was first reported in a single-case pediatric study (Kohlenberg, 1973). However, in their seminal study, Engel, Nikoomanesh, and Schuster (1974) defined the method that was replicated by many subsequent reports. Engel et al. used a manometric, three-balloon probe (described below) that simultaneously measures and allows for the reinforcement of three specific anorectal responses that maintain continence. The procedure simulated stool entering the rectum by distending a balloon placed within the rectum with air. The instructions to subjects were to attend to the sensation of rectal distension and contract the EAS immediately in response, but without increasing intra-abdominal pressure. The protocol used by Engel et al. addressed several essential factors that contribute to continence:

1. Awareness of sensory cues that normally signal impending loss of stool.   
2. Timely EAS contraction to the perception of rectal distension.   
3. Reduction of a maladaptive rise in intraabdominal pressure that frequently accompanies EAS contraction but is counter to storage.

Thus, the balloon distension procedure provided a sensory discrimination task that reinforced a response to specific cues that the patient could generalize to daily life. After one to four treatment sessions, four of the seven patients treated by Engel et al. (1974) achieved continence. Two improved significantly, and one patient did not complete therapy. Other uncontrolled clinical studies involved similar methods and obtained comparable results (Cerulli, Nikoomanesh, & Schuster, 1979; Wald, 1981a, 1981b; Goldenberg, Hodges, Hersh, & Jinich, 1980; Riboli, Frascio, Pitto, Reboa, & Zanolla, 1988; Rao, Welcher, & Happel, 1996; Glia, Gylin, Akerlund, Lindfors, & Lindberg, 1998).

Several researchers concluded that rectal sensitivity and a short-latency EAS response to rectal distension are essential for continence, because EAS maximum strength did not change with biofeedback. Thus, improving rectal sensitivity and the EAS response time to rectal distension became primary biofeedback goals. For example, Buser and Miner (1986) reinforced immediate perception of, and EAS contraction in response to, rectal distension in subjects selected for having measurable delays in rectal sensation. They reported a 92% reduction in incontinence after biofeedback.

However, it may be that EAS strength did not increase appreciably in these studies (Latimer, Campbell, & Kasperski, 1984; Miner, Donnelly, & Read, 1990), because reinforcement for EAS contraction was indirect, with the therapist using the feedback measures verbally to guide the patient. The degree to which a physiological variable can be shaped depends on the accuracy and immediacy of the feedback. Verbal reinforcement cannot provide the kind of precise sensory information needed to shape approximations of the desired motor response needed to improve the quality of EAS contraction. This is especially true when trauma or disease has compromised afferent and efferent EAS activity. These protocols were also limited in improving EAS strength, because little attention was given to control for increased intraabdominal pressure during EAS contraction, and session length was limited to only three 20-minute sessions—a time that seems insufficient to strengthen weak muscles.

In general, uncontrolled biofeedback studies for FI have shown favorable outcomes but some larger randomized controlled trials (Norton, Chelvanayagam, Wilson-Barnett, Redfern, & Kamm, 2003; Solomon, Pager, Rex, Roberts, & Manning, 2003) did not demonstrate any additional benefit of biofeedback over bowel management and anal sphincter exercises taught without biofeedback. More recently however, Heymen et al. (2009) randomized subjects with FI to either a PFM training program without biofeedback or PFM training with biofeedback. A run-up design controlled for nonspecific effects of bowel management. All subjects were initially given education and bowel management training, and only those who did not respond were randomized to a biofeedback or PFM exercise group. Not only did subjects who received biofeedback show greater reductions in FI but they also showed greater improvement in sphincter strength and reduced maladaptive abdominal muscle activity, as measured by EMG during EAS contraction.

cant improvement, with 44% of those reporting a cure having objective reduction in detrusor instability when measured by a posttreatment CMG. However, those improvements were not maintained at 5-year follow-up.

Most studies, however, used EMG biofeedback or pressure measures to reinforce PFM contraction rather than detrusor activity (Baigis-Smith, Smith, Rose, & Newman, 1989; Burns, Marecki, Dittmar, & Bullough, 1985; Burns, Pranikoff, Nochajski, Desotelle, & Harwood, 1990; Castleden & Duffin, 1981; Fisher, 1983; Henderson & Taylor, 1987; Susset, Galea, & Read, 1990; Rose, Baigis-Smith, Smith, & Newman, 1990; Van Kampen et al., 2000). There is broad variability in the methodology used in these studies. Most protocols make little effort to isolate the effect of the biofeedback from bladder training strategies and home PFM exercises. These are behavioral manipulations that are commonly used with biofeedback. Furthermore, most studies report treatment results in terms of reduced incontinence episodes but do not report changes in PFM function. Due to these flaws, in a review, Hay-Smith et al. (2001) concluded that PFM exercises appear to be useful in the treatment of urinary incontinence. Also, Herderschee et al. (2011) reports that some form of feedback, either verbal or instrument-mediated feedback, offers an added functional benefit over pelvic floor muscle exercise alone. However, it has not been determined if one form of feedback is more beneficial than others and if improvement shown in feedback groups is a result of greater time spent with a therapist rather than the protocol itself.

# Biofeedback for Urinary Incontinence

Since Kegel’s earliest reports, three different biofeedback methods have been used to treat UI. These methods have reinforced (1) bladder inhibition, (2) PFM recruitment, or (3) stable intraabdominal and bladder pressures during PFM recruitment.

In a single case study, Jones (1956) reported using a cystometrogram (CMG) procedure to condition a subject with what today would be termed “overactive bladder syndrome” to tolerate larger bladder volumes and to reduce anxiety related to bladder urge sensations. In a later study, Cardozo, Abrams, Stanton, and Feneley (1978a; Cardozo, Stanton, Hafner, & Allan, 1978b) used cystometric biofeedback to reinforce bladder inhibition in 27 female subjects with detrusor instability and urge incontinence over four to eight sessions. The authors reported a 84% subjective cure or signifi

Biofeedback protocols that do not control for changes in intra-abdominal pressure during PFM contraction have certain limitations. Increases in intra-abdominal pressure and abdominal EMG can be transmitted to PFM recordings and cause measurement artifact. Moreover, co-contraction from extraneous muscle during a PFM contraction may facilitate a stronger PFM contraction through neural processes. However, the associated increase in intra-abdominal pressure compromises continence mechanisms when the PFMs are weak, because with an increase in intra-abdominal pressure, bladder pressure increases as well. Because there has been little effort to research the effect of abdominal co-contraction during PFM contraction, we have a limited understanding of how different biofeedback protocols actually change the underlying physiology and function.

In contrast to measuring a single physiological variable, several researchers used manometric measures of rectal and external anal sphincter pressure, and CMG measures of bladder pressure, to reinforce stability in detrusor and intra-abdominal pressures at the same time that PFM contraction was reinforced (Burgio, Whitehead, & Engel, 1985; Burgio, Robinson, & Engel, 1986; Burgio, Stutzman, & Engel, 1989b; Middaugh, Whitehead, Burgio, & Engel, 1989; Burton, Pearce, Burgio, Engel, & Whitehead, 1988). This protocol simultaneously reinforced selective PFM contraction that was isolated from abdominal contraction, and demonstrated to the patient the relationship between PFM contraction and bladder inhibition. The manometric feedback protocol has been modified by some workers (Tries, 1990) to use surface abdominal EMG as a correlate measure of intra-abdominal pressure while intravaginal and/ or external anal sphincter EMG electrodes record PFM activity.

In summary, the literature suggests that UI can be reduced via a treatment package incorporating biofeedback, patient education, daily PFM exercises, and behavioral strategies that inhibit urgency. However, the mechanism for improvement has yet to be specified. Since multiple anatomical and neurological processes mediate bladder control, single-channel measurement protocols may not provide sufficient physiological information to optimally alter PFM and bladder function (Tries, 2004). One exception may be genuine stress incontinence. However, even for stress incontinence, Burgio et al. (1986) demonstrated an added benefit of multichannel biofeedback when used with Kegel exercises.

The next sections describe some of the instrumentation and outline the essential components of a biofeedback treatment package for bowel, bladder, and PFDs. The recommendations have evolved from personal observations of what has worked well in our clinics. Further empirical work is needed to determine which components of treatment are most useful.

# Instrumentation

To treat the many symptoms associated with PFM dysfunction and the various elimination disorders, biofeedback instrumentation and protocols must be sufficiently sensitive to measure several physiological functions that contribute to bowel and bladder control. Protocols must be adequately flexible to render the physiological recordings in a meaningful form that optimizes learning for any individual patient. Accordingly, the biofeedback instrumentation should include several recording channels that measure and display changes in PFM activity and abdominal muscle activity simultaneously. In our clinics, we use computerized equipment that measures four EMG channels. Early in our work, we used balloon manometry and CMG biofeedback following the protocols of Engel et al. (1974) and Burgio et al. (1985), but now we rely on a bladder scan to measure postvoid residual volumes and changes in bladder volumes for the treatment of urge incontinence or when trying to reinforce sensation to bladder fullness. This is often done when training children with precipitant urgency. In place of the rectal balloon that measures intra-abdominal pressure, researchers have used abdominal muscle EMG, which has been shown to be correlated to changes in intraabdominal pressure (Workman, Cassisi, & Dougherty, 1992).

However, the clinician should understand the limitations of using abdominal EMG in the place of rectal pressure. First, one may not be able to distinguish a Valsalva maneuver from an actual bladder contraction, because intra-abdominal pressure transmits directly to the bladder. A measure of rectal pressure displays a Valsalva response if present. Second, abdominal EMG measures become very attenuated in obese individuals. To address this problem, one should make three adjustments: (1) use a very high-gain setting to amplify the abdominal response; (2) increase the interelectrode distance; and (3) move the EMG electrodes more laterally on the external abdominal oblique muscle, where there may be less adipose tissue, and training the patient in the left lateral position, so that the adipose tissue is diverted to the left side of the body. Also, with careful observation of a patient, one can detect whether he or she is using extraneous muscle or holding his or her breath during attempts at PFM contraction. However, departures from standard placements limit the reliability of measures over sessions. Regardless of these limitations, abdominal EMG is a convenient method to measure and reinforce specific PFM contraction in the absence of extraneous abdominal contraction that would increase intra-abdominal pressure.

Figure 32.4 illustrates various types of EMG recording probes used to measure PFM intravaginallly. In our clinics, we use the intravaginal probe on the far right of Figure 32.4. This probe has several advantages other than its smaller size. Positioned above the urogenital diaphragm, it records EMG activity without dilating the vaginal tissue.

![](images/d7cfef16db1692ce2db7b2bdeb3fe8786c2a3a16cceb8165b59dd0c705dde008.jpg)  
FI GURE 32.4. Various vaginal EMG probes. To the left of the ruler, three vaginal probes are shown, which dilate the vagina and protrude from the introitus. To the right of the ruler, the Eisman–Tries intravaginal probe is seen in two views to show the electrodes fixed on one end of the convex surface, with the electrode wires extending distally when the probe is placed within the vagina. The flattened surfaces of the probe conform to the crosssection of the vagina, so that when it is positioned just above the urogenital diaphragm, the flattened portions of the probe face anteriorly and posteriorly, therefore positioning the electrodes laterally.

Moreover, it can be used to record PFM activity during voiding (Eisman & Tries, 1991).

anal sphincter, and also at the proximal portion of the anal canal. It therefore reflects EMG activity of the deep external anal sphincter and levator ani muscles, separately from the distal anal canal EMG (Eisman & Tries, 1993). The ability to measure EMG from two sites within the anal canal is useful, because the two areas have differ

Figure 32.5 shows various EMG probes that are used to measure EMG activity from the anal canal. In our clinic, we use the multiple electrode probe (MEP). The MEP measures EMG activity at the subcutaneous or distal portion of the external ent innervation and functions. Because the MEP is able to identify EMG specific to the levator ani, clinical observations suggest that it is valuable in treatment of stress incontinence not only in women but also in men after prostatectomy. This observation is consistent with the anatomy. Recall that the levator ani muscle is proximal to the urogenital diaphragm and continuous from the pubis back to the coccyx. It functions as a support to the bladder neck, vagina, and anal canal. Therefore, in using the MEP, practitioners can differentially reinforce contraction of the deeper levator ani muscle, which normally provides tonic support to the bladder neck to maintain continence. As such, the MEP may be useful in the treatment of stress incontinence in women who cannot use, or do not feel comfortable using, a vaginal recording probe (e.g., women who are older and frail, women with vaginitis, and young girls/children).

![](images/076475357a710db9fea48e8ab5bd4a122be25674dcbb908212c50a308c642a52.jpg)  
FI GURE 32.5. Various anal canal EMG probes. To the left of the ruler are three anal canal probes that have a single pair of longitudinally placed electrodes that summate EMG activity from the entire length of the anal canal. The smaller multiple electrode probe (MEP) is shown to the right of the ruler. The MEP measures surface EMG activity from two sites within the anal canal. The two bipolar electrodes adjacent to the T-handle measure subcutaneous external anal sphincter activity at the distal portion of the anal canal, while the proximal pair record activity from the deeper external anal sphincter and puborectalis muscles. The T-handle remains external to the anal verge when the probe is positioned.

However, in many women, we use both the MEP and a small Eisman-Tries intravaginal probe shown in Figure 32.4 to obtain simultaneous measures from three different sites within the pelvic floor. Both PFM sensors are used with a measure of abdominal EMG. Thus, in our clinics, we obtain four EMG measures during an assessment of PFM function (see Figure 32.6).

It is often the case that when measuring at three sites in the pelvic floor, one measure shows especially weak or uncoordinated recruitment. Measuring at multiple sites in this way ensures that one can reinforce recruitment of the PFM at the weakest site and thereby condition optimal muscle recruitment throughout PFM.

# A Biofeedback Treatment Protocol for El imination D isorders

# Medical Assessment

Before a biofeedback evaluation and treatment begins, a medical assessment and physical examination are required to rule out neurological disorders, abdominal masses, or other possible causes of incontinence. A rectal examination is done to assess rectal sensation, sphincter tone, the bulbocavernosus reflex, the prostate gland, and the presence of fecal impaction. In women, the skin condition of the perineum is assessed, and genital atrophy, pelvic prolapse, and urethral diverticula are ruled out. In men, the perineum, the foreskin, and the glans penis are assessed for abnormalities. One also checks for lower-extremity edema, because when some persons assume a gravity eliminated position while sleeping with legs brought up to approximately the same level as the trunk, there may be fluid reabsorption with increased excretion of urine (diuresis) and excessive urination at night (nocturia). The physical examination helps determine whether a patient needs further laboratory tests. A urinalysis is a basic test when there is urinary incontinence. One also must determine whether there are postvoid residual volumes (Agency for Health Care Policy and Research, 1996). The reader is referred to Staskin et al. (2009) for a more complete summary of the medical assessment recommended for adults with urinary and fecal incontinence.

![](images/c035e1a44ecd5d09b625e7fdfd79ee8d27182f51ddd0e443049f28cb0a4bc591.jpg)  
FI GURE 32.6. Schematic of the abdominal probe, Eisman–Tries intravaginal probe, and MEP placement in a sagittal view of the female pelvis.

# Verbal Interview

In the verbal interview with the therapist, a complete history is obtained, including the onset and development of incontinence and associated symptoms. In our setting, patients are sent a questionnaire that they are asked to fill out and bring to their first session. In addition to providing basic medical history and a list of medications, patients answer questions that cover aspects of bladder, bowel, and urogenital function. They also fill out a bladder voiding diary for 48 hours and a bowel diary before the verbal interview (see Appendix 32.1 for the bladder and bowel diaries). Before the physical assessment, the therapist reviews these materials with the patient to understand his or her current function and the potential relationship of symptoms to other medical problems.

# General Questions

1. When did bowel and/or bladder symptoms begin? What were the changes in function over the years?   
2. Were there any precipitating illnesses, surgery, or social/psychological issues associated with symptom onset?   
3. What type of protective garments or devices does the patient use, and what is the frequency of pad change? Are other supportive devices used, such as a pessary, tampons, a catheter, or a penile clamp?   
4. What is the effect of symptoms on lifestyle? What are the direct and indirect costs to the patient (e.g., increased laundry and cost of protective padding)?   
5. What are the patient’s goals for treatment?

# Bowel Function

1. What is the frequency, size, and stool consistency of bowel movements?   
2. Is it necessary to strain to evacuate? Is discomfort associated with the passage of stool?   
3. Is there any feeling of incomplete evacuation? Is incomplete evacuation followed by incontinence?   
4. What are the effects of various foods and other factors on bowel function?   
5. Is there bowel urgency (i.e., the patient fears incontinence will occur if toileting is not immediate)?   
6. What is the approximate time from this urge (if any) to accidental loss of stool?   
7. What is the frequency, amount, and stool consistency of incontinent events? (The patient is asked to distinguish stains from small amounts of stool, small amounts from large amounts, and liquid stool from solid stool.)   
8. Can the patient control flatus? Can the patient pass flatus? 9. When do incontinent events occur? For example, do they occur after meals or following an incomplete evacuation, during the night, or just during the daytime hours?   
10. What is the relationship of symptoms to diet or activity?   
11. Is sensation for stool in the rectum blunted? Can the patient distinguish gas from stool?   
12. What is the percentage of bowel movements controlled?   
13. Are there factors that increase or decrease the likelihood of incontinence (e.g., type of activity, availability of restrooms, and anxiety)?   
14. Is assistance required to have bowel movements (i.e., laxatives, enemas, suppositories, or manual removal of stool from the rectum)?   
15. Is there abdominal cramping? Is it associated with evacuation and/or meals?   
16. Is pelvic or anal pain a problem? Describe fully.

# Bladder Function

1. Does UI occur with an increase in intraabdominal pressure (e.g., walking, bending, going up stairs, coughing, sneezing)?   
2. Is there any control once UI begins?   
3. Do sudden increases in intra-abdominal pressure give rise to sensations of urgency?   
4. Does the patient experience continual or intermittent leakage?   
5. Is there urine loss without awareness?   
6. What is the volume of urine loss with each activity?   
7. What can trigger bladder urgency (i.e., running water, cold weather, changing positions, coughing, or approaching the toilet)?   
8. Can bladder urgency be inhibited and if so, for how long?   
9. Does urgency occur with a full or empty bladder?   
10. Does urgency lead to UI, and what is the volume of urine lost with each urge-incontinent episode?   
11. What are nighttime voiding patterns (i.e., frequency, volumes, degree of UI)?   
12. Does the patient show heightened vigilance to toileting needs (e.g., does the patient void before going out of the home “just in case”)?   
13. Do certain beverages (e.g., caffeinated and NutraSweet products) increases symptoms? A record of 24-hour output is useful to determine adequate hydration (see Appendix 32.1). If a patient reports output to be less than 1 liter, then input can be increased. If a patient’s output is more than 2 liters, then input should be decreased.   
14. Is there a relationship between emotional arousal and symptoms?   
15. Does the patient report any voiding hesitancy, interrupted stream, a poor flow rate, postmicturition dribble, incomplete bladder emptying, or an inability to void with perceived bladder fullness?   
16. Can the patient stop the flow of urine once it has begun?   
17. What is the frequency of cystitis or bladder infection, perineal pain, and perineal skin irritation?

Accurate recording of symptoms in the diaries is essential for the initial assessment. The type of information collected in the diaries can be modified for ongoing assessment as treatment progresses, after the therapist begins to understand the problem and treatment goals are determined. For example, initially, it is desirable to have the patient record voiding volumes before treatment begins, but this type of detail may not be necessary in the weeks of treatment following the initial evaluation. The bowel diary is important when patients are using various laxatives and cathartics or when a bowel management program is being implemented. The therapist should address noncompliance to diary keeping, because if a patient is capable but unwilling to keep it, treatment may not be practical.

# Assessment with Instrumentation

Properly licensed or certified therapists whose State Practice Acts allow for the assessment of PFMs may conduct the physical assessment. This starts with an examination of the vagina if one uses a vaginal probe for the biofeedback. If one uses a probe in the anal canal, then one examines the anal canal and rectum. This should be done to ensure appropriate placement of the recording electrode and to assess whether the instrument measures provide a valid representation of PFM function. The following discussion outlines the EMG and a general manual PFM assessment that is used for bladder, bowel, and other PFM disorders. It does not cover specific internal assessment of trigger point areas within the pelvic floor; the reader is referred elsewhere for this information (Travell & Simons, 1992; Herman, 2006; Wise & Anderson, 2008).

For the vaginal examination, the patient’s position is supine with the hips and knees flexed, thighs slightly abducted, and feet flat on the examination table. For the anorectal examination, the patient is in the standard side-lying, left lateral position, with knees and hips flexed slightly. The left lateral position is the standard position for rectal examinations, because the descending colon is on the left. Also, the abdominal electrodes are superior to the table, positioned on the right abdominal muscles to decrease cardiac artifact. A pillow between the patient’s knees provides a comfortable resting position that can be maintained over a session.

During the digital examination, the practitioner should note the resting tone of the perivaginal or anal canal muscles. With perivaginal palpation, one can assess the pubococcygeus muscle of the levator ani group. In the rectum, one feels the distal external sphincter just inside the anal canal. One palpates the puborectalis muscle portion of the levator about 2.5–4.0 centimeters from the anal verge. One does this by gently moving the examination finger posteriorly toward the coccyx.

Then the practitioner asks the patient to contract the PFM, as if trying to stop the flow of urine or passage of stool. The instruction is to hold the contraction for 10 seconds. The patient is cued to contract around the examiner’s finger while breathing normally. Although some lower abdominal contraction is normal, it should not be excessive. With the therapist’s other hand on the abdomen, the patient is cued to reduce abdominal activity as much as possible while contracting the PFMs. After the first contraction, there is a 10-second rest period. Then the patient is asked to contract for another 10 seconds. The therapist can grade the palpated contraction using a modified Oxford Grading Scale, with grading as follows: 0, no contraction; 1, flicker; 2, weak; 3, moderate with lift; 4, good with lift; and 5, strong with lift. See Bo and Sherburn (2007) for a comprehensive review of PFM manual assessment of the vagina.

After the manual evaluation, the therapist positions the PFM EMG recording probe or probes. With most women seen in our clinics, we use an intravaginal probe and MEP, which are designed for use together. These probes allow for the collection of EMG from three PFM sites (the distal anal canal, the proximal anal canal, and the intravaginal site; see Figure 32.6). If these smaller probes are not used, either a vaginal or an anal probe is used to obtain a single measure of PFM function. A caution is in order: The larger probes displayed in Figures 32.4 and 32.5 are not designed for use in pairs. In our clinic, all PFM recordings are obtained with a bandpass filter that can maximize the muscle signal (e.g., 15–80 hHz).

The therapist then places bipolar surface electrodes on the right abdominal oblique muscle, about 4 centimeters apart and 4 centimeters from the midline, with the rostral electrode placed laterally to the umbilicus and the caudal electrode placed perpendicular to the rostral electrode, just above the anterior superior iliac spine. The abdominal EMG channel uses a 100-to 200-Hz bandpass filter to minimize cardiac artifact.

Next, the patient should be asked to contract the PFMs as if trying to stop the passage of gas, stool, or urine. The therapist should avoid suggesting that the patient “contract as hard as you can,” because this exhortation often results in maladaptive abdominal contraction. The EMG record should be examined for PFM response latency, the signal’s amplitude, the stability and endurance of the contraction, and the time it takes for the EMG signal to recover to baseline after the patient is told to relax. Also, the therapist should note the degree to which the abdominal muscles contract during PFM contraction. The patient should contract for 10 seconds several times, with at least a 10- to 15-second relaxation period between each contraction. After two to three 10-second PFM contractions, the therapist should note whether the EMG shows a decrease in PFM amplitude and an associated increase in abdominal or gluteal muscle activity. Such changes indicate muscle fatigue. The baseline EMG activity should be observed between contractions, especially any increase in baseline irregularities or EMG “spiking,” which could be interpreted as muscle irritability. Then the therapist should ask the patient to gently “evacuate” or “push out” the anal probe, while measuring perianal muscle relaxation and abdominal contraction.

An EMG record of a normal PFM contraction should show the following:

•	 Relatively low and stable baseline.   
Rapid rise to a maximum contraction.   
•	 Stable contraction for 10 seconds.   
•	 Short latency to return to baseline after stopping the voluntary contraction (see Figure 32.7).

Normally, the PFMs relax precipitously prior to, and during, voiding and defecation. This relaxation response can be assessed in two ways. Using the MEP, we ask a patient to perform two evacuation trials of the anal probe after the contraction trials. We ask the patient gently to push out the MEP as if “passing gas” for 10 seconds. After the first evacuation trial, a rest period of about 20 seconds is followed by a second evacuation trial. Measures are obtained during simulated evacuation trials to determine whether the PFMs as a group show normal muscle response flexibility, seen as relaxation below the resting baseline during the evacuation maneuver.

![](images/12811f6f1f67eef2a56999dca914e6612446c06a267fead5c28c14f2733edfd7.jpg)  
FI GURE 32.7. EMG records over 36 seconds from an asymptomatic nulliparous woman during a voluntary contraction during the time marked by the event markers. The four tracings represent EMG from (1) abdominal muscles (upper dark), (2) intravaginal muscles (upper light), (3) inner anal canal (lower dark), and (4) outer anal canal (lower light).

It is also possible to assess a voiding dysfunction by measuring PFM EMG while the patient is voiding. Abnormal patterns of abdominal straining, detrusor–sphincter dyssynergia, voiding hesitancy, and interrupted stream can be observed during an EMG-recorded void. The therapist can estimate flow rate by timing the void, then dividing volume voided by the time.

One compares the patient’s EMG with normal PFM function. Treatment then proceeds to shape the patient’s responses toward a normal model. “Shaping” refers to the gradual modification of the patient’s responses through positive reinforcement of successive approximations to the ideal response.

# Biofeedback Treatment Goals

After identifying the patient’s functional problems and observing PFM EMG abnormalities, the therapist should devise a treatment plan with specific short-term and long-term goals. The longterm goals are expected functional outcomes; the short-term goals describe the training components by which the patient will achieve the functional changes. The treatment goals listed below are typically used to document progress in quantifiable terms. However, the lists are limited, because goals vary for each patient, depending upon the presenting complaints.

Long-term or discharge goals may include the following:

1. Decrease the frequency of UI/FI episodes.   
2. Decrease bowel and/or bladder urgency.   
3. Decrease voiding or evacuation frequency or, alternatively, increase evacuation or voiding frequency.   
4. Decrease the number and size of protective garments needed.   
5. Decrease voiding and/or evacuation dysfunction and associated symptoms.   
6. Decrease pain associated with specific aspects of urogenital and/or anorectal function.

Short-term, physiological goals may be as follows:

1. Reinforce PFM and EAS contractions toward greater stability, duration, and amplitude, to improve strength and supporting muscle tone. 2. Reinforce selective PFM/EAS contractions

without abdominal and gluteal contractions. This decreases competing responses that would increase intra-abdominal pressure, which is associated with incontinence.   
3. Improve the PFM coordination by shaping phasic contractions with a short response latency and immediate recovery to baseline after voluntary contraction ceases.   
4. Reduce chronically elevated PFM activity associated with pelvic pain, voiding dysfunction, or evacuation disorders.   
5. Reduce strain voiding or evacuation by reinforcing PFM/EAS and abdominal muscle relaxation during voiding and evacuation.   
6. Increase functional bladder capacity, if abnormally small.   
7. Improve the PFM coordination activity in response to urgency. When the patient feels bowel or bladder urgency, a well-executed PFM contraction can inhibit rectal or detrusor activity.   
8. Reinforce inhibition of the maladaptive abdominal contraction during bowel or bladder urgency.   
9. Improve discrimination of sensory cues that antedate uninhibited bladder or rectal contraction and incontinence. This should be done so that the patient learns to activate sphincter activity in a timely manner to inhibit urge and avoid incontinence.   
10. Provide a home program to generalize skills learned in the clinic to the home situation. Update the home program based on changes seen in each training session.

In summary, the primary short-term treatment goal is to optimize PFM responses that normally mediate bladder and bowel control. The patient’s diary helps the therapist quantify the degree of dysfunction in terms of the number of incontinent episodes, pads used, voiding/evacuation frequency, and voided volumes. The diary also documents, for third-party reimbursement, functional and objective changes with treatment. Physiological measures document bladder capacity, endurance of stable PFM contractions, percentage of improvement in amplitude of PFM contraction over baseline, and percentage of reduction in abdominal contraction.

# Training Selective PFM Contractions

If the patient complains of bowel or bladder urgency, treatment is initially focused on teaching selective PFM and limiting excessive abdominal co-contraction. This is done because, with urge, the maladaptive habit of abdominal contraction may increase intra-abdominal pressure and therefore is a maladaptive response, as it encourages stool or urine loss rather than storage. Second, proprioceptive feedback for PFM contraction is a function of the amplitude of the contraction. Thus, when the PFMs are profoundly weak, proprioceptive feedback for their contraction is diminished. If there is considerable afferent activity from substituting abdominal or gluteal muscle contraction, such activity can easily mask the weaker afferent signals from the PFMs. This “faulty feedback” in turn limits actual PFM recruitment and subsequent strengthening attempts. Moreover, afferent activity from the substituting muscles becomes associated with attempts to control urgency. In this way, the ineffective muscle substitution becomes conditioned to sensations of urgency, and the probability for incontinence in such situations is not reduced.

Thus, to reestablish control over urgency, the patient must learn to inhibit the competing and maladaptive stimulus–response patterns. Furthermore, the patient must replace the faulty patterns with effective PFM contraction, which is known to suppress smooth muscle activity via pudendal nerve afferent activity. Toward this end, one systematically shapes selective PFM recruitment, using the various features of the biofeedback protocol.

The therapist instructs the patient to contract the PFMs without contracting abdominal or gluteal muscles, displaying to the patient simultaneously the PFMs and abdominal EMG. The therapist advises the patient that the initial aim of treatment is not to produce a PFM contraction of maximum amplitude. Rather, the aim is to contract the PFMs selectively, with little co-contraction of extraneous muscles and without undue effort.

Adjusting the sensitivity settings of the feedback display permits the therapist to tailor the shaping procedure to match the patient’s strength and motor control. For example, one may display abdominal EMG on a scale of 0–16 mV, whereas one expands the display of the PFM signal on a scale of 0–8 mV. This can help to reinforce submaximal contractions of the latter to help dissociate them from abdominal contractions. As skill and PFM strength improve, the therapist can display the PFM contraction using a smaller gain to reinforce contraction of greater amplitudes.

To improve PFM endurance, gradual increases in the duration of each contraction should be reinforced. As the patient improves, the contraction may be extended up to 30 seconds.
