# APPENDI X 32.1. Bladder and Bowel Diaries

(Please keep the following record for a 24-hour period—before your scheduled appointment.)

1. In the first column, mark the time (day and night) you urinate and have a BM.   
2. In the second column, mark the volume (amount) of urine (or BM); please measure the urine using a plastic measuring cup or jar. Do not bring with you.   
3. In the third column, mark the time (day and/or night) accidents occur.   
4. In the fourth column, describe the amount of the accident; for example, “small” is a few drops of urine, “large” is enough to soak through outer garments if a pad is not being worn,   
5. In the fifth column, describe what you were doing at the time of the accident, or what caused the accident.

BLADDER DIARY   
![](images/2b396291eb839a5fc174bfeea9a55a90300fe53fbf0b66fe4f62832620a7776d.jpg)

NOTE: For urinary incontinence, please do not urinate right before your scheduled appointment in the clinic; we will need you to empty your bladder as part of our evaluation.

BOWEL DIARY   
![](images/b26bb5a494f2434aeaf52b842adb189182e1d188ab28998f1a40a9361840fa28.jpg)

1. In the first column, record date of evacuation.   
2. In the second column, record the time of evacuation.   
3. In the third column, record the time of stool leakage or seepage.   
4. In the fourth column, record the stool consistency (HB = hard balls; FH = formed and hard; FS = formed and soft; SUF = soft and unformed; WD = watery diarrhea.   
5. In the fifth column, record the amount of evacuation or leakage (VS = less then ¼ cup; S = ¼ to ½ cup; M = ½ to 1 cup; and L = 1 cup or greater).   
6. In the sixth and seventh columns, check whether you needed to strain or evacuation was incomplete.   
7. In the eighth column, if assistance was needed indicate the type: L = laxative (fiber supplements are included); D = digital support; S = suppository; E = enema; M = manual removal.   
8. In the ninth column, indicate whether an urge was present when you evacuated or leaked stool.   
9. Under comments, indicate any other symptoms, such as pain or abdominal cramping.

# Performing Artists

Marcie Z inn and Mark Z inn

It is nearly impossible to discuss music performance anxiety1 (MPA) outside of the context of physiology. MPA appears to be named as such due to the apparent anxiety symptoms reported by musicians when they experience psychosocial difficulties within the arts. When discussing MPA, DSM-5 diagnostic criteria (Amercan Psychiatric Association, 2013) are not often used, creating several difficulties in diagnosis and proper treatment. One difficulty is that much research regarding MPA relies on subjects’ retrospective accounts to ascertain morbidity and comorbidity, creating major problems in response bias. However, within that literature are several peer-reviewed studies utilizing retrospective data that indicate performing artists have a multitude of psychiatric symptoms, with lifetime prevalence rates reaching 40 to 70% (Buckley & Manchester, 2006; Fry, 1986; Zaza, 1995). To move forward in this field, much more tightly controlled research is needed (Kirk, 1995). Additionally, because psychiatric disorders (e.g., anxiety, phobia, depression, and posttraumatic stress disorder) are among the most threatening health conditions for all people, almost always underlie trauma, and are reported in artists in high numbers (Figure 33.1), it appears that the translational aspect of that research, which would result in the ability to control the stress response, could have far-reaching effects for artists.

# Etiol ogy of Music Performance A nxiety

A growing body of evidence indicates onset of physical and psychiatric symptoms for students in the arts during childhood (Fry, 1987; Lee, 1997; Lockwood, 1988; Shoup, 1995), demonstrating that MPA may be the final common pathway of a complex etiology involving childhood stress and trauma (Clark & Agras, 1991; Donnelly, 1991; Fehm & Schmidt, 2006; Kajantie & Raikkonen, 2010; Lupien, McEwen, Gunnar, & Heim, 2009; Marshall, 2002; Pani, Porcella, & Gessa, 2000; Piekarska, 2000; Plaut, 1990; Raineki, Cortex, Belnoue & Sullivan, 2012) rather than a primary anxiety or mood disorder. Furthermore, it is now understood that people with posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD) often meets the criteria for other psychiatric disorders before the actual onset (Koenen et al., 2008; McEwen, 2007, 2008), which means that the future patient with PTSD appears to be having subclinical symptoms long before his or her problems are diagnosable. Here we present evidence, clinical and scientific, that what is known to be “MPA” is, for most, a series of traumatic stress reactions (Wolfe, 1990; Sapolsky, 2005), most of which date back to early childhood (Kajantie & Raikkonen, 2010; Lee, 1997; Lupien et al., 2009; Munhoz et al., 2006) and present in the adult artist as overwhelming anxiety and depressive symptoms.

![](images/f40ffafa88bcfc0f99e88c79ac1685acebe8eff97ab1d34a42257882b5581647.jpg)  
FI GURE 33.1. Psychiatric problems of performing artists in 1986, replicated in 1990. All norms were significantly lower than artists’ subtest scores at both times of testing. Note. SCL-90, Symptom Checklist 90 (Derotatis, 1983). Data from Cohen and Kupersmith (1986) and Miller and Kupersmith (1990).

Since trauma involves a wide range of emotion, cognition, and physiology (Foa, Keane, Friedman, & Cohen, 2009; Maldonado & Spiegel, 2005), there is evidence that repeated traumatic stress experiences, not simply “anxiety,” plague many in the arts, rendering the term “music performance anxiety” a misnomer. Since MPA is nearly universal in the arts (Fishbein, Middlestadt, Ottaati, Strauss, & Ellis, 1986; Plaut, 1990; Wolfe, 1990), the study of trauma in the arts should be a priority (Mirescu, Peters, & Gould, 2004). We therefore coin the term, “music performance trauma,” or MPT, to capture the traumatic stress nature of what is happening to arts students and professionals everywhere.

# Traumatic Stress  in the A rts

To expand one’s understanding of trauma in the context of learning and playing music, we examine other significant factors that are known to produce trauma and traumatic stress in other populations. One potent social problem for any population is the threat of public humiliation, degradation, and status loss (Bergner, 1995; Cox & Kenardy, 1993). In the arts, this possibility of public shame and humiliation, which exists for adults and for nearly all artists, began in childhood. Then, if the person continues in the arts, he or she rehearses the maladaptive coping skills throughout their lifetime, creating multiple schema and other overlearned beliefs. How can such a thing occur? To date, primarily due to lack of oversight or accountability, arts teachers are untrained in psychology, neuroscience, or child development (Kruger & Dunning, 1999; Lee, 1997).2 Teachers are typically trained in their art but not in how to manage young children with whom they interact daily. To make matters worse, young children form emotional bonds with their teachers, which creates vulnerability that is not apparent to the onlooker and is unknown to the child or family. The child’s socioaffective system, still in its infancy, is molded around belief systems and socialization created by the arts teacher. For example, arts teachers typically use psychological control—global attributions about selffailure—to persuade young children to practice,3 ignoring the fact that multiple problems drive children’s behavior. One salient example is the 7 days between lessons (reinforcements) and no oversight during those 7 days.4 The failures inherent in this system are repeated and interpreted by the teacher and often the parents as failures in the child. Then, the recurrent shame interactions resulting from the degradation ceremonies,5 superimposed on basic ontogenetic events, mediate negative effects on development and created lasting physiological changes in the orbitofrontal cortex, causing alterations in circulating stress-induced corticosteroids and adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) that set off and maintain the stress response, as well as alter long-term potentiation and associated synaptic activity (brain modeling).6

Therefore, the characteristics of music performance trauma can be best understood as PTSD. The psychological symptoms (e.g., amnesia, dissociation, and derealization), and the physical symptoms (excessive muscle tension leading to chronic pain, headache, tremor, sweaty hands, etc.), severely disrupt the ability to organize and carry out goal-directed behavior, which prevents the cortex from performing integrative functions necessary for musical learning and performance.

# Trauma as t he Primary Et iological Fact or in M PA  (MPT)

Psychological trauma causes functional deficits as a result of an extremely stressful event or events (Foa et al., 2009; Maldonado & Spiegel, 2005). A traumatic event can be a one-time experience (Foa et al., 2009) or a result of multiple traumatic stress experiences that are extremely overwhelming. All experiences that produce trauma have, as their common denominator, the truncation of the individual’s ability to cope (Cohen, Mannarino, & Deblinger, 2006; Freyd, 1996; Kajantie & Raikkonen, 2010; Koenen et al., 2008). The primary diagnostic signs of trauma include dissociation, avoidance, emotional numbing (a form of dissociation), and hypervigilance (Cohen et al., 2006).

# Princip le 1. Inabilit y t o I nt egrat e Thought s and E mot ions

Principle 1 is that the traumatized person is rendered unable to thoroughly integrate thoughts and emotions, which in turn produces overwhelming feelings of helplessness and hopelessness. This principle is then amplified by the person’s greatly truncated ability to participate in anything resembling the traumatic event (Cohen et al., 2006; Hopper, Frewen, van der Kolk, & Lanius, 2007) due to response generalization. Because the trauma is not visible, it may appear that the person is simply not trying; inadequate practice is a common attribution from others. Pathological dissociation is a nearly universal during trauma (Maldonado & Spiegel, 2005; Resick, Suvak, Johnides, Mitchell, & Iverson, 2012) and appears to be caused, in part, by the disrupted memory and attention states that occur during trauma. In some cases, trauma survivors are so successful in separating themselves (erecting an amnesia barrier) from their trauma that they may deny it ever happened (Cohen et al., 2006). When memories of events and their associated emotional content are split off from consciousness, a situation is created in which there is little cognitive function, making the person appear lifeless and to have limited abilities.

What is the phenomenology of a traumatized person like? Trauma is not just a reaction to a highly stressful event; it overwhelms the emotions, behaviors, and cognitions, and transforms the person’s experience into a very different world in which the person believes he or she cannot tolerate the fear (Bergner, 2009; Drajer & Langeland, 1999). Trauma typically evolves into a chronic long-term disability (Bergner, 2009). While these diagnostic signs and symptoms differentiate trauma from anxiety and stress, anxiety and stress are nonetheless part of the symptom picture.

# Case 1

Joann had built a very successful career as a pianist. After having won two major competitions, she had a full concert schedule and a recording contract from a prestigious record label. However, she routinely reexperienced the severe criticism she had heard all her life, from childhood, through adolescence, and into her adult years from all of her teachers, telling her she was lazy, unmotivated, and that she would never be a good musician or “get anywhere” in music. Her first teacher even slapped the tops of her hands with a ruler, and all of her teachers would scream at her when she missed any part of the score. To make matters worse, her teachers would scream that she was going to ruin their reputations as teachers if she performed in public with such shoddy musicianship. This situation scared Joann so much that she would withdraw for days at a time, refusing to come out of her home. After a time, she would literally drag herself to the piano, working against all of the psychological and stress-related symptoms she was having every time she practiced, based on the belief that if she practiced enough, the self-talk would go away. When it came time to perform, she had to perform numerous rituals in the days and weeks before the performance, including regurgitation just before the performance, carrying a lucky rabbit’s foot in her pocket, and counting the piano keys 10 times before playing. When she went on stage, she always felt as though she was in a dream and not really there. She never did like her performances, stating openly that she hated piano. Decades later, in an interview for a magazine, Joann told the interviewer that she was abused in her piano lessons but she deserved all of it, and that it made her a better pianist.

The vignette in Case 1 is typical, in that most arts students have no idea about how to deal with what they are experiencing, so they often turn to superstitious behavior and/or substance abuse; believing that what they are experiencing is “their fault,” they continue with the same teacher(s) and receive the same abuse for years. Those who question their teachers largely do not stay in the arts, or they are ejected from the arts. An experiential viewpoint is that the person’s entire understanding is significantly transformed, and these transformations dramatically impede the ability to learn, practice, remember and/or perform (participate in the arts). These events become “practiced into” the person’s entire playing regimen (scales, pieces, etc.) through classical conditioning.7 When full MPT is triggered—typically, right before a performance8—the individual’s entire world is significantly altered in a manner that is typical of a trauma reaction (Bergner, 2009); during trauma, perceptions are dramatically and immediately “morphed,” creating a qualitative shift in self-regulation, perception, and memory. The defenses typically used to keep the overwhelming events out of mind9 are ineffective right before a performance. If the individual uses dissociative defenses (disruptions of normally integrated functions) to separate him- or herself from the emotional trauma, statedependent alterations in memory and mood also occur (Maldonado & Spiegel, 2005). These reactions run counter to the skills needed to deliver a good performance, and the artist is trapped on stage trying to make the best out of a bad situation rather than delivering an artistic performance. Depending on the level of trauma, however, most cannot recover at all on stage and ultimately quit performing.

# Principl e 2. It Is the Stress Resp onse Th at Is Causing the Physiol ogical  Symp toms

The second principle in this article is that these intense stress symptoms (Sapolsky, Romero, & Munck, 2000; Sapolsky, 2000a, 2000b) are also expressed in the periphery via vasoconstriction (cold hands) and extreme-state muscle tension and pain (Tschudi-Madsen et al., 2011), causing psychophysiological symptoms that literally prevent individuals from playing their instruments (Yoshie, Kudo, Murakoshi, & Ohtsuki, 2009). In the 1988 International Conference of Symphony and Opera Musicians (ICSOM) survey (Fishbein et al., 1988), MPA was the primary nonmusculoskeletal problem described by respondents, and was strongly associated with psychophysiological symptoms. These symptoms typically accompany at least some amnesia (Brugues, 2011a) for parts of the performance, or the amnesia may occur in their lesson, rehearsals, or other situations that resemble the original trauma. Learning theory tells us that these symptoms readily generalize, become overlearned, and cued into every aspect of the musical situation. This is why, in the arts, anxiety is not a primary diagnosis (Clark & Agras, 1991; Radstaak, Guerts, Brosschot, Cillessen, & Kompier, 2011),10 and if it is seen as primary, the result for most will likely be treatment failure (Figueredo, 2009). The diagnosis of any type of anxiety or depression does not account for the multifactorial nature of the issues that most musician patients experience (Brodsky, 1996; Nagel, 1988, 1993, 2010), such as major depressive disorder (Hildebrandt, Nubling, & Candia, 2012; Spahn et al., 2001) or dysthymia (Raeburn, 1999, 2000) and somatoform disorders (Spahn et al., 2001), including hyperventilation (Studer et al., 2012), psychoticism, paranoia, and obsessive–compulsive disorder (OCD) (Miller & Kupersmith, 1990); and dissociative phenomena (Swart & Niekirk, 2010). The etiology of anxiety or depression alone as primary diagnoses using DSM is only a small portion of the variance of MPA (Brugues, 2011a; Hildebrandt, Nubling, & Candia, 2012; Gratto, 1998; Steptoe & Fidler, 1987; Zinn, McCain, & Zinn, 2000). The reason MPA cannot be considered a primary anxiety or depressive disorder is that anxiety treatments having demonstrated effectiveness for other populations (Ginzburg, Solomon, & Bleich, 2002; Milross, Taylor, & Bryant, 2012) as well as musicians (Clark & Agras, 1991), either do not significantly reduce anxiety in artists or they appear to be effective initially but do not last after therapy terminates (Chang, Midlarsky, & Lin, 2003; Nagel, 2010). See Figure 33.1 for compelling data indicating the wide range of problems experienced by performing artists.

Therefore, for vulnerable individuals,11 walking onto a stage, into a lesson, or simply playing for any audience12 can become an unpredictable, fearful, and humiliating experience Suddenly logic, positive self-statements, cognitive restructuring, relaxation, and probabilities mean nothing. The amnesia factor in dissociation and trauma makes therapy difficult (Resick et al., 2012), requiring a therapist who has training in trauma. Dissociation can, and does, occur in small ways that are disruptive to the individual, such as small lapses in concentration or awareness, and perceptual distortions, such as depersonalization (Perez-Fabello & Campos, 2011). Another way dissociation appears is when past severe experiences are reexperienced in the present. An example would be a person with PTSD hearing a car backfire but believing he or she hears a gunshot. In the arts, reexperiencing typically is triggered vicariously through witnessing someone else play their instrument; intensity heightens if that person has trouble during his or her performance. These vicarious phenomena may be brief but intense, taking over the individual’s perceptual set, rendering his or her worldview as extremely narrow and focused on only one or two aspects of the situation, which is not nearly enough for artistry. Nothing seems real; it is as if one is in a fog. If MPT has happened before, the individual reexperiences all of the extreme stress and relives prior events as though they are happening in the present. The person has entered a pathological state in which his or her ability to engage in deliberate action (deciding what one wants to do and doing it) and participate in society (in this case, play the instrument for another) is significantly reduced (Bergner, 2004). The real self, and the creativity that goes with it, is suddenly gone (Masterson, 1988), so that the artist no longer has access to the very thing that is the core of why he or she is in the arts. To make matters much worse, these symptoms are typically accompanied by multiple somatic symptoms that also derail the artistic performance. Taken together, this constellation of internal events results in a status change, relegating the artists to “lower status” (Bergner, 2009) because he or she cannot seem to “handle it” with regard to musical performance. Phenomonologically, it is this sequence of events that every artist fears most and only a few overcome.

# Principl e 3. Status  Loss

In the arts, as with most professions, status is highly important. Bergner (1995, 2004, 2009; Brandfonbrener, 1998) characterizes the aforementioned set of circumstances as a loss of status in the community; a status assignment is degrading when it diminishes the ability of one to be a member of the community (i.e., the local performing arts community or the arts community at large), and Sapolsky (2004c) points out how status loss greatly increases vulnerability to stress-related disease. The threat of the accompanying stigma (loss of status) (Goffman, 1963) and the defamation that typically accompanies it cover a number of factors, including patronizing attitudes from others (Dinos, Stevens, Serfaty, Weich, & King, 2004), which causes severe stress in and of itself (Sapolsky, 2005). In this case, the artist would experience a degrading status assignment, which occurs when one’s peers and immediate social network deem one a “loser,” a “nothing,” or as having some sort of mental derangement; this carries with it a hyperresponsive stress response (Sapolsky, 2005). Repeated reductions in status harm the self-concept (the person comes to believe that he or she is unacceptable, does not make sense, and lacks credibility), and left unaltered, trigger major depression, severe anxiety, and even dissociative states, due to the extreme and chronic stress (Sapolsky, 1999, 2000b, 2004a). In the arts, therefore, the threat of status loss and accompanying humiliation largely drives psychological and somatic symptoms (Sapolsky, 2005). It is this vicious cycle that creates in many artists/clients the feelings of helplessness and hopelessness that become self-fulfilling prophecies, with concomitant “out of control” beliefs that one has little or no personal agency.

# Th e Psychoph ysiol ogy of the A rtist

Before we proceed, we must draw three distinctions. First, in music performance, the music is supposed to be an exact replication of the musical score. Of course, this scenario is not humanly possible (there will be error somewhere); hence, we see part of the problem (the quest for an errorfree performance vs. reality). In science, if we see a study claiming no error, then we are very suspect, because we know that everything in life has an error term. In the arts, the illusion exists that perfection is really possible (Lhevinne, 1972). It is against this illusory standard that learning, practice, and performance are judged, which, of course, validates pathological perfectionism. Second, a very serious problem is that arts study is begun in early childhood, when children are developmentally incapable of thinking divergently, planning, carrying out plans, asking relevant questions, selfmonitoring (to the degree that one must do so to study a musical instrument), and most of all, when one cannot tell the difference between abuse and nonabuse (Kajantie & Raikkonen, 2010). A child also makes numerous inferential errors due to immaturity, such as believing that one is “not good at (piano, violin, etc.)” because one has to play the piece way more times to learn it than other kids do.13 Children also have extremely maladaptive self-criticism due to their lack of “metacognition” (ability to monitor and evaluate one’s own cognition), modeling from their teacher (Piekarska, 2000) and parents (Donnelly, 1991). The teachers do not understand child development and believe any observed problems lie within the genetic makeup of the child (Kruger & Dunning, 1999).

The third distinction is the high levels of dissociation and repression found in the arts. These similar constructs make relevant psychological information literally unavailable to artists due to the amnesia barrier that is inherent in them (the person is not able to access the needed information) (see Note 13). As a consequence, the experience is felt in the body (the stress response creates the somatic symptoms due to a series of endocrine and neural actions that have been turned on too long and are activated too often), so the artist will only seek help for the body,14 believing that if he or she follows certain rules set down by other artists he or she will obtain a cure (Resick et al., 2012). Thus, we see a population of individuals who were thrust into an adult world as children, who largely have amnesia for the psychological events that drive their symptoms, and who seeks help from medical practitioners or alternative therapists when the primary problem is located in their central nervous system (brain). Torres and Bergner (2010) remind us that the threat of public humiliation is a primary trigger for psychophysiological symptoms in any profession. Taken together, the biopsychosocial symptom picture we have presented is the etiology of MPA.

# Status Dynamics and the A rts

#

Stanley, a young pianist, wants very badly to enter the world of elite classical piano playing. He well knows that this world is carefully screened by classical piano competitions, most of which are not held every year, and all of which have very early age cutoffs. Stanley has noticed a troubling pattern and does not know what to do about it; he feels fine and performs at high levels until he goes before an audience. Literally, it is when he walks onto the stage that the problems start. Stanley literally “chokes,” perspiring profusely; he has tight muscles, nausea, cold and sweaty hands, and feels like he is not really there (or in a dream). When he sits down to play, he is overcome by an overwhelming urge to get up and leave, but he persists. He starts playing but suddenly cannot remember what notes come next. The keyboard does not look real, and he panics a little about finding the correct piano keys. He cannot think “straight” in that he cannot focus on the music or any aspect of the artistry. He is now focused solely on getting through the recital with as little “damage” as possible. He panics because he cannot remember each and every note. He frantically tries to remember the score itself but cannot, and he is flooded by feelings of being out of control and thinks of selfdisparaging remarks, including being a loser, being lazy, not practicing enough, and generally not being prepared. Stanley is conducting a degradation ceremony. He barely gets through the recital. A few months later, at the next recital, the same sequence of events happens again. Stanley cannot make eye contact when someone asks him how his music is going. The degradation ceremony replays over and over in his mind. As time goes on, he feels more and more unworthy of even entering a competition. After a few years, Stanley developed a focal dystonia, and the arts physician diagnosed overuse syndrome (Buckley & Manchester, 2006). That ended Stanley’s career, which he never felt he had in the first place.

Ossorio (as cited in Bergner, 2009) defined one’s status as the crucial determinant of the range the person is “allowed” in society. Status is in relationship to the outside world and can be defined by an occupation (e.g., high school principal, garbage collector, concert pianist), a role (e.g., someone’s mother, a good friend), in terms of one’s preferences (e.g., a cat lover, a movie buff), and all statuses contain a range of behavior potential. From the status dynamic perspective (Bergner, 1995), effective help for Stanley would have to focus on status enhancement or, more succinctly, on helping Stanley find out that he has the desired status, that he has had it all along and has not realized he had it. Stanley, and many others like him, unknowingly believes he is a victim (very low status). Having failed to recognize his real power, he has not exploited it for himself. Believing that he is just lazy, the only change he can envision is becoming a person who never stops working, which put him at risk of overpracticing, which likely has contributed greatly to his focal dystonia and chronic muscle pain.

# Case 3

Sarah, a 10-year-old, has taken piano lessons for 5 years. Sarah’s practice habits are that she feels tremendous relief after her lesson; so much so that she does not initiate practice until 2 or 3 days before her next lesson. This is when she begins to get the feeling of dread and anxiety, but every week she tells herself that she will be able to “pull it off” this time. Sarah’s parents are aware of what she is doing but believe that if Sarah is “cut out” for piano, then she will practice, and if she does not practice, she is not “cut out” for piano (they believe her practice is a direct effect of some type of genetic predisposition). Sarah’s teacher knows she is in this pattern and, as with most of her other students, she has resorted to playing the game “prove it” with Sarah. In that game, Sarah has to prove that she is not a lazy, unmotivated product of “Gen X” (or whatever the current heuristic is). Sarah is supposed to go into her lesson and prove that her teacher is wrong about her; she continually seeks to “set the teacher straight.” The problem is that it never happens. Even when Sarah does practice, it is never good enough for the teacher. The teacher fears that any positive comments will “ruin” Sarah by reinforcing Sarah’s inherent “laziness.” Sarah believes deep down that these problems are not within her control. She also believes that other kids “out there” taking lessons have no trouble practicing and learn their pieces “overnight.”

The other problem Sarah faces is that this teacher has insisted that she play in the year-end “student evaluations” sponsored by the teacher’s colleagues in the local music teachers’ association. Sarah got extremely nervous and was so upset the previous year that she has no recollection of the entire event, even to this day. Her teacher and parents tell her that she performed well, but Sarah felt that they were patronizing her. She likewise does not think about the upcoming evaluations and spring recital (which also goes badly). She tries to think about them but cannot get an image in her mind and cannot think about how she wants to do in them.

Of course, there are variations of these cases. What is important here is to understand that the teacher was conducting a degradation ceremony at nearly every lesson, with the large “ultimate” degradation ceremony coming at the end of the year, when Sarah had to participate in the student evaluations (go get critiqued). Due to her extremely young age, Sarah believed the teacher and thought she had her best interests at heart, when in fact Sarah was always nearly frozen with fear, had very little memory of performances, and thought she was the problem. In order to cope, Sarah became her own perpetrator and, as she grew older, engaged in her own private degradation ceremonies over and over, time after time. It became so common for her to believe that she was not good enough, not talented enough, not cut out for music lessons, and so forth, that these labels played back in hear head nearly continuously. Worse yet, many of these scenarios involved such intense self-coercion (i.e., “I am a lousy pianist and must become a great pianist or I am no good”) that Sarah had to have cocaine daily to help herself feel better. Bergner (1995) points out that if someone seeks to change him- or herself into a more tolerant person through self-criticism, he or she will become extremely self-critical, not more tolerant (Bergner, 1995). Whatever the person is doing—holding him- or herself to too high standards without any mitigating circumstances, ignoring or minimizing positive facts about the self, and so forth—the fact is that the person is his or her own perpetrator. Only low self-confidence, low self-esteem, and the like will result until the person changes, and attempting to change through self-criticism into the opposite will only create more pathology.

Since the amnesia Sarah reports is from dissociation and her avoidance plus dissociation are diagnostic signs of trauma (Cohen et al., 2006), and dissociation is the partitioning off of mental processes that are too painful to integrate into one’s consciousness (Maldonado & Spiegel, 2005), they are both innate human responses, present in everyone, and are engaged when personal control and safety are challenged in an overwhelmingly stressful situation. Sarah learned to dissociate just enough that she never trusted herself in any performance situation. As an adult, she gave up piano.

# Case 4

Martha is a music teacher who has had a lot of teacher training,15 in addition to her bachelor’s degree in Piano. She recently decided to seek training in a new teaching method, which ended up in licensing for her.16 So, she was using her license in her advertising to help herself acquire many new students, as well as transfer students. Her studio was filling up quickly.

Martha has had no training or supervised clinical experience to help herself with demanding parents,17 so when parents started phoning her and coming in with unreasonable demands, she thought at first that she should fulfill those demands. The problem was that as the demands increased, the student’s capabilities appeared to decrease; Martha strongly suspected that her students were not practicing as much as they reported; they professed to daily practice for a long time on those days. Some students would come in and roll their eyes when she asked them to do something new, while others refused to try. The parents were pushing her to change lesson times for them (push others out of their time to accommodate the demanding parents), telling her that their children would be motivated by “pieces off of the radio,” defending their children when they did not bring in all of their books, then demanding that Martha give them extra lessons because she was failing with their children. They also demanded that Martha give a recital of her own, and Martha was scared that she would not do well in front of these hypercritical people, so she continually made up excuses. She tried and tried, and failed, to get a recital ready, so she never did do one.

What ended up happening was that Martha became angry, resentful, and hypercritical of her students and started placing them in competitions. She stopped responding to their parents by never returning their calls or e-mails, and cutting them off if they attempted to speak with her personally. If the students complained, she quickly put them down by acting shocked and disgusted that they would ever say such a thing to her, their teacher. Her tone was that of shame an humiliation. She informed them repeatedly that it was her reputation that was at stake, and their performance was a direct reflection on her, so they had better go “out there” and show everyone what a great teacher she was. Martha herself had stopped performing years earlier, placing the emphasis on her students’ performance. She pulled the focus off herself by using angry disdain with anyone who asked anything of her, and she used her students to create a career. In this way, Martha avoided looking at her own problems, which she routinely projected onto her students. All of her unresolved problems created a need in her to try to resolve those problems. Without facing the real problems, however, people continually re-create the scenarios that were traumatic to them so as to have another chance at resolution. Her students were not lazy, but she thought they were, and she treated them ”as if” this were so. Her students were doing nothing to bring on her anger, but she stayed angry at them, justifying her anger with inflated reasons that contained only a small part of truth. The real tragedy was that her students were learning to do the same thing to children in their future.

# Th e Stress R esp onse

In psychophysiology and biofeedback, our main concern is learning to control the stress response through psychophysiological self-regulation. We understand how stress-related somatic symptoms come about. Stress is a biological event, so the stress response involves a number of neurotransmitters and hormones that trigger the release of catecholamines (epinephrine and norepinephrine), which in turn trigger the hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal (HPA) axis within the sympathetic nervous system (Charney & Manji, 2004; Habib, Gold, & Chrousos, 2001; Hermann, Scholmerich, & Straub, 2000; Schutsky, Ouyang, Castelino, Zhang, & Thomas, 2011). Adrenocorticotropic hormone (ACTH) then enters the bloodstream and triggers the release of glucocorticoids, which serve to modulate the stress response over a longer period of time. Due to the pathological nature of the stress response when it is turned on too often and/or too long (Galli, 2009; Yehuda & McEwen, 2003), the stress affects musical performance through PTSD symptomatology. A number of psychiatric and personality disorders are the direct result of an unresolved stress response (Sapolsky, 1994), mostly indirectly through modulation of a system that must work optimally for peak performance and/or learning. During stress, both catecholamines (epinephrine and norepinephrine) and glucocorticoids (the central nervous system’s version of cortisol) increase cardiac output (Schelling, 2003) and activate energy from fat and liver cells (Anagnostis, Athyros, Tziomalos, Karagiannis, & Mikhailidis, 2009), while all the time decreasing nonessential activities such as reproduction, digestion, and even growth (Sapolsky, 2000b). At the same time all of this is happening, the immune system is at first enhanced for a brief time, then it is inhibited (Kemeny, 2008; Padgett & Glaser, 2003; Reiche, Nunes, & Morimoto, 2004). This cascade is meant to release stored energy into the body system, but unfortunately, over time, glucocorticoids, other neurotransmitters and hormones, and just the chronic activation (chronic “red alert” status) damages the nervous system, especially the brain through proinflammatory effects (brain inflammation) (Bierhaus, Humpert, & Nawroth, 2006; Johnson, Leopold & Loscalzo, 2006), changes in gray matter that underlie depression (Dantzer, O’Connor, Freund, Johnson, & Kelley, 2008), cell death, and weakened neurons (Murta, Pitossi, & Ferrari, 2012). Chronic stress causes brain inflammation (Garcia-Bueno, Caso, & Leza, 2008), alters synaptic transmission in the hippocampus (the brain area necessary for learning and memory), creating hyperexcitability in the hippocampal formation that has been linked to seizure activity and other central nervous system (CNS) impairments associated with inflammation (Murta, Pitossi, & Ferrari, 2012). Stress also kills hippocampal cells in large numbers and weakens existing cells, which then die during the next time they are flooded with glucocorticoids (Sahay & Hen, 2007; Sapolsky, 2005), resulting in memory impairment. Then there is the evidence that chronic stress is a major causal factor in psychopathology (Porterfield, Gabella, Simmons & Johnson, 2012; Sapolsky, 1994), which, of course, is a large factor in the etiology of MPA (Salmon, Schrodt, & Wright, 1989). Chronic stress even negatively affects the ability of the body to react to social disruption (Hanke, Powell, Stiner, Bailey, & Sheridan, 2012). What does this mean for our concert artist? It means that all of the symptoms we have discussed come to bear on his or her ability to play. All artists have to have elite use of their bodies to play their instruments, and in some cases, the body is the instrument. That means the artist is out of control early on due to his or her entire physiology going into “red alert” mode, creating the same adverse reactions whenever the stress response is on overload. Then stress response is maintained by feeling out of control, which triggers more stress, resulting in unpredictability, so the feelings of being out of control only make it worse, creating a vicious cycle. Often, what ends up happening in music performance is that the pieces are played inappropriately, or worse yet, with many memory slips, as a result of the transduction of the threat into maladaptive behavior (the performance problems covaried with the physiological issues); these performance difficulties arise from very subtle problems that may not necessarily be wrong but are inappropriate, such as problems controlling the interpretation, tempo, and so forth, to massive deficits in one’s ability to play at all. During this process, regardless of the intensity of the MPA, the artistry is lost and the playing becomes mechanical (D. Pollack, personal communication, March 4, 2004), with the intent of the performer lost with regard to interpretation or some of the performance simply forgotten. Perhaps the worst outcome of this cascade of events is the psychological problems experienced on stage while performing, partly triggered by the artist’s incomplete control over the piece and the panic inherent in the situation. Memory (recall) problems are a result of hippocampal and related network malfunctions (Gomez-Ramirez & Sanz, 2010; Sweatt, 2010) that, of course, trigger even more stress due to the loss of control of the performance. Derealization or depersonalization occurs often (DSM-5; American Psychiatric Association,

2013), and many musicians report feeling as if they are not really there, as if they are just observing their own actions. So, taken together, the psychophysiological symptoms (cold hands, sluggish muscle movements, impaired fine motor control, etc.), trauma reactions (partial dissociation, memory issues, panic, recall problems, etc.), and severe status loss cause the performer to miss spots that he or she never misses in rehearsal or practice, and the more out of control he or she feels, the greater the intensity of the reactions. The mind can be toxic.

# Getting Help —the Musician’s  Paradox

Chronic stress, over time, damages the body (Alexander, Devries, Kigerl, & Popovich, 2009; Anagnostis et al., 2009; Bierhaus et al., 2006; Chrousos, 2000; Conti et al., 2008; Kemeny, 2008; Liston, McEwen, & Casey, 2009; Margarinos, Verdugo, & McEwen, 1997), including the CNS (Bierhaus, Humpert & Nawroth, 2006; Chrousos, 2000; Hayley, Poulter, Merali, & Anisman, 2005). However, the idea that chronic stress-related problems (Moldin, 1993; Figueredo, 2009), produced by chronic stress hyper-responsivity across multiple organ systems (Sapolsky, 2004a; Hayley et al., 2005), produce enough damage to qualify as the newest killer in the 21st century (Padgett & Glaser, 2003; Sapolsky, 2004a; Simon et al., 2006) is still not a well-known or talked-about concept. Even though patients, including musicians, with stress-related medical problems (mostly called somatization disorder; see Note 10) comprise an estimated 60% or more of patients in primary care (Rief, Hessel, & Braehler, 2001), these patients are still labeled as somatizers, which means that the symptoms are “just stress,” or imagined.”18 Very few see the somatization for what it is—the symptoms of the stress response. Instead, people are labeled, and once given, that label means that they are either imagining their symptoms, overreporting symptoms, or faking it. The social stigma, degradation, and humiliation that go with it impel many to stop seeking help.19 Since stress-related problems do cause physical symptoms (Sapolsky et al., 2000) and those symptoms can actually disable, shorten the lifespan, and ultimately kill the patient (Sapolsky, 1999, 2000a, 2000b, 2004a), the stressed (somatizing) patient is experiencing fairly consistent and extreme symptoms and therefore will consult multiple doctors, believing that the problem is physical, with an incomplete understanding or no understanding of the vast problems created by chronic stress (Poleshuck et al., 2009; Reiche et al., 2004; Raineki et al., 2012). In this scenario, the doctor has incomplete knowledge of why the patient is seeing him, and the patient has no knowledge of the source of his or her symptoms. Therefore, going to a doctor with stress-related symptoms will likely trigger shame in the doctor for not being able to treat the patient (Fiester, 2012), setting off the cascade of managing the patient until the patient leaves the practice (Kroenke, 2007; Schwenk, Marquez, Lefever, & Cohen, 1989). Most often, the patient is then seen by the doctor as a psychiatric case, not as someone in need of medical help, and the patient feels ashamed, upset, and stubbornly continues trying to find doctors who can help, starts seeing practitioners of alternative ”medicine,” or gives up (deWaal, Arnold, Eekhof, Assendelft, & Hemert, 2008). By the time the musician finds a psychophysiologist, he or she will probably be extremely wary, will have seen numerous doctors and alternative therapists, and is in no mood to be told the problems lie in his or her head (Sharpe & Carson, 2001).

When one is ill, regardless of the illness, one has “illness behaviors” that are largely produced by proinflammatory cytokines and other neurotransmitters (Capuron & Dantzer, 2003; Conti et al., 2008; Dantzer et al., 2008; Tizard, 2008), and hormones (Johnson et al., 2005), that act on the brain to effect changes in metabolism (Kronfol & Remick, 2000) and immune responsivity (Marques-Deak, Cizza, & Sternberg, 2005; Schiepers, Wichers & Maes, 2005) such as fever, increased need for sleep, anorexia, anhedonia and weight loss (Beloosesky et al., 2007; Tizard, 2008).20 Despite these events, some people have learned not to appear “sick” (probably to avoid the illness behavior stigma), so by the time the patient, regardless of profession, lands in a medical clinic, the problems can be years old and extremely overlearned, and most patients are unaware of part or all of the daily triggers (Carroll et al., 2011; Derakshan & Eysenck, 1997b). Behavioral habits cue and maintain the problems. For these patients, a large number of the adaptations that have taken place over the years appear maladaptive to a clinician (e.g., anxiety, avoidance, substance abuse; negative affect; Kroenke, 2007; Rost, Dickenson, Dickenson, & Smith, 2006) but, to the patient, the adaptations are just “good coping skills.” To the clinician, they represent enormous treatment hurdles.

How can psychosocial perceptions make one sick? The psychosocial perceptions that, in part, create the threat fairly continually trigger the stress response, and the continued maladaptive perceptions, partly influenced by the stress response itself, maintain it long after the initial threat is gone (Conti et al., 2008; Demyttenaere, Bonnewyn, & Bruffaerts, 2006; Poleshuck et al., 2009; Rief et al., 2001; Ringel & Drossman, 2001; Tizard, 2008), creating a viscous cycle that is very difficult to break. In other words, one keeps oneself stressed in the absence of the stressor by complaining about it and reliving it, and keeping the effects of stress alive that way. It appears to be the cycle itself that plays a huge role in maintaining the problems associated with the stress response.

So, in walks the performing artist who knows his or her body well (part of the training) and absolutely needs his or her body to perform well so that he or she can perform well. This person, the artist, will likely complain more and present with an extreme symptom picture. What happens to this person in the system? This scenario creates the second part of the problem, which is enormous resistance to being sent to a psychotherapist, or even a stress management expert, to help solve the psychosocial problems present in the arts (Manchester, 2009, 2011; Schneider & Chesky, 2011). It just does not make sense to be sent to a “shrink” for muscle pain, tendonitis, and so forth. Remember that most people in the arts who come for help are professionals, and they are seeing you for professional problems. They do not want to be labeled with a “psychiatric disorder,” due to stigma and status loss. To them, they are successfully blocking their emotional problems through repression, dissociation, or both, so they firmly believe the problems are in the body. Of course, the problems they see you for are what is triggering and maintaining the stress response (Yehuda & McEwen, 2003), but they do not know this. In the arts, please remember that no one can appear “weak” or “needy.” Pathological narcissism is very common in the arts, so artists have extreme issues surrounding vulnerability, control, and grandiosity, like other high-level professions. Even the Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act (HIPAA) guidelines are often not administered perfectly, and protected health information can be leaked to prospective employers, colleagues, or others. Goffman (1963) described how people, to avoid the discretization of stigma, either underparticipate in therapies that would help them or fail to participate at all. Corrigan (2004) describes diminishing self-esteem and how stigma severely limits participation in society and health care. In the arts, stigma is as prevalent as it is in any other social group, so the added pressure of the appearance of the artist to the public and to peers (Nagel, 1988) affects employment (usually a university music department). Any public figure is a target for being discredited, and artists, whether well-known or not, fall into that category. The threat of public degradation and humiliation, it seems, is a potent psychosocial trigger for the stress response, and it can keep the artist from seeking relevant help or staying in therapy long enough for the therapy to work.

# Repress ive Coping Styl e (Repress ion)

Another significant part of the problem is the construct of “repression,” which can be defined as an interpretive bias for anxiety, whereby people underreport physiological and psychological symptoms of negative affectivity despite strong physiological evidence of those constructs (Derakshan & Eysenck, 1997a), presumably due to the stressbuffering effects of the construct (Ginzburg et al., 2002). Repressors appear to have a strong interpretive bias toward underestimation of their own anxiety and stress levels (Nagel, 1993; Gabbard, 1979), even when they are in anxiety-provoking situations (Derakshan & Eysenck, 1997b; Weinberger, 1990). Repressors are excellent at motivated forgetting. Craske & Craig (1984) found significant negative correlations between self-reports and physiological measures of performance anxiety in pianists.21 We discovered repression as a problem in the performing arts when we were testing a biopsychosocial model (Wickramasekera, 1995) on university piano majors who reported very little overt performance anxiety (Zinn et al., 2000). We tested musicians in Illinois, Texas, and California, and found that the level of repressive coping (high defensiveness correlated negatively with negative affect) was in the clinical range (above the 75th percentile) and negatively correlated with measures of anxiety regardless of geographic location. We likewise found a significant negative correlation between peripheral vasoconstriction, as measured by finger temperature, and repressive coping. Weinberger (1990) originally operationalized the repressive coping style as a mixture of negative affectivity (catastrophizing, neuroticism, anxiety, etc.) and defensiveness, and more recently, Sapolsky (2000a) has described it as a lethal personality characteristic in terms of the stress-related damage that is done to the individual as a result of this maladaptive coping skill. Taken together, these data suggests chronically high sympathetic activation in the participants of these studies, and possibly in performing artists in general, due to their ability to compartmentalize their negative affectivity (psychopathology) for extended periods of time, but not on stage.22 As mentioned earlier, many artists and musicians report that they do not feel anxious until the performance is imminent, or even until they go on stage (Brugues, 2011a; Manchester, 2009, 2011).

In summary, ideally, when the stress response activates occasionally, does its work, then deactivates, it is adaptive and does little or no harm. It is there to keep us alive, and without a stress response, we do die. Therefore, it is not the responding per se to the environmental or internal threat, but when imbalanced too long, the stress response then becomes maladaptive (Sapolsky, 2003; Margarinos et al., 1997). For the most part, in today’s society, the stress response is maintained mostly by purely psychological events, such as having an annoying neighbor, a domineering spouse, or a rude colleague at work. In the arts, it appears that the stress response is turning on and staying on too long mostly due to the psychosocial factors within the arts (Nagel, 1988; Spahn et al., 2001), such as degradation ceremonies, extreme perfectionism (Liston, Frost, & Mohr, 2003), dominance hierarchies (Barton et al., 2008; Schneider & Chesky, 2011), negative cognition (psychopathology; Craske & Craig, 1984; Liston et al., 2003; Zinn et al., 2000; Manchester, 2011), and the activation of implicit memories dating back to childhood, since those in the arts began participation in early childhood (Kendrick, Craig, Lawson, & Davidson, 1982; Kiecolt-Glaser, 1999; Lee, 1997). All of us have the ability to stay in the stress response for purely psychosocial reasons, and that process is not different in the arts. What is different in the arts appears to be some of the triggering and maintaining mechanisms, the unusual situational variables that exist (audience demand for perfection, lack of social support, underutilization of positive coping responses, trauma throughout childhood, etc.; Gabbard, 1983) and the necessary condition that their bodies work extremely well during every performance (no cold hands, tense muscles, etc.).

Pain (chronic and acute) is one area that presents significant disability for artists (Poleshuck et al., 2009). As we have seen, depression, anxiety disorders, paranoia, somatization, and other psychiatric disorders are present in the arts at a significantly higher rate than in the normal population (see Figure 33.1). These disorders are among the most threatening mental health problems, and they are triggered and maintained by chronic stress (Marques-Deak et al., 2005; Murray et al., 2010; Schiepers et al., 2004) and further exacerbate trauma, creating the psychophysiological events that then interact with the stress and trauma. Treatment of the psychophysiological events through treating the psychological disorder and the stress itself simultaneously could make a significant difference in the treatment of these disorders.

# Stress and Memory in the Performing A rts

In the arts, memory is everything. Pianists always perform from memory, and others who have the score in front of them rarely look at it. Stress severely limits all forms of learning, memory-making, and recall (Bangasser & Shors, 2010). Therefore, any sort of learning or memory issue ends up being a potential career-ender for a large number of artists. Emotional state is the primary modulator of all cognitive functions (Chrousos, 2000; Crofford, 2002; Gratto, 1998) and both are powerful modulators of the executive functions (Gruber & Goschke, 2004). The frontal lobe—the seat of the executive functions in the cerebral cortex—is now considered to be part of the limbic system due to its extreme interconnectedness with the limbic structures. Cognitive function is therefore not a passive process in which we all take part; it is instead is a product of physiological and psychological variables such as our glycemic load, overall health, prior experiences, attitudes, beliefs, current environmental stimuli, memories, and genetic data. When one’s cognition becomes impaired, then the executive functions (e.g., all aspects of memory, planning, metacognition, attention, simultaneous processing, successive processing, inhibition and other related mental processes) likewise become impaired. Furthermore, if the frontal lobes are stressed, the fronto-parietal network is affected, limiting access to the perceptual part of the brain; the more it is impaired, the less cognitive function will be available. In the arts, cognition, especially the parietal, temporal, and executive functions, are everything. During any type of cognitive impairment, the performance is likewise impaired (Wong, 2007), even if one’s physiology is operating well. We have seen throughout this chapter how this phenomenon can occur.

“Memory” is basically the storage of a learned item, and “recall” is the process of retrieving the stored, learned item for use (Sweatt, 2010). For this chapter, we divide learning into hippocampus-dependent learning and non-hippocampusdependent learning (Leahey & Harris, 1989; Sweatt, 2010). This distinction is important here due to the tremendous damage that glucocorticoids do to the hippocampus (Smith, 2005; Sorrells & Sapolsky, 2007; Warner-Schmidt & Duman, 2006) and the ongoing damage that is done to the hippocampus (Sterlemann et al., 2010) and surrounding structures as a result of ongoing stress. Movement memories are the one type of memory that is not hippocampus-dependent. This is a fine distinction, since spatial awareness, essential to movement, is hippocampus-dependent (Sweatt, 2010). Several types of learning take place when one learns and remembers the music, the dance, or one’s lines in a play. The various types of learning include conscious and unconscious learning, declarative learning (the type we think of as “learning”), associative learning, fear learning, procedural learning, and implicit learning. Remember the two vignettes at the beginning of this chapter? What type of learning was taking place in those two individuals? How do you think those children will fare in the arts as an adult? Why?

There are many models of memory, all of which are relevant here (Sweatt, 2010), but none of these are a focus of clinical work with a client. Understandably, during any form of MPA, memory is affected to some extent. For work with musicians, however, it is good to know about another type of memory that has been discussed in the learning literature—perceptual learning (Hussain, Sekuler, & Bennett, 2009; Karni & Bertini, 1997; Liu & Watanabe, 2011). We call it simply “musical memory.” Musical (perceptual) memory can be accounted for by Bruner’s theory of categorization, and this type of memory representation is called a “cognitive representation” (Leahey & Harris, 1989), which is a mental model of the event—in this case, a piece of music. Representations can be complete or incomplete, simple or complex, and contain categories, attributes, and perceptions. Cognitive representations are encoded in the parietal cortex and superior frontal gyrus of the frontal lobe. Therefore, a cognitive representation of (for example) a piano sonata by Beethoven would contain every note, rhythm, nuance, rest score marking, tempo marking, and any other aspect of the piece. The top artists create accurate and complete cognitive representations of their repertoires, then they play from that representation. One example is Glenn Gould, a Canadian concert pianist, who was able to develop the mental representation of the piece he was about to play without ever physically playing the piece. He would sit with the score, play the piece in his head (imagination), and make the representation; then, when he knew the representation was accurate, he would play the piece. Many of his recordings of J. S. Bach, Beethoven, and other composers were made at the CBS recording studio in New York, with Glenn actually playing the piece for the first time, or after practicing it just a few times. It was learned utilizing prior representations and networks (Lefort, Boniface, & Girau, 2010), making every subsequent piece easier to accomplish this way. The key is building an accurate representation of the piece, then playing from that representation.23 Perceptual memory is a type of memory that has not been well studied. The closest we can come to it is the study of mental models and representations (Sporns & Honey, 2006) and of neural representations in cognitive neuroscience. However, individuals in any profession has several discrete mental representations of what they do.

It is a fairly well established fact that stress has profoundly negative effects on all types of cognition (Liston et al., 2009; Roozendaal, McEwen, & Chattarji, 2009), including neural (cognitive) representations. When a person goes on stage and attempts to play from the representations of the pieces he or she knows, stress will greatly limit his or her ability to access the representations. When that happens, the person panics and attempts to pull together parts of the representation—just the notes, or the movement memories, or something, right there on stage. It is this process that all artists dread and attempt to avoid at all costs. The selfreport is “memory loss.”

Then there are the long-lasting consequences of stress—the practiced-in responses, many of which are maladaptive—will impair the artist’s initial learning to the performance. This recurring theme in the arts—the effects of stress at all levels—again illustrates the need for more protocol development in psychophysiology for artists. Most of all, we want to again say that stress in the performing arts is such a problem that often it is the problem. That, in our opinion, brings it squarely into the area of psychophysiology.

# Social Hierarchy and the Performing A rts

Neurological insults are the consequence of longterm stressful events (Larzelere & Jones, 2008), but one type of stress in the arts that receives little attention is the stress of dominance hierarchies. Succinctly, social rank affects both physiology and health, and social ranking is just as active in the arts as it is in any other profession (Goffman, 1963; Guptill, Zaza, & Paul, 2000; Halleland, Harris, Sornes, Murison, & Ursin, 2009). Musicians routinely place themselves in positions to be ranked, thinking it will motivate them or possibly boost their careers in some way. In music, the ranking rituals take place in just about every situation, regardless of how innocuous the situation appears to an outsider. Some of these rituals include recitals, year-end rankings (the person plays and gets a score), talent shows, and even Internet “festivals” that are just more rankings. As the person grows, the rankings become more difficult; the stakes become higher. Some enter competitions and most play in master classes, a venue in which some famous teacher critiques the player. Of course, these venues are not simple rankings; they end up being part of the artist schema (long-standing, overlearned maladaptive beliefs) in the form of self-degradation. For adult artists, the criteria become the number and quality of their performances (Halleland et al., 2009), but the ranking literally begins at the first lesson (Lee, 1997; Palac, 2006), because teachers believe that the ranks will create motivation among the students.24

Another time-honored intervention is critiquing. The idea is that if the artist does not want the critique then that person is not a true artist, or just does not want to “hear the truth.” The critiquing starts in the student’s first year of lessons; even 4-year-olds have to be publicly “critiqued.” The notion is that the critiquing will serve as motivation is misinformed and does not align with what is known about child development. The other problem with this viewpoint, however, is that there are no standards, no certified professionals, no training for teachers. Teacher training consists of going over the methods books so the teacher gets insight as to how to present the books. In music, there is nothing that even remotely resembles the training that MDs, PhDs, schoolteachers, preschool teachers, etc. have received, so the people who are critiquing do it as a result of their own abuse history simply because that is all they have. It is difficult for someone in medicine, psychology, psychophysiology, nursing, and so forth, to imagine this, but in music instruction, the “teacher” is only trained in his or her own ability to play, nothing else. There is no supervised clinical experience. There are no courses or mentoring so that they learn the nuances of human behavior. This, we believe, is why they resort to the critiquing—they have no other way to induce “practicing behavior” in their students.

# Negative Affect and Cl inical  Presentation

So, overall, due to the sources and types of threat in the arts, the single most salient psychophysiological problem is the chronic activation–reactivation of stress (McEwen, 1995) resulting from and creating more traumatic responses in musicians. As we have seen, the sources of threat are not necessarily obvious to observers. It may be obvious to us that artists are suffering from stress–response symptoms (Furuya, Nakahara, Aoki, & Kinoshita, 2006; McEwen, 1995), but in order to work effectively with the artist, one must be able to get to the bottom of that person’s life history. In many cases, the artist/patient may not believe that his or her lessons as a child were stressors. Most people in the arts report that they either had no problems (extreme denial or repression) or their own personal choices were the sole cause of their problems. Both points of view are not based in reality, but they do help us understand the source of the longheld beliefs of musician clients who may come to your practice. Small children cannot understand the motives of adults and deal with adult complexity, so they are at risk either to disregard their negative feelings or believe that they were the cause of them, not understanding that it was the adults who did not do their job (Donnelly, 1991). These overlearned beliefs mostly emanate from the child introjecting the messages of parents and music teachers. The symptoms of the stress response may be masked, and since the symptoms largely present themselves within the context of something about the music study, the clinical presentation may be episodic and specific to something that is little understood by the physician (Woolfolk, Allen, & Tiu, 2007). Stated differently, the patient will very likely report poorly during the initial interview and may appear to be a poor historian—for practical purposes, patients are poor historians, but in the arts, the most likely cause of poor reporting will be socially desirable responses learned along with everything else in the arts. Another problem is that the symptom picture may be vague due to inadequate, or musical-context-driven reporting by patients. Their clinical presentation may not match their problems. In the performing arts, a patient not only reports the same psychosocial factors as other patients (Kemeny, 2008) but he or she also has an entirely different set of triggers (problems) that create somatic symptoms or maladaptive behavior, and the symptoms often only occur in the context of the artist playing his or her instrument (Brandfonbrener, 1998). When these clients present in a medical clinic, it is very likely that they will be given a diagnosis of somatization disorder (Henningsen, Ziimmermann, & Sattel, 2003; Serban-Schreiber, 2000), partly due to the high number and intensity of reported somatic symptoms coupled with the low number and intensity of diagnostic signs observed by the physician (Brandfonbrener, 1998). Unfortunately, they will likely be sent home with a provisional diagnosis of somatization disorder, despite the fact that many of them do not meet the DSM-5 criteria (American Psychiatric Association, 2013) for somatoform disorders (Kroenke, 2007; Lawe et al., 2008) due to the nature of the stress response and how it is expressed in the arts. In other words, musicians’ complaints do not necessarily match those of people in fields other than music, and this is probably the most salient reason they are the underserved population that they are reported to be.

# Summ ary

We have discussed the myriad problems associated within the rubric MPA. We hope we have successfully presented the argument that MPA is not anxiety and simple anxiety treatments will not help. We have some recommended treatments that will help the biofeedback practitioner/psychophysiologist treat artist/clients with some success. (Please check www.marksschwartzphd.com, since these protocols are posted there, and we will continue to post new protocols over time.)

# Notes

1. Here, we refer to artists as anyone who is in the arts, regardless of age or status. This can mean a child, age 3, in his or her first lesson. 2. One well-known program for teaching music makes numerous claims about personnel being “experts in child development.” If one reads the website more thoroughly, their “child development training” consists of a 3-day workshop only. The arts lag behind nearly every other child service, including day care. Day care providers are expected to have much more education in behavior, development, and so forth, than a music teacher.

3. We find that all piano students make these global attributions in the absence of anyone doing it for them. We also find that students readily devalue their efforts. It is therefore the role of the teacher and parent to catch and reframe these attributions before they take hold in the child’s psyche.   
4. Right now, society expects the lesson once per week without parental involvement; this is detrimental for a child. Thinking that the practice problem is a motivational failure, teachers continually attempt to find creative ways to overcome these problems.   
5. Degradation ceremonies are humiliating, shaming rituals intended to give the teacher control over the child (in this case, an underpracticed child).   
6. There are many more changes at the level of the central nervous system than those listed here.   
7. The piano, the pieces, the exercises, the bench, the score—everything associated with the piano—become the conditioned stimuli, making trauma (the CR) exceedingly difficult to treat in the arts.   
8. Performances include lessons, rehearsals, and playing anywhere. For most people with MPA, playing for an audio or video recorder is a potent trigger.   
9. The defenses most often used tend to be projection, repression, dissociation, displacement, devaluation of others, and idealization of the self or others.   
10. The concept of negative affect occuping the bulk of variance in stress-related disorders is codified in the diagnosis of somatization. In medicine, the word is “psychogenic.”   
11. Not everyone experiences trauma; those who do not will recall their entire experience and will have performed well, but despite having few symptoms, they still may be unhappy with the performance. However, if a performing artist does not have trauma in his or her background, it is unlikely he or she will become a patient.   
12. This belief, of course, has no basis in reality. They have no normative data about how many times anyone plays the piece to learn it. Basically, children ae not aware of all their cognitive biases and distortions.   
13. This speaks for the children, since much dissociation and repression is learned in childhood as a defense against trauma.   
14. In the arts, there is a great deal of focus on the body. One popular trend is called “body mapping,” which is a product of this hyperfocus. Other alternative therapies in the arts likewise focus solely on the body, in the belief that patients’ symptoms are the disease.   
15. Teacher training involves no psychology, child development, and so forth. It is only about how to teach the

pieces, and it generally covers the music books available on the market.

16. There are no federal, state, or local licensing laws for arts teachers. The only licensing is a form of franchise agreement in which the author of the music training program licenses the teacher to use it. Society has so ignored this segment of the population that these individuals literally “fly under the radar” of all forms of scrutiny.

17. There is no supervised training in the arts—none. Arts teachers have only their own memories of their experiences on which to call in dealing with the problems that come up, and this is all that they have. 18. Synonyms for “somatizer” include “hypochrondiac,” “psychogenic,” and even “factitious disorder” (faking it). 19. In medicine, somatization disorder is the popular diagnosis by exclusion. Of course, it is not a diagnosis at all (Woolfolk, Allen, & Tiu, 2007). A diagnosis by exclusion is achieved by ruling out what it “appears not to be,” then assuming that the disease or malady is something else (usually somatization, hypochondriasis, etc.). 20. For an excellent discussion of “sickness behavior” and why patients have the symptoms they report, see Dantzer (2001) and Dantzer et al. (2008). 21. Repressors underreport in all types of anxiety measures (State–Trait Anxiety Inventory, ctastrophizing measures, etc.). The best way to diagnose them is to observe the strong negative correlation between patients’ self-reports and their physiology. Another way is to give them a scale such as the Marlowe–Crowne Social Desirability Scale (Loo & Thorpe, 2000). 22. A few artists are so successful that they are able to repress even onstage. Others have found ways to repress onstage but they only play in limited situations, thereby limiting themselves to a truncated list of opportunities. 23. Some musicians represent the printed score more readily than they respresent the sound of the piece. Some pianists are especially adept at this and make their living off their ability to glance at a score and play it. Paradoxically, they do not memorize their music well. 24. We have found (through numerous teachers’ organizations) that ranking is so common that all types of student interaction involve ranking in some way.
