Zaza, C. (1995). Playing-related musculoskeletal disorders in musicians: A systematic review of incidence and prevalence. Canadian Medical Association Journal 158(8), 1019–1025.   
Zinn, M., McCain, C., & Zinn, M. A. (2000). Music performance anxiety and the high risk model of threat perception. Medical Problems of Performing Artists, 15(2), 65–71.

# Sports

Vietta Sue Wilson, Wesley E . Sime, and T imothy Harkness

It was very important to visually see my brain working on a screen, because it was   
there in black and white. No tricking my own mind to think this or that . . . it   
made me aware of things I did not realize were happening. —Professional tennis player

I know what you mean about failing to stay with my intentions during performance. I think I probably have changed my mind in midswing at least 10,000 times throughout my career.

—PGA player (known as much for his anger outbursts as for his golfing success), upon seeing his brain by NF during his golf swing

# Models and Th eory

We believe that biofeedback (BF) and electroencephalograpy biofeedback or neurofeedback (EEG BF or NF) in sports are prime opportunities to develop and fine-tune many of the skills needed for performance in sports. Typically, the mental skills found to be important for sports include selftalk, imagery, goal setting, energy management, attentional focusing, confidence, and competitive readiness (Williams, 2010). Additionally BF/NF may benefit fatigue reduction, injury prevention, recovery, and the learning/execution of skills.

BF in sports has a long history of inventiveness with coaches, athletes, and sport psychologists designing or adapting devices to further their sports training. Examples include using a carpenter’s level on a Canadian canoe or kayak for information on body movement while on the water; standing with one foot on each of two scales to measure body pressure changes during shooting events; scotch tape on the forehead to alert the athlete to muscle contraction; or a coach shaking hands with his athletes to determine skin temperature and sweating in the hands, which can be an accurate indicator of not only readiness for competition but also fear of failure.

The use of professionally administered BF/NF in sports has taken a firmer foothold in professional sports, with soccer clubs in Italy, England, and Spain (Wilson, Peper, & Moss, 2006), and ice hockey teams in Canada incorporating BF/NF into their training programs. Olympic teams from North America, Europe, Asia, and South Africa continue to increase the number of participants who are provided BF/NF in a specially designed room (Lake, 2010). Private clubs in many sports are now purchasing BF/NF equipment for gymnastics, baseball, judo, tennis, and other sports. Some clubs use professionally trained consultants, whereas others buy the equipment and their sports personnel “learn as they go.”

BF/NF practitioners in sports have essentially followed the paradigms used in the traditional application of BF for medical and psychological purposes. More recently, models of how BF/NF can be integrated into the sports setting have been proposed (Blumenstein, Bar-Eli, & Tenenbaum, 1997; Kamata, Tenenbaum, & Hanin, 2002). This review reports only those studies that have used athletes given that there is a significant difference between them and the nonathletes who are often studied in research.

Most of the work published about BF/NF in sports consists of field studies or clinical interventions, with very few well-controlled research designs. Our goal in this chapter is to provide a summary, not an in-depth, critical review of the literature, to illustrate the possibilities. Hence, caution is necessary in applying this synopsis of the literature. The absence of appropriate control groups, unclear methodology and statistical treatments, inclusion of nonvalidated outcome measures, and the like, leave us unable in many instances to make strong inferences. However, to us, the encouraging results reports seem to provide helpful guidance and suggestions for future research. Additionally, very few applications have been conducted within actual sports settings, due to prohibition of the use of equipment within the sport. Readers interested in more in-depth reviews of BF in the sport literature are referred to other sources (Ash & Zellner, 1977; Blumenstein, BarEli, & Tenenbaum, 2002; Collins & McPherson, 2006; Hatfield & Landers, 1983; Leonards, 2003a, 2003b; Petruzzello, Landers, & Salazar, 1991; Sime, 1985; Tenenbaum, Corbett, & Kitsantas, 2002; Wilson & Hamilton, 1983; Zaichkowsky & Fuchs, 1988). For reviews focusing on EEG and EEG BF in the sports literature, readers may find other research of interest (Gruzelier, Egner, & Vernon, 2006; Hammond, 2007; Hatfield, Haufler, & Spalding, 2006; Hatfield & Hillman, 2001; Lawton, Hung, Saarela, & Hatfield, 1998; Vernon, 2005).

To establish the efficacy of BF/NF in sport, one needs to demonstrate at least the following:

1. That the system being monitored is relevant for optimal performance, and changes in this system are associated with improved performance (or the typical functioning of that system is “good enough” and does not need superior functioning). For example, hand or foot temperature may not adversely affect a marathon runner, and training this system may not change performance in marathon times.   
2. That the athlete has deficiencies in the relevant modality during performance.   
3. That training of the relevant system results in

the athlete learning how to monitor and control the system, and he or she can do so under the stress of competition.

Thus, the findings and suggestions in this chapter should be viewed as preliminary explorations, with the hope that others will also begin to build BF/NF clinical and research programs that are specifically applicable to sports. There are many places within a sports training regimen where we believe BF/NF can be helpful, varying with the sport, the athlete, and the current stage of the training program. For example BF/NF could be incorporated with the following:

1. Physical training: Strength and conditioning, injury prevention, injury rehabilitation, training zones (e.g., heart rate), muscle and central nervous system fatigue assessment.   
2. Sports skills training: Effective versus ineffective body responses (muscles, heart rate, respiration rate, brain state, etc.), fine-tuning of skills, consistency of performance, knowledge of when one is “in the zone.”   
3. Tactical training: At present films are used immediately after a play, as in football, as knowledge of results. Feedback could be provided to the person while watching or knowledge of his or her performance through BF/NF.   
4. Mental training: Skills used by elite athletes include imagery, goal setting, thought control, arousal management in competition with plans, coping strategies, and so forth. Precompetition readying plans could be facilitated with BF/NF.   
5. Social training: BF/NF could be implemented to provide feedback about interpersonal reactions with coach, competitors, and teammates.

# Requirements Unique to Sports

Basic physiological and psychophysiological studies of athletes have identified aspects unique to elite performers that should be considered when developing BF/NF within the sports setting. For example, the mental control of skills of elite athletes is different from that of those who are less skilled, which in turn is different from that of nonathletes (Vallacher, 1993; Wulf & Su, 2007). Further, experts differ from those who are less skilled even when they activate muscles performing the same skill (Janson, Archer, & Norlander, 2003). Tyc, Boyadjian, and Devanne (2005) found differences in cortical representation depending on the muscles that were used in the sport, which they ascribed to training. Even elite athletes in the same sport may differ from each other psychophysiologically to a greater degree than those investigated in analogue laboratory settings (Kamata et al., 2002).

When involved in movement, the changes within the individual complicate the psychophysiological responses; thus, what may have been optimal in the nonmovement setting (i.e., training in the office), may not be optimal during competition. For example, when moving, muscle fatigue is more pronounced in men (Hicks, Kent-Braun, & Ditor, 2001), and respiration changes are different for women or individuals with smaller body sizes (Sheel & Guenette, 2008). Attention requirements for observing displays may be attended to in a different manner once the body is in motion (Van der Kamp, Rivas, Van Doorn, & Savelsbergh, 2008). Thus, the uniqueness that occurs between sports and in specific settings within sports calls for individual and sports situation interventions. For example, during a rally in tennis, players may benefit from learning to control the brain and body state that promotes relaxing or activating the appropriate muscles, while focusing their attention in an “aware” manner to adjust to the environment and their opponent, and to make an appropriate strategy choice before hitting the ball. However, to return a fast serve, players may need to learn to “react” and not think, because the brain–body state will be different. Both situations are open to training with BF/NF, with creative development of protocols and displays that can assist the player to overlearn the appropriate skills.

In golf, there are multiple distractions, visually and auditorily compounded by the doubts and fears in the mind of the golfer. Respiration, heart rate variability (HRV), and electromyography (EMG) may condition the player to become so absorbed in the information gathering and processing that he or she becomes oblivious to external, as well as internal, chatter. Then, as the preshot routine gradually winds down toward the point of action, the imagery of the shot and the steadfast commitment to strategy and intention becomes crucial to success as the golfer lets go and swings.

# Models for T raining  BF/NF

Training models have been proposed by several authors, but no single model has received thorough testing. An early model for suggesting how to enhancing performance was Wilson and Bird’s (1981) “Optimal Performance and Health Model” for determining what is needed and then what BF/ NF training may best address the need to improve performance while persevering health.

# Developing an Optimal Performance Model

The model begins with a task analysis, focusing on what is needed to succeed. Next is a detailed person assessment, conducted to determine whether the person has what is needed to succeed. If yes, the next step involves training/coaching, which focuses on control of body and mind by incorporating feedback. Reinforcement for proper behavior, interspersed with time for recovery, equals enhanced performance, as well as good health.

The first step for optimizing performance is to determine specifically what range is needed in each sport in each situation (physiological, psychological, social, etc.). Among these are size requirements for a center in basketball, a gymnast, or a football lineman; availability of fast-twitch muscle fibers in sprinters; motivation to persevere across time; and finances or opportunities to obtain training. Additionally, perfectionist traits (always having to do things correctly), attentional focus abilities, and control of mind states may be elements necessary for success. The second step is to determine whether the athlete has these elements, through thorough assessment, and then to prescribe the amount of training necessary to attain these characteristics. The third step is to train the athlete through deliberate practice (Ericsson, 2007) to obtain or improve the elements that will most improve sport performance. This training requires feedback of results (outcome; is the ball in or out?) and feedback on performance, that is, what the body was doing to obtain results. This is where BF/NF is essential for objective states about what the performer is doing/thinking during good and poor performances. BF/NF skills need to be integrated with the other sports skills with sound principles of skills learning and deliberate practice. Finally, the athlete is rewarded for obtaining or progressing toward the objective—winning and improving. The BF/NF training needs to be “periodized” (Bompa & Haff, 2009) within the total training of the sport with appropriate work–rest ratios, then transferred and applied in the competitive environment.

Typically, BF/NF assessment and training include one or all of the following, generally integrated with mental training skills: frontalis surface electromyography (SEMG), trapezius SEMG, sport-specific location SEMG, palmar electrodermal activity (EDA), heart rate (HR), and HRV, respiration rate (RR) and location, hand surface temperature, and EEG at FC for relaxation and attention (see Figure 34.1).

Blumenstein et al. (1997) published a model on how to incorporate BF with mental training for sport. Their “Wingate five-step approach” used HR and electrodermal BF as aids to teaching parasympathic (calming) and sympathetic (activation) control. The BF/NF was integrated in stages (introduction, identification, simulation, transformation, and realization) that included relaxation training, imagery, simulation, video rehearsal, and training in competitive settings. Their textbook outlines in detail the use of the model with sports teams (Blumenstein, 2002).

Utilizing Hanin’s (2000) extensive work on identifying the individual zone of optimal function, which uses an athlete’s retrospective reported emotional levels and whether the athlete is in or out of the “zone,” Tenenbaum’s group (Edmonds, Tenenbaum, Mann, Johnson, & Kamata, 2008; Filho, Moraes, & Tenenbaum, 2008) advanced the model through linking physiological (e.g., HR, EDA, SEMG, HRV) and perceived arousal and performance through a mathematical probabilistic model. By manipulating multiple introspective data from the athlete, feelings associated with gradients of affect (both arousal and pleasure) and physiological and performance measures, they determine an individual’s best zones for performance and their associated probabilities of better performance. They named this the “individual affect-related performance zones” (IAPZ), because the procedure promotes the identification of individual differences within each player and sport. It takes into account the interactive nature of several emotions during early and late phases of competition. It also identifies the BF modality to which the participant responded most strongly in competition, which is then used for training. Additionally, when athletes were performing at or near optimal levels, they reported experiencing elevated levels of arousal, and felt that their emotions were pleasant and helpful.

# Response Modalities

In this section we present each modality, along with a brief history of the research performed with athletes. The seminal review of BF by Zaichkowsky and Fuchs (1988) is incorporated, along with the addition of studies located since that review. Clinical suggestions on how to apply that modality to assessment and training for different purposes within the sports setting follow each review. We have included examples in which BF/NF has been or might be used to facilitate the training of athletes. Information on how to use BF/NF to address injuries is not covered, because this requires a specialized set of information and techniques that are critical to quick recovery. Finally, references are provided so that the reader may locate the specifics of studies.

![](images/a6978b40219327f38ef962e90105849f59098165d348e8d404ddeade09dd37fd.jpg)  
FI GURE 34.1. A sample summary of a psychophysiological profile of an athlete performing tasks, followed by recovery periods for several modalities.

# Respiration Rate, Heart Rate (HR), and Heart Rate Variability

Physiologists have documented the effects of training and competition on RR and HR in athletes for decades; HRV is a more recent research topic. From this they have prescribed specific exercises to enhance respiration and heart functioning, mainly for fitness enhancement (Burke, 1998). BF of the ventilation and muscle responses of runners on a treadmill, compared to control and distraction conditions, was found to reduce RR and ventilatory measures significantly (Hatfield et al., 1992). HR has been trained as both a knowledge of results (outcome) and as BF (a process within the person); most endurance athletes have programmable, portable home trainers, with the goal of training to remain within the specified fitness “target zone.” By providing relaxation with HR BF, long-distance runners were able to lower their HR, ventilation, and oxygen consumption in a manner that was not attributed to training (Caird, McKenzie, & Sleivert, 1999). Burrill (1990) found that providing HR BF improved performance with junior-level competitors in the biathlon. Wilhelm and Roth (1998) were able to show that movement effects on HR could be distinguished from changes in HR due to mental stress, but no studies were found that assessed athletes.

Lacey and Lacey (1974) provided a framework suggesting that changing HR by deceleration makes possible a faster reaction time. In the 1980s, HR deceleration was noted to be associated with improved skills performance in archery and shooters; the more skilled performers had more HR deceleration (Hatfield, Landers, & Ray, 1984; Landers et al., 1980). Boutcher and Zinsser (1990) found that both novice and elite golfers had more shallow breathing and held or exhaled their breath during putting tests. HR dropped before, during, and after the putts for both groups, while the actual average HR of the elite golfers was slower overall. Radlo, Steinberg, Singer, Barba, and Melnikov (2002) noted that for novice dart throwers using an external attentional style, HR decelerated prior to release, while throwers using the internal focus had increases in HR. Prapavessis, Grove, McNair, and Cable (1992) provided rifle shooters with thought stopping, muscle relaxation, and HR BF. Compared to a placebo control group, the trained shooters’ anxiety, gun vibration, catecholamine, and HR were reduced. Carlstedt (2004) reported a case study in which he provided HR deceleration feedback to a tennis player with positive results.

HRV, or the amount of time between heartbeats (interbeat interval), has been established as a reliable measure of the autonomic nervous system, particularly the parasympathetic response, and has been shown to be an important BF modality for enhancing heart health. Increased HRV, as has been pointed out in reviews of the literature (Porges, 2007; Thayer & Lane, 2009), is associated with improved physiological, emotional, and cognitive performance, which is believed to be due to increased communication between the heart and brain. Athletes have been shown to have higher levels of HRV when compared to nonathletes, which was attributed to increased parasympathetic activity (Puig et al., 1993).

HRV BF has been reported to improve reaction time and relaxation in wrestlers (Vaschillo, Visochin, & Rishe, 1999), batting performance in baseball players by 60% (Strack, 2003), and a golfer’s scores, as well as a reduction in stress (Lagos et al., 2008). HRV was also found to be a viable and convenient measure of arousal in golfers in precompetitive situations (Murray & Raedeke, 2008). One study (Tanis, 2008), however, indicated no improvements in performance for female volleyball players who trained with HRB BF.

# Training of RR, HR, and HRV

Athletes are first assessed at rest, under stress, and during recovery. Breath holding is ascertained by observation of the raw tracings of RR for flat lines among the typical roller coaster curves of inspiration and expiration (see Chapter 13 in this book for measurement details). If the athlete shows any anomalies of breathing (shallow breathing, hyperventilation, muscle overuse, etc.), then respiration training is conducted (Peper & Tibbetts, 1994), which is followed by training in HRV. Seldom do we conduct HR BF directly, because athletes generally have very low resting HRs as a result of fitness training, and we have not found a reason to change HR. Rather, HR is monitored for deviation from baseline as one possible indicator of overtraining. The athlete is routinely trained to demonstrate respiratory sinus arrhythmia (with HR going up during inspiration and down with expiration). At the same time that HRV is being tracked, the athlete is asked to maintain a resonate frequency of about 0.1 Hz, which is easily accomplished by slowing the breathing rate to about 5.5 breaths per minute (br/min). Figure 34.2 shows a typical athlete’s synchrony or coherence after one session of training. The respiration curve does not perfectly track the stair step HR changes, but with a peak frequency of .09, it is very close to being an excellent respiratory sinus arrhythmia (RSA) pattern. This athlete does not have perfect RSA, but compared to his typical breathing pattern (asthma and allergies), this is a recording of his much improved breathing. After the athlete demonstrates the ability to regulate RSA with the desired HRV, a home trainer is assigned. The athlete is asked to complete at least 10 sessions of 6 minutes a day in which RSA is achieved. The home trainer is subsequently checked on computerized equipment to confirm that the desired goals were met during home practice. For some athletes, HR is monitored during training, and when possible during competition. The sport performance results (number of correct hits, or errors) are noted with the HR. If the HR at a certain level, such as 180 bpm, is associated with errors, or creates feelings of anxiety/loss of confidence, the athlete purposely activates a breathing routine to slow the HR back into a zone for optimal performance.

# Surface Electromyography

By the 1930s, researchers and coaches held the general belief that relaxation is essential for maximum performance (Lawson, 2000). Early on, Jacobson (1936) documented the effects of relaxation on general stress, and he believed that college athletes, due to their experience in muscle control, should have better relaxation skills than nonathletes. He developed the first EMG device and compared the two groups using needle electrodes placed in the biceps. While most athletes generally relaxed better, some did not know how to relax. Although Jacobson’s work was important, the foundation of using EMG BF for sports is based on the pioneering work of Basmajian (1967), who demonstrated that a person could be trained to control muscle movement as small as one muscle fiber. SEMG is relatively inexpensive and typically can be used in sports settings without interfering with performance. In fact, sports is control of muscles!

Typically, SEMG of the frontalis muscle is combined with relaxation techniques to lower self-reports of “anxiety” or to enhance feelings of relaxation. DeWitt (1980) reported positive results in SEMG lowering and performance ratings, while Tuksomoto (1979), Brik (1984), and Zachkowsky, Dorsey, and Mulholland (1979) generally demonstrated lowering of SEMG but with no corresponding improvements in performance. It now seems naive to think that lowering one set of muscles would improve performance, especially when the chosen muscle group was not identified as necessary for the performance or interfering with the individual’s performance.

![](images/2e36aa9dba145563a8552e50beb166640d84fafa91873eb3ddf615c8b053713d.jpg)  
FI GURE 34.2. Respiration data after one session of training.

Improved results with SEMG have been reported when specific muscles relevant to the sport performance are targeted. Wilson and Bird (1981) targeted the hamstring muscles of gymnasts and demonstrated improvement in flexibility while Cummings, Wilson, and Bird (1984) trained SEMG of hamstring muscles of sprinters and demonstrated improvement of flexibility on retest.

Since the need for relaxation in sports was recognized and advocated by coaches more than 70 years ago, the initial attempts to enhance relaxation through SEMG BF were not without foundation. Porges’s (2007) sophisticated research has noted the interaction of the muscles, especially of the face and head, with the brain and heart. This suggests possibly some effect from the facial SEMG BF in earlier studies that also may have influenced the autonomic nervous system and helped to calm the athlete. The effects were probably not sizable enough to alter either sports technique or possible general overarousal that may have influenced performance. While we include facial muscle relaxation in the athlete’s precompetition arousal control routine, we suggest that targeting specific muscles involved in specific sports actions, such as goal kicking or free throws, works more directly and quickly on technique essential for performance. A current example of muscleand sports-specific EMG is Harkness’s work with golfers (reported in the next section).

# Training Using SEMG

The SEMG assessment used in the office typically includes one channel on trapeziums for shoulder tension and a second channel on the frontalis for overall face/neck tension. Sometimes the second channel is sports specific, such as muscle activation in the arm or wrist in shooting sports. Typical microvolt levels of athletes, based on measurement of hundreds of athletes, are below 3 mV, with the suggestion that higher microvolt levels typically mean unnecessary shoulder contractions. Almost all athletes are then trained to identify and control muscle contractions, especially in the shoulders. Olympic track coaches, such as Francis (personal communication, C. Francis, 1987) and Kraaijenhof (personal communication, H. Kraaijenhof, 1988) advocate training runners to reduce shoulder tension so as to increase leg speed. Research (Ferris, Huang, & Kao, 2006) confirms the belief of track coaches that one’s arm motions can enhance or hamper leg speed.

The SEMG is then assessed and trained in the sports setting after the athlete demonstrates control in the nonsports setting. For example, after a tennis player learned muscle relaxation of her shoulders off the court, SEMG telemetry was used to assess and train her on the court, because she was unaware of the increased shoulder tension during play. The top frame in Figure 34.3 illustrates the rhythm of her shoulder tension as she hits the ball. When she stops hitting (490 seconds) she should be relaxing the shoulders, but she still is producing over 50 mV of muscle tension. After being reminded to let go of the tension (approximately 508 seconds), she significantly reduces her muscle tension to about 3 mV. Her coach consistently reports that she “plays too tight.” She still has to be reminded to let go of the tension in her shoulders between points; learning to release takes many repetitions. The lower graph shows her EDA or sweat response when she stops hitting (the EDA while playing is not reliable due to movement artifacts), suggesting that emotions continue after the physical act of playing. Her increase in EDA (at approximately 515 seconds) probably is a reaction to commands from off court to let go of her shoulders. Note that her emotional responses are still evident approximately 30 seconds after stopping the rally.

We have also used modified frontal–posterior neck (FpN) placement in a case study, with one sensor over the masseter muscle and the other in the posterior cervical region, because this placement is believed to be ideal for validating the importance of relaxed face and neck muscles just prior to motor performance, especially those sports involving fine-motor tasks such as putting in golf. Residual tension created by teeth clenching may mask the critical perception necessary for success in delicate movements such as putting. This method was used successfully in consultation with a well-known professional golfer, Payne Stewart (Sime, 2004). Payne was amazed to see that his posterior cervical and trapezius muscle tension levels were related to successful putting performance. Very quickly it was determined that Payne could use the audible signal from the SEMG to track the gradual decrease in muscle tension in the last few seconds of his preshot routine. He was encouraged to relax his muscles prior to taking back the putter for each attempt. The protocol for this training was determined by simply exploring various options with the player. The next step was to set the threshold so that the feedback stopped near the lowest level he could reach under these conditions. After each putting trial, adjustments in threshold were made to make reaching “no sound” just prior to initiating the putt, which made the task more challenging. Payne was intrigued by this new form of enlightenment associated with his preparation for playing more relaxed under pressure. Shortly after this single session of training, Paine’s putting average improved, and he won the U.S. Open a few weeks later.

![](images/73774df124a7f1eb8b311e114a5843fe51a1563eb530d19aa82a15645ae401b4.jpg)  
FI GURE 34.3. The SEMG monitored with telemetry during a tennis rally is shown in the upper tracing. The lower tracing is of an electrodermal response (note that the equipment did not work for EDA during movement (at approximately 500 seconds).

# Multiple SEMG Assessment and Training for Sports‑Specific Skills

Harkness (2009b) has shown the value in using multichannel EMG for the assessment and then training of proper mechanics for golf. He suggests that there are problems with the current use of video as the standard for determining a good golf swing. There are two aspects to technique: getting into the right positions to hit the ball, and using the right muscles to hit the ball. Prior to the advent of videos, when golf coaches used the naked eye alone to analyze swings, they were sensitive to both aspects. But the problem with using video, especially with its ability to slow or stop the action, is that it emphasizes positions over muscles. What most golfers, and apparently most coaches, do not know is that it is all too easy to be in the right positions but use the wrong muscles.

Harkness (2009b) has developed a swing analysis called the “quantitative EMG” (QEMG), by placing sensors over key swing muscles in the golfer’s body. In Figure 34.4, a wireless system, FlexComp Infiniti, measures muscle activity from up to 10 sensors and sends the information by a wireless system to a computer.

Harkness (2009b) has analyzed golfers ranging from professional players to “low-handicap” elite amateurs, and the results have been surprising. His interpretation of the new data suggests the following about the golf swing:

1. Invisible errors: Swings that look almost identical to the naked eye and on video can hide significant variations in muscle use. Even elite golfers can have large muscle variations from swing to swing, of which they are unaware.

![](images/b82a8b744e4547e82b17920470e70e26d884e07e5355451e5f03a667b6491066.jpg)  
FI GURE 34.4. A wireless SEMG system for measuring up to 10 channels of muscle tension in sports.

2. Points of power: Different golfers use different muscle groups to generate the power that drives the swing.   
3. Predictable errors: Straight shots have clearly different muscle activity from pulls and pushes.   
4. Optimal performance training: Muscle activity can be trained, and these hidden errors can be corrected, creating a more consistent swing and a more controlled and confident golfer.

This information was based on QEMG measures of the golfer and highlights the individual’s problems with his or her swing. The same equipment can be used for the retraining of muscles.

The inconsistent firing of muscles produces predictable errors that can be captured with the QEMG. The golfer hits many golf balls; the QEMG data are statistically analyzed for what muscle activity is associated with either a good or a poor shot. For example, in Figure 34.5, a golfer tends to pull his shot when his left gluteus activity exceeds his right gluteus activity, and to hit it straight when his right gluteus exceeds his left gluteus activity.

It is important to train the correct muscles, which create the “power points,” until they become a conditioned reflex, and to untrain the incorrect power points, until they are no longer a conditioned reflex. These reflexes are typically deeply wired into the nervous system from extensive practice, and it takes precise and discerning work with SEMG to overcome them and create new ones. The most efficient way to do this is through QEMG BF training with a skilled consultant who can measure and interpret finite and discrete results. Without this information, even by concentrating on “working this muscle” or “driving hard from here,” there is substantial erratic variation between optimal and poor shots, as observed with elite athletes.

# Special Considerations

Not only a thorough understanding of the muscular system is helpful for the consultant but also knowledge about the sports-specific requirements and skills subsets within each sport is essential. For example, Janson et al. (2003) have shown different SEMG firing rates and peak amplitudes to be optimal depending on the skills level of the athlete. Sports requirements are different even within the sport of shooting—trap and skeet shooters require different skills, even when they utilize very similar weapons of fire. If the researcher is in doubt, the coach should be consulted as to the needs for the sport and the particular athlete.

One purpose of training is to develop awareness, usually through up–down training, both off and on the playing field, because it has been shown to be critical to long-term success in sports. The athlete then needs the ability to use the skills of appropriately adjusting the muscles during stressful times. In our experience, athletes can learn how to relax or coordinate the muscles very quickly. What takes time is the consistent integration and use of these skills in practice and competition.

![](images/6139b85996200a3d673486040e534a2f4d2b77de5568f2c5e791df9958b97cbb.jpg)  
FI GURE 34.5. SEMG of right gluteus maximus activation for two different swings: one with optimal gluteus activation, the other with suboptimal gluteus activation.

# Peripheral Skin Temperature

Sympathetic nervous system responses inherently drive hand skin temperature down in the face of competitive pressure. Cold hands are classic indicators of stress reactions, but cold hands in sports may not always be a deterrent to high performance. For example, hand temperature decreases in movement are typically due to blood flow demands of the working muscles and may or may not be due to stress. Thermal BF has been shown to be effective in facilitating improved performance in cold weather sports, such as hockey and curling (Kappes & Chapman, 1984). This may be important to the athlete’s dexterity in manipulating equipment in outdoor sports.

Donaldson (personal communication, 2007) and Wilson (first author) have observed that cold hands are typical in elite endurance athletes (skaters, marathon runners, etc.) in nonstress situations. They do not believe cold hands in the elite athlete’s are always signs of stress; rather, the athlete’s blood flow has adapted to the sport requirement and due to habit, the athlete’s hands do not revert to normal temperatures when out of the training/ competitive environment. It is as if a “new norm” has been created. We have no evidence that this impedes recovery or health, and we could locate no research that addresses this topic.

In a series of clinical studies, Peper and Schmid (1984) examined the hand temperatures of athletes prior to and after competition. For foil fencers, they found that hand temperature dropped an average of 3°C from pre- to post-competitive times. For rhythmical gymnasts, Peper and Schmid found that during imagery, the temperature remained stable with a mean of 31.2°C. For elite levels of gymnasts (state, national, Olympic), approximately 80% had a pre- to post-competition reduction in hand temperature, with an average drop of 1.3°C, from 29.3°C to 28.0°C. The authors noted significant reductions in temperature for some of the gymnasts who had an error in their routine. The use of peripheral temperature for fencers and rhythmical gymnasts seems logical given that these sports use hand movements to control the sword and implements (hoops, balls, ribbons).

# Training with Temperature BF

Hand temperature has been assessed using both computer-based technology and portable devices. Placement is typically on one of the fingers, but the back of the hand has also been used.

When using a laser temperature probe for assessment (see Figure 34.6), which is available inexpensively at most auto stores, one has to be careful about targeting the same spot on the body from the same distance each time. Temperatures may vary 2–3°F depending on placement and ambient temperature. These are easy to use in sport settings, because one can be away from the athlete and still obtain measurements and observe gross changes quickly. Repetitive measures can thus be made at rest and during practice, and in both comfortable and stressful competitive environments.

# Special Considerations in Sports

Cold hands may affect participants in specific sports, such as archers, shooters, gymnasts, golfers, or others in physical training. Soviet researchers (Baskin & Sleptchuk, 1990) studied the temperature of normal adults and athletes under nonstress, practice, and competitive situations. Although results are not available in English, it is noteworthy that the researchers considered temperature to be worthy of study. Comparison of good and poor performance can be correlated with the preperformance temperature to see whether the temperature is important to success. For example, performance in the long jump was more affected by time of day, not the athlete’s temperature (Reilly & Down, 1986). Harkness (2009a) reported that Olympic shooting Gold Medalist Abhinav would sometimes respond well to temperature training, and at other times not be able to raise his finger temperature by 1°. He speculates that Abhinav probably shot his Olympic Gold–winning 10.8 with icy cold fingers.

![](images/15ddf07458d58a076aac259a20724c732a3a54d0fed01a94dbc6dfec658f1c8a.jpg)  
FI GURE 34.6. A laser temperature unit used to assess temperature before a swimming competition.

When an athlete has very cold hands, below 80°F, the traditional questions regarding migraines, Raynaud’s disease, or high anxiety should be explored. For example, a national level 14-year-old female in a winter sports had temperatures in the 70s at the beginning and end of the first session. She reported no migraines, Raynaud’s disease, or stress. In the second session, the low temperatures remained, but before more thorough questions could be asked about her cold hands, she indicated that she would be missing the few next sessions because she was scheduled to have heart surgery. Following the surgery and recovery, her hand temperature has been consistently in the 90s. Extreme readings should be retested, then investigated to determine why the system is so atypical, with referral to the appropriate personnel, if needed. Medically challenging symptoms and syndromes can often be discovered when dealing with presumably “healthy, elite, athletes.”

# EDA, Galvanic Skin Response, or Sweat Response

The use of EDA or “sweat response,” often called galvanic skin response (GSR) or psychogalvanic reflex (PGR), has a long history in psychology. Generally, EDA was used to assess expression of emotions and was considered an autonomic concomitant of individual differences in trait anxiety. Early researchers (Harmon & Johnson, 1952; Johnson, 1951; Moscou, 1971) explored use of GSR to study prestart emotions of athletes. Gracz (1977) used EDA to measure sweat responses prior to serving in tennis, in order to determine the range of values associated with having a better serve. EDAs that were too high or too low were associated with poorer serves. Higher sweat responses were noted in the precompetitive state of cricketers, compared to substitutes waiting “on the bench” before competition, suggesting higher anxiety and stress associated with competition (Sunaina, Anand, Suresh, & Singh, 2006).

# Training of Electrodermal Response

The EDA responses are assessed along with the initial intake profile, in the search specifically for emotional responses in simulations of time pressure, high expectations, and competition. There is no “normal” baseline, because intraindividual differences are significantly affected by heredity, skin density, and so forth (see Peek, Chapter 3, this volume). It is atypical, however, to have an athlete above the 30–60 microSiemens range not express stress or other exciting or distressing emotions, and this high emotional level can generally be confirmed in the EEG or other modalities confirming a stress response. In addition to assessing initial responses to stressors, it is equally important to evaluate the athlete’s recovery to baseline. Athletes who have a tendency to evaluate or judge after an event typically do not return to their baseline levels of EDA (or emotions).

Recording EDA in the office setting is extremely valuable when asking the athlete about the training/competition from the past week. EDA typically spikes well above baseline when the athlete is “emotionally involved” and the clinician can probe to see what is causing the increased responses. If the athlete has an emotional response to a particular issue (failure, conflict, injury, a fall, etc.), he or she is shown the reading and asked to “park” or stop thinking of the emotions of the event. The athlete may initially report that he or she is not worried, concerned, and so forth, but often it takes many sessions to lower the EDA, which may reflect concern about the true issue at hand.

Whether or not the athlete is dealing with a significant issue, he or she is asked to demonstrate the ability to “park” emotions. This is facilitated by asking the athlete to increase EDA responses, then lower them. The use of imagery of past events is often invoked at this point or, if a video of past performance is available, the athlete is asked to watch it. Some advocate that an athlete should not be allowed to play after being injured until the EDA has returned to preinjury levels. Desensitization becomes the goal. Athletes are often provided a GSR home trainer to practice first during nonstressful times, then during stressful situations. When they feel they have mastered the emotions of practice, they are asked to use these learned skills when there is conflict with significant others at home, which may be a true test of emotional control!

# Neurofeedback or EE G BF

As noted earlier, because there have been several reviews on the use of EEG in sports, here we present only a synopsis. The assessment and training of EEG with athletes requires the same standards and practices as those certified in EEG BF for clinical purposes. It is assumed that those wishing to conduct NF have had training and supervision with a qualified professional due to the complexity of EEG training and potential adverse reactions (Hammond, Stockdale, Hoffman, Ayers, & Nash, 2001). The important difference in sports is that the clinician needs to understand fully the tasks that the athlete needs to perform in order to train purposeful states. NF can be used to help athletes focus their attention during times of boredom and distraction, extend the length of time they focus, measure and train imagery, enhance productive thinking, limit negative or unproductive thinking, enhance awareness of thoughts and feelings, and get “into the zone.” Clinicians also need to extend the benefits of NF to the competitive process through integration with good sports psychology skills. Additionally, we recommend training under simulated competitive conditions to enhance the transfer to the actual competition. When possible, EEG assessment and training should be done during the actual sports performance, which is possible in shooting sports, or before the shots in golf or tennis. We have not found equipment that will reliably and accurately measure EEG during movement.

In the subsequent sections, we discuss two research areas, then present a clinical protocol that was used in the training of an Olympic champion.

While Wilson’s model notes that what the athlete brings to the setting is relevant to training, there has been a little research to find out the athlete’s predisposition to performing well during stress. It is possible to determine whether athletes have different abilities that may influence their ability to perform, and in fact, the clinician/coach may make changes in how to teach these athletes based upon this information. For example, alpha rhythms at rest are associated with better information processing during cognitive and sensorimotor tasks (Del Percio et al., 2007). Additionally, Wilson and Hamilton (1983) found that track and field athletes who were in the top half of ability, based upon performance results on their team, had more O –T alpha at rest than athletes in the bottom half of the team. Wilson, Ainsworth, and Bird (1985) found that athletes who had more O –

T alpha at rest with their eyes closed performed better under the stress of volleyball competition (as noted by the ratings of two coaches) than did those with lower alpha readings. These findings could identify those athletes who need training to perform under the stress of competition. Recently Babiloni and colleagues (2010) used low-resolution tomography (LORETA) and found that alpha at rest, in eyes-closed condition, in the parietal and occipital regions was higher in elite karate athletes compared to lesser skilled karate athletes and nonathletes. They replicated these findings in elite rhythmical gymnasts compared to nonathletes. Whether athletes are genetically blessed with enhanced alpha or it is a result of training to become an elite athlete needs to be investigated with a longitudinal study. Elite athletes are different! And how they are trained in BF/NF would be different than training those who are less skilled athletes.

Hatfield et al. (2006) presented a comprehensive review of the EEG sports literature that supports our use of NF in sports. Interested readers are encouraged to examine their extensive analysis of this research.

A very simplified interpretation of their conclusions includes the following:

1. The demands of the sport result in use of task-specific cortical resources in an efficient manner; that is, the same amount of work is accomplished but with less cortical activation or effort.   
2. Expert performance is associated with quieting of the left hemisphere, and in some cases, quieting of the right hemisphere.   
3. Tasks are performed better if the person learns to become more “automatic” rather than engaged in “thinking.”   
4. NF can be used to create the changes in cortical activity that are associated with expert performance.

These findings can provide clinical and research guidance as to how to study and train athletes with EEG. Caution is advised, because many factors influence results. For example, while most researchers suggest increases in alpha are associated with improved performance, ShelleyTremblay, Shugrue, and Klein (2006) found that increases in beta activity, rather than a reduction, occurred for golfers during more successful putts. They ascribe this difference to having novice golfers in their test population.

# Training of EEG

We use a single-channel EEG assessment that includes an evaluation of four ratios that we believe are related to being able to perform well during sports. First, the mean power theta/beta ratio is used to determine whether possible clinical attentional disorders (attentional deficit disorders, autism, etc.) are possibly present and therefore in need of further assessment by a professional. An athlete with brain-related disorders can compete at the highest levels in sports but to lessen the chance that more serious problems underlie the athlete’s clinical issues and to provide better training protocols, the athlete should be referred to a professional for a full workup (see Monastra et al., 2005). If the theta/beta ratio is normal (compared to Monastra’s database), but one sees other atypical EEG findings, such as spindling beta, lack of alpha, or there is an extensive history of school failure, reading disorders, or inability to attend, the athlete should be referred. In our protocol, linked ears with the active electrode at Cz is used to determine the predominant frequencies while the athlete is at rest, completing the Stroop test, math, reaction time task, imagery, and anticipation and noise stressor with a recovery period between each event. The frequencies at 7, 8 and 9 Hz are also evaluated at eyes-closed rest compared to a recent sports image for an assessment of potential imagery ability. Typically, those who report high imagery ability (able to incorporate detailed, multisensory qualities that have vividness and clarity) have a higher frequency during the imagery compared to the resting state. Not all athletes who report good imagery ability display the same frequency, and some athletes report “feeling,” “talking,” or ‘knowing,” yet do not show high frequencies at Cz.

Following an interpretation of the first ratio, the next three ratios are based on the work of Thompson and Thompson (2006), which focuses on interpretation of meaning and data standards. The second ratio is called “problem solving” and suggests that the athlete can process information from outside the body (6–10/16–20 Hz),; the third ratio is called “intensity/tries too hard” (19–22/11– 12 Hz), and the last ratio is called “busy brain” (26–34/13–15 Hz).

In this example (see Figure 34.7) using the resting eyes-open baseline, data in the first column labeled C to the right of the top graph, was from the athlete who had an individual peak alpha frequency of 10, which is average for an adult. If there is considerable slowing, in the 8’s, the person is referred for a professional evaluation, because a concussion or other problems are a possibility. From the first column on the left side of the page labeled “events baseline,” the theta/beta ratio is in the normal area for an adult, suggesting the ability to pay attention. The score in the next row, “problem solving,” reports ability to take in external information and is again normal. The third measure, “intensity/tries too hard” is below the level we consider too high or interfering with performance, or a value of 1.5. The last measure, “busy brain” is above 1.5 for several tasks, which is higher than typically reported by athletes. This athlete confirms that he or she engages in self-talk to the point that it interferes with performance. Accordingly, we proceeded to downtrain “busy brain” with this athlete while watching for concomitant improvement in the actual sport performance.

![](images/e8c9f9bb0c314b9aa6b853fc3934ef1dc38bc4b8c46866afcdc58965eacd9e76.jpg)  
FI GURE 34.7. A summary of the EEG assessment used with athletes.

When there is a confirmation of a problem by both the EEG brainwave frequency and the self-report, that ratio is targeted for training. For example, if “busy brain” or negative self-talk is considered the biggest problem, calming (alpha enhancement) and self-talk reduction (high beta suppression) are targeted at C and sometimes F . If wandering attention is the reported problem, then theta suppression at C is begun and sensorimotor rhythm (SMR) enhancement is pursued to encourage the correct state for performance.

For more details on typical training of an athlete, see Strack, Wilson, and Linden (2010) and the program of Harkness, discussed below.

# Mul tipl e BF/NF  Programs

Several studies have been reported that used multiple BF and NF measures. For example Peper and Schmid (1984) used EDA, temperature, and HR BF to achieve congruence in cognition, emotions, and stress reactions while facilitating team performance in various dance routines in an elite rhythmical gymnastic competition. In this Olympic sports consultation, BF was paired with mental rehearsal to enhance concentration and coordination between partners to achieve high levels of performance. Temperature was measured remotely by infrared laser technology in order to provide assessment and feedback at critical moments prior to competition.

The athletes reported feeling less stress and more performance enhancement benefits from the program.

Kavussanu, Crews, and Gill (1998) compared SEMG with a multimodal BF (SEMG, EEG, and HR) and a control group for the enhancement of basketball free throws. No significant results were shown for either BF group.

Using the Wingate five-step approach as the protocol for providing BF, which uses HR and EDA, Blumenstein et al. (2002) found improvement in running and swimming. Edmonds et al. (2008) used nine experienced race car simulation drivers and identified each driver’s optimum IAPZ.

One group received HR and EDA feedback on optimal arousal, another group received feedback on poor arousal, and the control group received attention control instructions but no feedback (but the amount of training was not specified). While still emphasizing the importance of individual results, the group data suggested that BF integrated with other skills was an effective tool to help the drivers self-regulate their affect-related performance.

# Extended Case Study of an Olympic Champion

A single-case example of an extensive and comprehensive program for the delivery of BF/NF to sport is that of Harkness (2009a) with the athlete who won the first ever Olympic Gold Medal for India. Harkness reported, “I think the success in my consultative approach with him, was in my understanding of how and why pressure would manifest in technical errors, and where to intervene to correct these errors. Abhinav is the consummate professional, a supremely talented and determined athlete, and was already a World Champion when he came.”

To qualify for an Olympic final in shooting, one probably needs to shoot 56/60 bull’s-eyes. In the final round, shots are scored with decimal points. So a shot that hits the bull’s-eye with the middle of the pellet scores 10.9, while a shot that hits the bull’s-eye with the edge of the pellet scores a 10.0. Abhinov shot a 10.8 with his final shot to secure the Gold Medal—0.2 mm off perfect, from 10 meters away.

In terms of the NF/BF training provided him, a range of modalities was used, and about 150 hours of training were completed in the 6 months prior to the Olympics. Every day began with diaphragmatic breathing using abdominal and thoracic stretch sensors. Early in the intervention, electrodermal response was trained until Abhinav’s control became too subtle for the device to detect. Down-training EDR while watching DVDs of his competitive performances seemed especially helpful. Peripheral temperature training was a hit-andmiss modality and was not continued as the revelation of low temperature levels could have been discouraging and a threat to his motivation.

The first area trained with NF was up-training low alpha at T3 (to quieten his internal monologue). HRV frequency training to gain control over low- and high-frequency amplitude showed initial improvements that eventually would be lost as competitions drew nearer. Eventually we (the athlete and Harkness were in agreement) stopped doing HRV, because poor scores could have been interpreted by the athlete as a harbinger of poor shooting.

Finally, the athlete and Harkness trained RSA at six breaths a minute, three breaths a minute, and two breaths a minute, with 40-second pauses. These long pauses were very useful as still target shooting happens during breath holding. When shooters are anxious, they may underestimate their breath-holding capacity, and feel under pressure to trigger before they run out of breath. Learning to overcome an inhalation stimulus removed some of the time stress of triggering. Issues associated with the preshot “long gaze,” forgoing the eyeblink reflex in the period before critical moments, were also important to address.

The challenge when training NF for performance is achieving a combination of acute focus without mental busyness. Many practitioners uptrain high alpha (11–13 Hz) as a form of performance training, because high alpha is considered to be associated with high performance. We do see higher levels of eyes-closed high alpha with elite athletes, compared to nonathletes, but we see lower levels of eyes-open high alpha. Not every athlete fits this pattern. With Abhinav, alpha was up-trained while theta was down-trained at T . At C and O up-training was at either 12–15 Hz or 15–18 Hz for a sharper narrow focus of attention.

When some athletes are asked what up-training a particular frequency feels like, they just smile and shrug their shoulders. By contrast, Abhinav had excellent self-awareness, so he was able to give precise feedback on the sensations of any wavelength trained. This ability allowed us to work as an experimental team with what frequencies seemed to give the best response.

# Post‑Olympic Feedback

Before the Olympics, the frequencies and sites to train were not entirely clear. The debate about what to train at C , P , and O centered around the dilemma of needing Abhinav to be extremely calm, so that he would not tremble as he sighted, but he would also remain alert and reactive enough to trigger as the bull’s-eye came into the center of his sight.

Post-Olympic feedback from Abhinav indicated that T3 alpha helped him the most to shoot calmly during the heat of competition. He felt most comfortable training at 10–13 Hz at P , but broke world records when training with 15–18 Hz at C . This is the frequency Harkness (2009a) and Abhinav ended up focusing on the most. Even though Abhinav did not like it as much as the 10-13 Hz at Pz, he felt it resembled a competitive state more accurately. Harkness and Abhinav also downtrained 22–26 and 26–30 Hz at Pz to lessen busy brain activity.

Harkness and Abhinav stopped NF training after 80 hours. The initial training was done “in the chair,” then moved to the practice range. They spent equal amounts of time training in the chair and while shooting.

Subsequent to the Olympics, Harkness has been training athletes to down-train beta at T instead of up-training low alpha. This seems to correspond more to silencing the internal monologue. Additionally, down-training prohibits subtle jaw muscle tension from influencing the results, because athletes can “cheat” on an ”up-training” strategy and increase their scores by subtle jaw tension. Also, Harkness has been down-training theta at F3 and down-training 15–38 Hz at F4 to enhance the “approach” strategy in the left hemisphere while lessening the “avoidance” strategy of the right. Harkness’s clinical impression is that this enhances confidence and aggression.

# BF/NF Combined with Other Sports Strategies Imagery

Imagery has been found to be effective in enhancing performance in sport (Driskell, Cooper, & Moran, 1994; Feltz & Landers, 1983; Wilson & Shaw, 2012). Imagery is not as effective as actual performance, but when every small improvement may mean the difference between success and failure, it is understandable why imagery has become ubiquitous in mental training regimens. Current research has focused on whether imagery works better in the competitive environment (Smith, Wright, Allsopp, & Westhead, 2007); in short, it does. Smith and Holmes (2004) found that a video and audiotape helped golfers image in real time. There is no absolute evidence that kinesthetics is more powerful than visual images or whether the view of the image from the internal or external perspective is important. Holmes (2006) provides a review of these issues.

Pioneering work (Jacobson, 1936) indicated changes in SEMG when athletes image. More sophisticated SEMG equipment yielded equivocal support for imagery’s effect on performance (Bird, 1984; Clark, 1960; Deschaumes-Molinaro, Dittmar, & Vernet-Maury, 1991; Hale, 1982; Harris & Robinson, 1986; Jowdy & Harris, 1990; Noel, 1980). Wang and Morgan (1992) and Wuyam et al. (1995) found that ventilation and heart rate increases were proportional to imagined exercise. Radlo (2007) reported that EEG BF with active imagery improved maze tracing performance better than passive imagery or a control procedure.

The scripts used in imagery, the time taken to complete the image, and the choice of positive versus negative imagery are all factors to be considered when using imagery with BF/NF. For a more complete review on how to implement imagery in sports, please consult Morris, Spittle, and Watt (2005) or Williams (2010).

# Viewing Films

Blumenstein, Bar-Eli, and Tenenbaum (1995) advocate having athletes watch a video after they have learned to self-regulate using multimodal BF. Holmes, Collins, and Calmels (2006) reported that manipulating the posture of shooters while watching the film (sitting, standing, standing and holding the gun) yielded few differences in brain activity except at T . They ascribe the lack of differentiation to the dominance of the visual mode. In another use of films, DeMichelis (2009) had the athlete watch a film of when he was injured, while monitoring his psychophysiological state. The film was viewed repeatedly until the athlete’s psychophysiology state returns to the same level as that in his preinjured state. The athlete was then deemed ready to return to competition.

# Feedback after Good or F ail ed T rials

An interesting question is whether the knowledge of results (KR) should be provided when the performer has not had success. While using neither athletes nor a recognized sport, Chiviacowsky and Wulf (2007) found that results may be instructive for BF/NF. Using a beanbag throwing task, no differences were found between providing KR during practice and not doing so; however, there were significant effects on delayed retention tests. These findings are viewed as supporting a view that feedback has motivational properties. Additionally, using the Stroop test, alpha was shown to increase following correct responses, but there was no alpha increase following errors. Carp and Compton (2009) further suggested that cortical arousal plays a role in modulating behavior. This may suggest that the training of athletes should include both failed and successful trials.

# Attention to D etail after Becoming  an E xpert

Wulf (2008) has established that athletes perform better if they adopt an external attentional focus (the effects of their action) rather than a more internal attentional focus (on movements themselves). Wulf’s study suggests that to optimize performance of an elite performer who has overlearned a skill, an internal attentional focus may inhibit the “automatic” behavior and actually decrease performance quality. This suggests that the focus of the highly trained athlete should be “Just do it.”

# Th e Future of BF/NF  in Sports

We believe that BF/NF in sports will lead the way in future research and application, because performance in sports has measurable outcomes, participants are highly motivated and have differing degrees of expertise, and the subject pool is universal. The use of BF/NF by Olympic and professional teams/athletes will mean that more monies will be invested to develop smaller and easier to use equipment, with better telemetry. Just as BF and NF are currently being integrated into medical surgical simulation (Zachkowsky, 2010), sports will begin to integrate BF/NF with sports-specific simulation. Home BF/NF trainers for all important modalities, with ties to coaches/clinicians via the Internet, will become part of a typical training program for elite athletes.
