# Pediatric Applications

Timothy C ulbert and Gerard A . Banez

Health care service and delivery paradigms for children continue to evolve, and as they do, clear recognition of the complex interplay of mind and body is evident in virtually all manifestations of disease, illness, health, and wellness (Taylor, Goehler, Galper, Innes, & Bourguinon, 2010). In addition, 1 in 6 children is currently identified as having some sort of developmental disability (Boyle et al., 2011), and 1 in 10 children in the United States have a mental health diagnosis of some kind (AAP [American Academy of Pediatrics] Committee on Psychosocial Aspects of Child and Family Health Task Force on Mental Health, 2009). The mainstream pediatric literature continues to see an increase in the publication of studies on mind–body techniques (McClafferty, 2011), and this increase parallels the growing recognition of emotional–behavioral problems in at least 10–20% of pediatric primary care visits (Starfield & Borkowf, 1969; Sharp, Pantell, Murphy, & Lewis, 1992; Kolko, Campo, Kelleher, & Cheng, 2010). In the last 25 years, mind–body techniques have been demonstrated to be an effective primary or adjunct treatment for a wide variety of health problems across the lifespan (Astin, Shapiro, Eisenberg, & Forys, 2003; Kohen, 2010).

In addition, parents and their children are increasingly seeking out holistic, nondrug strategies to treat common medical and mental health problems (Kemper, Vohra, & Walls, 2008). For children and teens, biofeedback training offers an ideal, user-friendly vehicle for teaching about the power of mind–body connections while enhancing self-efficacy (Torjesen & Olness, 2009). The field of biofeedback as applied to pediatric populations is poised to make a major contribution in both the assessment and treatment of many common childhood biobehavioral (or psychophysiological) disorders, because it provides concrete evidence for mind–body links and offers a means to address both somatic and emotional–behavioral components of these complex problems (Culbert, Kajander, & Reaney, 1996; Smith, 1991; Barowsky, 1990; Torjesen & Olness, 2009). Additionally, biofeedback represents a user-friendly treatment method that can be easily delivered in any pediatric health care setting in a time- and cost-efficient manner.

Scientific research and years of clinical experience across multiple settings suggest that biofeedback and other mind–body techniques are safe and effective, and will play increasingly prominent roles in well-child care, school settings, performance enhancement of all kinds, and the care of children with chronic illness and disability (Gertz & Culbert, 2009). This chapter briefly reviews the relationship of biofeedback to other relevant areas of holistic pediatric care; describes features of biofeedback as applied to children and adolescents that differentiate it from adult approaches; and reviews biofeedback applications for specific childhood disorders. (Note that biofeedback for other pediatric applications (e.g., pediatric headache), electroencephalographic [EEG] biofeedback for attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder [ADHD], biofeedback in neuromuscular rehabilitation, and heart rate variability [HRV] training are all reviewed in other chapters of this book and are not covered extensively in this chapter.)

# Background

# Why Biofeedback, and Why Now?

As is the case in adult medicine, pediatric health professionals continue to see an increasing percentage of patients presenting with psychophysiological disorders and psychosocially mediated morbidity. Studies suggest that as many as 25% of children and adolescents experience these multidimensional problems (Brugman, Reijneveld, Verlhulst, & Verloove-Vanhorick, 2001). As we identify more young patients with mind–body challenges/imbalances, it is important to develop and offer therapies that explicitly acknowledge this linkage and that assist children with both components. Biofeedback provides an ideal strategy with which first to educate pediatric patients about this mind–body link, then to help them develop appropriate self-regulatory skills (Culbert et al., 1996; Attansion et al 1985; Gertz & Culbert, 2009). One can imagine the relevance of this strategy on a spectrum that includes health/ wellness promotion, stress management/prevention strategies and peak performance training on the one hand, and the remediation of specific psychophysiological dysfunction and support of multidimensional chronic illness concerns on the other.

Biofeedback is culturally syntonic with today’s computer-savvy youth; therefore, it provides an immediate “connection” and point of multimedia interest that can help to engage pediatric patients in ways that traditional talk therapies may not (Culbert, Reaney, & Kohen, 1994). In our experience, this engagement in the process of understanding mind–body connections and the cultivation of lowered states of arousal often enhances positive rapport with children or adolescents and allows them to “open up” in ways that standard therapeutic approaches may not (Culbert, 1999).

For the therapist, biofeedback lends precision to the process of behavioral therapy, particularly when “relaxation” is a goal. “Relaxation” is often subjectively defined as feeling “loose,” “good,” or “comfortable.” Biofeedback allows a therapist to define it more clearly as a state of lowered sympathetic nervous system (SNS) arousal and/or a more desirable overall balance in autonomic nervous system (ANS) activity. In addition, biofeedback can allow a therapist to observe a patient’s physiological reaction patterns in real time when discussing certain key topics or themes. This is then helpful in demonstrating to a client the mind–body connection in concrete, immediate terms. For children, this aspect of treatment is powerful in that it actually shows how “a change in your thinking has caused a change in your body’s reactions!” Particularly in pediatric patients with somatization disorders and stress-mediated symptoms, this invaluable information and the insight it provides, allows them to move forward more quickly in the therapeutic process.

# Biofeedback in the Context of Complementary Medicine

Another reason that biofeedback is becoming an attractive strategy in pediatric health care is that it offers an effective nonpharmacological alternative for certain childhood disorders (e.g., headaches), engages children more actively in their own treatment, and provides a “life skill” that will be useful over time.

The movement toward more participatory, natural, self-directed therapies is an essential factor in the growth of the area of complementary and alternative medicine (CAM; Gaudet, 1998). The National Center for Complementary and Alternative Medicine (NCCAM) of the National Institutes of Health describes five CAM domains, with biofeedback falling within the domain designated as “mind–body therapies” (https://nccih.nih.gov/ health/integrative-health#types). CAM generally refers to a group of diverse medical systems and health care practices that are not presently considered to be part of mainstream, allopathic medical practice and are therefore not typically taught as part of the standard medical school curriculum or reimbursed by insurance providers (Plotnikoff, Kemper, & Culbert, 2008). CAM is being replaced by terms such as “integrative” and “holistic.” Integrative medicine refers to a system of care that embraces the best of conventional and complementary therapies, and views health broadly as a function of mind, body, and spirit together. Principles of integrative medicine also reflect support of the body’s natural healing capacities, the importance of the patient–provider relationship, and the health of the whole person in the context of family and community (Kemper et al., 2008).

CAM use is growing in pediatrics as it is in the adult population, with the use of mind–body therapies (including biofeedback) leading the way (Kemper et al., 2008; Culbert, Olness, & Vohra, 2010; McClafferty, 2011; Sibinga & Kemper, 2010). A 1998 survey of pediatricians (Sikand & Laken, 1998) revealed that more than 50% of those surveyed reported that they would be willing to refer a pediatric patient for a CAM therapy. The follow up in 2004 confirmed this trend (Sawni & Thomas, 2007). The CAM therapies these pediatricians identified as being the most likely for referral were biofeedback and relaxation training. Epidemiological studies identify the use of CAM by children and adolescents as ranging from 11% of patients in pediatric primary care, up to 85% of patients in pediatric hematology/oncology settings, and 73% in one group of homeless youth (Spigelblatt, Laine-Ammara, Pless, & Guyver, 1994; Kemper & Wornham, 2001; Breuner, Barry, & Kemper, 1998; Post-White, Fitzgerald, Hageness, & Sencer, 2009).

The accumulated evidence supporting the safety and efficacy of mind–body therapies, including biofeedback, suggests that they have moved well beyond the category of CAM and have earned a placed in mainstream medical and behavioral health practice (Gertz & Culbert, 2009). In addition, the mechanisms of therapeutic benefit of mind–body techniques are now being better defined. Extensive studies in psychophysiology and psychoneuroimmunology have begun to delineate more clearly the bidirectional relationship of mind on body and body on mind (Kohen, 2010).

nosis, diaphragmatic breathing, meditation, progressive muscle relaxation, and autogenics. These skills all empower patients to focus their minds in ways that positively influence their bodies (Gertz & Culbert, 2009). In children’s language, this means teaching them to “be the boss of your body!” (Culbert & Kajander, 2007).

Self-regulation techniques can also be described as strategies that help people to discover and cultivate their innate healing abilities. Examples of such techniques include the voluntary modulation of specific physiological functions, the directed use of mental imagery and therapeutic suggestion, selfmonitoring, enhancing somatic awareness, and control of positive and negative self-talk tendencies. These strategies are commonly blended with biofeedback, other techniques (e.g., aromatherapy, acupressure, journaling) and lifestyle changes (e.g., sleep, exercise, diet) to create self-care “toolkits” for kids in clinical practice.

# Physiological Control

Research over the past 30 years demonstrates that children and adolescents are capable of voluntarily modulating physiological processes, including peripheral temperature, muscle activity, breathing, brain electrical activity, and certain aspects of immune function, such as salivary immunoglobulin A (Labbé, Delaney, Olson, & Hickman, 1993; Hewson-Bower & Drummond, 1996; Olness, Culbert, & Uden, 1989; Delaney, Olson, & Labbé, 1992). In fact, studies indicate that children are generally better than adults in many self-regulatory skills. Children make excellent biofeedback subjects (Attanasio et al., 1985), for reasons that include the following:

# Sel f‑Regul ation Skills

Training of self-regulatory ability, when considered within a developmental context, is consistent with children’s natural drives for mastery and autonomy (Dixon & Stein, 2000). “Self-regulation” is the ability to control inner states and responses with respect to thoughts, emotions, attention, and physiological function (Bell & Deater-Deckard, 2007). Furthermore, it appears that self-regulation is a complex process operating concurrently at physiological, attentional, emotional, cognitive, and behavioral levels, and that a network of central nervous system (CNS) structures associated with behavior and regulation work together (Thayer & Lane, 2000).

It is useful to think of biofeedback within a class of skills that involve self-regulation, including hyp

•	 Children are more enthusiastic.   
•	 Children are less skeptical about self-control procedures.   
•	 Children have confidence in their special abilities.   
•	 Children have more psychophysiological lability.

Because children are developmentally prone to use of fantasy, it is important to understand the role that mental imagery may naturally play in biofeedback and relaxation therapy. The links between certain thoughts or types of mental imagery and specific physiological response patterns are well documented. For example, mental rehearsal of an activity, such as an athletic routine or playing a musical instrument, can result in ANS responses that are similar to the activity itself—often with increased SNS activity, including increased heart rate (Lee & Olness, 1996; Wang & Morgan, 1992). Children can be taught to cultivate certain kinds of mental imagery as a way to achieve a desirable level of control in a certain physiological parameter. For example, utilizing imagery about warm thoughts and relaxing, pleasant sensations can facilitate peripheral temperature change. Clinical experience suggests that images of relaxing experiences will more typically facilitate a lowered state of SNS arousal, as evidenced by lower heart rate, increased peripheral temperature, or decreased electrical skin conductance.

In addition to mental imagery, certain emotional states are capable of eliciting strong physiological response patterns (Cacioppo, Klein, Berntson, & Hatfield, 1993). Directed cultivation of emotions such as love, warmth, and feelings of safety and support may help in eliciting desirable patterns of physiological response, such as improved coherence of heart variability patterns, decreased electrodermal activity (EDA), or increased peripheral temperature (Institute for HeartMath, 1997).

To date, no true “standards” have been established for “normal” baseline measurements of ANS functions in children and adolescents, such as peripheral temperature, EDA, or surface electromyographic (SEMG) activity. Boyce et al. (1990) assessed the physiological concomitants of stress and anxiety in preschool children, and noted a wide range of individual responses across a variety of autonomic and immunological measures. The lack of normative data on physiological baseline data in children is a limiting factor in research in this area.

It is also clear that, like adults, certain children may demonstrate significant lability across a number of physiological measures, while others may be “hot responders” in only one modality. In our own experience, it is not necessarily true that children need to achieve absolute thresholds in terms of a given physiological response to achieve symptom resolution. For example, adult protocols commonly call for a peripheral temperature above 92°F or an EMG level below 5 mV. Many pediatric patients show improvement in target symptoms, with a physiological response that is trending in the right direction consistently.

Biofeedback is a prototype self-regulation technique ideally suited for children and teens, who are commonly computer literate and find the video game-like format appealing (Culbert et al., 1994).

Success requires control of emotions and attention in the training process to achieve results in modulating physiological activity in the desired therapeutic direction. Through the use of self-initiated and self-guided strategies, individuals participate actively in the control and remediation of impairing symptoms. With the acquisition of self-regulation skills such as biofeedback training, children and adolescents are encouraged to take “ownership” of their personal challenges, then to utilize their talents and abilities in facilitating their own progress toward the attainment of the desired level of health and wellness. Self-regulation skills are truly life skills, which assist children and teens in adapting to ever-changing life circumstances and stressors in an ever-changing environment. Many children today lack self-control with respect to a healthy lifestyle, including issues of diet, exercise, and sleep hygiene. Acquisition of self-management strategies at a young age provides an ideal opportunity for the developing person to learn self-control, enhance his or her sense of self-efficacy, and move toward a healthier lifestyle.

# Cl inical Appl ications of Biofeedback with Ch il dren

Disorders of Elimination (Encopresis and Enuresis)

Biofeedback has been used in the treatment of urinary and fecal incontinence, either as a primary treatment modality or as an adjunctive therapy. Biofeedback for these disorders monitors the physiological mechanisms that underlie or are associated with the child’s wetting or soiling difficulties (e.g., EMG monitoring of a weak external anal sphincter), and treatment effects are specific to the type of biofeedback. Research on biofeedback for elimination disorders continues to grow, as do the clinical implications.

# Urinary Incontinence

The urine or enuresis alarm has been described (Schwartz, 1987, 1995) as a biofeedback device for children with nocturnal enuresis. The modern urine alarm consists of a small, transistorized unit that is worn on the body and activated with urination during sleep. A typical urine alarm protocol (Houts, 2008; Schmitt, 1990) requires the child to use the alarm every night during treatment. When the alarm rings, the child is to wake, go to the bathroom, and finish urinating. Afterward, the child is to put wet sheets and clothes in the laundry, place a clean sheet on the bed, and reattach the alarm. Use of the urine alarm continues until the child achieves 14 consecutive dry nights. A simple overlearning procedure is then implemented to prevent relapse and build confidence (Houts, 2008).

A substantial body of literature, including numerous controlled studies, has documented the efficacy of the urine alarm as a treatment for nocturnal enuresis. In their systematic literature review, Mellon and McGrath (2000) identified the urine alarm as a necessary and, for many children, sufficient component in the treatment of enuresis. They concluded that the urine alarm is an effective treatment that meets criteria for the highest level of empirical support. Compared to desmopressin acetate (trade name: DDAVP) and imipramine, the two drugs commonly used in treatment of enuresis, the alarm fares best in terms of cure and relapse rates, risks, and cost (Schmitt, 1997).

While these drugs may be helpful over the short term, they do not produce a permanent cure. The cures associated with the urine alarm endure and hold up over time. The clinical challenge in successfully implementing urine alarm treatment is the time and effort required for both the child and parents. In our practice, we have found that clear, accurate instructions and appropriate expectations (e.g., treatment may require 12 weeks or even more) are essential. Evaluating and eliciting child and parental motivation and commitment are critical to outcome. It is important to convey that the urine alarm can be a very effective but labor-intensive method. For children who have not responded to other treatments, compliance with the urine alarm may be enhanced through combined use of a urine alarm and DDAVP. After the child is dry for 3 weeks, the dosage of the drug is decreased gradually. Combined use of an alarm and DDAVP reportedly is most helpful for children with severe wetting, and for those with behavior and familial problems (Bradbury & Meadow, 1995).

More conventional biofeedback techniques are increasingly used in the treatment of children who have dysfunctional voiding and diurnal enuresis. For example, when detrusor-external urethral sphincter dyssynergia is associated with diurnal incontinence, EMG and urodynamic biofeedback are used to train coordination of detrusor contraction and sphincter relaxation. Biofeedback therapy for adult urinary incontinence is common, and emerging evidence suggests that it shows promise for children with daytime wetting (e.g., Combs, Glassberg, Gerdes, & Horowitz, 1998; Porena,

Constantini, Rociola, & Mearini, 2000). Vasconcelos et al. (2006) found that biofeedback training enhanced voiding training for patients ages 5.9–15.2 years with dysfunctional elimination syndrome. Fifty-six patients with dysfunctional elimination were divided into two training groups. Both groups adhered to a voiding and drinking schedule, received instruction on adequate toilet posture, maintained voiding diaries, and went through proprioceptive and pelvic floor training (Kegel exercises). One group also received biofeedback training. Though both training regimens reduced urinary incontinence and urinary tract infection, only those patients who received biofeedback training showed a decrease in postvoiding residual urine. A more recent study (Kaye & Palmer, 2008) indicated that biofeedback with animation may be more efficacious than nonanimated biofeedback in treating dysfunctional voiding and its concomitant urinary symptoms. Participants were 120 girls who presented with urinary complaints and exhibited dysfunctional voiding on EMG uroflow. Sixty girls were treated with biofeedback using EMG tracing alone (nonanimated), and 60 girls were treated with an animated biofeedback system. Dysfunctional voiding resolved in 95% of patients in both groups, and postvoid residual reeducation indicated similar results. Children in the animated biofeedback group achieved success in significantly fewer sessions. Biofeedback may also have a role in the treatment of giggle incontinence (Richardson & Palmer, 2009). Participants in this research included 10 females and two males, ages 6–5 years. They underwent four or more sessions of biofeedback techniques to learn Kegel exercises. When at least four sessions were performed, patients had a full response, including heightened external urinary sphincter awareness and muscle improvement.

# Fecal Incontinence

Typically, biofeedback treatment of fecal incontinence, specifically, encopresis, involves some combination of the following: training to discriminate the sensation of rectal distention, strengthening of the external anal sphincter via EMG training, training in the synchronization of internal and external anal sphincter responses, and, for those with pelvic floor dyssynergia or paradoxical contraction, training in the coordination of abdominal and pelvic floor musculature for elimination. The type of biofeedback used is a function of the physiological mechanisms hypothesized to underlie the child’s soiling. For example, if a child’s soiling is felt to be associated with poor sensation of the urge to pass stool, training to improve rectal sensation is indicated.

The majority of existing research appears to focus on biofeedback treatment of encopresis associated with pelvic floor dyssynergia or paradoxical contraction. “Pelvic floor dyssynergia” refers to the abnormal closure of the anal canal during straining for defecation. Children with dyssynergia squeeze their buttocks and hips during attempts to defecate and are unable to relax their external anal sphincter. These abnormal defecation dynamics are thought to develop in response to past painful bowel movements. To control the amount of stool being passed and protect against pain, the child squeezes the anal canal during defecation. Assessment of pelvic floor dyssynergia uses SEMG electrodes to monitor abdominal muscles and an anal sensor (or SEMG electrodes just outside the anal opening) to evaluate the child’s ability to maintain external anal sphincter relaxation while contracting abdominal muscle. If there is dyssynergia between the two muscle regions, biofeedback training is used to teach appropriate responses.

Numerous uncontrolled studies suggest that this type of biofeedback is an effective adjunctive treatment for encopresis (see Loening-Baucke, 1996, for an excellent review of this literature). In a more recent investigation, Byrne, Solomon, Young, Rex, and Merlino (2007) reported that more than 70% of patients in their large series (513 consecutive patients) demonstrated improved short-term outcomes with biofeedback treatment. Improvements were seen in incontinence scores, patient assessment of continence, quality of life, and maximum anal sphincter pressures. The findings from controlled studies, however, have not been as positive. Though some controlled investigations have found that biofeedback is beneficial (e.g., LoeningBaucke, 1990; Wald, Chandra, Gabel, & Chiponis, 1987), other studies have reported that the addition of biofeedback to standard medical management does not increase effectiveness of treatment (e.g., Cox, Sutphen, Ling, Quillian, & Borowitz, 1996; Cox, Sutphen, Borowitz, Lovatchev, & Ling, 1998; Nolan, Catto-Smith, Coffey, & Wells, 1998; Van der Plas et al., 1996), even for those children who positively evidence pelvic floor dyssynergia. In one study that suggested the benefits of biofeedback treatment (Loening-Baucke, 1990), no statistically significant differences in the control group were found at long-term follow-up (Loening-Baucke, 1995). Brazzelli and Griffith’s (2006)

review of 18 randomized trials with a total of 1168 children with fecal incontinence revealed no evidence that biofeedback adds any benefit to conventional treatment in the management of functional fecal incontinence in children. Twelve of the 18 studies they reviewed involved biofeedback. The results of controlled studies have been described by some investigators, such as LoeningBaucke (1996), as disappointing. Others, such as McGrath, Mellon, and Murphy (2000), point out methodological problems (e.g., subject selection, varying types/methods of training) that complicate the process of evaluating the efficacy of this treatment.

At present, existing data do not support the use of biofeedback as the sole treatment for encopresis. In clinical practice, we encourage consideration of brief biofeedback training, typically no more than two to four sessions, for children who have pelvic floor dyssynergia (Catto-Smith, 2007) and have not shown a positive response to standard medical management. To be most helpful, such training needs to be provided within the context of a comprehensive biobehavioral treatment of encopresis, including bowel clean out, regular sitting schedule, medications, and dietary restrictions/recommendations. The addition of a home biofeedback component may not improve clinical outcomes. Croffie et al. (2005) found no significant differences in outcomes between a group treated with biofeedback in the laboratory alone and a group treated with biofeedback in the laboratory and at home. Participants were 36 patients with chronic constipation, for whom at least 6 months of conventional treatment had failed, and demonstrated dyssynergic defecation. They concluded that feedback is beneficial for some children with chronic constipation and dyssynergic defecation, but supplemental biofeedback did not improve long-term outcomes.

# Chronic and Recurrent Pain

Chronic and recurrent pain in children and adolescents is a significant problem, with high prevalence worldwide (McGrath & Finley, 1999). As is the case with adults, research on pediatric pain has expanded dramatically, and new approaches to pain have crossed disciplinary boundaries.

As described by Andrasik and Schwartz (Chapter 20, this volume), biofeedback should arguably become the treatment of choice for pediatric headache. In addition, clinical experience suggests that biofeedback and self-regulation techniques such as relaxation/mental imagery and diaphragmatic breathing are also very helpful in working with a variety of pediatric pain issues. These include chronic pain (e.g., pain associated with juvenile rheumatoid arthritis, cancer, sickle-cell disease, and burns, as well as recurrent abdominal pain [RAP] and fibromyalgia), needle phobia, and acute procedural pain, although the literature is somewhat limited (Knudson-Cooper, 1981; Lavigne, Ross, Berry, Hayford, & Pachman. 1992; Varni, Walco, & Katz, 1989; Shapiro et al., 1995; Yaster, 2010; Evans, Tsao, & Zeltzer, 2008, Tsao, Meldrum, Kim, Jacob, & Zeltzer, 2007).

Children’s pain is modified by both ascending systems, which are activated by sensory stimuli, and descending pathways, which can be activated by psychological factors. There is unanimous support in the literature for psychological approaches to pain when dealing with children (Palermo, Eccleston, Lowandowski, Williams, & Morley, 2010; McGrath, 1990). Strategies include educating a child about the pain when it is procedure-related; maximizing a sense of participation, choice, and control with the child; and using techniques such as breathing, relaxation, positive self-talk, distraction, and imagery. Although the mechanisms of therapeutic change are not entirely known, these techniques are quite effective. The “neuromatrix” and “gate” theories of pain suggest the ability of the CNS to down-regulate pain conduction via descending pathways (Melzack, 1999). Melzack’s neuromatrix paradigm describes a complex interplay of CNS and PNS components with learned, conditioned, and inherited pain reaction patterns. One likely mechanism by which biofeedback modulates pain perception is via inhibition of pain signal activity in the spinothalamic tract via enhanced PNS activity via the vagus nerve.

The anxiety that accompanies pain is often as important to address as the pain itself (Tsao et al., 2004). Biofeedback can be creatively incorporated into treatment strategies for children with needle phobia/procedural pain and anxiety, as the following case illustrates.

Ana, a 12-year-old, was referred for self-regulation training for control of severe needle phobia. She needed immunizations within a few weeks to go on a family trip overseas. The referring pediatrician had been unsuccessful in gaining Ana’s cooperation for these vaccinations. She screamed and cried uncontrollably, and her parents and the professional staff were at a loss as to how best to help her.

Ana only recalled one other experience a few years earlier, when she had venipuncture that required more than one attempt to get the sample. She recalled feeling “out of control” as people held her down. Since that time, she had had a strong fear of needles. She expressed her desire to help herself.

During the first session, Ana was introduced to basic EMG biofeedback and breathing therapy, emphasizing control and mastery themes. Ana enjoyed this and did well. During the next session, she was introduced to EDA as an easy way for her to track her own nervous system’s anxiety responses to needles. Over that and the two subsequent sessions, Ana focused on controlling her SNS response with graduated exposure to elements of the vaccination experience. She learned to control her breathing, heart rate, and SNS activity, as reflected in maintenance of low EDA readings. She particularly liked the biofeedback screen called Kaleidoscope, in which she erased a complex design of colorful lines and shapes as she decreased her EDA to the desired threshold level (below 5 micromhos).

A plan for a vaccination visit was then discussed and role-played using actual equipment, but stopping short of giving Ana the injection. Ana practiced by playing the nurse and giving a play vaccination, maintaining good control, and noticing how proud she felt. The following week in the biofeedback room, with her EDA sensor attached and running, Ana demonstrated her self-regulation skills by keeping her EDA at desired levels, staying in control, and receiving her vaccination with minimal distress. She was quite proud of her accomplishment.

Needle phobia is a common problem in a variety of settings for children of all ages (Tsao et al., 2004). Many children report afterward that the anticipatory anxiety and emotional distress are often worse than the actual “shot” itself. As Ana’s case illustrates, direct desensitization with the monitoring of a relevant physiological modality such as EDA (or breathing or heart rate) offers an excellent opportunity to extinguish fear and reinforce the desired behavioral change. The engaging nature of visual feedback and the ability to set concrete “goals” are helpful for many young children in reinforcing their sense of control. Finally, the use of these techniques can heighten curiosity and thereby enhance the focus on something other than the injection pain. Even if simply used for distraction, biofeedback-based approaches can provide great benefit for children during procedural pain. Also, by reinforcing an enhanced sense of control in young patients, biofeedback training serves as a great metaphor for mastering pain (e.g., “If you can control this physiological function, you can also control the pain”).

Studies suggest that up to 34% of the world’s children suffer from functional RAP (Faull &

Nicol, 1986). In a recent study, Humphreys and Gevirtz (2000) evaluated the efficacy of four treatment protocols, three of which included biofeedback, in the treatment of RAP. The study involved 64 children (mean age 9.75 years) with diagnosed RAP, who were randomly assigned to one of four groups:

1. Fiber-only comparison group.   
2. Fiber and biofeedback-assisted cultivation of lowered arousal.   
3. Fiber, biofeedback, and cognitive-behavioral intervention.   
4. Fiber, biofeedback, cognitive-behavioral intervention, and parent support.

The biofeedback component involved thermal biofeedback. All three treatment groups (groups 2–4) demonstrated significant within-session increases in peripheral temperature. Subjects reported significantly reduced abdominal pain. The authors concluded that increased dietary fiber coupled with biofeedback-assisted cultivation of lowered arousal was effective and efficient as a treatment for RAP.

Studies suggest that thermal feedback is a helpful component in pain control for individuals with arthritic joint pain related to bleeding disorders such as hemophilia (Varni, 1981; Varni & Gilbert, 1982). Temperature feedback may also play a supportive role for children with sickle-cell disease (Hall, Chiarucci, & Berman, 1992).

# Neuromuscular Rehabilitation

The benefits of biofeedback in rehabilitation have been discussed (see Fagerson and Krebs, Chapter 31, this volume); however, a few comments on pediatric applications are warranted. Child psychologists, physiatrists, and physical therapists are documenting the many benefits of EMG work in pediatric rehabilitation. Children with spina bifida, cerebral palsy, traumatic brain injury, burns, and complications related to CNS tumors all may benefit from biofeedback (primarily EMG) (Allen, Bernstein, & Chait, 1991; Brown, Nahai, Wolf, & Basmajian, 1978; Finley, Niman, Standley, & Wansley, 1977; Cataldo, Bird, & Cunningham, 1978; Toner, Cook, & Elder, 1998; James, 1992; Nash, Neilson, & O’Dwyer, 1989; Colborne, Wright, & Naumann, 1994; Murdoch, Pitt, Theodoros, & Ward, 1999). Therapeutic applications of biofeedback in this area include

•	 Proprioceptive awareness development   
•	 Muscle reeducation   
•	 Strength and endurance training Muscle tone down-training Gait analysis and retraining Bowel and bladder control training Correction of drooling Head control and postural training Enhancement of speech   
•	 Treatment of torticollis

Pediatric psychologist Jeff Bolek (1998), who practices at the Cleveland Clinic Children’s Hospital for Rehabilitation in Ohio, appropriately points out that with EMG biofeedback in this setting, the type of reinforcer used with each pediatric patient is vitally important. Asking patients what they prefer for a reward is a start. One adolescent with whom he worked was happy to have a particular song played. Some children with brain injuries prefer tactile stimulation, such as vibrating rubber tubes placed around the shoulders. The olfactory sense is not processed by the thalamus; therefore, scents may be a desirable reward. Battery-operated toys that are connected to the biofeedback equipment, and that “turn on” (via a relay box) when a desired threshold is attained, are also favorites in his clinical work. The design of reinforcement intervals and the scheduling of training sessions (blocked vs. intermittent) are crucial to success as well (Bolek, 1998).

Other biofeedback applications related to rehabilitation include SEMG for apraxia of speech (Morgan & Vogel, 2009) and stuttering (Block, Onslow, Roberts, & White, 2004).

# Repetitive Behavioral Patterns (Habit Disorders, Impulse Control Problems, and Tics)

Patients with tics, Tourette’s disorder, thumbsucking, nailbiting, bruxism, rumination, and picking behaviors may derive substantial benefits from behavioral therapies (Peterson, Campise, & Azrin, 1994) and self-regulation training, which includes biofeedback (Kohen, 2013; Culbert et al., 1994). Repetitive behaviors often share a characteristic of being somewhat automatic and (for many children) stress- or anxiety-related. Some problems, such as tic behaviors, are largely involuntary but can be voluntarily suppressed for varying lengths of time. Others, like thumbsucking, are really more under volitional control, and when brought to conscious awareness can be fully eliminated.

Table 35.1 identifies several of the common elements seen in repetitive behavior patterns and their associated treatment strategies. Biofeedback can help with each of these elements in a habitual or recurrent behavioral pattern.

Thumbsucking provides a good example. To bring the thumbsucking behavior into focus and awareness, we have patients identify their “highrisk” times of day, when they would tend to exhibit this behavior the most. We then have them focus their practice efforts during those times. We have them self-monitor and rate themselves on a visual analogue scale as to how much thumbsucking they are doing at a given time each day. With better awareness of their pattern, they can then begin to interrupt or change it, so that it is not so “automatic.” We can assign a competing behavior that is more adaptive (e.g., squeezing a “koosh” ball) as another way to interrupt the usual cycle, to be done each time they feel the urge to perform the target habit.

Biofeedback can be a helpful self-awareness teaching tool (Bakal, 2001). For example, in patients with thumbsucking, we have utilized forearm EMG biofeedback as way to increase selfawareness and, at the same time, interrupt the automatic nature of the behavior. EMG sensors are placed on the forearm of the same side of the body as the thumb that a child prefers to suck. An EMG threshhold value is then set, so that when the child’s arm is resting comfortably in his or her lap in a relaxed manner, the child is rewarded with engaging audio and visual feedback (spinning shape, more points, a green light). When the subject brings the hand up to his or her mouth to suck the thumb, the threshold event is triggered, so that the enjoyable feedback (audio and visual) he or she was receiving freezes (and in one software program it can actually be set to display a red light). With several repetitions, the child becomes much more aware of the arm and thumb coming up to the mouth, and the muscles that this movement involves. The child can then learn to practice being more aware of this by closing his or her eyes and visualizing a traffic light image. When the child is moving the arm and thumb up, he or she can see a red light, which is the cue to stop. When the child is keeping the thumb out of his or her mouth and resting the arm comfortably, he or she can visualize a green light, which is the reinforcing cue to continue with that behavior. The child can then incorporate this sequence into a mental imagery (self-hypnosis) exercise to be practiced several times a day in the appropriate setting. In addition, for children in whom anxiety and stress “drive” or maintain a habitual behavior, treatment to lower SNS arousal (any biofeedback modality) can also be useful. These biofeedbackbased approaches are blended with the appropriate operant elements in an overall plan.

TA BLE 35.1. R epetitive Behavior Patterns and T heir Associated Treatment Strategies   
![](images/d0ebe294c7407b9b1efe8e89de325c529eadede0f93b947401f8596829a6e999.jpg)

Culbert has found that relaxation therapy for children with mild to moderate tics and Tourette’s disorder is often very helpful in reducing tic frequency and severity, and in facilitating a sense of control for these children (Kohen & Botts, 1987). SEMG work in and around the area of motor involvement is useful, as is more general biofeedback for lowered arousal. It is well described for children with tics/Tourette’s disorder that tic behaviors (vocal or motor) are more likely to occur with increased frequency and severity in the context of emotional arousal, whether this is associated with stress, excitement, or rage (Michultka, Blanchard, & Rosenblum, 1989). Therefore, emotional self-regulation therapy via biofeedback is useful for enhancing emotional and somatic awareness, and in cultivating control of lowered states of arousal. O’Connor, Gareau, and Borgeat (1995) describe the successful use of EMG training in nine patients with tics to improve discrimination of levels of muscle contraction and to modify muscular control. Although these were adults (ages 23–49 years) with tics, the findings are applicable to pediatric patients as well.

A few studies have indicated a possible role for biofeedback in the treatment of pediatric rumination syndrome. “Rumination” is defined as the repeated regurgitation of food, with weight loss or failure to gain expected weight, developing after a period of normal functioning. The partially digested food is brought back into the mouth and then ejected from the mouth or reswallowed (American Psychiatric Association, 1994). In individuals with mental retardation, the prevalence of rumination is 6–10% (Fredericks, Carr, & Williams, 1998). Khan, Hyman, Cocjin, and DiLorenzo (2000) described a multimodal approach for both nutritional and behavioral rehabilitation of 12 pediatric patients with rumination disorder (age range 9–19). None of the children in this case series were developmentally delayed. A combination of cognitive-behavioral therapy with relaxation and biofeedback was used in seven of the 12 patients with good benefit. Unfortunately, the authors do not give any methodological detail in terms of the specific type of biofeedback that was provided.

EMG biofeedback with a masseter placement can be useful in treating bruxism in children and adolescents (Feehan & Marsh, 1989; Schneider & Peterson, 1982).

EMG biofeedback with a masseter placement can be useful in treating bruxism in children and adolescents (Feehan & Marsh, 1989; Schneider & Peterson, 1982). It has also been used as a treatment in pediatric habit cough (Labbé, 2006).

Children with impulse control deficits may struggle with emotional regulation and be quick to anger. Studies suggest that biofeedback, arousal management, and relaxation can be effective components of therapeutic programming for children, adolescents, and adults with anger management and emotional regulation problems (Snyder, Kymissis, & Kessler, 1999; Corder, Whiteside, & Haizlip, 1986; Kahn, Ducharme, Travers, & Gonzalez-Heydrich, 2009). Our clinical experience also indicates that biofeedback can be a useful adjunct in behavioral therapy approaches for children and adolescents with anger management and impulse control problems. Many times these maladaptive behaviors occur in response to predictable situational or interpersonal triggers and result in somewhat stereotypical (negative) emotional arousal patterns and associated behavior.

Biofeedback can help children to understand and get better at discerning states of emotional arousal early, then allow them the time and strategy to deescalate and make an appropriate behavioral choice. This fits well into the traditional five-step cognitive-behavioral problem-solving framework (Braswell & Kendall, 2001):

1. Stop! (Take a deep breath.)   
2. Identify the problem. (Ask yourself, “Why am I feeling this way?”)

3. Generate a few response options. (What can I do about it?) 4. Choose the best option and do it! 5. Evaluate what happened. (How did it go?)

# Mental Health Disorders (Anxiety and ADHD)

Anxiety, fear, and stress evoke the familiar “fight-or-flight” repertoire of psychophysiological responses in people at all developmental life stages. Modern research has established that anxiety disorders are among the most common of all childhood psychiatric disorders, yet scientific study of effective treatments remains quite limited (Bernstein & Borchardt, 1991). Anxiety and stress-related problems include panic attacks, somatoform disorders, separation anxiety, specific phobias, school avoidance, posttraumatic stress disorder, and generalized anxiety disorder. This group of relatively common childhood conditions represents a domain for which biofeedback, relaxation, and self-hypnosis are very promising (Gagnon, Hudnall, & Andrasik, 1992; Gil, Perry, & King, 1988; Hobbie, 1989; Kendall, Howard, & Epps, 1988; Young, Montano, & Goldberg, 1991).

A study by Kendall et al. (1988) demonstrated that a cognitive-behavioral treatment program including relaxation therapy was effective for children diagnosed with anxiety disorders. Although in this study the relaxation technique was not taught with biofeedback, it supports relaxation as a useful element. A systematic review of cognitive-behavioral approaches for the anxious child reveals that many of these successful approaches include a relaxation and/ or breath control component (Kendall et al., 1988). Other mind–body and complementary approaches are promising as well (Kemper et al., 2008).

In the authors’ experience, some individuals with panic attacks (including children) who have been treated very effectively with breath control therapies, including “capnography” (the measurement and feedback of end-tidal CO2 values). Physiologically, people with anxiety, particularly panic attacks, often hyperventilate (overbreathe), driving their CO values down; this in turns upsets the acid–base balance of the body. In response, the body decreases cerebral blood flow, which can then contribute to cognitive distortions that perpetuate the panic cycle and interfere with selfcontrol and recovery. When these patients learn to breathe properly, to a normal level of exhaled CO2 (38–42 torr), they restore proper physiological balance and are then able to think more coherently, which enhances the ability to utilize behavioral strategies in recovery.

Clinical experience suggests that EDA, HRV, peripheral temperature, and diaphragmatic breathing (pneumography or capnography) are all quite helpful for young patients with anxiety/stress symptoms (Wenck, Leu, & D’Amato, 1996; Nada, 2009). Using home and school training devices, such as temperature-sensitive dots or bands, also helps children self-monitor and self-cue in settings where they experience SNS overarousal. HRV has also been beneficial for test anxiety in schoolchildren and college students (www.heartmath.org; Henriques, Kefer, Abrahamson, & Jeanne-Horst, 2011).

Children and adolescents may experience stress in many forms: physical, medical, cognitive, emotional, psychosocial, and cultural. Biofeedback and relaxation therapies can help pediatric patients cope with a variety of stressful conditions and situations. Our experience suggests that stress management approaches incorporating biofeedback as a component of group therapy with children and adolescents are also very helpful and engaging. Techniques such as progressive muscle relaxation, self-hypnosis, and autogenics can also be very useful when integrated with biofeedback strategies in this population (Smith & Womack, 1987).

In addition to EEG biofeedback, which is covered by Neumann, Strehl, Birbaumer, and Kotchoubey (Chapter 6, this volume), peripheral biofeedback has also been used as an adjunct approach for children with ADHD (Lee, 1991). HRV biofeedback holds promise as well as an option to improve cognitive and behavioral performance (Lloyd, Brett, & Wesnes, 2010).

# Sleep Disorders

Disorders of initiating and maintaining sleep are fairly common in childhood and adolescence; in fact, surveys indicate that 25% of children and adolescents experience some sort of sleep disturbance (Mindell, 1993). Many times, childhood sleep disturbance includes a behavioral component or is secondarily related to a psychological disturbance (e.g., anxiety or depression). In addition, the sleep system is exquisitely sensitive to stress. Stress, anxiety, and excessive psychophysiological arousal can all inhibit normal sleep patterns. For these reasons, biofeedback and related strategies are often very useful for pediatric sleep disorders (Taylor & Roane, 2010).

A successful strategy for children or adolescents with sleep-onset difficulty, as described by Barowsky, Moskowitz, and Zweig (1990), involves teaching these patients to reduce psychophysiological arousal through bifrontal EMG and thermal biofeedback. As they become more relaxed and less aroused from a CNS standpoint, they transition into sleep more easily, thus decreasing sleep latency. Diaphragmatic breathing, EDA, and bitrapezius EMG work are also useful. A study that included adolescent patients with insomnia demonstrated that those receiving both frontal EMG biofeedback and progressive muscle relaxation showed improvements in sleep-onset time relative to controls (Freedman & Papsdorf, 1976).

For children with insomnia, night terrors, and nightmares, the coupling of relaxation therapy with positive, comforting mental imagery at bedtime can provide an effective approach (Kohen, Mahowald, & Rosen, 1992; King, Cranstoun, & Josephs, 1989).

# ANS Dysfunction

Clinical experience, as well as the knowledge of the etiological role of ANS imbalance, suggests that children with disorders of ANS dysfunction, including Raynaud’s disease/phenomenon, reflex sympathetic dystrophy (now called “complex regional pain syndrome”), hyperhidrosis, essential hypertension, and irritable bowel syndrome, may benefit from biofeedback targeted at SNS overactivity, albeit the pediatric-specific literature is limited (Barowsky, Zweig, & Moskowitz, 1987; Ewart et al., 1987; Freedman, Ianni, & Wenig, 1983; Lightman, Pochaczevsky, Aprin, & Ilowite, 1987; Olsson & Berde, 1993). In our clinical experience, EDA, peripheral temperature, HRV training, and breath control work have been particularly useful in these populations. In patients with Raynaud’s disease/phenomenon, learning to warm the fingers and toes in the face of a cold stressor in the office provides a useful generalization strategy.

# Chronic Illness

Behavioral treatment procedures, including biofeedback, are finding wide application as adjunctive treatments in the management of children’s chronic, debilitating medical conditions (Masek, Fentress, & Spirito, 1984; McQuaid & Nassau, 1999). Pediatric subspecialty care has evolved over the last 40 years to the point that many children with significant childhood chronic illnesses can be medically stabilized and have significantly improved longevity. However, being stable from a medical standpoint does not ensure that these children are functioning without impairment. Chronic illness can have many biobehavioral, academic, and social sequelae that we are beginning to see more clearly. Biofeedback and self-regulation are ideal strategies to help pediatric populations with chronic illness to optimize function and performance across all domains of daily activity. Psychological and behavioral treatments in the context of chronic illness have particular relevance in four domains: pain management, treatment compliance (adherence), health locus of control issues, and stress management.

# Asthma

In 1982, Creer, Renne, and Chai suggested that the investigation and treatment of asthma requires a collaboration between medicine and psychology, because the physical and psychological factors are closely related. It is generally well accepted that behavioral interventions as adjunctive treatments for asthma are essential for many patients. In some children, asthma episodes may be emotionally triggered. Effective biofeedback-based interventions have included bifrontal EMG, airway feedback, pneumography, capnography, and HRV (Kotses & Glaus, 1981; Kotses et al., 1991; Lehrer, Sargunaraj, & Hochron, 1992; Lehrer et al., 2006; Ritz, Meuret, Wilhelm, & Roth, 2009). In a 2000 study, Lehrer, Smetankin, and Potapova reviewed 20 cases of children with asthma who did exceptionally well utilizing respiratory sinus arrhythmia (RSA) biofeedback and the Smetankin method of breathing.

# Cystic Fibrosis

Spirito, Russo, and Masek (1984) described the need for behavioral interventions and stress management in children with cystic fibrosis, and the successful application of biofeedback, relaxation, and behavioral counseling in a group of patients ages 13–23 years. Delk, Gevirtz, Hicks, Carden, and Rucker (1994) examined the effects of respiratory muscle feedback on lung function in a group of subjects ages 10–41 years. A significant treatment effect was observed for the biofeedback group relative to controls, with improvement in several pulmonary function measurements. Bingham, Bates, Thompson-Figueroa, and Lahiri (2010) described a breath biofeedback computer game using a digital spirometer for children with cystic fibrosis.

# Insulin‑Dependent Diabetes Mellitus

It has been suggested that patients with insulindependent diabetes mellitus (IDDM) may be particularly susceptible to stress-induced hyperglycemia and to stress effects on metabolic control. Therefore, stress management training has been proposed as one option for enhancing glycemic control in these individuals. Rose, Firestone, Heick, and Faught (1983) used a multiple-baseline design in a study of five adolescents with poorly controlled IDDM. Improved diabetic control occurred when behavioral treatment was introduced. A study of biofeedback-assisted stress management included a 17-year-old individual with IDDM who experienced benefit from a protocol that included frontal EMG (Rosenbaum, 1983). CAM use in children with diabetes has also been reviewed (Shapiro & Rapaport, 2009). Real-time biofeedback of glucose levels is also being evaluated (Danne, Lange, & Kordonouri, 2008).

# Alcoholism

Brief biofeedback-assisted autogenic therapy (n = 12) led to enhanced sense of internal locus of control after the training procedure, relative to a control condition (n = 13), for adolescents with alcoholism. The treatment protocol included four weekly sessions of finger temperature monitoring during a taped autogenic relaxation exercise (Sharp, Huford, Allison, Sparks, & Cameron, 1997).

# Hematology/Oncology

Studies in a variety of childhood cancers and hematological disorders (e.g., sickle-cell disease) support the role of self-regulation therapy for children. Many children with cancer are using CAM. Specifically, a number of studies support the use of relaxation, mental imagery and self-hypnosis, distraction, relaxation, and cognitive-behavioral strategies for the procedural pain and anticipatory nausea issues that are commonly encountered (Kuttner, Bowman, & Teasdale, 1988; Labaw, Holton, Tewell, & Eccles, 1975; LeBaron & Hilgard, 1984; Kaufman, Tarnowski, & Olson, 1989; Zeltzer, Dolgin, LeBaron, & LeBaron, 1991). Video games are also helpful as a cognitive distraction in reducing chemotherapy-related distress (Kolko & Rickard-Figueroa, 1985; Redd & Andrykowski, 1982). In our experience, biofeedback protocols for children can be set up like a video game experience. Therefore, biofeedback can be a very helpful adjunctive technique for this population. A study in adults with chemotherapy-related side effects of cancer treatment indicated that temperature and EMG biofeedback reduced some physiological indicators of arousal after treatment, but that relaxation alone reduced nausea and anxiety during chemotherapy (Burish & Jenkins, 1992).

Taken together, this literature supports the benefits of lowered arousal, directed therapeutic imagery, breath control, and distraction. There was no study specific to the use of biofeedback for this purpose in children, but the fact that relaxation is generally supported as helpful suggests that biofeedback can be appropriately employed for this purpose. Our clinical experience with many oncology patients supports this notion as well. Training in controlled diaphragmatic breathing as measured by pneumography, coupled with positive mental imagery, has been very useful for these patients. Research into immune system function suggests that children can voluntarily learn to modulate certain aspects of immune function with self-directed relaxation/mental imagery techniques (Olness et al., 1989; Hewson-Bower & Drummond, 1996; Noll, 1988).

# Gastrointestinal Conditions

In addition to the application of biofeedback described earlier for fecal incontinence, recurrent abdominal pain, and irritable bowel syndrome, other studies indicate that multimodal peripheral biofeedback as an adjunct treatment for pediatric dyspepsia can be helpful (Schurman, Wu, Grayson, & Friesen, 2010). HRV biofeedback as a way to regulate ANS function cyclic vomiting syndrome may also be beneficial (Slutsker, Konichezky, & Gothelf, 2010).

# Learning Disorders and School Performance

Although it is beyond the scope of this chapter to cover this topic in detail, it is important to point out that biofeedback with children is moving beyond clinical health care settings and into the schools. Over 30 years ago, Joe Kamiya (1979) wrote: “The potential role of biofeedback in education is unique. Whereas other modes of education are basically addressed to the individual’s development of interaction skills and coping abilities with the external environment, biofeedback is a way to help the individual cope with the internal environment” (p. 290).

Interest in biofeedback as a means to enhance learning and classroom performance seemed to peak in the 1970s, with a number of innovative programs in Colorado, Minnesota, South Dakota, and Indiana. More recently, Daniel Hamiel (personal communication, March 2000) reported great success with a biofeedback-based stress management program for all ages in schools in Israel. According to Hamiel, teachers in the United States are also finding emotional management training that includes HRV biofeedback very helpful in settings for emotionally/behaviorally challenged youth. Neurofeedback, as described by Thatcher (Chapter 7, this volume), is helping children with a variety of learning and attentional problems. Peripheral biofeedback modalities such as EDA and EMG continue to have utility in school settings for children with ADHD, learning difficulties (e.g., poor handwriting), and performance anxiety.

Biofeedback has great promise as an intervention strategy for children in a variety of educational settings, and is an ideal tool for enhancing physical, attentional, emotional, and social regulation skills. Studies indicate that children with learning disorders (LDs) and those with a variety of cognitive deficits can be successfully coached in relaxation, biofeedback, and self-regulation. For example, a group of children with LDs was able to demonstrate twice the peripheral temperature control ability of children without LDs after five biofeedback sessions that included a consistent reinforcer (Hunter, Russell, Russell, & Zimmerman, 1976). In one early study, Guralnick and Mott (1976) described breath control therapy with an 11-year-old male diagnosed with perceptual– motor disabilities in an LD program. This boy demonstrated a chaotic, shallow breathing pattern, marked by his inability to control the volume of inhalation–exhalation. This led to problems with speech production patterns. Eight 30-minute sessions of strain-gauge-based biofeedback resulted in markedly improved voluntary breath control.

Mangina and Beuzeron-Mangina (1992) described a psychophysiological assessment method that differentiates children with LDs from children without LDs, based on bilateral EDA differences. They went on to define the relationship between “optimal physiological activation” states during certain types of cognitive stimulation and Mangina’s method of optimizing these states.

Several authors have described the use of EMG biofeedback in the treatment of writing problems with school-aged children. Studies have utilized bifrontal EMG (Hughes, Jackson, Dubois, & Erwin, 1979) and handwriting (Hoy, Egan, & Feder, 2011) and forearm EMG (Carter & Russell, 1980; Cobb & Evans, 1981). In these studies, the main goal was to reduce muscle tension to a level that allowed for easier, more fluid writing. This may suggest that, in general, as children become more relaxed, they can experience performance improvements—perhaps in part because of reduced muscular and ANS “interference” (Andrasik & Attansio, 1985).

# Treatment Pl anning

# General Considerations

In work with children and adolescents, each encounter must be crafted in a way that attends to the specific developmental stage, sensory preferences, attention span, personal interests, and learning style of the patient at hand. This requires not only appropriate knowledge of developmental stages and capabilities but also flexibility on the part of the therapist. The length, frequency, and content of biofeedback training sessions may need to be adjusted to fit the unique needs of each patient, with younger children often needing shorter, more frequent sessions. Single sessions can be broken up by changing feedback modalities, games, or audio settings. Young children can easily utilize mental imagery to enhance the relaxation process as they imagine “being in a favorite place.” They may also tolerate and in fact benefit from more directive approaches. For older children, it is often useful to appeal to their desire for mastery and give more detailed explanations of therapy goals.

Our clinical experience suggests that, as a general guideline, most pediatric patients can achieve positive outcomes with six to 10 sessions lasting 30–45 minutes each, scheduled 1–2 weeks apart. All children should be asked to participate in daily self-monitoring as part of their treatment plan. Rewards are appropriate early in the treatment process as way to reinforce cooperation and practice. Daily practice at home and/or school is essential for success and can be facilitated with instructional handouts and practice CD recordings.

The “discern–control–generalize” model offers a useful paradigm within which to consider therapy and session planning with pediatric clients. In the “discern” phase, clients are taught about mind–body links and assisted in discriminating differences in states of relaxation and tension. In the “control” phase, children are coached to master specific skills (e.g., diaphragmatic breathing, decreased trapezius muscle tone) and to achieve consistent trends or goals for a specific physiological function over a defined period of time. In the final and most important stage, the “generalize” phase, patients learn to apply their self-regulation skills in the real-life situation at the desired time. Helping children to transfer the skills learned in the office includes identification of environmental cues and situational triggers that remind them when to use these abilities. Parents and teachers may get involved at this step and assist in cueing the children when desirable. Mental imagery is commonly utilized as a practice strategy at this stage to offer the chance for “imaginal” or in vitro rehearsal of a real-life situation in which the individual wishes to apply a new skill.

# Therapeutic Communication

Careful attention to the content and structure of language is a key ingredient in therapeutic communication with children and adolescents (Reaney & Kohen, 1998). Daniel Kohen, Karen Olness, and others in the field of pediatric hypnosis and hypnotherapy have contributed to a large volume of literature on this essential aspect of all pediatric clinical encounters (Kohen & Olness, 2012). They point out that children frequently and spontaneously move in and out of altered states of awareness, within which they experience heightened suggestibility. Careful attention to the way in which language is used with children in these “alternative states” is essential. Markers of these “hypnotic” states include focused attention, absorption, and curiosity. Many children who are receiving biofeedback therapy clearly (and actually quite commonly) move into an altered state as they become relaxed. In this state, attention is narrowly focused, they are more aware of internal events and sensations, and they are ready to facilitate change more readily (as they experience physiological and perceptual shifts).

While in such a state of consciousness, children are more receptive to therapeutic suggestion. Therefore, language used during these encounters must be carefully tailored to do the following:

•	 Convey positive expectancy.   
•	 Be permissive (as opposed to directive).   
•	 Maximize the fit with the child’s interest and personality.   
•	 Respect and reflect developmental ability.

•	 Evoke a sense of wonder/curiosity. •	 Include appropriate suggestions to enhance functioning and/or control of symptoms.

With biofeedback, game formats (some of which are described below) lend themselves nicely to therapeutic metaphors involving notions of selfcontrol relevant to a given child and his or her presenting problem. In working with pediatric populations, it is not enough for clinicians to be technically competent; they must also be able to approach each child in a developmentally sensitive manner, to establish rapport, and to utilize words and language strategically.

# Technical C onsiderations

# Modalities

Table 35.2 lists the basic biofeedback modalities, with explanations in children’s language (in italics).

# Hardware and Software

Children in today’s society are accustomed to viewing high-quality video graphics in TV, video games, and movies. Biofeedback training that uses game formats is appealing for many youngsters and serves as an engaging treatment “hook.” Video games are designed to be “intrinsically motivating,” and we can learn from this technology and design the same features into successful pediatric biofeedback treatment strategies (Loftus & Loftus, 1983). The three essential elements identified are those of fantasy, curiosity, and challenge. Selecting and varying audio and video screens with pediatric patients can be essential in holding attention and “connecting” with young patients. Some examples of biofeedback systems/devices that we have found useful in pediatric practice are described below. Reference is made to certain sample feedback displays that are highly detailed and in color. (Interested readers can find reproductions of these displays at www.marksschwartzphd.com).

1. The Nexus/Biotrace System (www.stensbiofeedback.com) offers an effective platform for peripheral biofeedback with children and teens, and many visual and auditory biofeedback options, including the ability to trigger external devices for rewards such as a DVD player or games.

2. Wild Divine has two systems that children and

• Hand/finger warmth (thermography [peripheral temperature]) The more relaxed you are, the warmer your hands and fingers get.   
• Muscle tension (electromyography [EMG]) The more tense/stressed you are, the tighter your muscles can get.   
• Breathing (pneumography [PNG]) When you breathe in you can let your belly get bigger; when you breathe out it should flatten out again.   
• Heart rate (photoplethysmography [PPG]; electrocardiography [EKG]) When you are excited, scared or angry your heart beats fast. When you are calm, your heart beats slower.   
• Heartbeat pattern (heart rate variability [HRV]) When you are excited, scared, or angry, your heartbeat pattern is more random. When you are in a better balance in mind and body, your heartbeat pattern gets more rhythmical and looks like “rolling waves.”   
• Sweat gland activity (electrodermography [EDA]) Like what they use for a “lie detector” test—the more excited or stressed you feel, the more sweat glands open up on the surface of your palms.   
• Brain waves (electroencephalography [EEG]) Your brain creates electrical impulses that can be measured—some are faster (beta waves), others are slower (alpha and theta waves), and some are seen during sleep (delta waves).

teens can enjoy. One is titled “Journey to Wild Divine” and the other is “Relaxing Rhythms.” Both systems offer fun, graphic, game-style interfaces in which physiological changes trigger animations or reward the client with new adventures.

3. Heartmath, LLC (www.heartmath.com) produces an HRV-based hardware–software package called the “emWave PC” for all ages. It includes interactive games that require improved ANS balance to trigger a reward animation and an “emotional visualizer” function that combines calming graphics and music. It also features a new driving game for teens called “Dual Drive.”

4. Thought Technology’s Biograph software, together with Procomp Infinity Hardware (both available from www.thoughttechnology. com), offers several child-friendly game animations/graphics, including a morphing “smiley face” and a moving sunset; it also has a relay feature that can trigger an external device as a reward. Moving animation screens on the Biograph X disc with appealing musical instrument, digital interface audio song options include a cartoon dog, a spaceship landing sequence, a bowling game, and a flower that opens and closes at a preset threshold.

# Portable Devices

Our experience supports the potential benefits of utilizing portable trainers and/or home biofeedback systems as a way of promoting more successful generalization/transfer of skills as a practice aid. Seeing the same or similar biofeedback information at home or school reinforces the experience the child/teen had in the office and allows him or her to check to see that they are “doing it right” and achieving the desired state of ANS balance.

Useful “gadgets” for home practice in our experience have included:

Peripheral Temperature: digital temperature stress thermometers (www.cliving.org), biodots (www. biodots.net), dermatherm bands (www.sharn. com)   
EMG: U-control trainer for tight trap/neck muscles, also for relaxation breathing training and for pelvic floor SEMG training, as well as anorectal biofeedback training (www.thoughttechnology.com)   
HRV Training: emWave 2 (www.heartmath.com)   
Dry Alarm: used for nocturnal enuresis training (www.bedwettingstore.com)   
Home Biofeedback Games: Wild Divine System, Healing Rhythms (www.wilddivine.com)   
AudioVisual Entrainment: Proteus (www.mindmodulations.com) and DAVID units (www. mindalive.com)   
Breath Control Training: Resperate Unit (www. resperate.com)

# Prioritizing, Sequencing, and I ntegrating  Strategies

Biofeedback training can proceed concurrently with conventional treatments, such as prescription medication, subspecialty cares, psychotherapy, and physical therapies. It can also be integrated/ coordinated with complementary options such as massage, healing touch, manipulative techniques (osteopathic and chiropractic), acupuncture, and so forth. Certain adjunctive techniques such as aromatherapy and therapeutic music can be used as part of the actual biofeedback training session to enhance relaxation. Other mind–body techniques such as mental imagery and progressive muscle relaxation are commonly used in biofeedback training as well. The holistic clinician also addresses lifestyle issues (e.g., sleep, time management, diet, exercise) as important elements in the healing matrix.

It is important not to “overwhelm” the patient with an approach that involves too many therapies concurrently. Careful discussion with patients and their families with attention to personal preferences, financial, and convenience issues must also be weighed as treatments are selected, agreed upon, then sequenced over a reasonable time frame.

Communication with all of the relevant health care specialists involved is, of course, always suggested to coordinate care, and is of particular importance if biofeedback training may lead to the possible reduction or discontinuation of medication. Effective communication with children, teens, and their parents is also critical to establish motivation for change, to identify factors that may mediate against successful change, and to frame this process with a sense of positive expectancy and active participation on the part of each patient.

# Th e F uture

As Kohen (2010) pointed out, “A relationship between the mind and body has been debated, acknowledged, described and refuted for millennia” (p. 267). Biofeedback makes this mind–body connection real and visible for kids of all ages. It has become evident that pediatric health care professionals and educational professionals urgently need training in a new array of mind–body skills that can be helpful in confronting the new psychophysiological and psychosocial morbidities challenging today’s youth and families. We know that the effects of stress and psychosocial dysfunction may well continue to play out in adulthood. Studies now indicate that over half of all visits to primary care physicians are related to psychosocial problems that present as common physical complaints. How better to reduce or prevent the occurrence of stress-mediated illness in adults than to teach self-regulation to children and teens! Biofeedback and related self-regulatory skills should become core competencies in pediatric health care training as a way of addressing these problems. Wickramasekera, Davies, and Davies (1996)

stated, “Clinical, educational and research projects are needed in non-psychiatric primary care settings” (p. 230) and they went on to propose that psychophysiology laboratories be established in primary care residency programs.

The psychophysiological disorders that represent a growing component of the “new morbidity” in pediatrics are not going to go away. Because of this, it is fair to say that the future of biofeedback and applied psychophysiology in pediatric medical, educational, psychological, and communitybased settings is limitless. It is exciting to imagine the possibilities.

What if every child and adolescent received “stress inoculation” coaching as part of standard wellchild care or as part of their health education curriculum at all levels of schooling?   
What if every child diagnosed with asthma, diabetes, cancer, or other chronic or life-threatening illness received intensive training in self-regulation skills via biofeedback-based protocols, at the time of diagnosis?   
What if every pediatric inpatient learned about nonpharmacological, self-regulatory strategies, including biofeedback, to control procedural and postoperative pain and anxiety?   
What if every child with chronic pain (headaches, abdominal pain, myofascial pain, complex regional pain syndrome) learned mind–body strategies as standard, necessary components of treatment?   
What if every school had biofeedback equipment readily available, so that kids with anger management, attentional, or performance anxiety issues could learn about ways to self- regulate these functions through biofeedback training right in their classroom environment?   
What if all kids had access to optimal performance “coaching” for musical, athletic, public speak

ing, and other performance activities?

Pediatrics as a field has begun to move in a positive, more holistic direction with the publication of Bright Futures: Guidelines for Health Supervision of Infants, Children, and Adolescents (www.brightfutures.org). Bright Futures guidelines are intended for health professionals and educators, provide a set of recommendations and materials reflecting the consensus opinion of more than 100 multidisciplinary experts, and emphasize a biopsychosocial approach to well-child care and anticipatory guidance. The ubiquitous effects of stress on youth in modern society are also acknowledged. Goals espoused in these guidelines include a mandate to transform the process of health supervision to include giving each young person ways to cope with stressful life experiences, have high self-esteem, acquire a sense of self-efficacy, and the belief that he or she can succeed in life. The benefits of collaboration between pediatric primary health care providers and mental health professionals in helping children with stress management and related adaptive and coping strategies are also reviewed. Therapeutic approaches contained within the fields of applied psychophysiology and biofeedback can play a key role in these efforts as ideal, childfriendly strategies.

Research on the use of biofeedback and selfregulation skills with young people has grown substantially in the past 30 years, and study results are exceedingly positive in almost all areas examined. Furthermore, children and adolescents find biofeedback very desirable, engaging, and nonthreatening as a treatment strategy. This makes it an ideal therapeutic vehicle for addressing the biobehavioral, psychosocial, and academic challenges confronted by today’s youth.
