# Work‑Related Musculoskeletal Disorders

Susan Middaugh

# Introduction and Ep idemiol ogy

According to the World Health Organization (1985), a disorder is work-related, by definition, when work procedures, work equipment, or the work environment contribute significantly to the cause of the disorder. Work-related musculoskeletal disorders (WRMDs) are a major source of lost productivity, higher employee health care costs, and disability. Accordingly, these disorders have been a major focus of research at the National Institute for Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH; 1996), and extensive reviews of this literature are available from NIOSH (Bernard, 1997) and the National Research Council (NRC; 1998).

There is current recognition that WRMDs are not limited to acute injuries that occur during performance of heavy-duty or hazardous jobs. WRMDs are a substantial and persistent problem in light-duty jobs such as computer operation and light manufacturing, in which muscle load is low but static postures and repetitive motions are common. Over 50% of all occupational illness reported in the United States fell into this category in1992, and the incidence had tripled over the preceding 7 years (Rempel, Harrison, & Barnhart, 1992). A major source of this increase has been cervical and upper extremity (shoulder, arm, and hand) pain in office workers whose jobs now require extensive use of computers and video display terminals (VDTs) for word processing, data entry, and other computer-automated tasks (Hagberg & Wegman, 1987; Kuorinka & Forcier, 1995). Studies indicate high prevalence rates of 41% for occurrences in the previous year for pain in the cervical/upper extremity areas in newspaper employees using VDTs (Bernard, Sauter, Fine, Petersen, & Hales, 1994); 44% for current pain in the cervical/shoulder area in office workers (Bergqvist, 1995); and 27% for “almost constant” cervical discomfort in data entry workers (Sauter & Schleifer, 1991). In these workers, the source of pain and dysfunction is attributed to accumulating tissue damage that develops through long-term, daily overload of muscles and other soft tissues (e.g., tendons and nerve sheaths) due to factors such as work posture, speed, repetitiveness, and force.

Repeated surveys in the past decade indicate that reports of musculoskeletal pain are no longer rapidly increasing, but prevalence remains high (Leijon, Wahlstrom, & Mulder, 2009). WRMDs continue to comprise the majority of occupational illnesses (U.S. Department of Labor, 2002), and NIOSH surveys indicate that the percentage of administrative support workers who reported “pain in the arms” was 32% in 2002 and 30% in 2006 (Waters, Dick, & Krieg, 2011). Clearly, WRMDS are a continuing and substantial problem.

# Physiol ogical Th eory and R esearch

How does a light-duty, low-force, high-repetition task lead to musculoskeletal pain and impairment? Static work postures and repetitive movements have been identified as risk factors in epidemiological studies (Bernard, 1997; NRC, 1998), but how do these work activities produce localized muscle pain such as upper trapezius myalgia? Two current theories emphasize different, but not mutually exclusive, physiological mechanisms.

# Localized Muscle Fatigue

A muscle contraction increases intramuscular pressure, and this can mechanically impede circulation in a muscle or microcirculation in a fraction of a muscle. Muscle contractions that are sustained too long, or repeated too rapidly for circulation to be reestablished between repetitions, can interfere with muscle metabolism and promote localized fatigue with associated pain (Chaffin, 1973). This theory suggests that frequent, brief periods of muscle rest are important to prevent fatigue and eventual pain, and many studies have supported this conclusion. For example, as little as 2 minutes of continuous muscle contraction at relatively lowforce levels (5–10% of maximum voluntary contraction [MVC]) can produce physiological signs of muscle fatigue (Hagberg, 1981a). Muscle rests as brief as 2 seconds, if frequent, can offset the effects of fatigue (Hagberg, 1981b).

# Cinderella Hypothesis

With a low-level, static (continuous and invariant) muscle contraction, a few individual motor units that are recruited first (low threshold, Type I) can stay active for prolonged periods and cease firing only when the muscle relaxes completely. These “Cinderella” fibers—those performing a disproportionate share of the work—are selectively damaged (Hagg, 1991; Waersted, 2000). This theory suggests that variation in the amplitude (force) of muscle contraction is important to ensure recruitment of multiple motor units that will share and rotate the workload and prevent excessive use of a small fraction of the available muscle fibers. This theory further suggests that periods of complete muscle relaxation are needed to ensure that these workhorse motor units are turned off during rests.

The NRC (1998, p. 8), after extensive review of the physiological research on soft tissue responses to physical stressors, concluded that “local muscle fatigue occurs at low contraction levels when maintained for long periods, and inflammatory muscle responses can occur in humans subjected to repetitive or prolonged loading.” The NRC further noted that these effects also involve the neuromuscular control system; that is, fatigue and pain in one muscle or muscle group can alter the complex neuromuscular interaction that is required for coordinated and efficient performance of work tasks. This degradation of coordination and efficiency, in turn, can further stress the fatigued and painful muscles and also impact secondary muscles that are now required to assume more of the workload. This provides a ready mechanism for perpetuation and spread of musculoskeletal pain symptoms.

# Th e Work–Rest C ycl e

The theory and research cited earlier highlight the importance of the work–rest cycle. Normally, a working muscle quickly relaxes to a quiescent baseline during brief rest opportunities. Even a seemingly continuous activity, such as data entry or assembly work, consists of rapid alternations between muscle contraction (on) and relaxation (off) as the workload rotates from one muscle to another or the worker pauses to check a data entry. This pattern of frequent alternations between muscle contraction (work) and muscle relaxation (rest) during performance of work tasks is termed the work–rest cycle. A muscle that does not relax during these frequent, brief opportunities to rest will essentially contract continuously. This leads to rapid muscle fatigue and eventual pain. Numerous applied research studies from work-simulation laboratories and worksites indicate that the work–rest cycle is highly relevant to development of work-related musculoskeletal pain. An introduction to this extensive literature can be found elsewhere (Sauter, Dainoff, & Smith, 1990; Moon & Sauter, 1996; Kumar & Mital,1996). A brief overview of key findings that are relevant to this chapter is provided here.

Jonsson (1982) recorded EMG during work samples to measure the extent of muscle contraction (electromyographical [EMG] amplitude) that is present for 10% (peak), 50% (median), and 90% (static) of the time. This technique has proved useful for comparing muscle workloads for different jobs. Pain complaints are more frequent in jobs with higher static EMG. Most relevant, there seems to be no safe static level. Even a very lowamplitude muscle contraction (1% of MVC or

6–10 microvolts [mV] root mean square [RMS]) if present 90% of the time or more, increases risk of pain; that is, a muscle needs to be “off” (completely relaxed) more than 10% of the time while performing work tasks.

Other investigators have focused on methods that compare individual workers with respect to EMG amplitude (work), as well as the frequency and duration of spontaneous muscle pauses (rest) during job activities (Matthiassen & Winkel, 1991). These pauses, termed “gaps” or “rests,” are brief periods (one-fourth of a second to a few seconds) of complete muscle relaxation, defined as EMG below 0.5% MVC (3–5 mv RMS). This provides a more thorough categorization of both the “on” and “off” periods in working muscle. Studies have compared light assembly (Veiersted, Westgaard, & Anderson, 1993) and office (Hagg & Astrom, 1997) workers with upper trapezius pain to their pain-free coworkers. On average, workers without pain had a greater frequency and duration of gaps–rests while performing work tasks. Absence of rests did not invariably lead to pain in every individual; however, 20% total rest time (sum of all gaps–rests during the work period) was protective; no worker with 20% total rest time had pain. Workers without pain also spent more total time (summed over the work sample) at peak EMG levels. This indicates that variability in the amplitude of muscle contractions is also protective; however, variability alone does not compensate for lack of sufficient rest.

Work–rest cycle problems are also evident in studies of individuals seeking treatment for musculoskeletal pain. Investigators have reported slow and incomplete relaxation of the upper trapezius, as compared to pain-free controls, for cervical and upper extremity pain in musicians (Moulton & Spense, 1992); for work-related upper trapezius myalgia, and fibromyalgia (Elert, RantapaaDahlqvist, Henriksson-Larsen, Lorentzon, & Gerdie, 1992); for cervical pain following whiplash (Nederhand, IJzerman, Hermens, Baten, & Zilvold, 2000); and for cervical pain and headache (Middaugh, Kee, & Nicholson,1994; Middaugh, Kee, Nicholson, & Allenback,1995). Middaugh and colleagues (1994, 1995) found that over 85% of patients with cervical pain and headache showed delayed recovery of relaxed baseline (>15 seconds, Figure 36.1, top) for the upper trapezius muscle following brief contractions (shoulder shrug) compared with 19% of a non-pain group. Muscles normally relax in 2 seconds or less.

![](images/27d2eb404c04c3c92ff2c59b3b1656eb9f248839ee6eea600c998349d8c79b9a.jpg)  
FI GURE 36.1. Delayed muscle relaxation following contraction (top) with time line in seconds (bottom). Raw EMG (25–1000 Hz) recorded over the upper trapezius muscle. First marker indicates instruction to shrug the shoulder up; second marker indicates instruction to relax the shoulder. Muscle has not relaxed to baseline at 15 seconds.

This body of applied research provides substantial evidence that both the extent and the timing of “on” and “off” segments of a muscle’s work–rest cycle are critical to development, and prevention, of muscle fatigue and pain. Accordingly, procedures that are designed to improve the work–rest cycle, such as EMG biofeedback, are valuable components of intervention programs for WRMD.

# Interventions for Prevention and Remediation of WRMD

# Ergonomics

The large majority of interventions to date for WRMD have focused on ergonomics. The goal of ergonomic interventions is to reduce to a minimum the biomechanical and postural workload on muscles, joints, connective tissue, and other structures (e.g., nerves, eyes) by redesigning workstations, tools, and tasks. Well-designed studies have convincingly demonstrated that improvements in ergonomics can reduce WRMD by as much as 20–30%, without a decrement in productivity in telecommunications workers (Aaras, 1994) and office workers (Mekhora, Liston, Nanthavanij, & Cole, 2000).

It is possible, however, to work incorrectly within an ergonomically correct workstation. Even minor deviations in workstation arrangement can induce substantial muscle overuse. Figure 36.2 illustrates the impact on the upper trapezius muscle when VDT workers simply sit too far back from their keyboard and have to reach slightly forward for the keyboard while word processing. Middaugh et al. (1994) recorded EMG within-subjects (20 painfree students) under two conditions (order of presentation randomized, with a 5-minute rest inbetween). In the ergonomic condition, participants performed a continuous word-processing task for 15 minutes while working in an ergonomically correct position: good back support, elbow at their side, forearms parallel to the ground, and fingers placed on the keyboard without reaching forward. This task is accomplished with a small increase in muscle activity from resting baseline (hands in lap). In the altered condition, the same task is carried out while reaching forward a few inches to make contact with the keyboard (30 degrees of shoulder flexion). Word processing in the altered position required greater muscle activity than the ergonomic position (minute 1, p < .01) and also led to signs of rapid fatigue, as indicated by the increase in EMG from minute 1 to minute 15 (p > .01; Enoka & Stuart, 1992). There was no indication of muscle fatigue while word processing in the ergonomic position.

![](images/7ccf1fb9021be8c214d0808485a448e30b593888c228640dd51ff4050df8e4ba.jpg)  
FI GURE 36.2. Upper trapezius EMG (mean mV) for a single group of 20 normal subjects during 15 minutes of continuous keyboard operation in two different work positions: an Ergonomic position and an Altered position with 30° of shoulder flexion. The rapid increase from minute 1 to minute 15 for the Altered position indicates muscle fatigue.

# Work Organization

Other interventions have focused on work organization variables such as rest breaks and task rotation. A field study by NIOSH (Galinsky, Swanson, Sauter, Hurrell, & Schleifer, 2000) investigated the effects of two different break schedules on data entry workers at the Internal Revenue Service (IRS). Employees alternated, at 4-week intervals, between a conventional break schedule (two 15-minute breaks per shift) and a supplementary break schedule (four additional 5-minute breaks, one per hour). Both schedules included a 30-minute lunch period, and all workers were encouraged to get up and walk away from their ergonomic workstations during each break. Ratings of musculoskeletal discomfort in the neck/shoulder, upper extremity, and back were significantly lower with the supplementary break schedule (p < .05) and there was no decrease in productivity (key strokes, documents entered, error rate) despite 20 minutes less work time per day.

There is also benefit from frequent short breaks, termed “microbreaks,” typically lasting a few seconds to a few minutes. VDT workers who show fewer spontaneous pauses during word processing have reported greater trapezius discomfort and fatigue, and cues to pause frequently (15 seconds every 6 minutes) reduced subjective reports of discomfort (Hagberg & Sundelin, 1986). Computer programs are now available that can cue breaks as well as microbreaks (Peper & Gibney, 2006).

The problem is, muscles that fail to relax quickly and completely will not benefit from the rest opportunities provided by breaks and microbreaks. A relevant prospective study of light assembly workers found that upper trapezius activity, measured by EMG during the first minute of accidental machine stops in which workers passively waited for the equipment to restart, was significantly higher (p < .05) in workers who later developed work-related upper trapezius pain compared to coworkers who did not (Veiersted, 1994). Similarly, a case–control study indicated that upper trapezius muscle activity, measured by EMG during brief nonwork periods (standing still during an equipment calibration period) correctly classified 73% of manual workers with neck/ shoulder pain versus nonpain controls (Vasselgen & Westgaard, 1995; Westgaard, Jansen, & Jensen,1996).

# Cognitive and Psychosocial Factors

Cognitive demands can increase muscle activity in excess of that required to perform a given task (muscle overuse). A study by Waerstedt, Bjorkland, and Westgaard (1991) compared upper trapezius EMG activity within subjects during a reaction time (RT) test in response to simple versus complex VDT displays. Although no difference was found for the group as a whole, there were large individual differences. A subgroup (eight of 18 individuals) had large increases in EMG (p < .005) while processing the more complex VDT display, and this EMG elevation was present bilaterally even though the RT test was performed right-handed. For VDT workers, time pressure can increase the speed and force of key strikes and also reduce spontaneous, restorative pauses. Perceived time pressure can produce the same physical effects as an imposed work deadline (Sauter & Swanson, 1996). An excellent review of research on links between cognitive–attentional factors, prolonged low-level muscle activity, and WRMD is available (Waersted, 2000).

An interesting and relevant issue, beyond the scope of this chapter, is the extent to which factors such as emotional stress, depression, and nonrestorative sleep are direct generators of workrelated musculoskeletal pain independent of mediation by muscle overuse. In many (if not most) instances, the effects of cognitive and psychosocial factors can be accounted for by their activating effects on working muscles (Moon & Sauter, 1996; Lundberg et al., 1994; Westgaard,1999). This muscle overuse can quickly become habitual and reinforced by changes in muscle length and strength and joint range of motion (ROM) that are not reversed solely by eliminating the initiating cognitive or psychosocial problems. At the same time, it is difficult to eliminate habitual muscle overuse without addressing cognitive and psychosocial factors that may reinstate it. Accordingly, stress management procedures, social support and cognitive-behavioral therapy are an integral part of intervention programs for WRMD.

# EMG Biofeedback

Biofeedback procedures, in which surface-recorded EMG is used to provide auditory and/or visual display of muscle activity, can readily evaluate how muscles are actually being used by the individual worker for job activities and also train that worker to use his or her muscles more efficiently. It is this unique capacity to target the muscles directly that makes EMG biofeedback procedures so useful for treatment of WRMDs. EMG biofeedback procedures can be used to fine-tune ergonomic interventions, train workers to relax muscles quickly and completely during breaks and microbreaks, and teach workers to minimize the impact of cognitive demand and psychosocial factors on working muscles. The following studies amply illustrate these applications of EMG biofeedback for prevention and treatment of WRMD.

Patkin (1990) has described the use of a small EMG feedback device that provided an auditory signal proportional to muscle activity as part of a rehabilitation program for VDT workers seeking treatment for upper extremity pain. EMG feedback was used to demonstrate, and correct, excessive use of forearm muscles (wrist extensors) caused by striking the keys too forcefully or by holding the wrist and hand in maladaptive positions while keying (wrist extension and ulnar deviation). He observed that muscle overuse—muscle activity greater than needed to perform a given task—is a common and overlooked source of muscle fatigue and ache. He pointed out that it is common in sports and music to retrain skilled movements to be more relaxed and efficient, and the same approach should be applied to work activities.

Parenmark, Engvall, and Malmkvist (1988) included EMG feedback in a training program for newly hired assembly line workers in an effort to prevent the high rates of work-related neck and upper extremity pain that typically developed in these workers. New ergonomic workstations were installed, 33 new workers were hired, and all went through a 5- 6-week training program that included assistance with individually adjusting their workstations. EMG feedback was used to train 15 of the new workers (Trial Group) to adjust patterns of movement until the upper trapezius load was kept below 10% of MVC, and all were able to accomplish this. The other 18 workers (Control Group) did not receive this EMGassisted training. At follow-up, after 48 weeks on the job, the Trial Group had 54% fewer days of sick leave due to upper extremity problems than the Control Group (p > .05), with no difference in piecework performance. The investigators concluded that it is possible to reduce muscle load during work tasks; however, it is not sufficient to install ergonomically designed workstations. It is also necessary to modify the movement patterns of the individual worker and “this could be done by giving movement instructions using EMG” (p. 145). They further suggested that such on-thejob training be carried out early, before harmful working techniques are established.

Peper and colleagues conducted a series of studies of office workers and college students who regularly used computers (Peper, et al., 2003; Peper, Gibney & Wilson, 2004). During intensive computer tasks, respiration typically shifted to a rapid, shallow, chest-breathing pattern. Also, muscle activity in the upper trapezius and forearm muscles, recorded by EMG, was greater than needed for keyboard and mouse tasks, and continued during brief work pauses. Awareness of these physiological effects—attributed to cognitive and attentional demand—was poor. These investigators also found that faulty ergonomics could increase muscle overuse, but ergonomic solutions alone did not eliminate it; muscle overuse was often present even when working in an ergonomically designed workstation. Based on these studies, Peper and colleages (2004) developed a multicomponent program designed to prevent repetitive strain injury (RSI) in computer operators. Their Healthy Computing program includes (1) respiratory and EMG biofeedback training; (2) improvements in work station ergonomics; (3) changes in work style, including frequent brief breaks at the computer plus longer breaks away from the computer; (4) simple exercises that can be done at the computer or worksite; and (5) social support for implementing changes (Peper & Gibney, 2006). EMG biofeedback (upper trapezius, deltoid, and forearm muscles) is used to demonstrate the effects of faulty ergonomics, minimize muscle activity during keyboard and mousing tasks, and train rapid muscle relaxation during frequent microbreaks, a few seconds in duration, with hands placed in the lap. In an outcome study (Peper et al., 2004), computer users randomly assigned to a 6-week Healthy Computing program (Training Group) were compared with an untreated Control Group. Prior to the study, no participants had reported musculoskeletal problems to their supervisors or received medical treatment for such problems; nevertheless, most reported symptoms of muscle discomfort (some severe) in one or more body areas, and generalized tiredness associated with prolonged computer use. By the end of the study, the Training Group reported greater reductions than the Control Group on subjective ratings of muscle discomfort in the neck/shoulder and wrist/hand (p < .01), and generalized tiredness (p < .05).

Each of the interventions described earlier— ergonomic, work organization, cognitive/psychosocial, and biofeedback—makes a unique and essential contribution to prevention and remediation of WRMDs, and none is a stand-alone intervention. An optimal intervention combines these in a coordinated program. The Healthy Computing program of Peper and colleagues (2004) combines these interventions in an effective preclinical prevention program that is designed to be carried out in an office setting. My colleagues and I combine these interventions in an effective clinical treatment program for individuals who are referred to a medical facility. Our program and the clinical biofeedback protocols that we developed are presented below.

# Biofeedback for T reatment of Work‑Rel ated Muscul oskel etal  Pain

The EMG biofeedback protocols described here arose from clinical research and extensive experience treating participants in a multidisciplinary Pain Rehabilitation Program (PRP) at the Medical University of South Carolina1 (MUSC). This program treated a wide range of pain problems; however, the majority of patients had musculoskeletal disorders, and many of these were work-related. As a group, working-age participants (up to age 55) improved significantly (p < .01) from admission to discharge on measures of pain, medication use, emotional distress, physical capacity, and hours worked per week. Success rates, based on a combination of these measures, were 78.3% at discharge and 71.4 % at 1-year follow-up. A detailed description of the PRP and our research on clinical outcomes is available elsewhere (Kee, Middaugh, & Pawlick, 1996; Middaugh & Pawlick, 2002; Kee, Middaugh, Pawlick, & Nicholson, 1997). An outline of the PRP treatment components is provided in Table 36.1.

• Behavioral psychology: Psychological assessment; stress management; cognitive-behavioral therapy; pain education   
• Physical therapy: Therapeutic exercise: strength, flexibility, aerobics; nonphamacological modalities for pain reduction   
• Biofeedback: Physiological assessment; relaxation training; EMG biofeedback for muscle training, posture, and ergonomics   
Occupational therapy: Ergonomics; posture; pacing of work and rest; activities of daily living at home and work   
• Physician care: Medical assessment; medication management; consults or procedures as indicated

The focus in this chapter is on the biofeedback component and the EMG biofeedback protocols for individuals with cervical and upper extremity pain, and headache, for whom repetitive work activities, especially prolonged computer use, are major contributors. Our clinical observations, supported by our research studies, have found a high incidence of three interrelated problems: (1) overuse of the upper trapezius during activities with delayed and incomplete relaxation following contraction; (2) forward head posture with rounded shoulders; and (3) a rapid, shallow, chestbreathing pattern. Typically, all three problems are habitual; that is, they are present during a wide range of daily activities—not just work activities—and often present at rest (sitting or standing still). All three problems are reinforced by tight muscles in the neck and anterior shoulder girdle, weak muscles in the upper back and posterior shoulder girdle, and decreased ROM in the neck and shoulders. The lower back is often involved as well. The biofeedback component is designed to reverse these three interrelated problems, working closely with physical therapy (PT) on exercises to stretch and strengthen muscles and occupational therapy (OT) on posture and ergonomics. When the biofeedback therapist is also a PT or an OT, these components may be combined. Excellent illustrations of the locations and functions of all muscles mentioned in this chapter can be found in Travel & Simons (1983). Good sources of information on ergonomics for VDT workers include the Krames Communications patient education booklets, Pascarelli and Quilter (2004), and Peper and Gibney (2006).

# Evaluation Protocol

Evaluation begins with the patient sitting (not reclining) in an armless chair with back supported, hands in the lap, and feet flat on the floor. Upper trapezius activity is evaluated by EMG recording (two or four channels, 20–1000 Hz, mean mV) first while sitting still, then during three repetitions of three different gentle movements (shoulder shrug, shoulder abduction to horizontal, shoulder flexion to horizontal), with 15 seconds between repetitions. The therapist models, and the participant follows, all nine test movements to standardize the movements and the timing: 3 seconds to move into position, a 3-second hold, and 3 seconds to return to the starting position. No mention is made of “relaxation.” The patient is simply told, “Shrug your shoulders up, hold, let them back down”; “Raise your arms out to the side, hold, put your hands back in your lap”; and so forth. The goal is to observe how the muscles are being used during these simple movements and how well the patient is automatically relaxing after each movement ends, as a test of the work–rest cycle. Then, 15 seconds after the ninth repetition, if the muscles have not fully relaxed, we test the ability of the patient to voluntarily relax with the instructions, “Now think about relaxing your shoulders.” We note the extent to which the muscles relax and how well relaxation is maintained. Often a muscle relaxes briefly, only to resume contracting almost immediately (rebound). Next, the patient is asked to stand (arms hanging at the side). The upper trapezius normally contracts as the person stands up, but should quickly relax again during quiet standing. If the muscles are not fully relaxed after 15 seconds, we repeat the instructions to relax. Finally, the patient is asked to sit back down to provide a comparison of muscle activity while sitting, from the start to the end of the evaluation sequence. The upper trapezius muscles normally contract when the head is turned to the right or left, so the patient looks straight ahead, the therapist sits or stands in front of the patient to model the movements, and the EMG display screen is positioned behind the patient so the therapist (but not the patient) can view it.

Figure 36.3 shows evaluation findings for a patient with long-standing posterior cervical headache and bilateral upper trapezius pain (greater on the right) that is aggravated by many activities, including computer work. As the evaluation begins (two left panels), both the right and left muscles are completely relaxed (< 2 mV) while sitting still. As the individual is coached through three shoulder shrugs (S) the left upper trapezius relaxes to 4 mV (mild elevation) between each repetition, while the right upper trapezius does not relax and escalates activity with each repetition. Similar profiles were found for shoulder flexion and shoulder abduction (not shown). In the two right panels, the patient sits still (after completing the nine test movements), then stands and is instructed to relax the shoulders (IR). This evaluation shows muscle activity that is within normal limits or mildly overactive on the left. On the right, the upper trapezius muscle is markedly overactive: it reacts strongly to repetitive contraction and fails to relax (< 4 mV) after contraction (automatically) or after instructions to relax (voluntarily).

![](images/3be6e4c09587649f1126fdf27ab88ae3d7cf88a72f7ed527945aef3fc08c0c71.jpg)  
FI GURE 36.3. Upper trapezius EMG (mean mV) during evaluation of a 46-year-old woman with posterior cervical headache and bilateral upper trapezius pain (R > L) The left upper trapezius shows a relatively normal profile, while the right upper trapezius shows a strong reaction to shoulder movements and posture change (from Sit to Stand) and an incomplete response to instructions to relax the shoulders (IR).

# Treatment Protocol

EMG biofeedback-assisted muscle training in our program is dynamic and designed to improve muscle control during activities at work and at home. It begins with basic muscle control skills and progresses to applying these skills during increasingly complex activities. Homework is focused on applying these skills, outside of sessions, without biofeedback. As the EMG biofeedback sessions begin, we also teach gentle exercises (to be carried out at home) to stretch and strengthen cervical and upper body muscles. We address basic posture and also teach appropriate use of ice, heat, and handheld vibrating muscle massager for effective selfmanagement of muscle soreness. This PT content is described in detail elsewhere (Middaugh, 1998).

The following protocol provides an outline of the EMG biofeedback-assisted training tasks in the order in which they are typically introduced. The protocol is carried out in 50-minute sessions, one to three times per week, for a total of five to 12 sessions depending on problem complexity and whether the PT content is included in the biofeedback sessions or in separate sessions. Figure 36.4 shows our upper trapezius, posterior occipital and midscapular electrode placements. The upper trapezius placement uses an anterior to posterior alignment that reduces EKG artifact and tends to equalize the EKG artifact for the right and left upper trapezius recordings. EMG recordings with this alignment correspond closely to simultaneous EMG recordings using the standard electrode alignment (neck to shoulder; Banks, Middaugh, & Morrisette, 1997). The midscapular electrodes are angled to minimize EKG artifact and reduce right versus left EKG discrepancy. Lumbar electrodes are placed vertically at L4.

![](images/81b02736ce02c7ad6391472510da3c79517a9189c8c8811e307099dafbdc1fae.jpg)  
FI GURE 36.4. Electrode placements for surface EMG recording.

# Progressive Muscle Relaxation and Diaphragmatic Breathing

Session 1 begins with progressive muscle relaxation (PMR) carried out with gentle (not forceful) movements, following an audiotape, while sitting (not reclining) in an armless chair with good lumbar support to promote generalization to everyday situations. We record upper trapezius EMG bilaterally and digital skin temperature (small portable unit), and observe respiratory pattern and rate (stopwatch). Common findings are co-contraction of the upper trapezius (e.g., the shoulders tense when toes are curled or eyebrows raised); poor relaxation of the upper trapezius following contraction; and rapid, shallow breathing with breath holding during the “tense” phases. Homework includes practice of PMR. The goal is to learn (1) how to discriminate between muscle tension and muscle relaxation in major muscle groups; (2) how to contract one muscle group without cocontraction of the others, with special attention to the upper trapezius, and (3) how to keep breathing as the muscles contract. In following sessions, patients are taught diaphragmatic breathing (DB), first supine (this is easier), then sitting (this is harder), with a goal of six breaths per minute, emphasizing slow exhalation. Patients are introduced to passive relaxation (“Think about relaxing the muscles”) and mental imagery to enhance autonomic nervous system (ANS) effects (hand warming) and mental calm. When imagery is a problem, autogenic phrases, timed to slow exhalation, may be substituted.

It is important to recognize that muscle relaxation is often uncomfortable at first. The upper trapezius can become short and tight when the shoulders are habitually elevated. When this shortened muscle relaxes, it is stretched as the shoulder lowers to a normal position. The stretched muscle often responds by quickly contracting again to return to the habitual shoulder position. This is the source of the rebound seen in the EMG during evaluation and biofeedback training. When discomfort is a problem, PMR can be carried out with a pillow placed on the lap to support the weight of the hands and forearms. In most cases, it is enough simply to explain that muscle relaxation is also a gentle stretching exercise and to encourage the patient to relax the shoulders briefly but frequently during the day as part of Spot Checks. Over a week or two, these muscles become comfortable in the relaxed position. The PT interventions (exercises, heat, ice, vibration) also help this process.

# Spot Checks and Mini‑Relax

The clinical goal is not simply to teach improved muscle control, breathing, posture, and ergonomics, but ultimately to change the way the individual goes through the day. This requires frequent practice of new skills during the day, until they become habitual. The process begins with Spot Checks assigned as homework in Session 1. The patient is asked to check frequently what his or her muscles and breathing are doing throughout the day (“Whenever you think about it, continue with whatever you are doing and notice . . . ”), then briefly correct whatever problem is noticed (if hands are in fists, relax them; if holding breath, resume breathing). At the start of Session 2, the patient is asked what he or she noticed.

Once the patient learns DB and the rudiments of posture (head up, chin level, shoulders relaxed down), usually by session 3, the Spot Check is replaced by the Mini-Relax, to be carried out every 20 minutes during the day. This process is prompted by a timer, set for 20 minutes, used for at least a couple of hours each day until the patient starts remembering without it. The instructions are as follows:

•	 Take one deep breath in, then focus on relaxing your face, shoulders, and arms as you slowly breathe out.   
•	 Notice and correct your posture.   
•	 Take five diaphragmatic breaths: Breathe in slowly from the diaphragm, then focus on relaxing your muscles, from head to toe, as you breathe slowly out.

The Mini-Relax takes about 1 minute and is carried out with eyes open, while sitting, standing, or walking, alone or in a room full of people. To reinforce this, the therapist and patient practice a Mini-Relax together while sitting, while standing, then while walking in the hallway. As the treatment program progresses, the posture corrections become more specific and also include ergonomics; for example, noticing and correcting forward head posture and work posture. Patients tell us that the Mini-Relax is one of the most useful things they are taught in the program and that, over time, it becomes automatic. They notice when their muscles are tense, breathing is rapid, or posture is slumped, and they find themselves taking a deep breath and starting a Mini-Relax.

# EMG Biofeedback: Basic Upper Trapezius Control

# Wor k–Rest  Cycle

EMG biofeedback training of the upper trapezius also begins in Session 1, after PMR, using a visual display with a 4 mV threshold, color-coded for the right versus left muscles, a 0–20 mV vertical range, and 90-second horizontal sweep. We begin in sitting and repeat the same shoulder/arm movements as in the evaluation (discussed earlier), but now the patient is asked to (1) attend to the feedback display; (2) “notice what it feels like” as they “shrug up, hold, relax back down”; and (3) focus on relaxing the shoulder muscles for 15–30 seconds to prevent (or correct) rebound. The training goal is EMG < 4 mV. Once the patient can relax with feedback (FB), we alternate FB trials (eyes open) with nonfeedback trials (NFB, eyes closed) to encourage attention to intrinsic bodily cues and to give patients the confidence that they can relax the muscles without EMG feedback. As with PMR (discussed earlier), the upper trapezius may be uncomfortable when fully relaxed, and biofeedback training is introduced with a few repetitions, not extensive drill. The patients are encouraged to attend to upper trapezius relaxation during PMR practice and Spot-Checks.

EMG biofeedback then focuses on training relaxation after repetitions of shoulder abduction and forward flexion (as in the evaluation), initially with FB, then alternating with NFB trials. The message is that the upper trapezius normally participates in a wide range of arm movements all day long (e.g., when reaching for a toothbrush, scratching the head) and the goal is to relax these muscles quickly and completely when the movement ends (hands back in lap). Homework is to attend to muscle relaxation following arm movements during the day.

# Co ‑Contr action

EMG biofeedback then targets upper trapezius co-contraction during hand activities. When the upper arm and the elbow are at the side of the trunk (with the shoulder neither flexed nor abducted), the upper trapezius should stay completely relaxed while the hands and forearms are used for activities such as keyboarding. Yet a habitually overactive upper trapezius co-contracts during hand and forearm tasks. Biofeedback training begins, sitting with good lumbar support, hands in the lap, elbows at the side of the trunk (almost touching the chair back) and the upper trapezius completely relaxed. The task is to focus on leaving the upper trapezius relaxed while slowly lifting the hands and forearms a few inches off the lap, parallel to the floor, palms down, as if placing the fingers on a keyboard (elbow stays positioned at the side). If the upper trapezius co-contracts, the patient keeps the hands/forearms in the new hand-off-the-lap position and focuses on relaxing the upper trapezius in that position. With a little practice of this sequence, the upper trapezius will stay relaxed as the hands/forearms are raised, lowered, and moved right and left as if floating over an imaginary keyboard (elbow staying at the side). EMG feedback is then used to demonstrate the relevance of positioning the upper arm/elbow at the side during hand tasks. Starting with the hands/ forearms off the lap, palms down and upper trapezius relaxed, the patient observes what happens to the upper trapezius (increasing EMG activity) as he or she slowly reaches forward a few inches (a few degrees of shoulder flexion, elbow moving forward), then returns to the elbow-at-side position (decreasing EMG). The same thing occurs as the patient moves the right elbow a few inches out to the right (a few degrees of shoulder abduction) away from the trunk (EMG increases) and then returns (EMG decreases). The message is that when the elbows are at the side, keyboarding is a light-duty task that requires only forearm/ hand muscles, but when the worker has to reach forward to touch the keyboard, or reach to the side to touch the mouse, the cervical/shoulder muscles are forced to contract continuously to hold the upper arm in this forward (or sideward) position. This produces substantial muscle fatigue over the course of the working day. Homework is to notice where the elbow is positioned during daily activities (driving, keyboarding, hand work), then try to do these activities with the elbow at the side and upper trapezius relaxed. This is one of the most important things our patients learn.

# St anding and W al king

EMG biofeedback focuses next on relaxing the upper trapezius while standing. The goal is to relax (< 4 mV) in good posture: head up, looking straight ahead, shoulders relaxed, arms at side, knees slightly loose and not locked back. Good posture is relaxed and balanced, and not forced, military style. If the upper trapezius muscles are still tight, it may be helpful to stand at first with hands in pockets to support the weight of the arms. The next task is to alternate standing up (then relax) with sitting down (then relax). With each change in position, the upper trapezius briefly contracts (this is normal) but should quickly relax in the new body position. Homework is to attend briefly to relaxing after standing up or sitting down during the day.

The next task is to walk with the upper trapezius relaxed. This training is carried out in a hallway with a small portable set to beep when the muscle exceeds a threshold set 1 or 2 mV above the relaxed standing baseline, to allow for normal arm swing. The patient stands with muscles relaxed and focuses on leaving the shoulders relaxed (no beeps) while walking slowly, head up, looking straight ahead (not down at the floor). After a few steps, the upper trapezius typically begins to intrude and triggers beeps. The patient stops, stands still to regain relaxed control, and then resumes walking. This sequence of walk–stop– stand–relax–walk can be frustrating at first. It may be helpful to start with hands in pockets. We limit initial practice to 5–10 minutes, and assure the patient that he or she will soon be able to walk with these muscles relaxed for longer stretches, first at slow speed, then at normal speed. Upper trapezius activity while walking is very sensitive to posture: This muscle relaxes easily with head up/ shoulders relaxed down, but contracts as soon as the patient leans forward, hunches the shoulders, or hangs the head down while walking. Homework is to attend to head/upper body position and shoulder relaxation when walking during the day. It is not unusual to make little or no progress in the first practice session, then to make rapid progress in the next session.

# EMG Biofeedback: Advanced Upper Trapezius Control

EMG biofeedback training next focuses on upper trapezius relaxation during common daily activities at home and work, in combination with posture and ergonomics.

# Writing and  Re ading

Upper trapezius biofeedback is carried out with the patient sitting, with the back supported, and the writing surface close to the body. The forearms rest on the desk, so the elbows can be slightly forward of the trunk as long as the shoulder blades stay in contact with the chair back. The patient is to look down by tilting the chin down (not by hanging the head forward) and not lean the trunk forward or support the weight of the upper body on the arms. Biofeedback training begins with relaxing the upper trapezius while sitting still and holding a pen in contact with the paper, then writing the ABCs while keeping the muscles relaxed. Auditory feedback is convenient for this task. The upper trapezius often contracts as the first letter is written, so the training sequence is write A, stop (pen still on paper), relax, write B, stop, relax, and so forth. With focus and a little practice, the patient will be able to write continuously and keep the shoulder muscles relaxed. At first, the writing will be very messy, but it will improve as the patient leans to use the hand without co-contracting the upper trapezius. Useful alternatives are to write on a clipboard propped against the edge of the desk (other end in the lap) or propped on a pillow or book bag on the lap. With these positions, the elbow can stay at the side, and many find this to be easy and comfortable. We train on all three methods, and homework is to practice all three and also to adapt the basic principles to other hand activities.

The key to keeping the muscles relaxed while reading is position. With biofeedback, the patient keeps the upper trapezius relaxed while reading briefly in each of the following positions. First is to read at a desk, in the basic position as for writing, discussed earlier, but with the reading material propped at an angle on the desk (propping it against another book works). This way, the hands do not support the weight of the book but rest lightly on the desk to hold the pages down and turn them as needed, relaxing after each page turn. Alternatives are to prop the book against the edge of the desk, on a pillow, or a book bag on the lap while sitting, or on a pillow on the stomach while reclining. Homework is to practice reading with these methods and also to adapt the basic principles to other hand activities.

# Dri ving

Distance driving is a problem for many individuals with cervical and upper extremity pain. The key is to sit sufficiently close to the wheel for the elbows to be as close to the side as feasible. Ideally, the elbows are at the side, or slightly forward, when both hands are placed on the bottom half of the wheel (4:00 and 8:00). In this position, with good lumbar and upper back support, the neck, shoulder, and back muscles can stay relaxed and comfortable for distance cruising. For maneuverability in traffic, one hand is placed on the upper half of the wheel (EMG increased) while the other remains on the lower half of the wheel (EMG decreased), but these hand positions are alternated often, so the right and left upper trapezius muscles have frequent rests. We start EMG feedback training in the laboratory, with simulated driving, first keeping the elbows at the side and upper trapezius relaxed while holding the imaginary steering wheel at 4:00 and 8:00. We then say, “Show me how you usually hold the steering wheel in your car while driving.” This usually makes the basic point about the relationship between arm position and muscle load. Then we practice with one hand on the upper half of the imaginary wheel (muscle on that side will be active) and the other on the lower half (muscle on that side is to relax), then alternate the upper and lower positions between the right and left hands. Homework is for the patient to observe his or her body position and muscles while driving, and to apply the methods learned during biofeedback. A visit to his or her parked car with a biofeedback portable and a lumbar support cushion is helpful. An OT is indispensable when more complex ergonomic adaptations are needed.

# Keybo ard , Mouse , and  Other Wor k‑Rel ated Acti vities

EMG biofeedback for computer work is introduced in an earlier session (see “Co-Contraction,” earlier) that trains upper trapezius relaxation while using the hands/forearms for simulated keyboard use, elbows at the side. Biofeedback training continues at a computer workstation, usually with a portable, using audio feedback and a threshold set to 1-2 mV above relaxed baseline. The first task is to keep the upper trapezius relaxed while using the keyboard, at first slowly and then more quickly, with the elbows at the side, forearms parallel to the floor, wrists straight, hands floating over the keys and no contact with the wrist rest. The next task is to relax quickly and completely during frequent brief pauses (micro-breaks), placing the hands in the lap for a few seconds. An alternative is to let the arms hang at the sides or to rest the side of the palm (not the wrist) on the wrist rest. During pauses, the hands and forearms do not relax well if they remain in a palm-down, “ready to work” position; they do relax quickly if rotated into a neutral, thumb up, little finger down position, on the wrist rest or in the lap. The next task is to keep the upper trapezius relaxed while using the mouse and during pauses in mouse tasks (hand in lap or rotated to neutral). The key is to locate the mouse pad so that the elbow can be close to the side; for example, on a bridge placed over the number keys on the right or a clamp-on platform just above or below the keyboard. As needed, a site visit is carried out, with a portable biofeedback unit, to check workstation ergonomics and to fine-tune muscle control under work conditions.

As needed, EMG biofeedback training is extended to other problematic work activities using the same approach described earlier: Detect sources of continuous (static) muscle activity and eliminate these with a combination of EMG biofeedback-assisted muscle training, improved posture, and improved ergonomics. This is typically carried out with portable biofeedback equipment in conjunction with OT, often as part of worksimulation or work-hardening activities and, when possible, in a visit to the worksite.

# EMG Biofeedback Training: Posterior Occipital Muscles

Forward head posture with rounded shoulders is a common problem in VDT workers. This posture is often attributed to ergonomic problems such as bifocals or VDT screen placement; but ergonomic continuously to counter the effect of gravity. The farther forward the head position, the more these muscles contract (Morrisette, Middaugh, Lam, & Quig, 1997; Middaugh & Morrisette, 2000). Forward head posture also interferes with normal use of cervical muscles and joints; for example, during cervical rotation, ROM is reduced, posterior neck muscles are overused, and anterior neck muscles are underused. This imbalance strains muscles and joints.

![](images/5225fc8110b7ec6d690adcaa960199a5555969ba7dc70af480039909b9e1fef5.jpg)  
FI GURE 36.5. A forward head posture of 25°.

improvements such as new glasses or workstation adjustments are seldom sufficient. Forward head posture, illustrated in Figure 36.5, quickly becomes habitual and is closely associated with pain in the head, neck, and midscapular regions (Braun, 1991; Griegel-Morris, Larson, Mueller-Klaus, & Oatis, 1992). Forward head posture is a problem, because the head is normally balanced on the spine, and little muscle activity is needed to keep it balanced there. As the head shifts forward of this balance point, the posterior occipital muscles—a set of deep muscles at the back of the neck that connect the base of the skull to the cervical and upper thoracic vertebrae—are forced to contract

EMG biofeedback training is very useful for correction of this common problem. We introduce posterior occipital biofeedback after upper trapezius training and after the patient has started PT exercises. Patients with forward head posture and rounded shoulders have tight cervical and pectoral muscles that constrain them in poor posture, and the patient needs to improve flexibility in order to assume a normal posture without strain and increased pain. Optimal head posture is approximately 3–5°, while 15 to 20+° is common in our patients, and our goal is 7° or less. The posterior occipital muscles lie relatively deep, so EMG is recorded using widely spaced electrodes placed on the neck just below the base of the skull (Figure 36.4). In this location, the upper trapezius is largely tendon and contributes little to the EMG signal. We also use a visual biofeedback screen with a high resolution (0–10 mV vertical scale) and a training goal of < 2 mV. Figure 36.6 shows EMG recorded during the initial session of posterior occipital biofeedback training for a 33-year-old woman with long-standing tension-type headache and cervical pain. In (a) she is sitting with her habitual 17° of forward head posture. In (b), recorded 15 minutes later, she has been coached, with verbal instructions and EMG feedback (as below), into an acceptable head position (7°) and EMG has decreased to our training goal (< 2 mV).

![](images/9b8e6f0c1069307ee0660b6d9e16c091c1f28c24f84040254c630bd9971ea0c0.jpg)  
FI GURE 36.6. Posterior occipital EMG (mean mV) for a 33-year-old woman with tension-type headache and cervical pain during (a) sitting baseline, 17° of forward head posture, and (b) EMG biofeedback training, 7° of forward head posture.

Biofeedback training begins with coaching the patient gently to push the chin further forward and briefly hold an exaggerated forward head position. This strongly elevates posterior cervical EMG, clearly identifies the faulty head position, and demonstrates the negative impact on the muscles. Then the patient slowly moves the head back toward neutral (keeping the chin level, not tilted up), stops just before the head position feels forced or uncomfortable, and relaxes in that corrected position. It is important that the patient not force the head into a corrected position. Rather, the patient should gently shift the head back and balance the head in the new position. Simultaneous recording of the sternocleidomastoid (SCM) muscle can help prevent excessive correction. The SCM will contract a little as the head is moving back toward neutral but relax as the posterior occipital muscles relax in the corrected position. If SCM EMG stays high, the patient is forcing the new position. This “turtle” exercise (Middaugh, 1998) is repeated, alternating FB trials (eyes open) with NFB trials (eyes closed) until the patient can identify a balance point at which the head position is at least partially corrected and comfortable, first while sitting, then while standing. A portable EMG with auditory feedback is then used to practice maintaining improved head posture while walking, writing, reading, and during computer work. The key to good head posture and relaxed posterior occipital muscles during eye–hand activities is to start with the head in a balanced position, looking straight ahead, and then look down by tilting the chin down—not by hanging the head forward. This entire training sequence usually proceeds rapidly, over one or two sessions, if carried out after upper trapezius training and after progress on PT exercises.

# EMG Biofeedback Training: Other Muscles

# Midsc apul ar  Muscles

Many patients with cervical or upper extremity WRMD also have some discomfort in the midscapular muscles that connect the shoulder blade to the thoracic vertebrae, such as the rhomboids and the lower trapezius muscle, or the thoracic paraspinal muscles that connect the thoracic vertebrae with one another. This problem is typically related to upper trapezius overuse and the associated postural and ergonomic problems described earlier. That is, when the upper trapezius is overused, the lower trapezius is typically underused, and this muscle imbalance leads to uncoordinated scapular motion during upper extremity activities. When there is muscle imbalance, we have found the most effective approach is to reduce activity in the overused muscles: When we do so, the underused muscles resume participation, and balance is restored. Accordingly, for midscapular pain, we use the same upper trapezius and posterior occipital protocols described earlier. When midscapular pain is a prominent complaint, we evaluate the midscapular muscles (electrode placement as in Figure 36.4) and the upper trapezius muscles simultaneously, using four channels. We also monitor all four muscle sites as biofeedback training begins with upper trapezius training. If midscapular muscles are overactive, then biofeedback training of these muscles is carried out simultaneously with upper trapezius training, using the same training protocols.

# Lumb ar Par aspin al  Muscles

Many patients with cervical and upper extremity pain also have some pain in the lower back. In VDT workers, this can often be attributed to sitting with the back unsupported while leaning forward to reach a poorly positioned keyboard or to see a computer screen placed too far away. The VDT worker whose chair is too high may sit on the edge of the chair so the feet ran reach the floor. EMG evaluation of the lumbar paraspinal muscles (electrodes placed vertically at L4) usually shows a completely relaxed lower back when sitting with good lumbar support (< 4 mV) (Middaugh, Peters, Ben-Kuan, & Halford, 1999). When lumbar pain is present, biofeedback training begins with the upper trapezius and posterior occipital training protocols described earlier. Biofeedback training of the lumbar muscles is added, primarily to reinforce the importance of good back support (and use of a footrest if needed) during VDT work. When lumbar symptoms are prominent, lumbar biofeedback training will also be carried out in a standing position, with the focus on correcting posture faults. Standing with knees locked back, an anterior pelvic tilt, and a forward head position forces a continuous contraction of the lumbar muscles. EMG biofeedback is very helpful in correcting these problems. The approach is to first exaggerate the poor posture, observe the negative effect on the low back muscles, then correct the posture (knees loose, neutral pelvis, shoulders relaxed, head up and < 7°) to relax the lumbar muscles. Lumbar biofeedback is also carried out while walking, using auditory feedback and a training sequence similar to that for the upper trapezius (discussed earlier): stand, relax, then walk. If the feedback beep is triggered, stop, stand, relax, then walk, and so forth. Since the lumbar muscles are normally active during walking, the feedback threshold is not set at the standing baseline, but several mV higher. This is set individually, so that the feedback signal is silent as long as the patient walks with good upper body posture, and lumbar EMG is relatively low but beeps when upper body posture shifts forward to elevate lumbar EMG. For patients with major lumbar complaints, and those not in office jobs, biofeedback training also includes lumbar muscle training during advanced activities, usually as part of OT work simulation and work-hardening activities. Also, PT exercises will be more extensive.

# Concl usions

The biofeedback procedures presented in this chapter are those we have found to be most effective in our research and our clinical program for treatment of WRMD in VDT workers. Our approach is (1) to eliminate continuous (static) muscle activity in key muscles during daily activities through a combination of muscle relaxation, improved posture, and improved ergonomics, and (2) to reinstate a normal work–rest cycle by training muscles to relax, quickly and completely, during daily activities. Our primary focus is on the upper trapezius for many reasons. This is a crossroads muscle that, by virtue of its connections at the base of the skull, cervical vertebrae, and scapula, is widely involved in movements of the head, neck, and upper extremity, as well as upper body posture. It is also sensitive to cognitive demand and psychosocial stressors, perhaps due to its participation in the startle response (Jones, Hanson, & Grey, 1964). Accordingly, it is not surprising that this muscle is susceptible to overuse, fatigue, and pain. It is also not surprising that biofeedback training of the upper trapezius has widespread beneficial effects, not only reducing symptoms in this muscle, but also improving muscle coordination and posture throughout the upper body. The posterior occipital muscles (at the junction of the cranium and spine) and the lumbar paraspinal muscles (at the crossroads of the trunk, pelvis, and lower extremities) are other key muscles with widespread impact that may be helped by biofeedback training.

We cannot precisely measure the contribution of the biofeedback component to overall outcome of our multicomponent Pain Rehabilitation Program. However, we have been able to identify problems of muscle overuse that differ between our patients and normal individuals prior to treatment (Figure 36.7, left chart) and document improvement of these muscle problems following EMG biofeedback training (Figure 36.7, right chart; see Middaugh et al., 1995; Middaugh, Woods, Kee, Harden, & Peters, 1991). EMG biofeedback is unique in this ability to target the muscles; that is, to evaluate directly and retrain problem muscles in the individual worker.

![](images/9ff94b8d6bf0cffa8f1a9cc4d1b69773d47ddd5118d52ef4c740866d3f8d24b6.jpg)  
FI GURE 36.7. Left panel: Upper trapezius relaxation, following contraction, is delayed and incomplete in headache patients (N = 35) compared to normal subjects (N = 32) (p < .01). Right panel: Upper trapezius relaxation in headache patients (N = 12) improves to normal values after EMG biofeedback training.

# Note

1. This program is no longer in operation.
