P a r t X

Th e Frontier and a Nod to the Future

# Biofeedback of Slow Cortical Potentials in Epilepsy

Ute Strehl

# Overview

Slow cortical potentials (SCPs) of the electroencephalogram (EEG) reflect cortical excitability. Negative SCPs appear in animals, as well as humans, before and during epileptic seizures, which are followed by positive potential shifts after their abatement. This leads to the hypothesis that epilepsy involves a deficit in regulating cortical hyperactivation. Operant learning and behavioral principles have been used to develop a treatment program to control SCPs and to teach patients to cope with seizures. The program is described in this chapter, and predictors of outcome are reported. (As in several other chapters, italics on first use of a term indicate that the term is included in the glossary at the chapter’s end.)

# Ep il eps y: Ep idemiol ogy, Cl ass ification, and Prognosis

Epilepsy is one of the most common neurological disorders. For every 1000 persons, there are five to 10 active cases. Epilepsy is most likely to occur at two periods: the first 10 years of life and after age 60. The annual incidence of new cases is about 50 per 100,000 (Hauser & Hesdorffer, 1990).

The causes of epilepsy are diverse but in most cases unknown. In young children, genetic factors, congenital defects, and developmental disturbances are the most common causes; in older adult patients, mostly cerebrovascular diseases are found.

Epilepsy can be described as a group of neurological conditions that are characterized by recurrent seizures. These seizures are the result of a disturbed balance between excitation and inhibition of neurons that are located predominantly in the cerebral cortex. Depending on the specific underlying pathophysiological conditions and clinical signs, partial (focal) seizures are distinguished from generalized seizures. Partial seizures consist of initial activation of neurons limited to one area of the cortex, the “focus.” Patients may have more than one focus. According to the classification of seizures and epilepsies, a scientific classification within focal seizures is not yet possible (Berg et al., 2010). Clinical signs of a partial seizure may include motor symptoms, somatosensory or special sensory symptoms, autonomic symptoms, or psychological experiences. In contrast, when the onset of a seizure involves both hemispheres, seizures are classified as generalized. Generalized seizure subtypes are tonic–clonic seizures, absence seizures, myoclonic seizures, clonic seizures, tonic seizures, and atonic seizures (Berg et al., 2010).

The degree of severity of the illness is extremely variable. Most patients behave completely ordinarily between seizures, and in many cases friends, colleagues, and even relatives do not recognize that a person has epilepsy. On the one hand, only 5% of patients with epilepsy are handicapped or mentally disabled and need special education. On the other hand, suffering from seizures is a frightening condition; for many patients, the seizures seem to be unpredictable and accompanied by a complete loss of control. In the public opinion, epilepsy is often misunderstood as mental illness, and patients can be discriminated against.

# Treatment

Medical treatment of epileptic seizures with antiepileptic drugs (AEDs) is specific for the seizure type. Treatment is normally initiated with one single drug type. If the patient does not become seizure-free, another drug suited for that seizure type is prescribed. The treatment may be continued by gradually changing AEDs and/or combining different drugs. Patients who continue to have seizures after 2 years of treatment are considered to have drug-resistant epilepsy (Bourgeois, 1994). Based on community and hospital-based studies, it is estimated that about one-fourth to one-third of newly diagnosed patients will have intractable epilepsy (Reynolds, 1994). For these patients, surgery becomes an option. Because of medical, psychological, and social reasons, only about 12% of patients with intractable epilepsy can be operated upon, and about 8% become seizure-free after a neurosurgical intervention. Thus, at least 15–17% of all patients cannot be treated by standard medical interventions and are therefore prime candidates for behavioral treatments, which are also applicable as adjunct methods to pharmacological and/or neurosurgical intervention.

Therefore, treatment strategies in a behavioral medicine framework have become an option for patients within the last several decades. Efron (1956) first reported classical conditioning of aura disruption. The most systematic behavioral training was developed by Dahl (1992), who integrated principles of identifying and controlling antecedents of seizures, teaching patients relaxation techniques, utilizing contingency management, and implementing other behaviors that are incompatible with seizures (“countermeasures”).

Since the early 1970s, biofeedback of electroencephalographic (EEG) rhythms (Lubar, 1977; Sterman, 2000), as well as respiration parameters (Fried, 1993), has been used in epilepsy therapy.

As reported by Tan and colleagues (2009), 36 studies on neurofeedback in epilepsy were identified by an extensive search in literature databases. Among those 10 studies that could be included in the meta-analysis, nine used sensorimotor rhythm (SMR) training, while one trained slow cortical potentials. This training protocol was developed in the late 1970s by Niels Birbaumer and colleagues (1999). They developed a physiological model wherein SCPs of the EEG are viewed as reflecting the level of cortical excitability. Negative SCPs can be observed in animals as well as in humans before and during ictal discharges, and positive SCP shifts follow the abatement of seizures. This model has led to the hypothesis that epilepsy is characterized by a problem in restraining the hyperactivation of neurons. Accordingly, suppression of cortical negativity should correspond to a state in which epileptic discharge is attenuated. Thus, seizure frequency may be reduced by training patients with epilepsy to suppress cortical negativities. Two multicenter studies to date have shown that patients are able to learn to regulate their SCPs, and this treatment has been found to yield a significant decrease in seizure rates (Rockstroh et al., 1993; Kotchoubey et al., 2001).

# SCP Sel f‑Regul ation

Until recently, devices for SCP feedback were not commercially available. In the previously cited studies, SCP self-regulation was taught with homemade laboratory equipment. In their research, Kotchoubey et al. (2001) used the so-called Thought Translation Device (TTD; see Birbaumer et al. (1999). Today, equipment with a high-pass filter can be used that allows the therapist/researcher to monitor very low frequencies. This high pass filter is also called a “time constant” (see Neumann, Strehl, Birbaumer, & Kotchoubey, Chapter 6, this volume). The time constant should be set at least at 10 seconds. The SCPs are recorded at the vertex (C ), referred to one or both mastoid electrode(s). For detailed information about technical requirements as hard- and software, signal processing, and artifact control, see Neumann et al., Chapter 6, this volume, or Strehl (2009).

The patient sits in a comfortable armchair, with a notebook computer or personal computer in front of him or her. Depending on the discriminative stimulus, the patient has to produce SCP shifts in either a negative or positive direction. Feedback is given by a graphic symbol, which moves during each feedback trial upward (negative shift) or downward (positive shift). Trials with required negativity and required positivity are randomly distributed, with each occurring 50% of the time initially. During the second training phase, 67% of trials require positivity. In order to assess learning and generalization of SCP skills in everyday life, feedback trials are mixed with transfer trials in which the graphic symbol is not shown. The length of each trial is 8 seconds; 100 trials constitute one session.

The patient is carefully monitored by the therapist, who may remain next to the patient or sit in an adjacent room. The therapist needs to observe the patient’s EEG data online on a separate screen and can intervene if necessary. After the end of a session, patients may be invited to observe trialby-trial data, in order to understand where (and probably why) they were successful or unsuccessful.

As shown in Figure 37.1, the whole treatment program is divided into three parts. The first phase includes at least 20 sessions over 3 weeks. Thereafter, patients are given an 8-week break from coming to the laboratory, so they can concentrate on practicing and applying the strategies they have learned in everyday life. Finally, in a sec

![](images/3c4a913e4651804799f2589cadd8385d4797037fac5979b6a77f09b7e4097ae9.jpg)

FI GURE 37.1. Schedule of behavioral training for epilepsy. Patients are instructed to continue working on exercises after completing this program. Because they have no EEG equipment at home, they have to get used to performing the exercises in imagination. Returning for booster sessions in the laboratory/ clinic helps to reassure patients that they are still able to control their SCPs.

ond, intensive phase (15 sessions within 2 weeks), emphasis on feedback is withdrawn in favor of transfer exercises.

# SCP Sel f‑Regul ation in a Behavioral Medicine F ramework

In a behavioral medicine paradigm for medical diseases, the symptoms (in this case, the epileptic seizures) are conceived as behaviors that are largely influenced by both their antecedents and their consequences. In addition to the neurological examination, a behavioral analysis concentrates on a careful description of internal (physiological states, emotions, cognitions, behaviors) and external (behaviors of other persons, changes of behavior plans) events. This analysis is based on a detailed seizure diary.

The aims of behavioral self-control training are to increase knowledge and perceptual skills about antecedents and early signs of seizure behavior, to change reinforcing contingencies, and to transfer self-control skills to everyday life conditions.

Thus, treatment includes behavioral change of seizure-eliciting and seizure-reinforcing behaviors, as well as the psychophysiological intervention to control the brain states that are thought to induce seizure activity. Depending on the results of the behavior analysis, various other intervention strategies besides SCP training are incorporated for sensitizing self-perception. These may include relaxation training, stress management, cognitive restructuring, activity assignments, social skills training, education of patients and significant others, changes in reinforcement patterns, coping with illness, and addressing qualityof-life issues.

# Eval uation

Results of the evaluation of the program are reported in detail elsewhere (Kotchoubey et al., 2001). Progress in self-control of SCPs can be shown by the increase in differentiation between mean SCP amplitudes for positivity and negativity conditions, as depicted in Figure 37.2, which illustrates how patient AB made progress in the second phase of training in producing required positivity and negativity. Additional measures can be evaluated by determining the percentage of hits, changes in power spectra, and so forth.

![](images/0c704affee1af834eb5885f6144d3c7097237666397291ec14e6b5c85b2d1895.jpg)  
FI GURE 37.2. Slow cortical potentials (SCPs) of patient AB, averaged across 20 sessions of treatment phase 1 (upper panel) and 15 sessions of phase 2 (lower panel). From Strehl, Kotchoubey, and Birbaumer (2011). Copyright 2011 by Schattauer GmbH. Reprinted by permission.

Changes in seizure frequency are documented by seizure diaries. A statistical decision about the significance of seizure decrement can be computed on the basis of a week-by-week sequence analysis (Künkel, 1979). With the probability of type I error at a1 = .05 and a probability of type II error at a1 = .10, the reliability of individual changes in seizure frequency during a 52-week follow-up period versus the baseline period was tested. The criterion of seizure decrement was set at 50%. Figure 37.3 shows the cumulative number of seizures following the therapy in the patient whose SCP amplitudes are presented in Figure 37.2. As can be seen, the decrease in the patient’s seizure frequency became significant in week 32 of the follow-up period, and after week 42, no more seizures could be observed. Ten years after the end of treatment, another follow-up evaluation was completed with 46% (N = 19 out of 41) of those patients who took part in the SCP treatment. The statistically significant decrease of seizures since the end of treatment was confirmed. In addition patients were still able to self-regulate SCP (Strehl, Birkle, Wörz, & Kotchoubey, 2014).

# Th erapists and Patients

In a behavioral medicine framework such as this, therapists should be licensed psychologists who are qualified to conduct psychophysiological procedures. Neurological consultation before and after treatment is necessary. Criteria for the patients are less clear. Contrary to common sense, cognitive and personality variables have not been found to have predictive power. It was shown, for example, that a patient with mental retardation and organic brain disorder was able to follow the self-control training and to reduce seizures considerably (Holzapfel, Strehl, Kotchoubey, & Birbaumer, 1998). In another study, patients with higher Depression and Hysteria scores on the Minnesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory were found to have a better outcome in seizure reduction. Instead, physiological features—such as the average SCP amplitude at the beginning of training, and the location of the epileptic focus—seem to be much more important. Larger negative amplitudes covary with less success, and patients with a left temporal focus profit less than patients with bilateral foci, multiple foci, or a right temporal focus (Strehl, Kotchoubey, & Birbaumer, 2011). On the other hand, the two patients with the best training results had a left-side focus. Therefore, it cannot be concluded with certainty that all patients with a large SCP amplitude or a leftside focus should be excluded from the program. Rather, the therapist should try to adapt training conditions to maximize the potential for improvement (e.g., by extending the duration of active treatment phases and/or by placing more emphasis on cortical positivity tasks or using advanced shaping schedules). The software used should allow these variations if the therapist is sensitive enough to the patient’s needs.

# Gl oss ary

Absence seizures. Seizures with sudden onset and interruption of ongoing activities.

Atonic seizures. Sudden diminution of muscle tone,

# Patient AB 0.82 seizure per week during baseline

![](images/d36cdd733dac9d28077426ded22a324eea255b60aa6ffb2a780f3470d417d398.jpg)  
FI GURE 37.3. Sequence analysis of seizure frequency after the end of active treatment. The two straight lines indicate the limits of significance for the two types of errors. Specifically, the lower (dashed) line stands for the .05 significance limit for the type I error; if the actual cumulative number of a patient’s seizures (shown by the gray line) drops under that limit, a 50% seizure reduction as compared with the baseline is regarded as significant. The upper (solid) line stands for the .10 significance limit for the type II error; if the actual cumulative number of seizures rises above that line, the lack of 50% improvement is regarded as significant. As long as the actual cumulative number of seizures remains inside the corridor created by the two straight lines, the outcome is uncertain. From Strehl, Kotchoubey, and Birbaumer (2011). Copyright 2011 by Schattauer GmbH. Reprinted by permission.

which may lead to dropping of the head and slumping to the ground.

Aura. Altered sensations at the very first sign of a partial seizure (see below), which allow patients to use selfcontrol techniques in order to interrupt the spreading of neuronal discharge.

Clonic seizures. Generalized seizure with lack of the tonic component.

Generalized seizures. Seizures in which the first clinical signs (e.g., impaired consciousness, bilateral motor manifestations) indicate that both hemispheres are involved from the beginning. Neuronal discharge is widespread in both hemispheres.

Ictal discharge. An abnormal firing of neurons, producing electrophysiological activity that can be observed as seizure activity in the EEG.

Myoclonic seizures. Single or multiple jerks, generalized or confined to the face, trunk, extremities, or (groups of) muscles.

Partial (focal) seizures. Seizures in which the first clinical signs indicate that the initial activation of a group of neurons is limited to a part of one cerebral hemisphere.

Seizures. The result of a disturbed balance between excitation and inhibition of neurons. They are classified on the basis of clinical manifestations and EEG signs. For practical reasons, the EEG criteria are not mentioned here. For further information, see Berg et al. (2010).

Slow cortical potentials (SCPs). Negative or positive polarizations of the EEG that last from 0.3 seconds up to several seconds. They originate in depolarizations of the apical dendrites in cortical layers I and II that are caused by synchronous firing, mainly from thalamocortical afferents. Functionally, they constitute a threshold regulation mechanism for local excitatory mobilization (negative SCPs) or inhibition (positive SCPs) of cortical networks. SCPs are typically seen preceding either voluntary movement or an expected imperative event.

Thought Translation Device (TTD ). Direct connection between the brain and a computer controlled by SCPs and used for communication.

Tonic–clonic seizures. Often known as “grand mal seizures”; the most frequent type of generalized seizures. In most cases, onset is signaled with a cry or moan, caused by a sharp tonic contraction of muscles. The tonic stage is followed by clonic convulsive movements. At the end of the seizure, deep respiration occurs with relaxation of all muscles. The patient awakens slowly from unconsciousness.

Tonic seizures. Muscular contraction of the whole body, usually with a deviation of the eyes and of the head to one side.

# Traumatic Brain Injury, Quantitative Electroencephalography, and E lectroencephalographic Biofeedback

Kirtley E . T hornton

Traumatic brain injury (TBI) affected 1.4 million people a cost of \$60 billion in 2000 (Centers for Disease Control and Prevention [CDC], 2008). The causes of TBI are falls (28%), motor vehicle accidents (20%), struck by/against events (19%; e.g., sports activities), assaults (11%), and blast injuries in the Iraq war (CDC, 2008). Up to 90% (Torg, 1991) of TBI cases are classified as mild (MTBI) and do not involve prolonged coma periods. The term “mild” refers to the patient’s state of mind and behavior at the time of the initial assessment, with such instruments as the Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS), but do not necessarily reflect the seriousness of their injuries or outcomes. “At least 5.3 million Americans, 2% of the U.S. population, currently live with disabilities resulting from TBI” (https://www.braintrauma.org/tbi-faqs/ tbi-statistics/).

However, these figures are suspect as underreporting is prevalent. Fifty-six percent of patients who were not identified by an emergency department (ED) as having a MTBI were subsequently identified by interview as having a MTBI (Powell, Ferraro, Dikmen, Temkin, & Bell, 2008). The ED identification rate of MTBI was highest when there was a loss of consciousness and lowest for the symptom of confusion. The problem of underreporting of brain injury is also evident in the sports arena (McCrea et al., 2003) research, which reported that only 47% of concussions are reported by high school football players. Specific groups that are more vulnerable to the effects of a TBI include women, children, older adults, and individuals with a previous history of a concussion.

The “spontaneous” recovery period for the MTBI patient was originally thought to be 3–6 months following the injury, with persistent symptoms sometimes being perceived as psychological in origin (Binder, Rohling, & Larrabee, 1997; Kashluba, Hanks, Casey, & Millis, 2008) or malingering. Other research has indicated a 50% recovery of symptoms within the 3-month time frame (Dikmen, McLean, Temkin, & Wyler, 1986). Rates of 1–20% of patients with MTBI have been reported to have symptoms for more than 6 months (Beetar, Guilmette, & Sparadeo, 1996; Deb, Lyons, Koutzoukis, Ali, & McCarthy, 1999; Leininger, Gramling, Farrell, Kreutzer, & Peck, 1990; Levin & Eisenberg, 1991). However, other research (Veeramuthu et al., 2015) has reported continued neuropsychological deficits at the 6-month postaccident time in attention (57% of subjects), language (63% of subjects), memory (33%), spatial functioning (52%), and executive function (70%). A study from the United Kingdom (Thornhill et al., 2000) reported that of 2,995 young people and adults who sustained mild TBI, 1,400 subjects (47%) had disabilities after 1 year of the head injury.

However, long-term physical measurements counter some of the previous psychological and neuropsychological research. For example, the protein S-100B serum is a peripheral biochemical marker of neural injuries, including reactive gliosis, astrocytic death, and/or blood–brain barrier dysfunction (Wong, Rooney, & Bonser, 1999). In one study, 50% of the MTBI group had raised S-100B serum concentrations and showed significant neuropsychological deficits, as well as a twofold increase in severity of dizziness, memory problems, and headaches at the 6 months postinjury.

Other MTBI-sensitive physical measurement technologies involve the event-related potential (ERP), diffusion tensor imaging (DTI), functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI), and the quantitative EEG (QEEG). ERPs relate to the magnitude of brain’s electrical response pattern after a stimulus and are measured in milliseconds (msec). The response can involve several waves of different amplitudes and at different time delays from stimulus onset. In the patient with MTBI, the P300 has been shown to have lower amplitudes and increased time delay (from stimulus onset). The P300 is a positive wave occurring at about 300 msec after the stimulus and generally recorded in the parietal lobes. This negative finding has been related to the subjective symptoms reported by athletes with MTBI (Lavoie, Dupuis, Johnston, Leclerc, & Lassonde, 2004). Headaches and concentration problems were both negatively correlated with the amplitude of the P300 component (Gosselin, Theriault, Leclerc, Montplaisir, & Lassonde, 2006). Of particular importance is that this effect was not influenced by the length of time since injury (Lavoie et al., 2004). Thus, the concept of “spontaneous cure” does not find a correlate in physical measurements.

Other cross-sectional design research on the spontaneous cure question has reported similar findings. For example, it has been reported that the QEEG effects in a TBI (moderate to severe) population were not correlated with the time interval from the injury to the QEEG evaluation (Thatcher, Biver, McAlaster, & Salazar, 1998). Thornton (2000a) initiated the hypothesis that “time does not heal” in the MTBI case. In a group of 32 patients with mild to moderate TBI, Lexicor’s spectral correlation coefficient (SCC) and phase beta (32–64 Hz) deficit pattern did not correlate with the time since injury (17 days to 27 years, mean of 3 years, N = 88). Similarly, it has been reported in a DTI study that 19% (of 249 whitematter fiber bundles) indicated discontinuity on fiber tracking in a MTBI group but no significant relations between the time interval after injury and DTI results (Rutgers et al., 2008). DTI is a measure of the water diffusion alignment patterns.

The fractional anisotropy (FA) value measures the directional diffusivity of water, with a low FA reflecting a breakdown in the white matter. In conclusion, there is little evidence that the brain spontaneously cures the underlying physical damage.

If there is no spontaneous cure, then what does the brain do as a response to the brain injury? Evidence from fMRI studies indicates that the patient with TBI generates greater cognitive effort during the task than do normal controls to produce a similar level of performance (Braver et al., 1997). There is some indication that increased activity in the frontal lobes may be the correlate of this greater effort. In moderate to severe TBI patients (3 months postinjury) research has shown that there is increased frontal lobe activity (fMRI study) compared to a control group of patients with orthopedic injuries (OIs) on a cognitive task (Scheibel et al., 2007). The greater the severity of the TBI, the greater the frontal activation pattern, particularly in the subcortical structures. The degree of activation level, however, did not relate to success for the TBI group, but increased activation did correlate positively with the success within the OI group. Thus, the TBI group responds differently to a task than a normal group. Similarly, fMRI hyperactivation in a group of high school athletes assessed 1 week after injury predicted a longer recovery period than that for athletes without hyperactivation (Lovell et al., 2007). The patient with MTBI has demonstrated hypermetabolism (compared to controls) in midtemporal and frontal subcortical locations during a continuous performance test (CPT; 1–5 years postinjury, positron emission tomographic [PET] study; Gross, Kling, Henry, Herndon, & Lavretsky, 1996). A different pattern showing diffuse brain activation was found in athletes on memory and sensorimotor tasks relative to preconcussion baseline. The increased activations on the sensorimotor task were mostly located in the frontal–temporal areas, while the level of performance was unchanged (Jantzen, Anderson, Steinberg, & Kelso, 2004). The response pattern may also be considered a compensatory response. The effects of MTBI on working memory and simple reaction time were examined in an fMRI study of patients with MTBI and controls. The MTBI group had significantly increased activation compared to normative group during the high working memory load condition, particularly in the right parietal and right frontal (dorsolateral) regions. The MTBI group’s performance was slower in response speed on both the simple reaction time and distractibility tasks of the CPT (McAllister et al., 1999). In conclusion, the response pattern of the patient with TBI is characterized by increased (compared to patients in a normal group) activation levels, a larger number of locations involved, and right hemisphere activation.

# TBI and the QEE G

How the MTBI affects QEEG measures has been the focus of a number of investigations using eyes-closed data. The MTBI research employing discriminant function analysis has reported an accuracy rate of 90% and above in discriminating between MTBI (moderate and severe cases not included) and a normal group (Thatcher, Walker, Gerson, & Geisler, 1989). The discriminating variables included, in the MTBI patient, increased frontal theta coherence (Fp1–F3), decreased frontal beta (13–22 Hz) phase (Fp2–F4, F3–F4), increased coherence beta (T3–T5, C3–P3), and reduced posterior relative to power alpha (P3, P4, T5, T6, O1, O2, T4). The Thatcher et al. (1998) study used fMRI and QEEG eyes-closed data and concluded that gray matter lesions were related to decreased microvolts on alpha and beta bandwidths, and white matter lesions were correlated with increased microvolts in the delta bandwidth. Neuropsychological deficits were positively correlated with increased delta microvolts and negatively with a decreased alpha and beta EEG amplitudes. Research has also reported that the higher delta (1.0–3.5 Hz) microvolts were associated with lower functionality measures (Functional Independence Measure and Functional Assessment Measure) in a TBI group (Leon-Carrion, MartinRodriguez, Damas-Lopez, Martin, & DominguezMorales, 2008). There was a positive correlation between beta (12–30 Hz) coherence values and the functionality measures, which were both low in the TBI group. A review of numerous studies with the moderate (GCS score of 9–12) and severe (GCS of 4–8) TBI with eyes-closed data have shown increased theta and decreased alpha power (microvolts) and/or decreased coherence and symmetry deviations compared to normal groups (Hughes & John, 1999).

Although these reports present a pattern, there are several problems in this area, which include (1) use of a low-frequency range (32 Hz and below); (2) eyes-closed data; (3) lack of activation procedures; (4) empirically understanding exactly what QEEG variables relate directly to the poor cognitive performance of a patient with MTBI; and (5) differences in coherence formulations. Thornton (1999, 2000a, 2003) demonstrated that extending the frequency range to 64 Hz provides critical data regarding the dominant QEEG correlate of the MTBI patient’s cognitive problems (i.e., SCC and phase deficits in the 32–64 Hz range). The broad SCC and phase deficit is a more convincing pattern than Thatcher et al.’s (1998) pattern of increases and decreases in coherence and phase values in different locations. The report by Thornton and Carmody (2010) provides an analysis of the QEEG correlates of differences in mental states (eyes-closed, visual & auditory attention, auditory memory) and negates the assumption that eyes-closed data allow us to predict accurately how the mind operates in a task condition. The Thornton (2003) report presents the QEEG correlates of success and failure for the patient with MTBI. There are several hardware and software mathematical definitions of the coherence concept, which involves the similarity of waveforms between different locations within a frequency range. The relations between these definitions and between the respective values and cognitive effectiveness have not been fully explored. However, the relation among SCC (Lexicor Medical Technologies, 753 Broad Street, Augusta, GA), phase values, and cognitive success has been researched (Thornton, 2001).

In the Thornton (2002a) study, a flashlight metaphor was employed for the first time to describe SCC relationships. According to the metaphor, each location sends out a “beam” within a frequency to all other locations. The Thornton (1999, 2000a, 2003) studies indicated that there is a broad deficit in the flashlight SCC and phase values of the beta (32–64 Hz) band in the patient with MTBI, which is most prominent in frontal locations (Thornton, 2003) and between the homologous locations (F –F , P –P , etc.) which reflect the damage within the corpus callosum (Thornton & Carmody, 2009b). It is assumed that the SCC and phase values are tied to white matter activity, and that lowered coherence/SCC and phase values reflect the negative effects of a MTBI on the cortical–cortical white matter and/or the cortical–subcortical white matter (Destexhe, Contreras, & Steriade, 1998). Thornton (2003) also reported a right-hemisphere compensation pattern in patients with MTBI. In a normal population, the correlation between successful auditory recall (paragraphs) and the QEEG variables involve SCC alpha relationships predominantly in the left hemisphere (T3, F7, C3, P3) and right frontal (F ) locations. However, within the MTBI group, the positive correlations between recall performance and the QEEG variables involved the right temporal (T ) location phase beta and left frontal (F ) phase beta , indicating a shift to the right hemisphere and higher frequency. An additional analysis (unpublished) of the activation differences (listening to paragraphs vs. listening to a pen tapping) between the two groups (normal vs. MTBI) indicated that patients with MTBI significantly increased T SCC beta values to seven of the 18 other locations, while the normal group decreased these T SCC beta1 values, reflective of a compensatory approach.

Thornton and Carmody (2009b) provided further data on the MTBI patient’s QEEG brain response pattern during an auditory memory task. The comparison between the MTBI group and the normal group indicated that the MTBI group had higher frontal beta1, beta2 relative power values and decreased delta relative power values, as well as diffusely located decreased SCC and phase beta values, with decreased SCC alpha mostly in the left hemisphere during the input and immediate auditory recall tasks. When the normal and MTBI groups were combined, memory performance was negatively related to frontal beta activations and positively related to increased delta values and SCC and phase values. The Thornton and Carmody (2009b) results are the opposite of those of Thatcher et al. (1998), in which higher (than normal) values of delta were found in the TBI group and related to white matter damage, and lower values of beta related to gray matter damage. Thornton and Carmody (2009b) found higher values of beta in the MTBI group and lower values of delta. Thornton (2014) further examined these relationships and was able to obtain 100% accuracy in the discrimination of head injury from normal subjects by employing the coherence and phase values in the 32–64 Hz range and frontal relative power of beta2 (32–64 Hz) values.

Thornton and Carmody (2009a, 2009b) have proposed the coordinated allocation of resources (CAR) model of brain functioning. The model asserts that performance on any particular cognitive skill (auditory memory, reading memory, problem solving, etc.) is a function of the use of different electrophysiological variables (relative power, SCC, etc.) and locations. While different cognitive skills may employ similar QEEG variables and locations as other skills, each skill has an overall unique pattern of activation. This model is implicit in medical imaging technology studies that report location activation during cognitive tasks. However, these studies often do not indicate what is correlated with performance.

In specific relationship to MTBI, Thornton and Carmody (2009a) concluded that the main deficit in patients with MTBI is the lowered SCC and phase values in the beta (32–64 Hz) range, and these deficits could be effectively addressed with EEG biofeedback. Thus, although spontaneous cure does not repair the physical damage, EEG biofeedback can lead to functional improvement of the SCC deficit.

# EE G Biofeedback for T raumatic Brain I nj uries

The field of cognitive rehabilitation has been the traditional approach to the remediation of the cognitive problems in the patient with a TBI. However, the research frequently has not documented significant results. Thornton (2000b, 2002b) and Thornton and Carmody (2005) initially reported some case studies documenting improvements in auditory memory ability in the adult with MTBI and 20 children with reading disabilities and attention deficit disorder. Thornton and Carmody (2008) reported on improvements in auditory memory of 19 patients with MTBI. The intervention approach employed the CAR model of brain functioning. The results of different studies employing different cognitive intervention approaches (strategies, computer, medications, variations of EEG biofeedback) were examined with respect to effect size, confidence interval statistics, and cognitive task (attention, memory, and problem solving). Clinical recommendations were based on confidence intervals and standard deviation (SD) effect sizes. Most of the standard approaches (computers, strategies, medications) were not recommended due to nonexistent effects. Mild recommendations were concluded for medications and imagery for paragraph recall, computers for word lists, and EEG biofeedback for attention and problem solving. The only moderate recommendation was for the CAR-model-guided EEG biofeedback approach for auditory memory (paragraph recall). The interventions obtained a respectable 2.61 SD improvement in paragraph recall.

# A Case Exampl e of EE G E ffects

The following case example demonstrates the effectiveness of the CAR model of EEG biofeedback in an MTBI case. The patient was 14 years old when he enrolled in our program. The mother reported that when he was 8 years old, he was hit in the head (left frontal) by a baseball bat. He was not rendered unconscious but was in severe pain, with localized swelling and a deep cut of the left eyebrow bone area. The pain and discomfort continued for a week. He experienced a significant drop in grades after the accident.

The patient underwent an activation-guided QEEG evaluation. The procedure employs nine different cognitive tasks, which include three tasks for auditory memory, three tasks for reading memory, a problem-solving task (Raven’s matrices), visual and auditory attention, and an eyesclosed task. QEEG data are collected during all of the tasks. The auditory memory task requires the subject to listen to four short paragraphs separately, containing about 20–24 pieces of information. The QEEG is recorded during (1) the initial listening period, (2) the patient’s silent immediate recall of the paragraph, and (3) a silent recall period about 20 minutes later. The patient states out loud what he or she recalled during the silent period immediately following the silent recall period. Similarly, the QEEG is recorded during (1) one reading task (100 seconds), (2) the silent recall period of the reading material, and (3) during a delayed reading recall task. The data were examined for artifacts, which were marked for deletion by the software, compared to a normative database, and then examined on all available variables. The variables discussed here include what follows.

# Relative Power

The microvolts of the particular band are divided by the total microvolts generated by all bands at a location, averaged over the epoch time period. This measure is insensitive to skull thickness, as it measures the total microvolts at a location and determines the percentage of the total microvolts that are within a particular frequency range.

# Spectral Correlation Coefficient

The SCC is the average amplitude similarity between the waveforms of a particular band in two locations over an epoch. This variable is defined within a particular frequency range.

The analysis of the data revealed three central negative findings. The figures and tables present the pre- and posttreatment data. At the mother’s request, the patient underwent 139 sessions of EEG biofeedback during a 3-week period at a rate of eight sessions a day for 6–7 days a week. During the treatment, the individual was engaged in reading, listening to novels on a CD, and problem solving. The intervention problems focused on inhibiting delta and increasing beta (32–64 Hz) in posterior (T5, P3, Pz, P4, T6, O1, O2), frontal (F7, F3, Fz, F4, F8) and left temporal (T3) locations, as well as increasing SCC values in the alpha to beta2 frequency predominantly in left-hemisphere locations (T5, P3, O1, F7, F3, T3) as indicated by deficits in the evaluation. The negative findings were as follows:

1. Analysis of the raw EEG data indicated persistent but intermittent delta waves throughout the entire record of some 2500 epochs (epoch = 1 second). Figures 38.1 and 38.2 presents the visual display of the pre- and postevaluation data.

Figure 38.1 presents the pretreatment raw EEG data. The delta waves occurred every several seconds and lasted for several seconds throughout the entire evaluation. Figure 38.2 presents the typical epoch for the postevaluation data. The absence of the delta waves was quite evident throughout the entire sampling period. However, there was still some evidence of delta waves at T3 (three intervention sessions) and T4 (no intervention sessions).

![](images/fb5f85cb04f2f7053d8ef175b99b33d44e095b0d5fdc12ee2616171afb33f284.jpg)  
FI GURE 38.1. Pretreatment raw data.

![](images/5570f6ff2499148c84a50c12fa60f7b88795829a42c7e656b36f121012ce80a0.jpg)  
FI GURE 38.2. Posttreatment raw data.

The relative power values of delta in posterior locations presented in Table 38.1 reflect the changes between the pre- and posttreatment reading task (different content).

2. The subject was employing his right hemisphere for verbal tasks. Thornton (2006, International Society for Neuronal Regulation [ISNR] presentation) reported on data that indicated an increase in left posterior (T –P –O ) positive correlations between beta relative power values and reading memory as the age increased in the normative database. Initially the child employs right posterior locations (T –P –O ) and increases beta (13–32 Hz) relative power values to increase their memory score. As the child matures, there is a switch to left posterior beta activations to improve memory performance. However, the relative power values of beta and beta are about the same for left and right posterior locations for the child and adult normative sample. Due to either the MTBI or a developmental anomaly, this patient had higher right (vs. left) posterior beta levels, especially in the O –O locations (see Table 38.1). This imbalance suggested a right posterior preference for processing verbal information, which needed to be addressed and changed. The treatment was able to accomplish that task, as is evident in Table 38.1.

Table 38.1 indicates the reduction in the relative power values of delta, the increased values of beta1 and beta2, as well as the increased use of the left posterior region during reading. The left posterior increase is most evident in the beta1 frequency, particularly at the O1 and O2 locations where the gains were the largest, while beta remained at higher levels in the right posterior compared to the left posterior locations. One of the treatment goals was to increase the left posterior beta activations during reading.

3. The patient also demonstrated significant deficits in the coherence beta2 relationships, particularly under reading conditions, as indicated in Figure 38.3, which shows the SD difference of the patient from the control group during the reading task.

The values in the circles represent the subject’s SD difference from the normal group’s reading values. The values that increased 2 SD or more are indicated on the second line of head figures with a thick, black circle, while changes of 1 SD or more are indicated with a thinner black circle.

Figure 38.3 reflects significant improvements in the SCC of the beta frequency. Much of the treatment was directed toward increasing lefthemisphere SCC values. The approach appeared to have the desired result, as the greater increases were in the left hemisphere SCC relationships.

The patient’s auditory memory improved from 26 (initial evaluation average for immediate and delayed recall of four stories) to 36, and his reading memory score improved from 62 (initial evaluation) to 81.5. He also reported qualitative subjective changes, noting that he never knew he could “feel this good.”

TA BLE 38.1. Pre- and Posttreatment Relative Power Values during Reading Condition   
![](images/b4f4d790b9fe8c104428b6ef494630afe328d40afae6942ba350dde086c00bee.jpg)

![](images/178925a02bc4f1330d07e498d72169614a76172c2cf9b91dfefd9302345af212.jpg)  
FI GURE 38.3. Pre- and posttreatment spectral correlation beta2 SD values during reading.

# Concl usion

Patients with MTBI show a distinct difference from a normal sample in their QEEG response pattern, which is characterized by increased frontal beta activations, decreased SCC and phase relations between cortical locations, and a possible switch to right-hemisphere activity in response to task demands. EEG biofeedback guided by the CAR model of brain functioning has been shown to be an effective intervention for the auditory memory problems of the patient with MTBI. An EEG biofeedback example of a patient with MTBI demonstrates that the electrophysiology of the brain is very responsive to the operant conditioning methodology, and cognitive function can improve as a result of precision interventions.

# Biofeedback Interventions for Autism Spectrum Disorders

Lynda Thompson, Michael T hompson, James W. G. T hompson, and A ndrea R eid‑Chung

When working with autism spectrum disorders (ASD) a multimodal approach, including diet, medications, speech therapy, psychotherapy, behavior modification, and education, is typically used (Green et al., 2006). In the past 15 years, two new approaches have been added. These are electroencephalographic (EEG) biofeedback (BFB), also called neurofeedback (NFB), and peripheral BFB. BFB now includes heart rate variability (HRV) training. Both NFB and HRV training appear to have long-term effects when applied to other disorders. Examples are NFB for attentiondeficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) (Gani, Birbaumer, & Strehl, 2008) and HRV for stress management (Lehrer, 2007).

ASD includes both autism and Asperger syndrome (AS) (Asperger, 1944; Wing, 2001). Autism is a disorder of neurodevelopment resulting in pervasive abnormalities in social interaction, communication, and imagination, usually combined with repetitive behaviors and restricted interests. DSM-5 does not distinguish the criteria for autism and Asperger’s although the DSM-IV included Asperger’s disorder and had the main difference being that there are no significant delays in language development or cognitive development in Asperger’s disorder (American Psychiatric Association, 2000, 2013; Macintosh & Dissanayake, 2004; Simpson, 2004; Wing, 2001). DSM-5 mentions Asperger’s but not as a separate diagnostic category. The authors have used the term “Asperger’s syndrome” in their ADD Centre and in their publications because both the symptoms of this syndrome and the QEEG findings are distinct and different from classic autism. The reader will note that because the DSM-5 has discontinued the use of Asperger’s disorder as a separate disorder. When using the DSM-5 the diagnostic category is 299.0, Autism spectrum disorder without language impairment and without intellectual impairment. Language proficiency is a main feature of those with AS, in contrast to children with autism who have severe language limitations. High-functioning autism (HFA) can seem so close to AS that the two terms are often used almost interchangeably. In clinical experience, clients with AS are quite different from those with autism in terms of their emotional responsiveness and interest in others. The term “pervasive developmental disorder (PDD) has also been eliminated from the DSM-5. Such children are described well in older literature on childhood psychoses and autism (Thompson & Havelkova, 1983).

Patients with AS tend to talk about their own special interests too much and fail to read the other person’s nonverbal cues of boredom or annoyance. They may use pedantic phrases or a voice that is monotone and lacks prosody (intonation, loudness variation, pitch, rhythm). The latter symptoms may be termed a “motor aprosodia” (Ross, 1981) and, in the electroencephalogram (EEG), one may observe EEG differences from the normal database at the F6 electrode position of the expanded 10-20 electrode placement system (Chatrian, Lettich, & Nelson, 1985). F6 is over an area in the nondominant (right) frontal lobe that is the homologous site to F5, which is near Broca’s area in the left hemisphere. These frontal sites were the first areas identified as mirror neuron areas. In children with autism their EEG differences, as compared to a normative database, may be seen at both F3 and F4, which are the 10-20 sites closest to frontal mirror neuron areas. The resting state, eyes open EEG of a 10-year-old male client diagnosed with autism, is shown in Figure 39.1. It is a pattern that may also be seen in some clients with AS or with an anxiety disorder.

Anxiety is a key symptom in ASD. Some adults with AS are initially diagnosed with anxiety or panic disorder. Anxiety may be most apparent with any transition or change in routine. Using Low Resolution Electromagnetic Tomographic Assessment, usually shortened to LORETA, (Pascual-Marqui, Esslen, Kochi, & Lehmann, 2002), Brodmann area (BA) 24 in the anterior cingulate is often identified as one source of quantitative EEG (QEEG) findings that differ from a normative database (Figure 39.2). In our clients, this has usually been observed in the 3–8 Hz range and/ or 13–14 Hz and high-frequency beta above 20 Hz (Figure 39.3). In clients with an ASD, in addition to anxiety, this may correlate with other important symptoms, including poor modulation of emotions, inattention, and executive functioning difficulties. The Cz electrode site in children and FCz in adults have, at our center, been the primary sites for beginning NFB training in these clients because of hypothesized influence on the anterior cingulate, which lies below these sites, and because these sites are relatively artifact free and have been used successfully when conducting NFB with children who have attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder. (See Monastra & Lubar, Chapter 30, this volume).

# Neuroanatomical F indings

Right frontal and right parietal–temporal junction abnormalities may correlate with aprosodias, as mentioned; that is, deficient expression of emotion in voice, facial expression, and gestures (motor aprosodia) plus difficulty reading social cues, gestures, and tone of voice (sensory aprosodia). In addition, Shamay-Tsoory, Tomer, Berger, Goldsher, and Aharon-Peretz (2005) have hypothesized that prefrontal brain damage may result in impaired social behavior, especially when the damage involves the orbitofrontal and/or ventromedial areas of the prefrontal cortex (but not the dorsolateral areas in their research). These authors note that prefrontal lesions resulted in significant impairment in the understanding of irony and faux pas. In contrast to the patient with damage to the amygdala, who cannot correctly understand the significance of another person’s anger or aggressive behavior, the patient with orbitofrontal damage recognizes the significance of other people’s emotions but may fail to modulate his or her behavior as the social situation changes. This kind of impairment could lead to difficulty in correctly recognizing the intentions of others and thereby lead to inappropriate behavior (Bachevalier & Loveland, 2006). In their article, Bachevalier and Loveland posit that developmental dysfunction of the orbitofrontal–amygdala circuit is a critical factor in ASD. Imaging studies have shown differences, as compared to neurotypical children, in the density of gray matter at the junction of the amygdala, hippocampus, and entorhinal cortex. In our clients, LORETA used with the Neuroguide program consistently shows EEG abnormalities in these regions (L. Thompson, Thompson, & Reid, 2010; Neuroguide, 2007). The fusiform gyrus and the superior temporal lobe are noted in many articles to be involved in ASD, and Porges’s polyvagal theory specifically notes that these areas may not be appropriately inhibiting the central nucleus of the amygdala, which may lead to an increase in sympathetic drive and a decrease in myelinated vagus activity (Porges, 2004, 2007). Myelinated vagal activity is associated with a person feeling that he or she is in a safe environment. Both hearing (affected by the strapedius muscle in the middle ear) and facial expressiveness are influenced by vagal innervation, and Porges postulates that vagal dysfunction could therefore result in some of the symptoms observed in these clients, such as not listening appropriately and lack of facial expressiveness (Porges, 2003, 2004, 2007). The importance of noting these areas that are reported as deviant in the general literature on ASD is that clinical observations using EEG show these areas to be outside the database norms using LORETA (Thompson & Thompson, 2009, 2010, 2015b). Thus there is concordance between the neurofeedback literature, clinical experience using QEEG, and research on ASD using other imaging techniques, such as fMRI.

# Th eories for U nderstanding the Symp toms of A SD Supp ort NF B I nterventions

# The Mirror Neuron System

The mirror neuron system (MNS) is postulated to be involved in the imitation of movements, and perhaps in copying appropriate social interactions, as well as being critical to understanding and predicting the behavior of others. Important MNS areas in the left hemisphere (there are corresponding areas on the right) include frontal near F5, the temporal pole, the temporal–parietal junction, the anterior insula, and the anterior cingulate gyrus. Each area is postulated to have mirror functions that correspond to the functions of that area of the cortex. Mirror neurons have strong connections to the limbic system, including the anterior cingulate (Iacoboni & Dapretto, 2006). The cingulate and the insular cortices both contain mirror neuron cells (Ramachandran & Oberman,

2006). A functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) study demonstrated that activity of the MNS is correlated with empathic concern and interpersonal competence (Pfeifer, Iacaboni, Mazziatta, & Dapretto, 2008). It has also been shown that children with ASD have reduced activity in MNS regions during tasks that require the child to mirror facial expressions of different emotions (Dapretto et al., 2006). The reason for reminding the reader of this mirror neuron system is that, in children with ASD, with 19-channel QEEG and LORETA source locations, these areas are found to be the source of EEG activity that differs from that of children without ASD (> 2 SD from normative database means (Thompson & Thompson, 2009, 2010, 2015b).

Jaime Pineda and his colleagues (2008) reported on two studies using neurofeedback in children with autism. This work demonstrated more appropriate mu suppression after NFB. The intervention was based on results concerning lack of mu suppression in children with ASD when they viewed videos of children moving their fingers and hands. Training was done at C4 to increase what these authors termed “mu,” defined as 8–13 Hz and 10–13 Hz activity, respectively, in the two experiments in which the subjects were children with high-functioning ASD. They report improved facial recognition and it was posited that the training had an influence on the mirror neuron system in the right hemisphere. Mu, which is measured at C3 and C4 across the motor strip, is found in conjunction with motor quiescence and is attenuated when there is movement of the contralateral hand, or thinking about moving one’s hand, or watching someone else moving his or her hands. (Whether the training was to increase mu, alpha, SMR, or some combination of those brain waves is an interesting question for research, because mu was identified not by its morphology or reactivity but by its frequency range and location in this research.)

# Theory of Mind

Theory of mind (ToM; which is sometimes more accurately called “theory of others’ minds”; Hill & Frith, 2003) involves the ability to “mentalize about both the self and others” (Abu-Akel, 2003). This model implicates the posterior brain (parietal and temporal regions) in representational thinking, the prefrontal regions for the application and execution of ToM, in addition to the medial prefrontal cortex (anterior paracingulate cortex), the temporal–parietal junction, and the temporal poles. ToM proposes that a fault in any component of these aspects of the social brain can lead to an inability to understand aspects of social communication. Intuitive understanding of others, especially understanding what they are feeling or thinking, has always been understood to be a core deficit of those with ASDs (Thompson & Havelkova, 1983). The reader will note that these are also areas referred to in the previous discussion of mirror neurons. The amygdala is also implicated (Adolphs, 2003; Adolphs et al., 2005) and the reader will see the overlap here with the salience landscape theory (Ramachandran & Oberman, 2006). They also mention findings of less connectivity between the occipital and temporal regions. Abnormal connectivity between these regions is a finding that we observe in the EEG with these subjects using coherence analysis (a measure of phase-locked connectivity) (see Figure 39.4).

# Weak Central Coherence

The weak central coherence theory seeks to explain some symptoms that are not subsumed under the previously discussed ToM. These include the need for “preservation of sameness” and also the special interests and talents of those with AS. Children with ASD seems to be flooded with inconsequential details and/or memories, without grasping the context or the Gestalt. They get stuck on details and do not see the big picture. Defensive behaviors may include rigidity, repetitive movements, and obsessive and even perseverative behaviors. Weak central coherence probably involves a lack of appropriate connectivity between areas of the brain. “Connectivity” in this discussion refers particularly to connections between the posterior sensory processing areas of the brain (including lingual gyrus) and the frontal areas that modulate responses to the sensory input (“top-down” modulation). One cause of this deficit could be a failure of normal developmental “pruning” in early life that eliminates certain brain connections and optimizes the coordination of neural functioning (Hill & Frith, 2003). Resulting perceptual overload in turn may theoretically be partly responsible for “autistic” withdrawal. A reasonable, researchable hypothesis is that our findings of gross EEG coherence abnormalities with clients who have an ASD (Figure 39.4) and our observations of symptomatic improvements that accompanies normalization of these coherence abnormalities correspond to the weak central coherence theory of Hill & Frith. In addition to coherence training, we always increase sensorimotor rhythm (13–15 Hz). We hypothesize that once the thalamus is properly gating the incoming sensory information (the result of sensorimotor rhythm [SMR] training changing thalamocortical firing patterns), the client with ASD may be less overwhelmed by sensory stimuli.

# Executive Dysfunction

This third cognitive theory was advanced to explain features that do not appear to be subsumed under the former two theories. Executive functioning (including attention, planning, inhibition, and mental flexibility) appears to be impaired in those with ASD. The Tower of London (ToL) test (1982) can evaluate many aspects of executive functioning, and those with an ASD score poorly on this test. The ToL involves 10 trials of changing the position of colored rings, placing them on three pegs to copy a pattern shown by the examiner. It requires the subject to inhibit immediate responses, plan, shift mental set, use working memory, initiate a thought-out response, then monitor and evaluate the results of that response. The required cognitive functions all depend on good prefrontal functioning. Part of this may be the dorsolateral prefrontal cortex near F5, an area seen to be outside EEG database norms in many of our clients with ASDs. Improvement in performance on ToL has been reported in children with AS who received neurofeedback training (Knezevic, Thompson, & Thompson, 2010). Another interesting study carried out at the ADD Centre examined the effect of NFB on children’s emotions after reading either a happy or an unhappy story. After NFB training, patients with Asperger’s syndrome responded like the neurotypical control group and rated affect as more positive after reading a happy passage (Martinez, 2003). We hypothesize that the improvements observed in these studies could be related to our training over the anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) at Cz and FCz because the ACC influences both the Affect and Executive networks (Thompson & Thompson, 2015a). The ACC has numerous connections, including links to the left dorsolateral frontal cortex (between F3 and F7 in an area that also was noted earlier to contain mirror neurons). Improvement in executive functions using Cz placement for NFB to decrease 4–8 Hz and increase 12–15 Hz has been reported by Kouijzer, de Moor, Gerrits, Congedo, and van Shie (2009). They measured NFB improvements using the ToL and the Milwaukee Card Sorting Tests (Osmon & Suchy, 1996).

![](images/04c636f16a99eb12d455f862108df1c1b094bf29ef2491906862c516b9a0a05e.jpg)  
FI GURE 39.4. Linked Ears montage eyes closed for DU, an autistic male, age 10 years, 9 months. Dark lines indicate > 3 SD from Neuroguide database normal. This figure shows hypercoherence for the most part between anterior and posterior sites. In contrast, the hypocoherence is between hemispheres. It further shows hypercoherence, with the exception of beta 12–25 Hz hypocoherence F3 to F8 between the two hemispheres.

# Polyvagal Theory

Stephen Porges (2003, 2004, 2007) has noted that flat facial expression, poorly modulated tone of voice, and poor listening skills are related to the neural pathways that regulate the striated muscles of the face and head, and that reduced muscle tone in this circuit correlates with less expressiveness in voice and face, less eye contact (eyelids droop), and slack middle ear muscles (strapedius) that make distinguishing human voices from background noise more difficult. In addition, Porges has discussed the neurophysiological interactions between what he terms the “social engagement system” and the hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal (HPA) axis, the neuropeptides of oxytocin and vasopressin, and the immune system. He has noted that training of myelinated vagal tone should be helpful in the ASDs (Porges, 2003). We would add that this BFB “bottom-up” training synergistically combines with our NFB “top-down” training potentially to decrease a key symptom of anxiety, in addition to other symptoms observed in ASD, an idea that we presented as a systems theory of neural synergy at the International Society for Neurofeedback and Research (ISNR) annual conference in 2008. NFB training over the ACC, which influences the entire limbic system and the brainstem nuclei, may also affect the autonomic system through feedback loops to the insula. (The right insula relates mainly to sympathetic and the left to parasympathetic functions.) (Devinsky, Morrell, & Vogt, 1995). We hypothesize that HRV training or, in the early days, just teaching effortless, diaphragmatic breathing, may have been important in our success with clients along the autistic spectrum. Porges’s polyvagal theory supports our rationale for adding relaxation-oriented biofeedback to neurofeedback (Gevirtz, 2010).

# Neurofeedback T raining

As noted earlier, our understanding of the functional significance of different areas of the brain, as elucidated in the small booklet that correlates Brodmann areas to 10-20 sites (Thompson, Thompson, & Wu, 2008) and in the text-book, Functional Neuroanatomy Organized with Reference to Networks, Lobes of the Brain, 10-20 Sites, and Brodmann Areas (Thompson & Thompson, 2015a), corresponds to theories concerning brain dysfunction in those with ASD, as outlined earlier. Doing a 19-channel QEEG assessment, combined with a stress assessment (used with adolescents and adults) of psychophysiological variables, has led to interventions that combine EEG feedback with other biofeedback modalities. Normalizing the EEG usually involves using one- or twochannel NFB training, such as training at FCz (to influence the ACC) and at T6 (to decrease the sensory aprosodia symptoms). The ACC influences the entire limbic system, the amygdala– hypothalamic–pituitary–adrenal (AHPA) axis and the autonomic nervous system. It also links to frontal areas of the cortex influencing attention and executive functioning. Training at the Cz and FCz sites is hypothesized to influence the ACC and its affective, executive, and attentional functional networks. The connections from the ACC to functionally corresponding areas of the basal ganglia and thalamic neuron groups would then be involved in feedback loops affecting functionally related cortical areas. This may help explain why good results were achieved with most clients with training at a single site. Usually slow frequencies (2–5 Hz, 3–7 Hz, or 4–8 Hz, depending on which frequency range was highest on the individual client’s initial EEG profile) and high-frequency beta (20–35 Hz) are reduced, while low-frequency beta and even low alpha may be either increased or decreased depending on the QEEG assessment findings. However, the frequency bands and the decision to enhance or inhibit are based on the QEEG and on observing a correlation between the client’s symptoms and the targeted frequency band. We must also take into account that many of our clients have biofeedback training to encourage effortless diaphragmatic breathing and, more recently, HRV training. The vagal feedback through the medulla (nucleus solitarius) to the thalamus and the limbic system (including the hypothalamus and the anterior cingulate gyrus) could theoretically be an additional important factor in the positive outcomes seen at our center. The combination of NFB affecting cortex–basal ganglia–thalamocortical networks, with peripheral BFB augmenting the NFB effects on these functional networks, fits our systems theory of neural synergy (Thompson & Thompson, 2009).

In Figure 39.1, note spindling synchronous beta at F4, FP2 (25 Hz, 5 SD), and F7 (20 Hz, 3.4 SD) and high-amplitude slow wave at T6. The general pattern shown by this child is one that frequently is found in people with anxiety disorders and ASD (autism): excessive high amplitude slow wave activity, a dip in amplitude in the low alpha range (8–10 Hz), and higher amplitude than expected beta. In particular, there is high amplitude 2–5 Hz, and high amplitudes at various frequencies between 12–36 Hz. The specific frequency ranges and sites vary from person to person. In general, the very high delta and theta is anterior (frontal). One may also see high-amplitude low-frequency activity in the upper theta range of lower alpha range, at 7 or 8 or 9 Hz at frontal and central sites in patients who have Asperger’s syndrome, but this is not found in autism, which is characterized by extremely low-amplitude activity in the lowerfrequency alpha range of 8–10 Hz (often –1 to –2 SD) and seen especially at posterior locations (Pz). This low amplitude alpha is a difference found in patients diagnosed as autistic but is not usually found in Asperger’s syndrome.

A function that correlates with alpha oscillations is disengagement from processing sensory information that is not relevant. Some brains, such as those in people with ASD, do not disengage appropriately—they are overloaded by sensory information and, rather than high-amplitude alpha oscillations, we see higher amplitudes in the beta range. In addition to biofeedback (HRV) and increasing SMR at C4, these EEG findings suggest that interventions should focus on training at FCz and Cz to decrease low-frequency delta–theta and high-frequency beta (may be spindling beta with source derivation in the anterior cingulate gyrus). In patients who are diagnosed as autistic and who have the low-amplitude, low-frequency alpha in the QEEG assessment, the practitioner may add NFB at Pz to increase low-frequency alpha while being careful to avoid dissociation occurring if there is an increase in amplitude of 9 Hz activity.

As noted earlier, EEG training can be combined with BFB, usually HRV training (Lehrer, 2007). We usually increase SMR (12–15 Hz), which may have a stabilizing effect on a cortex that is unstable and easily kindled (Sterman, 2000). When enhancing SMR, it is important to identify a site on the sensory motor strip where there is no highamplitude spindling beta in the 13–15 Hz range. In addition to the decreased activation observed at T6, another factor that may, in the future, prove to be a helpful “marker” for ASD could be the “mu” rhythm response. In ASD, there is evidence of a reduction in mu rhythm suppression during action observation (Oberman et al., 2005). In our experience, however, mu is not observed in the majority of clients. Therefore using this as a training parameter for NFB, as suggested in an article in Scientific American (Ramachandran & Oberman, 2006), would not be our initial approach. Abnormalities in coherence are commonly found in those with ASD (Figure 39.4), and training for normalizing connectivity between the parietal lobes and the temporal and frontal regions may prove to be beneficial. Coben (2005) has reported on normalizing coherence using single-channel sequential training of the sites found to be deviant on QEEG analysis. Sequential (bipolar) training may have an influence on the phase relationship between EEG activity in the targeted frequency ranges at the two sites. We have observed normalization of coherence after amplitude training. At the ADD Centre we have added LORETA Z-Score NFB (LNFB) in cases where the child can tolerate having the 19-channel cap on during training, and it is our impression that LNFB is quite effective for coherence and phase training between relevant Brodmann areas in these patients (Thompson & Thompson, 2015b). How effective will require more research. Theoretically, LORETA Z-score training may be a promising approach that will reduce the number of sessions required for improving functioning. However, not all findings outside database norms are pathological, so these clients must be carefully watched to make sure the therapist is not inadvertently training the client to normalize compensatory sites that perhaps should be outside the database norms, as is explained in The Neurofeedback Book: Second Edition (Thompson & Thompson, 2015b).

In LORETA neurofeedback (LNFB), the z-score variations are calculated using a comparison of the patient’s EEG data with a normative database. The Brodmann areas of interest for a particular patient are chosen by matching that individual’s symptoms (rated for severity) to BAs that are known from the literature to relate to those functions. The continuous neurofeedback display usually comprises bar-graphs, animations or movies, plus line-graphs of time versus Z-scores at each site. Thus, the display reflects changes in the Z-score deviations from the database at all (or a percentage of) the chosen BA locations. In this manner, the patient is rewarded for maintaining brain wave activity within a designated Z-score range (say, –1.5 to +1.5 SD) at all the designated BA sites, also called regions of interest (ROIs), that are important for that patient.

The advantage over traditional NFB is increased specificity of the training and the possibility to train activity at locations not otherwise identifiable by conventional surface EEG (Thompson & Thompson, 2015b). For further information on details of how LNFB is done, the reader is referred to a book by Robert Thatcher (2012).

Transcranial direct current stimulation (tDCS) is being used with some patients who show very low activation in a defined area of the cortex that is relevant to their symptoms. For example, with difficulties with verbal comprehension and a finding of high theta and low 15–18 Hz beta over Wernicke’s area at the left temporal-parietal junction, the practitioner might consider placing the anode over this site. tDCS involves the application of very small direct electrical current (DC) of about 1 to 2 milliamperes (mA) between an anode (saline-soaked pad of about 25 to 38 cm2) and a cathode (saline-soaked pad of about 38 cm2). The anode and cathode can be placed either at two sites on the scalp or one site on the scalp and another site on the body, such as the opposite shoulder. The anode (positive) stimulation will result in excitation of the underlying superficial cortical neurons (but the opposite for neurons deep in the sulci). The tDCS is applied for a short period of time—perhaps 8 to 10 minutes—prior to doing NFB. The application of the anode over a cortical area will result in depolarization of the pyramidal cells making them more likely to fire and thus increase negativity (activation) of the cortex.

Stimulation with tDCS is just done with selected clients. The more universal adjunctive approaches used at the ADD Centre relate to diet, sleep, exercise, and parenting techniques. Once those have been optimized, often using outside resources, such as a nutritionist-dietician with expertise in special needs children, we then prescribe training that combines NFB and BFB and metacognitive strategies. This will initially address the symptoms that interfere with the child being able to interact constructively with caregivers, including, in the following order: anxiety, impulsivity, attention span, executive functions, and finally, understanding and responding to social interactions. The trainers must model a calm, relaxed, focused, concentration directed both to the child (almost telepathically) and the feedback screen. The trainers may use minimal verbalization in the early sessions if the child has autism. The keystone to the client’s difficulties is anxiety, so this is addressed first. We begin by decreasing anxiety using HRV (or just tummy breathing in young children or those who cannot have a respiration belt and a pulse sensor on without fidgeting with them). This is combined with NFB focused usually over the ACC, though beta spindling at other sites, such as the right frontal cortex, may also be addressed. If the child is not at a level to receive biofeedback for HRV, he or she is just taught diaphragmatic breathing (“tummy breathing”) or hand warming. This is combined with SMR training, often at C4, which also helps to stabilize the cortex, decrease impulsivity, and calm the client. In addition to affect modulation, training at a central location (FCz or Cz) will also help to increase attention span and concentration, and improve executive functions. The literature now supports the efficacy of NFB for symptoms relating to attention span (Arns, De Ritter, Strehl, Breteler, & Coenen, 2009) and the long-term effects of NFB for ADHD (Gani, Birbaumer, & Strehl, 2008).

As sessions progress and the client becomes relaxed, calm, and focused, the NFB is combined with cognitive and social strategies that place more emphasis on executive functions and social awareness. The latter may involve some training near T6 (activation) and in the mirror neuron areas such as F5. Coherence abnormalities that remain after amplitude training has been done would then be addressed. When LNFB is used, amplitude, coherence, and phase deviations are addressed simultaneously.

Some clinicians have reported on the emergence of difficult behaviors when providing treatment based purely on QEEG Z-score findings. We have observed that a small number of patients may appear to regress in the early stages of NFB treatment. Two possible reasons need consideration. First, in children with abnormal development, deviant amplitude and coherence Z-scores might, in part, reflect compensatory mechanisms. Thus care should be taken when attempting to “normalize” QEEG findings. Second, the child with autism has arrested development. Treatment allows these children to begin to progress through the normal stages of development that should have been negotiated at an earlier age. As these children move through the equivalent of rapprochement, they may enter what has been termed an “aggressive– depressed” stage (Thompson, 1990; Thompson & Patterson, 1986). The child may begin to test limits. At this juncture the caregivers must be careful not to reverse the child’s forward movement in development. The caregiver, while carefully setting appropriate limits, should reinforce the child’s sense of independence while still meeting his or her needs for dependence. These children may be going through what is commonly called “the terrible twos” but at a much later age, which makes their behavior more difficult to deal with because they are much bigger and stronger, even more determined, and emotionally vulnerable (higher anxiety). Thus, as the child moves forward in stages of separation–individuation, he or she appears to be acting out but is really exploring autonomy, power, and control in the world. Rather than “put the child down,” you should join in, then redirect. This general principle applies equally to moving the child from the waiting room to the NFB training situation as it does to helping the child shift activities in the home or other school and social situations. The practitioner (or the parent) joins in what the child is doing, then introduces what the two of them are now going to do together. It is important to meet the child’s dependency needs while allowing some independence and control.

Remember that NFB is not a stand-alone intervention. Other interventions are typically being used in conjunction with NFB + BFB, such as speech and language training, dietary approaches, special educational placements, and other behavioral training. At the Comprehensive Neuroscience Center in Menlo Park, New Jersey, significant improvements have been documented in children with autism after hyperbaric oxygen treatments, and Dr. James Neubrander, head of the center’s clinic, then added neurofeedback training to use a combined approach. For each individual client, the right mix of interventions has to be created for optimal improvement.

# Concl usion

Over the past 15 years, a few papers and presentations about interventions using NFB for clients with AS have appeared (Coben, 2005, 2006, 2007; Jarusiewicz, 2002; Linden, 2006; Pineda et al., 2008; Reid, 2005; Solnick, 2005; Thompson & Thompson, 1995, 2003, 2006, 2007a, 2007b, 2009, 2010, 2015b). These articles all note favorable clinical outcomes in case series, some with a large number of cases, such as 159 clients reported on at the Biofeedback Foundation of Europe annual meeting in 2007 (Thompson & Thompson 2007a) and later published (Thompson, Thompson, Thompson, & Reid, 2010). Of particular interest for NFB providers are findings that EEG differences, augmented by LORETA analysis of QEEG data, identify many of the key areas reported in the literature to be abnormal in those with ASD (Thompson & Thompson, 2010). The neuroanatomical differences reported in numerous studies parallel EEG differences and provide an impetus for systematic investigation of the question of whether correcting amplitude, phase, and connectivity differences (identified through comparisons with a normative database) can lead to normalization of symptoms in people who have diagnoses along the autism spectrum. It may also be discovered that, in some clients with symptoms of ASD, some of these differences from the normal database actually represent positive compensatory mechanisms. Other interesting research questions involve the efficacy of combining NFB with BFB. The latter approach has research support from Stephen Porges, who has posited a relationship between vagal nerve activation and symptoms observed in ASD. There does appear to be a strong rationale for combining NFB and BFB, particularly HRV training, because both promote calmness and better self-regulation, which are preconditions for successful social interactions.
