# Brain–Computer Communication

An Alternati ve Communi cati on Channel for Paralyzed Pati ents

Tobias K aufmann, Niels Birbaumer, and A ndrea K übler

In the third edition of this book, we introduced the Thought Translation Device (Kübler, Winter, & Birbaumer, 2003), that is, a brain–computer communication system based on self-regulation of slow cortical potentials (SCP). By regulating their SCP amplitude, users were able to control a cursor on a computer screen. The German alphabet was split into two subsets. One subset was presented at the top of the screen (top target), and the other, at the bottom (bottom target). Communication was established by steering the cursor to the top target (up-regulation of SCP amplitude; selection of character subset 1) or to the bottom target (downregulation of SCP amplitude; selection of character subset 2). After each selection, the selected subset was split again in two and the procedure reiterated until a single character was selected. The system proved its high value as communication was established with severely disabled persons who were not able to use a muscle-driven communication device (Birbaumer et al., 1999; Kübler et al., 2001).

Since the first brain–computer communication by patients in the so-called locked-in syndrome (LIS; Birbaumer et al., 1999), studies with the aim of establishing more rapid and reliable brain– computer interface (BCI)-driven communication almost exploded (Wolpaw & Wolpaw, 2012a). Different components of the electroencephalogram (EEG) have been tested as input channels for

BCI-based communication with severely paralyzed patients, the most prominent of which were sensorimotor rhythms (Neuper, Müller, Kübler, Birbaumer, & Pfurtscheller, 2003; Kübler et al., 2005) and event-related potentials (ERPs; e.g., Sellers & Donchin, 2006; Piccione et al., 2006; Nijboer et al., 2008; for review, see Kleih et al., 2011; Mak et al., 2011). Taking into account the current state of the art, the ERP-based BCI has won the race in terms of reliability and speed. Although it does not imply a traditional neurofeedback approach, it uses externally evoked brain activity as input and, most importantly, it is the only BCI that has been implemented to control devices for activities of daily living and placed in commercial standard software for assistive communication (Zickler et al., 2011). For a review of the current state of the art on brain–computer interfacing, see Wolpaw and Wolpaw (2012b). Thus, in this chapter we introduce the ERP-based BCI for communication in the locked-in state. (As in other chapters, italics on first use of a term indicate that the term is included in the Glossary at the chapter’s end.)

# Th e Locked‑In Syndrome

Patients with diseases leading to severe or total motor paralysis are unable to communicate their needs and feelings (either orally or by keyboard). Such patients are in the LIS (Plum & Posner, 1966; for review, see Smith & Delargy, 2005), a condition described impressively by Jean-Dominique Bauby in his famous book The Diving Bell and the Butterfly (Bauby, 1997); Bauby developed the syndrome after a stroke in the brainstem. Usually sensory and cognitive functions remain intact. Hemorrhage in the anterior brainstem (mainly in the ventral parts of pons cerebri) or nonhemorrhagic stroke in the ventral pons can cause the LIS, which includes tetraplegia and paralysis of cranial nerves (Patterson & Grabois, 1986).

In “classic” LIS, vertical eye movements as well as eyeblinks remain intact, whereas in “total” LIS, patients lose all abilities to move and communicate (Bauer, Gerstenbrand, & Rumpl, 1979; Hayashi & Kato, 1989). Although LIS is usually caused by pontine lesions, it has also been observed after lesions in other brain regions (Chia, 1984). Tumors, encephalitis, and brain injuries localized in the ventral midbrain may also result in LIS. Other causes of total motor paralysis are degenerative neuromuscular diseases, the most frequent being amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS). This disease involves a continuously progressive degeneration of central and peripheral motor neurons. Most often, paresis begins with the lower extremities, then moves to the hands and arms, finally paralyzing breathing and swallowing, as well as facial muscles. At the final stage, patients can stay alive only with artificial feeding and ventilation (de Carvalho & Swash, 2011).

Quality of life and the will to live in paralyzed patients depend essentially on the maintenance of communication (Bach, 1993; Lulé et al., 2009). To provide these patients with a means of communication independent of voluntary muscle control, BCIs have been developed.

# BCI s Based on ER Ps

ERPs are signals in the human EEG that occur in response to presentation of stimuli (for review, see Polich, 2007). Such stimuli can be presented in different modalities (i.e., visual, auditory, or tactile). Farwell and Donchin (1988) suggested using visually evoked ERPs for BCI-based communication. Users were presented with a 6 × 6 matrix containing letters from the alphabet. Rows and columns of the matrix were flashed in random order, and users were instructed to count the number of flashes of the letters they intended to spell. Such system implements a socalled oddball paradigm; that is, a relevant stimulus (the target letter; the odd) is presented among a plethora of irrelevant stimuli (all other letters). Relevant stimuli elicit prominent ERPs that can be detected from the event-related EEG. Hence, conclusion about the intended letter can be drawn from classification of ERPs. A prominent ERP typically elicited in such oddball paradigm is the P300, a positive deflection that occurs around 300 ms poststimulus at centro-parietal electrode sites (Sutton, Braren, Zubin, & John, 1965; for review, see Polich, 2007). The previously described BCI is therefore often referred to as P300-BCI or P300-Speller. Herein, we refer to it as ERP-BCI, due to its usual dependence on different ERPs, not just the P300 (e.g., Kaufmann, Hammer, & Kübler, 2011a).

# Hardware and Software Setup

Figure 40.1 displays schematically the typical arrangement of an ERP-BCI setup; Figure 40.2A shows a person using the system. ERPs are usually recorded from central, parietal, and occipital electrodes, as P300 can be obtained best from centro-parietal electrodes (Picton, 1992), whereas occipital electrodes contribute with negative potentials (e.g., N200; Blankertz, Lemm, Treder, Haufe, & Müller, 2011). Different electrode setups (number of channels, positions) have been compared in terms of BCI accuracy (i.e., correctly selected letters). Usually, sufficient accuracy can be reached with eight or more electrodes (e.g., Hoffman, Vesin, Ebrahimi, & Diserens, 2008). Figure 40.2B displays a suitable configuration. Signals are recorded from either passive or active Ag/AgCl wet electrodes. Dry electrodes are under development and display great potential in terms of applicability (for review, see Mak et al., 2011). For passive, wet electrodes, the skin needs to be prepared with abrasive electrode gel underneath the electrode to ensure that electrode impedances remain low (usually below 5 kW).

Signals are amplified with an EEG amplifier (e.g., g.USBamp from g.Tec Medical Engineering GmbH, Austria; or BrainAmp from BrainProducts GmbH, Germany) and digitized at a sampling rate of typically 256 or 512 Hz. There are several BCI software implementations freely available (e.g., BCI2000 software; Schalk, McFarland, Hinterberger, Birbaumer, & Wolpaw, 2004; www. bci2000.org) and OpenVibe (Renard et al., 2010; http://openvibe.inria.fr).

![](images/9667ccc09a2e8035931055ef3b55b7e2e133e6a15d8fc17b1d1a231eb10364c0.jpg)  
FI GURE 40.1. Schematic depiction of a typical brain–computer interface. Electrical signals are acquired from the brain. After digitizing and filtering, informative features are extracted and translated into a command (e.g., selection of a letter from the alphabet).

In the case of visual stimulation, a computer screen is placed approximately 70–100 cm in front of the user and displays a matrix consisting of numbers, letters, commands or even word suggestions of a predictive text entry system (Kaufmann, Völker, Gunesch, & Kübler, 2012). Auditory stimulation can be performed with either ear phones (e.g., Halder et al., 2010) or speakers placed around the user (e.g., Schreuder, Rost, & Tangermann, 2011). Tactile stimulation utilizes small stimulation units placed around the waist (e.g., Brouwer & Van Erp, 2010) or other body locations (e.g., on participants’ fingertips; van der Waal, Severens, Geuze, & Desain, 2012).

![](images/f685077e460cc0248cfae3cf5986c133989e0a59bc0b3fbadf2cb80043ebb8ab.jpg)  
FI GURE 40.2. (A) A patient with ALS using an ERP-BCI spelling system. (B) Potential electrode placement. Centro-parietal electrodes cover the area of the P300 potential, whereas occipital electrodes cover the area of the N200 potential.

# Steps for Mastering an ERP‑BCI

All steps are explained for visual ERP-BCIs. However, most of the explanations transfer well to auditory or tactile ERP-BCIs.

# Step 1: Instruction

It is important that users become familiar with the paradigm and task before the calibration session (Step 2) is started. Instructions can be presented verbally or in written form. However, it is recommended to use the running BCI paradigm along with verbal instruction, so users can immediately see how the characters are displayed in the matrix, the stimulation procedure, and the task of counting the flashes of the intended character.

# Step 2: Calibration Session

Before the system is able to classify ERPs from the user’s EEG signals, individual EEG data need to be acquired in a calibration session. From this data, a classifier can be set up in Step 3. Usually, the calibration session is performed with one or two short words. For convenience, the word should be displayed to the user (e.g., on top of the matrix). Also, it is recommended to display separately the current target character to be spelled. Figure 40.3 gives an example of a typical matrix display.

A typical calibration session starts with presentation of the matrix, calibration word, and current target character (first character of the calibration word) presented on a screen. A brief message (e.g., “Waiting to start. Please focus on the first character”) announces the beginning of the session, and the user focuses on the current target character in the matrix. Then, all characters are flashed with a high number of stimulation sequences (e.g., 15 sequences) and the user counts the number of intensifications. Usually a sequence consists of all rows and all columns flashed once, but other flash patterns have also been introduced. Importantly, all stimulations are triggered and stored along with the acquired EEG signals. Consequently, these triggers can be extracted later on in Step 3 for detection of ERPs. After each stimulation cycle (in our earlier example, after 15 sequences), the current character changes to the next character in the word, and the user shifts attention to this character in the matrix. These cycles continue until the end of the calibration word. The user may then take a break while calibration data are analyzed.

![](images/ec903eb470889b119b7a39afb2dbfc505414af3e7dde79106ac702c9e986c027.jpg)  
FI GURE 40.3. Typical display for a calibration session with an ERP-BCI–based communication application. On top of the screen, the calibration word (in this case “BRAINPOWER”) as well as the current character to spell (in this case the character “B”) is presented. The rest of the screen covers a matrix of characters that are available for selection in the spelling application. Stimuli are presented, for example, by highlighting each row and column of the matrix in random order. By focusing attention on the intended character, users count the number of character flashes. Each flash will then elicit ERPs that can be detected from the acquired brain signals. By analyzing user-specific ERP responses, it is possible to identify the intended characters.

# Step 3: Offline Analysis

Offline Analysis can be performed using Matlab (The Mathworks, USA) or precompiled tools (e.g., P3Classifier for BCI2000 data).

First, timing of all target character triggers and nontarget character triggers is obtained from the continuous recordings. For example in a 6 × 6 matrix flashed with 15 sequences (row/columnwise) and a calibration word of 10 characters, the amount of target versus nontarget triggers are:

where RT is the row target, CT is the column target, RNT are the row nontargets, CNT are the column nontargets, SEQ are the sequences, and

CHAR are the characters. From Equations 40.1 and 40.2, it is evident that the ERP-BCI constitutes an oddball paradigm: stimulation of rare but relevant targets within a plethora of irrelevant nontarget stimuli.

Average ERPs can be computed, when averaging all EEG signals following a target trigger and separately averaging all EEG signals following a nontarget trigger. This process is performed for each electrode separately. Figure 40.4A gives an example of such average at electrode Cz for a person with ALS, displaying a typical P300 in response to target stimuli. Figure 40.4B displays the average ERPs for all EEG channels (in this case, 12 channels at positions Fz, FC1, FC2, C3, Cz, C4, P3, Pz, P4, O1, Oz, O2).

Next, a classifier is trained to identify the presence or absence of ERPs. In the following spelling sessions, this allows for identification of the characters intended by the user. Many classification methods have been introduced to ERP-BCIs (for review, see Kleih et al., 2011), among which StepWise Linear Discriminant Analysis (SWLDA) is most commonly used (Farwell & Donchin, 1988; Krusienski et al., 2006; for details of the procedure, see Krusienski et al., 2008). During the process of feature selection, a SWLDA classification model is built by step by step, adding spatiotemporal EEG features (i.e., signal times and channel locations) and scoring their contribution to classification (i.e., in every step, all spatiotemporal features that are in the model at this step are reviewed to find those that contribute most to classification). Depending on their contribution to classification (i.e., on the achieved scores), features can then remain or be removed from the model. Those features that remain in the model will be checked in the next iteration, and so forth, until no iteration improves the model any further. Consequently, the final model comprises those features that contribute best to classification accuracy. Finally, corresponding feature weights are computed for all spatiotemporal features that remained in the model and are entered into the BCI software for online classification in Steps 4 and 5.

![](images/e2ed85e486ca1894b72320579acbd87ce5cc365844c9ff44b08516cb968be735.jpg)  
FI GURE 40.4. Event-related potentials of a patient with ALS. (A) Average at electrode Cz across 300 target (solid line) versus 1500 nontarget stimuli (dashed line) in one calibration session. Between 200 and 300 ms, a P300 peaks after target stimulus presentation. Standard errors of the mean (SEM) are displayed transparently behind the curves (±SEM). (B) Grand average ERPs for all 12 electrodes at positions Fz, FC1, FC2, C3, Cz, C4, P3, Pz, P4, O1, Oz , and O2. In this patient, classification of target characters is mostly based on the P300, with little contribution of negative potentials (N200) on occipital electrode sites.

estimate the number of sequences from the already acquired calibration data. Classification accuracies are computed when entering different amount of sequences into the classifier model. For example, first, the classifier is built using only one sequence from the data, and classification accuracy is estimated. Next, the procedure is repeated with two sequences, then with three, and so on. This results in an offline estimation of classification accuracy with different amounts of sequences (see Table 40.1).

Free-spelling is the final step, the goal of the entire procedure. Patients are advised to begin with short words to become accustomed to the new task (i.e., not only to copy a word but also to come up with

# Step 5: Free‑Spelling

# Step 4: Copy‑Spelling

The aim of copy-spelling sessions is to test the online performance of the classifier model built in Step 3. Again, the user is required to spell a predefined word, as in the calibration session. However, in contrast to this session, after each stimulus cycle, ERPs are classified online (i.e., after a predefined number of stimulus sequences [see below] is presented, data are automatically analyzed and classified to determine the most likely target). Feedback on target selection is then provided directly after each stimulation cycle. Consequently, performance can be directly observed by the user and “miss”-classification has a direct effect (i.e., selection of an erroneous letter, not intended by the user). From the accuracy achieved in copyspelling session(s) it can be decided whether calibration needs to be performed again (e.g., whether accuracy is below 70%, a performance level suggested as minimum level for communication; Kübler et al., 2001).

Also, the number of sequences used for flashing the matrix can be varied to investigate how many stimulation sequences are needed without a drop in performance. This is a tradeoff, because a high number of sequences entails low communication speed, whereas a low number of sequences bears the risk of low performance. To determine the optimal number of sequences, several copyspelling sessions can be performed online with different number of sequences and direct observation of performance. From this, the tradeoff border can be determined. However, a faster way is to offline

TA BLE 40.1. O ffline Estimation of Classification Accuracy with Different Numbers of Sequences   
![](images/d1b5c094b557fd3e900f0400ef1814065fcbee23de89af9b82e6bb51dcb20e7a.jpg)

Note. From the data presented in Figure 40.4, classification of ERPs was performed. When taking all data (15 sequences from the calibration session) into account, classification accuracy of 100% is achieved. However, flashing the matrix with such high number of sequences in the copy- and free-spelling sessions entails slow spelling speed. Also, performance may decrease rapidly due to the high demands placed on the patient (attention to many stimuli). From offline estimation, we see that accuracy may still be 100% when flashing the matrix with eight sequences. Thus, eight to 10 sequences may better meet the patient’s requirements of spelling with high accuracy, yet be fast and less demanding.

a word on their own and think of how to write the word correctly). Finally, it should be possible to write complete sentences and even longer messages. Such free-spelling sessions may well benefit from predictive text entry systems (see Kaufmann and colleagues [2012)]).

# Recent Developments in ERP‑BCI

During the past 20 years of ERP-BCI research, a plethora of adaptations to improve communication speed have been suggested. Herein, we describe only a few exemplary, recent developments.

At the beginning of this chapter, we described several severe disabilities that entail loss of motor control and thereby the ability to communicate. ERP-BCIs constitute a promising alternative communication channel. However, it has been shown that gaze control is necessary to operate the previously described matrix paradigm (e.g., Brunner et al., 2010; Treder & Blankertz 2010; Kaufmann, Holz, & Kübler, 2013a). Thus, other paradigms have been suggested that are independent of eye gaze. For example, Treder and Blankertz (2010) tested a paradigm that presents stimuli in the center of the screen. Targets and nontargets appear in the same place and therefore do not require spatial shift of focus.

Erroneously selected items of the matrix are reducing spelling speed, because they have to be corrected and therefore reduce satisfaction with the device. In an exemplary study, the error-related negativity, typically seen after an error, was used to correct letters in an online ERP-BCI (Spüler et al., 2012). In healthy subjects, and importantly, in those with ALS, bit rate (i.e., correctly communicated letters per time unit) was increased when using this correction method. Importantly, patients could increase bit rate when the classifier was precalibrated on other users’ data, which is crucial, because setting up a classifier to detect the error-related negativity requires averaging a considerable amount of data.

In Step 4, we described the tradeoff regarding the optimal choice of number of sequences used for flashing the matrix. Instead of using a fixed amount of sequences, researchers suggested that the number of sequences in every stimulation cycle be dynamically adjusted. After each sequence classification is performed, and based on the outcome, the system decides if more stimulation sequences are required or if decision on target selection can already be made with fewer sequences (for review, see Schreuder et al., 2013). This approach was found to increase spelling speed.

![](images/e22d6f76b9f69ae000d14b00a5740c97ef50992afe5b4d77ac72fe5d5b0da020.jpg)  
FI GURE 40.5. Grand average of N = 36 healthy participants using a face paradigm. Presentation of faces elicits face specific ERP components (i.e., N170 and N400f). Thus, the signal-to-noise ratio is increased and targets can be classified with higher accuracy as compared to classic character highlighting. (A) Grand average ERP at electrode Cz. Standard errors of the mean (SEM) are displayed transparently behind the curves (±SEM). (B) Grand average ERP for all 12 electrodes at positions Fz, FC1, FC2, C3, Cz, C4, P3, Pz, P4, O1, Oz, and O2.

Another approach to increase spelling speed is to shift the previously described tradeoff (i.e., to retain high accuracy although decreasing the number of sequences). Kaufmann, Schulz, Grünzinger, and Kübler (2011b) introduced faces as stimulus material in ERP-BCIs. Instead of lightflashing characters, faces are transparently overlaid, and the user’s task is to count the number of face-flashes. Faces elicit additional ERPs involved in face perception and face processing (see Figure 40.5). Thus, the signal-to-noise ratio of the event-related EEG is highly improved, and fewer stimulus sequences are necessary to achieve high performance. Importantly, when validating this approach in patients with neurodegenerative disease, the benefit was even more pronounced than in healthy participants, and some users who could not achieve reliable communication accuracy with the classic approach performed highly reliably when using faces (Kaufmann et al., 2013b). Modification of stimulus material is therefore a promising direction in ERP-BCI research.

Furthermore, the ERP-BCI approach has been implemented to control standard assistive technology software for text entry, e-mailing, and Internet surfing, and it was evaluated by end-users with severe motor impairment (Zickler et al., 2011). “Effectiveness” referred to how accurate and complete users accomplished the task. “Efficiency” related to the invested costs (i.e., users’ effort and time) to effectiveness. “Satisfaction” was the perceived comfort and acceptability while using the product. The study yielded high performance levels; therefore, the approach proved effective. It was also efficient because the control of the BCI was not tiring for users and therefore did not impose too large a workload. The BCI was judged reliable, and learnability of the complex BCI controlled assistive technology software was high. Consequently, users were quite satisfied with the device. However, obstacles such as the EEG-cap (electrode placement, gel, washing patients’ hair) and the low speed compared to muscle-driven input remain and are subjects of intensive research.

# Outl ook

We have dealt in this chapter with ERP-BCIs for communication purposes. Today, ERP-BCIs are no longer used for communication purposes only; they are also used for control of other systems (e.g., for entertainment applications, robotic arms, and wheelchairs). Other BCI systems that use, for example, sensorimotor rhythms as input signals, experienced an extension of application as well. First studies show their value in the field of stroke rehabilitation (e.g., Ang et al., 2011). Also, growing attention is being devoted to BCIs for entertainment. As BCIs improve in terms of performance, it becomes mandatory to choose the BCI that best fits the needs and requirements of users. For communication, ERP-BCIs are still considered the best choice. [However, other non-EEG systems like near-infrared spectroscopy show initial promise to fit the requirements for communication with patients in complete locked-in state (GallegosAyala et al., 2014).]

# Gl oss ary

Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS). A disorder causing progressive loss of control of voluntary muscles due to degeneration of nerve cells in the brain and spinal cord. Most often, paresis begins with the lower extremities, then moves on to hands and arms, finally paralyzing breathing and swallowing, as well as facial muscles. In the end stage, patients can stay alive only with artificial feeding and ventilation. In most patients with ALS, control of eye muscles, sphincters, and a few facial muscles is retained up to the end stage of the disease.

Brain–Computer Communication. Allows for communication without use of natural motor-dependent communication channels. See also brain–computer interface.

Brain–Computer Interface (BCI). A system that acquires brain signals, extracts informative features, and translates them into a digital command (e.g., selection of a character from a communication application). BCIs can be controlled without muscular activity, which renders the systems feasible for patients in the locked-in state.

Brainstem. In the mature human brain, the medulla, pons, and midbrain.

Encephalitis. Inflammation of the brain. The condition is frequently associated with viral infections.

Event-Related Potentials (ERPs). Signals in the electroencephalogram that are triggered by external or internal events. For a detailed introduction to ERPs, see the textbook Luck (2005).

Locked-in Syndrome (LIS). The inability to communicate (i.e., the intact brain is locked in a paralyzed body). In the classic form, vertical eye movements, as well as eyeblinks, remain intact. In the total form, no voluntary muscle movement is possible. The classic LIS is caused by a stroke in the pons.

Oddball paradigm. Stream of visual, auditory, or tactile

stimuli. Usually, a high number of irrelevant stimuli are interspersed with rare, but relevant, target stimuli. The EEG typically shows a P300 to target stimuli.   
P300. An event-related positive deflection in the EEG time-locked to a relevant stimulus in a stream of irrelevant stimuli (i.e., it is typically evoked in an oddball paradigm) occurring around 300 ms poststimulus.   
Pons Cerebri. Part of the brainstem that lies between the medulla (caudal portion of the brainstem) and the midbrain; appears to constitute a bridge between the right and left halves of the cerebellum (large part of the brain with motor functions).   
Slow Cortical Potentials (SCPs). Negative or positive polarizations of the EEG that last from 0.3 seconds up to several seconds.   
Tetraplegia. Paralysis of both lower and upper limbs. Also called “quadriplegia.”

# Substance Use Disorders and Neurofeedback

Estate M. “Tato” Sokhadze, David L. Trudeau, Rex L. C annon, Eugenia Bodenhamer‑Davis, and R ichard E . D avis

Substance use disorders (SUDs) are among the most common psychiatric conditions and result in serious impairments in cognition and behavior. Acute and chronic drug abuse, as well as withdrawal, result in significant alteration of the electroencephalogram (EEG). EEG biofeedback has been employed for the treatment of alcohol (Peniston & Kulkosky, 1989) and mixed substance abuse (Scott, Kaiser, Othmer, & Sideroff, 2005) in residential inpatient populations. More recent outpatient approaches have used quantitative EEG (QEEG)-guided neurofeedback (Gunkelman & Cripe, 2008), neurofeedback (NFB) integrated with motivational interviewing (Sokhadze, Steward, Sokhadze, Hollifield, & Tasman, 2009), and LOw Resolution Electromagnetic TomogrAphy (LORETA) NFB (Cannon, Lubar, Sokhadze, & Baldwin, 2008). A recent review of the state of the art of QEEG and NFB in SUDs (Sokhadze, Cannon, & Trudeau, 2008) and prior reviews (Trudeau, 2000, 2005a) detail the efficacy ratings and clinical utilizations of NFB for SUDs. In these reviews, NFB is rated “probably efficacious” as an add-on (adjunct) treatment to other therapies, namely, 12-step programs and/or cognitive-behavioral therapies or other types of psychotherapies or residential programs. NFB is not yet validated as a stand-alone therapy for addictive disorders and cannot yet be considered a mainstream therapy for addiction. Many persons with SUDs have comorbid conditions that need to be considered in designing a treatment plan that incorporates NFB. These include mental conditions such as depression, bipolar disorder, posttraumatic stress disorder (PTSD), attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), and so forth, which may require separate NFB treatment for those specific conditions either preceding NFB treatment for addiction, or incorporated into it. This approach may require separate assessments during the course of therapy to determine response and the need for changing protocols or seek other treatment (i.e., medication or psychotherapy to integrate into the treatment plan update).

# Preval ence and Ch aracterization of SUD s

The SUD commonly referred to as “drug addiction” is characterized by physiological dependence accompanied by the withdrawal syndrome on discontinuance of drug use; psychological dependence with craving; the pathological motivational state that leads to active drug-seeking behavior; and tolerance, expressed in the escalation of the dose needed to achieve a desired euphoric state. Drug addiction is a chronic, relapsing mental disease that results from the prolonged effects of drugs on the brain (Volkow, Fowler, & Wang, 2003).

The incidence of substance abuse is staggering, leading to behavioral, cognitive, and socially adverse outcomes that incur substantial costs to society. The Office of National Drug Control Policy (2001) estimated the overall costs associated with drug abuse (health care costs, productivity losses, other effects, crime related costs, etc.) was \$160.7 billion in 2000. In 2007, 19.9 million Americans were estimated to be illicit drug users (Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration [SAMHSA], Office of Applied Studies, 2008). Many patients seeking treatment for addiction (40%) have multiple drug dependencies and psychiatric comorbidities. Information from epidemiological surveys indicates that drug addiction is a common phenomenon and is associated with significant effects on both morbidity and mortality. Large individual and societal costs of drug abuse make research and treatment of drug addiction imperative (French, McGeary, Chitwood, & McCoy, 2000).

Current treatments for substance abuse apply various individual- and group-based therapeutic interventions, case management, structured residential programs, values-based programs, cognitive-behavioral therapies, pharmacological interventions, motivational techniques and other methods, with some of these being combined. Because substance abuse is complex and associated with other comorbid conditions, it seems unlikely that a simple or single approach will satisfy the needs of all patients. We believe that integrating NFB has the potential to enhance the effectiveness of more mainstream alcohol and drug abuse treatment strategies.

# Studies of EE G Biofeedback in Subs tance Ab use T reatment

The Peniston Protocol (Alpha–Theta Feedback)

Early studies on self-regulation of the alpha rhythm (Nowlis & Kamiya, 1970) elicited substantial interest in the potential clinical applications of alpha biofeedback for treatment of SUDs. Several uncontrolled case studies and conceptual reviews on alpha EEG training for alcohol and drug abuse treatment were reported, but the impact of alpha biofeedback training as a therapy for SUD was not significant (Brinkman, 1978; DeGood & Valle, 1978; Denney, Baugh, & Hardt, 1991; Goldberg, Greenwood, & Tainzor, 1976, 1977; Jones & Holmes, 1976; Passini, Watson, Dehnel, Herder, & Watkins, 1977; Tarbox, 1983).

Most of the studies to date regarding EEG biofeedback for addictive disorders have focused on simultaneous alpha and theta biofeedback, first popularized by the work of Eugene Peniston (Peniston & Kulkosky, 1989, 1990, 1991). This approach employed independent auditory feedback of two slow brain wave frequencies, alpha (8–13 Hz) and theta (4–8 Hz) in an eyes-closed condition, to produce a hypnagogic state. Patients were taught prior to NFB to use what amounts to success imagery (of sobriety, of refusing offers of alcohol, of living confidently and happily) as they drifted down into a relaxed reverie state guided by auditory alpha– theta feedback. Peniston reported that repeated treatments resulted in long-term abstinence, as well as changes in personality profiles. The role of psychological and personality shifts in facilitation of positive outcomes in Peniston’s studies was specifically outlined by his proponents (White, 2008). Alpha–theta feedback, first employed and described by Elmer Green and colleagues (Green, Green, & Walters, 1974) at the Menninger Clinic, was based on Green's observations of practiced meditators during meditative states, when he noticed that increases in theta amplitude were followed by initial increases in alpha amplitude, but then a subsequent drop-off of alpha amplitude (theta–alpha crossover). When feedback of alpha and theta signal was provided to subjects, states of profound relaxation and reverie were reported to occur. The method was seen as useful in augmenting psychotherapy and promoting individual insight.

The first reported use of alpha–theta feedback in a treatment program for SUDs, which included group and individual therapies, was in an integrated program started in 1973 at the Topeka Veterans Administration (VA). Daily 20-minute EEG biofeedback sessions (integrated with EMG biofeedback and temperature control biofeedback) were conducted over 6 weeks, resulting in free, loose associations, heightened sensitivity, and increased suggestibility. Patients discussed their insights and experiences associated with biofeedback in therapy groups several times a week, augmenting expressive psychotherapy (Goslinga, 1975; Twemlow & Bowen, 1977; Twemlow, Sizemore, & Bowen, 1977). These initial studies advanced the utility of biofeedback-induced theta states in promoting insight and attitude change in alcoholics, with the assumptions that biofeedback induced theta states associated with heightened awareness and suggestibility, and that this heightened awareness and suggestibility would enhance recovery. Clinical outcome data regarding abstinence were not reported, however.

In the first reported randomized controlled study of alcoholics treated with alpha–theta EEG biofeedback, Peniston and Kulkosky (1989) described positive outcomes. Their subjects were male inpatients, all of whom had established, chronic alcoholism and multiple past failed treatments, in a VA hospital treatment program. Following a temperature biofeedback pretraining phase, the experimental subjects (n = 10) completed fifteen 30-minute sessions of eyes-closed occipital alpha–theta biofeedback. Compared to a traditionally treated alcoholic control group (n = 10), and nonalcoholic controls (n = 10), alcoholics receiving brainwave biofeedback showed significant increases in percentages of EEG recorded in alpha and theta rhythms, and increased alpha rhythm amplitudes (single-lead measurements at international 10-20 system site O1). The experimentally treated subjects showed reductions in Beck Depression Inventory scores that were comparable to those of the nonalcoholic controls. Control alcoholic subjects who received standard treatment alone showed increased levels of circulating beta-endorphin, an index of stress, whereas the EEG biofeedback group did not. Thirteen-month follow-up data indicated significantly reduced relapse in alcoholics who completed alpha–theta brain wave training as compared to the control alcoholics, defining successful relapse prevention as “not using alcohol for more than 6 contiguous days” during the follow-up period. In a further report on the same control and experimental subjects, Peniston and Kulkosky (1990) described substantial changes in personality test results in the experimental group as compared to the alcoholic controls. The experimental group showed improvement in psychological adjustment on 13 scales of the Millon Clinical Multiaxial Inventory (MCMI) compared to the traditionally treated alcoholics who improved on only two scales and actually became worse on one scale. On the 16 Personality Factors Test (16-PF), the NFB training group demonstrated improvement on seven scales, compared to improvement on only one scale among the traditional treatment group. This small-sample-size study employed various controls and blind outcome evaluation, with actual outcome figures of 80% positive outcome versus 20% in the traditional treatment control condition at 4-year follow-up (Peniston, Marriman, Deming, & Kulkosky, 1993).

The protocol described by Peniston is similar to that initially employed by Twemlow and colleagues and Elmer Green with two additions: (1) temperature training and (2) guided imagery scripting. Peniston introduced temperature biofeedback training as a preconditioning relaxation exercise, and an induction script to be read at the start of each session. A number of case series studies have replicated the initial findings of Peniston in terms of improvements in personality, mood, and long-term abstinence with alcoholics; however, attempts to employ alpha–theta training with mixed substance abuse, especially stimulants, has not met with the same success (Callaway & Bodenhamer-Davis, 2008; DeBeus, Prinzel, RyderCook, & Allen, 2002; Fahrion, Walters, Coyne, & Allen, 1992; Finkelberg et al., 1996; Kelly, 1997; Saxby & Peniston, 1995). These studies support the alpha–theta biofeedback protocol as a valuable adjunct therapy to conventional substance abuse treatment in alcoholics. For a complete review of studies and a more detailed critical discussion, see the articles by Sokhadze et al. (2008) and Trudeau (2000, 2005a, 2005b).

# The Scott–Kaiser Modification of the Peniston Protocol

Psychostimulant (cocaine, methamphetamine) addictions may require NFB protocols other than alpha–theta training. Persons who are cocainedependent are cortically underaroused during protracted abstinence. QEEG changes, such as a decrease in high beta (18–26 Hz) power, are typical for withdrawal from cocaine. Cocaine abusers who are still taking drugs often show low amounts of delta and excess amounts of alpha and beta activity, and these changes may persist in abstinence, whereas chronic methamphetamine abusers usually exhibit excessive delta and theta activity. Thus, cocaine and methamphetamine users may need a different EEG biofeedback protocol, at least at the beginning stages of neurotherapy (Sokhadze, Stewart, & Hollifield, 2007; Sokhadze et al., 2008).

Scott and Kaiser (1998) describe a protocol combining attentional training (beta and/or sensorimotor rhythm [SMR] augmentation with theta suppression) with the Peniston approach (alpha–theta training) that was first tested in a population of subjects with mixed substance abuse, rich in stimulant abusers. The beta protocol they employed is similar to that used in ADHD (Kaiser & Othmer, 2000). Hence, beta feedback was provided until Test of Variables of Attention (TOVA) measures normalized, then the standard Peniston protocol without temperature training was implemented (Scott, Brod, Sideroff, Kaiser, & Sagan, 2002; Scott et al., 2005). The findings of chronic EEG abnormality and high incidence of preexisting ADHD in stimulant abusers suggest they may be less able to engage in the hypnagogic and autosuggestive aspects of the Peniston protocol. Furthermore, eyes-closed alpha feedback as a starting protocol may be deleterious in stimulant abusers, because the most common EEG abnormality in crack cocaine addicts is excessive frontal alpha (Herning, Glover, Koeppl, Phillips, & London, 1994; Prichep et al., 2002).

Using their approach, Scott and Kaiser (1998; Scott et al., 2002, 2005) described substantial improvement in measures of attention, and also of personality changes similar to those reported by Peniston and Kulkosky. Their experimental subjects underwent an average of 13 SMR–beta (12–18 Hz) NFB training sessions followed by 30 alpha–theta sessions during the first 45 days of treatment. Treatment retention was significantly better in the EEG biofeedback group,especially at the initial SMR–beta training stage. One hundred twenty-one inpatient drug program subjects were randomized to NFB or treatment-as-usual conditions and followed up at 1 year. The patients at the intake stage were tested and controlled for the presence of attentional and cognitive deficits, personality states, and traits. The experimental group showed normalization of attentional variables following the SMR–beta portion of the NFB, while the control group showed no improvement. Prior to treatment, both groups revealed similar patterns of Minnesota Multiphasic Personality Inventory (MMPI-2) scale elevations; mean scale scores were near or exceeded the clinical cutoff for six scales for the experimental group and five for the control group. Following intervention, the experimental subjects demonstrated statistically significant changes beyond the control subjects on five of the 10 clinical scales of the MMPI-2. Only two of six clinical scales remained above clinical cutoffs for the experimental group, while all five remained near or above clinical cutoffs for the controls. Subjects in the experimental group were also more likely than the control group to stay in treatment longer and to complete treatment. Finally, the 1-year sustained abstinence levels were significantly higher for the experimental group than for the control group (Scott et al., 2005).

This approach has been applied successfully in a treatment program aimed at homeless crack cocaine abusers in Houston, Texas, with impressive results (Burkett, Cummins, Dickson, & Skolnick, 2003). Two hundred and seventy male addicts received 30 sessions of a protocol similar to the Scott–Kaiser modification. One-year follow-up of 94 treatment completers indicated significant improvement in maintaining a regular residence, being employed or in employment training, and evidencing low rates of subsequent arrests. Selfreport depression and anxiety scores dropped significantly. Furthermore, 53.2% reported no alcohol or drug use 12 months after biofeedback, and 23.4% reported using drugs or alcohol one to three times after their stay, a substantial improvement over the 30% or less expected recovery rate in this group. The treatment program resulted in substantial changes in length of stay and completion. After the introduction of NFB to the residential, faith-based regimen, length of stay tripled, beginning at 30 days on average and culminating at 100 days after the addition of NFB. In a later study, the authors reported follow-up results on 87 subjects after completion of NFB training (Burkett, Cummins, Dickson, & Skolnick, 2005). The follow-up measures of drug screens, length of residence, and self-reported depression scores showed significant improvement. These large-scale, interesting studies do not have any control conditions and are best thought of as single-group outcome investigations that lack key controls.

# QEEG‑Guided NFB

A number of QEEG abnormalities have been described as specific to suspected neurotoxicities associated with chronic stimulant abuse (reviewed in Sokhadze et al., 2008). Basically, this technique involves the use of QEEG to identify patterns of EEG that deviate from standardized norms, and individualized EEG biofeedback protocols to address them. Preliminary results of QEEG-guided NFB therapy as an approach in treatment of outpatient drug- and alcohol-dependent patients are promising (DeBeus et al., 2002; Gurnee, 2004; Gunkelman & Cripe, 2008). Gurnee (2004) presented data on a series of 100 sequential participants with SUDs who were treated by QEEGbased NFB, with marked heterogeneity of QEEG subtypes and corresponding symptom complexes. Gunkelman and Cripe (2008) reported a case series using QEEG phenotype-guided NFB in an integrated clinical treatment. The authors identified two QEEG phenotype patterns and used individually selected SMR, beta, alpha–theta, and/or the cingulate overactivation-related NFB training protocols. This pilot study demonstrated neurocognitive improvements and high abstinence rates in the polysubstance-abusing subjects. More information is needed regarding QEEG-guided biofeedback and addictive disorders. Specific patterns of QEEG abnormality associated with specific substance use toxicity, such as those found in stimulant abuse or alcohol abuse, or with comorbidities such as ADHD, PTSD, or traumatic brain injury (TBI), suggest underlying brain pathologies that might be amenable to individualized EEG biofeedback. These approaches would likely be individualized rather than protocol based, and would be used independently or in conjunction with classic alpha–theta training.

For example, Davis and Bodenhamer-Davis (2010) have summarized their clinical experience using a QEEG-based approach to NFB in both a comprehensive residential addiction treatment facility and in outpatient settings. By including a pretreatment QEEG assessment along with symptom and psychometric assessments, their approach conforms to standards of NFB practice published by the International Society for Neurofeedback and Research (ISNR; Hammond & Kirk, 2008). The approach also incorporates NFB protocol components that are research supported. Although protocols are individually tailored in QEEG guided NFB, the fact that there are certain EEG phenotypes that predominate in the SUD population (Gunkelman & Cripe, 2008) permits description of a fairly typical QEEG-guided protocol that a practitioner might expect to use.

After completing QEEG, symptom, and psychometric pretreatment assessments, NFB is usually initiated at frontal and central vertex sites F and Cz (as well as surrounding hemispheric sites in some cases). The fronto-central vertex sites usually show z-score abnormalities on QEEG’s of patients with SUDs, regardless of the type of addiction and co-occurring disorders. QEEG abnormalities at these sites also are associated with transdiagnostic symptoms common to the SUD population, such as obsessiveness, compulsivity, impulsivity, concentration/attention problems, anxiety, insomnia, cognitive impairments, and depression. When working with patients with alcoholism, special attention should be given to the reduction of excessive high-frequency beta activity (> 20 Hz) that may be present at central sites, especially at Cz. This marker of EEG hyperexcitability was found to be the best predictor of alcoholic relapse, regardless of the form of treatment a patient received. The Peniston protocol’s singular emphasis on augmenting posterior alpha and theta waves appears in most cases to reduce, but not eliminate, this excessive high-frequency beta at central sites (Callaway & Bodenhamer-Davis, 2008).

After targeted EEG amplitude reductions and symptom improvements are observed at frontal and central sites, other cortical locations identified in the QEEG can be addressed. In practice, this second stage of treatment often involves suppression of excessive alpha typically seen in central, parietal, and right posterior temporal regions in patients with cocaine addiction (Prichep et al., 2002) or sex addiction (Sims, Davis, BodenhamerDavis & Sherlin, 2006). Finally, if remaining symptoms and QEEG assessment concur, a Peniston protocol can be used to increase posterior alpha and theta, as well as supplement psychotherapeutic and personality change goals of the individual’s treatment. By the time this third stage is reached (after at least 20 sessions), as few as 10 alpha–theta sessions may be needed to reach final treatment outcomes.

Because Peniston reported the possibility that patients who have experienced trauma may have emotional “abreactions,” dissociation, or flooding responses to emergence of emotional memories during alpha–theta biofeedback, he recommended a modification to his protocol to process such reactions therapeutically, described in detail in Peniston and Kulkosky (1991). Subsequently, additional modifications of the Peniston protocol have been successfully tested, including suppression of 2–5 Hz during alpha–theta training to prevent sleep (Scott et al., 2005).

Peniston considered processing of repressed memories to be one of the therapeutic goals of alpha–theta biofeedback. He also believed the theta–alpha crossover phenomenon observed during the training process to be an important therapeutic component of his protocol. These claims received some empirical support in a study that found a significant correlation between psychometric measures of therapeutic outcome and the occurrence of theta–alpha crossovers during alpha–theta biofeedback (Johnson & Bodenhamer-Davis, 2009). The potentially complex nature of addictive disorders, including chemical dependency and sex addiction, as well as the frequency of trauma experienced by many patients in this population, requires that NFB practitioners who undertake alpha–theta, or “deep states” training, be experienced therapists, qualified to handle trauma disorders, various mental disorders, and complex dual diagnoses (Demos, 2005).

# Some Theoretical and Practical Recommendations for NFB in the Addiction Field

From a historical perspective, therapies for addiction treatment have targeted the reward-reinforcing neural systems in attempt to interfere with the pleasure-inducing effects of drugs. One example of such an approach is application of drug antagonists (e.g., naloxon for opiate addiction or aversive therapies for alcohol dependence such as disulfuram or naltrexone) that have been employed with alcoholics. New drugs, designed to reduce excessive alcohol consumption or drug abuse by producing strong aversive reactions, became available as well. No study has examined the combined effects of NFB with these therapies, but there is little reason to believe that there would be any negative interactions. Opioid substitution (i.e., methadone maintenance or suboxone replacement therapies for opioid addiction) are well accepted and widely acknowledged as efficacious. There is no systematic research for NFB per se in conjunction with these substitute pharmacotherapies except some recent pilot reports (Dehrghani-Arani, Rostami, & Nadali, 2013), but there is no obvious reason why NFB could not be of value in cases in which there is a comorbidity such as anxiety or depression. Definitely more attention should be focused on combining NFB with contemporary pharmacotherapies adopted in routine addiction treatment. NFB can be positioned as a viable complementary treatment to psychopharmacology in drug-dependent patients enrolled in substitute therapy.

Current trends of intervention in addiction are based on more recent scientific discoveries that have identified the brain circuits that underlie not only reward mechanisms of the fundamental aspects of drug addiction, but also other aspects, such as craving, euphoric state, anticipation of reward, motivation, anhedonia, learning, memory, interoception (i.e., sensitivity to homeostatic visceral cues), and inhibitory control deficits, all of which are recognized as key contributors to addiction development. These discoveries have widened the range of novel targets for different biobehavioral treatment approaches, including NFB.

Several novel applied psychophysiology-based approaches exist for treating addictions with NFB. One of them involves incorporating monitoring of drug cue reactivity, craving, and emotional states in addicts enrolled in treatment using QEEG, evoked potentials, and other psychophysiological reactivity assessment methods. To improve addiction therapy outcomes and prevent the risk of relapse, it would be feasible to modify the cognitive, attentional, affective, and behavioral mechanisms that mediate the motivational and emotional processes involved in drug-cue and drug-related environment reactivity response. Thus, the goal is to modify the overlearned and consolidated response to drugs and drug-associated conditioned stimuli that are often accompanied by craving, dysphoric states, and negative affect. Volkow et al. (2003) consider effective monitoring and modification of drug cue reactivity to be an efficient treatment strategy to prevent progression of drug abuse into drug dependence, and to lower the chance of relapse in drug-dependent addicts in treatment. A possible effective treatment approach for drug dependence might consist of emphasizing the risk associated with exposure to drug-related stimuli and environments, and consequent cognitivebehavioral or applied psychophysiological regulation (i.e., NFB) of responses to these stimuli in a controlled and monitored environment in which both subjective reports of craving and physiological responses are recorded and analyzed.

Another approach that may enhance treatment outcomes is to use NFB as a part of desensitization therapy. It is well known that there is an attentional bias toward drug-related cues in patients with drug dependence and that the extent of this bias would change depending on the current clinical conditions of the patients enrolled in treatment arms under study. It can be proposed that patients treated with such neurotherapy, accompanied by positive emotion training, would show a smaller attentional bias to drug cues. This would result from reduction of the motivational salience of drug-related cues, although through different moderating mechanisms, either by decreasing craving or by modifying behavior using NFB aimed at training positive emotions. NFB combined with desensitization behavioral therapy may result in alteration of physiological reactivity to stimuli associated with drugs by reducing craving and attenuating withdrawal symptoms, most likely through a modification of the reward response. The moderating mechanism underlying the predicted reduction of craving and negative affect in NFB therapy is different, because it can be ascribed to a cognitive remediation and behavioral shift toward other types of positive reward and through a discouragement of the perpetuation of maladaptive dysphoric states.

The approach suggested above fits with the model of addiction and strategies of intervention proposed by Volkow, Fowler, and Wang (2004).

The authors suggested that the treatment of drug dependence should focus on decreasing the reward value of drugs while increasing saliency and motivational values for natural rewards. The approach considers strategies to reduce conditioned drug behaviors and increase frontal executive functions as potential moderators. Furthermore, to achieve more prolonged results, the strategy of biobehavioral intervention in the field of addiction must incorporate techniques that reeducate patients to control and self-regulate their emotional and motivational states, and train them to relearn induction of positive affect in an attempt to try to reestablish the normal biological, cognitive, behavioral, and hedonic homeostasis distorted by drug abuse. In the “allostatic model of addiction,” Koob and LeMoal (2001) suggested that continued drug use may gradually deteriorate natural hedonic homeostasis and result in development of a higher threshold for the amount of emotionally positive stimulation needed to experience reward and positive affect states. Active drug use and withdrawalrelated alterations in neural structures involved in the stress response are well known, and these neuroadaptave changes in stress circuits, according to Li and Sinha (2008), may contribute to the increased salience of drug and drug-related stimuli in a variety of challenge or “stress” contexts (Robinson & Berridge, 2009; Sinha, Garcia, Paliwal, Kreek, & Rounsaville, 2006), and may contribute to reduced coping ability, poor behavioral flexibility, and deficient problem-solving capacity during increasing levels of stress or emotional challenges in substance abusers. As proposed in our conceptual and review articles (Sokhadze et al., 2007, 2008), drug abusers may develop hypersensitivity not only to drug-related but also to stress stimuli and present similar psychophysiological reactivity to both drug- and stress-related cues. This may partially explain the high rate of substance use and anxiety disorders comorbidity. Proposed emotion self-regulation and stress management training skills, combined with NFB, are part of a potentially beneficial strategy to change significantly the extent of implicit reactivity to drug cues, to promote resilience to stress, and to increase positive emotionality during either experimental exposure or in a real-life environment.

Several other practical considerations may be helpful for NFB application in additions. Information about the QEEG signature of patients enrolled in addiction treatment might be useful in selection of NFB protocols. Specific patterns of QEEG abnormality associated with specific substance use toxicity (e.g., those found in stimulant abuse or alcohol abuse, or with comorbidities such as ADHD, PTSD, depression, or TBI) suggest underlying brain pathologies that might be amenable to individualized NFB protocols. QEEG patterns and abnormalities depend significantly on whether the subject is still currently using, the history of drug use, and the stage of withdrawal or abstinence. An NFB protocol selected for an individual client with an SUD should be directly related to the level of current substance use or abstinence, especially in classes of drugs such as heroin, where withdrawal syndrome results in substantial physiological manifestations, including transient QEEG changes.

Careful diagnosis of other mental comorbidities present in addiction is an essential step before the start of the treatment. Even though there are no reported systematic studies of EEG biofeedback treatment of commonly occurring comorbidities of SUDs, it makes sense that clinical neurotherapy study protocols consider the presence of ADHD, TBI, PTSD, depression, and other pathologies often associated with neurotoxicity induced by drug abuse. Such an approach may improve outcome, especially in conventional treatment resistant participants.

Among other recommendations for future development of neurofeedback in the field of addictions treatment should be the listed advantages of application of an increased number of channels in EEG recording (e.g., higher spatial sampling rate). This leads to important developments related to brain activity source localization and training using LORETA NFB and other similar techniques of advanced feedback.

Along with traditional neurocognitive tests (TOVA, IVA+Plus, CNS-Vital Signs, etc.) that are well recognized in neurofeedback research for assessing outcomes based on reaction time data, there may be added value in incorporating more standardized tests commonly used with event-related potential (ERP) recordings to assess executive functions in addicts. The Continuous Performance Test, go/no-go task, Stroop test, Eriksen flanker test, Posner spatial attention test, and others are among those to consider. Some of these tests are sufficiently sensitive to assess recovery of cortical inhibition function commonly known to be impaired in patients with SUDs. Testing emotional reactivity and responsiveness is another important domain to consider in standard emotional evaluations. Techniques based on QEEG and ERP methods may help to obtain more effective evaluation of the affective state of recovering addicts as well.

A very important objective for future NFB treatment for SUDs should include attempts to integrate neurotherapy with other well-known behavioral interventions for drug abuse, such as cognitive-behavioral therapy (CBT), motivation enhancement therapy (MET), and so forth. As a population, drug addicts are very difficult to treat, are characterized by a low motivation to change their drug habit, and are reluctant to enter either ambulatory or inpatient treatment. CBT and MET are powerful psychotherapeutic interventions to help bring about rapid commitment to change addictive behaviors. These behavioral therapies are especially useful to enhance compliance for drug-dependent individuals and facilitate their NFB treatment engagement.

Drugs of abuse can impair cognitive, emotional, and motivational processes. More QEEG and cognitive ERP testing research is needed to characterize the chronic and residual effects of drugs on attention, emotion, memory, and overall behavioral performance. More research is needed also to relate cognitive functionality measures to clinical outcome (relapse rate, drug screens, psychiatric status, etc.). Such QEEG–ERP studies may facilitate the translation of clinical neurophysiology research data into routine practical tools for assessment of functional recovery in alcoholism and addiction treatment clinics. Some of the previously described QEEG assessments at the pretreatment baseline might be useful as predictors of clinical outcome and relapse risk. Incorporation of cognitive tests with EEG and ERP (e.g., P300) measures into cognitive-behavioral and NFBbased interventions may have significant potential for identifying whether certain QEEG–ERP measures can be used as psychophysiological markers of treatment progress (and/or relapse vulnerability), and also may provide useful information in planning cognitive-behavioral and neurotherapy treatment in substance abuse comorbid with mental disorder.

# Concl usions

While it is not our aim in this chapter to provide an exhaustive treatise on the state of NFB research and addictive disorders, it is necessary to point out that neurotherapy is a new and developing field. Substantially more information is needed about many aspects of NFB and SUDs to validate clinical utility and efficacy. Even though there are no reported systematic studies of NFB treatment of commonly occurring comorbidities of SUDs, it makes sense that clinical EEG biofeedback treatment study protocols consider the presence of ADHD, TBI, PTSD, depression, and drug-associated neurotoxicity. EEG biofeedback treatment of ADHD may be important in prevention for children and adolescents at risk for developing SUDs (Trudeau, 2005b). It may be possible that EEG biofeedback therapy for childhood ADHD may result in a decrease in later life SUDs (Wilens, Biederman, & Mick, 1998). There have been no reported studies of the effect of NFB treatment on prevention of SUDs to date.

Drugs of abuse can impair cognitive, emotional, and motivational processes. More QEEG and cognitive ERP research is needed to characterize the chronic and residual effects of drugs on attention, emotion, memory, and overall behavioral performance. More research is needed to relate cognitive functionality measures to clinical outcomes. Such QEEG–ERP studies may facilitate the translation of clinical neurophysiology research data into routine practical tools for assessment of functional recovery both in alcoholism and addiction treatment clinics. With the advances made in the last several years, we hope that continued interest will be generated to study brainwave biofeedback treatment of addictive disorders. Effectiveness in certain “hard-to-treat” populations (conventional treatment-resistant alcoholics, crack cocaine addicts, cognitively impaired substance abusers) is promising. Based on published clinical studies and employing efficacy criteria adapted by the Association for Applied Psychophysiology and Biofeedback and the ISNR, alpha–theta training, either alone for alcoholism or in combination with beta training for stimulant and mixed-substance abuse, and combined with residential treatment programs, is probably efficacious.
