# More Frontiers and Further Forward

# Mark S. Schwartz and F rank A ndrasik

Since the previous edition, the frontier for biofeedback and applied psychophysiology has continued to expand, with many intriguing, creative, and important applications and heuristic implications. This chapter provides a broad selection of such entries into the frontier.

# Writer’s Cramp : A Task‑Specific D ystonia

The rare and very disabling disorder writer’s cramp is typically resistant to treatment. One prevalence estimate is 69 per million (Nutt, Muenter, Aronson, Kurland, & Melton, 1988). The onset is insidious in most patients. Spontaneous remission for months or years reportedly occurs in about 5%, but relapse is also common. For about half of patients, writing difficulty is the only symptom. For many, there is progression to other manual acts, as noted below.

Experts continue to disagree about the etiology of writer’s cramp. Some of this disagreement may result from the health care specialties of the investigators and the types of patients who seek their help. These divergent views result in different classifications of subtypes. Marsden and Sheehy (1990), for example, distinguish a “dystonic” from a “simple” type. In the dystonic type, there is involvement of other manual actions from the beginning. Persons with simple writer’s cramp can complete other manual motor tasks without difficulty. These authors also distinguish a type that they term “progressive” writer’s cramp, which begins as simple writer’s cramp. These persons develop difficulties with other hand actions, such as using eating utensils, shaving, threading a needle, and applying cosmetics. Although not clearly dystonic by Marsden and Sheehy’s criteria, this type also often includes “subtle neurological signs.” Spontaneous and involuntary dystonic muscle spasms of an arm, neck, or other limbs occur in a few patients. This is the basis for these authors’ considering writer’s cramp as a focal dystonia.

Different researchers may obtain different results in part because of treating patients with different features. Writer’s cramp may not be a unitary disorder, and patient selection is probably very important. For diagnosing this disorder, Mishima, Kitagawa, Hara, and Nakagawa (1992) emphasize that

the most basic and important point is that a patient has difficulty in hand movements only when writing. . . . If a patient has difficulty in a voluntary movement which is clearly different from handwriting, at the beginning . . . [the problem should] not be diagnosed as writer’s cramp. When hand motions such as holding chopsticks and buttoning clothes, which include similar movements to writing, have gradually become disturbed . . . [the disturbance] can be diagnosed as writer’s cramp. (p. 104)

These authors, then, distinguish types of writer’s cramp on the basis of patients’ handwriting and surface electromyography (EMG). Some persons complain of a chronic disturbance but not one that is severe enough to prevent writing. They only want to preserve ideal writing. These individuals may not be good candidates for biofeedback (Mishima et al., 1992). Mishima et al. divide the patients with “objective” disturbance into three types. In the “spastic” type, muscle tension related to writing remains very high, thus preventing smooth movements. In the “tremor” type, there is tremulous movement when attempting to write. The third is the “dystonic” type, the most severe and the most difficult to treat. There are “bizarre movements . . . such as flexion and/or a torsion of the wrist and an extension of the fingers, which never previously appeared during normal writing” (p. 105).

Some professionals link emotional factors (chiefly anxiety and anger) to the cause, persistence, and relapse of writer’s cramp in some cases, although Marsden and Sheehy (1990) disagree. This link is not clear, and many professionals consider organic factors as primary. Some persons develop other, more widespread neurological diseases (Marsden & Sheehy, 1990). However, practitioners often need to assess whether psychological factors are evident and treat them when relevant. For example, relapses reportedly occur during times of increased stress. Secondary gain from the symptom may be a factor complicating treatment in some persons.

# Treatments

Reported treatments have all had mixed results (for details, see www.marksschwartzphd.com). An early review (Ince, Leon, & Christidis, 1986) of EMG biofeedback for writer’s cramp concluded that despite results showing improvement in some patients, biofeedback reached “its near demise” as a treatment for writer’s cramp. More recent reports of surface EMG (SEMG) biofeedback to treat writer’s cramp showed more promise, especially the studies of Koller and Vetere-Overfield (1989), Murabayashi et al. (1992), Mishima et al. (1992), O’Neill, Gwinn, and Adler (1996), and Deepak and Behari (1999). Thus, SEMG biofeedback remains a logical and viable approach for some persons, and there are published cases in which SEMG biofeedback was successful.

The article by Deepak and Behari (1999) is also supportive and encouraging. They focused the

SEMG biofeedback on the large limb muscles (the brachioradialis, involved in forearm flexion, or the triceps). The 10 patients treated had dystonic postures and hypertrophy of the large muscles. They provided four or more biofeedback sessions (the average and range of sessions were not specified, but not many total sessions were used when compared to those in the Japanese series) plus daily practice. Nine patients improved 37–93% in handwriting, discomfort reduction, and pain reduction. Seven of these showed more than 50% improvement.

# EEG Sensorimotor Biofeedback

There is one report of restored striatal dopamine 2 (D2) binding (i.e., increased transmission) after EEG sensorimotor biofeedback (Berger et al., 2007). The rationale was the prior research indicating cerebral senorimotor abnormalities among persons with writer’s cramp, specifically reduced striatal D2 binding. In five patients, Berger et al. (2007) documented single-photon emission computed tomographic (SPECT) changes that correlated with handwriting improvement to “nearly normal levels.” This was consistent with the view that “writer's cramp results from a plastic adaptation of a rectifiable nigrostriatal dopaminergic system and that effective sensorimotor training leads to increased efficacy of striatal dopaminergic transmission” (p. 170).

# Guidelines for Evaluation and Treatment

Practitioners may find it helpful to consider the following when treating writer’s cramp:

•	 Obtain a medical/neurological evaluation, as well as a detailed history and description of the symptoms, all fine motor difficulties, and progression.   
•	 Classify patients according to one of the systems described earlier.   
•	 Consider stepped care with brief intervention for recent-onset symptoms in a young patient.   
•	 Prepare patients for the possibility of a lengthy treatment.   
• Consider general relaxation, as well as deep relaxation (1–5+ minutes) of writing muscles between writing periods.   
Record and provide feedback from multiple EMG sites simultaneously (e.g., use at least two sites; five or more sites are ideal). Consider upper trapezius, deltoid, biceps, brachioradialis, forearm flexors and extensors, and abductor pollicis brevis or thenar eminence.   
•	 Record during different types of writing (e.g., very slow vs. normal rate; simple figures and letters vs. sentences and paragraphs).   
•	 Have patients use different writing instruments with different degrees of difficulty, such as ballpoint pens, pencils, and mechanical pencils, and different thicknesses of writing instruments.   
•	 Start with nontargeted muscles—the secondary arm muscles and movements (e.g., triceps, brachioradialis)—and progress to muscles more involved with writing.   
•	 Have patients start without holding a pen or pencil or hold it without intent to write. Have patients progress from writing of easier figures, symbols, and letters to more difficult attempts, such as single words and sentences.   
•	 Have patients progress from slow writing to faster writing.   
•	 Include auditory and visual feedback.   
•	 Ask patients to practice writing several times a day for 5–10 minutes each while relaxing all arm muscles.   
•	 Include transfer-of-training procedures.   
•	 Plan gradual relearning with successive approximations in small steps.   
•	 Consider portable SEMG biofeedback for augmenting home practice.   
•	 Collect follow-up data to at least 1 year.   
•	 Consider EEG sensorimotor biofeedback (Berger et al., 2007) if the previous and other indicated approaches are insufficient, unless and until there is sufficient further support for this approach.

but how this occurs remains uncertain. Cortical plasticity and reorganization (Flor, 2002, 2006; Flor & Andrasik, 2014) have support as one explanation, but available evidence is insufficient to explain the spectrum of PLP (Weeks et al., 2010). Many treatments have been tried, with none showing clear and superior results.

Some researchers (Weeks et al., 2010) believe mirror therapy (Chan et al., 2007) is the “most promising therapy.” However, other studies, with supportive underlying models have shown that SEMG and/or temperature biofeedback are of value (Sherman, Arena, Sherman, & Ernst, 1989; Sherman, Devor, Jones, Katz, & Marbach, 1997; Flor, Birbaumer, & Sherman, 2000).

“Burning” and “cramping,” alone or together, are two very common descriptions of PLP, as is “tingling.” Other less common descriptions are “shooting,” “shocking,” “stabbing,” “throbbing,” and “twisting” pain. The focus on and distinction between burning and cramping are important, because Sherman et al. (1989) suggest that skin temperature biofeedback is more effective for burning pain, and that EMG biofeedback is more effective for cramping pain.

# Phantom Limb  Pain

“Phantom pain” is pain experienced in a body part that no longer exists because of traumatic or surgical amputation. The missing body part is usually, but not exclusively, a foot, leg, hand, or arm (Weeks, Anderson-Barnes, & Tsao, 2010), hence the focus on “phantom” limb pain (PLP). The incidence estimates vary, but reports are often in the 50 to 85% range (Weeks et al., 2010) for those with missing limbs. In one large study of 124 amputees (Kooijman, Dijkstra, Geertzen, Elzinga, & van der Schans, 2000), 40% reported PLP as “always” present, while another 20% described it as being experienced “a few times a day.”

Combined peripheral and cortical mechanisms are presumed to interact to cause phantom pain,

According to this model, SEMG biofeedback from the major muscles of the residual limb is the recommended modality for cramping phantom pain. Skin temperature biofeedback to help increase peripheral blood flow is the modality that Sherman’s group uses for burning pain. Relaxation procedures are an important part of both treatments. Several studies support the use of biofeedback for PLP (Sherman & Sherman, 1985; Sherman et al., 1989, 1997, 1989; Harden et al., 2005). The contributions of Sherman and colleagues to understanding and treating phantom pain with biofeedback date back to Sherman (1976, 1980), McKechnie (1975), and Sherman, Gall, and Gormly (1979). The original series of 16 cases was followed for 1–5 years, and 88% of these patients maintained their pain reduction (Sherman et al., 1989). Sherman and colleagues’ treatment consists of EMG and/or thermal biofeedback and at least muscle relaxation. They expressed cautious optimism about their report of thermal biofeedback for 30 patients, whose pain was presumably related to peripheral vasoconstriction (Sherman et al., 1989). Their rationale for relaxation and thermal biofeedback is straightforward: Decreased blood flow to the residual limb results in phantom pain, usually involving burning and sometimes throbbing and tingling sensations. Sherman et al. point to the evidence for the multiple lines of logic that support the probability that the reason for PLP is due to changes in blood flow in the limb. For reasons that are not clear, there is no mention of these successes for biofeedback in the invited review by Weeks et al. (2010) or on the Mayo Clinic website. However, WebMD includes relaxation therapies and biofeedback.

In an interesting case study, Belleggia and Birbaumer (2001) successfully treated a 69-year-old man with phantom pain in the right arm 3 years after an amputation at the right wrist following an accident (see www.marksschwartzphd.com for more details). The intervention involved increasing awareness of stump muscle activity by SEMG biofeedback and temperature biofeedback for the burning phantom pain. “Telescoping” (retraction of the phantom limb into the stump) occurred during treatment, with the phantom fingers “moving” to the stump and without the patient’s perceiving the arm. At 3 and 12 months, the pain rating was 0. At 12 months, there was more telescoping, with only some fingers vaguely being felt on the stump.

PLP is related to cortical reorganization of the somatosensory and motor cortex; hence, it reveals “plasticity,” or the ability of the brain to change its topography or physical characteristics as a result of events that affect the body—in this case, loss of a limb and biofeedback-assisted intervention (Birbaumer et al., 1997; Knecht et al., 1995, 1998; Flor et al., 1995, 1998; Montoya et al., 1997, 1998; Ramachandran & Rogers-Ramachandran, 2000; Flor, 2002; Flor & Andrasik, 2014). Flor (2002) proposed that in PLP, there is a greater probability of later enhanced reorganization in the brain among patients who experience intense pain before the amputation. This is an extremely important, exciting, and very promising area of research, with substantial heuristic value and clinical implications. Flor (2002) reasoned that this occurs “by altering the peripheral input that enters the brain region that coded a pain memory, e.g. by using EMG or temperature biofeedback or by employing a sensory simulation protocol that provides relevant correlated sensory input to the respective brain region” (p. 223).

Another reasoned speculation by Flor (2002) is that providing biofeedback involving components of event-related potentials or EEG rhythms could result in altering the brain’s responses to pain, although systematic research support and documented cortical reorganization from EEG biofeedback had not been shown as of the time of that article. We could not find published research on these speculations.

There appear to be multiple ways to try and reduce PLP, with no single intervention preferred. There is a good rationale and published support for including at least peripheral biofeedback (i.e., SEMG and temperature) and relaxation methods. Research on PLP has resulted in some research support for the presence of cortical plasticity or reorganization, which is associated with changes in pain and related sensations, but much more basic and applied research are needed.

# Treating Peopl e w ith I ntell ectual D isabil ities

A special relaxation approach developed by Shilling and Poppen (1983), termed “behavioral relaxation training” (BRT), has been a main focus. Its potential value was supported by Lindsay, Baty, Michie, and Richardson (1989), who treated anxiety in people with severe intellectual disabilities, and a case report by Michultka, Poppen, and Blanchard (1988) about successful application of BRT to treat the migraine and frequent tensiontype headache symptoms of a 29-year-old male patient with a severe intellectual disability. Calamari, Geist, and Shahbazian (1987) were among the first to speculate about the value of biofeedback (in this case, EMG) when working with individuals who are intellectually disabled.

Treatment of fecal incontinence has been found to be of value with varied populations. However, in one study, children with fecal incontinence were treated less successfully in a multidisciplinary program that included biofeedback for children with ADHD, intellectual disability, or parent–child relational problems, thus indicating caution and potential benefit from early assessment and treatment when psychosocial comorbitities are present (van Everdingen-Faasen, Gerritsen, Mulder, Fliers, & Groeneweg, 2008).

# Treating Peopl e Diagnosed with  Schizoph renia

People diagnosed with schizophrenia have a variety of symptoms for which biofeedback might help, and biofeedback may be a useful adjunct to arousal reduction techniques in persons with schizophrenia (Ford, Stroebel, Strong, & Szarek, 1982; Spaulding, Storms Goodrich, & Sullivan, 1986). However, people so affected obviously also have a variety of behaviors and other characteristics, including psychomotor deficits and medication effects, that interfere with relaxation therapies and biofeedback-assisted procedures. Using relaxation and biofeedback with such patients is extremely challenging, and goals include changing behaviors such as autonomic dysregulation, muscle tension, and/or cortical interhemispheric asymmetry.

A useful guide and perspective on relaxation therapies and biofeedback in multimodal treatments of patients with schizophrenia was presented by Spaulding et al. (1986). They focused on the vulnerability–stress model of schizophrenia, in which persons with schizophrenia are assumed to have lower thresholds for disorganization that contribute to vulnerability. Stress increases arousal, which brings many competing responses to the same strength, leading to intrusion of inappropriate responses. Interventions that reduce arousal and lower the strengths of competing responses should reduce psychological deficits. These authors concluded: “Arousal-reducing, attentional, and cognitive interventions are appropriate for . . . schizophrenic disorders. . . . Remediation . . . may facilitate the effectiveness of neuroleptic medications, social skills training, and family therapy” (p. 560).

Early publications regarding applications of biofeedback to this population involved a total of 26 patients in studies by Pharr and Coursey (1989), Acosta, Yamamoto, and Wilcox (1978), Nigl and Jackson (1979), Weiner (1979), and Keating (1981); 45 inpatients in studies by Wentworth-Rohr (1988); and a single case report by Stein and Nikolic (1989). More recent research also supported the value of various self-regulatory treatments for this population (e.g., Rickard et al., 1993; Starkey, Deleone, & Flannery, 1995; Schoenberg & David, 2014) with few negative side effects (Rickard, McCoy, Collier, & Weinberger, 1989).

There also is evidence that patients with schizophrenia can learn to regulate cortical activity (Gruzelier, 2000; Schneider et al., 1992; Schoenberg & David, 2014). With further work and development, such approaches may prove to be particularly helpful for targeting the attentional, motivational, and arousal dysregulations that characterize patients with severe psychiatric disturbances.

# Treatment Considerations

The following treatment considerations and guidelines are based on the experience and clinical wisdom of the late I. Wentworth-Rohr (1988)

and Liberman, Nuechterlein, and Wallace (1982), as reported by Spaulding et al., 1986).

•	 Select cooperative patients who are not overtly psychotic.   
•	 Consider patients in the prodromal, postacute, and chronic stages.   
•	 Eliminate all clutter and other distracting stimuli in the treatment room. Divide the tasks into simple steps. Use shortened versions of relaxation procedures.   
•	 Use graphic charts with clear and simple information to show patients their progress. Use praise for proper and correct responding, and mild criticism contingent on improper and incorrect responses. Pay meticulous attention to patients’ questions and apprehensions. Devote extra time to making sure that patients have a clear and concrete understanding of the source of the signal.   
•	 Consider special treatment procedures for this population. Consider carefully explaining that relaxation techniques “take one’s attention and mind and placing them where one wants to.” Discourage free association and any techniques or statements that suggested “letting the mind go blank or empty.” Remind patients “to maintain their attention (passively) on their body regions, rather than to allow their minds or attention to drift off.” Encourage “recollection of an experience of selfadequacy and a sense of tranquility.” If the frontal EMG falls to less than 2 microvolts (100–200 Hz) and respiration becomes obviously slow and shallow, then lessen the relaxation depth by instructing patients to recall specific scenes or images apparently to avoid having patients fall asleep or slip into “ego regression.” Expect physiological changes to occur in the desired direction, even with patients taking a variety of very potent psychotropic medications.   
•	 Do not expect new symptoms to develop. A small percentage of patients may show worsening of symptoms, but this is unlikely to be due to treatment. Use uncluttered visual displays. For example, display one or two channels rather than more. Keep other information off the screens if possible.   
•	 Repeat steps often before moving to new tasks.   
•	 Recognize that muscle tension is often much higher in these patients than in most other patients.

•	 Consider role reversal when teaching relaxation and using biofeedback. For example, allow a patient to teach you a procedure with and without the biofeedback. •	 Expect that significant reduction of psychophysiological tension may start within the first sessions, but that successful reduction of symptoms and maintenance are likely to require nine or more sessions with most of these patients.

A diagnosis of schizophrenia and the presence of psychotropic medications should not deter practitioners. The combination of medication with biofeedback treatments may be needed for these patients.

# Ess ential T remor

Essential tremor (ET) is very common (Metzer, 1994; Louis, Wendt, & Ford, 2000b). Louis et al. (2000b) report that at least mild and detectable tremor occurs in almost all older adults, and that in at least one activity, it is of at least moderate amplitude in about 1 in 3 older adults. ET is the most prevalent idiopathic neurological movement disorder (Lundervold, Belwood, Craney, & Poppen, 1999); it is present in an estimated 14% of adults. The prevalence of ET increases significantly with age (Louis et al., 1995) and is slightly but consistently higher in men than in women (Salemi et al., 1994). The average age of onset is 45 years, but about 29% of patients show onset before age 30. Longer duration correlates with increased disability. A positive family history is reported in more than 60% of patients (Koller, Busenbark, & Miner, 1994). There is no known relationship with Parkinson’s disease (Pahwa & Koller, 1993). Many patients with ET express social embarrassment about their condition (Pahwa et al., 1995). Metzer (1992) reported embarrassment, shame, and stress among 65–81% of patients. Persons with ET also have difficulties with activities of daily living (ADLs) and social situations.

Treatment for ET often involves pharmacological approaches. Drugs such as propranolol and primidone are reported to be helpful in about 40–65% of patients, as are benzodiazepines. However, this may be an overestimate of success (Bain et al., 1993), due to unreliable measures and problems with experimental methods (Lundervold et al., 1999). Alcohol ingestion is effective at reducing tremor in as many as 74% of patients (Koller et al., 1994). Thalamic stimulation and botulinum toxin (Botox) injections have also been used with varying success in treatment (Britton, 1995). Cognitive and behavioral therapies have been shown to be effective with patients who have a fear of showing bodily symptoms in public (Scholing & Emmelkamp, 1996).

Behavioral relaxation training for tremor management has been successfully used by Chung, Poppen, and Lundervold (1995) and Lundervold et al. (1999). Their treatment has led to statistically significant reductions in clinical and self-report tremor ratings, ADL disability, and social anxiety. In the 1999 study, Lundervold et al. reported on two patients ages 73 and 83, and provided good support for the efficacy of relaxation for ET in older adults. The methodological features of note in this work are the use of “extended baselines, a controlled, single-case experimental design, reliability of observations, systematic changes in multiple variables, and use of statistical tests . . . [and] use of a significant other to provide ratings” (p. 132). The authors also note that “relaxation . . . may serve as the first step in a multi-step biobehavioral rehabilitation model for ET” (p. 132).

About 50% of patients with ET have head tremor. This condition creates stress, embarrassment, decreased confidence, and anxiety. Horizontal movements are known as “no-no” tremor, and vertical movements are known as “yes-yes” tremor (Rapoport, Braun, Aviv, & Sarova, 1991). Most patients who experience ET of the head also have a tremulous pattern of activity known as “titubation” (Bradley, Daroff, Fenichel, & Marsden, 1996). Other muscles, such as those of speech and the lower extremities, are less often involved.

In a case study (Schwartz et al., 1997), a 60-yearold female who had had the “no-no” form of head ET for 11 years was treated using relaxation and physical therapy exercises within a single-case B-C-B-D design. Phase B was the combination of relaxation and physical therapy treatments. Phase C was physical therapy, which included axial extension of the neck, tensing and releasing upper back muscles, isometric neck strengthening, and stretching and range-of-motion exercises for the neck. Phase D was relaxation (guided instructions for passive relaxation, autogenic-type phrases, and diaphraghmatic breathing), augmented by four biofeedback sessions. The patient reported much embarrassment and social inhibition due to the tremor. She noted that stress (being with other people in social situations, as well as family stress) increased the tremor.

Symptom ratings on a 0- to 3-point scale were kept over 45 days and involved 810 hours of symptom intensity assessment. Analysis of these ratings showed a statistically significant reduction of head-shaking symptoms in response to the combination of relaxation and physical therapy exercises versus either treatment alone (p < .005). The patient reported engaging in both types of procedures, usually three times a day, every day. Her subjective global impression was a 60% overall improvement, and often 100% (i.e., she was able to go several hours a day, and sometimes a few days, without noticing any tremor).

Follow-up 5 months later involved 12 days of completing a symptom log. She reported continuing the relaxation and physical therapy for 2.5 months but then stopping both because of her busy schedule and symptom progress. The average rating of symptom severity for the 12 days of logging was 0.32, with all ratings below 0.4. Home treatment, consisting of relaxation and physical therapy exercises, was instituted for 14 days, with considerable improvement reported. She was then asked to stop the relaxation procedures for 14 days to help isolate the source of effectiveness. After only 6 days the tremor had worsened, so relaxation was restarted and physical therapy exercises continued for 21 days. Physical therapy exercises were then stopped, but once again the tremors quickly worsened (after 4 days). Both treatments were then resumed.

Analysis of self-reported symptom ratings at posttreatment indicated not only significant reductions in tremor symptoms (as noted earlier), but also reduced anxiety and improved social functioning, with a combination of daily relaxation and physical therapy exercises compared to either physical therapy alone (p = .006) or relaxation alone (p = .005). There was no significant difference in terms of treatment effect with regard to the first and third phases of treatment, when both relaxation and physical therapy occurred. The patient reported that her husband and other persons had commented favorably on her overall improvement. She was able to go to the beauty parlor with more confidence, less anxiety, and minimal tremor.

# Select Considerations for Assessment and Treatment

We make the following suggestions for practitioners treating ET:

•	 Consider muscle relaxation therapies. •	 Consider including EMG biofeedback-assisted

procedures to help detect dysfunctional neuromuscular patterns and to correct sensorimotor functioning (Lundervold et al., 1999). •	 Use a baseline of several days to a few weeks, if practical. •	 Use multiple and reliable measures of tremor, if possible (e.g., the 10-point severity/disability scale developed by Bain et al., 1993). See also Montgomery and Reynolds (1991) for 10-point self-ratings, and Louis et al. (2000a) for other measures. •	 Use multiple raters, if practical.

# Visual D isorders

Published studies regarding biofeedback applications for ophthalmological disorders have a long history (Rotberg & Surwit, 1981; Halperin & Yolton, 1986; Trachtman, 1987, 1990) and are listed with references by Giorgi, Contestabile, Pacella, and Gabrieli (2005). Examples include visual acuity/accommodation, strabismus, nystagmus, benign essential blepharospasm, intraocular pressure, and macular degeneration.

We still admit being myopic about accommodation of biofeedback applications to visual acuity and most other applications for ophthalmic disorders. We have also avoided aligning our focus on strabismus and have oscillated about nystagmus. For interested and select readers, the references for this section will be eye-opening and help them improve their focus (more information and links, see www.marksschwartzphd.com).

In a noteworthy study, Giorgi et al. (2005) reported “significant subjective improvement of visual acuity in 130/179 eyes” (p. 393) in 110 patients with several different vision disorders resulting in reduced visual acuity (e.g., age-related macular degeneration [114 eyes], myopic macular degeneration [39 eyes]). This was compared to a placebo group for which there was improved visual acuity in only two of 47 eyes among 34 patients. The biofeedback process and device was described as “visual training with infrared photo-stimulation using a specially designed biofeedback instrument, termed the ‘improved biofeedback integrated system’ ” (p. 393). This was a larger scale study following the initial pilot study and detailed description of the initial biofeedback apparatus (Contestabile et al., 2002). Other noteworthy articles are by a different group of investigators (Vingolo, Cavarretta, Domanico, Parisi, & Malagola, 2007; Vingolo, Salvatore, & Cavarretta, 2009) using a new and different and instrument (MP-1 microperimeter) and “biofeedback strategies aimed at improving retinal sensitivity and fixation stability” (p. 128). The first article focused on 27 eyes among 15 patients with age-related maculopathy. “All patients displayed an improvement in visual acuity, fixation behaviour, retinal sensitivity and reading speed” (Vingolo et al., 2007, p. 185). The second article was a pilot study of low-vision rehabilitation, with 9 eyes in five patients with macular disease. They report significantly improved reading speed presumably due to “increasing attentional modulation . . . [that] “help the brain to fix the final preferred retinal locus. . . . Audio feedback facilitates stimuli transmission between intraretinal neurons as well as between the retina and brain” (Vingolo et al., 2009, p. 127).

Benign essential blepharospasm is one type of involuntary eyelid spasm. SEMG biofeedbackassisted treatments were described by Peck (1977), Roxanas, Thomas, and Rapp (1978), Rowan and Sedlacek (1981), Brantley, Carnrike, Faulstich, and Barkemeyer (1985), Rotberg and Surwit (1981), Murphy and Fuller (1984), and Surwit and Rotberg (1984) reported case studies of patients treated with SEMG biofeedback who achieved various degrees of successful reductions in spasm frequency. In current clinical practice, biofeedback is sometimes recommended in mild cases or in patients who are not responding to the best treatments.

We also found it relevant that the Mayo Clinic website, which does not include any biofeedback or related interventions in their content on vision disorders/conditions, does include the following statement in the glaucoma content: “Stress may trigger an attack of acute angle-closure glaucoma. If you're at risk of this condition, find healthy ways to cope with stress.”

It is also worth noting a recent paper by Trachtman (2010) details the relationship between vision and the hypothalamus and implications for treatments of ocular diseases.

In summary, one still needs powerful binoculars to view the location of biofeedback and related interventions on the frontier for vision and oculomotor abnormalities, but they are still visible and in the past few years have become yet more visible.

# Dermatol ogicall y Rel ated C onditions

The rationale for using various applied psychophysiological interventions, including biofeedback and relaxation therapies, has been the long-term view that psychological and biobehavioral factors contribute to some dermatological disorders. There is still support for a meaningful relationship between some dermatological conditions and various nonpharmacological applied psychophysiological interventions, including biofeedback (Shenefelt, 2003, 2005; Chuh, Wong, & Zawar, 2006). “Biofeedback can improve cutaneous problems that have an autonomic nervous system component” (Shenefelt, 2003, p. 114).

# Psoriasis

Part of the rationale for using biofeedback and other applied psychophysiological interventions for psoriasis involves the recognition that emotional factors and psychological stress can affect the onset and/or aggravation of symptoms (Heller, Lee, & Koo, 2011). Heller et al. referenced eight clinical studies that support the hypothesis that stress exacerbates psoriasis. Of these, a few were prospective studies (e.g., Picardi & Abeni, 2001; Verhoeven et al., 2009). They also note the study in which Fortune et al. (2003) reported that high amounts of worry (compared to low-levels) might negatively impact improvement rate (i.e., slower clearance of lesions) in patients receiving medical treatment (i.e., psoralen plus ultraviolet A [PUVA] treatment).

Additionally, in a study of 37 patients treated with ultraviolet B (UVB) or PUVA therapy, KabatZinn et al. (1998) found that stress reduction may help to accelerate the rate of clearance. Patients were randomly assigned to either an audiotapeguided meditative stress reduction exercise during the light treatment, or a control group receiving the light treatment alone. Study findings revealed that patients in the stress reduction group reached the clearing point (or the point at which less than 5% of the baseline level of psoriasis remained) more rapidly than controls in both UVB and PUVA therapies.

Furthermore, when using UVB or PUVA treatments, there is indication from Kabat-Zinn et al. (1998) that for patients with moderate to severe psoriasis, the rate of skin clearing can be enhanced over that of control patients, even with only an audio-taped meditative stress reduction recording during sessions. Although the samples were small, the effect was pronounced. Based on these and other studies reviewed by Heller et al. (2011), one can reasonably conclude that there is support for the contention that psoriasis onset and worsening is impacted by stress, and that stress management can play a role in helping such patients.

However, data supporting the value of using relaxation and thermal biofeedback are limited to only a few individual case studies (e.g., Winchell & Watts, 1988; Goodman, 1994) and a small case series (e.g., Keinan, Segal, Gal, & Brenner, 1995). Employing temperature biofeedback is logical in some respects, but how changes in temperature might be playing a mediating role is very unclear, because skin cooling was the focus in the study by Keinan et al., while the study by Goodman (1994) focused on warming of peripheral sites (for more information, see www.marksschwartzphd.com). The effects of mindfulness meditation, another applied psychophysiological approach mentioned earlier, provides further support (Kabat- Zinn et al., 1998). These supportive reports are nonetheless very tentative and preliminary.

# Skin Ulcers

The application of thermal biofeedback for the treatment of skin ulcers has received support from Shulimson, Lawrence, and Iacono (1986), Guthrie, Moeller, and Guthrie (1983), Rice and Schindler (1992), and Rice, Kalker, Schindler, and Dixon (2001). These studies have included patients with and without diabetes, although most reports involve diabetic skin ulcers. The Rice et al. (2001) study had many excellent methodological features and the results were very supportive of the value of the combination of patient education, relaxation, and a single-session of thermal biofeedback. The treated group was compared to a control group that received the same standard medical wound care and biweekly office visits, plus “instructions to relax 15 to 20 minutes daily . . . using a selfselected method” (p. 134) such as music, television, or daydreaming. Fourteen of sixteen (88%) of the experimental (E) group showed healed ulcers compared to 7/17 (41%) in the control (C) group. All four of the C group patients who crossed over to the E group showed complete healing. Healing rate was significantly faster for the E group, and ambulation ratings were significantly higher for the E group.

Furthermore, improved sensory nerve function in large myelinated fibers of the peroneal nerve was demonstrated. Average warming within a session increased significantly for the E group from Session 1 to Session 2, from 2°F to 5°F. Documentation of the results involved an ulcer-scoring measure, healing rates measured by changes in wound area, photographic documentation, an ambulation measure, and ratings of clinically positive healings by podiatric physicians who were unaware of group assignments.

Treatment was comprehensive and included cessation of smoking, adhering to non-weightbearing recommendations, topical and oral medications, daily wound care, and avoiding trauma and new wounds. Patient education included fostering considerable patient responsibility for selftreatment, recommended lifestyle changes, new skills, and encouragement. Combined relaxation procedures included muscle relaxation, breathing, and self-generated self-statements for warmth and heaviness, and a portable temperature device for providing temperature feedback from the volar side of a great toe (for more useful information for practitioners derived from this study, see www. marksschwartzphd.com).

Rice et al. (2001) recognized the limitations of their small sample (especially the homogeneous ethnicity of the upper Midwest), but this does not detract from the value of the results. The authors provide logical reasons for including thermal biofeedback with relaxation (e.g., helping teach patients “the underlying concept of vascular physiology” [p. 134], and helping them “know that they were relaxing properly” [p. 139]). However, accomplishing this in one session—presumably (although this is not clearly stated) at the beginning of treatment and before relaxation procedures started— would be a lot to expect. The focus of the intervention was then on relaxing at least once a day, 5 days each week during the 4–12 weeks needed for the ulcers to heal fully. Compliance with this was reported as very good. One can assume that these patients used the relaxation procedures more often than once a day, although this was not assessed.

More research is obviously needed, but this is a significant advance in an otherwise impoverished area for such a serious condition. Nonhealing or poorly healing foot ulcers occur with several medical problems that involve peripheral arterial disease. Perhaps some patients will at least be able to increase the success of medical/surgical procedures by utilizing similar approaches.

The report by Ward and Van Moore (1995) on finger ulcers and scleroderma is mentioned only because of the inclusion of biofeedback with medical, nonoperative treatment. One cannot separate the effects of the interventions that resulted in healed ulcers in six of 15 hands, and healing that remained at a 2-year follow-up.

In one study of solitary rectal ulcer syndrome, biofeedback was part of a behavioral approach that also included retraining of toileting, pelvic floor muscle coordination, posture, and the use of abdominal muscles during defecation (Vaizey, Roy, & Kamm, 1997). These British researchers reported on the role and usefulness of these therapies and procedures for a group of 13 consecutive patients with this syndrome, including five for whom prior surgical treatment had failed.

Persons with spinal cord injuries cannot feel the discomfort in their gluteus maximi areas so they do not change postures to relieve the pressure; they therefore develop decubitus ulcers. A new biofeedback device developed by Verbunt and Bartneck (2010), a pad with many sensors, is placed under the person and provides tactile feedback to help prevent these ulcers. We hope that this will lead to more rehabilitation research.

# Primary Hyperhidrosis

In White’s (1986) review of major medical treatments for primary (idiopathic) hyperhidrosis, he noted that biofeedback and psychotherapy are worth considering for some patients. In their review, Hashmonai, Kopelman, and Assalia (2000) also noted that “hypnosis, psychotherapy, and biofeedback have been beneficial in a limited number of cases” (p. 211). The only reports of biofeedback treatment we found each involved a few cases (Duller & Gentry, 1980; Farrar & Hartje, 1987; Kawahara & Kikuchi, 1992; Alvarez, Cortes, & Rodriguez, 1993; Singh & Singh, 1993). See (www.marksschwartzphd.com) for more information about these reports. We could locate no further clinical cases or research using biofeedback for primary hyperhidrosis.

The highly selective endoscopic transthoracic limited sympathotomy for palmar–plantar hyperhidrosis (Atkinson, Fode-Thomas, Fealey, Eisenach, & Goerss, 2011) has become “the single best technique for curing patients with palmer, axillary, or plantar HH [hyperhidrosis]” (Krasna, 2011, p. 717). It has a very high degree of success and as important, or moreso, an extremely low rate of severe compensatory hyperhidrosis, resulting in extremely few cases of profoundly increased new sweating elsewhere for which there is no treatment. See the links in www.marksschwartzphd.com for more details.

We suspect that a combination of factors resulted in the lack of further biofeedback studies, and the development of these successful and minimally complicated surgical procedures is surely one major factor. This does not negate the rationale for considering biofeedback for persons for whom surgery is not an option or not preferred, and for whom conservative medical approaches have been unsuccessful. Perhaps there will be a resurgence of interest in trying biofeedback and in investigating for whom this might be a reasonable option. Until then, this remains a part of the frontier. See www. marksschwartzphd.com for some guidelines when using electrodermal biofeedback for hyperhidrosis.

# Chronic Idiopathic Uriticaria

There also is support for a significant role of psychological stress in the triggering or worsening of chronic idiopathic urticaria (CIU; Berrrino et al., 2006; Malhotra & Mehta, 2008). The latter article concludes that there is an indication for a “role of relaxation therapies and stress management programs in chronic diseases such as . . . chronic urticaria (p. 594). Yet we found no reports of biofeedback or related applied psychophysiological approaches for CIU.

# Insomnia

Insomnia (i.e., primary and secondary) affects about one-third of adults (Morgenthaler et al., 2006). Insomnia contributes significantly to many other symptoms and disorders, and excessive daytime sleepiness (often due to insomnia) interferes significantly with the efficient use of relaxation therapies, biofeedback, and other applied psychophysiological therapies for various symptoms and disorders. Various therapeutic relaxation procedures and biofeedback are among the effective interventions to help people with various types of insomnia (Morgenthaler et al., 2006). This conclusion has received much support from research dating back several decades (e.g., Freedman & Papsdorf, 1976). For reviews of behavioral/ nonpharmacological interventions (including relaxation and many others), see Morin (1993), Rosen, Lewin, Goldberg, and Woolfolk (2000), and Morin et al. (1999). For the latest practice parameters of psychological and behavioral treatments of insomnia, see Morgenthaler et al., 2006). Among the conclusions of this official report of the American Academy of Sleep Medicine are the following:

•	 Based on three randomized controlled trials, multicomponent therapy (without cognitive therapy) is effective and recommended in the treatment of chronic insomnia.   
•	 Relaxation training is an effective, recommended therapy in the treatment of chronic insomnia. In addition to the prior report, this was based on four additional randomized controlled trials. Relaxation procedures includes progressive muscle relaxation and autogenic training.   
•	 Biofeedback also is an effective and a recommended therapy in the treatment of chronic insomnia; this is unchanged from the earlier practice parameter, as there were no additional studies.

Relaxation procedures are typically provided to alleviate sleep-onset insomnia, and sometimes to help people return to sleep after interruption. Part of the rationale is that many persons with insomnia are not aware of their tension or they have difficulty letting go of tension and arousal. For many, there are intrusive thoughts at bedtime that interfere with sleep onset, and the relaxation procedures reduces the thoughts and interference they cause.

For many persons, relaxation procedures without biofeedback are sufficient. For many, biofeedback (commonly EMG biofeedback) provides useful additional information and help in learning to relax more efficiently and with more confidence (Hauri & Esther, 1990).

However, many persons with insomnia do not show excess physical tension or psychological anxiety; they are sufficiently relaxed in bed and can be uncomfortable trying to relax further (Hauri, Percy, Hellekson, Hartmann, & Russ, 1982; Hauri, 1991; Morin, 1993). Persons with insomnia, like those with other conditions, need to understand the rationale for the use of relaxation procedures and to accept these techniques as appropriate for them. One implication is the importance of assessing a person’s tension/arousal before recommending relaxation. Hauri (1991) reminds us that psychological, muscle, and sympathetic arousal correlate only modestly with each other; thus, people often show elevated tension/arousal in some but not other areas.

In an ideal clinical practice, a practitioner could and would assess multiple types of tension in a person’s own bed, at bedtime, when the person is trying to start sleep (Hauri, 1991).

Increased tension can and does exist under these conditions in some persons who are not excessively tense during the day. However advantageous this practice might be, it is not practical and, as Hauri (1991) states, it is “cumbersome.” He has described the procedures he uses in a book chapter. He begins by monitoring one channel of frontal EMG and hand temperature while the patient reclines on a bed in his office. After observing the EMG and temperature for a period, he instructs the patient to “relax as deeply as possible, just as if you wanted to sleep” and continues his observations for another 5 minutes. The clinical questions he considers include the following (Hauri, 1991, p. 71):

•	 “Is the patient relaxed when no demands are made, but tenses considerably as soon as relaxation is demanded?” “Does the patient relax deeper and deeper over a course of 5 minutes, or does the patient become more and more tense as time progresses?”

Hauri (1991) reports starting with various relaxation procedures, sometimes with biofeedback assistance, if there are signs of increased muscle tension or sympathetic arousal. He strongly recommends that patients initially avoid the use of relaxation procedures at bedtime in order to minimize failure experiences. When they develop the skills and confidence in their relaxation skills, he then shifts to using the relaxation at bedtime or later during the sleep period after sleep interruption.

The mechanisms by which relaxation therapies work are not clearly (or necessarily usually) due to muscle relaxation or other forms of cultivated low physiological arousal. The cognitive distraction or cognitive refocusing effects that result from focusing on somatic relaxation, as well as the effects of other cognitive-behavioral approaches, are thought to have important roles in the mechanisms by which various relaxation procedures work (Borkovec & Fowles, 1973; Hauri, 1991; Morin et al., 1999; Edinger, Wohlgemuth, Radtke, Marsh, & Quillian, 2001). Focusing on relaxation procedures helps people focus away from cognitive activities such as worry, anxiety about not sleeping, review of the day’s activities, and planningrelated cognitions.

Consider informing patients that part of the rationale for the use of relaxation and cognitive refocusing procedures is as follows:

“Imagine yourself pinned down in an earthquake, or partially hidden behind enemy lines with enemy soldiers close by, and you need to remain awake for many hours beyond normal in order to survive. The natural biological method to remain awake is to use isometric muscle tension and/or cognitive activity to remain awake and sufficiently alert for lengthy periods. That’s the ‘good news.’ The ‘bad news’ is that these ‘hard-wired’ features of our nervous systems also serve to keep us awake in the safety of our beds. Various relaxation and cognitive refocusing procedures allow the brain to allow sleep to start by sufficiently reducing the physical and psychological/cognitive tension and arousal.”

Multicomponent interventions (Hauri & Linde, 1990; Morin, 1993; Ancoli-Israel, 1996) are typical in clinical practice. Another type of biofeedback that might have potential use for insomnia is EEG feedback. Hauri et al. (1982) used theta and sensorimotor rhythm (SMR) feedback, each of which was helpful for different persons. Those with anxiety and tension benefited from theta feedback but not SMR, and only SMR was helpful for those who were already relaxed but still having sleep problems before treatment. However, we do not know how this approach compares with relaxation therapies, other forms of biofeedback, and other therapies, and the report by Morgenthaler et al. (2006) did not mention EEG biofeedback.

Research provides support for the value of psychological and behavioral treatments for older adults with primary, secondary, and mixed insomnia (Morgenthaler et al., 2006). This report noted eight studies of older people (i.e., average age, more than 60 years). Seven of these studies were randomized controlled trials, including five studies demonstrating the effectiveness of psychological and behavioral interventions in older adults.

# Insomnia Secondary to Medical or Psychiatric Conditions

Morgenthaler et al. (2006) also concluded that, based on 11 studies, including four randomized controlled trials, psychological and behavioral interventions are effective in the treatment of secondary insomnia. Although biofeedback per se is not included in these treatment packages, and might not be needed, there is still a good rationale for including biofeedback for selected persons if the practitioner is tailoring the intervention to the individual.

# Phonatory (Voice) Disorders and Performance1,2

Information in this section is from the excellent review by Maryn, De Bodt, and van Cauwenberge (2006). The topics include input devices such as a microphone to measure acoustic energy, SEMG, and laryngoscopic biofeedback. The table of procedural aspects in effective studies of phonatory biofeedback lists 36 articles,3 of which 18 are group or case studies, or reports of results obtained using these three modalities. Fifteen of these 18 studies supported the benefits of biofeedback, although only two studies used a true experimental design and the reviewers noted cautions and limitations. The positive results were for decreased laryngeal tension, improved voice quality, and/or resolved dysphonia. See www. marksschwartzphd.com for selected additional references and abstracts.

# Nausea and V omiting

Anticipatory nausea/vomiting (ANV) reaction associated with chemotherapy remains a problem for a sizable minority of patients (Roscoe, Morrow, Aapro, Molassiotis, & Olver, 2011); Morrow et al., 1998). Classical conditioning is the most accepted explanation for how ANV develops (Dadds, Bovbjerg, Redd, & Cutmore, 1997; Stockhorst, Rnck, & Klosterhalfen, 2007; Roscoe et al., 2011). The unconditioned stimulus is the chemotherapy; the unconditioned response is the initial nausea/ vomiting (N/V). After repeated pairings, the previously neutral stimuli (e.g., medical and nursing staff and hospital sights and smells) become conditioned stimuli that are able to elicit the conditioned response.

There is extensive research support for various applied psychophysiological treatments for ANV associated with chemotherapy (Roscoe et al., 2011; Figueroa-Moseley et al., 2007; Carey & Burish, 1988; Burish & Jenkins, 1992; Yoo, Ahn, Kim, Kim, & Han, 2005; see www.marksschwartzphd.com for earlier references). Whether or not practitioners use biofeedback to assist relaxation depends on their preferences, the tailored needs of patients, and practical considerations.

Cyclic vomiting syndrome (CVS) is rare and evaluation and treatment challenging (Talley, 2007). The first application of heart rate variability (HRV) biofeedback, combined with cogntivebehavioral therapy (CBT), to a patient with CVS was reported by Slutsker, Konichezky, and Gothelf (2010). They successfully treated a 13-year-old boy and provided follow-up at 6 months. Functional vomiting is “very rare” (Talley, 2007) and treatment also is challenging. Although the rationale for relaxation and/or biofeedback for selected patients seems justified, surprisingly, we found no published reports. One of us successfully used relaxation therapies for several patients with functional vomiting many years ago (Schwartz, 1977).

# Idiopathic Scol iosis

For the 2–4% of adolescents with “idiopathic scoliosis”—a lateral curvature of the spine that is severe enough to produce truncal deformity, a creative and ambulatory biofeedback device was used to enhance their awareness of incorrect posture and facilitate correct posture (Dworkin et al., 1985; Miller, 1985; Birbaumer, Flor, Cevey, Dworkin, & Miller, 1994; Wong, Mak, Luk, Evans, & Brown, 2001). Biofeedback also provides “activation of specific groups of the patient’s muscles to correct scoliotic curvature” and “develops learned muscle control through the patient’s own nervous system rather than forcing contraction with extrinsic electric currents” (Dworkin, et al., 1985, p. 2497). For a description of the biofeedback device, the rationale, and its successful application for 12 adolescents with progressive idiopathic scoliosis, see the previously cited references.

Birbaumer et al. (1994) successfully treated patients with both scoliosis and kyphosis, a forward curvature of the spine. Treatment was beneficial for more than half of the patients in a series of 13 patients in Hong Kong with progressive adolescent idiopathic scoliosis (Wong et al., 2001). These authors reported that the scoliosis in most of the 13 patients remained under control after 18 months. Five completed their skeletal growth without curve progression. Four others were still wearing the device. Four of the other seven had curve deterioration and switched to a brace, and three others stopped the biofeedback early.

Patients typically prefer this biofeedback device to a rigid spinal orthosis, and most easily learned to use the feedback. For advantages and disadvantages of this device, see Wong et al. (2001) and Dworkin et al. (1985).

Conservative treatments for scoliosis, the need to tailor treatment to each individual, and the rationale for biofeedback to facilitate ensuring “that local correction translates to the automatic maintenance of the corrected body posture in a standing position” (p. 1) was discussed by Nowotny, Nowotny-Czupryna, & Czupryna (2010).

# Herpes

The rationale for considering stress management interventions for recurrent herpes simplex is the presumed link between psychosocial stress and recurrences. Reports of applied psychophysiological treatments for reducing genital herpes outbreaks among people with frequent outbreaks are sparse (Longo, Clum, & Yeager, 1988; VanderPlate & Kerrick, 1985; Burnette, Koehn, Kenyon-Jump, Hutton, & Stark, 1991). There are no reports since the previous edition of this volume; see Schwartz & Andrasik (2003) or go to www.marksschwartzphd.com.

We recommend that therapists consider providing relaxation therapy to patients for whom medication is either contraindicated or ineffective, or who continue to experience adverse effects or distress. We do not know the effects of relaxation on milder cases, or on patients for whom stress is less of a factor or not a factor. For example, some recurrence of symptoms stems from physical irritation of the affected area. In conclusion, this application remains a frontier outpost.

# Sickl e‑Cell C rises

For people with sickle-cell disease, emotional distress may be a factor increasing the risk for symptom crises that include headaches, generalized pain, and shortness of breath due to restricted oxygenated blood, along with more serious symptoms (e.g., strokes, kidney failure, and other permanent organ damage). Emotional distress may increase the risk of stress-related symptoms along with the crises. This is the rationale for considering applied psychophysiological interventions as adjuncts to treatment.

We found no further reports of biofeedback or related applied psychophysiological interventions that were published since the two uncontrolled case studies noted in the third edition (Thomas, Koshy, Patterson, Dorn, & Thomas, 1984; Zeltzer, Keller, Dash, & Holland, 1979), the combination applied psychophysiological successful intervention for eight patients reported by Cozzi, Tryon, and Sedlacek (1987), and the comprehensive, multidisciplinary approach for management of sicklecell syndrome pain reported by Collins, Kaslow, Doepke, Eckman, and Johnson (1998).

# Erythromel alg ia

Erythromelalgia (a.k.a. “erythemalgia” and “erythmalgia”) involves sudden and intensive spastic dilation of blood vessels, or “paroxysmal vasodilation.” Symptoms include burning pain, increased skin temperature, and redness. The feet and, less often, the hands are usually the sites of the symptoms. The name comes from Greek words meaning “red” (erythros), “extremities” (melos), and “pain” (algos). Erythromelalgia can be idiopathic or secondary to other diseases.

Biofeedback is typically not mentioned on websites regarding this disorder. For summaries of treatments, natural history, and a summary of the only two published cases involving biofeedback (Putt, 1978; Cahn & Garber, 1990), see Schwartz and Andrasik (2003) and www.marksschwartzphd. com.

There is a slight chance that biofeedback might play a useful role in the treatment of some patients with this disorder. Selecting patients with the primary form is probably wiser. Collaboration with physicians who are knowledgeable about this disorder is essential. Practitioners should consider four or more temperature channels, because of the variability that occurs across digits and the need to check both feet and both hands. Many sessions over many months are expected. Consider providing at least some of this therapy pro bono or within a research project, because of the embryonic stage of this treatment for this disorder and the length of time it involves.

For more information, consider contacting the Erythromelalgia Association, 200 Old Castle Lane, Wallingford, PA 19086, 610-566-0797(www. erythromelalgia.org) or the National Organization for Rare Diseases (www.rarediseases.org), and/or Davis, O’Fallon, Rogers, and Rooke (2000).

# Psychoneuroimm unol ogy and C ancer

Research supports the conclusion that biobehavioral factors and the brain influence the immune system (Gruber et al., 1993; Ader, Felten, & Cohn, 1991; Halley, 1991; O’Leary, 1990; Bovbjerg et al., 1990; Donaldson, 2000). Furthermore, there is support for the negative effects of stress on immunological activity (Ader et al., 1991; McDaniel, 1996). There is also support for the contention that biobehavioral strategies influence measurable immune system changes and are of potential therapeutic importance (e.g., see Gruber et al., 1993; Halley, 1991). These strategies include relaxation and biofeedback. Examples of other behavioral interventions that are within the rubric of applied psychophysiology and could benefit the immune system include social support (Kiecolt-Glaser et al., 1985), guided imagery and relaxation (Gruber, Hall, Hersh, & Dubois, 1988; Gruber et al., 1993), immune system imagery (Rider et al., 1990), musicassisted cell-specific imagery (Rider & Achterberg, 1989), self-disclosure (Pennebaker, Kiecolt-Glaser, & Glaser, 1988), and visualization or mental imagery (Donaldson, 2000). Most of the studies reporting relaxation and biofeedback to be beneficial to the immune system involve various types of normal samples (Peavey, Lawlis, & Goven, 1985; McGrady et al., 1992). However, there are also reports with patient samples (Gruber et al., 1988, 1993).

Gruber et al. (1993) used an applied psychophysiological treatment package with 13 patients who had had modified radical mastectomies and were lymph-node-negative. This study combined relaxation procedures, guided imagery, and frontal EMG biofeedback in a controlled single-crossover design. Seven patients started treatment; the other six patients in a delayed-treatment group eventually participated in the treatment package. The intervention influenced immune function in immune assays in the desired direction, according to multiple pre- and posttreatment comparisons. It produced “statistically significant effects primarily on T-cell populations including natural killer cells. Antibodies were minimally affected” (p. 14). Note that “several weeks to months were required for changes to reach statistical significance” (p. 14); this led to the speculation that “long-term effects of behavioral intentions . . . are cumulative” (p. 15). The investigators provided necessary and ethical cautions for interpreting their data. They also acknowledged the complex and unclear relationship between immune changes and reduced physiological activity; the relationship is not linear. They speculated on potential mechanisms and focused on altered plasma levels of cortisol. Basic and essential questions and challenges to consider in this domain include the following:

•	 Do biobehavioral and central nervous system factors change the immune system in both undesired and desired directions? For example, Gregerson, Roberts, and Amiri (1996) reported that “high and low absorbers [as measured by the Absorption Scale] had diametrically opposite immune responses [mucosal immunoglobulin A in saliva] to relaxation with imagery” (p. 162) among a group of undergraduates.   
• Can one help teach the immune system to make significant changes in desired directions with applied psychophysiological strategies such as relaxation and biofeedback?   
•	 Can one elicit immunological changes among patients that reach statistical and clinical or “real-world” significance?   
• Which immunological conditions and diseases respond to applied psychophysiological strategies? Do changes among patients last long enough to have long-term effects on preventing or reversing immunologically related diseases?   
Which applied psychophysiological strategies result in immunological changes that reach statistical and clinical significance?

Clearly, the answer to at least the first two questions is “yes.” The answers to the others remain more challenging and elusive. One must continue to speculate about the mechanisms of change. For example, do these include physiological relaxation; cognitive changes associated with the relaxation and biofeedback procedures; changes in hormones, catecholamines, plasma levels of cortisol, or other chemicals; or some combination of these? This uncertainty is not different from that present with many interventions, including other applications of biofeedback and relaxation, many medications, and psychotherapies.

Major challenges remain in this field. Research must confirm results with more samples of patients and show the clinical significance of the immunological changes beyond statistical significance. Furthermore, research must clarify the active ingredients in the strategies. The frontier settlements for immunologically related disorders will remain for a long time.

The promise remains for a role for applied psychophysiology in effecting immunological changes of clinical significance; however, the complexities and challenges are monumental. The land is still untamed and replete with obstacles. Well-meaning inhabitants of the settlements still strive to find the formulas to bring this area into the heartland. Until that happens, prudent practitioners should be very cautious about applications and claims about results.

As a footnote to this topic, it is well worth noting that patients with advanced cancer can learn relaxation procedures taught by nurses or instructional media in an oncology ward, and that the benefits of these procedures include less cancerrelated pain and increased comfort (Sloman, Brown, Aldana, & Chee, 1994). There is also a report supporting the use of relaxation therapies and imagery, or CBT, for treatment-related pain in patients receiving bone marrow transplants (Syrjala, Donaldson, Davis, Kippes, & Carr, 1995).

# Th e U nconscious

Credible scientists have scrutinized the concept of the “unconscious,” and have concluded that “the reality of the unconscious is no longer questionable” (Loftus & Klinger, 1992, p. 761). However, some academic psychologists go beyond skepticism and suggest that the concept “unconscious cognition” does not belong in psychology (Greenwald, 1992). The clinical, theoretical, and research implications of psychophysiological assessment and interventions for unconscious processes include many topics, with much overlap among them. Interested readers should consider the pertinent concepts and topics, discussion, and references listed in Schwartz and Associates (1995).

It has always been recognized that at least some, and often much of the psychophysiological learning and changes that take place during biofeedback occurs without awareness and at an unconscious level. This is true for all biofeedback modalities and many applications. This is not unique to biofeedback. It is valid for many parts of psychotherapy, athletic skills, musical skills, and most learning, especially motor learning.

# Quantum Biofeedback

The rationale for including this odd topic in this section is twofold: (1) The so-called “quantum biofeedback” system claims to work entirely on an unconscious level, and (2) the claims by advocates of quantum biofeedback are not only part of the frontier but also on the fringe that many authors, ourselves included, in the mainstreams of biofeedback would consider this “over the edge.” We are not taking up space in the text for this topic but we include some links and content of interest in www. marksschwartzphd.com.

# Notes

1. This section was formerly called “Functional Voice Disorders” in the third edition of this volume.

2. Voice disorders and performance comprise a subarea of the generic hearing disorders and language therapy. See Maryn, Bodt, and van Cauwenberge (2006) for a list of references for hearing disorders, resonance disorders, and neurogenic speech disorders, including facial paralysis and fluency, swallowing, and respiratory disorders. These are not included in this text due to space and time limitations. 3. See www.marksschwartzphd.com for the five references included in the third edition of this volume but not in Maryn et al. (2006).
